comments.commentBody: This sounds great, but the photos seem to show shredded savoy cabbage and not green cabbage – which is what I call "cabbage" and what you usually find in coleslaw mix. A Caesar coleslaw made with regular cabbage mixed with crunchy croutons served immediately after dressing sounds like it would be an effort to eat. Savoy cabbage, on the other hand, seems a perfect fit, since it's a much more tender. I think the recipe should have pointed out the differences between the two.
@JET, if you used green cabbage would you just let is marinade in the dressing in the fridge for a few hours?
If you sauté those bread cubes in a little butter (salted), they'll stay crisper, taste better, and you won't need to turn on the oven.
@Penny toast the croutons in the toaster oven if you have one- after tossing in olive oil and seasonings, Also quick and easy
is that nappa or savoy cabbage?  doesn't look like regular cabbage.  if savoy, don't you have to soak in water to rid it of bitterness?

@caesar fiend
I wish they’d use the real ingredients. Food stylists don’t always follow recipes to the letter. They use what they think looks best in the photo aesthetically.
@Cooksalot Very true.

That’s definitely savoy cabbage in the photos. I’ve been doing vegan Caesar (just search for the cashew based no oil added version here on NYT cooking)w a mix of savoy and finely cut lacinato kale forever because it travels so well: my favorite roadtrip lunch by the side of the road that feels fancy. Throw in some croutons or air fried chickpeas at eating time. Regular green cabbage does not do the trick savoy is the way to go.
I usually use anchovy paste instead of the whole anchovies.  Same umami, easy to use with a squeeze from the tube, no waste, or left over smelly tin, and keeps in the fridge forever.
Is that Napa cabbage in the picture?  As another commenter noted, you should specify. Thanks.

Made this tonight. Quick and easy and my husband and I both enjoyed it. The dressing was delicious - I added a splash of fish sauce instead of anchovies and it worked great - and the crunchy home made croutons were a hit. Definitely plan to make this again.

The cabbage shown is Savoy Cabbage, not regular green cabbage, and not the cabbage that would be found in a bag of coleslaw mix.  As a fan of cabbage, not all cabbages are created equal!  Nor are they good for all uses.

I use Savoy Cabbage year round for use in both raw, stir fried, and soup and stew dishes.  I have never had an issue with it being bitter.  But suggest you get the freshest produce possible.  Napa Cabbage would also be an ok substitute, but it has less flavor than Savoy.

Shredded cabbage as the basis for a salad is smart. It adds texture, it’s more economical and you can easily find uses for the remainder of the cabbage. Tuna salad made w/shredded cabbage and a little olive oil is ideal in hot weather.
Please specify best cabbage to use: Green, Savoy, Napa?

I’m sorry but does it really matter what kind of cabbage is in the picture? Use whatever cabbage you like or use coleslaw mix if you are busy. 
Also not sure why this would be a chore to eat as on person said.
Straight up green cabbage is very chewy v. romaine. Add in the croutons, and you do have a chore on your hands for a big whole bowl full. A side, maybe not, like slaw.

you gotta talk about the cabbage...so many options
personally I would do a blend of green and savoy

I tried this recipe and agree that the cabbage is a wonderful textural substitute for Romaine. Having said that, the shortcut of using prepared mayo instead of egg yolks is a nonstarter. Melissa should know better.

@Darren
Some fear raw/coddled egg yolks, or they may be pressed for time. The mayonnaise is a decent substitute. Use what you prefer, but there is no shame in the mayo game.

As I said above, i believe Melissa is speaking to a BROAD audience. Not everyone wants to make their own mayo! Melissa does know better as she knows that probably more people would make this recipe because they aren't making their own mayo!
She does.
This may be an excellent salad, but without Romaine I would not consider it to be a Caesar salad.
Could you doctor up a store bought Caesar dressing? Maybe add a splash of Worcestershire, some fresh lemon juice and minced garlic?
what temp is done raccichioraddichil is also a great crunchy alternative
On aluminum foil, torn chunks of fresh French bread, olive oil, butter, shaved small chunks of Parmesan, black pepper, 350 oven. Toss and scrap with a spatula!  Delicious and clean up, easy!
A great recommendation on the torn bread and the roasting of same. Thanks

This is one of those that makes me go "hmmm..."

Fully granting the fact that cabbage holds up better than romaine, cabbage also has that funky bite that, to me, completely changes the overall flavor profile, even when under the best of Caesar dressings.

Meanwhile, the recipe itself is intriguing and I look forward to trying it.

And I'm rarely a stickler about names for dishes that have substituted ingredients, in this case, I'm thinking to offer it as "Caesar Slaw".
@Yoda Maybe cabbage holds up better, but I know I’m not the only person who likes day-old Romaine salads.
I used green cabbage which I salted to let it wilt a bit while the croutons were toasting.  Not really a Caesar but very good.
You can swap out mayo or raw egg yolk for silken tofu. It’s a great emulsifier and doesn’t have the mayo calories nor the raw yolk salmonella.

Thank you for the suggestion of using silken tofu instead of mayo. I wish the recipe writer would include vegetarian and lower fat/calorie substitutions in her original recipe. So many people prefer lighter, healthier dishes with the same flavor profile. As written, each serving of this salad would contain 25g of fat, which is a third to a half of the adult daily allowance.
What is the dark green leafy vegetable in the photo?
@AMARIA Looks like someone snuck some kale in there!

Basically, this recipe is coleslaw with Caesar salad dressing. indeed delicious!
I make Caesar salad dressing Weekly. I also make homemade mayonnaise. I have no idea why they’re adding what seems like commercial mayonnaise to this recipe. You have all the ingredients; you've got your own “mayonnaise” base for Caesar dressing, or coleslaw dressing for cabbage salad.
for me, I don't want to make mayo using raw egg etc. It's just easier and if you use a high quality mayo it's just as good!

I browned the croutons in a pan with butter and olive oil combined with some garlic powder. Then I coated them with a bit more oil, tossed with the parmesan and put them in my air fryer to crisp them up a bit and melt the cheese.  I used green cabbage and a little bit of purple cabbage to add some fun!  We have always used LIMES to make our Caesar salads, so I used limes instead of lemon juice.  I have not put the dressing on the cabbage yet...still letting it sit to meld the flavors.
It's not a Caesar salad without a raw egg yolk.  This recipe is the one I already use (with egg).
Please call it something else. We are diluting original recipes by changing ingredients, in this case the main ingredient, and continuing to use the name.
Napa cabbage is my fave.
I just tried this. It’s just okay. I think I’m sticking with the original version. I’m not sure why I tried this, as I have never had a problem with plain old Romaine.
Delicious! We added lemon zest to the recipe to brighten it up and it made a big difference. Enjoy!

I live in Mexico and I last week I had a perfect Caesar, prepared table side, with anchovies and romaine. I had forgotten how good a true Caesar is and I would rather wait for the real thing. This is the first time I have ever disagreed with Melissa Clark. I believe that cabbage would make a good salad, and cabbage is the preferred garnish for many Jalisco dishes. I am just glad I had a true Caesar again.
I used green cabbage. The ingredients didn’t meld. It had the flavor of Caesar salad but was kind of off. I wish I had used Savoy or Napa cabbage instead.
Any suggestions for a vegetarian substitute for Worcestershire sauce?
@PeaceIsTheWay the umami could be found with coconut liquid aminos perhaps? Or nutritional yeast maybe? I don’t know for sure but they might be worth a try
Could I use ice berg lettuce in this instead of cabbage. I thought I was buying a cabbage but bought an ice berg lettuce. Chat GpT said i can not.
@Salad tossed you COULD, but the iceberg will definitely wilt faster

Of course you can, and it will be delicious, I’m sure.  I just wouldn’t chop the iceberg as finely as you would the cabbage, as its leaves are not as firm as the cabbage’s.  And next time you’re at the grocery store, you’ll buy the cabbage and make the recipe again and that will be very good, too.
And I make eggs Benedict by putting ham, egg, and hollandaise on a bran muffin.
On the mayo quandary, I think a decent compromise between home-made and store-bought is Kewpie Japanese mayo. Available in many supermarkets and all Asian groceries.

Printed this off when it had NO comments; wish I'd checked back! Used half a loaf of Publix Tuscan Style Roasted Garlic Loaf to make croutons, baked in toaster oven, as another reader suggests. They were GREAT! Used a 16 oz. bag of Publix coleslaw mix & carefully picked out all slices of core (vs. leaf). Let veggies & dressing sit in fridge for 2-3 hours before stirring in croutons & serving. Made a very "interesting" Caesar coleslaw. May try again with Savoy cabbage, if I can find it, or not.
No. Just no. If you want to make a salad with cabbage, fine, but do not call it a Caesar salad. That's like making a bourbon cocktail and calling it a martini.

Great! In my frazzled pre-party state, I used the greater attachment on the cuisinart instead of the shredder, so the cabbage was… very easy to pick up with a fork. While not intentional, this actually worked pretty well for a casual gathering where you might be balancing a plate in one hand. Flavor was great. Made the dressing in the same cuisinart. I would call it Caesar slaw for sure. Skipped the croutons given we served it with hot dogs (with buns) and potato salad.

This sounds delicious. I’m planning to use bagged angel hair green cabbage from the grocery store. Slaw is always my cheating easy extra side dish at a BBQ but I struggle finding a dressing I like. Thanks for this suggestion! As the cabbage I’m using is finely sliced, I’ll only dress in batches so it doesn’t get too soggy.
Using cabbage is a good idea. Added some shredded leftover chicken for a one dish meal.

I've recently been using ox-heart cabbage. Think it's other names are pointed cabbage, sugarloaf, or sweetheart cabbage. Just found it at the farmer's market this year. I've been using it in place of lettuce.
It's sweet, mild, and its leaves are tender. It should work well in this recipe.

After seeing all the "what kind of cabbage" questions, I emailed Melissa today (happy 4th), and she responded "You can use any kind of cabbage. They all work. Use your favorite kind."  I'm off to make it with standard green cabbag, which I  bought yesterday for this recipe (to go with the slow cooker BBQ pork which is all done now).
Heresy perhaps (perhaps just a different dish), but avocados and good cherry tomatoes are wonderful additions to any version of Caesar salad.

one of my all time favorite salad dressings (from Pyewacket, in Chapel Hill, NC) substitutes umeboshi (salt cured Japanese plums) for the anchovies. they add a dimension that is hard to describe and very wonderful. add to taste.

I love anchovies too; just try this once.
Made this salad on the 4th for family.  Was not a winner...  Somehow the dressing did not mesh well with the flavor of the cabbage.  But I see that most folks are happy with it from the reviews.

This is a pancake recipe I’ve used for years which originally comes from a series of thin hardcover books by Michael Fields that my mother had in the 1970s. A long time ago I started using half buckwheat and half white flour.
The key thing I found in my experience, is that by using yogurt instead of buttermilk, the battery retained its leveling much better for two additional days. I usually have to add a splash more of milk to the recipe. The other thing I learned from Michael Field is if you’re going to use blueberries roll them in flour first And then shake them in a sieve. It keeps the blueberries from bleeding as much.
1 cup flour
1 cup buckwheat flour
2 tsp baking powder
1 tsp baking soda
1 tsp salt
1 tbsp sugar

2 eggs
1 cup yogurt
½ cup milk
3 tbsp butter, melted

@Jonny S: I have been using the recipe for Whole-Grain Pancakes by Ali Slagle, and we like it a lot. 

1 cup W/W flour
3/4 cup A/P flour
1/2 cup cornmeal
1/4 cup rolled oats
The recipe calls for buttermilk, but yogurt works well, with a bit more milk. The whole grains are stored in the freezer.
@Jonny S melted butter in the mix is key to getting that nice crispy edge, while maintaining moistness inside.

Following a recipe in The Joy of Cooking, there is enough melted butter in the pancake batter itself that you don’t need oil or butter in the pan. It comes out with nice crisp edges, but less oiliness on the surface.
Any tips for making these gluten free?
@Bonnie, that’s my go-to. It’s foolproof and quick.
@Bonnie that sounds great! Melissa’s Pancakes were Delish, but I wish they weren’t that oily… ( the crisp on the top was cool, and crunchy)

I just finished the NYT article about mistakes to avoid when making pancakes, and I have a question.  It is recommended that if the batter rests overnight in the refrigerator, to add the leavener(s) “just before frying.”  Wouldn’t it be necessary to then rest the batter the minimum 10 minutes?  Thank you.

@Miriam
See paragraph three of the “Tip” section where the directions for making the batter ahead of time are given. You are right on target.

I might be alone on this one, but I personally do not think it is a "mistake" to not let the batter rest. I actually like my pancakes flat, and I think fluffy ones are disgusting. We're all different. :)

Me too. Too much is oversize, overly sweet and cake-like these days, from pancakes and muffins to biscuits and cobbler topping. I'm not crazy about cake and want my baked goods to be the classic versions.
Nods to my 1970's home ec teachers who taught us about letting the batter rest.

Great tips. I'll add one. Buttermilk varies in consistency from almost like yogurt to relatively thin. This dramatically changes the consistency of the batter. I find that I need to thin the thicker buttermilk with milk to keep the consistency right.
@Etaion Shrdlu Agreed!   My pancake batter was way too thick as written.

I just finished eating the best pancakes I ever ate. Why were they so good? My wife is an excellent cook, but she did not know about letting the batter rest. But that applies only to the first batch of pancakes. After that, the batter has rested while the first batch is being cooked. So why were the pancakes so good? I am going to guess that my pre-softened butter was the secret. I never used pre-softened butter. This time I did. Together with all the other tips, the pancakes were heavenly
@Steve Conrad. I heat maple syrup and butter together
@Srp that sounds decadent – and messy.
@Srp That’s a smart idea. I’ll have to remember that in the future.
My #1 recent pancake-making discovery: use ghee for frying rather than butter + oil. Doesn't burn. Is plentiful. Can be stored at room temp.
Ayeea, now you're talking, thanks gourmand

@Matt
And it’s easy to make your own. When I make it for Indian food recipes I make a bunch and put it into separate jars. I use a small mesh sieve and coffee filter. It’s great for those that are lactose intolerant because the milk solids are removed! Also, it tastes wonderful.

@MelissaClark You've recommended a pan not too hot and not too cold, which is the most common error I have when making otherwise delicious pancakes with a very similar recipe from a 1998 Cook's Illustrated. It is difficult to stop a heating pan from overshooting, whether you're on gas, induction, or electric coil. Many of us today have infrared or quick read thermometers these days. How about giving us a target temperature? I'd like to see NYT Cooking be the first to alway include a target temp!
@Grandpa Don cooking in coastal NC yes, this would’ve been so helpful.

My recipe calls for separating the eggs, beating the whites to a froth, and adding that to the batter just before cooking. I thought this was a standard step for making the best pancakes and am surprised that no one mentions it. What's up?
If you watch the video, you'll see that beaten egg whites make a minimal difference, one that Melissa feels doesn't justify the extra bowl and extra work.

No need to separate the eggs. Waste of time. I use kefir and 2 teaspoons of baking powder to 1/2 teaspoons of baking soda. The key really is to let the batter rest. I also like adding the butter at the end and I use 1/4 c measure. Let the batter rest and no need to separate and whip the egg whites.
you didnt watch the video she did
Best pancakes I have ever made! Added a little extra buttermilk, I prefer a less cakey consistency. Otherwise, the oil/butter combo was a game change for a slightly crispy edge.

Never heard of "frying" pancakes or using so much fat that you can swirl it around a tilted pan. No wonder the pictured pancakes look rather gnarly. Pancakes are properly baked on a griddle or pan that has only a skiff of fat, not enough to run. I make whole-wheat/white flour pancakes with a I970s Better Homes &Gardens recipe, using a 12" cast iron skillet, three at a time. They are a beautiful smooth, even, light golden-tan color, the mark of a good classic recipe and a skilled cook.

"You can refrigerate the batter without the baking powder and soda mixed in for up to 2 days. Stir them in 10 minutes before cooking."

Won't the overnight rest also develop gluten, on top of the previous day's mixing? How do you evenly mix in the baking soda/powder at this point without over-mixing?
It was 9pm. I didn’t have dinner. Now I do. Thanks, Melissa!

During my 80 years of living, I have eaten many pancakes. Without the shadow of a doubt, these are the tastiest, fluffiest, the best pancakes I have ever eaten. I think the 10+ minutes of rest time makes a difference. I also appreciated the detailed explanation at every step. Thank you!
It's kind of suizid, but I like it!

We will need to try this on Sunday!

One question, though - can buckwheat flour be substituted for about half the flour? (I love buckwheat pancakes!)
@dgi have added buckwheat flour, 1/2 cup,  many times, and yes it adds a very distinctive flavor to pancakes
Thank you for confirming, @Gregbee!

These are the best pancakes ever.  Melissa Clark is a genius.  I agree that resting, using full fat buttermilk, and keeping the size to about 4 inches are keys to great pancakes.  Adding fruit like blueberries or chopped fresh peaches pushes them over the top.

I don't think I've ever seen full fat buttermilk in the grocery store (and yes, I have Whole Foods stores in the neighborhood, and no, I buy something there only when I absolutely cannot get the item elsewhere). Even Kate's Buttermilk is 1.5 percent milkfat.

I don't often make pancakes, but will bookmark this for next time I do.  And I don't have time right now to watch the video, but definitely will do so.  I trust (and love) Melissa Clark as a cooking expert.  I've missed her videos, which used to be a regular feature.
My stove has a built-in griddle.  Any tips on time and temperature for that?
@Carol Hoffman, a few drops of water should bounce and sizzle on the griddle when it is hot enough (Melissa shows this briefly in the video, testing the heat on her frying pan).
@Bonnie I think it was evaporate and sizzle, with the bouncing and skittering indicating too hot
Great insider info for this recipe. Best tip of all: for best results use pure maple syrup and nothing else. OK, nice imported butter, too. Enjoy!
Imported from where, Wisconsin?

This is basicslly my recipe. Sometimes I substitute some oatmeal for the dame amount of APflour. You can add almost any seed: Chía, flax, sunflower, Black sesame, etc. Be carefull with adding sugar, too much will make the outside burn before the inner portion is done.

This recipe is good but has way too much butter. 2 Tbsps of butter is more than enough. Also, as mentioned by another commentator, separating the eggs and folding in the beaten whites  to the batter just before you begin to fry the pancakes produces amazingly fluffy pancakes.

"You can refrigerate the batter without the baking powder and soda mixed in for up to 2 days. Stir them in 10 minutes before cooking."

I’ve tried keeping leftover batter overnight on several occasions and the next morning the color has always changed to a grayish purple.  Never been hungry enough to try cooking them.
what if i prefer a more thin pancake?  should i add more milk?

The family pancake recipe I learned from my dad was very similar with one different technique. My dad separated the eggs and whisked the whites to fold in quickly & gently after the batter rested. We found this kept a lovely fluffiness in great pancake!
I love this recipe!

Oh Melissa, you are so consistently in my recipe book... This is exactly how I make my pancakes and I was with you every step of the way. I wish we could cook together, you are really my cooking twin. I use 1 tbsp of white vinegar and whole milk and/or 2% yogurt thinned with milk if I have it. I like a little tang so I use a little more baking soda and your pan was a smidge too hot, I like it a little less brown and medium is plenty high enough.
I use full fat Greek yogurt instead of buttermilk and they are our weekly Sundsy treat. Excellent article and video. Thank you! I learned I need to rest the batter. Excellent tips and recipe.

I use apple cider vinegar instead of buttermilk because I always have it on hand:

240g AP Flour
37g sugar
6g course salt
7g baking powder (aluminum free)
4g baking soda

280ml whole milk
37ml vegetable oil
7g vanilla extract 
40ml apple cider vinegar 
2 eggs

I mix with a spatula until flour streaks are gone and let it rest for about 10-15 min before cooking.

I will give the resting part a try, but it has been my experience that pancakes need to be cooked as soon as the dry ingredients are mixed with the wet ingredients. the batter should be poured into the griddle ASAP. The activation of the baking soda with the acid immediately bubbles up and this makes fluffier pancakes. Mine will rise to a half inch even if  I use whole wheat pastry flour.

@eas I just made this exactly as specified in the recipe, but halved it for 2 people. I used a 1/3 cup dry measuring cup to pour the batter, and got exactly 6 pancakes, 3 per person. Perfect amount for 2 people. Next time I'm going to try to make them bigger by using 1/2 cup batter per pancake, which should make 4 pancakes vs. 6. (Again, I halved the recipe.)
Easy get a box of Hungry Jack Pancake mix = consistently good maybe not great then again it's just a pancake which certainly doesn't compare to a good waffle.

Loved watching the video and learning the variety of tips and tricks.  Fun!  And I think Melissa Clark is very brave to take on the humble pancake- they are full of family traditions and opinions.  Lol.
For yummier pancakes with great texture, it's worth looking for a high quality local buttermilk. We love one that comes in a butter-yellow bottle from a butter maker in Maine.
I really wish cooks would stop including this mysterious ingredient "buttermilk" in their recipes. I have never seen buttermilk for sale, anywhere, in any store.
It's in the section with the whipping cream usually.  Grocery stores almost always have it.

@Wendy B. Just ask the people who work in the store where the buttermilk is. I can find it here and at stores in small towns. I prefer whole fat but Melissa said you could use either. If you can’t find buttermilk, follow her instructions for adding vinegar or lemon juice to regular milk.
I always use a neutral oil in the batter. It makes a much tenderer pancake, and I notice no difference in taste. (Plus, I don’t have to remember to melt and cool butter.)

I have been making pancakes for years even before I was a teenager, probably. By far these were the best. I added blueberries and lemon zest. Fluffy, crispy edges, tasty as all get out. Perfect and simple Father’s Day breakfast. Another NYTCooking keeper!

What makes pancakes especially fluffy is separating the egg whites and whipping them until they peak.  You add the egg whites at the end by folding them into the batter and then letting the batter sit/rest on the counter for 10 minutes while heat your pan and get everything else ready.
This is the Joy of Cooking recipe, that has been the standard for years. With just a little more buttermilk and added vanilla.
What about high altitude?

This were great in consistency and appearance, and I learned so much as all the tips were extremely helpful; I cooked on a cast iron griddle and the high temp oil-butter combo was absolutely key for the surface. However, I did find them a tad bland, as in too floury. I expected them to be more like the classic diner buttermilk pancake, that is, more sweet. Yes, the addition of syrup obviously helps, but I wonder if upping the sugar (or doing a mixture of both honey and sugar, instead of either-or) would get these a touch sweeter, not cloyingly so, but enough to where one does not need loads of syrup to compensate. My wife and daughter loved them, and praised them as the best ever (truly!), but perhaps my expectational sweet tooth got the better of me here.

Made these for Father’s Day. Used low fat buttermilk because that is all they had at the market. Otherwise followed recipe exactly. These were crisp, fluffy, delectable. And easy. Won’t wait til next year to make these again! Thanks Melissa💚

I’m a former short order cook and this recipe is spot on. Letting the batter sit makes all the difference. Of course, I found this out by just working at a restaurant and having the batter sit out and I noticed that the longer the batter sat out the better the pancakes were. You have to be patient with pancakes. I don’t care how hungry your crew gets, they have to wait.☺️
@Wendy B. it's in dairy in every grocery store I've ever been in! Near, but not with, milk. Likely near cream.
They came out perfect!! I served them with a blueberry compote on top instead of syrup.
I've been adding a couple tablespoons of malted milk powder to batter. It adds an element of sweetness

This recipe is just about identical to the Betty Crocker cookbook recipe I’ve been using since 1976. It was an accident when I realized that, being distracted by an early morning phone call, I’d let the batter sit nearly 15 minutes before cooking, and the pancakes were quite improved compared to my usual output. The next change didn’t happen until 2024, when I saw an Instagram reel of a diner [Clinton Street Baking Co.] cooking pancakes and was fascinated by the perfect color they achieved on their beautiful cakes. I messaged them about the “how”; happily, the response was prompt: use ghee on your griddle. I do and my pancakes are delicious and light and picture-perfect.
I used that one for years but this one is better because it uses melted butter and more of it. The Betty Crocker recipe uses 2 tablespoons of melted shortening or oil and no vanilla.
Almost perfect, just a bit too salty. Will cut down on the amount next time.

This is great if you have an hour to make the perfect pancake. Also not a “great way to start the day” . Straight white carb, fat and sugar will make you feel like going back to bed.
I prefer Arrowhead Mills Buckwheat waffle and pancake mix. Even my picky eater grandchildren love them. Berries are always added to the batter. The pancaked are a beautiful cocoa color due to the healthy buckwheat flour. Only a dab of margarine and about a tsp of sugar needed, if at all. Berries add natural sugar.
Arrowhead batter mix is sold at many stores. I get mine from Whole Foods.

6 Tablespoons in the batter alone does seem like a lot. I am sure it makes for great flavor.
Might there be some modification to reduce the butter in this traditional, classic recipe? The additional dairy also adds some fat.

Thanks to Jonny S. for the recipe from Michael Fields. I will give it try someday.

Resting the batter 20 minutes before cookng worked swell.  Added whole milk to a box pancake mix.  Tossed the first pancake, which was too thin and not browned enough.  The next 4 were right on  Used 2 tbsp of organic OJ to get the acid in the mix, and the results were better than without, and I couldn't taste the OJ, other than a slight sweetnbess.
I've had good results using a cake of active yeast in lieu of other ways to make pancakes rise — an the yeasty taste is a bonus as well.
how many many pancakes of what size ( say 4" pancakes) does this make?
When I save batter to cook the next day, it starts turning brown and develops a different taste - ways to prevent this?

We've been making Melissa's version--calling for oats and cornmeal and a third egg--for ages. I'm getting better at consistency, and I would add that the old cooks' advice 'mix or blend with a few quick strokes' is also germane (see "just combined" per recipe). My second tip would be to seek out real buttermilk--we drive to get Kate's Buttermilk, which we first discovered in Maine, but available around NYC. My dad used to talk about drinking it on a hot day--I can imagine doing that with Kate's
Bread flour vs all purpose flour: is there a difference with regards to pancakes?

I follow an old recipe my mom taught me very similar to this one, except with a few added steps: I separate the yolks and whites, adding the yolks to the buttermilk, adding a splash of vanilla extract, then incorporating BROWNED butter into wet mixture, mixing these wet ingredients with a hand beater; then, adding that to the dry ingredients incorporating perfunctorily—yes leaving some lumps; then, beat egg whites lightly and FOLD it into the batter.
What temperature should I set my electric frying pan?
I use 350F, start there, see how that works, adjust up or down as needed.
Does anyone know what temperature I should set an electric griddle to for pancakes?
What do you think of whey used in lieu of buttermilk, thinned yogurt or kefir?
Harv is correct!  Whip the egg whites separately and fold them in last.
The recipe I use is almost exactly the same as this, except I brown the butter first before adding it to the batter.
Where does one find the chart listed in the video?

@Gale S Read her notes after the steps ("refrigerate WITHOUT baking powder and soda"):

You can refrigerate the batter without the baking powder and soda mixed in for up to 2 days. Stir them in 10 minutes before cooking. Note that cold batter will need an extra minute or two in the pan to cook through.
@Jake Refrigerators need to be PLUGGED IN to work! (purple? 🤢)

So, I had a plan to make pancakes this morning, and it seemed like fate that Melissa's recipe video came out on YouTube yesterday. And I just bought a cast iron pan to boot! 

Unfortunately, despite a batter rest (15 mins), the pancakes were still flat. They weren't sweet enough. And trying to cook them in cast iron was a disaster. Thankfully, I had my trusty electric nonstick griddle close at hand, and after the first (burnt) batch, I switched to it. I'm using Alison Roman's recipe next time.
@Upstate Tattooed Lady I am glad I wasn’t the only one who found these not sweet enough! I don’t need diabetes with my breakfast but the sugar didn’t come through at all.

I will try this tomorrow! I have been isi g the same recipe online for years and it calls only for baking powder - 3.5 tsp + buttermilk which I bake Frej with vinegar or lemon juice. The measurements fit the other ingredients are also very different. Will try this one!
Anyone ever sub in some almond flour in the batter?  Did it affect the texture, the taste?

If you use yogurt, it needs to be “runny” enough to mix in. If you make your own yogurt and strain the whey, you’ll need to add the whey to the dry mix else you’ll get a batter that won’t pour.

@David, if your batter is too thick because you use yogurt (or the buttermilk is extra thick), just thin it to the proper consistency by adding a bit of milk—maybe a tablespoon or two at a time. You can add quite a bit before it messes up the chemistry of the leavening. Trust your instincts on pancakes. If you’ve made them successfully, you know what the batter should look like.

Clark states, "The perfect pancakes have crisp edges, fluffy centers . . ." Having made thousands of different pancakes, the crucial feature is a moist center. Frying in oil or butter helps seal in moisture, but a dry, pre-heated, nonstick griddle over medium heat is faster *if* you keep the batter thinner than Clark does, about 60 sec. per side. See Better Homes and Gardens New Cookbook, Corn-meal Griddle Cakes for a good buttermilk model, and a delicious, moist pancake.

@Grandpa Don cooking in coastal NC : Don't worry about actual temperatures. Heat the pan to about medium, medium high and as it's heating up flick a few drops of water on it and when they start to skitter don't turn it up any higher than that. Put the desired amount of batter in the pan, wait for almost all the bubbles to pop and the pancake to get dry around the edges, flip it and cook it for no more than 1 or 2 minutes longer. Check at the 1 minute mark so it doesn't overcook. If it burns in that amount of time, turn the heat down.

the absolute best pancakes are from a yeast based overnight batter. I use a king arthur flour recipe for many years and it makes pancakes that my then picky kids would eat. 2 C flour (less 3T) . 2 cups buttermilk, either 1/2 t of instant yeast or 20g ripe starter. Let rise overnight. mix salt (1/2t) baking powder (2t) and baking soda (1/2t) with flour, whisk 2 eggs. add dry ingredients and whisk in. add melted butter (1/4c) and egg mix to overnight batter and let rest for 10 minutes or so. cook

I have an induction stove, which I love, but I can't find a good pancake pan to use on it. Does anyone have a good suggestion? At this point I am willing to spend some $$. I have given up on pancakes at home; Now I only make them when camping, on a gas stove!!
@Beth Messina I suggest a large cast iron fry pan, like the one Melissa Clark uses in the video. If it’s well taken care of (never washed with soap) the pancakes won’t stick. Good luck!

@Beth Messina  Since you aren’t supposed to use a cast iron skillet on an induction cooktop, I suggest a carbon steel pan. These are smoother and not as heavy as a cast iron pan, but can give a similar result. Look for a good quality pan. Alton Brown uses only carbon steel and may have shared a recommended brand at some point. Do not get a “non-stick carbon steel” pan. In my opinion they don’t give the same result as a cast iron pan. Used and cleaned properly a carbon steel pan will become non-stick.

My grandmother cooked the best pancakes and she often added corn to the batter - either fresh off the cob or canned (drained of all liquid). My four siblings and I always asked for “corn cakes”. Similar to my recipe that I have made for my kids over the years, and they now make for theirs:
1/3 c whole wheat flour
2/3 c ap flour
1 t baking powder
1/2 t baking soda
1 cup buttermilk
1 egg
1 T cooking oil
Mix dry. Mix wet. Mix together. Do not overmix. Hot griddle or pan. Nob of  butter, crisco, bacon greese, coconut oil or beef tallow. Pour in 1/4 cup batter. Cook until bubbles form all over and then flip. 
No salt, sweetener or vanilla. Opt. sliced bananas or blueberries before turning.

What is chili crisp?

Chile crisp is a crunchy, savory Chinese condiment made by infusing neutral oil with dried chiles and crispy fried aromatics, like garlic and shallots. Unlike smooth chili oil, the star of the show is the thick, spoonable texture of the savory, fried ingredients.
@Sally everytime I eat chili crisp I feel like I have sand grains in my mouth. I find it so unappealing yet the flavor is fabulous. Maybe it’s the brand I’m using.
@RAG Chili crisp makes everything better. I swear by Lao Gan Ma.

I have learned one important lesson from NYT Cooking over the years: If Melissa Clark suggests something interesting, try it before you dismiss it! As written, this looks like a warm dish. Should the pasta be chilled before adding the raw ingredients?
@Trina, the clue is in the title of the recipe 😊

@SF Cook - I think the folks who are asking about it ARE reading the title which says this is *supposed* to be a cold dish, which is why they are confused. It says you CAN make it a few days in advance, but it doesn’t specifically say you should do so. And it doesn’t tell you to chill it before serving. So, made *as written*, it would likely still be slightly warm if not chilled before serving.

@Trina - Personally, I am considering making it completely (in advance of when I need to serve it), and then chill it before serving (stirring as mentioned in the intro to redistribute the sauce before serving). I worry that chilling just the noodles before adding the raw ingredients would cause my noodles to clump.

I'll be reverting to my Thai recipes, which are much more flavorful.
Curious if you used all of the 2/3 cup pasta water. I'm considering this dish for a party, but I don't want to try it if it doesn't work as written. Thanks!

@Dale totally agree. I’ve made many such noodle salads and loved them. This one was bland and
A HUGE disappointment after all that work and so many ingredients. We decided it deserved a 2 out of 5. Usually like Melissa Clarke’s recipes but now I’m going to be cautious.

@Dale.   I’m
With you.  Awful

This sauce was overwhelmed by the amount of peanut butter. I would cut that in half and skip the crystalized ginger. Also I added a chopped jalepeno pepper and would have added more cucumber in long match sticks.
We loved this recipe. We decided to substitute thinly sliced cabbage for half of the pasta and thought the textural contrast was great! Thanks Melissa.

This was okay but I like other cold noodles (like the Sam Sifton takeout noodles) better.  Even thinned with a lot of pasta water, the peanut butter sauce got too stiff after being in the fridge, and the flavor was very one-note.  The ginger bits were interesting but too sweet for my taste.  It reminded me of late-90s fusion cuisine, honestly. Which might be your thing! To each their own.

Outcome was so delicious when made as the recipe was written for ingredients that I wished I had not scaled it back by half last night so that there would have been leftovers to try cold this morning!  The crystallized ginger (a staple at our house) is a genius ingredient here.  I chose spaghetti (Rustichella d'Abruzzo) as noodle option because I always have it on hand. Next time I'll try subbing in equivalent volume of different raw veg (maybe carrot and red bells) for cucumbers.

This is terrific and the candied ginger absolutely puts it over the top. Surprising pops of spicy sweetness. I added firm tofu to bump up the protein even more (though at 22g, it’s sufficient for most). I like the additions others are suggesting. Snow peas would be an excellent choice.

I’ve made peanut sauce for noodles my whole life, but the fresh ginger in this one was a welcome surprise and lifted it to a whole other level. It made a beautiful burn in the back of the throat with the spicy. Melissa Clark always delivers! Served it with crushed peanuts and extra soy and hot sauce on the table so people could doctor it as they liked.

I halved the noodles and sauce ingredients, but kept the cucumbers, green onions and lime the same. I served it with chopped peanuts, extra chili crisp, lime wedges, chopped red bell pepper and leftover (pork meatballs) from the Thai Inspired Chicken Meatball soup recipe. I used brown rice and millet ramen noodles from Lotus Foods.
This was not for me! The sauce was sludgy and the texture was off. I usually love Melissa Clark’s recipes . I trust her palate and find her recipes super accessible. A rare miss.

Made this for work lunch and quick weeknight dinner. If doing this, keep the cucumber separate, even uncut, and add before you eat. After a few days the mixed in sliced cucumber got really soggy. That fresh crunch is a nice contrast. Probably would do the same for the herbs too.

I seem to be the exception here but I found this to be extremely heavy on the peanut butter. I was hoping for silkier and lighter but the full cup of peanut butter just dominated and seemed to completely weigh down the noodles. Far too much and not balanced with the other flavors. Followed the recipe as written.

Made many NYT recipes over the years and this has to rank very near the bottom of the list. Made exactly as written: Way too much peanut butter, almost flavorless (no depth, not spicy, not herbal), and has an odd, almost slimy texture in the mouth. Cooking a whole pound of noodles yields enough for an army, so (depending on family size or the number in the barraks) you’ll be stuck eating it for several meals. The idea is good and worth fooling around with, but this recipe fails. Sorry for the negative comment.
@Paul, totally agree with this. I had a very similar experience. Why it rated 5 stars I don’t know, as now that I’m reading the comments there are more than a few like yours.

This is one of those recipes that makes me wonder if there is a typo or if anyone is actually testing them. I made this exactly as published and there was WAY too much peanut butter. It overpowered everything. Couldn’t taste the ginger, lime or chili crunch and even when I added more chili and soy sauce, it really just tasted like I threw a jar of peanut butter on some noodles. I may try again with half the peanut butter. (I used Trader Joe’s crunchy. Maybe a different brand would work better. )
@Paula Glazier, I used Wegman’s organic crunchy PB and had the same experience.
My favorite chili crisp brand is Mr. Bing.

By far the best peanut noodle recipe I’ve made. Chili crisp and ginger really make it special. Added a couple dashes of rice vinegar for acidity and a tablespoon or so of fish sauce for flavor. Will definitely make again

Made this last night. Tasty, but I was a bit scattered and because the recipe didn't mention it, I didn't rinse the noodles in cold water after draining. (I've made cold noodle dishes before; I should've remembered to do this.) This means the dish ended up being served hot, which was not the recipe's nor my intention. Still good, but not what I wanted to eat on a hot and humid day. So, just a suggestion: Rinse noodles well in cold water after reserving pasta water and draining.
agreed - I remembered to rinse and was glad I did!
Sorry but this one gets a thumbs down. the sauce is relatively bland and contrary to the narrative, leftovers were inedible- a soggy mess. i will not make it again.
@Shelly J-W Agree!

Seems like the recipe leaves out a step:  chilling in the refrigerator for a few hours, which also allows the flavors to mingle.  Otherwise it's "Warm Peanut Ginger Noodles."   I added rotisserie chicken and served cold on a hot summer day.  Delicious!
My family adds shredded, boiled chicken to our peanut butter noodles. It's nice with chicken.
The candied ginger is absolutely wonderful in this dish! It adds an amazing pop of sweetness against the spicy chili crisp and rich peanut butter. Amazing!!
Re: Chili Crisp, you can get what I think is a pretty good one at Trader Joe's,

I rely on Melissa Clark and this sounds great but a full cup of peanut butter for 6 servings is too rich for me. Any suggestions for lightening it up, do I use less of the sauce or does anyone have a suggestion.
@Resiliant this is more like 8 servings, tbh…
this sauce is perfect, melissa. i served it over blanched and sauteed broccoli rabe and changed nothing else.  leftovers in a jar in the fridge for something else tomorrow.  thank you!

This was very good as written, although I used less pasta and more cucumbers (Persian and not sliced that thin) and more lime juice.  The pop of crystallized ginger was a great idea and I wouldn't have thought of it. Chili crisp mainly adds heat, so you can substitute crushed red pepper flakes.  It is in the international or Asian sections of many grocery stores.  I love the Momofuku brand.  It is addictive and is great on scrambled eggs, on sandwiches, etc.

This is a flexible recipe. The only change I would make, really, is that the  next time I make this, I’ll probably make about half the sauce. 

Use whatever crunchy vegetables appeal to you. Along with the cucumber, I added some sliced red pepper. Because I had it, I used cilantro, mint, and basil as garnish.

I didn’t feel like going to the grocery store, so I used a combination of Asian sesame paste, tahini, and the remains of a crunchy peanut butter jar. I’d say I used about half sesame paste and half peanut butter. 

The candy ginger didn’t appeal to me, so I didn’t use it, but that threw off the balance of the flavors. When I ate it, I drizzled on some hoisin sauce.

I’m sitting outside on a hot day with a Bell’s Oberon, totally overeating this and loving every minute. Is crystalized ginger the same thing as candied crystallized ginger? I bought the candied at Trader Joe’s and used the Squiggly noodles from TJ’s too. I like the sweet/spicy balance of the candied but wonder about the difference. Added a very thinly sliced red bell pepper but otherwise made as written.
There is plenty of protein in this recipe to qualify as a "full meal". 2 tablespoons of peanut butter is a full serving, and a sixth of a cup is more than that.

@Bobby how are two tablespoons of peanut butter a full serving of protein? That is only 7-8 grams, and a full serving from peanut butter alone would need 6 tablespoons. In this recipe, the presumption is that you get 11-12 T from about 3 T of the peanut butter, and another 11 or so grams from 3 ounces of noodles. But that’s a lot of calories  - I plan on making this tonight, but will cut back on both the peanut butter and noodles.

This is such great idea for a salad to riff on. More or different veg. I didn't have crystalized ginger so used regular and added a bit of sugar to the dressing. I think next time I'll try a lot more lime juice and add fish sauce for more of a Vietnamese vibe. Definitely will add to the regular repertoire. Thanks for sharing it.
Excellent suggestions.  Some chopped bok choi would be good for an added crunch texture.

I wish the recipe stated what should have been obvious to me: it has to be made and then refrigerated at least several hours before serving if you want the salad to be *cold*, as the recipe title promises.
The photo looks more like fettuccine than spaghetti, linguini or Chinese egg noodles.
Based on the instructions, I don't understand how this is a cold salad? Nowhere does it say rinse noodles under cold water, or refrigerate for an amount of time at the end.

@Eric Yes, but also nowhere does it give cooking or heating instructions for the sauce, which would make it a hot dish. Room temperature is probably more accurate rather than cold, but that’s not a standard term for dishes.
Pouring hot noodles over a room temperature sauce can't be called by cold by any definition of the word cold @Julia M
you need to cold rinse the noodles if using authentic Chinese Egg noodles!  Otherwise the residual heat will form a blob before you can add the sauce.

@Greg in London Thank you for this note. Went thru the hassle of getting authentic Chinese noodles with no English directions only to be left with a blob of a finished product. It was delicious but still.

This is the first time a Melissa Clark recipe has failed me. Followed the recipe exactly and ended up serving a big bowl of noodles in peanut butter gloop to my family. An embarassing, unpleasant meal.
I wonder if a poached egg would work well on top of these noodles for some extra protein
As far as I'm concerned, an egg on top of almost anything is a good idea. :-)
I cup of peanut butter seemed like too much, it made the sauce too sticky and heavy and fatty.

I've written this before, but whenever sesame oil is mentioned, I have to wonder if it is toasted sesame oil? I usually assume it is but I wish the recipe would specify, because plain sesame oil is a more subtle flavor.

I used Teddie's chunky natural peanut butter, followed all the directions but the dressing is too thick - doesn't pool to the bottom at all.  I added the pasta water as suggested but didn't want to keep adding more than the 2/3 cup.  Not sure the best way to make the dressing less sticky.

Cut this recipe in half unless your family is used to eating cold noodles. It’s takes a lot of prepping but tastes pretty good the first day. I tried to keep it for three days but found that the (English) cucumbers got all mushy and nasty. If there is a next time, I’ll substitute zucchini for the cukes.
Or you could keep the cukes separate from the noodles and add when you serve it. This is what I do.
Made this as directed.  Tasty but not a wow.  Wonder if it might benefit from a bit of cooked chicken or pork.

For a gluten-free version, I use 100% buckwheat soba noodles. I also use grated ginger rather than crystalized and julienne the cucumbers because they mix better with the noodles and still retain their crunch. Either way, gluten or gluten-free, it's simple and divine!
Why do you lay the cucumbers on top and not mix them in right away? You mix them in with the pasta anyway, correct?

Made extra sauce and split between spaghetti and Pad Thai rice noodles for my GF friend, rave reviews on both, fabulous leftover (or made the day before?). Loved the cucumber, might add julienne red bell pepper.
I made this the other day and it was very good. However in future I would double the sauce and veg and halve the noodles. That way it can be more of a standalone meal and less of a side.
I desperately want these to be the noodles of summer, but they are still short on salt and acid. No matter what I try. Maybe more salt in my pasta water?

I used a whole lime - maybe 2.5 tbsp of juice, and a bit more soy sauce than called for.  And probably half again as much chopped crystallized and fresh ginger since my husband is a ginger-holic. I really liked this zippy, refreshing salad.

Great recipe! Inspired by other comments, I added a 10 oz bag of Trader Joe’s shredded cruciferous vegetables (kale, Brussels sprouts, broccoli, green and red cabbage). I found it important to thin the sauce quite a bit with the pasta water.
How do you get the noodles to be curly like in the foto?
Blue Dragon brand Cantonese egg noodles very closely resemble the noodles shown in the photo.

Made this recipe this evening. Would’ve benefitted from more instruction about how to cook fresh Chinese egg noodles since I don’t usually use them for pasta. I also found the peanut butter sauce to be very difficult to mix up before adding the pasta water. Finally the flavors were a bit meh, without adding a lot more soy sauce and lime juice. Maybe this will have more taste after it’s been chilled in the fridge.

I made as directed but had to tweak when it was complete. In the accompanying photo, the pasta appears very light rather than colored by the dressing. A photo of my dish would show brown-red pasta. The dressing was a bit heavy per the directions but definitely delicious. I needed more soy sauce and more chili crisp but I added this to my serving. Others liked it as is. I also served it with a bowl of ice cold julienned kale and lots more mini cucumbers cubed. The vegetables helped lighten it.
I'm a little confused because this recipe is called "Cold Peanut Ginger Noodles" but according to the recipe the noodles are served shortly after cooking, so they will be at least warm?
Too much Peanut butter, would use 1/2 as much. Add fresh garlic, more ginger and a crunchy veggie. We used chickpea pasta which wasn’t ideal.

If you haven’t make cold noodles before: the cukes give it crunch. You won’t get that by pouring 1 lb. of warm noodles over thinly sliced cukes atop the dressing. If you mix in cukes & cilantro just before serving (& store separate from noodles in fridge) they’ll add more texture to your bowl l. If I add peanuts and/or snow peas, ditto; mix in @ mealtime.
Way too much peanut butter. Was thick and gloopy despite adding lots of pasta water.

sauce is very unbalanced and might benefit from using less peanut butter and adding some rice wine vinegar in addition to the lime juice. Thinned it with equal parts water and it was still very thick*using ¾ cup crunchy peanut butter, not 1 cup, unless you want it very rich, and adding shredded rotisserie chicken, shrimp, tofu, or edamame*lime sam Sifton takeout noodle better
Trader Joes many, many years ago used to sell a noodle dish similar to this. It was brilliant! Have no idea why they discontinued it. Can't wait to try this recipe!
What noodle would be best for making this glutrn-free?
Jovial brand makes a good gluten-free egg noodle. I'm planning to use that.
We used rice noodles.
Why crystalized ginger?

@DP

I agree. I love crystallized ginger but it’s candy

@Beth
Guessing that, instead of the usual ingredient of plain sugar, the candy provides the sugar instead, and in a more interesting way.

How do I keep the noodles from sticking together? This is invariably my problem with noodle dishes—once they go into the fridge, they turn into a clump. 

(In case it matters, I use plant-based noodles—rice noodles, alkaline wheat, ramen—not egg noodles.)
after draining the noodles in a colander you can just rinse them under cold water. this will prevent them from sticking to each other.
Natural or sweetened PB ?
Why not just add sweetener separately?  It seems like unsalted, natural, crunchy, unsweetened peanut butter would be your best choice.
@djk - You ask about natural or sweetened PB.  I used natural and it was delicious.
@djk Natural for our household, but your tastes may differ.
Do you rinse the cooked noodles under cold water? It says “cold” in the name. But seems like it’s missing this step.

Kelly S.: You ask "Do you rinse the cooked noodles under cold water? It says “cold” in the name. But seems like it’s missing this step." The text at the top of the recipe notes "It holds up very well in the fridge. Just toss well before serving since the dressing tends to pool at the bottom of the container." In addition, since the recipe calls for some of the starchy pasta water, rinsing the noodles wouldn't make sense since the starchy pasta water adds starch.
Add tofu.
Has anyone made it without the chili crisp? I'm a spice-heat wimp so even the tiniest amount makes it too spicy for me.
I cut the chili crisp from 2 T to 1.5 T.  You could use some, girl.  The flavor is exquisite.  1 tsp?
Yes! It's tasty even without it.

My favorite chile crisp is Lao Gan Ma (a grandma on the label). Inexpensive and delicious. The Woks of Life website recommends the following prepared chile crisps: "Sze Daddy Taiwanese Chili Sauce, Lao Gan Ma Spicy Chili Crisp (a long-time pantry staple), Fly By Jing Sichuan Chili Crisp, Yun Hai Su Five Spice Chili Crisp".
Any suggestions for the best Gluten Free noodle for this dish?
I'd do buckwheat soba noodles.
@Katrina. My question as well.  Have you since tried it with a GF noodle?
@Jeanne the recipe provides noodle options.  chili crisp can be found at any grocery store these days
My favorite Chile Crisp is Genieve Ko’s NYT recipe. So easy to adapt heat, crunch etc. very easy and inexpensive to make
@Dtrout once drained and mixed with the peanut dressing, it won't be hot
I buy my kale in bags, already cut up & trimmed.   Any idea how much of that I should use to equal ‘one bunch’?
It was yummy, I made it yesterday but couldn’t find the ginger in my small town . It was still very nice on a hot day
I’m not a fan of crystallized ginger but I plan to add a few slices of pickled ginger, available from the Asian grocers. And 1tsp fish sauce.

I made this following the recipe exactly and really like how straightforward the recipe is and how tasteful the dish is. I served it with Katsu Chicken (using the NYT recipe). The fresh ginger in both the Katsu sauce and the peanut noodles helped connect the two dishes. I added a bright fruit salad to bring some color to the plate.
Just made this w/the following mods: no cucumber, smooth peanut butter, no crystallized ginger.  So easy.  Absolutely delicious.
I’m going to modify this for a single serving using a noodle packet from Momofuku.

Love this - I added a bit of carrot and mung bean sprouts for more crunch but otherwise, followed the recipe.

I might add a dash of maple syrup next time to give a bit of sweet and I'm curious how it will taste tomorrow. Excited to make this again!

Made this tonight with what I had on hand which was gluten-free spaghetti noodles. Did not have any crystallized ginger and to me, this salty-spicy flavour profile also needed a bit of sweet, so I added a tablespoon of maple syrup. Overall pretty good and I suspect it might be one of those dishes that will taste even better the next day after the flavours have had time to marinate.
@Nadz. Thank you!  Will try GF noodles on hand.
Should I use toasted sesame oil or non toasted?

My mom can't handle spice levels above the occasional pinch of crushed red pepper flakes. Has anyone tried this adding the chili crisp at the table? My husband likes his food super spicy, and I'm constantly working to balance between the two.

Made this for dinner last night per recipe and it is a fabulous dish!  
We all are fans of cold sesame noodles and this one has a very deep and delicious flavor.
We are three- all adults.  And we nearly ate the whole recipe.
And then I thought about the one full cup of peanut butter and how much that it is fat and calorie wise.  I know its healthy fat, but even heathy fat can be too much fat! 
I'll try 3/4 cup next time or maybe even a half cup.  It was so saucy I think I could get away with it.

I didn't have the chile crisp or the crystallized ginger but added cabbage and red onion and 1/2 nut butter and 1/2 crunchy peanut butter !! Delicious.  Will definitely make again with all of the ingredients.  I'm curious why no rice wine vinegar? There's another recipe I make for carrot salad which is very similar except has the rice wine vinegar.  it's very yummy as well.

One of the funniest things about NYT cooking comments is when a recipe has an ingredient right in the name and readers tell you to omit it without even trying. 
The candied ginger isn't just for sugar, it's a great balance for the heat, and also brings a nice, chewy texture. Why not be adventurous and try it? I loved it! PS I ordered it easily overnight through a major retailer.

This is really tasty! I used natural unsalted crunchy peanut butter. Since the recipe doesn’t really specify, I thought I’d comment that this type of pb created a good flavor profile. It also seized up a lot while mixing. I started adding pasta water per the recipe, but given the salt that was being added by that method, I switched to ice water. I kind of wish I’d loosened it even more than I did because the noodles were pretty heavily coated. Carrots were great addition as was a block of tofu similarly seasoned and air fried. I saw some other comments about crystallized ginger being candy, but it wasn’t overly sweet at all and a really subtle ginger addition.

Crystalized ginger seemed wild to me, but I trust Melissa so I made it as written and it amazing.  It provides a gingery heat and wonderful backbone to the dish. A perfect update to the Sam Sifton classic .

Just finished preparing this for the first time.  A lot of potential.  BUT.  I think this is to peanut forward for me.  it needed more acid.  Also, the ratio of pasta to the other ingredients was overwhelming.  I'm leaving on vacation tomorrow and bringing this and had to raid every suitable veggie (red cabbage, chopped sugar snaps, etc) to help balance this dish.  Still good flavors with a little tweaking
I added chopped dry roasted peanuts on top.
Added sauteed crispy tofu bits, fresh basil. I halved the pasta (whole wheat spaghetti) and it was still too much. I'd add more cucumbers, and minced jalapenos could be a great addition.
I followed this recipe exactly and it was so bland!  What could I have done wrong? I even added more chili crisp and soy sauce at the end after I tasted it, but that didn’t help.  Any ideas?
@Minouche That was my experience, too. Not a fan.

I think if I were to make this next time, I'd halve the peanut butter.  I had high hopes but maybe I didn't thin out out the sauce as much as I should have.  I did add sliced jalapeno to the cucumber/green onions  as I had one handy.

Totally yummy version of the classic! I used udon noodles and they worked great, but since it was just for the 2 of us, I used our standard amount of noodles and made the full recipe of the sauce so to have a stash of it in the fridge. Because I was a bit leery of using  2 whole tablespoons of chili crisp, I started with 2 tablespoons of chili garlic sauce and then added chili crisp to taste. Worked a charm! The sliced cukes had a tendency to stick together, so I will do small cubes next time.

I made this and added broccoli, shallots, garlic, red cabbage, peanuts, and a little more peanut butter than was called for. When adding water to peanut butter dressing I heated the water in my kettle first for an easier whisk. Chefs kiss - good for meal prepping!
Has anyone made this with fresh ginger instead of crystallized?
The recipe uses both fresh and crystallized. I would just increase the fresh ginger.

Really good, though I needed a lot more than 2/3 cup of pasta water to get the sauce to the right consistency. More like a cup and a half. And added extra sesame oil and soy sauce too, otherwise it would have been too pasty.
This was my exact experience!!!
Make this a couple of days ago and I have to agree with Melissa, the crystallised ginger added a really delicious and unexpected note. I might even add more.  Great salad for the summer.

The magic of peanut butter strikes again! This is delicious! 
My changes- did not add the crystallized ginger, used two limes, used the cilantro, should have used more pasta water (but not much more). 
I used the Trader Joe’s Chili Onion and kept it at 2 tbsp which was just right for me. The cuke soy sauce drizzle was about 1 more tbsp.

I added two tablespoons of agave syrup and it really popped after that. I think my crystalized ginger is lacking any sweetness so it needed something. Also I used GF rice noodles (pad thai style) and GF tamari. Family members are gluten sensitive.
I'm not sure how this was rated five stars.  I found the dish just a really old set of flavors, that to me honestly didn't work together.  I ended up throwing away a good portion of the food.
makes a ton!
Wonderfully flavourful light summer dinner. Can be served at room temperature definitely a make again. No need to fuss about purchasing Chili crisp use your favourite hot sauce to taste.
I added torn squeezed firm tofu and corn kernels to make it a one bowl meal. Good for a quick summer supper but did not move the earth for me

I made it with rice noodles and liked it a lot. I added some toasted sesame oil (in addition to the plain sesame oil) for some more nuttiness. I also added some rice vinegar for more tanginess. Pretty easy to make and satisfying.

Thanks for the info that this worked with orzo.  We had it tonight with noodles - excellent -  but I would like to do it for a buffet and find that noodles can be difficult for people to handle.  I thought of gnocchi - boiled or steamed, not fried, as fried gnocchi should be served as soon as they are cooked - do not hold well, and certainly not in the fridge.  Orzo sound ideal.  For alternative veg, yellow beets, zucchini, broccoli florets, snow peas, peas could all work.
Maybe couscous could work as well?
Has anyone made this dish with beans instead of noodles? I’m inclined to give that a try.
One dissenting vote on the crystallized ginger: I skipped the crystallized ginger and wish I hadn’t— it was tasty but a little flat without it!

We used a new jar of Chili crisp and a different brand than we had previously tried and enjoyed. The result was noodles that were too spicy. Also the very thinly sliced cucumber disappeared into the noodles and didn’t provide any crunch so I would slice them a bit thicker.

So tasty! My noodles came in a 10oz package so I wound up with more dressing than necessary — siphoned some from the bowl and into a jar before adding the noodles and veggies. I also added sliced snap peas and red pepper in addition to the cucumber. Halved the chile crisp for the sake of the toddler palates, but served chile crisp on the table for adults. 10/10 will make again. Leftovers for lunch were the highlight of my week!
This recipe calls for a pound of Chinese noodles which I think was double what it should have been. Otherwise a terrific meal.
Requires a lot more punch. The little bits of crystallized ginger are delicious on the  2nd or 3rd day when they have softened.

So awfully sweet and gloppy, we couldn't eat it. Threw most of it out. Next time, omit the maple syrup or honey, hoisin is already super sweet. Instead add one or two tablespoons rice wine vinegar and an equal amount of vegetable stock to the mix. Way too many sesame seeds. I'd add some sliced cabbage and celery with the garlic and scallions to tone down the stickiness of the noodles. Oyster sauce works much better with noodles than hoisin.

For the questions so far:
~ serve cold or hot? 
Made as written, only I added the hot pasta to the sauce 1st, then added room temp salted and then drained & rinsed English cucumbers. This way the sauce "melted" into the pasta and didn't cook the cukes. Chilled in fridge overnight so flavors got happy together and ate cold for lunch, 97° outside. Refreshing and delicious.
~ candied ginger? 
Personally I thought it added nice sparkles of joy. 
~ to chili crisp or not to chili crisp? 
Tho I like it, my gut doesn't. Left it out. Yummy anyway. 
Hope it lasts thru the heat wave. Cook once, enjoy for days from fridge, just need to clean bowl and fork. Happy.
I think at least the first half of your comment was supposed to be added to a different recipe, as there is no hoisin or maple syrup/honey in this one
@Heather I think it's to do with salting them and allowing the juices to come out before mixing in, do you get more sauce

My experience was a disaster, unlike your other fans. The peanut butter would not turn into the creamy mixture. I put it in my Small blender and am surprised and grateful the motor lasted. My husband and I ate some for supper. It tasted fine but was not pleasurable to eat because the mixture adhered to see noodles causing sizeable soft lumps. I added PERFECT CHEF sesame ginger salad dressing making it edible. I had my hopes up about this recipe but won't make it again. What a mess to clean up!

@Judith Benson, this was my experience. I had to add a lot of pasta water to get the creamy texture that was necessary for a sauce and using fresh Chinese egg noodles (cooked first), I did not find this dish to be very tasty without using a lot more soy sauce, ginger, and sesame oil. One cup of PB is quite a lot and needs more additional flavors than a few tablespoons can provide. Also one cup of PB ratcheted up the calories and fat. The crystallized ginger was a nice addition.

I'm confused too about this dish being cold- I will just rinse the noodles in cold water, then as someone else suggested, give them an ice bath. Can't wait to make this- we are having a heat wave for the next 10 days.......
@Sam I made at night when house was a bit cooler and will refrigerate … wish me luck!

I had high hopes for this, but I think it was way too much peanut butter. Next time I’m going to cut it in half and add a teaspoon of Fish sauce and maybe a splash of rice wine vinegar. The sauce was too gloppy and cloying and way too much peanut butter.
I halved the recipe, but 8 oz of Chinese spaghetti (before cooking) was too much noodle for the sauce. Also didn't use crystallized ginger.
Should the sesame oil should be toasted sesame oil or the nearly tasteless plain sesame oil?
@Yeaster I use toasted and it seems like the way to go. A bit more depth.

This was a huge hit, with 2 small mods:

1. Halved the egg noodles and backfilled with a bag of frozen / pan-fried pork gyoza.

2. Cut the cucumbers into sticks instead of slicing. Not sure why it was better but...it was better.

I like this recipe but the end result texture was kind of mushy. I struggled when mixing it because the cucumbers seem to all stick to the sides of the bowl and not get mixed in with the pasta. I added some shredded rotisserie chicken.

This recipe should not have 5 stars, sorry. This recipe uses way way too much peanut butter and like others pointed out, the sauce ends up being very hard to work with and the flavor profile ends up being way too rich and salty.

@DK totally agree, mystified by the 5 stars.   A full cup of PB yields leaden glop.  As others have noted, you’ll get an unappealing looking reddish brown color, not the lovely photo from the food stylist.
Me too - I just posted - it was truly awful - the sauce totally swamped the whole thing.

I made this today using pad thai rice noodles. I skipped the candied ginger, but used a little more fresh ginger and grated up some lime zest with it. I chopped fresh cilantro and basil leaves together too for the garnish. 

Really enjoyed this. Served with cold roasted chicken on the side. Would also be a great side for fish. But looking forward to enjoying the leftovers as a main for lunch tomorrow.

A question:  does it matter whether you use “natural” peanut butter or the processed kind?

@Lynn P. L. I’ve found that for recipes, processed peanut butter melts better. Natural, which we buy, tends to separate - oil on top - and dry out in the bottom of the container. I used the last of a jar and had to really work to whisk out the clumps.
@ES I use the little “blender” attachment for my hand mixer and mix the oil into the peanut butter, takes a few minutes. (Hang on to the jar!) Then keep in the fridge and it will not separate.

What is Chile crisp and where do I get it?

Chili crisp is a condiment made of fried chili peppers and oil, with other ingredients depending on the brand. In a large supermarket it'll be in the Asian food sections, in an Asian food market in the condiments section. You can find it online if it's not in your store, or you can do a rough sub by sauteeing chili flakes in canola.

I'm tempted to slice the rhubarb very thinly and roast using this method, then running a stick blender through the lot and canning it as rhubarb butter.  The pH of rhubarb is low enough to safely water-bath can it, and preserving this in jars will save my limited freezer space.
@Laura in NJ rhubarb loses its color when canned. Still tastes good but not as pretty.

@Laura.  Sugar, glucose, fructose, maple syrup- all the same to the body.  Processed the same, impact the same.  Not healthier than plan white sugar. Different taste and texture but definitely not healthier.

@Deborah PhD biochemist here. Glucose and fructose are in fact processed differentlly in the body, via different metabolic processes. Evidence shows that high consumption of large amounts of fructose leads to poor health outcomes, Look up the work of Harvard endocrinologist Robert Lustig for more.

Has anyone tried using rhubarb as a complementing component in a savory, non-dessert type bake? I’m thinking of roasting alongside bone in, skin on chicken breasts or thighs, sheetpan style,…perhaps with something like brussel sprouts. Maybe toss some kind of toasted nuts tossed in at end for crunch and texture. Does that sound appealing?
@QT Pie yes! I think there is a chicken recipe here. You can also make a bbq sauce (Victoria sauce) and last year I made rhubarb Kimchi that was so good.
@QT Pie Persian cooking uses rhubarb as a vegetable.

@Mark S. Simply rinse, dry, and slice your rhubarb. Put into large ziploc bags and freeze flat. You can measure out a pound at a time if you like so that each bag has roughly the same amount.
The best part is that you don't have to precook the rhubarb before using. The freezing breaks down the cell wall structure and everything is nicely mushy.

I smile at mention of rhubarb. The school I went to in England, on the western edge of the Lake District, age 7 yrs - 12  ['58-63] served meals cooked from the produce of the school kitchen garden & local farmers. Everything entirely fresh/organic.

One year the kitchen garden had a massive glut of rhubarb. We were served rhubarb jam with every breakfast, rhubarb crumble, rhubarb pie, rhubarb tart, unadorned rhubarb & custard. Our teeth were under acidic attack from rhubarb ad naus. Basta!
@Christopher Nation I’m surprised you still like it!

Delicious. As I write this, I am eating the roasted rhubarb with roasted cherries over mashed sweet potatoes topped with sunflower seeds for breakfast. Pretty and tasty. At 74, I had never tasted rhubarb until today. I plan to make up for lost time. Thank you Ms. Clark for this all your other tasty recipes.

@Mark. We freeze a lot of 'barb, Just chop it into whatever lengths suits your needs, maybe 1-2 inches, spread it on a flat surface, cookie sheet or such, freeze, then bag. Keeps well because of all the water.

So good!  Have been roasting rhubarb for several years with vanilla and sugar and freezing.  Goes beautifully with any sort of simple lemon cake.  Spoon over plated slices and top with chopped pistachios. A family favorite.

My husband loves rhubarb crisp but hates it when the sauce is runny. I solved this problem by roasting it dry without sugar before putting it into the recipe. Takes a lot of the moisture out and adds a bit of browning to the flavor.
here in the cooler western NC mountains, rhubarb thrives all summer until heavy frost.

I often toss a handful of frozen raspberries in right when it comes out of the oven. (Nigella says that cooked strawberries have the texture and consistency of slugs, and although I have never eaten a slug, I agree.) I've not cooked with cardamom pods, what do I need know when using with this recipe?

@Linda you can crush them in a mortar and pestle or use a spice grinder. You can buy decorticated cardamom, which is the seeds minus the pods (I use a *lot* of cardamom and do this). Burlap & Barrel sells the seeds in a convenient little grinder. 

Enjoy!

Quick AI search says: While both are forms of added sugar, pure maple syrup is unrefined and retains beneficial antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals that white sugar lacks. It also has lower Glycemic index. 
Of course all concentrated sugar should be consumed in moderation, but it’s fair to say that using maple syrup may have some added benefits if you like the flavor. 
Look forward to trying recipe no matter what sugar I use. ;)

Folks, AI and Google (as in comment below) are not sources - they are means of assembling information from other sources. What you really need to know is whether those other, original sources are reliable. A scientific paper, or somebody's blog? Folklore? Fad diets and nutritional quackery? Replicable, verifiable lab research? Empirical experiments? Cultural experience? It matters where the information comes from.

@Pretzel 
Healthcare provider here. Too much carbohydrate in any form is an enormous health problem. 
Maple syrup does have small amounts of minerals and antioxidants. Honey too. The speed at which the body processes various sugars varies a bit (slower is better), if that matters to anyone. 
But personally I think the more important issue is how these crops affect local economies and workers, and how they impact the environment. Cane sugar is probably the worst in that regard.
Roasted rhubarb is lovely with cardamom and orange too

@cassiadk
I’m adding orange and cardamom! Thanks for the suggestion!

I grow my own rhubarb. When I pull it, I chop it into 1 inch pieces, throw it in a freezer bag as it is and add to the bag as I pull more. I find there’s no need to blanch it first. Take out as much as you need and leave the rest in the freezer.  I’m just finishing last spring’s harvest. If you’re roasting it, it will need to be thawed. If you’re cooking stove top, you’ll barely need to add any water as the frost from freezing, it will melt. I use mostly sugar, but add a dollop of maple syrup and grate a bit of fresh ginger into it.

I made a delicious rhubarb compote the other day, w farmstand rhubarb, peeled apples, strawberries and vanilla.  So wonderful, and  sweet as can be!!  And no added sugar~ who would’ve thought this is possible?!  Try it!!  So wonderful, will do same for pie!
