Didn’t have vanilla bean or cardamom so added one tsp of vanilla extract as soon as took out of oven. Plus, I “smushed” some of the rhubarb with the back of my spatula to release the juices. Had over vanilla ice cream with slivered almonds on top. Nummy!

I discovered, thanks to a NYT recipe for Rhubarb Blueberry Pie, that throwing in a couple of handfuls of fresh or frozen blueberries, is a great addition to stewed rhubarb on the stovetop. 
It cannot replace all of the sugar needed but can certainly reduce the quantity. In the end it’s a very personal choice on the level of tart/sweetness.

@Mark S  I usually freeze it raw, cut into chunks and put in zipper-sealed freezer bag with air pressed out as much as possible. It does well for a few months but I find it will dry out after that and loses its remaining moisture by becoming extra runny when cooking. I find rhubarb cooked in sugar as in this recipe, or in pies and crisps, holds up better to long-term freezing.

I love a Mark Bittman recipe from his NYT Minimalist column, about 2001. Combine 1 pound rhubarb, washed and trimmed, 1/3 c sugar, and a pinch of saffron in a small, covered saucepan over low heat. Cook about 20 minutes, stirring very occasionally, or until the rhubarb becomes saucy. Add salt and/or more sugar to taste. Sauce will thicken if you cook it longer. I use this with fish as Mark suggests, and also over yogurt, ice cream, cake, etc etc. It keeps well in the fridge for quite a while.

I had about half the amount of rhubarb called for in the recipe and I supplemented with roughly an equal amount of cut up strawberries, since I've always liked the combination. I cut the rhubarb smaller, more like 1/4 inch pieces. Using what I had in the house, I threw in some powdered cardamom, almond extract, and only about 3 tablespoons of maple syrup. (Note: I like maple syrup. It adds flavor and interest so it may be easier to use less. But I'm not the same Laura who said it was healthier...) It was delicious by itself, both sweet and tart, and also great over good vanilla ice cream. The rhubarb I bought was red. I understand from other sources that this is sweeter and therefore may need less sweetener.

I had a bit less than a pound from a neighbor's garden, so I added a 1/4 c of sugar (demerara because I've been looking for ways to use it up), and it took about 13 minutes to cook.  I put it in a mason jar, and watched the liquid level rise - it gets really juicy.  It tastes great, not too tart or sweet, and I look forward to adding it to yogurt and ice cream~
@Rolien: Sugar in the US is NOT necessarily vegan. Bone Char  is used to refine it. Organic Sugar is vegan as far as I know.

It was beautiful paired with pouring custard.  In the spirit of experimenting, I used 1/3 cup sugar to 3 cups rhubarb (could have used a little more if it wasn't paired with something sweet) and also added orange zest, juice of half an orange and coins of ginger.

15-20 minutes at 425 would make for very overdone, mushy asparagus, in the opinion of a long-time gardener/consumer of said vegetable. Start checking at 5 minutes. The smaller stalks will, of course, cook faster than the bigger ones. They go from just right to overdone in seconds.

This is my go to process for dinner. It’s just me so I don’t like to heat up the kitchen with the oven. I have a Panasonic toaster oven that is excellent. Radiant heat cooks quickly and doesn’t require preheating. Line the pan with no stick aluminum foil. Put the protein on the pan along with halved small potatoes, season and cook.I keep some seasoned olive oil concoctions in the refrigerator for this. After the appropriate amount of time add the veggie…asparagus, broccolini, zucchini, etc. plus more olive oil, lemon, seasoning cook another 10 - 15 minutes. If fish is your protein of choice remove it from the oven. The timing is instinctual you’ll get better at it with practice. Your solo dinner in the time it takes to catch up on the local news. Healthy, minimal clean up. A glass of wine = perfect.

Delicious and so juicy. I used 4 breasts (no bone or skin) because that’s what I had. Added a chopped Yukon gold potato for 10 minutes first, then followed the recipe to a T. Oh, also added a sliced leek. So not quite a T, but it was phenomenal.

Used boneless skinless chicken breast. Suggest cooking it at the same time as the asparagus. Check rest for doneness at about 17 minutes. Excellent low-calorie low-fat meal.
I have trouble with the first seasoning. 4 tsps (2 salt, 1 coriander, 1 paprika) don't even begin to cover my chicken pieces. I doubled it.

This recipe was excellent.  I used boneless, skinless Chix thighs and let them sit in the fridge for 4 hours with the spices on them.  They were still a little frozen, so I roasted them for 10 min before adding the asparagus.  Roasted again for 17 min.  Added some chopped dill to the dressing, and my husband kept saying how good the dish was.   This is a keeper..

So good! I used bone-in, skin-on chicken breasts and added a pat of butter under the skin before baking. The scallions were an amazing addition to the asparagus – I will always make it that way from now on!

Amazing dish - so simple and the cleanup was nothing!  (My husband liked that.)  The chicken skin was still crispy and the asparagus was cooked perfectly with a little crunch.  But the winning part was the dressing!!  So snappy and bright - it made the dish.  I think it would make a great dressing for an arugula salad, as well…  Five or six stars in our house!!  Thanks, Melissa - your recipes are always excellent!  I could read one without knowing the author, but when I see the herbs at the end, I know it’s you!

The timing of this didn't work for me. I used skin-on/bone-in chicken breasts that didn't finish cooking in the specified time, and the asparagus was overcooked in 15 minutes. The flavor of the dressing and herbs were just "okay" and overall kind of bitter. I used lots of expensive ingredients here, so I'm disappointed.
For Vegetarians Lemon Tofu with Asparagus ~ this recipe would suit well.

I made this tonight for seven people. 
Doubled the recipe.  
Used mint and parsley for my fresh herbs, and followed the recipe precisely. I was liberal with the lemon and zested/juiced xl lemons.  
Served it with mashed potatoes. 
It was fabulous!
Delicious!
This recipe looks great-will double the sauce and then probably move the whole operation to the grill.  No reason to fire up the oven during these nice spring temperatures.

I really prefer some measurements even in casual cooking. If you saw the sizes of my lemons, you’d understand part of my issue. Looking for approximate measure of lemon juice and lemon zest. Also the recipe says to add the zest, but it really doesn’t say zest the  lemon, which I assume is what you want?
Totally agree, Linda!  My wife has the same issue, her lemons are far larger than average, so more guidance is always appreciated
If you know that your lemons are extra large, isn't that sufficient to adjust the juice and zest?
Made this tonight and it was wonderful. I added some blanched green beans and some grape tomatoes to the asparagus and scallions. The dressing really adds a lot. Thanks for a great recipe!
I love the dressing but I felt it needed to be tossed with the asparagus and chicken and schmalz and go back in the hot oven for a minute or two and it worked!
This was lovely. So fast and easy. There was plenty of fat in the pan from the chicken, so pulled back on the olive oil for the vegetables, and I always use less salt. The dressing made the dish!

I made this tonight and it was delicious. The only variation was to use 3 TBS instead of 4 TBS of EVO (for healthier recipe). My suggestions would be less dressing on the aspargus than the chicken. Otherwise, this is an easy, delicious meal. Thank you Melissa.
Very nice recipe. I will add more lemon juice next time .

This was a rare NYT disappointment; while it combines good flavors, the overall effect is far too bland. Tripling the mustard might help. Also, the recipe needs 2-3 times more asparagus for balanced proportions.
Balanced proportions? Who is the arbiter of that?
Used boneless, skinless thighs and it was delicious and moist. Will make again!
I used a whole chicken with the marinade and then added carrots instead of asparagus. All else was the same. Fantastic!

I mixed chicken thighs with chicken breast cut into 2-3 pieces. I used only half of the lemon juice and did not use all of the lemon dressing on the sheet pan at the end and it was still plenty lemony. I used the remainder of the lemon dressing as salad dressing with a touch of balsamic added. It was delicious. Will definitely be adding it to the rotation.

Made quite close to as instructed and was delightfully surprised - the dressing is a lovely addition that I wasn’t sure was needed, though I drizzled it only on the asparagus and dipped the chicken in it only after I ate the wonderfully crisp skin. Of course locally grown organic chicken was probably key.
Really delicious and easy.  Used boneless thighs and cooked for 25 minutes
Absolutely fantastic, Queen Melissa! Cooked just as written with the first fresh cut asparagus from the garden mint was a delightful addition. Will try dill next time.

Delicious. I used bone-in chicken thighs, cut off excess (not all) skin and fat. Dried, seasoned, let sit about an hour. Cooked 20 minutes at 400 convection roast, checked temp, added asparagus and green onions, cooked another 15 minutes. Grated only one garlic clove, but it was large, along with zest and juice of a lemon, a bit extra Dijon, roasted cumin, and the olive oil. Did it first, so it had time for the flavors to meld. All in all, simple, easy, excellent one pan dinner.
Picked fresh asparagus right out of the garden. Great recipe! It is a satisfying and zesty. Used boneless chicken breast and kept my eye on it- turned out great. Definite-do over😄
Really easy to make!

This is just a delicious dish.  I used bone-in skin-on thighs.  The first time I made this as written - fantastic.  The second time I cooked the thighs in the dressing - super fantastic.  Served with roasted potatoes and broccolini - so dang fine!
Bone-in skin-on thighs are perfectly cooked roasted @425 for 30 minutes.
That doesn't take into account the size of the thighs or whether they were straight out of the Frigidaire.

This was delicious. The changes I made were used boneless chicken breast because I don’t like dark meat. I can’t have salt so I was generous with seasoning and added smoked paprika. The dressing is super yum. I added dill, mint and basil because I grow them. Leftover dressing will be delicious on salads. The asparagus was overcooked, next time I’ll cook it for 5 minutes. Definitely will make again.
Ended up using about twice as much spice as called for, because I used the shake-chicken-in-a-bag method of applying spice mix. So... don't do that?
This is really good!  We loved it. So easy too

Just made this for dinner tonight- it was a big hit and will stay in the rotation! I used 6 bone-in skin-on thighs- since there are just 2 of us we'll have leftovers and I saved the schmaltz for a rice dish, but we devoured all of the asparagus. I followed the recipe exactly but, after tasting the dressing, added 4 drops of honey to soften the bite a tiny bit. Lovely dish!

I planned on making this last night, but realized I already used the asparagus I had bought earlier in the week, so this recipe is now called Lemon Chicken with Broccolini, Cauliflower, and Mushrooms in our house.  Don't tell my wife!  She liked it!

Great dinner when asparagus are in season. For the 2 of us, I used four small bone-on, skin-on thighs but made all of the dressing. Roasted it all in a cast-iron frying pan. Very nice and easy. 
Not sure that the coriander added anything; would probably switch into some sort of spice blend. The paprika did color the thighs nicely.
Sauce overwhelmed the fresh tender local asparagus. But was really good on the chicken!

This was delightful and tasted rich and buttery even though no butter is included. I used boneless thighs and put them, my (fat) asparagus and sliced scallions all on the pan at once, roasted about 25 minutes turning once. Perfecto! I also suggest snapping asparagus into two-inch chunks, so much easier to eat. The suggested herb mix made me nervous until I tasted it. Yum all around!
used thighs and breasts and added a few small yellow potatoes to sheet pan.

Terrific recipe, will make again
Made on 2 sheet pans because I added small potatoes halved tucked around the chicken

Rave reviews. Made mixture of skinless boneless breast and thighs to satisfy 
Used boneless skinless breasts and thighs to satisfy everyone.  Added small red potatoes to pan.  They roasted perfectly. Made 1.5x sauce. Don’t need whole bunch of scallions. 5 stars

I made this for dinner for a couple of friends.  It was so easy and so good.  I followed the recipe for the seasoning and the dressing, but I used boneless skinless thighs - easier to cook and easier to eat.  I roasted them at the same time as the asparagus for 20 minutes at 400.  The chicken and the asparagus were perfectly cooked. Served with rice.  Everyone loved it.
I used 4 bone-in chicken thighs and adjusted the seasoning quantity a bit but otherwise made it as written. So good! Bright and spring-y flavors! Wonderful!

I have made it twice,  just as described, with 2 breasts and 3 thighs (large, bone in, skin on) and it is delicious. The sauce is lovely, and the chicken was perfectly cooked. I used a meat thermometer to be sure the breasts were in the 165 F range before pulling.
Outstanding !
@kilioopu. Moved from Northampton, Ma. 13 years ago. STILL miss Hadley Grass. It is the very best!
@Anna Lea Kantor had to look that up. Made me smile.

Lovely easy dish. Used 4 whole legs/thighs. Marinated in the dry rub 4 hours. Took another reader’s suggestion and mixed 3/4 lb small halved red potatoes with the same spice mix and cooked them with the chicken, then added the asparagus for the last 18 minutes of roasting (I had thick asparagus). Followed sauce instructions and it was the right amount. For leftovers: Roasted another 3/4 lb potatoes 20 minutes, added leftover chicken & sauce plus fresh asparagus to sheet pan, roasted 15-20 min. Will make again.

So simple and so delicious. The lemon-garlic dressing is perfect. I wanted a Mediterranean vibe so used za’tar on chicken and in the dressing. I look forward to leftovers tomorrow. I also look forward to serving when family and friends visit.
Made this last week as written and LOVED it!  Double the sauce as it is so good.  Also made a second time with brussels sprouts and also very good.

This was yummy. I don't like dark meat so used boneless breast. The lemon dressing was so delicious. I can't use salt so assertive flavors are great so my food has enough flavor. I also added the pan drippings. Asparagus and scallions were overcooked but the charred flavor was good so I ate them anyway. Next time I'll cook them cook 5 minutes less. Definitely will make again.
very good; cooked the chicken for 40 min and the asparagus (fat ones) for 10

Delicious!!  I used leg quarters and roasted an additional 10 minutes before adding asparagus to the pan. Internal temperature reached 185 and then let rest for 10 minutes before serving. We will definitely add this recipe to our rotation.
Made this last night and was phenomenal. Baked it on the top rack of the oven to get the skin a bit more crispy and also added a little bit of Worcestershire sauce to the dressing.

The work vs results ratio here is excellent. You don't even need to make the sauce. Just sprinkle with lemon juice but I am partial to the sauce .  The scallions - or spring onions or shallots really add to the asparagus. Or sprinkle some sesame seeds on them.   Put some new potatoes in a casserole dish with a little oil and voila - everything was done at once, crispy and delicious.
Love so much. Next time, will make even more of the lemon sauce!

title: Roasted Sausages With Chickpeas and Spinach Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: Cooking is not an exact science. If a recipe says cook until the sausages are golden, about 10 minutes, that is guideline not an absolute. The cook is constantly observing, testing, and adjusting. That’s what a good cook does. If it takes another three or four minutes so be it. Or more!

So delicious, and the crisped chickpeas really made the whole dish, adding nuttiness and texture. I used kale instead of spinach for extra volume. After broil finishing sausage and chickpeas, I removed them from the pan, then added 1/2 cup chicken bone broth + vinegar/garlic sauce and scraped off all the delicious brown bits and tossed with kale, then put back in oven at to cooked for 6-8 mins till kale browned.
Sauce reduces, so recommend salt to taste at the end of cooking not before.

@Jung
Crispy chickpeas are like the prize inside!
Based on the temp and cooking time the sausages are raw.  The recipe should be more specific.
@watercupcake. Use a thermometer.

1) what should be  the internal temp of the sausages? 
2) what is the best/quickest/safest way to defrost frozen sausages?
@Alicia Gordon: According to Amazing Ribs.com all uncooked sausages should be cooked to 160F or 71C. Pre-cooked sausages don’t need that much time.

Sausages cooked fine. The note at end of recipe said to add time if the sausages are cold. The 4 stars came from the dressing and the spinach. The spinach didn’t wilt which would have helped. With the dressing, it was scant and needed more of an oomph. Will make again, but next time I’m doubling the dressing and whisking in some Dijon.

Really good and easy. Added chopped bell peppers to the chickpeas and they cooked up nicely in the same amount of time, and I followed the recs from commenters and zhuzhed up the dressing with a teaspoon of dijon and a drizzle of chile crisp. Will make again.

Sounded good, but even better!! delicious!! I made per the recipe with one addition: added a chopped fennel bulb first and roasted for a few minutes before adding the chickpeas. Also put the spinach on the pan with the chickpeas and back on the oven for a couple of minutes to wilt it.

Definitely the least flavorful thing I’ve made from NYT. The spinach didn’t wilt, the beans got dry, this is a never repeat. And I even did cumin and red pepper at best. I can say this is a base on which you need to add/spice heavily.
Should this be sausages with raw meat or pre-cooked?
Definitely cook these sausages until done
@Mimi raw, fresh sausages, not the processed pre-cooked packaged ones!

Easy base - Made the following modifications based on other comments- 

To sausage/chickpeas, added:
3 total cloves of garlic
1 tsp crushed red pepper
1 tsp crushed roasted cumin
Handful of goat cheese after it came out of the oven 

To dressing, added:
2 total cloves of garlic
1 tsp Dijon
The sausages are cooked for up to 15 minutes..at high temp. Pretty sure they’ll be done.

I added a spiralized zucchini after taking out the sausages and let that get hot together. There wasn’t going to be enough veg otherwise, with only the spinach. My sausages were cooked through after 17 minutes and it was an okay meal all in all. It served three of us though, not four.
Added a cup of frozen corn to the sheet pan which provided a nice sweet note
This was delicious and easy.  I love cumin seed and greens.  The spinach didn't really wilt, so I popped the pan back into the oven for less than a minute and it was perfect. Very yummy!

Very happy with this recipe.  For the sauce, I used 1T dijon, 1T olive oil, 2T white wine vinegar, & ~1T minced garlic.  Whisked to blend.  We prefer kale -- sautée in a covered skillet just until it changes color & wilts a bit, then layered over chick peas and under sausage. Crisp under broiler until you're happy with it.  Toss everything with the dressing & enjoy.  This recipe is a keeper.
Crispy garbanzo beans make this thing.

Great recipe. After reading the comments I doubled the spices and used spicy chicken sausages. I also used a cast iron skillet instead of a pan. It retained enough heat to easily wilt the greens at the end. (I used mixed "power greens" since that was what I had). Will definitely make this again. And share.

I made this supremely easy dish with plant-based Field Roast Italian Garlic & Fennel vegan sausages (4 sausages to a pound) and the dish was delicious. I also made Martha Rose Shulman's Whole Wheat Focaccia (also a NYT recipe) so the meal was completely vegan (unless you could the yeast beasties in the focaccia)--and full of flavor and substantial. This is a dish (and combination) that I will put on repeat. I did not make any other alterations to the recipe. I didn't need to--it is perfect.

500 is way too hot for EVOO, I used regular olive oil.

My Italian sausages (raw) were very thick and cold, so I ended up needing about 5 more minutes on this (which resulted in the chickpeas being borderline but still good and crispy).

I had nature farmer market spinach on hand not baby, so when the sausages were done I put the spinach on the sheet pan and put it in the very hot oven for just a minute or two before pulling it out to mix everything together. It wilted nicely.

Overall a very low effort and yummy dinner. Could serve with bread and olive oil to stretch this between more people, or if it’s just 2-3, chickpea is the carb.
@Deb Graza makes an olive oil that you can use with  high heat. It’s nicknamed “Frizzle” on the bottle. The smoke point is 490°. It’s not at 500° but close.

@Debi 
Perhaps you might want to try Algae Oil. It works perfectly in high heat cooking, has a very slight buttery taste and is available by mail order or a Whole Foods

We enjoyed this sheet pan dish. I generally followed the recipe, but I didn’t have cider vinegar so used balsamic instead. I also added about a dozen halved grape tomatoes that needed to be used up. Like others, I removed the sausages and put the pan back in the oven for a minute or two after I added the spinach. It wilted nicely.

My husband saw this recipe in the NYT and went to shop for the sausage and spinach that day.  I was glad to add an item to our usual rotation of dinners, but didn’t expect much from the recipe.  Wow - I was wrong.  The cumin and red pepper flakes, the sausage and the slightly wilted spinach with a tiny vinegar note - just delicious.  
I made the dish in a large cast iron pan, roasted the sausage and chickpeas for 20 minutes at 450 degrees (the oven big enough for the pan doesn’t have a broiler).

This will be a weeknight repeat at our house! Low effort but tasty, I swapped a bit of spicy smoked paprika for the chili flakes. Cooking times & temperatures were about right for chicken sausages, spinach needed an extra minute in the still-hot oven to actually wilt.

Easy to make and my incredibly fussy 18 year old loved it!! Score! She even ate leftovers and was craving it the 3rd day. Thank you!
I used Trader Joe's chicken sausage - it's precooked so just needed to be heated up
This was so easy and delicious. I did add a little ground mustard to vinegar before missing with spinach.

This was really good! Delivered exactly what was described and the chickpea spinach salad tasted way better than I thought it would. I ended up just tossing the chickpeas and spinach mixture in an actual bowl because the cooking dish was too shallow
Is it really necessary to broil the sausages at the end. I’ve been roasting sausages for years now and I just turn them to brown all sides
Both my teenage boys loved it and that is a tall order!
Why is it always toss “in the pan”? How does one even do that without making a huge mess? Why not toss in a bowl and then put in the pan????

I use chickpeas frequently in salads and the leftover canned ones would “turn” before I used them up. So I tried freezing leftovers but the texture when thawed was mushy. Okay, I admit to being picky!! So I tried cooking dried beans and the result is fantastic. I’ve used both Camellia and Rancho Gordo beans with similar results. Freezing them works well as does roasting them after freezing. I am now cooking them in my new Instant Pot which is a bit trial and error but I shall persevere.

I followed the recipe exactly but it would have been more interesting if I hadn't. Chickpeas and sausages are both proteins to me and some wilted spinach is barely a vegetable. So definitely not a full meal. This is very awkward to eat. Chickpeas roll all over the places on their own and sausages need a sharp knife. Besides tripling the flavouring I'm not sure what else I'd do to make this recipe work. Curious to see what other cooks do.

A couple of tips:  as you mentioned, using a knife to cut the sausages is a good idea; and you can use the same technique to eat the chickpeas as you would to eat, oh, I don't know, peas or something like that.
You can eat with a tablespoon, supplementing a knife and fork.  ("This is very awkward to eat. Chickpeas roll all over the place")

Very disappointing, structurally. If anyone was able to get some chickpeas, spinach and sausage on the same forkful, I’d love to hear how. Eating a bunch of chickpeas, then some spinach, then sausage, not as satisfying

I read all the comments, and here’s how you fix the bland. More seasoning! More garlic, more cumin seeds, more red pepper. I used half a pound of sweet sausage, sausages and half a pound of hot sausages. Absolutely delicious and definitely not bland. Additionally my spinach wilted fine. I just stirring it around in the hot chickpeas with their hot oil. I did use the meat thermometer, and it seemed like I needed an extra three minutes for the sausages to be done, but not overdone.  definitely a keeper!

Very easy.  Recommend you paper-towel-dry the chickpeas a bit before starting.  To help the greens wilt a bit more, in step 5 put the roasting pan on a burner whilst you're tossing the spinach with the beans.  (as an aside, see her 2019 recipe "Crispy Spiced Chickpeas . . . " for another seasoning option)
By your note, I'm guess that the chickpeas should also be rinsed. Is that correct?
Don’t understand all the raves. At all. I had a cold and was hoping that I’d lost my sense of taste. But no one else had a cold. Total dud.

I typically take taste liberties when cooking but did this one by the book. In a cast iron pan. It was good, not great. It needs a sauce!---the chickpeas were dry and the one teaspoon of cider vinegar didn't do much. I've read the comments about whisking some dijon mustard in. If I make it again i'll make a sauce.

Great idea for a recipe! I only had extra virgin olive oil on me and did not want to burn the oil or smoke up the kitchen, so I cooked the chickpeas and sausage for 15 minutes instead at 450F. Turned the sausage every few minutes and rotating the pan halfway through cooking. Also turned it up to 500F for two minutes after the initial 15 minutes, to get some color on the sausage and crisp the edges of the chickpeas.

I also swapped the cumin seeds for garam masala, which has cumin powder in it, and used two teaspoons of it and a dash more. Really wanted to see the chickpeas well covered in the seasoning.

Last, I did throw the chickpea/spinach/vinegar mix back into the oven for two minutes after I mixed it all together. Just to take the edge off of the garlic, as my wife's stomach can't handle raw garlic well. Quick and tasty weeknight meal.

Terrific and quick.  I added a red bell pepper and some onion (just because I had them) with the chickpeas.  I rotated the sausages half way through broiling so they were browned all over.  Everything was done beautifully.

Spinach did not wilt at all from heat so I popped it back in the still warm oven for a few minutes. Add aspaparagus just before broiling and that worked well. I would double the amount of dressing next time.
Has anyone tried subbing Swiss chard or arugula for the spinach? Or any other green veggies? I don’t always have spinach on hand.

@Mollie, for another one-dish NYT Cooking recipe that I love that has sweet potatoes or squash instead of chickpeas, I put a bed of arugula down on the plate and put the hot sausage/sweet potatoes over it. The hot items wilt the arugula nicely and you don't need a sauce.
The NYT Baked Tofu recipe worked great to substitute the sausages in this recipe
Will this recipe work with vegetarian/vegan sausages?

I made this last week, with a couple of modifications.
I pierced the sausages all over with a fork and nuked them for a while to drain off some of the fat.
2 cloves of garlic made me laugh.... I used about 6. Because garlic...
My wife and I enjoyed the dish, and got two dinners from it.

To paraphrase Mark Twain: "Too much of anything is bad... but too much garlic is just enough."

Mr. Clemens was speaking of "good whisky", but I find the formula works for garlic just fine!

I have a Portuguese soup recipe almost identical to this - just throw sausage, chick peas, and a chopped onion into a pot, cover with water and boil.  Blend using an immersion blender, then reheat and add spinach or kale to steam them leafy greens.  Great winter soup.
I made this last night and it was delicious - and the entire family enjoyed it!  I loved it because it is healthy, light and EASY.

To thaw quickly: Save some durable plastic trays from frozen food items. To thaw sausages, place them (in their package) in a tray about 25-50% bigger than the package and fill the tray with tap water. Set the tray on a drain board so there's air circulation all around. Sausages thaw in an hour or less. For a super-quick thaw, arrange the tray so that a slow trickle from the tap can run out of the tray and into the sink. Sausages can thaw in as little as 15 minutes.
Wait until the sausages are room temperature before starting.
Followed the recipe as written using turkey sausage. The whole thing was a bit dry and didn’t really come together flavor wise.

i used Trader Joe's Sweet Potato gnocci instead of the roasted chickpeas (because, kids) and grilled the sausages much longer than estimated in the recipe - i think this called for thin sausages which could broil nicely, but mine had considerable girth and needed more time. Great idea for an easy dinner.

Tried this and followed the recipe exactly. It was "okay" and "I don't hate it" but it really needs some sort of sauce or a thicker dressing to pull everything together. It's fast and easy to make but that's not enough for me to want to make it again.

Delivers a good effort to flavor ratio. Make sure your chickpeas are really dry before you season and roast them, or they won't crisp up so much. I used ground cumin and smoked paprika and it was fine. I used a mix of hot and sweet Italian sausages and they came up to 170 internally after the designated 15 minutes. I had to put the spinach back under the broiler just briefly, as in 30 seconds, to wilt it.
It's fine. The recipe as written has a rather low sausage:other stuff ratio, and the other stuff needs more vinegar and seasoning than the recipe calls for.

I used chicken sausage, and I only had 1 can of garbanzos, so I used cannellini beans as my second can. I also broiled the sausage 5 minutes and flipped them to broil an extra five minutes. Delicious! Will cook again.
Love! Add a chopped fuji (or other) apple to the baking sheet. Add whole grain mustard, honey, and olive oil to the vinaigrette.
Had chicken buffalo sausage so I added a twist. Along with the vinegar and garlic I added a couple of teaspoons of homemade blue cheese dressing to the chickpeas and spinach. Delish!

Made last night. Did not like as much as expected. For one thing, roasting at 500 degrees set off every smoke alarm in the house. If making again, we would use lower temp, cook for longer. The particular sausages we used turned out to be way too salty, although that's not the recipe's fault. We doubled the dressing recipe and add Dijon as recommended by other commenters; that worked out well. The chickpea/spinach part of the dish was great (we did roast the spinach a bit after adding).

Cumin is very a distinct flavor and tends to overwhelm other spices and natural flavors. I feel about it the way some people (my husband for one) feel about cilantro: enough already! Paprika is a nice substitute, added toward the end.

In reading the recipe to prep dinner, i am a bit confused by the directions.  do i preheat to 'roast' (425?) & then broil? --OK, i can do that. i can 'flip' sausage(s) IF i want to brown on all sides. Tee is right: adjust according to YOUR oven, YOUR taste YOUR liking.  keep an eye on it first, and next time, follow what you liked the 'last' time.   Adjust. This is simple recipe.  Be creative & inventive & cook to YOUR liking. (google basic cooking instruction if using UNcooked sausage)
recipe says to roast, then "turn on broiler"
Not a winner for us.  Sausages cooked fine as instructed, but spinach did not wilt and chick peas were dry.
Made as directed and whole family loved it!   I also think, you could leave the sausage off of it and it would make a great side dish.
Hmm. Sounds like an easy recipe but unfortunately my old gas oven does not have a 500 degree setting.  Only up to 425.  I wonder if at the 425 setting would make any difference?
If I try this, I will slice the sausage on an angle like the photo, then put in the over to roast. That should make sure they are cooked, and maybe even get some carmelization. Did anyone try this?
Can you add other greens like Kale?
I always parboil raw sausage for about 10 minutes.
"I always keep sausages on hand in the freezer. "  Are these raw or cooked?
Has anyone tried this with frozen spinach. If so, how did it turn out?
I’m going to try it with vegan Italian sausage, thanks!!!
I have no working broiler in my stove from the 1950s.   How might one adapt this recipe for the oven?
Can you use frozen spinach? Will it wilt down the same?
@EJK frozen spinach is usually cooked already and can be thawed, drained and warmed up with the meal.

I’ve done something similar for years with the Trader Joe’s spicy jalapeño chicken sausages, onions & red or yellow sweet peppers. Inspired by this recipe, I’m going to start adding a can of beans (spouse doesn’t care for chickpeas) to make it more filling and stretch the leftovers!
Can you use chicken sausages for this that are already cooked?
Can you use sweet pineapple sausage?

Loved this! I added 2 minutes to the 15 minute cook, turning sausages half way through. took sausages out and added 6 oz spinach and would have liked a little more...I turn off oven and put the baking dish back in oven for 1.5 minutes to help spinach wilt. I really liked this dish but it would have been a bit better with more "sauce"...maybe would try adding some chisken broth next time (as someone suggested).
How many spinach and chickpea recipes have I received over the past few months? I lost count. I can do better ...

Simple, easy and yummy. And quick.  I made it with lamb sausages and founded it needed something so I threw some crumbled feta on top. Also the spinach did not fully wilt so I put the chickpeas and spinach in a bowl and covered it for a few minutes.  I will keep this recipe to make next time I need something quick and easy.

This is a very efficient and nutritious meal. Garbanzos (Rancho Gordo, not canned) never got crispy, butchered tasty. I used pre-cooked smoked chicken sausages and added cherry tomatoes. Next time I’ll use several spices and more of them, and maybe some hot honey.

For once, a simple and quick recipe using just a few ingredients!Once again I tried and failed to grate garlic, so I ended up mincing it. It is a good weeknight dinner. Next time I think I will wilt the spinach with a brief steaming.
@fran Haselsteiner we use a microplane zester that works really well for grating garlic
Are the sausages raw or precooked? I need clarity.
Sausages are raw.
Would this work baked instead of broiled?

Easiest fast dinner and surprisingly tasty! I used kale instead of spinach and used a lot more. I also added a chopped up zucchini for more veg. I just added more salt and cumin seeds. I also used chicken sausages. I will make this on repeat!

This is a good recipe, but definitely use more vinegar and salt! The garbanzo beans just soak both all up. I think sherry vinegar would also do well as a replacement for apple cider vinegar if you don't have it. I did a mix of spinach and arugula, and they did wilt nicely just mixing them in the hot beans.

This is really a recipe that you have to make your own rather than one to follow to the letter. I added some turmeric and coriander, and some broccoli rabe, garlic and cheddar sausage I found at the store.

I'm a little slow in the kitchen but I was able to go from start to table in 25 minutes - add that to the fact that it can be literally zero prep (I didn't even need a knife!) and my 13x9 fits in my toaster/broiler, it's perfect for hot days after work when you just don't feel like cooking.
If your room temperature brats are "raw" after 15 minutes above 500 degrees you might consider checking on your oven.
Like some other comments- cook at 450 /475 in a cast iron pan- double the dressing - add some Dijon and garlic- mix garlic and red peppers in with the chickpeas and sausage- yummy
Hey folks-I don’t have all spinach but a mix with other greens..do you think I can make them work if I wait and don’t put the hot sausages on top but perhaps let them cool for a bit first?
I cooked it exactly as the recipe said and it was DELICIOUS. Definitely going Into my rotation. Cooked it for a friend who also loved it.
This was very easy to make, and delicious. This recipe will be on regular rotation at our house.
Really quick and easy and so good. I used malt vinegar instead of cider vinegar, and also added some Dijon mustard to the dressing. I will definitely make this again.
This was ok, but it felt like something was missing. I used chicken sausage, and I wonder if it might be better with pork sausage, for a little more juicy, greasy goodness.

Just had this for dinner- it's so quick and easy! Changing out the sausage and some of the spices will add variation. Tonight I used a double smoked garlic sausage, with extra cloves of garlic, some cayenne and smoked paprika with ground cumin. Forgot I was out of ACV so used red wine vinegar. Next time, I'll double the vinegar, because that really perks things up, and definitely use more spice.
Such an easy and yummy weeknight meal. I didn't taste cumin so probably won't bother with it next time; the garlic and the red pepper are doing fine without it.
Super easy and loved the flavors.
Interesting ingredients.  Tastes ok but wouldn't make it again.

Roasted some wedges of spanish onion tossed with a bit of oil for 10-15 mins before adding the chickpeas. Also added a teaspoon of dijon mustard to dressing as someone suggested. Used twice as much spinach. Worked a treat!
A little more garlic and oil and toss with some pasta.
Needed to add chicken stock at end for moisture

Used savoy spinach (rough-chopped) and hot sausage links (casings removed).  Rinsed and drained the chickpeas and quick-dried them between paper towels before seasoning.  Used both cumin seeds & cayenne.  Made to direction except turned the sausages and stirred the chickpeas at 5 min.  Substantial meal.  Agree that the finishing vinegar-salt-garlic could be doubled, if made again.
The silkiness of the spinach combines nicely with the roasted chickpeas. Any good sausage will work.
Tasty, but a bit dry. I'll try adding some chapped tomato if I make it again.
I used veg. sausage. And I cooked it in the air fryer. I dressed the spinach while the other things cooked and that worked well. I think it would be good to add some other vegetables.
And don't forget - though the description above didn't say so, you can use plant-based sausages too!
Made this recently and 5/6 of the family really enjoyed it! Followed the tips (increased the dressing by half & added more spinach by half). Will make again.
We enjoyed this as written. Used both cumin seed and red pepper flakes, sweet Italian sausage. Really good and easy.

Being from the UK, I'm struggling to understand the boiling technique. Is it just grilling and catching the drips? Or is that too simplistic?
I really would like to make this recipe but feel a little overwhelmed...
Well this was okay, we didn't spit it out, but no 5 stars.  Pretty dull. We ended up adding some corn and mashed new potatoes.  Needed more. I certainly will not make it again.
@watercupcake the cook should temp their meat!
@Alicia Gordon depends on what meat the sausages are made from. Safe cooking guidelines are easy to find online.

Husband loved this (I know because he said make it anytime!), I thought it was dry, even though I upped the vinegar and added another splash of oil at the end.  I think the culprit was the sausage - Boar's Head smoked instead of fresh sausage.  Oh, and added some paprika to the chickpeas to up the flavor.  At Jack's request, will make again but with fresh sausage!
I tried this as written and didn’t like it with chickpeas. Tried it tonight with white beans and loved it

Solid foundation recipe! I halved it and roasted in a 10-inch cast iron skillet. I added a dash of smoked paprika and the cumin seeds to the chickpeas for roasting. At the end, I threw in a handful of halved cherry tomatoes and a mix of greens my garden (mostly mizuna, dandelion, and baby kale). So good! Next time I might try subbing lemon juice for the cider vinegar to make it more summery.

Noting some commenters so far report their experience of the cornmeal topping being too little to cover the beans adequately, I wonder if the size of Dutch oven used might be a factor?  The instructions say "large Dutch oven".  At my house that would be my 7 qt size but that would be too big.  So I'm thinking the 5 qt - or 5 1/2 qt size might be more in the range of what is meant by "large" here?  That's the size I'm choosing for first try today.
Rather than using a Dutch oven I used my 12 inch cast iron skillet. Perfect.
@Pat I did the same and my corn bread only covered about half so I’m gonna double the cornbread recipe next time.
Made this tonight, halved. Everything tasted good but I would double the cornbread next time, it sort of dissolved into the chili instead of being a distinct thick layer like in the photo

Since I was just cooking for myself, I made a smaller batch of chili, stuffed bell pepper halves with it and topped them with the cornbread.  Baked on foil-covered cookie sheet.  I did need to cover them for the last five or ten minutes in the oven because the cornbread was getting browned.  The cornbread did overflow the peppers -- that's on me miscalculating how much I needed!

Tasted great, I had one for dinner and one for lunch at work.
Add black olives and corn for a heartier meal.
The version I grew up with was simply topped with polenta (a.k.a. Cornmeal mush).  I still prefer it because the texture is closer to actual tamales.
@Raydon yes! Thank you. With black olives.

Yes to adding black olives and doubling the cornbread—with buttermilk—!

My great Aunt who lived in NM used to make this when I was teeny-tiny. Her husband was a sheriff, she was the epitome of “a pistol” and she could COOK. 

I have some New Mexico green Chile powder from Spice House that I’ve been dying to try that may just put this over the top! Yum!

I used a 5.5 quart Dutch oven after seeing the notes and the cornbread recipe was perfect for it without doubling. Next time I’ll add corn! I used pickled jalapeños as well since I always have those on hand. I’ll definitely be making this again!

Proportion of chili to cornbread didn’t quite work for me. There wasn’t enough cornbread to fully cover the chili in my Dutch oven and the cornbread got a little dried out. I will try again but double the cornbread next time. Tasted great though!
Probably depends on how deep the chili is.
I have been making variations on this for years. We like to add peas, cubes of potato, or a shredded carrot (often all 3). Adds some flavor and nutrients.

I "discovered" pickled jalapenos in the jar during the pandemic when fresh ones weren't available.  Now I use them for most recipes that specify jalapenos.  For this recipe I put some in the filling, the corn bread and a bowlful at the table.  Yummy

Love pickled jalapeños! Another sub - canned chipotles in adobo sauce. After using one or two chipotles the first go, I puree the rest with their sauce and freeze. Cut off a chunk at a time to add to anything that calls for heat and smoky flavor.
@Jess: thank you for the brilliant idea to purée and freeze leftover chipotles!
@Jess Thanks for the brilliant way to keep chipotles for another day!

Made this weekend. The charred peppers are an important flavor; don't under char. I may use fire-roasted canned tomatoes instead of the regular whole tomatoes. Adding a 1/4 to 1/2 teaspoon of chipotle chili powder at the end also made a noticeable difference in the chili. Would consider adding some chipotle in adobo next time.
Cornbread just covered chili in dutch oven. Cheddar on top got golden brown and crisp around the edges. Definitely don't omit.

This was more effort than the tamale pie I normally make.. and it was worth it! We really enjoyed it and never missed the meat (I used black beans.) Next time, I might double the cornbread recipe, but this was also very nice with a thin crust of cornbread. I had some fresh avocado slices that we served with it. This was a really nice, warming dish, very well seasoned, and would be great for a crowd. I will make it again.

Terrific recipe. Important to work quickly once the dry cornmeal ingredients hit the wet. You've got less than a minute when the batter is easy to pour and spread over the chili. After that, the leavening action of the baking powder firms up the batter and makes it stiff.
Loved the meaty version as a child and this is even better!  Doubling the chili powder is key.  Thanks for a forward-version of an old throw-back.

Even after reading all the comments on what size dutch oven to use, I still had to make a second batch of cornbread to cover the chilli. Very disappointed in NYTimes, in never clarifying the correct size of dutch oven to use, even after years of publishing recipe. Especially when using the correct size is integral to making the dish!

I like to make vegetarian dishes and like chili so I decided to make this dish. I’m not sure what the point of chatting the vegetables is. I put all the veggies in a  smaller Dutch oven and cooked it on top of the stove. I had a package of corn bread mix. I following directions, put the mix on top of the chili and baked the dish for 20 minutes. It was delicious.

I used buttermilk instead of yogurt/sour cream and also doubled the amount of cornbread batter.  It covered the chili quite well in a large Le Creuset Dutch oven.  I also added fresh corn off the cob and it provided a lovely addition.  Would definitely make again.

Kate - I’ve tamale pie several times, and though I haven’t tried freezing it, I find the leftovers reheat well in the microwave for lunch.   For one person, however, I’d cut this recipe in half so you aren’t eating it for a week!
OR share with a neighbor. That's what I always do.
Why would I grate garlic cloves when I can just put them through a much simpler garlic press?
I substituted one can of the pinto beans for a package of beyond burger and it was amazingly delicious

We really enjoyed this recipe, we’ll definitely doing again. The recipe falls short of calling the size of the Dutch oven and after reading the comments I decided to use a 9inch one. It gave about 1 inch of cornbread on top, which I thought was a good ratio :)

This was absolutely delicious! We even loved the leftovers. I will make this again and again. I used a smaller, round casserole dish instead of my Dutch oven, based on comments from others. Well, it was too small, as some of the cornbread spilled over (thankfully, I had it on a sheet pan). Next time, I will use something in betwen the two sizes.

My husband made this "beloved retro casserole" for dinner this evening. He rarely reads the food section but did today and had to make this immediately! He always follows recipes exactly--and it was perfect. Still is--lot of leftovers! It does seem remotely like the things we made in home ec in middle school, but we never made anything remotely this spicy. Plus, we didn't Yowza! The jalapenos, poblano, and chili powder (we used hot) did their work. This will be on repeat for sure.

Per suggestions, I made half the amount of chili (two persons) and the full amount of cornbread and added corn and chopped olives. A delicious recipe! Regarding the comments about the amount of cornbread relative to the size of the pot, I used a Le Creuset dutch oven with a diameter of 7 inches across the top. The thickness of the cooked cornbread was about the same as the depth of the chili, so I could have used a bit less. That having been said, the cornbread was delicious, so no regrets.

This is so good and easy although it has several steps. I followed the instructions except I used 1.5 of the corn topping. The sayce or the entire chili can be made ahead. I made it in a casserole dish and it came out perfectly. Excellent leftovers to take to work for lunch also.

Okay, I made my second iteration of this tonight and it came out great! I added a cup of green lentils to it and canned corn, simmered for about 30 mins (so lentils will cook) then I made spoonbread by damn delicious to put on top. The lentils just gave it more weight and soaked up a lot of the wetness. The spoon bread is softer, sweeter, and extremely simple. Then I put shredded cheese on top! 😁

EZ saw your note which caught my eye - it sounds like you are talking about hominy corn ground into flour.   If so, what a GREAT idea!  I have always been a fan of the homonizing of corn, and of course grits.   (which, contrary to what is written on a bag of Bob's Red Mill polenta, is not "also known as grits".  Polenta is made from regular corn and grits from hominy corn)).   Would you please share where you obtained hominy corn flour?

"Grits" and "hominy grits" aren't the same thing, and hominy grits are almost impossible to find outside the southeast US (and not always easy there). If the package says "grits" and not "hominy grits" it won't have gone through the process that turns corn into hominy ("nixtamalization" if you want to research it).

I've never heard it called hominy flour, but masa harina would be the same thing, available in stores with a Latin American clientele.

@Janet H.
Kalustyan’s in NYC( foodsofnations.com) sells White Hominy Grits as well as Maseca and  the heirloom brand Masienda in a variety of colors

This is a new favorite recipe in my house. It's always amazing when I find something that all four of us will eat so I can now add this to the rotation.  Now I'm going to do that thing I'm not supposed to do and tell you how I tweaked it... I subbed in soy crumbles for one can of beans so it was kind of like my meatless chili.
I've made her Dinner in One tamale pie all ways - ground turkey, vegan crumbles, doubling bean - and found that the vegan crumbles were the surprising flavor win.

I plan to use for the cornmeal alkali-treated cornmeal which is more nutritious than ordinary corn meal.   I buy alkali treated corn kernels and grind them to produce corn flour. I think it tastes better.
Most folks would not go to this trouble and would use yellow cornmeal from the market. Most commercial cornmeal in North America is simply ground corn that has not been alkali treated. As long as one gets an abundance of B vitamins from other sources, this should not be a problem.

It sounds like you're talking about masa harina, where the grain has gone through nixtamalization. Soaking the corn in a lime-based solution unlocks Vitamin B and other nutrients. 

My local supermarket in the Boston suburbs (which has a growing Central American community) carries masa harina from Bob's Red Mill, King Arthur Flour, Goya and a couple of other manufacturers.
Most any big grocery store has Maseca or similar dried masa. No more work than using regular cornmeal.
Jiffy to the rescue for us lazy folk. Very good, used pinto beans and only pablano peppers.
Has anyone tried this with gluten free flour? Seems like it should work, especially given the very small amount of flour used.
@hsl yes. Can confirm GF flour works fine in cornbread.

Double, triple, or quadruple the chili base and freeze it!! That is the most annoying part of this recipe and once you’ve had it, you will want to have it again but, if you’re anything like me, won’t want to do all the roasting and blending and such. I leave half the seeds in one of the jalapeños for an extra kick. This is my new go-to vegetarian comfort food!
Amazing! The family loved it. I doubled the cornbread per the notes below and that was clutch. You have to bake it a bit longer to accommodate the double order of cornbread though.

In the various cooking jobs I have had I have had to cook vegetarian and vegan dishes. In tamale pie I had great success with TVP. as you do bossed the fresh vegetables and dried chilies good olive oil works great
Perfect and delicious recipe. Used one red bell pepper and two serranos because that’s what I had on hand. Delicious.
Made exactly as directed. Really really good.
Tamale pie never had corn bread topping.  It was made with masa harina, the same corn product used to make tamales  and tortillas.

Based on the comments here I poured the chili in a smaller Ditch oven. The cornmeal was way too little and semi sank in the middle. It’s in the oven now and I hope it’ll come out semi decent. But I highly recommend doubling or tripling the cornmeal.

This was delicious! I used a box of Jiffy cornbread mix since I had it in the cupboard already, just subbed for all the dry ingredients. Worked great! I would prob double the cornbread if you’re hoping for more of a thick wedge on top.
Delicious!! I am lazy so I made it with box cornbread mix (JIFFY brand) and used two small boxes, enough for twelve muffins, and it was perfect!

I have several notes about this recipe which I would rate as 5 stars rather than 4. First, the cook time is misleading. I cooked this meal with one other person as my sous chef, and it took at least two hours to do. Second, when I printed this recipe out, somehow the steps 3 and 4 got reversed. I don't know if this is a computer glitch, but it's imperative you blend before you put the mixture in the oven otherwise you would melt the blender. Finally, you can make this vegan easily.

Followed the recipe to the letter - 1 poblano, 2 jalapeños - and used a smaller Dutch oven. Cheese Cornbread came out crispy and perfect! One of the best dishes I’ve made, hands down. Highly recommend!!

I REALLY wish I had read the comments before getting to the last step- definitely double or triple the cornbread portion- it is thick and not at all spreadable, just sinks into the top of the chili and only covered about 1/3 of my dutch oven...

I didn’t bother with the first few steps. Diced 1/2 red onion, 1/2 yellow onion, green pepper, and the garlic. Sautéed in olive oil in a large Dutch oven, seasoned liberally. Smoked Paprika added depth. Used a can of crushed tomatoes, then doubled the cornbread. Big thumbs up from my kids!

This was delicious. I used homemade pints of Rotel (tomatoes/green chiles/onions) instead of the can of tomatoes and listened to the helpful reviews by making sure I used my smaller dutch oven. I used masa harina (fine grind) and that made all the difference for the topping. I do wish I would have doubled it though based upon what others had said. This is a great recipe to tailor to your tastes by adding additional spice and seasoning. I will definitely add this to the winter rotation.

This was delicious! Intended to make it for my family of four — including a 2 and 4 yo — but the chili wound up being a little too spicy for them. I gave them chicken tenders while dad and I had the tamale pie after they went to bed. Next time I might nix the jalapeños and double up on the poblanos to tame the spice level and then add hot sauce or pickled jalapeños on top to the adults’ servings after. It was hearty and tasty and a great vegetarian meal — saving this one!

I thought this was m pretty good once I increased the spiced level a bit. Regarding the corn bread topping: The first time I made it with double the recipe for the topping and that was way too much cornbread. The dish was about 60% cornbread. The second time I made it, I used the recipe's directions, and it was the perfect amount of cornbread. As someone said, it depends on the diameter of your casserole/ Dutch oven. I used a 10 inch wide Dutch oven.
Really good, but needs more cornbread. I'll double next time.

I veganized this by using Just Egg and vanilla soy yogurt for the egg and sour cream.  All other substitutions were straightforward.  The cornbread turned out great!  I was most worried about the egg substitute but it made light and fluffy cornbread.

I used a 4 quart Dutch oven. When I added the corn bread, it seemed like it wasn’t going to be enough, but it was. I might add a little cayenne pepper or several dashes of hot sauce the next time I make it.
One serving is over half the daily rec sodium intake for seniors; what are suggestions to reduce sodium but retain flavor for this one? Thanks.

Don't see my comment, so reposting; one serving has over half the daily limit for seniors. Is there a way to reduce the sodium but retain the flavor in this dish? It looks yummy but have to watch BP! Thanks!!!

@VickyATX. I stay on a low salt diet. My recommendations are: make your own pinto beans with no salt, reduce added salt and consider using Jane’s Crazy salt as a substitute. There used to be a salt free baking powder by Hain, but they quit making it, so I make my own out of potassium bicarbonate and cream of tartar. Finally, you can reduce cheese, but I wouldn’t leave it out of this recipe.

Used 2 boxes of Jiffy cornbread mix for topping. I also added some roasted salsa that I had left over. Made beans in the instant pot-I had a mixture of 3 different types which added some good flavor. It was a hit in our house-would make again.

(Blending with recipe for the same fromAmerica’s Test Kitchen): Double the tomatoes. Drain & set aside 2 cups of the liquid. Roast (at 475 for 30 min) all veggies after tossing in olive oil. Stir in cumin, oregano, chili powder  and roast for 10 more minutes. Puree with set aside tomato juice. considrr keeping the bean licker and double the polenta topping.  leave out honey from that mixture. Add a layer of Monterey Jack cheese to top of veggies (add polenta. (Bake at  425 for 20 min.

To solve for the too little cornbread issue, I cooked the chili in my 5.5 quart Dutch oven but then transferred it to a 3.5 quart cocette before topping with the cornbread. The cocette would have been too small to cook the chili without making a mess. It was perfect other than having to wash another pot and the pan that I put on the rack below in the oven to catch chili that boiled over (a minimal amount). I used jiffy mix for the dry cornbread ingredients. Delicious!
I feel that this recipe is perfect as-is. Roasting the vegetables first really makes the dish and substituting canned chipotle peppers or jalapenos would lose that flavor.

An outstanding vegetarian tamale pie! I included a handful of raisins for some sweetness,  as well as some green olives with pimento. A smaller cast iron potl is recommended, but it might need some more cornbread.
Use a 9 or 10" diameter pot and you will have enough cornbread for a 1/2 to 3/4" depth cornbread crust

I followed the recipe pretty exactly except for adding a cup of corn kernels to the chili. I’ve made vegetarian tamale pie before and found this recipe unnecessarily fussy. I will not broil onions and peppers again— sautéed would have been fine. I also thought it silly to make the chili in a “large “ Dutch oven— it didn’t even half fill our 2nd largest one. So that the top would brown I transferred it to a rectangular casserole ( didn’t fit all the chili but that was fine) .  Starting in a skillet could also work. Cornbread batter needed to be thinner to spread evenly, and could have been more generous (perhaps 1.5 times what the recipe says).

Instead of a Dutch oven, used our cast iron skillet (Lodge, 12-inch) and the chili fit perfectly. Doubled the corn bread mixture, as others helpfully suggested, and this fit nicely over the top. Do not omit the cheddar! This recipe was fun and delicious.

I was surprised how much liked this since chili isn’t really my thing. The topping quantity was just right; otherwise the cornmeal would overpower the chili. T didn’t spread well but once it was in the oven it spread out to cover the whole dish. The cheddar is absolutely necessary, in my opinion. It melds beautifully with the cornmeal topping.

Quite good -- I used poblanos, so needed to add some water to the sauce, otherwise, it would have been too thick. I added corn (left out one can of beans), and it was perfect. Like others, I doubled the amount of cornbread, and it came out just right.
Must double the cornbread recipe or it’ll sink into the beans. However! — these are some of the best chili beans I’ve ever made without meat.
I had to double the cornbread to make enough to cover the chili. And I would suggest greatly increasing the spices. As written, the recipe is much too bland.
Would Rotel work in this recipe?
yes yes yes!

I used a proportionate amount of Rotel in place of tomatoes, but also used one jalapeño and one-half a roasted and peeled Poblano in a half-sized recipe for the filling and full recipe of topping.  Worked very well  - also ate very well!
Has anyone made this ground beef-style as mentioned in the introduction? I would be interested to make it with beans AND beef!
Cornbread topping needs to be doubled, and needed dash of milk to reduce it spreading consistency.
Agree with more cornbread. Will also add more heat in the way of an additional jalepeno. Delicious, going into winter rotation.
Jess, thanks for that suggestion! I’m tired of throwing out half the can of chipotles - brilliant way to keep them longer term.

This was delicious and a great weeknight meal.  I did find it difficult to spread the cornbread on top of the chili, but even in areas where the cornbread was thicker, it cooked through.  Spice level as written is pretty mild so could increase easily for those who like it hot.
Masa harina is cornmeal made from kernels soaked in limewater, it gives that south-of-the border taste and I think heavenly scent.

I do not recommend doubling the cornbread topping as others have suggested. I did so because of said recommendations and didn’t like the result. I used a large doufeu Dutch oven and ended up with (good tasting) thick cornbread topping that totally masked the filling. And, it was difficult to cook through without burning the top.

Cornbread did not cover the whole top, but did form a distinct layer (w cheese) and was delicious. Would not double it personally bc I enjoy a higher bean:bread ratio. Ended up simmering the bean & tomato mixture for more like 20-25 minutes to get it to the consistency I desired.

I used a 3.5 quart dutch oven and it was just right. Some nights I want to take a little, but not a lot, of time to make dinner and I thought this recipe was relaxing to make - some chopping, some broiling, some stirring, and only a little waiting.  The scent of the spices was so warming and enticing. It was rich and delicious!  Thank you!

This was SO good!  Reading other comments, I just used an 8" casserole dish. The cornbread spilled over, but it didn't really matter because the end result was so good, we are having leftovers tomorrow night, and I HATE leftovers.

This recipe makes a quick, complete, and tasty weeknight meal. There were even leftovers to spare. I was worried the cornbread was too thick. However, I added it to the beans in several drops, and it spread well once it soaked up moisture from the beans. The cornbread to bean ratio was great once it was baked.

This was wonderful, worth making again and again. I took a few liberties. Since I had no peppers I used a 4 ounce can of chiles and one bottled/roasted red pepper. Two cans of beans I thought were enough. Not sure I saved any time but made it possible to come close to the recipe with standard items in the cupboard.

I made this last night. When I saw the amount of chili and the amount of cornbread mix, I knew I wanted a different ratio of beans to bread. So, I pulled out roughly half of the prepared chili beans and used the cornbread mix as written. Loved it. Even looked forward to enjoying my tamale pie for breakfast topped with a fried egg.

Followed the recipe exactly except totally agree with everyone that you need to double the cornbread recipe.  Any tips on how to put the cornbread mixture across top?  With double the amount it sank a bit in the middle.  Needed to improvise to spread it around.

I used a 5 qt Dutch oven, and there still was not enough cornbread. But the cornbread was very dry  - I'll use a different recipe for that next time. With Rancho Gordo black beans, the chile was delicious. Added some roasted corn in place of some of the beans.

I forgot to check the notes before making this, and as I spooned the entirely inadequate amount of cornbread into the pot I thought to myself, "This is why you read the notes first." haha

Happily, I had enough ingredients to make a quick second batch (minus the scallions). Double the cornbread, folks!

I made this last night and it was pretty good. I halved the chili part of the recipe but kept the cornbread the same. Once the cornbread dough was made it didn't cover the chili at all so I doubled it and added more BUT after it cooked it expanded so much - the chili to cornbread ratio was definitely off, but that's on me. The cornbread was also super dry - not sure if that's because I'm baking at altitude here in Colorado or if I baked too long (only 22 min) but it was way too dry for my taste.

Made this the other night, pretty much to specs, but the one non-purist change I made was to use a box of cornbread mix and it was perfect. It was Jiffy, which is available in the mid-Atlantic if not everywhere. I also used a combo of peppers: anaheim, poblano, red and jalapeno because I had them all on hand. And some smoked hot chili powder. Served with a dollop of plain Greek yogurt, more chopped scallions & jalapeno, and hot sauces. Will make again.
Waaaayyyyyyyy toooooooo much work for the payoff. Simplify or you’ll have a disaster zone to clean up before you sit down for a just-ok meal.

There are easier tamale pie recipes that I like better than this one. I made this and increased the cornbread by 50% which was just about right for us. We used a medium sized casserole dish rather than large. I thought the cornbread was a bit dry for my taste, but the rest of the recipe tasted decent.

Tasty! But it takes a *long* time to prepare and has so many steps and ingredients, I’m not sure I’ll make it again—or at least not on a weeknight. I’m an experienced home cook; this took easily 2 hours.

Good, but needed much more cornbread. I used my regular 2 cup recipe, and could have increased it. Cooked my own black beans but next time will probably use pinto beans. Needs more chili powder. I put a lot more scallions in the cornbread and more cheese on top. What it did not need was all that salt. I used only 1/4 tsp of the total called for, although of course I had salted the beans and there was salt in the cornbread part.

Just made this today and would not change a thing. I thought I'd want more cornbread on top but the layer was the perfect amount in our 10 inch dutch oven. I used Bob's medium grind cornmeal. I will definitely make this for guests.

Doubled the cornbread mixture, and it did cover all the chili. But the concept confuses me; the cornbread masa just sinks into the chili. Something is off with my ratios, because I also have like 6 inches of chili to half an inch of masa. Have to wait 25 minutes to see how it turns out, but I'm like a spiked up porcupine on edge.

I halved the chili part of the recipe but did use 2 cans of black beans. The broiling process made me nervous I was going to set off the smoke detector, so I might try roasting next time at 450. I used my heirloom orange le crueset Dutch oven because I thought my skillet was too small. I did not double the cornbread, but used Greek yogurt and a little half & half to make up for the thickness of the yogurt. I'm pleased with the way it turned out!

Agree with others that the prep is a bit time consuming, but the final result was delicious!  Even my deeply carnivorous fiancé proclaimed this “good”. I added some extra veggies that I needed to use up, so only did 2 cans of beans. I doubled the cornbread as everyone suggested, but that was maybe a smidge too much?  I cooked it in my 12” cast iron skillet and it was full to the brim. Interestingly, it tasted even better next day reheated for lunch. I also froze some, and that worked well too!

I made this last night in my 5.5 quart Le Creuset dutch oven and doubled the cornbread. The other thing I did differently is that I chopped the veggies finely (only 1 jalapeno) and sauteed them with the spices, then mixed them with the tomatoes and beans and simmered before topping with cornbread and baking. I just didn't feel like doing the whole roasted/blended thing. I mention this only because someone else might feel similarly and yes, it works and got rave reviews!

Fun. My college kid liked it, ate seconds. Lots of leftovers for lunches. Next time I will add frozen corn to the black bean chili. The cornbread recipe from Bob's Red Mill medium grind perfectly covered the chili. Make sure you thoroughly char the veggies for flavor

Amazing! And I don't usually say that about chilis. Followed the recipe except for charring the peppers and onion on the grill, using less salt, and omitting the honey. I only made half the cornbread and it made a nice mound in the center of a well-seasoned Lodge dutch oven. Nice mix of straightforward techniques here that I look forward to using again.

As you are broiling your peppers and onions, add some 3-4 garlic cloves and a 3-6 Colorado chilies to a pot of boiling water.  Boil for 10min.  De-seed the chilis.  Add those bad boys (along w some chitlipin peppers for some bonus heat) to the blender w everything else.

Have fun!

Made exactly as written! It was great! I read about others comments about the corn bread. If you mix just enough, the corn bread rose up about an inch! I couldn’t imagine doubling it like others said. It will be in our family’s rotation!

I did double the cornbread and made it in my 7 qt LeCreuset. It was great and easy to prep ahead. I might add more spice next time but not sad to eat these leftovers! It makes a lot. I did use 2.5 cans of black beans and some corn kernels.
Please don’t judge me - but one box of Trader Joe’s corn bread mix worked perfectly if time is short. Folded in scallions and cheddar just like the recipe. Hearty crowd pleaser!
love this recipe and very easy to flex additional ingredients to have some fun.  I've added chorizo and fresh corn....gone till the last lick

Was panicked to discover how little cornbread batter the recipe produced.  Only then read the recommendations to double it.  Fortunately, had the time and ingredients to do so.  Concluded after serving that doubling was too much — cornbread was too thick after rising when compared to the chili.  I think one-and-a-half times the recipe is probably closer to ideal.

To add more protein a squeezed block of firm or extra firm tofu chopped into small pieces (crumbles). The tofu meld with the beans and sauce and gives the feeling of ground meat. I do this with many recipes calling for ground meat. The tofu absorbs the flavor of the sauce.
Definitely a weekend dish. Lots of steps and clean up.

Here’s my completely different take, inspired by this recipe:
1  lb tomatillos 
2 large 1/2” cuts of onion (maybe 1/2 total onion)
2 poblanos
4-6 garlic cloves, skin on
1-2 jalepenos 

Broil all of the above, probably in separate batches.  Add to blender w 1 15oz diced tomatoes, and 1-2 cups fresh cilantro.

Cook salsa in the Dutch oven 3 min, add everything together.  Add boiled chicken, add beans of your choice (I also add boiled, diced potatoes).  

Serve w chopped salad, fresh pico, cotija.
There is no such thing as a “tamale.” The singular of “tamales” is “tamal.”

So I took the comments advice and doubled the cornbread portion. Ended up being probably 1/4 too much cornbread. The top was golden brown and delicious but overall the middle was not fully baked relative to the chili. I think the real jam is do 1 and a half cornbread instead of doubling. Delicious flavor though!

I mention the words Tamale Pie and everyone shouts out hooray! It's delish as written but I now double the cornbread topping recipe. It does make it a tad cornbread heavy but my family members just adore it. I also recommend using both the poblano and a bell pepper, just adds more depth of flavor balancing out the heat. We have a Celiac in the family so Bobs Red Mill gf med grind  cornmeal is my go to and I use any1:1 gf flour on hand. You can't go wrong with this recipe.

Definitely double the cornbread! I did all on the cooktop except for the cornbread part, that went into the oven. I followed the recipe otherwise and it was delicious. Additions can be made- corn, beef/steak, pickled jalapeños as a topping. If cooking for less than four HALF the recipe.
Easily double the cornbread measurements. In a 5qt Dutch oven, the recommended measurements covered about 2/3 of the chili
Great family weekend meal. Made recipe as written. A little extra work but worth it. Served with sliced avocado and sour cream.

This was great! The chili is very smokey - def. recommend broiling peppers and onions - it tastes delicious combined with the cornbread and sour cream.

I baked it in a large cast iron skillet after reading the reviews - recommended.

I used a boxed mix from Trader Joe's instead of making the corn bread from scratch. I used the entire box (with milk and an egg) and this was just the right amount - although probably a little too much if you want a smaller ratio of cornbread to chili.

I, too, halved the chili recipe but made the entire amount of cornbread.  We really liked it!  I also added some Trader Joe's soy chorizo to bulk it up a bit.  Half the recipe easily made four moderate servings.

I made this with Jiffy Cornbread mix (and used an extra half egg, honey, and sour cream to make a mini cast-iron of extra cornbread) and it turned out great! Shredded Mexican cheese blend on top.


I chopped up my peppers pre-char and it still turned out good. Used 2 cans of black beans and one of red kidney beans. 

I used cilantro paste and added extra cilantro into the sour cream topping for added freshness. 


Might add: scallions, black olive, corn in future, fure roasted tomatoes

I used half of  the chili for 4 people. If I were going to use all of it to feed more people, I would double the crust recipe. I also added some buttermilk to the crust since it was very thick and not so easy to spread over the chili. Very tasty!

My husband just said this was a great dinner. I didn't follow the recipe exactly. I had a lot of fresh tomatoes and I've been making great cornbread for most of my life. But otherwise, I followed the instructions. It was fabulous.

Hit! Made two versions for a family dinner - spicy meat version (included the jalapeños and used hot chili powder and added 1lb ground turkey) and the classic vegetarian (I added a can on if hominy beans for more texture). To both versions doubled the cornbread recipe and I added a 1/2 bag of frozen corn and used sharp cheddar. In the tamale pie I did 1/3 pinto 2/3 black beans and added diced up black olives.

I used a brazier for this recipe that is 3.75 qt. It feels like the ideal vessel here but even after doubling the cornmeal I still didn't have quite enough. 2.5 times would have probably been better. Kind of disappointing.
For the cornbread, I just use the Jiffy mix from the store.

I halved the bean mixture, added tomatillos, and kept the cornbread measurements the same. I used coconut yogurt in place of sourcream and no cheese to keep it dairy free and used gluten free flour. I cooked the cornbread separately because I wanted it to be light and dry enough to pour the bean mixture over the top, along with scallions and cilantro. It was really good!
@Sheila what did you replace the butter with? 1/4 cup oil?

This was a lot of work and I followed the recipe exactly. I used homemade Rancho Gordo beans. The end result was dry, overly salty, and not that interesting. And it made a ton so now I'm stuck with it. If I had it to do over, I would've just made a tray of nachos. Never again!

Another similar recipe asked for Spring form pan while using tortillas. I made the sauce with similar ingredients adding squash and olives.  In addition I found a store bought enchilada sauce to be helpful. Ser oven 400 ° for 20 minutes.  Wait 10 minutes to serve.

I added zucchini and roasted butternut squash to this, then doubled the recipe for the cornmeal topping. I found some really interesting cheddar cheese with some veins of blue and put that on top after it came out of the oven since my husband doesn't always want dairy. I loved this recipe and will definitely make it again when I have some folks over. I'll have leftovers for days!
The singular is tamal.

I loved this! It's everything I like in a meal. I'm not vegetarian, but I like to rotate in vegetarian meals at least once week. I used caned fire roasted tomatoes to complement the roasted peppers. So good!
How is it a tamale if it isn't using masa?

Followed others' advice and increased spices level and doubled topping. Could even be 2.5x topping. She has a heavy hand with salt, so for her recipes I always start with zero and adjust to my taste. Added 1 lb lean ground beef mainly because I wanted to say that for a vegetarian recipe. Sauteed rather than roasted veggies. Makes enough for a crew of lumberjacks - good b/c you're going to need a few to lift your loaded cast iron cookware out of the oven. 
Wagon trains ho! Git along lil dogies.
I used a typical large Lecrueset and cornbread didn’t come close to covering, would double cornbread next time

Wonderful recipe.  Just a note that the chili continued to deepen and improve in flavor quite a bit during the final bake in the oven.  Husband was on his way home from handbells and I stuck it in the oven thinking it'd be just okay, but it turned out extremely flavorful and perfectly thick in texture.

As noted in a couple other comments, some people might prefer reducing the chili proportion to 2/3 of what's in the recipe (two cans of beans, medium can tomatoes, etc).

The charred vegetable / tomato base makes this dish so deep and flavorful - well worth the extra step. I had a bunch of chard so I added the chopped stems with the onions and then stirred in the greens (off heat) before adding the cornbread topping for some extra veg. Everyone(!) went back for seconds.
GREAT! Use black beans. Char 2 pblanos on stove and one big onion in air fryer.
