Delicious but agree with others you can double the cornbread recipe and that is probably a better ratio especially for this many beans.
really like this but i definitely agree you should double the cornbread topping!
Love this and make it all the time!

okkkk, this was so delicious but i did change a few things: added a bag of sweet corn, only did 1 can of black beans (which i think fixed the chili cornbread ratio other reviewer talked about), i did a bunch of canned pickled jalapeños instead of roasting fresh ones just because i had them (but i did roast poblanos and onion, it’s a very necessary step), and then i did canned fire roasted tomatoes instead or regular. ALSO… i used jiffy corn muffin mix which i highly recommend because it was very easy and very delicious. i baked topped with very sharp cheddar and finished with a drizzle of hot honey. this dish reheats incredibly well i will definitely make it again

I took the advice of many of the comments and had great results. 

I subbed a box of Jiffy mix and added sour cream and honey as written. 
I did use a smaller Dutch oven and the cornbread was nice and thick and did not bleed into chili. Finally I baked a little longer and covered with foil for the last 10 minutes.

My favorite tamale pie recipe (that I've made for decades) is from James McNair's "Chicken" cookbook.  it's attributed as being Richard Nixon's favorite.  My Republican upbringing is long gone in the dust, but the recipe is definitely a keeper (although my husband eats around the olives).  Cornmeal, chicken, tomatoes, onions cooked together in a pan, then baked with cheese on top.  Delicious!
can you use boxed cornbread for this?
great flavor but I would increase the amount of the cornbread topping.

Delicious. Skipped the pepper-charring and just made chili with a red onion, home-pressure-cooked beans, lots of bell peppers, cumin, oregano, chili powder, and some chilis in adobo. Doubled the cornbread (big cornbread fan!) and it was perfect in my #28 dutch oven.
So good. I didn’t change anything. Definitely use a smaller size Dutch oven.

Tamal is the singular
Tamales is the plural.
There is no word tamale.

@Alexis

And yet, that’s what we call it. Tex Mex is great!

Instead of doubling the cornbread mix just use Melissa Clark's Brown Butter cornbread recipe to start with. Pour half of the batter into a skillet to brown in the oven then spoon a reduced (drier) chili mix onto the browned cornbread then top with the rest of the batter. Pop in the oven to finish. Really good.

Fine but time and effort for taste was disappointing. Doubled the cornbread as suggested by many and the ratio was way off. If I try it again I’ll skip that and maybe add corn to the chili. Although. Nah. Gonna skip it.

I've always made the classic, Joy-of-Cooking-inspired version of the topping, and it turns out I prefer it. This cornbread was richer, but it turns out we prefer the less-buttery version. In fairness, I didn't do it any favors when I put the topping on a casserole dish of leftover Superbowl-day turkey chili, straight from the fridge. The bottom of the cornbread didn't cook through. I should have heated the chili first.

Though the author says the recipe uses "fresh green chiles", it really doesn't. "Green chile" is a specific type of chile pepper grown in New Mexico, southern Colorado, and parts of Mexico. It is most similar to Anaheim chile, though it has more flavor. It doesn't taste anything like jalapeño or bell pepper, though...

This is definitely making into a regular rotation in my kitchen. I followed the recipe and at first thought the cornbread amount wasn't going to be enough but after it was done baking, seemed just right. I opted for all black beans and sour cream in the cornbread. Might try mixing the beans up a bit next time just for fun.

I only gave it three stars because we have several tweaks to make, My husband, (Mexican American) says this doesn’t taste like tamales enough to call it a tamale pie. But he said we should make it again and call it ‘bean casserole.’ We will do this! We added a morita pepper to the ‘tomato sauce’ to give it a smokey flavor.

Cooked this in a 12 inch cast iron skillet and everything fit well. Definitely liked the cheese on top. Substituted 1 tsp chipotle chili powder for 1 T of the regular chili powder. Would have liked more kick so will add more next time. Reheats well for leftovers.
I am so disappointed! At 425 for 25 the cheese topping burned! I didn’t notice the smell until it was too late. I still plan to enjoy this, just wish I had baked it covered.

This is just a big pot of chili with a thin crust of cornbread on top. This is not tamale pie, in my experience. I really knew that when I saw cornmeal instead of masa, but I wanted to try it.  I personally will only review a recipe if I make the actual recipe and not based on changes I made.
Pros: most def the charred veg! The cheese on top did add a nice extra sparkle to the flavor.
Cons: way too tomato forward for our tastes. I make a lot of vegetarian chili and this was not my favorite. But also, I didn’t want just  cornbread and chili!

Subbed 1# ground turkey for one of the cans of pinto beans, added to sautee at the onion/garlic step and this worked perfectly. 12 inch cast iron pan was the right size for cornbread mixture. The chili base with charred veggies has a great flavor on its own. This was a hit, will make again!!

Oh my goooodness, this is great! Smoky and delicious. Made this in a 4-qt. dutch oven and the cornbread topping was the perfect thickness. My few minor tweaks: fire-roasted tomatoes rather than plain, a third cup of canned corn added to the cornbread mixture, and a half-tsp. of smoked paprika because, yep, I add it to everything now.

Great recipe with a few tweaks. I sautéed 4 grated zucchinis in EVOO until they reduced in size by over half and were caramelized. Then I added half the onion to this mixture to sauté. I made the chili (mostly as written) besides that, adding a larger spice quantity and also some jarred green chilis I had on hand.

Per others suggestions, I doubled the cornbread and was happy with the ratio in my large Dutch oven. The cheese on top is a must!

This recipe is time-intensive (for my cooking skills) but made a lot of food and is super tasty! I would certainly make again.

Delicious. Made per recipe except added 2 small diced zucchini to the onions for a bit more juiciness and veggie variety. Cornbread seemed thin going on, barely covering the surface of a 5 qt dutch oven, but it rose substantially, and the cheese crust was so good.

It was truly a pie, very little liquid. Also, it is highly adaptable.
Recipe doesn't specify which kind of cornmeal. I assume yellow and not white?
As always, I'll use Bob's Red Mill medium grind cornmeal.  BTW, the cornbread recipe on the bag is outstanding. It calls for buttermilk, but I just clabber milk with lemon juice or vinegar.
The only difference between white and yellow cornmeal is the color of the corn it is made from. Use whatever you have.
This sounds good, but I'm cooking for one person. How does this freeze?
Maybe make it in muffin tins & freeze after cooking? (I cooked for 1 for a long time & found muffin tins to be my best kitchen tool: shepherds pie is especially nice!)
Too with Polenta
I doubled the topping on accident and it was the perfect amount!
I think I would double my regular chili recipe, eat chili when it's ready and use leftovers in tamale pie.
I moved the chili to a much smaller pan and still didn’t get the cornbread spread all the way across. I think I’ll stick with vegetarian chili with a side of cornbread.
Don’t add cheese to cornbread before baking.

I made this tonight. I used Bob' Red Mill medium grind cornmeal and had to add extra liquid (I used buttermilk because I had it on hand.)
I used dried red beans (1 1/2 cups) that I cooked in advance.
I also added 1 link of chorizo while I was sauteing the onions.
It took a lot of time but was very good

Really, really good. Cornbread topping didn’t cover the full circumference of my 5.5 qt Staub cocotte but there was still plenty of breadiness for my liking. I made one slight addition to the recipe and included 8oz of rough-chopped baby portobellos when sautéing the onions for the chili. It was a hit with our omnivore family of four.

The cornbread puzzled me. Whisking the wet ingredients together produced a solid mass, and when I tried to whisk that into the dry ingredients, it all stuck in the middle of the whisk. So I dug it out and mixed it with a wooden spoon. It was not possible to "spread" this over the bean mixture. I dropped lumps of it on top and tried to get them to mush together, which failed. Despite all that, the end product was good, but I just wonder whether the cornbread should have been wetter somehow.

I never use canned beans, soaking the dried and cooking them makes them taste so much better. Can anyone help me to convert 3 cans to the right amount of cooked from dried (minus the liquid in both cases, of course)?
I found the answer by googling. 6 cups of cooked, drained, dried beans.

I guess I should have read the comments before I made it. It's not even close to enough cornbread batter to cover the amount of filling. The batter has a thick sponge-like consistency and doesn't pour over the soft filling, it kind of just sinks into the chili. I had to scoop and drop bits of batter with a fork, like dumplings. First NY times recipe I've made and I have to say I'm kind of disappointed.
Definitely double the cornbread or you end up with a thin island of cornbread in the middle of your chili.
425 for 20 is a bit much. Took it down to 375 halfway through. Maybe it’s just my oven though!
I thought the temp and timing were accurate. Check your oven?

Made this tonight and followed the filling recipe exactly except I used only about half of the incredible sauce (which would make a killer enchilada sauce also) to the beans because it seemed it would be too wet with all of it. This turned out to be the perfect amount in my opinion. I topped it with a thick polenta instead of the corn bread, which is just my preference for tamale pie. The sauce really stole the show! Served with minced scallion, cilantro, and jalapeño mixedwith a squeeze of lime

I made the mistake of only buying one can of pinto beans so I added 1/2 cup of quinoa and 1 medium sweet potato. It turned out great, I just simmered everything for closer to 25 minutes. This will be in the winter rotation now, for sure.
This recipe is vey good w/impossible fake meat.  Just pan fry it and add it before baking
Double cornbread and use treated meal for vitamins!
What about using a cast iron skillet instead of a Dutch oven? Would that work?

You definitely want to trust the process for this recipe. I was worried the cornbread: filling ratio wasn’t going to be enough, but it was perfect. We dolloped the cornbread mixture on top to make a lid as evenly as possible, which expanded in the oven and even soaked up some of the chili liquid (it appears to yield a lot after simmering on the stove, but again, trust the process). Will definitely be adding this to my winter recipe repertoire.

I used a 3.75 QT Staub French Oven, and there was still not enough cornbread even then. I expected to be able to cover the entire surface of the chili, but there were many gaps. Next time I’d consider doubling the recipe, or maybe making cornbread on the side.

Next time I won't put the cheese on top of the cornbread mixture.  The cheese browned and slipped off the cornbread when we tried to cut it.  I would add the cheese on top of the bean mixture first and then top with the cornbread (I doubled the cornbread mix).  I would also add some fresh corn off the cob to the bean mix.

Very tasty.  Our cornbread stayed together as hoped, and it was delicious.  But it was only in several dollops, dumpling-like.  As Kate suggested, we should double the cornbread to cover the chili.  Next time I will add canned corn and black olives, which gave interesting texture to my mom's Tamale Pie in the 1950's.  Poblano peppers are pricy here now, but maybe I should have sprung for one in this case.

This was good..I did opt for a short cut and used the trader joes corn bread mix. It dominated and was too much plus this particular brand is very sweet...had never used before so that was a surprise.  Overall a good recipe, next time ill use the recipe provided for the corn bread and see what happens.

Wow so delicious. I accidentally overheated my oven so it was done in 18 minutes and very brown (but the recipe is forgiving!!). Whole family loved. I did not use cheese and I thought the ratio cornbread to chili was okay but my kids would have been happy with more cornbread. I used one jalapeno and one fresno and I thought the spice level was fine (cooking for kids). Will make again for sure!

Made it as written except added 1/2 tsp of chipotle chili powder and divided it into three small foil pans. Dolloped the cornbread on and spread it a thin layer. Perfect. Had one tonight and froze the other two. Definitely loved the cheddar on top. Would maybe add corn next time.

Acknowledged reviewers’ conclusions about the need for more tamale/polenta topping. Do not double that recipe component however - 1.5 x original amt is more than enough. 
Tasty recipe

Both my partner and I thought this was delicious.  I made the recipe as written but used Bob's Red Mill cornbread recipe for the cornbread.  I expected the cornbread to be cooked all the way through as the photograph for this recipe suggests, however my cornbread was runny underneath.  Still tasted great though.  Next time I plan to add corn and black olives to the chili.  I might cook the cornbread separately to avoid it being runny unless anyone has a suggestion as to what I did wrong.

I made the mistake of not reading the grocery list thoroughly and ended up buying coarse corn meal instead of  fine cornmeal. Coarse cornmeal needs to soak roughly about 45 mins., which I completely forgot to do. So the end result was a crunchy top cornbread over the chili, but it was still very good overall. (I doubled the cornbread recipe as many suggested). Since we didn't have poblano peppers and chili powder , I charred jalapenos, and Korean hot green and red peppers. I was very good!

This was quite tasty.  The grocery was out of jalapenos so I used Serrano peppers.  They worked great.  Like other reviewers, I noticed that the cornbread did not cover the top of the chili, so I put dollops of dough on top and that worked fine.
Used an Anaheim pepper with the jalapeños. used 1.5 times cornbread and mixed Mexican-blend shredded cheese.
Made as written and the whole family enjoyed it. Kinda wish I had doubled the cornbread topping, tbh.

Great recipe! I doubled the batch. I used green bell peppers and canned jalapenos, as that is what I had on-hand. I used an extra green bell pepper. It was great! 

Agree with other commenters about doubling the cornbread portion of the recipe, relative to the chili part of the recipe.

Should have read comments before making it. Would’ve doubled cornbread. When I first made chili it was a little too thick so added a little water and maybe that’s why the cornbread fell into the chili but didn’t want chili to be super thick either.
Added 3 chipotle chiles in adobo.  Will double cornbread next time.
I would not double the cornbread. I did as others recommended - and it was way too much cornbread.

Made this according to the recipe except I didn't have a dutch oven. So I baked it in an 8x8 Pyrex. The family consensus was chili and cornbread go well together, but should be kept separate. The 23 yo suggested, "It's like shepherd's pie and chili had a baby, but it was an ugly baby & nobody would play with it on the playground."
I neglected to read the notes first, so will double the cornbread next time and add a can of corn which I forgot to include.

Instead of cornbread, I made 'cheese grits'  5 cups water to 1 cup of grits, cooked slowly, plus about 2 cups shredded mozzarella, topped with cheddar cheese.
Filling:  As listed but with adding soy crumbles and roasted mushrooms, plus some soy sauce.
This was ok, not great. Very dense. More like a heavy side dish. Will not make again.

This was delicious! And yes this is going to be one of those reviews where I tracked a few things. Ton increase veggies I subbed one large zucchini small dice on cae of beans, skipped the jalapeños because the 2 tbs of chili powder was all we could handle, and used a cornbread mix to save time/effort, and it was incredible!!! my son and I cooked and ate it while dad was out of town (he can’t really tolerate any spice at all), now I find myself wanting him to go away again so I eat this more
Like others, I found that this dish needed maybe double the cornbread.  It ended up in lumps half-sunk into the chili.  Also, can't say that we loved it. Oh well.  Next!
Used chickpeas, cornbread absorbed liquid (I used Jiffy).  Cook with lid on so less liquid evaporates

This recipe didn’t work for me. The salt was way too much and the spice balance was off. As others have mentioned, the cornbread was super weird. Thick and way too little to cover the chili. Also there was no way to spread that thick batter over the chili. I ended up dotting it over the chili with my hands, and then trying to even it out a little with the back of a spoon.

Yum!!!  I thought it made a lot, even when I halved the recipe.  Added a little fresh corn into the cornbread batter- like I always do when making cornbread.  This recipe was a hit with everyone.  Thank you!
Add jackfruit!!! Saute with the onions/spices step...marvelous protein and texture addition, also makes it go further. ; P

Make double cornbread 
Add green chillies
Double the cornbread, otherwise quite good. It's a great weeknight meal if you make the pepper sauce the night before.

Took the advice of others and doubled the cornbread recipe and the amount was perfect for my likes. The cornbread was a bit denser than I expected (think of it more like drop biscuit consistency) and did not spread well over the chili. I would probably thin it with milk and/or butter next time to make it spreadable. Also please note that this makes a LOT. I started off using a 12x2 cast iron and the chili alone barely fit, so I switched to a 12x4 stainless sauté pan which worked. Tasted great!
I made it in a 5.5 qt. Dutch oven as suggested, but there was not enough cornbread.
This was wonderful. I used pinto beans and medium cornmeal.

Really tasty! I doubled the cornbread for my 13 qt Dutch oven. Also added pickled jalapeños to the cornbread instead of scallions. I think in the future I’ll make the cornbread with half cornmeal half flour and brown sugar instead of honey. Curious how that would bake as it would likely be more of a batter than a dough.
Just 1/2 cup cheese

Charred onions - I made them ahead, then I had to make more, because they got snacked.
Charred Hatch peppers - done in Hatch season, then frozen.
'Soy crumble' I had in the pantry, they thicken the bean juice.  Roasted mushrooms also.
I wanted 'tamale' not corn bread, so I made 0.5 cup corn meal simmered in 2.5 cups water, added a cup of shredded mozzarella.  It made a thick, solid topping.

This recipe has a lot of basic flaws. The peppers take far longer to char. The tomato flavor overwhelms everything—I would use half as much tomato or less. The spices are so mild that it tasted like spaghetti sauce—I tripled the chili, cumin and oregano and also added about a tablespoon of chipotles en adobo. The mixture is so soupy that you can’t spread the corn mix on top, you have to daub it. And there isn’t nearly enough cornbread to cover— double the amount. Weird fail.

Very tasty.  Used a can of crushed tomatoes as you puree anyway.  Cornbread stayed on top but was thin--okay from my perspective and I used pepper jack cheese on top. Used a combination of dried black beans and pinto beans and cooked the beans instead of using canned beans. The charred peppers definitely added something to the sauce.

I've made a lot of tamale pies over the years, as did my mother, and IMHO (and my wife's) this is as good as any I've had. I did add a small can of sliced black olives and some frozen corn, as suggested by others, and they did kick it up a tad, as did the dollop of sour cream on each serving. Worth the effort, and unlike many recipes, I found the estimated prep time to be pretty realistic.

Easy and delicious! However, this recipe should be updated to double the cornbread batter, the mixture is very thick and should also include milk or buttermilk. I used a 5.5 quart le crueset dutch oven and the batter covered half and I was being frugal with my spoon sizing.

I happen to like hominy and almost always include it in my chili regardless of the recipe and this was no exception. So I added two 15 oz cans of hominy with a few additional tweaks to accommodate my personal taste - more jalapeno, more cumin, 2x cornbread, added the diced onions and cilantro at the end which keeps them more noticeable vs cooking down and getting lost - all just personal tweaks to accommodate my personal taste preferences. Otherwise, a solid recipe from which to riff. Enjoy.

Made this a few days ago and it was really bland. The bean chili was under seasoned and the cornbread topping was underwhelming. Pretty disappointing because I thought the roasted jalapeños and poblano in the sauce would’ve added great smokiness and spice. I doubt I’ll make it again but if I did I’d want to increase the quantity of peppers & onions and add more cumin, oregano, and some cayenne. To the cornbread topping I would add corn and probably jalapeños as well.

Wonderful! I dbld the crust as someone suggested. Used yogurt instead of Sour Cream. Green pepper instead of the poblano. Dbld the garlic. Cooked in a cast iron skillet from Saute to the bake. I wasn't in the mood to grate cheese so placed thin slices of cheddar on top. Placed skillet on a Pza pan in case of run off. Brilliant recipe. Gone in one day! 4 adults! Gerry in Oregon

I made this for a Super Bowl party yesterday and it was a hit! The spicy mix and texture of the chili is complemented really well by the sweet, cheesy cornbread. As others have stated, I also need to work on finding the right sized pot to make the dish in. The cornbread mix doesn't yield much.

This was an epic fail. Husband and I ate it but both agreed we didn’t want to eat it again. Went in the garbage. Can’t recall ever having done that. Followed the recipe to a T. Realized after making the cornbread that it wouldn’t cover the chili so I made another batch. So thick difficult to spread. I had a tube of TJs polenta and sliced some of that to fill in the empty spots. Probably would have been better to just use the polenta.
This reminds me of a Moosewood recipe that used prepared polenta…
Agreed, the cornbread ratio was off. I froze half the chili, used a square Corning dish to assemble the pie with the remaining chili, and the balance was perfectly delicious.

The cornbread topping tasted chemical-y. I used aluminum-free baking powder, but I wonder if such a small portion of cornbread needs so much baking powder? I might try again with less baking powder, or use another cornbread recipe. 

Other than that, this was enjoyable. I reserved half the chili, and baked the pie in a deep dish pie plate. The proportion of cornbread to chili was good and we have leftovers to make another, with more cornbread.
I’ve made versions of this for years. Really like this one as it has some heat!
This is the "meatiest" veggie chili I've ever made. I doubled the cornbread. Delicious. It was really great reheated the next day.  A keeper.

Double cornbread mixture for blue Dutch oven
Maybe in oven for 15 minutes instead of 20. Check for doneness at 15

Not sure why there is so little cornbread. I transferred the chili to a way smaller dutch over because there was so little cornbread and it still was just separate little dollops as opposed to the full layer in the photo. Proportions were WAY off. If you make this, please double the cornbread recipe.

Made this tonight for dinner and loved it! I accidentally only got 2 cans of black beans instead of 3, so I subbed in about the same amount’s worth salsa verde chicken I had in the freezer. If I had it to do over again, I wouldn’t put shredded cheese on top - the cornbread topping was delicious! I did double the topping based on some reviews and I’m really glad I did. I’ll definitely make this again!
This was disappointing. I liked the concept. But by the time the cornbread is done, the chili dries out. I’d rather just have chili and cornbread with consistencies I can choose.

I did not follow the recipe and cut in half the tomatoes.  Wrong decision.  Also, I used a cup of dried beans, which turned out not to be enough.  I did some corrective work though by roasting some more green bell pepper and another jalapeño and adding these to another can of tomatoes, blended these together, and added them to a can of beans, pulled off the remaining cornbread topping and replaced it over the now delicious and adequate amount of beans and tomatoes.  The result was now excellent.
Don’t worry if it’s liquidy - it will cook out. Cook it on the stove longer!

I halved the chili but made the cornbread as is - this was a good ratio for a 9” Dutch oven IMO. I had just enough cornbread for a thin layer but it spread out and rose nicely when it cooked. I found the chili too tomato heavy but might make again with some tweaks. It made enough to serve 4-5.
Very delicious recipe!  I did double the cornbread though so it had a nice thick crust.  Absolutely making this again!

Loved the idea. It’s a versatile recipe. I used what I had—dried pinto beans that let me make two batches. The second batch was for a friend. I had maseca flour, so used that to make a top layer. I am sure cornbread would be nice too. Thanks!

Next time I make this, I’ll make the chili in a deep cast iron pan instead of a Dutch oven and double the cornbread recipe so that it covers the wider top. I doubled the cornbread recipe for the Dutch oven and it was way to thick a layer and absorbed all the liquid from my chili. I also added corn.

I have a 5 or 5.5qt Dutch oven and I was worried about the cornbread topping too, but it turned out just right. I did use Icelandic yogurt because it was all I had, which is a lot thicker than some other yogurts- I think it made the batter a little drier, which is this case was perfect!

I was  honestly surprised how tasty this recipe was and how much we enjoyed it! I used a whole onion because half didn't seem enough when roasted (and I like onions). I thought the charred vegetables gave it a really good flavor. As others mentioned, I would double the cornbread next time. It was more like corn bread islands on top of the bean mixture. I used a 6 Qt Dutch oven.
Double the cornbread batter - you won’t be sorry.
Doubling the cornbread is a must. Otherwise v good
So good, but my cornbread was a little crumbly? It was still good but I thought it would be more "bread" like. Overall amazing, and so glad I cooked this as my first NYT Cooking recipe!
Doubled the cornbread and made this in a large cast iron skillet. Perfect!

This was a lot of work for an underwhelming dinner. I followed the recipe to a T, and the chili was just okay and the cornbread topping was kind of dry. I had doubled the cornbread per others' suggestions, and the batter was really thick - I had to plop it on like dumplings. Not going to make this one again.

Made it for the first time for the family tonight.  Overall, it turned out tasty, the chili was flavorful and the cornbread was moist.  The only downside was that the cornbread only covered about half the chili.  If you have a large dutch oven, I’d highly recommend doubling the cornbread amounts - next time, that’s what I’ll do.

Tasty and fun to make! 
+1 to doubling the cornbread like others noted
Definitely double up on the cornbread.
Great recipe!  I doubled the cornbread - highly recommend.

Added carrots and corn to the filling
Double the corn bread and topped with cheese 

Next time would also add corn to the corn bread, and if we wanted cheese with mix it into the cornbread

This isn’t really tamale pie, which is made with masa harina. It’s chili and corn bread, very well accomplished. I like the ratio of chili to corn bread and halving the recipe was easy. Like others, we used our cast iron skillet, and let the chili sit for about an hour after an hour of simmer. Made corn bread with TJ’s GF flour with excellent result.
Instead of cornbread,  I layered corn tortillas on top of the chili, then covered them with pepper jack.  Used a 12 iron skillet instead of a Dutch oven.  Excellent!

Very long prep time. I felt like I was flying and start to finish was 1 3/4 hours. It is tasty, but I am going to pare back on chilli powder in future recipes, and I enjoy spicy seasonings. I find the NYT recipes are too spicy when calling for chilli powder. I loved how the cornbread turned out. Rave reviews. I doubled the cornbread recipe. It was just the right amount.  Took me 3 times as long as called for to broil the jalapeño and green pepper and onions. I wish the NYT had prep time listed.

Definitely double the cornbread, and mix the cheese into the batter rather than topping the crust with it. This is excellent baked in a 9x13 pan, where the double crust provides the perfect balance of cornmeal and filling. Very nice recipe; the thickened sauce gives the dish a heartiness so that it’s not just beans and onions in a thin broth. The charred veggies add depth.

I made this last night but added 1.5 ground Turkey after the spices and half a can of black olives and zero beans. I cooked it in my 10” le creuset cast iron pan. Agree the crust needs doubled. This is a very thick recipe and it does not spread. Dough is more like a cobbler, but it spread together as it cooked over half the top and formed an even crust. Used a bare sprinkle of 3 Mexican cheeses on it. Had no poblanos sadly but used 3 jalopenos. Yummy!
I did not the jalapenos so did without..very good anyway!
I found there was not enough cornbread to cover the chili so I quickly made a second batch. Next time I will simply double the amount up front!
Definitely double the cornbread recipe

The chili came out really dry. I’m worried about that after I had it all assembled, but I double checked and I had followed the recipe. If I make this again, I’m going to find a way to make the chili a bit juicier. The cornbread mix looked so dry that I added about half a cup of buttermilk, and that worked well.
Cornbread is maybe enough!
Fabulous.  I left all peppers out for my kids and it was a big hit. Will definitely make again.

The flavor on this dish was great. Roasting the peppers and onions improved the flavor profile. I used unsalted beans, but found the dish a little salty for my liking. I didn't have enough of the cornbread mix to cover the entire dish, so I made a little island of cornbread in the middle and surrounded it in a golden sea of cheddar. The recipe says to use a large dutch oven, which I did for cooking the chili. I transferred it to a smaller cast-iron skillet for baking for more coverage.
The cornbread was difficult to spread and was waaaay too little. It was more like drop biscuits or dumplings.

We made this last night and it was delicious. Followed the instructions as written, except my husband poured in about a quarter cup of Crystal, and I topped the cornbread with grated habanero cheddar. Between the two, this dinner was probably a lot hotter than some people might like it. But we loved it.

I made a few alterations from Melissa Clark’s recipe. For the chili, I roasted the garlic with the other vegetables and puréed it with the tomatoes. For the cornbread, I added milk to smooth out the consistency and mixed the Cheddar into the batter. I decided not to add the scallions, and chopped them up as a garnish.
Do you cook the dish with the cornbread on it at 425 degrees? That was the temp for broiling the vegetables.

I made this in a cast iron skillet - didn’t need to double the cornbread, it fit perfectly (it spreads when in oven). Also made in a toaster oven and needed more like 15-18 min, not 20-25, to get the top nicely brown.
We just made the bean portion, skipped the cornbread topping.  We really really enjoyed the beans!  Fresh and bright tasting
Delicious and simple to make.
Used my 6 QT dutch... to large for the cornmeal to cover the overall top. I recommend smaller 5QT dutch. Tasty recipe tho.
Has anyone omitted the 2 tbsp all-purpose flour, or tried a different type of flour? (Gluten free)
Britt M re your question about subbing GF flour for the 2 TBSP regular all purpose flour, it works fine to do that.  We did when cooking for GF eater.
@Britt M I used almond flour and it worked fine.

This did take 90 minutes to make, but it wasn’t hard. A good recipe to make for when people are gathered in the living room watching a game and I’d rather be nearby cooking something. I tried doubling the cornbread after having trouble spreading the first batch over the top of the chili but found it was way too much topping after it baked. The roasted peppers were good. I’m putting this in my “maybe” pile.

Yummy!  Made this for friends last night, followed the recipe exactly, it was a huge hit.  Everyone loved it and described it as a good comfort food.  Served with scallions, sour cream and sliced avocados, a very satisfying dish.  Will be making this again as is, no need to change the recipe at all.  Delicious!

this was a great recipe,  I sauteed some mushrooms an extra poblano and some chayote with
the onion.  so good.

Great dish.  Didn’t want to go to store and the clock was ticking,  so I subbed a smoked hatch chili w tomatoes 12 oz can for poblano.  Worked great!  Also added some Beyond Meat crumbles for texture.

This makes a tasty meal loved by all members of the family. I add a cup or two of Trader Joe's frozen roasted corn to lighten it a bit, both in color and taste.  I also cook the chili part a couple of days ahead since it does take some time to make and that also enhances the flavors.  I put together the corn bread batter as the chili re-heats and follow the recipe from there.  The corn bread is more like dumplings on top rather than a pie crust and that works just fine.
I add carrots, mushrooms and broccoli

I will definitely keep this as my go to veg chili. The puree base is flavorful and spicy (left seeds in 2.5 jalapeños). Could not find corn meal but had a box of stonewall kitchen cornbread mix that’s just cornmeal, flour and leavening so i used about a cup of that in place of the dry stuff. Turned out great. The topping sunk into the chili for me somewhat and turned tomato color so didn’t look like photo. But proportions were fine with a 5q dutch oven. Size of pot matters.
Double the cornbread recipe next time. Cilantro is a good addition. Cheese, obviously.

I thought this recipe made too much work with the broiling and blending etc. It was a veg Chili with cornbread on top. And not enough cornbread. It dried out. Next time I will do just that - make veg Chili and top it with a nice thick layer of cornbread. Less mess.
This is so, so good. I did a 1.5x version of the cornbread and it was perfect in my 5.5 quart Dutch oven.

Just made this for my book group. A total success.  The only change I made was to add a couple of carrots, diced, and a third jalapeño.  I made it in a large cast iron skillet and served it with a corn salad. 

I made a double batch of cornbread but didn't use it all.  The math is way easier to double a recipe than to make 1.5 times as much and I cooked the leftover amount separately so now I have a cornbread biscuit for breakfast tomorrow.  

Great recipe!
I doubled the cornbread recipe, added a couple of ears worth of shucked corn to the chili, and was generous with the cheese topping.  Delicious!

So so so delicious!

1.  I used two boxes of jiffy cornbread muffin mix and followed the directions on the box, which calls for 1/3 cup of milk and one egg per box.  The box also says to set the oven for 400 so I did that instead of the 425
2. The recipe calls for three 15 oz cans of beans, however I only had two on hand so that’s what I used. It was not watery like I suspected.
3. I would recommend making the purée in advance, as it was the most time consuming step.
Def double the cornbread. Also, much better cornbread recipes out there. Add corn to beans too for texture. Also added ground turkey instead of third can of beans.

Made this last night with the guidance of several helpful comments on this recipe. I ended up baking mine in a 15x11 casserole dish and doubled the cornbread topping. While the topping is awkward to add, I think letting the chili sit in the casserole pan in the fridge to chill for about an hour helped. It didn't melt into the chili, but instead sat on the top and was spreadable with a spatula. Certainly a very tasty and filling vegetarian meal!
Good for a crowd since it makes a lot of food. I liked the flavor and found it to be comforting and hearty. Not sure it would make it into my heavy rotation though.
Make in a 9inch pot (12 was too big)
Delicious chili, but cornbread not so good. I doubled it based on others' notes; not sure I would again.

This came out delicious! I did double the Cornbread recipe, though I did find it to be quite dry. Next time I'm going to top with Cornbread casserole (soft, crunchy on top and with deliciously gooey cheese). I also added lentils to the chili to make it a little heartier! The blending of the roasted veggies is now going to be part of my chili making process!

I agree the size of the dutch oven matters concerning the cornbread.
"Large" is too ambiguous.  My dutch oven was way too large and I doubled the cornbread recipe.
This was really delicious and totally agree with comments about needing more cornbread to cover. I’m using my grandmother’s aluminum Dutch oven and it’s big. Definitely will halve recipe next time.
Definitely adding this to the regular rotation and plan on saving time by freezing batches of the puree. Delicious!
first time I made this I ignored the tasting of onions and peppers. Second time I followed the recipe loser and it was fantastic! Sour cream is a must.

I used a dutch oven for preparing the base then poured it into a 9x13 baking pan because I was meal prepping for the week and wanted something I could stick in the refrigerator. I doubled the cornbread and it still wasn't enough to fully cover the dish! I would riff on this and find a different recipe next time with proportions that are more balanced - as made, this is extremely heavy on the beans.

I made this last night and we loved it. I read comments about doubling the cornbread recipe, but made the quantity specified in the recipe. I only have one Dutch oven, which is 11 in. wide. The cornbread didn't quite cover the top of the chili (there were some openings around the edge), but it turned out fine. I don't think I would have wanted any more cornbread topping, it baked up sufficiently thick. I added a cup of corn to the cornbread, and let chili cool 5 mins before covering.

Is so delicious - and fun! When I reheat the leftovers and the cornbread gets really crisp and almost blackened it’s even better - so just a note about how long we leave it in for. Maybe longer than I was originally prepared to do.

Also cooked in a 12 inch cast iron, which worked well. I doubled the corn bread, but would go with the recipe next time—ratio turned out to almost 50/50 bean layer to corn bread, and I prefer more bean.

Very delicious! Everyone in our family- from toddler to grownup- loved it. Like others, I doubled the cornbread recipe. We don’t have a Dutch oven, so I baked it in a 9x13 glass dish. No one was sad about the amount of cornbread!

Agree with others who say there’s not enough cornbread topping; I doubled it because I was using a rectangular baking dish, and that was about right.  Also I more or less skipped the first two steps to make it very easy and quick.

I wish I had paid more attention to comments about the cornbread topping. There REALLY isn’t enough. I had to dollop it kind of like dumplings. It was still tasty, though. I added 2 carrots and a bell pepper diced with the onion and garlic, in addition to the roasted poblano and jalapeño. The purée with tomato makes a really nice base that I will probably use in other chili recipes, too!
Has anyone tried freezing this? Any thoughts or suggestions on that?

Novice chef here. 1st recipe I am trying. I'm at the grocery store. Right before check out, I read that a Dutch oven is needed. They're like $100-$300! Unfortunately, I don't have that kind of money. Reading that a cast iron skillet might work, which I don't have. Lesson: Read the entire thing first!
Any large enough casserole dish will work.

I used a box of vegetarian jiffy mix and substituted jalapeños for scallions. Came out really good. Also added some soy chirizo crumbles to the onions and garlic mixture before I added black beans and tomato puree m
Can you make the chili one day, refrigerate, and then bake with the corn bread the next day?
Yes. You can also use canned chili or better yet, deli chili from Whole Foods or any store that carries their own homemade style chili.

Ended up doubling the cornbread topping since I baked it in a casserole dish. Did a mix of dark red chili, poblano and chipotle pepper powder for the chili powder and it was delicious! The leftovers were also super good!

I really wish I read the comments first. The bean portion of this turned out fine. I kinda want to add more veggies, like corn kernels, to it. Use a different cornbread recipe — and more of it. This cornbread barely covers the beans. Maybe NYT can tell us what size dutch oven they’re using for this?
Older family recipes use simple cornmeal mush instead of the cornbread topping, with or without added cheese.
I couldn’t find any cornmeal so had to use grits instead and it was still absolutely delicious! Will definitely make again!
Used an 11-inch cast iron skillet. Chili fit but had to double cornbread to get even a thin layer on top.

Has any one made this ahead?  Wondering if there is any reason why the whole dish sans the cornbread couldn't be a made a day or two ahead then thrown back in the pan and topped with cornbread for the bake.
Can this be frozen -- before or after baking?

Sometimes you should just ignore the comments .... I halved the chili since there are just two of us here (used 2 cans of beans, one black / one pinto - this worked well). But, I kept the full cornbread recipe based upon the claims of others that there's not enough. I baked it up in a deep dish pie plate. It was too much cornbread - it really puffs up and fills out. That said, flavors are fantastic and we'll make this again, although next time I'll halve everything.

I made this recipe last night as written, except I added a red bell pepper to the red onion in the filling. It was delicious and will make it again, but will double the corn bread recipe, since it only covered half the top of a large dutch oven
Made in a cast iron and doubled the cornmeal recipe, it was delicious!

I used my 7qt Dutch oven and doubled the corn bread recipe as suggested by other reviewers. For me it was too much of a bread topping. I felt like a portion, when scooped out, was 3/4 cornbread and 1/4 chili. If I did it again, I’ll stick with a single portion.

This was great. I used some leftover mung beans, rice and other veg in the base but followed the seasoning in it as per recipe.  I used a cast iron skillet which worked perfectly. I didn't think the cornbread dough would be enough but was able to spread it over most of the base (except may 1/2 an inch on the edges) and the thickness/ratio of cornbread to filling was just right as it puffed up quite a bit.

The filling is tasty enough that I'm going to try this again to try to get the timing on the cornbread topping right--I doubled the topping recipe and baked the minimum time and it came out too crispy.

I appreciate all the comments!  Reading them ahead of time, and not having the right size dutch oven, I used a skillet for the chili mixture, then transferred it to a large, oval corningware dish, where I topped it with the cornbread.  I also did the double-batch of cornbread, which was just the right amount.  My chili mixture seemed a bit on the soupy side, so I used a little corn starch to thicken it.  It turned out very nicely, but I think I would cut the salt back a little next time.
This is so cozy and delicious! Made exactly as written and just added a tiny bit of cheese. Absolutely perfect for a cold winter night.

Doubled the cornbread as others mentioned. Used half course cornmeal because I ran out of fine and it was fine. Had to more than double bake time though. Next time will try corningware instead of a deep Dutch oven.

This could benefit from extra spices, less whole tomato and the addition of tomato sauce or broth. It’s so tomato-forward that it’s hard to subdue the intensity. Doubled the cornbread recipe, based on other recs. Instead of topping and baking in the oven, decided on cornmeal dumplings. Plop spoonfuls into the soup, cover and cook on low-medium heat for about 15 minutes.
By default, this was incredibly under-seasoned. Took significant tweaks to bump the flavor. I definitely will skip making this particular recipe again.
Delicious and good looking, but cornbread not enough to cover, even thinly, a 10 inch diamter dutch oven! more batter/smaller diameter?
My picky eater kids (5 & 2.5) loved this recipe. I ended up just sauteeing the chili ingredients without roasting anything to cut down on time and pans.

very tasty but not a quick recipe to make.  The proportions for topping weren't sufficient to cover the pan so I would make it at least 1 c cornmeal and add some to other ingredients in topping.  But it tastes very good and feels like a healthy, hearty meal.
Tasty and very filling. Next time I’ll bake it in a casserole dish so it’s easier to store the leftovers. Trying to get it out of a Dutch oven without ruining the cornbread topping wasn’t easy.

I Halved the recipe, used a whole egg anyway in cornbread. In hindsight I would not halve the cornbread since half wasn’t really enough to top in my 2 qt Dutch oven. Loved the charred roasted veggies blended in the chili. Great flavor. Next time I might throw some green olives in the chili too. Served with simple calabacitas. So good!
Double cornbread recipe and I froze half the chili filling. Also added vegan meat and diced green chilies.
You forgot sliced olives. Always in a tamale pie
Super tasty. Doubled the cornbread recipe as others suggested. Perfect amount. Used Serrano peppers and and a couple of chipotle peppers in adobe sauce in the sauce.
My family loves this dish so much!  Don't skip the cheese!

this was really good but the cornbread soaked up a lot of liquid from the underlying chile so the result was more like layers:...nice crisp cheesy crust,soggy but delicious cornbread, beans and vegetables. any thoughts?
Tasty recipe, although I agree with the comments regarding the cornbread topping being too sparse--I'd double the amount next time.

This is delish! 
We made it with buckeye beans (cooked from our Rancho Gordo collection-about 4 cups cooked,) fresh tomatoes, coarse cornmeal, Greek yogurt instead of sour cream, and went light on the cheese. Added spices as written. 
Not using canned ingredients did add to the total prep time, but we had cooked the beans in advance, and as the recipe states the vegetable portion could be prepared ahead as well.

Flavor was Ok — nothing special — especially for the time involved in the process. Needed to make a second batch of cornbread, even though this was baked in an 8 x 8“ casserole dish. The cornbread stayed mushy on the bottom. We tried to spread it out into a thin layer, but that didn’t work due to the viscosity of the mixture. This is not one we’d make again.
Cornbread was Meh.  No sweetness or cornbread flavor.  A Jiffy box mix is better.  Chili was quite bland.  Added more Chili Powder and a can of Rotel Hot and it had a kick.

I made this late night. As comments suggested, I doubled the chili powder and and cornbread. I baked it in an iron skillet for about 15 minutes, as my oven was preheating for sometime. I will make this for years to come. Rave reviews from everyone who tried it.

This might be the best recipe or most successful that I’ve made on here, which is odd, but I do love it. The flavors are well balanced and the “cornbread” topping is so light and airy. I can see having turn with this and playing around with some different ingredients to see how it plays. High marks!

Considering that I've lost count of the number of times I've made Ms. Clark's tamale pie from her essential Dinner in One cookbook, I cannot wait to make this recipe. It appears only slightly elevated but, again, utilizes only pantry ingredients (with the possible exception of the peppers). I've no doubt that it will be aces.

My daughter requests this all the time. I make the topping it with Trader Joe's cornbread mix, I use the whole box of mix. It gives you quite a bit of topping so you need to bake it longer than 25 minutes, closer to 40. It's sweeter than the homemade version would be but it pairs well with lots of toppings. I just put a "toppings bar" on the table so everyone can build their own adventure.

Loved this!  Made the "mistake" of using Chipolte Chili Powder so 2 Tbsps was a bit much but a dollop of sour cream fixed that.  I did find that the cornbread topping was sparse, but I did use my large 7 qt size Dutch over.  That said, the topping held up remarkably well in the leftovers.
Add corn and guac :)
I found this to be one note flavor wise- it really is just a chili pepper forward chili with cornbread topping. It was fine but there are far better vegetarian chili recipes on here
This is a favorite in my house! I love the cheesy cornbread top. We serve it with sour cream and chopped cilantro.

Not sure about this.  I doubled the recipe like most suggested but ended up with what seemed like cornbread on beans.  Might just be picky because I live in Texas.  Next time I'll try it with masa, and not so much.  Perhaps some pickled jalapenos on the top.
Cut the chili recipe in half for 2 people but kept the cornbread same as suggested. Very good and tasty. Cornbread was rather thick - more like dumplings than a crust though.
My mother, who was absolutely not an adventurous or even happy cook, used to make a similar dish because my dad was raised mostly in southern California. Thank you for this recipe.
Do you have recommendations for the best 'Gluten Free' Cornbread brand/mix or recipe ? Thank you.
@Monica just use King Arthur measure for measure gf flour in any cornbread recipe, such as the one on your container of cornmeal. It’s perfect
I wonder if anyone has attempted to make this in a large skillet rather than a Dutch Oven?  It seems the cornbread might be enough to cover the chili.
@Pindie yes they have. Scroll through the most liked comments.

Looks Very interesting. 
Have issues with spicy food, but will endeavour to get the taste with something a little less spicy
Use sweet paprika, smoked or not as you choose
Tamale pie was my dad’s specialty in the 70s. However I was shocked and dismayed one time when he made it for an event and included olives LOL. He had omitted them at home due to picky kids.
Masa not cornbread. Also grits are not masa. Use some real dried chilis not chili powder. Beans in tamale pie? No. Put them on the side if you must.

@kim

This is the veggie version, so I welcome beans. Besides, I’m ok not being a traditionalist

Next time I'll lower the oven for the last baking step--425 was too high to bake the pie in the end, at least in my oven.  Caught it in time before it burned.  Just an FYI.  And it took me longer than 1 hour to prepare this.
Wonderful but absolutely double the amount of cornbread topping! The original recipe left quite a coverage deficit!

I'm happy to try this vegetarian version but I'm just kinda over sheet panning everything. It's not a one pot meal if I also gotta sheet pan the onions and peppers. I'll be tryin' a simpler method with just a skillet and large casserole dish.

This was delicious! I did swap ground beef (needed to use) for beans. I used Greek yogurt in the cornbread but that made it pretty thick, so I decided to thin out the mix with water (if I had any form of milk/cream I would have used that lol). The water thinned it out just enough so it was easy to spread on top of the chili. Cooked up perfectly in my Dutch. Will be making again!
Has anyone every tried this with actual masa before? So it’ll actually be more like a tamale? Would that work or just fall apart I wonder?
Tamale pie needs black olives in it and a cornmeal mush or polenta as the topping not regular cornbread. My grandma made this back in the 1940s and 1950s.  When I make it vegetarian I also add corn.
@Timaree This comment gave me smallpox
Skip the oregano. Wrong flavor.
Made it for two, not nearly enough cornbread topping.
My favorite version of this uses the actual ingredients for tamales: masa harina and lots of your preferred fat, some form of leavening. The texture is perfect.

I grew up eating tamale pie (rural Georgia). It was a good recipe pretty good for me but I had to make some substitutions to keep my hubby happy he’s a demanding customer?! Anyway he saw me reading this recipe on the Nut and he demands I make it. His favorite protein is hotdog and he’s on a protein diet so I cut some up and added (without skin so the texture is better) so I microwaved 5 hotdogs for about 15 minutes on highs so they were cooked through okay and added them chopped up and that was a good addition yummy we didn’t have tortillas corn meal so we just used bread thinly sliced on the top
Like others, I doubled the cornbread recipe. Didn't have either eggs or scallions, but the results were still delicious.
Should i drain the tomatoes?
I didn't...I just dumped them right in with the roasted veggies, used an immersion blender and it came out great.
Agree with (at least) doubling the cornbread.  Not sure how many beans I made - probably a full quart, but the topping didn't make it to the edges

Sad I didn't read the comments first. Must say I'm disappointed to have the picture flat out deceitful. Filling and topping are good but the ratio is tragically off and the timing provided is also off. Can't give it higher than a 3.
Chili mix needed a thickener. Made a quick roux to add, which gave more flavor.
Great recipe!  Loved the hearty and warming flavors.  However it did take a while to prepare and the kitchen was a disaster of dirty dishes and bowls and sheet pan and blender.

This was delicious and fun to make. For less spice, I substituted the jalapeño with part of a yellow pepper. Used three types of beans: black, pinto, and small red. Added a cup of frozen corn. I accidentally got a larger can of tomatoes than called for, but used it all and it worked out. Followed suggestions to double the cornbread, which was essential; there never would have been enough otherwise. But honestly the chili was so tasty, you could just have it with cornbread on the side.
The cornmeal topping in this recipe doesn't come close to being enough to cover the surface of the chili. I doubled it and it worked fine.

Eh, I found this underwhelming. Not sure what I did wrong, but the cornbread didn't cook through in the allotted time (it was goopy in the middle) and the whole thing lacked flavor, even though the smells through the house were amazing! Won't try it again.
This did not taste very good.
Satisfying and delicious! As others had suggested, I doubled the cornbread recipe - excellent call.
Flavor of the beans was good! As others suggested I made a double batch of the cornbread which turned out super dry and lacking flavor. Would make again with tweaks.
@Pat I was specifically wondering if my cast iron skillet would work and you answered my question, thank you!
Charring the vegetables added depth of flavor to the chili. The chili is very good even without meat or the cornbread. I used a medium size dutch oven and there was enough batter to cover the chili.

This was tasty, rich, and satisfying even for someone who is not a vegetarian.  I opted for whole milk yoghurt in the cornbread topping, and I found the topping to be a sufficient amount.  Also, I only had medium grind cornmeal, and it worked fine.
15 oz can of beans its equivalent to 1.9 cups

Made as is, and while it’s tasty I would never make again. Way too much effort, especially with the pan charring veggies before making chili purée. There are equally as good versions of this on the internet that create far less dishes and take half the amount of labor. Also as many have commented by no means is that enough cornbread, it makes a thin crispy layer on top not what I hoped for. Pass.

I had problems with the toping.  The chili was delicious. I wouldn't change anything about that part. I used the yogurt instead of sour cream- maybe that was the problem??? I think next time I may try making them separately.

Not enough cornbread topping!  Wish I had read the comments before making this recipe. I decided to bake it in a smaller dish and still did not have enough cornbread. I also used yogurt, and the cornbread batter was thick and difficult to spread.  I had to thin it out with a little milk.

Good cornbread topping. double recipe for the following filling, Weird Al's Tamale Pie:
1/2 cup olive oil
2 cups red onion, diced into 1/4 inch cubes 
5 cloves garlic, chopped
2 cups red, green, bell peppers, diced.  
2 tsp salt
3 cups cooked pinto or black beans
2 cups chopped tomatoes 
2 cups sweet corn kernels
2 tbs chipotle canned chiles, chopped
2 tsp cumin
2 tbsp. coriander spice

Instead of six cups of beans I added four cups of beans, one cup of corn and one cup of chopped olives.  I also prefer polenta to cornbread.  The cornbread soaked up a majority of the liquid.  It was still good but looked like a pan of fat cornbread.
I buy boxed cornbread and just make however much it calls for and it more than tops the whole thing. Makes the whole recipe easier too!

Used an entire bag of Rancho Gordo pinto beans. Cooked the bean mixture in a large Dutch oven. Then transferred it to two 10” cast irons with double the topping to bake. Turned out perfect! Great for a game day party!
Substitute dutch processed cocoa powder for the rice flour/cornstarch and you have unbelievably good chocolate shortbread.

I've been making shortbread for over 50 years, using the recipe in the marvelous British Bero cookbook, and I've been getting compliments for just as long.  Three ingredients - sugar, salted butter, and flour, in the 1:2:3 ratio by weight.  Zap them all together in a food processor until they are well combined, then form the dough however you wish.
Laurie Colwin, the food writer, has a similar recipe. I'm convinced the rice flour is what elevates hers, too. Tempting to add some lemon zest.
@A: I’ve always added cornstarch or potato starch. They all have the effect of preventing gluten formation, which results in a tender and crumbly cookie.
Lemon zest sounds like a great idea. Please post if you try it and let us know how it works out.

Feel free to riff on it with lemon zest, even a touch of lemon extract, pistachios... My dream is to devote several weeks to making many different variations of shortbread, just to see which ones are best. And then doing it again in case I'm wrong the first time...

I have made this recipe many times, using the rice flour instead of cornstarch. I use 1/2 cup of granulated sugar (I think 2/3 cup would be too much) and press the mixture into a greased 13x9x2 pan. Prick it all over with a fork to prevent any rise. I sprinkle Demerara sugar on top immediately after removing them from the oven. Cut into squares (to sizes of your liking) while they are still warm or they will shatter if they are cold.
@Melanie did you freeze the dough before placing in the tray/pan?
In the history of cookies, 2 dozen shortbread cookies have never lasted a week in a household. How did you test?

This recipe is a classic shortbread that I make every year at Christmas, even the rice flour.  However, superfine sugar is preferable to regular sugar- what the Brits call caster sugar.  It makes a superior biscuit. I also add vanilla.  It is not classic but it enhances the flavour.  Shortbread is best a few days after it has been baked because it cures.  Rice flour adds a crispness which is very pleasant.
I've found that using powdered sugar, the kind with cornstarch, instead of granulated sugar + rice or cornstarch, makes a particularly tender cookie.
I make  these cookies using a food processor with very good results

For those who've mentioned the ratio, it is by weight, not by volume.

So measure your ingredients on a scale if you want to be as close to the "origina" authentic shortbread.

@Euphemia Thompson The ratio is not followed no matter how you calculate it. The recipe as written here calls for 100g sugar (1 part), 225g butter (which should be 200g in order to equal 2 parts), and 250g flour (which should be 300g in order to equal 3 parts).

Our family recipe calls for using salted butter. Our four ingredients are butter,  sugar, flour, and rice flour. Cook them in a slow oven, don't pre-heat it. You know it's right by tasting the dough - it shouldn't be too sweet.
I add pistachios and cranberries to my shortbread for color and crunch.

Please..... Highlander Shortbread, no "s". Similar to "bread". No plural needed.
Then you can serve a "piece of shortbread".
And of course, never refer to shortbread as a "cookie". It's shortbread, plain and simple.
My Scottish grandmother made these.

My son has a severe anaphylactic egg allergy, and as a result I have become an inventive baker of shortbread.  I make them all the time using just flour, salted Irish butter, sugar, and vanilla.  Sometimes plain, but many times with one simple additional ingredient I press into the dough.  My favorites (not together, but separate) include mini dark chocolate chips, dried chopped up rosemary, orange zest, or a bit of ginger and molasses.  Such a great cookie, the shortbread!
@Sarah You can also make most cookies very successfully using flax eggs 1:1 for the eggs in recipes :-)

One thing I have found is that white rice flour or cornstarch really impacts the texture of the cookie - I prefer rice flour because it helps give it a bit of a snap. Opt for cornstarch if you want a more tender crumb.
Can this be made with gluten-free flour?
@Cali I’ll definitely try!
In the intro article it is recommended to use European/cultured style for the slight tang, but I have made a very similar recipe and either work.
The only thing I would add is roll the shortbread cylinders in the sugar before putting in the fridge as it then adheres better.
Rather than rice try Scottish oats. I did and it worked out well.
Back home in England we always added ground rice to shortbread. It’s a rougher sandy texture than rice flour.

For those mentioning substitutions or additions, cornstarch, rice flour, chocolate powder, Scotch oats, please add the quantities by weight for those of us that would like to try your suggestions. Just mentioning that you added an ingredient without the ratio is a real head scratcher.

@Val If you’re making chocolate shortbread just substitute Dutch processed coca powder 1:1 for the rice flour/cornstarch (ie 36g). You can use regular cocoa powder but it won’t have as deep a chocolate color or flavor.
Try with rehydrated browned butter!
Most memorable shortbread experience I've ever had was while drinking a glass of Bowmore on the beach at dusk on Islay. Magic. These are very special with single malt, Scottish whisky.

This is similar to The Joy of Cooking version which I have always used.  It calls for powdered sugar and does not mention a flour additive, so I have never supplemented the flour with anything. I sometimes include rosemary leaves, fresh from my backyard and blended to a pulp... 2 tsp or so.  And yes, make a log, chill, roll it in the chunky sugar and make people deliriously happy.  =)
I also have always used powdered sugar, which contains cornstarch. I will try it and add the extra cornstarch, maybe just not as much?

First time, so I closely followed the recipe. Added zest of one lemon and baked for + 5 minutes to get a light brown finish. So simple, so tasty thanks

P.S. checked the oven temp while warming to 325. My oven goes beyond the desired temp by ~50F before settling back down to the set temp.  Settling took about 20 minutes. Am I alone with this problem?

My physicist husband explained it to me once-something about that when preheating, the oven does actually go beyond the temp. I can't remember why, but they all do this. He explained that if you want to start at the correct temp, you do need to wait a bit for it to "settle" back down to target temp. In other words, you figured it out and solved it correctly!!
@kirk. No.  The higher temps allows the walls to heat too, not just the ambient air.  A longer preheat allows temps to level off.

If I could only eat one cookie for the rest of my life, it would be shortbread. I tried this recipe. Cornstarch makes light and crumbly cookies, yes, but there is a distinct starchy mouth feel. @Fiona Doyle has the right idea. Flour, butter, sugar. 3:2:1. That’s the best way to make shortbread, always and without a doubt. No vanilla, no lemon, no chocolate, no cornstarch.
I made these with gluten free flour and they turned out great.
A waste of butter! Which isn’t cheap. Followed recipe to the letter. Took dough out of fridge, sliced it, and it crumbled to dust. Terrible.
would cultured butter make thses even better?
Therfe was a similar - great - recipe decades ago in the NYT Magazine.  So glad this was published now, as I’d lost the clipped copy in the course of life.  Absolutely terrific cookies.
I don't use European butter for cookies as they tend to spread.  Concerned that would happen with these cookies as well.  What's your experience been?
@Kay Chill your dough before baking and you shouldn’t have a problem.
There's nothing wrong with a treat now and then, but I'd love to see more recipes on this site that don't have sugar as a primary ingredient.

I agree that many sweet recipes on the NYT website are too sweet for my taste and I know to adjust the sugar down, substitute nut flour for some of the sugar, or skip the recipe altogther. This one  is 16% added sugar by weight, which is not bad, resulting in a scant teaspoon of sugar per cookie. In fairness, there are also plenty of recipes w no sugar available on the site...
@Bill Sugar is not the primary ingredient of this recipe. By weight it’s only 1/6. But if you want a savory biscuit instead of a sweet one just skip the sugar and add 200g of grated cheddar cheese.
1:2:4 is the standard shortbread ratio. If you can get your hands on caster sugar (finer than granulated but not as fine as confectioners), even better!
Can you freeze this dough?
Absolutely!
Yes. Slice into cookies and freeze on parchment or wax paper, then move to a freezer storage container. I have found they can be baked straight from the freezer or thawed.

I had cheddar cheese shortbread once and it was delicious. Anyone know how to make it? I was told to use a shortbread recipe … but I don’t know the correct measurements 
.
Help! I need the recipe that @MelissaClark says she can get in the oven in 10 minutes. This recipe looks wonderful, but I need the quick one. Where would I find that @VaughnVreeland?
This IS the 10 minute recipe referenced in the article, Melissa Clark Goes Big on Shortbread.
I made one batch with lemon zest and a few drops of lemon oil; and a second batch with 1/4 cup black cocoa and a teaspoon of espresso powder. It’s a wonder I did eat them all myself.

The perfect shortbread cookie, simple and elegant. I used the cornstarch option because I had it in the pantry. Purchased Demarara sugar for my second batch but find that regular sugar is just as tasty for coating the edges.  Next time I might try colored sugar!
Can the logs of dough be frozen and then thawed and baked off later?

Thank you for this recipe. They're so simple yet, even in Scotland, these weren't easy to find outside of pre-packaged variety. Wonder why more bakeries don't offer everywhere...could there be a better cookie with coffee?
I have never written a comment before but this shortbread is frighteningly addictive. So good it is  nearly impossible to stop eating them. Make it!
Would you use European-style butter, with its higher fat content, or is the recipe tailored to American butter?

We love, love, LOVE shortbread. However, due to heart issues we to really watch saturated fat. Has anyone tried making these with a vegetable oil spread, or half and half spread and butter. It won’t be the same, I know, but I am curious. Thanks.

Butter is necessary for flavor; otherwise you'll just have a clump of sweetened flour in cookie form. Maybe you can limit yourself to just one cookie a day and cut down on fat somewhere else? I know that's tough, but it would be worth it to be able to enjoy these.
Would a non-dairy butter work for these? Sacrilege, I know.

I don't have Demerara sugar, nor have I ever even seen it in the store. Is there an acceptable substitute? Perhaps a blend of granulated and brown? I resist the temptation to buy yet another single-purpose ingredient to clutter my cupboard and throw away a decade from now. It seems like half the recipes call for something unique. I know there are ingredients that can't be substituted without materially changing a recipe, but surely there's a workaround for sugar?

You have seen demerara sugar every time you go to the grocery store. The organic brand I use -because it’s what’s in the stores where I live- is called Florida Crystals,  It is raw cane sugar.
It is called Demerara Sugar because it originated in the British colony of Demerara, which is now Guyana.  Also, it is not a one time only product, use it in coffee, tea, cereal, almost any place you would use white sugar.  Google MasterClass to learn more on how to use demerara sugar.

"Sugar in the Raw" is widely available at groceries in the US, if that's where you are.  It is turbinado sugar, which is not exactly the same as demerara (smaller crystals for one thing) but should work just fine.
I find it in bulk at my local discount grocery. That way I can buy just a half cup, or however much I need.

Agree that cornstarch or rice flour are musts, and I have had great luck using confectioners sugar in place of granulated.   You can play a lot with add-is (candied ginger, citrus peel, fresh snipped rosemary,) but plain shortbread all by itself is hard to beat.

I've added lemon, lime, orange zest singly or in combination, cinnamon, cinnamon/nutmeg, cardamom--small amounts go a long way to changing up this shortbread. Is it traditional? No, but if I don't show up with some version at family gatherings, the Scots Irish side of the family will grumble.
Are there any tips for getting the sugar to stick to the chilled rolls?

@Name Kanthony
I let the logs sit at room temp for about 15 minutes to get them to sweat a bit. Then rolled in sugar … great coverage.

Christina Tosi (Milk Bar) has a recipe for potato chip shorties in her book "All About Cookies". The recipe is similar but with the addition of potato chips, adding fat, some texture, and a slightly savory quality to the shortbread. The recipe presents a perfect way to use up the last of that potato chip bag.
@Dan Leithauser. I make something similar with potato chips and chopped pecans. My kids love them.
Has anyone used grain flour in place of AP?  Specifically, Einkorn.
Can you successfully bake these in a toaster oven?
The cold rolls make it really hard for the sugar to stick.  I ended up putting sugar on the top, too.  Good cookies - I'll try them again with some rice flour
Does it matter whether I use European or American butter?
I'd like to make lavender shortbread cookies.  Any suggestions?

I always have Turbinado sugar in my cupboard but I don't have Demerara, so I'm going to sub it. I'm pretty sure it should work just fine. Maybe a little less crunchy? I LOVE shortbread, but all my other recipes call for mix-ins, and while I've swapped those, I've never made the bases without them to see if they hold up on their own.
It will, 100%.

While these were tasty I found the dough very dry, hard to roll and even after refrigeration, when cutting the dough crumbled in places. I did half the recipe so perhaps that was the issue. I used cornstarch. I could not roll in sugar as it did not adhere and really not needed as the shortbread was sweet enough.

@BR except I can NEVER find Dutch process in my stores (and I'm not going to order it specially when I can usually sub natural in most things) so that's my question: can I use natural/unsweetened in this?
@A Yes! Similar to the one my mum made. Confectioners sugar makes a difference.
This recipe is an opportunity for endless variations! Try lemon zest and lavender.
I have quite a bit of brown rice flour I use for other recipes.  How might this affect the shortbread vs white rice flour?
I used all flour;  made a double batch, adding one tablespoon vanilla to it. Incredibly tasty, had great crunch. It’s my new favorite cookie.
Delicious, but the cornstarch makes the dough very challenging to roll

wonderful taste, but wondering what I could have done better.  1. After chilling overnight, the sugar didn't want to stick to the  dough.  Brushing with water was a mistake. I let the second roll warm up a bit, but still not very sticky. Advice?  2 Being careful not to overwork the dough, their were cracks inside the rolls, so some of them totally crumbled. Great crumbs, but still....

@Holly Nelson I had the same problem. The cylinders fell apart and crumbled as I tried to slice them. I ended up with a half dozen slices and myriad crescent moons. (The sugar didn’t adhere to the cylinder either, so I sprinkled sugar 5 minutes into the bake when the dough was softer.)  I’m wondering if in trying to keep from overworking the dough I didn’t mix it thoroughly enough, and that’s why the dough fell apart?
I baked and took them to a Mother’s Day, family gathering last weekend. They were inhaled by all! I received so many compliments especially from my partner’s mom!  I just completed another batch.
Would brown rice flour work well, or does it have an entirely different flavor profile?
More like 20-25 minutes.

These were delicious. Being at a high altitude I found I needed to bake them a bit longer. They did spread somewhat when baking so leave space for that. I found that the raw sugar did not adhere well to the chilled dough, so I added a small sprinkle on the top of each cookie before baking, which worked perfectly.

My batch took twice as long as the 15-20 minutes in the instructions. There was no hint of "golden brown" until at least 30 minutes. My oven temp is accurate. I even cut them a bit thinner than the 3/8 inches in the instructions (I got 24 instead of 20). And they still took forever. Wonder what I did wrong?
Didn't have Demerara sugar, so I used Sugar in the Raw.  Try as I might, I could not get the sugar to evenly adhere to the dough logs.

I was very happy to find this recipe! It is the same or very similar to the one I used to make as a kid. This time, I used Sweet Rice Flour instead of cornstarch. They are perfect. I made them for my neighbor as a gift and they are still talking about them. They said they’ve never had Scottish Shortbread that was so good. They are demanding more, so today I’m making more!

Add 1/2 tsp cinnamon with the sugar, then roll the logs in Tajin seasoning instead of sugar: perfect dessert to accompany taco night. I have made these several times and have always had to bake them for 25-ish minutes instead of the recommended 15-20.
Look for a recipe for cheese coins or cheese pennies @Lois
Use cold Kerry Gold or Costco European butter!

I am not talented in the kitchen, but this sounds do-able to me. I would love a short video of this to see the rolling, shaping and how they come out of the boiling water to get my confidence up before I try.

If you want really authentic flavor, skip the boiling and instead immerse uncooked pretzels in a room temperature solution of water with about 4% by weight food-grade lye, them bake as usual. Itt only takes 30 seconds or so for each pretzel, and you'll get pretzels like a Bavarian Oma's.
I've read baking soda can yours similar results.

@Joe S a dip in lye solution is indeed key to the most authentic pretzel flavor. But it’s a whole different ballgame; lye is a dangerous chemical and can cause skin and eye damage if not handled carefully. Definitely worth the effort but some serious safety precautions are needed. Baking soda gets you halfway there with way less risk
I use melted butter instead of egg white, tastes better...
I love soft pretzels but the frozen ones leave a lot to be desired. I'm looking forward to trying this recipe!

Melissa Clark is so amazing! It's too bad that in the videos Kate Hudson and the other woman keep talking over each other detracting from the tutorial being given by Melissa. Less is more ladies, you're not that funny or interesting!
What is the David Tanis recipe?
See the link at the bottom of the recipe, under Tips>
@Suzanna there is no link in the app

@Alaska Sunny
Hello. I asked myself the same question. Then clicked on the word “homemade” where it lists the pizza dough in the app and it popped up.
Even with store-bought dough, these were very satisfying. By the time I got to my fifth pretzel, they were even looking pretty good. My grandchildren really enjoyed them.
I got lazy and rolled these with green onion tops and cheddar cheese and they make amazing breadsticks!

@Geri
