@Geri Feel free to; it’s not against the law.
@Alfrabro Cormac McCarthy said just because you can do something doesn’t mean you ought to.
Good as written. A little eggy for my taste - could use some matzo meal or something as an additional binder. Otherwise it was quite satisfying and the presentation is very nice.

@Harper
I see your point though I haven’t made this yet I dislike matzo meal. I think a little potato starch might be perfect, and some nuts sprinkled on top for texture?

Yes—I liked it, but I'd like the egg part to be more substantial. I'll be adding arepa flour next time I make it; farfel would make sense too. (arepa flour is a go-to thickener for me: precooked so it can't taste like raw starch).
The time listed on the top is way off.
@Dawn is the time too long or too short?

@Dawn yes 10 min to prep but 60-7in to bake…. Though not sure why so long to bake… it seemed ready after approximately 30 min… browned crispy edges… perhaps I am not understanding the texture this recipe is going for?
@Jk45 minutes did it for me.
I’ll chop all of the cauliflower. Slices look interesting but I believe will be more pleasant to eat.
Yes, I think for appearance I'd do the top as nicely arranged florets rather than the slices; surely slices would end up pretty tough? And the craggy florets should brown nicely.
Yes, I topped it with fairly short/shallow florets, and was happy with texture and appearance.
Seems almost identical to https://debraklein.com/wprm_print/cauliflower-kugel except she uses sweet paprika.
What does "saving the juices" from the grated onion mean? Are we supposed to save them and add them to the kugel mixture or save them for some other meal?
If you follow the recipe, the juices get mixed in when you add the scallions, paprika, all the salt and eggs to the onions....because the juices are there in the bowl with the onions!
The juice is in the bowl with the grated onion, so they're just saying to keep the juice with the onion, not drain it before you add the other ingredients.
I think since you add the other ingredients to the onion bowl it means to add the juice to the kugel, rather than draining the onions b/f adding the rest.

Made this today for my Passover Seder. I had to push down the cauliflower on the top, as it was going to fall off. Maybe another egg or some matzah meal to bind it more. Or just chop all the cauliflower. I sprinkled some more salt on the top after I cooked it, as it was a little bland. Also sprinkled extra paprika and parsley on top. I would add pepper next time and an extra scallion or two. Otherwise delicious! I would make this year round.
I’ve never made kugel but I’m wondering if you could add cheese to this.
@Mary Absolutely!  I was thinking some grated Parm.
@Mary not if you’re serving it at Passover Seder, which is usually a meat meal and we don’t mix milk products and meat (Kosher rules)
@Mary typically cottage cheese. But then you can’t serve along with meat.  If you’re actually kosher
This needs some matza meal to be perfect.
I wonder about half cauliflower and half sliced potato as a sort of middle ground…

Love this as I tend to avoid carb-laden food so kugel has been off the table. I'm wondering if it would be possible to make a sweet version, perhaps with a high quality sugar substitute such as golden monk fruit. Then use cottage cheese (my grandmother's recipe) and add cinnamon or other sweet spices.
I think I’d make your traditional recipe and just sub in cauliflower for the noodles.  I do this with many rice dishes, subbing in riced cauliflower. Works ver well

We really enjoyed this--light and healthy, but so satisfying and favorful! We were short on cauliflower so made up the difference with chopped onion and a small diced white potato. Definitely a keeper!

I used the handy recipe sizer provided by  NYT, but the cooking time indicated  for the smaller dish is the same as for the full sized dish. I love matzoh farfel. Could I toast some and add it?

Inexplicably the co-op had no green onions so I threw in a handful of fresh dill instead. Quite nice. I’ll make this again. I had this as a main dish. A little on the light side but very tasty and healthy.

Added about half a cup of matza meal; used half a red onion; sprinkled walnuts on top, Texture was good; browned nicely; no overpowering onion taste. Microwave reheat next day - only a small loss in taste and texture.

I’ve made this three times so far and each time is better than the one before. My modifications for version three include bulking this up with leftover brown rice and breadcrumbs. I also added in nutritional yeast, cheddar cheese, Greek yogurt and a small amount of cumin seeds. But the secret ingredient for batch three has got to be the chili crisp. ❤️ this recipe is a fabulous way to use a whole head of cauliflower.
Can you use riced cauliflower???

@Faith Herzog

I was wondering the same thing!
As for time, I would think an hour+ is too long for cauliflower or noodles.
Five stars Melissa, as always.  I’ll add some Parmesan next time as a veg entree, but great as it is.

Melissa Clark is my favorite NYT recipe creator, cauliflower is my favorite vegetable by far, and I like all of the other ingredients in this recipe as well! So, I was really anxious to try this recipe as soon as I saw it online this morning. Unfortunately, my husband and I both disliked it intensely! The reasons: (1) it smelled and tasted way too "oniony"; (2) the ingredients didn't really seem to go well together; and (3) the egg mixture was a rather unpleasant brownish taupe in color. Ugh!

Nice idea but too oily, salty and just insubstantial. Added about 1.25 cup plain boiled roughly mashed potato in anticipation of the kugel needing more substance, but it didn't help. I usually love Melissa's recipes, but this one needs some doctoring.
Added 3/4 c. matzo farfel to give it a little more body and 1/2 c. shredded cheese for a little more flavor (if you are not serving with meat).  Delicious!!

My husband made this today as a side dish for our second night Seder.  It looked beautiful - just like the NYT photo - and tasted fantastic.  A winner!  NOTE:  For the decorative topping, he had to cut a few slices from each half of the cauliflower.

2lb cauliflower did not fill the pan and it was under seasoned as directed. I used less oil, added a bit of matzoh meal as a binder and to balance the egg and oil. Also reserved some of the juices from the florets to drizzle over decorative slices, then brushed on a bit of oil to the top layer to prevent burning and drying out. Then I added powdered garlic and onion, more smoked paprika and some salt and pepper to the top. People said they liked it, but i felt it wasn’t living up to its potential.

Made this for Passover. Great addition and a nice change up. I took the advice of other commenters and chopped all the cauliflower bite-sized; I also added a bit of chopped broccoli for color. Next time I might add some fresh herbs as well.
Made for a Passover gathering last night. It was fine, but I would not make again.

As someone else mentioned, it was a bit more oniony than ideal, especially as an aftertaste—but I was having it as a main dish; as a side I might be fine with it. And I left out the scallions (forgot to get some). I might do quite a bit less red onion and add back the scallions next time.

I'm not convinced preheating the dish is important—unlike with potatoes or noodles, where the hot dish can make a difference, there isn't really starch on the bottom of the pan to get brown or crisp. I made it and did preheat the pan, but I suspect it adds needless complexity. Anyone tried without?
Curious if anyone knows why this calls for so much oil. I haven’t tried it yet but that’s the thing turning me off.
It looks like other folks had better results than I did: the flavor was good, but the texture was soggy and sad after cooking it for 85 minutes.
ok but this wasn't as attractive as I would have liked it. The flavor is nice (the second day too) but not worth the cauliflower.

Delicious. Easy to put together. I like method of the frothy liquid, turnout very stable and cuttable. Not eggy on bottom, more substantial. Making again. I only needed about 60 min until the edges were deeply frowned.
this just tasted like onion to me.  no problem with texture others mentioned.  just didn't care for this personally.  Ever Onward.

this is a good starter recipe, I did had 1/3 cup of matzah meal. Will add more spice on my next iteration, as well as more GREEN... maybe some barely wilted spinach. I mean, if we are messing with kugel... mess with it. My DiL had seconds, though, to it IS good ;)
This is not a kugel. It is a frittata. Vegetables and eggs.  That's it.  I gave it a try and nope.  Make it for breakfast for a light lunch, but NOT for a kugel replacement.
I love the idea of this dish, but it was very bland. It was fully cooked in an hour.
how do you keep a pan greased if you heat it to 350?  all the oil will slither down.

title: Roasted Carrots With Harissa Vinaigrette Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody:
just made these! harissa carrots are my fave side dish.

it made a lot of extra vinaigrette, I could have used 1 tablespoon of harissa and it would have been plenty. I used more vinegar too. It would be good on sweet potatoes and parsnips too.

my normal harissa carrot side dish uses the powdered form of harissa, and I like it better than the paste.
I have harissa powder. How much of it do I use? Thanks!
I would make a paste from the powder. Just add oil and water.
I also had the dried harissa. I used the same amount. No need to make a paste, it blended in nicely.

Very similar to a Martha Rose Shulman recipe for beets - I made a batch yesterday. Big difference is she recommended sherry vinegar rather than red wine vinegar. I find the sherry vinegar has a more complex flavor profile. But will give this one a try, love roasted carrots.

This was spectacular. The dressing makes a lot so you can easily reduce the amount but I think it would go great with other roasted vegetables too. I will try it on sweet potatoes or winter squash. The orange zest is a nice touch and adds a lovely citrus fragrance to the dish. Will add this dish to my regular rotation!
I wanted to love this, but it came out very sweet from the carrots and orange, which was not what I expected.
I am making these for our Seder tomorrow night. Can they be made in advance and refrigerated? Or served room temp?
Delicious! Added a bit of honey and orange jucice to the vinaigrette. Could have used 1/2 the harissa. Don't miss the step of putting the olive oil into the vinaigrette!

Delicious. Addictive. I used 1 1/2 TBSP spicy harissa (Mina brand) and 1 1/2 TBSP mild. Depending on my audience I would change that proportion. Skipped the nuts. Very pretty too. Use large carrots. This isn't a recipe for the delicate ones.
I'd love to make these ahead - today for tomorrow - any suggestions, please.

Roast the carrots 85% of the way. Refrigerate. Spread back on the sheet pan and reheat at 450 until hot and tender. Toss with vinaigrette. (I roasted in the morning and then brought it to a Seder and reheated it just before serving. Worked great.)

To Cara,
I only have powder harissa, so how much to use?
Thanks,
Carrie

Everyone liked these, but I am taking off a star only because there were not enough for 7 people, and it became overly fussy with all the garnishes.   Note:  the carrots shrunk a lot.   I used a 2 lb bag of multicolor carrots and supplemented with a few more.   But in the end, it only filled my smallest serving dish. 

The thyme roasted carrots alone smelled absolutely delicious.   I only used about 1/2 the dressing.  They probably needed only one garnish. The hazelnuts were a distraction.
Wonderful. Used large carrots and half spicy (Mina brand) and half mild harissa pastes. Excellent side dish for brisket and can see making this regularly.

I was excited when I saw Melissa Clark‘s recipe for matzo bread. I love trying to make food from other cultures. Mrs. Sartell, my neighbor when I was about 10 years old taught me how to make Mondelbrot. For reasons, I’ll never understand my mother would often have matzo available in our home for snacking. Melissa’s recipe felt inclusive to me, and I appreciated that I had an opportunity to peek into a culture, possibly learn how a sacred bread was made even though the recipe wasn’t correct. My matzo is far from perfect yet I will share it with my family tonight and hopefully have the breaking of my unkosher bread lead to peace in the world.
These are lovely, but they are not matzos.
@Dan maybe not for the more religious, but they’re good enough for us reform folks ( ;
@Sarah I am reform and they are not matzos. Unleavened bread is considered mandatory even in reform for the sedder.
@Nate this in unleavened. no yeast or baking soda. Should be fine for passover as long as you make it it the required time frame.

I make matza in the food processor. I often add fresh rosemary. Sometimes I roll it but I started using my pasta machine per my granddaughter.. My Atlas is old the highest # is 6. I roll it to 5. It is really delicious. The same ingredients. For kosher pasta no resting. It's supposed to be done in 18 minutes.
How about a gluten-free version?
@S. Beck   We make GF matzoh with oat flour and it works beautifully!
@Rose Attar Thank you! Do you use oat flour in this recipe? No added guar gum or psyllium?
No longer need to buy fire hook crackers for $10! I’m so excited. Will try with rosemary, garlic powder, oregano, the possibilities are endless.

What can one substitute for a pizza stone?  I saw a video showing a man making something like pita? English muffin ish?  a flat round disc on a grill and I do a version of fried bread (in butter or oil) have a standing batch of more or less sour dough in the fridge  -- in a cast iron skillet.

You don't have to be Jewish to love matzo!
terra cotta floor tiles are perfect, unglazed. either one large one or several smaller ones you group together on the rack in the oven
@Grabby old lady: I think it helps if you’re not! My non-Jewish friends love it. Go figure.
It’s best baked at 5 min in my opinion. It makes it crunchier.
Could you put this thru a pasta machine?
Yes. Roll it thin.
This is very similar, I'd say same as the regañá in the southwest of Spain. We eat it with Iberian ham of shell fish.
Thank you, Melissa!
Can i use this to make matzo balls?  I couldn’t find matzo in Ridgewood, Queens to make matzo ball soup.
Please describe exactly how to transfer the wafer thin dough to the pizza stone.  Thanks.

This recipe is very similar to Mark Bittman’s recipe also found in NYT Cooking.  That recipe adds somewhat more water, but makes 1 dozen.
I made it the first time a few years ago, and since then, my family always designates me to bring matzo to our Seders.
This year I made five dozen, and they disappeared in no time.
Last year I sprinkled poppy and sesame seeds on them, this year, only kosher salt.  I liked this year‘s better.
I bake them for 3-4 minutes per side, and flipped them once. yes, I can make them in less than 18 minutes, thanks to my food processor.

Actually, traditional matzo included olive oil. It's literally part of the Talmudic recipe. And Sephardic matzo still does. It was a rabbinical decision of Ashkenazi Orthodox rabbis in the late 1800s to turn matzo into a cracker of affliction.
Can this be done with whole grain flour?
Sure. Why not?

Saw this recipe and had to make immediately because I’m a marmalade fanatic (just wait till your forties, haters). Enough Robertson’s Seville Orange in the fridge for one batch. So fast, so easy, so chewy. Perfect cookie for a quick! 5 stars.
@Willow K Cannot wait to try, and I, like you, am a marmalade devotee. Bring on the bitter sweetness.
@Willow K and what about using ginger marmalade?  Add some extra powdered ginger in with the dry ingredients?  That might be excellent for ginger lovers!

@Willow K :) As a reformed “hater”, I started making my own based off of a NYTCooking marmalade recipe and, oy boy, yummeee! Love all things marmalade. And I get requests for it! Will use in the cookies. 

Curious about no eggs, and no vanilla in this recipe…anyone? What is holding it together?

I made as written.  I used good orange marmalade but did not get much citrus flavor.  I like the idea of adding citrus to oatmeal cookies so I will try again.  I think white sugar would allow more orange flavor to come through - the molasses in the brown sugar overwhelms.  I also will add orange zest and/or chopped up candied orange peel.

@Scout277

Same. Made these today. Super disappointing. Barely tasted any marmalade at all. I think if I make them again I will at least double it and maybe leave out the other sugar.
@Scout277 @Kate - read your comments before making today. Doubled the marmalade and halved the brown sugar. Super orangey and not too sweet. Came out great!

I really like this recipe! I used homemade Meyer lemon marmalade and added some chopped candied ginger to the batter. I also put little dab of marmalade on top of each ball before baking. I think this helped give a more distinctive marmalade flavor.

@Monika
Would you be willing to share your Meyer lemon marmalade recipe?  Sounds wonderful!  Thanks!
@Chris Miller I was going to ask the same thing!

Check out Nervous Nellies (Deer Isle, ME) Orange Marmalade. In January, they make marmalade base: chopped whole lemons, oranges and grapefruit cooked in a bit of water to soften the rinds. The citrus concoction is poured into 5-gal pails and frozen. Over the next 12 months, they thaw it to make marmalade about once or twice a month. By hand, they de-seed about 1,400 pieces of fruit, chop it into chunks and run it through the Cuisanart and then cook it for a few minutes in the kettle. Delish!

@Namara
Love Nervous Nellie’s & their marmalade! Will make these soon!
@Namara The best thing about Nervous Nellie’s is the drive out there on the Sunshine Road. It’s a sculpture garden and foodie treasure. By all means, buy the marmalade!
@Namara I ADORE Nervous Nellie’s! First came across them 27 years ago on a trip to Deer Isle and have been obsessed ever since! Will absolutely use their marmalade in this recipe.
120g marmalade just a little over the suggested amount and these are very orange-forward and perfect.

I subbed King Arthur Gluten Free flour but followed the recipe as written, using orange marmalade. Wow! These are the perfect sweet. I’m looking forward to trying other variations with other possible add-ins for different seasons. May be crazy- but I’m planning on lemon and basil.

Did you use GF flour due to celiac or GF sensitivity?  If so, did you use Certified GF oats?  I ask because I have celiac with some sensitivity to oats, sometimes even certified GF but I want to try these as I have been over one year without Gluten and am in remission, so perhaps not reactive to certfied GF oats.

@Susan I’m wheat and gluten ‘intolerant’, but not celiacs. I only eat Bob’s Red Mill oats (extra thick!) that are GF certified. I’ve never had a reaction but have from bulk sources, for example. I also use the same, ground in my own blender, for flour. And always use that in my oatmeal cookies as opposed to gf APF.  Again, fully understanding I am not living with celiacs. Hope this is helpful.
@JDM Yes, very helpful and thank you for taking the time to answer my questions.

This was exactly as billed - a quick and easy recipe when you’re hankering for something sweet.  And it’s also the perfect way to use some of the huge jar of Vonbees Honey Citron and Ginger tea marmalade that my husband brought home from Costco many months ago. I’m loving the chewy texture and citrus/oat combo. Will bake these again for sure.

These are delicious and easy to make. I'll definitely do it again. 

Notes:
1. I added orange zest and lemon zest.
2. I added a not insignificant amt of juice squeezed from a very sweet orange. To compensate, I added slightly more oats (a Tablespoon, maybe?) and brown sugar.
3. I took another commenter's advice and refrigerated the batter for a bit to prevent spread. This worked.
4. I took another commenter's advice and put a dollop of orange marmalade on the tops before baking.

Delicious.

Made exactly as written and as someone else noted I was disappointed by the lack of marmalade flavor.  They spread a lot as well so I would definitely refrigerate the batter before baking next time. I would be curious to know if using cane sugar and letting the marmalade infuse it ahead of time would help. I love this recipe in theory but with a little tweaking I think it could be next level.
@Deborahi refrigerated the dough ahead of time and they still spread. Not a winner.

@Deborah. So I just made them again with different brand marmalade, half brown, half cane sugar and refrigerated them for thirty minutes ahead of baking and they are much improved! Still trying for a stronger marmalade taste but definitely a keeper!

@Deborah
I believe that the brown sugar is needed to help with leavening, as the molasses in it reacts with the baking soda, which makes up for the lack of eggs. Or something to that effect. Not positive. Perhaps cutting the brown sugar to 50% and adding white for the difference, or just adding an egg and all white sugar? The food scientists amongst the crowd would know best.
Not being a marmalade fan, I used apricot jam and the cookies were still winners.

I found these cookies to be very disappointing.  I was looking for an orange forward flavor, but there was none. In fact, the only hint of the marmalade was a bitter aftertaste.  I used Bonne Maman which doesn't have a lot of peel.  Perhaps this was the issue.  I wish I had added some orange zest, but I wanted to make as directed.  The other test tasters in my house agreed that this recipe isn't a repeat.

@Sharon Lee Puttmann

The head note does specify a thick marmalade with plenty of peel, I suspect that that was indeed the problem.

I made these in full compliance with the recipe as written with one exception.  I used coconut sugar instead of brown sugar.  I used Dalfour orange marmalade.  Nothing to write home about. BTW, the cookies did not spread at all.  I am now thinking that I make the recipe as I did but I put a thumb print in the middle of the cookie where I place a small spoonful of marmalade.  It is on the calendar for next week!  The pastimes of a retiree.

@Susan
Think that you may need the molasses in the brown sugar to react with the baking soda.

I got excited when I saw an oatmeal marmalade cookie! Made the recipe almost “as is”. Took a suggestion on adding zested orange peel as the flavor was a bit lost with just the marmalade. Cookies came out delicious, crunchy on the edge and chewy in the middle. And best of all they were not super sweet! I did add walnuts, just because I like nuts in my cookies. Super good sweet snack! Will make again.
Sharon Lee Puttmann, Bonne Maman is a French brand (I live in France) that caters to French tastes, which means nothing too strong. Better to use an English brand marmalade.

Delicious!! I love how quickly and easily they come together, and as a marmalade fan, they’re a perfect grab and go breakfast! Think ‘bowl of oatmeal meets toast with marmalade’! Mine did flatten quite a bit so I might try pressing the dough into a 9 x 9 next time, and cutting them into bars.
I added 1/2 cup semi-sweet mini chocolate chips.  The mini ones are perfect - they go great with orange and don’t overpower the other flavors.

I just made these cookies as per the recipe using Bonnie Maman marmalade (not Nervous Nellies tho I know the place and we have several of her husband’s sculptures). The recipe made a good chewy cookie and very easy to make tho a small batch (16 cookies). I would not have known about the marmalade except that I put it in myself.  I love orange and oatmeal. Next time I’d add chopped candied orange. Trader Joe’s has excellent ones.

Made as written, with the exception of adding 1/3 cup of pistachios with the oats & using Measure for Measure GF flour. The buttery nuttiness balanced the marmalade well (used homemade, so it was on the sour side) and roommates could not tell it was gf, just that they wanted to eat them. Huzzah!

Easy peasy, so delicious! One bowl, minimal cleanup, fresh and bright and grrrreat with cold milk. Also, vegan if you use oil instead of butter. Another go to recipe from Melissa the amazing! NB: could prob heat away with half the sugar.
Used Wegmans peach preserves rather than marmalade.  Addictive!

These turned out great.  I added about half a teaspoon of orange zest just because I had some in the freezer.  The cookie was chewy and rich and the orange was subtle.  Very enjoyable.  I'm considering trying other preserves like sour cherry.

@Scout277  I've had good luck with Melissa Clark's raisin oatmeal cookie and just adding the zest of a couple oranges. I ease up on the spices so they don't overpower the orange. I also sub dried cranberries for the raisins.

I agree with several commenters that the orange flavor sort of faded in the baking. So I made some orange icing to spread a thin layer on the cookies and, voila, deliciously orange-y!  And they look good, too!

I love marmalade and orange flavour so wanted to try these.  I followed the recipe exactly (I measure ingredients) and baked them for about 11 mins on a higher rack in the oven.  Mine spread and I found them too dry.  Perhaps I should have baked them at a lower level.
@Fleury Mine, too. I ended up with tuilles.
I really liked these. I added one teaspoon of orange extract based on some of the comments that the cookies didn't have enough orange flavor. Yum! I'll make this cookie again.

Granted, I haven’t read all of the comments yet, but I’m surprised that so far I haven’t run across the suggestion to add chocolate chips to this recipe. Citrus and chocolate? Need I say more? Based on the comments I did read, I added a bit more flour and a bit more oatmeal to the recipe (no more than 25-30 grams each) to give this a more solid cookie batter. And I added 2/3 c dark chocolate chips. I hope to spring the results on my husband when he gets home, as he does not love marmalade. Based on my sampling, he will taste some bitterness. However, in my books and with a couple of tweaks, this recipe is a hit!

Made half-batch waiting for Lunar Eclipse at 6 am here in Maine. Sadly, the moon dropped below the tree line. But the cookies were delicious! Plenty of orange flavor with Trader Joe's Seville Orange marmalade. I did add a handful of unsalted, roasted pistachios, in chopped,  which added crunch.
Easy one bowl recipe. My cookies spread flat but the flavor was good. Next time I will chill a bit before baking.

Loved this cookie! I read all the comments and added the zest of 4 tangerines (had them on hand...orange zest would be fine, of course). Also was inexplicably out of butter (horrors!!!) so I subbed 6 T. mild olive oil for the 8 T. butter. I might add 1/2 tsp. vanilla extract next time, for fun. I'm not saying you must make them my way, just observing that a few tweaks, depending on what you have on hand, are fun and this cookie can take it! Gifted some to my landlady -- she raved, wanted more!
I wonder how these would taste with Fig Marmalade?

@Kathie Browne
Sounds delicious!
@Kathie Browne I am wondering the same thing, we have a fig-orange spread I want to use. I will report back!
@Kathie Browne I used Dalmatia fig spread and zested a clementine, they are delicious and I will make them again. I’ll do 2 clementines next time.

I can only find Bonne Maman marmalade near me. It sounds from the comments like Bonne Maman doesn’t have enough flavor for this recipe. I am thinking about adding some orange bitters to the marmalade for more flavor. Has anyone tried this? Will also cut sugar and add extra marmalade to top like some other comments suggest.

@Debby I am coming back to say I added 1/2 teaspoon orange bitters to my Bonne Maman marmalade and a dollop of marmalade on top of each cookie. They tasted great! Otherwise I made this according to the recipe, using a scale, and I chilled the dough before baking. My cookies spread despite that. I can’t figure out  why.

Made these cookies with Fortnum’s Burlington Breakfast marmalade and they were delicious. It has good-sized shreds of thick-ish peel, which I didn’t bother cutting down. The shreds that were exposed became caramelized and were extra tasty. I had hesitated about using the last of our fancy, imported pre-tariff marmalade as an ingredient. However, after opening a jar of standard supermarket marmalade (Bonne Maman), it was clear that would be a bad choice: it has very little peel in it.

Made this with Smuckers Sweet Orange Marmalade, dark brown sugar and added 1 TBSP of grated orange zest. Absolutely delicious! Even better the next morning. Will add a thumbprint dab of marmalade on top next time per other reviewers suggestions. Took some over to two neighbors. Both texted later asking for the recipe.
I subbed in lemon marmalade because that's what I had on hand, and added toasted, chopped almonds - absolutely delicious and so easy!

I was concerned about the marmalade flavour not shining, so I substituted white sugar for brown. I also put a small (less than 1 tsp) dallop of marmalade on the top of each cookie. Heavenly! Don't know if the sugar sub actually made a difference (I've never made these with brown), but the cookies are delicious.

Delish! Knew soon as I combine the marmalade with the sugar, etc., but I wasn’t gonna get those streaks of marmalade as pictured so when I added the flower and rolled oats and had stirred a couple of times, I added a few more dollop of marmalade and barely mixed in. Got the desired outcome, as well as some nice concentrated hits of orange. And result was a touched too sweet with the additional marmalade, next time may reduce the brown sugar by a tablespoon. Looking forward to making these super easy and lovely cookies again!!

Used orange marmalade and added golden raisins.  Sprinkled the top with McCormack's spiced orange finishing sugar (Demerara sugar, nutmeg, clove, dried orange) and they were out of this world.  Family requested - demanded? - a second batch the same day. Keeper.
Utterly delicious. No changes. I used my mum's lemon, ginger and gin marmalade. A stunningly simple recipe.

These are delicious.  First batch I did as instructed and super good.  The second batch I followed a readers suggestion and made a little hollow in the top of each cookie and put a dollop of marmalade.  Highly suggest doing this as the cookies are soooooo pretty and extra fun with the jammy  top

These are great! As usual, the comments are so helpful, thank you to everyone. When looking for marmalade today, I found some Mackay's Dundee orange marmalade, a lonely looking jar next to the Bonne Maman, and I decided to give it a try. I used light brown sugar, instead of dark, the zest of an orange, a small dab of the marmalade in the center of each cookie before baking. I  Used less sugar, and that gorgeous bittersweet marmalade, is just what I needed.. Its been a long winter,
These are fabulous!  I added a little orange zest and chocolate chips.  Absolute winner!

These are nice buttery cookies with a delicate marmalade flavor. I used home made Seville orange marmalade and added a lot of Valencia orange zest but wished for a little more tang. I'll make them again because they're truly a nice cookie, just lacking the punch I love about a good marmalade. I'll reduce the sugar next time and see if that helps.

Just baked these cookies from heaven!!  Easy to put together and I too love marmalade but honestly, any jammy fruit would do so I’ll be trying all kinds soon!!  Love that there are no eggs!  They baked well and turned out quit perfect without much effort!!  I added dark chocolate chips for the heck of it and again, heaven in a cookie:)!!!
I added some chopped bittersweet chocolate. Perfection.
Made exactly as written. Delicious! Next time I’ll add orange zest as noted by others.
Delicious and easy! I like the comments, and might add chocolate chips next time.

Made as written the first time. Was hoping for more orange flavor. 2nd batch: added some italian candied orange peel in a dice, orange extract about 1/2 tsp and who does not love chocolate and orange, some semi sweet choc. chunks and a swirl of extra marmalade into the completely mixed batter with a knife. Wow!!! They are now flavor bombs of my favorite combo, chocolate and orange. My family really liked them before and now, they are "Marry Me Cookies"! Lol!

Tried it with apricot cardamon jam as I didn't have marmalade. 10/10 delicious and so easy to make with basic pantry ingredients, just swap the marmalade for whatever (ideally chunky) jam you have. I'm gonna try with marmalade, cherry or black currant next time.

doubling the marmalade an subbing the sugar for honey is the way to go.  since marmalades can vary in sweetness, start from double the marmalade and test the marmalade mix for sweetness - it may not need anything more.  in my case, i added a heaping tablespoon of honey and it was enough.  followed the rest of the instructions/ingredients and outlined.

I used to make a similar recipe with my grandmother called "spicy four o'clocks"... so fun to see them pop up here! They are worth trying even if, like me, you don't like marmalade, since the flavour is quite mellow. Marmalade lovers might find them a bit tame, though.
I made them as written and found them to be delicious!!! Love the marmalade flavor with the brown sugar- nice and chewy! Will make again!
I used Bob’s Red Mill 1:1 Gluten Free flour and added 1 tablespoon of orange liquor, and these were delicious

Just made these as written with my homemade blood orange marmalade, as I had a couple of extra small jars. Quick and delicious! I thought the color would show up more, but the marmalade tinted the whole cookie rose-colored so the bits don't stand out as much. Delightful flavor - probably a little different from standard orange marmalade.
I made with 110 g of not especially sweet home made marmalade and 90 g dark brown sugar. Overall good but much too sweet. Next time I would use 45 g sugar.
Is there a good jam substitute for orange Marmalade?  Asking for a friend.
When I weigh the rolled oats, it measures 1 cup instead of the 1 1/2 cups in the recipe. I went with the weight.
@MariaMaria did your cookies spread? I used weight (and refrigerated the dough) and my cookies spread. I wonder if this is why?

I made them GF with King Arthur Measure for Measure and Bob's Red Mill certified GF oats (necessary if you are considering baking these for a celiac friend:).  they are so easy and quick to make, and incredibly delicious!  I barely had enough marmalade, but I agree with previous post that I would add a dollop on top next time during baking for more citrus flavor.  This will become my go to.  Note to GF bakers: you have to let them rest on the sheet pan before trying to transfer them to a wire rack.  They'll firm up a little bit more and then you can transfer them easily.
I can’t believe how easy this recipe is. Made these as written. i did chill the dough before baking. Delicious! I can see how whichever marmalade you use will change the flavor.

I really enjoyed these cookies and thought they were delicious. Easy to make, no special equipment needed. I had lemon marmalade to use up, and it worked well. I added the zest of one lemon and I think that’s what it needed (my marmalade didn’t have much peel so I think the citrus flavor would have been lost without it.)

I just made these and I am in love with this recipe. So quick and easy and yummy! We have a different idea of what a heaping Tbl is so I got 18 cookies - not 24. They are very soft when you pull them from the oven, but just let them set for a few minutes and they will crisp up.
these are delicious. I added zest from one clementine and about 30 grams of candied orange peel. I think the citrus flavour would have been to subtle otherwise
Ugh.  After reading comments, I added two tablespoons of orange zest.  The cookies spread like crazy and then fell apart, even though they seemed to be baked through.  Not a keeper
I found this recipe to have too much baking soda for the amount of oats and flour. Unfortunately this affected the taste. If I make again I will cut to at least 3/4 t.

Followed recipe as written.  Any flavor of orange was almost undetectable, so I put a small dab of marmalade on each cookie as soon as they came out of the oven.  Next time I ll try adding a tablespoon of orange zest to the batter

This might be my first review comment ever: WONDERFUL! Thank you for this one,
Batter was thick and easy to spoon out, maybe because I softened the butter in a microwave (not melted to liquid).

Unbelievably delicious and so easy - I've made this FOUR TIMES in four days (not good! Or, too good!). Made it exactly per the recipe the first two times; next two, added toasted pecan pieces (about 2/3 cup). Love the 'bendiness' and chew, and the brown sugar complementing the marmalade (used Bon Maman). So good. Total keeper of a recipe.
This recipe was love at first sight, so much so that I went out to buy oranges and made my own marmalade. The cookies didn’t let me down. They’re delicious!
Delicious, easy and quick to make only addition was orange zest.
I’m going to try the recipe as written, but will rub some fresh zest in light brown sugar.

Made these with fig jam in lieu of the marmalade.  I've had questionable outcomes with bitter marmalade that costs the earth, so I went safe with fig jam.  Surprisingly not that sweet, which I liked.  Good texture:  chewy in the middle with a slight bite on the surface.  Might attempt this with candied ginger and/or chopped dried figs in the future.  4 out of 5 stars.

Got the munchies and didn’t have anything around and then remembered this recipe from a few days back. I didn’t have marmalade so I substituted a jar of guava jam…. Mmmmm tasty!! Even my wife who doesn’t like cookies was enamored! Think I’ll stick to the guava… I did use 3/4 of a cup versus the 1/2 cup… maybe a full cup next batch.
Made these with Earl Grey Meyer Lemon marmalade by V Smiley, a local jam maker here in Vermont. Just the right bit of chewy, sweet, and slightly tart…I’ll be making these again!

I baked these pans at 11 and 12 minutes; at 11 minutes, they didn't hold together well coming off the pan. Eventually I used a spatula under the cookies and a table knife to push them off. As with others, I didn't catch much orange flavor, though I added orange extract. I think candied orange peel would bring more orange flavor, if you can re-balance for the lost moisture of the marmalade.

Tasty, but not special.
Hi I used what I had- mixed berry preserves and added some chopped walnuts. Cookies were great.

Made according to recipe  using marquis flour from Barton Springs Mill and La dalfour  marmalade.    They do spread and next time I will chill as others suggest.  I added pecans (I’m from TX and we love pecans in cookies).   They are much better on the second day.   Will do again …

I'm curious why the Cook Time mentioned at the top of the recipe is 20 minutes, but in Step 4  it says the time is 9-13 minutes. Quite a difference between 9 minutes on the short end and 20 minutes on the long end. I found it confusing, but just had to wing it. I am enjoying the cookies. Wish I would have read the comment about making a thumbprint in them to put some more marmalade into. Good suggestion. I'll try that next time. Thank you.
@Alison, the time at the top includes ingredient assembly and mixing, and the 9-13 minutes is just the baking. I hope that helps!

Added about a tablespoon of orange zest to the sugar as well as chopped dark chocolate with the oats and flour. Really fantastic results but I may top with turbinado sugar (or add some semi/milk choc) next time as they needed just a hint more sweetness imo.

Amped up the orange quite a bit: rubbed brown sugar with orange zest; added scant teaspoon of orange extract to marmalade batter (only because I happened to have it); chopped candied orange or dried mandarins (Trader Joe’s) soaked in 1 or 2 T Grand Marner or triple sec and add to batter at the end. Then pinky-poked the rolled cookies and filled with a tiny dribble of marmalade before baking. The minimal extra effort was worth it. Easy and delish! I’m now 2 batches in.

So easy and tasty. I used leftover strawberry jam from making hamentashen last weekend. I imagine any flavor of jam would be nice. Otherwise I followed the recipe exactly as written. I made dough balls that were 25 grams and had them in the oven for 10 minutes 30 seconds. Great for breakfast or with coffee or tea. My husband crumbled some and ate them with Greek yogurt!
Tastes like an elementary-grade construction project… with sugar. I even added c. 1/8 tsp of orange extract. I will try one more time but will overload with marmalade and add a 1/4 tap of extract.

Used white flour, as suggested, and added the rind of an orange. Did not melt butter, rather creamed room temperature (not melted) butter, sugar, and marmalade as done in traditional baking. Folded in rest of ingredients. Turned out perfectly with just the right amount of crunch, chewiness and orange flavor!
I found this too sweet. I used a traditional bitter Scottish marmalade, but still the taste was subdued and overwhelmed by sugar. American style sweet marmalade would be even sweeter.

Followed the advice from others, increased the amount of marmalade and reduced the sugar to 70 grams. I also added some chopped almonds. The cookies came out tasty and chewy. Definitely making this again.

Baked these yesterday for a dinner party, they came out amazing. I used Ikea Marmalade, which has lots of peel but is very finely minced, so the orange flavor was more subdued. I baked them longer so they came out pleasantly crunchy, like store-bought iced oatmeal cookies. I also modified the recipe slightly to make a chocolate chip version for people who wouldn't like the orange. Subbed an egg for the marmalade and added 1/2 C chocolate chips. Came out cakier than the orange, but in a good way
I put orange zest in as well to make the citrus pop out a bit more.

Made a change, since hubby can’t have butter - I used 6T lime-infused olive oil. Must have done the trick, since two of them evaporated from the cooling rack while I was out of the room. So easy and delicious, this will be on permanent rotation!

Fantastic and going straight to my keepers. Full of marmalade flavour, nicely chewy and crunchy at the edges. I used Roses Sweet Orange Marmalade - thick and full of peel, and added zest of 2 oranges as I was juicing them for another recipe. For the Aussies and NZers out there, it’s very nearly an Anzac biscuit with marmalade subbing for golden syrup, and more oats / no coconut. Used mostly white caster sugar simply because I ran out of brown. I know from Anzac biscuits it’s very forgiving this way. Next time will try the brown.

Used Bonne Maman marmalade and noticed that the bulk of the zest sat toward the middle and bottom of the jar.  Set aside the top "jelly" and went in for the middle.  Returned jelly to the jar to use for a chicken glaze.  Also added the coarse zest of a large orange (rubbed into the sugar) and used sprouted oats. Probably the quickest and easiest cookie I've ever made.  Lots of texture and flavor for little time and effort.  Once again, Melissa scores.

These were lacking in flavor and texture. They didn’t taste like marmalade and they were crumbly and not chewy. They tasted more like a dry breakfast bar and not a chewy cookie. They also need more sugar. I know it’s considered sophisticated to put less sugar in desserts these days, but these really need it. And the melted butter made these cookies tough to scoop, so this recipe could use a note to chill the dough before scooping.
These are a huge hit in my house.  Make them exactly as the recipe is written - super easy and quick to mix up.  Delicious.
Took these to a meeting. All were gone in 20 minutes. Great recipe.
These are really good and easy to make.  The only changes I made were to bake them on Silpats instead of parchment, and I added 1/4 tsp of King Arthur Fiori di Sicilia to amp up the flavor.
Maybe try yuzu marmalade?!
@Jerri. I used Yuzu honey tea  from HMart ( looks and tastes like marmalade.) Like many others we added the zest of one orange. Chewy and delicious!
This is THE worst cookie recide I have baked  in 50 years! Bland, dry, not a hint of citrus. A waste of good homemade marmalade.
Just made this morning and it is perfect

Question: if I want a crunchy version of this , what changes should I make?
*use caster sugar rather than soft brown?
* remove baking powder & use 1 teaspoon bicarbonate of soda?
*ground some of the oats to flour?
* adjust temperature or cooking times?
You seem like the people to ask

Made these as written and they did not disappoint. Came together really quick and they are addictive. I used a good quality marmalade that was not too sweet and more citrus forward and I was generous with my measurement. I like the idea of adding more candied peel or ginger next time.

I thought these would spread, similar to cowboy cookies, but they puffed up and were thicker than I would have liked.  I used an orange fig jam that I had purchased to use with a cheese plate, and the cookies lacked the bright citrus they needed.  As made, our family strongly prefers the cowboy cookie for an oatmeal cookie option.

I liked these cookies and followed the recipe using English Tiptree Orange Marmalade, which is thick and flavorful.  I added orange zest to make sure the orange flavor came through, and it did.  The cookies spread a bit too much, but I am at a high altitude and might tweek the recipe for that next time.
These were pretty good! Nice subtle orange flavor. They spread a little more than I was expecting while baking, so I might try them again and chill the dough before baking.
This has very good flavor, but next time I will mix the marmalade in with the just-melted butter. It will be easier to mix then. Nice recipe for this cold and rainy weather, as promised.
I love marmalade, and will try any recipe that includes it.  But you couldn't taste the marmalade (I used Bonne Maman). They turned out very dry.

Made a half batch - 8 x 30g. Used wholemeal flour, reduced sugar by 1/3. Added a large tablespoon or so of currants, vanilla, ground ginger, cardamom and a good amount of cinnamon. Refrigerated formed cookies for about an hour before baking. Slightly overdid the bake time at 17.5 minutes, but still good. Next time would bake for 16 minutes. Marmalade was one made by my sister-in-law, and quite bitter so cookies did not taste over sweet nor need extra peel. Happy husband. :-)

Good recipe with a browned butter flavor. Really needs thick cut marmalade as stated — I made with a thin cut, nearly shred less, and even adding zest, wasn’t as good as I think thick cut marmalade would be. Will try again with recommended thick cut.

I made the as-is + zest from one navel orange & a generous squeeze of its juice. after dropping the dough onto the trays, i popped them into the freezer for about 5 minutes before putting into the preheated oven. They came out deliciously soft and jammy in the middle with a little crunch on the outside. The kept their shape nicely while baking. Wonderful flavor!

Made exactly as written - came out perfect. Used a very chunky (peel-heavy) homemade calamondin marmalade. Did *not* chill dough and cookies held their shape. Easiest and fastest cookie recipe I’ve ever made. Delicious.
Double the recipe and add a bag of Trader Joe’s dark chocolate chips - so good

Excellent and quick cookies!  My heaping tablespoon gave me 19 cookies.  I admit that I overbaked these slightly so they came out a bit tough.  But the butter flavor came through really well and so did the marmalade flavor, which is nice because I used a generic store bought marmalade.  I will definitely make these again and I’ll watch the baking time more carefully next time.

Easy, quick, tasty cookies! No need to bring things to room temp. Simple ingredients. No need to chill the dough. I used 365 brand marmalade, but looking forward to trying it with others. I made the recipe as iis with the exception of adding some candied yuzu peel that I had on hand. Next time I might add some cinnamon, ginger or nuts but those things aren’t necessary to make a good cookie.

ok,these are so good!  the marmalade isn't dominant, but it adds a sophistication to this otherwise common cookie!  made it exactly as written. using a tbsp cookie scoop I got 20 cookies.  Will definitely try it with other marmalade, ginger or lemon or grapefruit.  This is going into my rotation for sure.

I sure wish we could post pictures of our results! I used a scant half cup of Stonewall Kitchen Blood Orange Marmalade, zest of an orange, and a half cup of white chocolate chips. Oh, and I added a dab of marmelade per another's suggestion. Yummy! These bordered on too sweet (to me) with my additions, but my husband liked them a lot and said they were "fancy."
i made these and although they had a good texture, the flavor was kind of bland, would try the zest addition as per another reviewer or even the thumbprint method but not a spectacular cookie.

Made exactly as written with Bonne Maman marmalade. Like others have said, the orange flavor was very subtle. Only the bitterness came through. Also, I found them too sweet. Took the whole 13 minutes to bake, but the texture was perfect. I'd be willing to try again, with more orange, less sugar, as they were so fast and easy!
Excellent and all gone!
great recipe! easy, quick and delicious! I used gluten-free flour and oats and  they came out great. might be my new favourite cookie.

Husband can’t have butter so used oil. Added zest of two oranges & scant 1/4 t orange extract. Upped marmalade to 105g.  Finally, threw in about 1.5T browned milk powder (trying to get some butter flavor in there. Made 25 cookies. Very orangey & chewy but think I’ll reduce sugar slightly next time.   Maybe add some almond flour. Very forgiving recipe.

I love citrus—especially orange—and oatmeal cookies, so this seemed like the perfect blend. The cookies were chewy and delicious but spread more than I expected, and the orange flavor was milder than I’d hoped. I melted the butter with the sugar and marmalade; next time I’ll let the butter cool a bit before mixing in the sugar, baking soda, and powder, which may help with the spreading. I’ll also add extra orange zest to bring out more of that bright citrus flavor. 🍊🍪
Gone in 5 minutes!  Made exactly as written.

I made it with plain Jane grocery store brand marmalade and still found these to be a hit! I thought they spread too much, but they are a great crispy-chewy combo. I will, however, let the melted butter cool off more before mixing next time, or chill the batter.

As some of the other subscribers had suggested I added a dollop of marmalade on top but the bites without the dollop had plenty of marmalade flavor. I used a strong Italian marmalade, candied ginger, and walnuts because I like them. I'll make these again.
Did anyone else have trouble with weight measurements?  I ended up with a thin batter.  Had to add 1/4 cup oats.

Mine came out nearly perfect.  I put my marmalade into a food processor to thin it out and I think that wasn't the way to go but they were still good.  My were so thick they wouldn't spread in the oven so I pulled them out and smashed them with a fork ala peanut butter cookies. Try them again, they are great!
I added minced orange peel from a blood orange, a quarter tsp of orange extract and a cup or more of macadamia nuts.  Excellent cookie.
Sea salt made them way too salty! Couldn't eat them.
Used organic orange tangerine marmalade and King Arthur GF flour.  Super easy to throw together.

I had one sheet of large/thick ones and a small sheet of smaller/thinner ones.  The larger took longer to bake, like 15-16 minutes but the smaller only took 9 minutes for edges to brown.  But because I put them on the lowest rack of my oven, the bottoms also got quite brown, almost burnt, but they tasted great anyway.  1/2way thru baking I pulled both out to smash with a fork. Very good cookies!

I followed other commenters and made a small divot in the top of the cookies and spooned a little marmalade into that spot. Also added 1/2  t. of grated orange peel. Might add more next time. Used a 1 T. scoop, which resulted in 32 cookies.  Cooked for 13 minutes, one sheet at a time. Such an EASY recipe - make again.

I added orange zest to the recipe, substituted half white sugar and brown sugar. Marmalade had chunks of peel. This produced an orange forward flavor with soft consistency. Delicious and my family consumed the entire batch in an hour.

Loved this cookies! I added some chopped chocolate to half the batch (couldn’t help myself) and they were delicious, though my husband preferred the ones without chocolate. I love marmalade so upped the amount. I also swapped in half a cup of oat flour.
Delicious and quick!  I doubled the marmalade and only had 6 T. butter in the house. Turned out perfect and did not spread. Next time will add orange zest punch up the flavor.

My husband doesn’t like marmalade, but liked these. Very reminicent of old fashioned lace cookies. Used Bon Maman marmalade. Also added 1/4 t cardamom (because I like it with orange).  Used the wrong size scoop, so only got 18 cookies. Which might not be a bad thing, because they won’t be tempting us for long!  

Great cookie to make when you have to whip something together quickly. Think it would be delicious crumbled over plain or vanilla yogurt for an impromptu dessert.
I'm curious as to why these don't call for an egg--whereas other oatmeal cookies do.

I just made these cookies in two sizes: tablespoon and teaspoon size.  The cookies do spread, so the tablespoon sized cookies are pretty large.  The teaspoon size are better I believe for a buffet table as they are not a big “commitment.”  They are very good. Mine were almost lacy. Maybe I should chill the cookies prior to baking so the middle is more of a “belly,” but they are nicely chewy — and crispy on the edges, so a very nice texture and taste. I used the light brown sugar so the orange flavor would come through better.  Nice recipe and a fun choice for a group.
I haven't made these yet but I was wondering about no egg in the recipe.  I would have thought an egg would be needed to "bind" the dry with the butter and marmalade.

I made these yesterday following the recipe exactly. I made a few test cookies first and found them to be a little bland. The balance of the dough, I added chopped pecans and cinnamon. That gave a bit more flavor. The orange flavor didn't come through very well so next time I will add orange zest as well. Good basic cookie.

I made this recipe exactly as written and my cookies really spread.  Some commenters are suggesting refrigeration if the batter before baking.  I would like to hear what the creator Melissa Clark has to say. My cookies sure didn’t look like the photo.

I made the recipe today and had to substitute orange marmalade with peach preserves. I made it exactly as directed and the cookies didn’t grow up very well and I was disappointed. I thought they’d be better.
First i thought its gonna be dry, but it was perfect, chewy and tastes really healthy. Will definitely recommend this.

These taste great…I used an Italian mandarin marmalade which I sourced from Caputos deli in SLC.  Pretty strong marmalade and you taste it.  Also added peel of 2 small mandarins .  I really like the flavour but these wouldn’t really hold together on the pan…they did melt together in the oven but are paper thin.  Not sure the tweak to get them to be a bit thicker.  I put it in the freezer for 30 min to chill the dough on the suggestion of others.

I’ve made these several times now and we’ve always enjoyed them but I think our favorite version was using Tassos orange & ginger marmalade. For those who enjoy ginger, it’s an easy way to add some ginger flavor.
I added almonds for crunch, some cinnamon, vanilla, and orange zest.

These were so delicious! I used homemade orange marmalade which I bet made them even better. I also added a little dollop of marmalade on top for some extra marmalade flavor. Perhaps a new go-to cookie recipe for me!

Mixed 1T breakfast earl grey loose tea into melted butter, let cool 10 mins. Used 75 grams brown sugar, added zest of 1/2 Meyer lemon to wet mix. Subbed Small Batch Jam Co Earl Grey Bergamot Marmalade (Pacifica, CA) instead of orange. Added 1/4 cup shredded coconut to 100 g flour-125 g  oat mix. Used 1.5 oz scoop for 18 cookies, and very slightly flattened. Baked 12 mins, turning sheets 1/2 way through bake. These cookies are SO good; just sweet enough! Held together perfectly. Will make again!

These are amazing! If you want a quick sweet treat, these are bomb you can use any kind of jam jelly whatever… But I use protein oatmeal so that at least it has a little something something healthy, besides a stick of butter and brown sugar, lol but this is really really good and I highly recommend. Could probably switch out brown sugar for food processed dates and or maybe coconut sugar, even if you wanted to use a plant base, but I’m not sure how well it melts but these are amazing and I highly recommend
Excellent! Even though I used room temp butter, cherry jam, and added vanilla. Riffable and extremely delicious!!!!

title: Spicy Marmalade Chicken and Sweet Potatoes Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: Wow, this was so very tasty! We use boneless-skinless thighs and reduced cooking time to 30 minutes. Then we put in under the broiler for 5 minutes to make things crispy. The squeeze of lime when serving is a perfect addition. I want to try adding some shallots or red onion next time because I think they would be great with sauce. The parchment paper got too wet. Foil or nothing next time. Thank you for another wonderful recipe Melissa Clark!

@Jim thank you for actually reviewing the recipe as drafted! I wish all  comments were this helpful.  It’s hard to find the valuable feedback in the midst of all the people rewriting a recipe they’ve never made.

@Jim - thanks for this - I also used boneless skinless thighs and this approach worked great! I made this twice - first as-is and the second time I added a little bit of ginger paste from the tube to the chicken marinade mix- great both times!!
Followed the recipe exactly & after 20 minutes my house is full of smoke & chicken skin is black!

Marmalades most commonly comprise about 60% sugar, some a significantly more, and some tarter brands a bit less. That's a high concentration of sugar.  And sugar quickly scorches in high heat, so I was surprised to see the oven temp set at 425,   But not surprised to hear of your chicken's black skin.

When I make a chicken thigh rub that includes brown sugar, I never bake at above 325. This gives just the right amount of caramelization and crispiness for the time it takes to cook the chicken.

425 degrees for a marinade/coating that is about 60% sugar is why your chicken skin is black and your house is full of smoke.  Sugar scorches quickly at such a high temperature. Any rub/marinade/coating that has that much sugar needs to be baked at something more like 325 degrees - the temperature at which caramelization takes place.

@CFXK 

I'm glad I read your comment. I came here from an email where Melissa is talking about making homemade marmalade and what to do with it. I would imagine homemade marmalade has less sugar and is better suited to this recipe. Unfortunately, this recipe doesn't mention anything about homemade marmalade despite also being written by Melissa.

I cooked this last night using a whole 5lb supermarket chicken that I cut into 8 portions. I started it out at 375° then turned the oven up to 425° after 20 minutes. The total cooking time was about 40 minutes and the skin was lightly charred - just right! ( I used Yukon Golds because we're not fond of sweet potatoes)
I added some ginger to the marinade
Sheet pan cooking seems to have become a trend, but this type of chicken dish is better cooked to perfection in a Pyrex dish. Less sticking and burning. And Pyrex is easier to clean.
I love cooking in Pyrex. Not only is it easier to clean; it goes nicely from oven to table too.

@Judy Weinstein
Agree! I learned the hard….and baked on..,way
Seems like a great way to ruin a sheet pan

This was one of the most flavorful meals I've ever made. The sweet potatoes were so tasty. Having read the comments about the burning sugar in the marinade, I started the sweet potatoes at 425 degrees for 10 minutes, then added the chicken and turned the temperature down to 350 degrees for 50 more minutes for perfect chicken. No other changes made to the recipe.

Coincidentally, I was just given some home-made lime marmalade from one of my neighbors. Thighs I had were boneless & skinless, but added extra olive oil and some duck fat to compensate. It's a weird recipe, but we liked it. Acid, sweet, umami.. do not skip the fish sauce. Paired it with some roasted romanesco. Miranda chose a mild red to go with it and I think she was right.
@Duke Ferris Are we supposed to know who Miranda is?

I made this exactly as written and it was lovely. By the time the chicken has nice crunchy skin, the sauce will scorch (this is a feature, not a bug). I lined my pan carefully with aluminum foil, but there were still patches of scorched sauce on the pan by the time it was done. These were later eradicated by pouring some water into the pan, heating on the stove top, and stirring until they dissolved. I would not change a thing.

I spread salted, peppered bone in skin on chicken thighs  covered in paprika and apricot jam at 375 for 45 minutes and then reduce to 350 for another 60 minutes. I marinate it initially in rice wine vinegar, soy sauce, brown sugar, sesame oil and cardamom. This sounds delightful using marmalade. Chunk onions and Yukons and place them in your roasting pan. Then the chicken thighs. No need to cover- just watch. Tender, juicy and utterly divine.
I’ve made apricot (marmalade) chicken since my college days.  It was always a great hit!  Please check your oven temperature.  I never had burnt skin, just slightly charred in places.

Very delicious and slightly unusual flavor combination. Loved it! Used orange marmalade, and might go a bit heavier on the red pepper flakes next time. Agree with a prior comment that it gave up more liquid than expected. I think this was a bonus. Just watch out toward the last few minutes of cook time to make sure the skin isn’t burning. Caught ours in time.

After reading all of the comments, I used Polaner Sugar Free Apricot Preserves instead of marmalade. I had 4 large chicken leg quarters, about 5 lbs., so I doubled the amount of sauce and marinated the chicken for 30 minutes, then baked it with potatoes at 375 degrees, basting every 20 minutes.  I checked the meat temp, and total cooking time was 90 minutes.  The chicken was so flavorful and juicy, and the skin was crisp and a lovely golden brown.
The potatoes were excellent but the meat was so-so. I used thighs and only marinated them for 30 minutes. I think they would have been better if I’d marinated them overnight.
Wouldn't the small potato chunks need less time or at most the same time as the bone in chicken thighs?  Why start them before the chicken?
I agree

My husband and I enjoyed this. I used some Trader Joe’s Sri Lankan mango chutney mixed with orange marmalade. I also added a tablespoon of gochujang for heat and Dijon mustard to cut the sweetness. Followed all other directions. 

I turned my gas oven to 400 instead of 425 and some of the pieces still got black… not sure why. I used Japanese sweet potatoes, which were delicious—crispy and creamy at the same time.

I added some turmeric and jerk seasoning to the potatoes. A delicious meal and it made enough for two nights. I would make again,  I just wasn’t thrilled about the super charred chicken.
@Tara I’ve made this several times with marmalade but tried tonight using only mango chutney., which I blended until almost pureed. It was delicious.
I solved the scorch problem by starting the thighs skin down and turning after 20 minutes.
Potatoes 425 for 10, add chicken 350 for 40. Also add paprika and some cayenne

It has potential, but there was not enough glaze and it scorched on top. I want to finish my jar of marmalade, but will cut the amount of chicken and potatoes to no more than 1 pound of each, and will lower the oven temperature.
@Judy Weinstein I was going to ask the same. I have a few nice rectangular casserole dishes, including pyrex, which could go straight to the table. What is this fixation with sheet pans?

Made this tonight. Followed exactly except:
- added an extra tablespoon of marmalade (Bonne Maman)-  
- 1 1/2 tsp gochujang instead of red pepper flakes 
- mushed the garlic with coarse salt into a paste for the marinade
- used 4 skin on, bone-in thighs and 2 smaller bone-in breasts (2 1/4 lbs total). Added all pieces at the same time, cooked 25 minutes 
- cooked in a 9x13 shallow baking dish
Everything was very tasty, not too sweet, chicken was moist, clean up was no issue with a little Barkeepers Friend.
I’ll make again!

Made this exactly as written and it was perfect - nothing scorched, and it was done right on time.  I was initially worried that it would be too sweet but actually the marinade/ sauce ultimately has enough soy sauce, fish sauce, olive oil and lime that the marmalade is a relatively small proportion and it comes out really well-balanced.

We made this delicious dish for dinner this night using lemon marmalade and boneless skinless chicken thighs. An absolute hit. But the leftovers! Just finished a glorious salad, this dish cold and served over a bed of green salad greens. Easily the best thing we’ve eaten this month and fabulous as the weather warms up. A lovely break from the ordinary and pretty enough for company.
The lemon version sounds amazing, Allie!  I will definitely give it a try.  I did the same with my leftovers -- tossed with greens for an amazing next day lunch.  Next time lets double the recipe!

Used chicken breasts (b/s, marinated, all I had on hand), cooked as directed (sweet potatoes first, followed by beastie) at 350 degrees. Will double the amount of sweet potato next time - those were hoovered up in a flash. Echo the sentiments of others, do not skip the fish sauce or the lime.
Not getting much of the "spicy" part and I added a mounded half tsp red pepper. The fish sauce was too forward for my taste. The sweet potatoes were a bigger hit than the chicken.
I actually used boneless skinless chicken breasts, pounded thin, and baked for less time and this was delish. I may have used more red pepper flakes because of my personal taste. Was a hit.
The cooking time seems very long and rather hot for a sugary marinade. I will try this, but I will cook it at 350 and likely covered as well.

As a vegetarian, I usually skip such recipes but all the ingredients here (besides the chicken) spoke to me! I made this for my husband (with chicken) last night and he loved it! Said it's worthy of making for  company. Now I am going to adapt it for myself! Thanks.
Some marmalades, such as St. Dalfour brand, are made entirely from fruit and juice, without added sugar, and will no char nor smoke at 435°.

Wow! This recipe is delicious and so easy to make! I made it as directed but used boneless/skinless chicken thighs with a 25minute cook time and it was perfect. So tasty, the potatoes were perfectly caramelized, and the chicken was moist and delicious. Next time I’ll try to marinade the night before. I’ll definitely be making this again.

The easiest to clean by far is a ceramic-coated sheet pan. Just like what MC is using in the photo. Things don't stick to them either.  Use dish soap and a sponge (no abrasives!!). I've had my cheap full and half size ceramic sheet pans for several years now, and they've held up fine, even under high temperatures & broiling. They may warp a bit under high temp, but return to their normal shape on cooling. The small warp doesn't affect cooking.

I had everything but the marmalade… to get this made in time for dinner, I used a can of whole berry cranberry sauce.  It was moist, sauce was caramelized and only the very edges of the sheet pan were too dark to serve …delicious and got two thumbs up from three generations at the table.  We served it with brown rice and broccoli and there were no leftovers.

This was very good but when I make again, I will not add the additional 2T of olive oil to the marmalade marinade. With skin on, bone in chicken, I personally felt that it made the marinade too oily. I did not have problems with the baking temperature as others have noted.

After quieting my smoke alarms from the scorching marinade, this recipe was definitely a let down. While no expert, it seemed like this should’ve been baked at lower temp for longer period so marinade did not burn but nevertheless I did not care for taste and won’t make again.
did anyone try it with chicken breasts?

I noticed a few comments asking for suggestions to swap for sweet potatoes.  I made this past weekend with shallots (peeled/quartered), fennel bulb (1/2" wedges), and cauliflower florets, and halved brussels sprouts.  It all went together really well and complimented the sauce nicely.  I had 3.5 lbs boneless skinless thighs, and split that and the vegetables amongst 3 sheet pans, and rotated the oven shelves every 15-20 minutes.

Swapped a couple bricks of extra firm tofu in for the chicken. Used coconut aminos in place of fish sauce. As a trial, I cooked half on a parchment lined sheet pan and one without. I recommend without the parchment. Got much nicer browning with some char without. Thought the marinade could’ve used another tablespoon of soy sauce, maybe all of the lime’s juice. Yes, your sheet pan will need to soak. I’d recommend serving it with a grain of some variety. Really dug the marinade and will employ another time!
I was reading the recipe and wondering if tofu would work. Thanks for reporting your results

Paddington Bear would not steer us wrong.  This is a keeper!  I used skinless/boneless chicken strips and went with the fish sauce option because I did not have either of the other ingredients. I used parchment paper to line the pan and adjusted the oven down to 400 degrees because my oven is known to cook hot.  Perfectly caramelized results.  I tossed in the little bit of leftovers with spinach/arugula blend, and it made a great lunch for the next day.

I had a jar of Calabrian orange marmalade so I used that and skipped the red pepper since the marmalade was already peppery and spicy. I also had skinless chicken breasts, and so I used them. Everything cooked in a Pyrex dish instead of the sheet pan (like someone said, it's easier to clean). Everything else was as printed--and it is a definite keeper---tart, sweet, spicy, and just GOOD.

Based on the reviews I reduced the oven temperature to 350 degrees.   Roasted the potatoes for 15 minutes, added the chicken and roasted for an additional 45 minutes.  I used the red sweet potatoes,  and my husband declared this dish "company worthy".
We liked this a lot. Recommend adding more soy sauce and garlic and not using parchment paper as it soaks up the great juices from the chicken. Just use a little elbow grease!

This was such a delicious sauce! I split it in half and tried to cook half in a countertop oven and the other half in the main oven and the one in the countertop one burned and the other one tasted absolutely fabulous. Next time I’ll use the full oven for it all, but did it exactly as written and absolutely loved it.
What would work as a substitute for chicken?
Pork would work well.
The recipe call for sweet potatoes, but those look like yams.

@Debbie Ritchie - You are probably the only other person I have ever seen that acknowledges these are yams not sweet potatoes!!! I have to hunt for actual sweet potatoes every holiday season - usually end up at Sprouts and even a few times Whole Foods - no one else seems to know there is a difference!!
True yams are rarely found in the USA, mostly in international markes. Sweet potatoes are available in both white and orange, sometimes called yams but from a completely different plant.@Brandy

I yam what I yam

-Debbie "Popeye" Ritchie
Should we use Thai or Vietnamese fish sauce for this?
This sounds great but I really, really dislike sweet potatoes. What would you recommend as a substitute?
I often swap butternut squash for sweet potatoes. If you don't fancy the squash, I think Yukons or fingerlings would work just fine.

Any suggestions for swapping out sweet potatoes?
Not a favorite.  Regular white/ gold potatoes?
@Cindy I’d try small Yukons or red potatoes.  Probably cook quicker than the sweet potatoes.
I was wondering the same thing. I think Yukon gold would be a good swap. Sweet potatoes are OK, but not a favorite!

@Cindy
Carrots might be a nice alternative
Would this recipe work with lemon curd instead of the marmalade?
Tell me about fish sauce. Where do I find it?  Is it shelf stable?

@Rebecca Red Boat has been recommended in these pages and it deserves the praise.  It is a tad pricey and is definitely not the run-of-the-mill Asian fish sauce you fine on grocery shelves. I stored it in the refrigerator and after several months it turned dark and very strong. I am going to buy another bottle and use it up more quickly. It’s a great addition to a lot of dishes instead of anchovy paste to enhance umami without tasting fish.

Would love to try this but I can't use soy sauce. Worcestershire sauce is supposed to be a good substitute. Does anyone know what the substitution is? It's not listed in the NYT Substitute List (link in recipe article). 

Also, what category of oils does walnut oil fall under?
Use tamari instead of soy sauce!
Surprised at some good reviews. I thought this was a flavorless dud. Oh well.

Made this last night and everyone loved  it.  I did line the pan in parchment like suggested and glad I did. It was juicy but very good!  I will try this recipe with salmon next and agree, pork would be good.

I doubled the recipe using 2 sheet pans.  Even with Silpat on one and parchment on the other, we ended up with 2 really messy, crusty pans. Luckily, my husband volunteered to do the dishes. And this for an OK, too sweet dish. I will not make it again.
Re: the sweet potatoes. "you don't have to peel them" -- oh yes you do! While I love skin-on white potatoes, sweet potato skin is just gross.

@Anne L.
I actually loved with the skin on-surprisingly good!

This was just OK. A little too sweet for my taste. I think it might be better with fingerling potatoes. I have a weird old oven and cooked it a few minutes longer than suggested and it still didn’t crisp up the skin. It might be better to cook the chicken on its own pan and finish under a broiler. That would have been too much for the potatoes. I agree with the comments saying it could use more crushed pepper for heat.

Ok this was great.  I used Maple syrup instead of marmalade.  I substituted shallot for green onion.  I also made a separate pile of beans with the marinade (white and garbanzo) at other end of the pan for the vegetarians.  Everything was fantastic. I did not do two shifts, I just cut the sweet potato in smaller pieces.  Parchment and no clean up. Love it!

Wow. just Wow. We went through the pandemic with Melissa Clark (thank you from the bottom of our hearts) and now we feel as if we have just been the recipients of a  fearless recipe that actually can take over Thanksgiving!
 This recipe was done by the book. It was incredible. No complaints. 
Our family is extremely into marmalade. We were fortunate to be able to use  Palmerstons's from Dunkeld, Scotland for a portion of the recipe and Bonne Mamman for the basic. This is an A+ recipe.

I made this exactly as written and it was perfect. Delicious, succulent, charred a bit in all the right ways. I sourced a locally made marmalade (I'm in Toronto, Canada). Sugar was third or fourth on a short list of ingredients, which might be why it didn't burn as badly as some others' efforts have. I lined a sheet pan with aluminum foil and the c whoe thing just peeled off nicely. I'll definitely make this again.

Delicious!  I followed the recipe’s ingredients exactly, but I couldn’t fit the all the chicken and sweet potatoes on one sheet, so I cooked them on two separate sheet pans after tossing the sweet potatoes in the remaining marinade after I removed the chicken.  I also used a 375 degree oven as suggested by others.  The chicken skin browned beautifully, the sweet potatoes caramelized wonderfully, and the dish was a hit!

For this, when I saw 425°, I read every single comment before reading the recipe. That was a big help...thanks to all who contribute. Now, I'll file this in my Recipes to Try folder with valuable extra information.

Loved this recipe. I don't eat dark chicken so used boneless breasts and used homemade apricot marmalade (that had pineapple in it too). Timing was great. Two meals for me and my partner. Will definitely make again. Terrific!
Absolutely a perfect recipe! Chicken thighs were moist yet cooked thru-will definitely make this again and again. Served with jasmine rice and salad.
I followed the recipe exactly as written and it is perfect! So amazingly delicious! I’m a couscous fan so added a bit of that on the side just for fun!

I doubled the recipe and had to roast the sweet potatoes in a separate pan.  Even so, the meal received rave reviews--tasty, moist, slightly sweet and tangy. The pungent fish sauce ingredient worried me at first but all the flavors eventually came together.

I used sugar free marmalade. It did not scorch at all. I couldn’t find a marmalade that didn’t have high fructose corn syrup so I just use the sugar-free one. It may have affected the taste of the recipe. I’m not sure. This recipe wasn’t as flavorful as I anticipated. It was tasty but I guess I expected more from the combo of ingredients.
My dad gave me one Michelin star tonight for this recipe. Delicious! I doubled the marinade/sauce. I wish I made some rice to soak up the flavors.
lovely. no trouble with burning. I marinated overnight.

Made the recipe just as instructed. I think it smells like feet. All my kids agreed with me. The smell was pretty off putting. I thought the taste was ok, but definitely not worth making again. Do not recommend. I don’t usually write negative reviews, so to be nice I will say, the picture made it look good Feet.

Terrific. Easy and special — this will be in our rotation. Loved as is and actually liked the charred bits in the thighs. To intensify the flavors next time I will up the red pepper flakes, actually add orange zest (the marmalade I used had minimal rind) and put some grated ginger in the marinade.
This was so good! The marmalade and tahini made for soft, sweet (but not too sweet) potatoes and delicious chicken. I had a bunch of other root veggies in the fridge and added them as well.
I made it just as written but cut the sweet potatoes in half for more browning. As in the photo. Delicious.
