How could this be modified for an air fryer? I have a small kitchen so keep my air fryer on my covered back porch, and comments about smoke make me think this would be a good plan!

did almost exact recipe. A1 instead of fish sauce because allergic. Breasts instead of thighs. pulled out package of frozen butternut squash cubes but it was ravioli :(. liked quite a bit. tonight I will cube, mix with rice, peas, bok choi and leftover squash.

Delicious but the recipe is very poorly written.  Let's start with preheating the oven.  Why does the lime go in after the sweet potatoes have been in the oven for 10 minutes? It's a marinade, it needs the acid.  35 minutes is too long, chicken was rubbery. :(
Baked at 375 degree. Followed the rest of the recipe as written. Delicious!

This was a simple and delicious recipe. Although I usually make recipes exactly as written the first time I try them, we don't care for sweet potatoes, so I substituted broccoli florets. I used skinless boneless thighs. After marinating them, I roasted them for about 15 minutes, then added the broccoli and roasted for another 12 to 15 minutes. This will definitely go into the rotation. It was excellent.
I made this exactly as instructed, using my homemade Seville orange marmalade, and it was perfect.

I made this as written except I didn’t measure the sauce ingredients exactly. I used run-of-the-mill Smuckers orange marmalade and cooked the chicken for 30 minutes after 10 for the potatoes. I had my exhaust fan on high, but didn’t have any smoke or burning issues. I lined the sheet pan with parchment paper and clean up was no problem. All three of us thought it was great and I certainly plan to make it again.
Delicious!

I made this recipe exactly as written. The only change I made was I cooked it in a glass 13x9 pan. I was not in the mood for chicken but the sweet potatoes are outstanding. The second day I shredded the chicken put it in with the leftover sweet potatoes and reheated it. Fabulous!

I always follow a recipe exactly the first time. This was so disappointing. Marinade was too watery, didn’t permeate the chicken, and the lime just made it more liquid. The scallions and garlic burned, the chicken didn’t have enough flavor and the lime juice at the end detracted rather than added. I am baffled by these 5 star reviews.
When I made this it turned into soup! Too much liquid, meant that nothing got caramelized. I had to spoon out the excess and crank the heat to make it edible. Good flavour ideas, but doesn't work.
This was really delicious!  Easy to make!!

excellent flavor with oven set at 400.  Skin crispy and a bit black but didn't taste burned. Added cut carrots with the sweet potatoes. They all  cooked together for the same length of time, about 30-40'.   I added a spurt of anchovy paste since I didn't have fish sauce.  I agree that boneless would be a better eating experience.
So excellent and easy. I had never used fish sauce before and it was a key ingredient. My husband asked when did I plan to make it again.
We used our homemade marmalade. added Brussels sprouts to sheet pan. Our chicken did not burn. It was tasty!!
This is delicious and reminded me I should make sweet potatoes more often.

Delicious. Not spicy at all. Made as directed using boneless/skinless thighs. Marinated about 8 hours. Added some brussels sprouts and the green parts of the scallions. Cooked the veges for 10 minutes at 100, cooked the rest for an additional 25 minutes, turning once, then finished under the broiler (500) for 5 minutes. Use foil. No smoke, no burning, no mess, amazing dinner.

This is a terrific recipe. Easy and full of flavor. The leftovers were even better the next day. Based on the comments, I cooked the sweet potatoes for 10 minutes at 425F, then added 2 lbs. boneless chicken thighs (my husband doesn't like bone-in) and roasted for 40 mins at 350F. No charring on the thighs, but still tasty. I also marinated the thighs for 6 hours before cooking.
When the chicken goes in, I turn the temperature down to 350 and cooked it a little longer, 40-50 minutes, so it won't burn.
When I added the chicken, I turned down the temperature to 350 and cooked everything a little longer, 40-50 minutes.  At 425 it tends to burn.
Potatoes 425 for 10, add skinless chicken 350 for 30.
Anyone make this the day before and reheat it -- or is serving it right out of the oven the way to go?  Thanks.

I am a vegetarian but make a lot of dishes with meat for my husband. I actually love cooking meat dishes, just don’t like to eat it. This recipe was pretty easy and fun to make. I followed the recipe as written except I used regular potatoes and followed some of the comments on cooking time and temp. Cook potatoes 425F first for about 25 min. Add chicken and decrease temp to 350F for 30min and the chicken was done, the potatoes were perfect!  Oh yeah, I also used boneless and skinless chicken. Maybe I actually didn’t stick that close to the actual recipe :)!!

Great and easy recipe that everyone in my house loved! I used a sheet pan with parchment but next time will likely use foil as the parchment was too wet. But I had no burning, no smoke and no black skin on the chicken. I only got to marinate it for 30 minutes and it was still tasty. It will definitely be on regular rotation at my house.

So I made this last night. It was delicious … but I did not have the thick sauce many reviewers mentioned.  After adding the lime juice, it became very thin and watery. STILL DELICIOUS, great enough for company.  Just wondering if anyone else had this result.  The sweet potatoes were flooding in it.  (All really delicious, and the sweet potatoes complimented flavours well.). I did follow rec of two posters:  added 1 T Dijon mustard and fresh (grated) ginger.

Another delicious Melissa Clark recipe.  The only thing I did different was to cook the sweet potatoes and chicken on separate sheet pans.  I like my vegetables well roasted, and that usually takes more time than recipes suggest.  There was plenty of marinade left in the bowl after I took the chicken out, and I brushed the sweet potatoes with that extra marinade, so they got the right flavor.

This may be my favorite new recipe of the year: delicious and versatile. We went with bone-in, skin-on thighs first time around and really enjoyed them. I cut some one-inch thick chops from a pork loin for our second outing and marinated them for  4 hours. They were outstanding.  I went with 400 with convection and monitored them closely. The chops got some nice char and didn't dry out. I lined my sheet pan with foil and was glad I did.
This was wonderful, love non-stick foil lined sheet pan dinners. I made a full order of the sauce and used most of it. Only 2 of us so 3 thighs were ample.
For sure foil line your pan.  Made a serious mess.  I’d consider a thinly slice jalapeño in the glaze next time.
I made with boneless pork chops and reduced cooking time to 400/12-14 min. Would recommend.

Too much liquid in pan so skin and potatoes not crispy - next time I won’t pour extra sauce over. Sweet potatoes were also overcooked - will not cook in advance next time. Very nice flavors and easy! Kids loved.

Works well with other vegetables as well. I had some potatoes, carrots, and parsnips that deserved to be used. 
Rather than a sweet marmalade I used Seville orange slightly bitter which created a nice tang.
I made this first with skin and loved it so I served it to friends but used skinless and it was just meh. Definitely indulge and keep the skin on!
This was delightful. So easy and melts in your mouth!
Skin was good . Chicken was juicy but not thrilling . The sweet potatoes were amazing ! BTW I had no issues with ruining my sheet pan

Winner winner chicken dinner. Used boneless chicken thighs  and a good British Seville orange marmalade which is a bit sweet/tart,  Worried that it would  burn based on some comments I added a little mayo to the marinade, would have tried plain yogurt if I've had it. Chicken was moist , sweet potatoes crispy, and everything was deliciois. I served with some broccolini for a nice contrast .  This is a keeper.  In fact it inspired me to set up a keepers box in NYT cooking, when  you cook a lot like I do, it's easy to forget the things that you would want to make again,

I had Rose's lime marmalade on hand, so I switched to orange zest and juice instead of lime to balance the flavors. I used some baby potatoes instead of sweet potatoes. I went with 20 minutes at 375 degrees and 15-20 at 425, which achieved a nice crisp skin and tender meat.

These muffins are delicious! I used 1/2 tsp. ground ginger & 1/2 tsp. cinnamon, greek yogurt, Meyer lemon zest, threw in some chopped pecans and used my homemade Meyer lemon marmalade. Very tasty, the marmalade pocket gives it a sweet surprise! Will make these again.

@Monika
I’d love to make Meyer lemon marmalade, where did you get your recipe?
@Elysa I also make meyer lemon marmalade (and it's what i used in these muffins!). In my case, I just substituted the meyer lemons for the oranges in a regular marmalade recipe. Worked out great.

I made these with white flour instead of whole wheat flour and they came out great. The recipe is spot on accurate for a dozen muffins. I cut back about 15g of the brown sugar and used pumpkin pie spice instead of just cardamom. I used the zest of a blood orange and regular Aldi brand marmalade and the marmalade made the muffins sweet enough. This is a nice alternative to a morning glory muffin or a bran muffin. I’m sure you could add nuts and raisins too, but being the first time I’ve made them I kept it to as close to the original recipe as possible.
@Yum! Will make again. Thanks for giving feedback on the original recipe!  I didn’t have marmalade so I used apricot jam - it was very good but the marmalade would have been even better!
These were a winner! But then Melissa Clark’s recipes generally are.

Besides posting excellent recipes, one of the main reasons I subscribe is to read the reviews and learn about others' tweaks and alterations. They're enormously helpful and interesting. Without them, I probably wouldn't subscribe. Can't understand why folks get bent out of shape around creativity.
Made them with 1:1 gluten free flour and they turned out great!
No need for substitutions- made exactly as written and turned out perfectly. Not sure why people that don’t follow the recipe feel need to tell us their recipe.  Get your own show.

I understand what you're saying, sometimes the recipe is perfect exactly as written, but from my perspective I enjoy reading how other people have approached the recipe. Sometimes ideas about how to substitute ingredients or tweak a little for personal preferences is helpful. Over the years, I have found such comments very beneficial in expanding my knowledge and creativity as a cook and baker.
@saga Well said!  I share your sentiments.

@Perfect as written! Sheesh. No need for hostility. Like the other commenter, I learn a lot from what other cooks do  and it really helps to know what works and doesn’t work substitution-wise if you don’t have everything in your pantry. but even more important, this is a community and it’s so much fun that everyone shares instead of lying on just one way communication from “an authority”.
I’m just enjoying one of these muffins right now.  I love the little surprise hit of the marmalade.  I’ll make these again and probably will add some chopped pecans.

These are incredible!  I made some changes based on what I had in my pantry/garden because I didn’t want to run to the store!  I also doubled down on all my tropical ingredients and it was soooooo delicious!
I substituted rolled oats for large unsweetened coconut flakes.  I used ground cardamom AND 2 tsp of freshly grated ginger.  I added a 1/2 cup chopped walnuts. I grow makrut (kaffir) limes on my patio, so I used this for zest instead of lemon.  And finally, in the ethos of tropical, I used passion fruit marmalade that I had been saving in my pantry for something special.  And this truly was that something special!

I usually try to make the recipes ‘as is’ first, but sometimes what you have is what you have, and it really turned out great 😊
I love this recipe. Not too sweet and the hit of bitter from the marmalade is perfect. I used meusli instead of just oats because it's what I had in the pantry; the extra texture was great!
If you’re wondering if they’ll come out well using pre-grated carrots (even though they’re not fine-grated), the answer is yes, yes they will.
Can this recipe be used to make a loaf of Carrot with Marmelade bread in a 9x5 inch loaf pan?  I don’t have muffin tins.

Long time NYT baker, first timer commenter: I just have to say, wow!! The heavenly cardamom-jam combo with the satisfying texture of the carrots and oats is perfection!! Steel cut oats were all I had, so I soaked them in warm water first to soften them up and they worked great. I did add a little bit of sweetened flaked coconut, cut back on the brown sugar, would highly recommend! I’m going to be the most popular person at work tomorrow when I bring these treats in!

Lovely recipe! I substituted raw grated butternut squash because was out of carrots and added a little more brown sugar because squash doesn’t have the same sweetness of carrots. Also added chai tea soaked dried cherries. Used the cardamom and also tsp each of cinnamon and ginger and baked the whole time at 350, but perhaps that’s a just a needed Colorado high altitude hack where flavors need more emphasis or else the sweetness takes over
Can I substitute other preserves, such as strawberry, for the marmalade?
Other comments have noted using raspberry or apricot jam.

These are quite tasty and extremely tender.  In fact, I think they might be bordering on slightly too tender since they don't hold together super well when removing them from the muffin liners.  
I agree with the other commenter, the cardamom gets lost, which is too bad because I love cardamom.  Not too sweet, and they will be a nice breakfast this week. Overall, a delicious muffin, but I might make a few tweaks the next time I bake them.

I used white flour instead of whole wheat and substituted butter milk for the yogurt because that’s what I had on hand. I also used a combination of cinnamon and cardamom with a few gratings of nutmeg. Additionally, I added raisins. The muffins turned out well. They make a nice, small breakfast treat.
Nice suggestions for the ingredients you had on hand. Very helpful- thank you.
Made exactly as written with raspberry preserves.  Simply delicious.
I like muffins, carrots and marmalade. My take is that combining them diminishes each of the individual ingredients.
I have a simple solution - don't make them.  Why comment if you're NOT interested in the recipe AND didn't try it?

I baked these pretty much as written, using white flour and the butter option.  They were good but not great.  (Maybe I needed a stronger flavored marmalade.)  But I found the sum to be less than its parts.  I couldn’t taste the cardamom at all.
@Christine VB, cardamom flavor can fade pretty quickly when it sits in the spice rack. Maybe you’ll get a better result with fresh cardamom?

@Sue
Agree. I keep the seed pods on hand, and then grind the seeds each time I use them.
@Sue, I buy cardamom seeds (not pods) and grind them in a spice grinder as needed. The flavor is much more potent this way. You can get them at an Indian or Middle Eastern market.

Contrary to what the recipe says, these weren’t as good a day or two after baking - they don’t keep their flavour and freshness well after the first day. We thought they were okay, but won’t be making them again.
Wow, these are fantastic. I was skeptical of the 100% whole grains but they are delicious! I used white whole wheat flour and my mother’s lemon marmalade. A keeper!
These are delicious! I used butter and oil and subbed the marmalade for apricot jam. Will make again!

Sunshine in a bite with the added benefit of whole wheat and carrots! Made as written. Next time I will play with ratio of cardamom to zest (orange) and I might try apricot jam, as well. These are light, bright, and delicious. Made with Just Eggs (egg substitute) since my grand princess is allergic to eggs. You’d never know and they were GONE in an hour between 6 of us including the 16 month old princess.

I love reading the comments; it's fun to play with recipes and see where it takes us. It's great to make the recipe as it is given - it's great to tweak it as well. These muffins are great with the recipe, as written and I fully intend to play with it when I make them again, incorporating some of the great ideas from other readers. That's the fun of cooking....
Really enjoyed, and pretty hearty. Next time will use a more generous hand with the marmalade as mine was largely absorbed into the muffin. There will be a next time!
I’m excited to try these. I love carrot muffins/cakes so much, and my husband especially loves marmalade!
Used mango chutney in place of the marmalade, loved the little zing!

Made these for Easter and they were gone by end of day.  I used a local whole grain flour, which is a mix of heartier flours, like oat, etc.  I also used a local lemon ginger marmalade which put the flavors over the top.  I am wondering if I can make mini cakes with this, adding a cream cheese frosting and the marmalade as filling..lamy reason this wouldn't work?

An “oops” turned into amazing muffins!  I was making a double recipe and was being SO careful until my mistake of ADDING all the marmalade into the muffin’s liquid bowl.   I was pretty upset!! But I rationalized that both oats and whole wheat flour need hydration, so I crossed my fingers!!  I also added both cinnamon and freshly ground cardamom in equal amounts, so a double recipe called for 2t of one of those suggested spices and I added 2T of each cinn and card.  The muffins are gorgeous and taste wonderful!  So!!  Thank goodness!!
These were great! Made as written. Not too sweet, nice texture, good citrus flavor. Will make again.

I made these with a few changes and they were a big hit for my meeting.
I used 1/2tsp. P. Spice and 1/2 tsp. Cinnamon. I used a traditional style orange marmalade ( Seville) and I added chopped pecans on top. I will put this one on repeat !!
Could I use olive oil?  That’s whats on hand!

Used butter, orange marmalade, and pumpkin pie spice because no cardamom.

Delicious!
Followed this recipe exactly as written using my homemade Seville orange marmalade, and the muffins are outstanding. I will make this again, and again, and again…
These are delicious! I added raisins. And plopped a Huge Tbsp of marmalade into the middle of each. I will be making these again!

Added half a cup of chopped pecans and 1/8 cup hemp hearts. Didn't have marmalade so used rhubarb jam. They were delicious and easy. I want to make them again with marmalade, will double the spice and thinking of adding coconut and raisins too to really gild the lily, but honestly great just the way they are. I will freeze and eat one a day for breakfast! Yum!!

These are delicious muffins. I was concerned about lack of sweetness (I love sweet things), but they're delicious as is. 

Also, hilarious there's an argument in the comments section of a recipe! As much as I don't care for acrimony, nice people are riled up abut their food. Perhaps as a needed break from politics.

Very delicious. And it seems fairly bomb-proof, as all good muffin recipes are. I doubled the recipe. As per recommendations in the comments, I added raisins and shredded coconut. Halfway through I realized that a) the yogurt in my fridge was vanilla instead of plain,  and b) I only had 3 eggs. So I made it with a mix of vanilla yogurt and sour cream, and 3 eggs plus one flax-seed "vegan egg." Turned out terrific!
Taste was good but muffins collapsed.
perfect do not change a thing
A total failure. These were claggy and - save the jam - the flavor was bland. Into the bin. A terrible waste of marmalade.
@Paul  Did you actually make the muffins or is this pure conjecture?
Anybody know what I can substitute for oats? Would rolled rye or barley work? Oats upset my stomach.
Try spelt flakes
Made candied peel recently and added approximately 1/4 cup. Yum. But then I love candied peel. Fun to put your own spin on things.

I made these with graham flour (cause that is what I had) and butternut squash from my garden.  I also just incorporated the marmalade into the batter because I live at 6000 ft and thought the batter needed a little more liquid.  They are terrific!  They rose beautifully, and are so delicate.  These muffins will not weigh you down.  I will get some whole wheat flour for my next bake, but I cannot imagine them being any better.  Thank you for the recipe!
Absolutely perfect recipe and delicious muffins. Thank you.
I am thinking cupcakes. Anyone frosted them?

Used King Arthur GF and added a bit more oats to make up for not using whole wheat flour.  Used Bob’s Red Mill GF quick rolled oats, cardamom, lemon zest, and EVOO (did not detect overpowering flavor).   Had organic tangerine orange marmalade (not as much peel but very good).   Fantastic!!!
Added just a little coconut and pumpkin seeds for texture. Also added some wheat germ. Love this recipe.
I followed exactly as written - perfect. Tastes wholesome, not like dessert. Perfect for breakfast.
Made these today without the marmalade (because I didn’t have any) and they were perfection! Lightly sweet with a wonderful texture. Ate two already!

I made the recipe lactose and gluten free for my family. I followed the 2x recipe with the following exceptions: used King Arthur 1:1 gluten free flour, Green Valley lactose free butter for the oil, and Green Valley lactose free yogurt (same portions). I substituted 2 pressed cups of peeled Evercrisp apples instead of carrots and apricot preserves for the marmalade. I baked 3 minutes at 425 and 13 minutes at 350. I monitored the tops of the muffins as the GF flour can cause recipes to burn quicker than with normal wheat flour. The muffins came out great!!
Worked out to be 9 muffins for me in a regular size muffin pan. I substituted apple sauce for the oil. These were very good.

These are so so simple and delicious! I used ground ginger only - I think the balance was perfect with the carrots and orange. Used 365 brand marmalade the sweetness level was perfect. They are also great with Greek yogurt and raspberries.
Kind of lacking in flavor and oven temp is way too high. Then out just ok. Wouldn’t make again
Maybe it was the kind of marmalade I used, but they came out very sweet. Too much for my taste.

Very good recipe! I am not a great baker but these came out with no drama. I found the marmalade slightly bitter and so might use a different preserve next time. I maybe should have grated the carrots more finely but they came out like muffins anyway haha
Good recipe, easy to follow.  I did however, make six large muffins rather than 12 mini muffins.  Somehow, the oats remained whole.  Did anyone else have the same experience?

I added 2 tbs of meyer lemon paste along with the zestes lemon and lemon juice for a bright finish. I swapped half the flour with flax meal and hazelnut flour. They turned out delicious! For the next batch I'm going to add well-dices plum and use my kumkwat cardamom jam.
Made as directed- judging by the two that disappeared from the cooling rack as my husband passed through the kitchen, I’d say they’re a hit!
Made these twice and my family gobbled them up both times. Delicious! Don't skimp on the marmalade.
These are perfect. Followed the recipe as written and they came out delicious! Everyone loved them. They taste buttery though I used olive oil. These are a new family favorite. Thank you MC!!!

I really love these muffins. For me now it's cinnamon and Bonne Maman pineapple passion fruit preserves instead of the cardamom and marmalade, otherwise I follow the recipe as written and it turns out fantastic. I've also subbed sour cream for the yogurt and it wasn't a problem.

Turned out great. Even won the approval of a suspicious 5 year old ! I used white flour, quick oats, ginger and cinnamon as they’re what was on hand. Shredded the carrots (missed the direction to grate) and gave them a rough chop and it was fine. Will definitely repeat.
I used cottage cheese instead of Greek yogurt and decreased brown sugar to 1/4 cup. So delicious, makes for a quick breakfast!

Love these muffins.  As I am always looking to reduce calories without sacrificing taste or texture, I reduced oil to 1/4 cup and added a 1/4 cup unsweetened apple sauce the second time I made it - turned out even better!  For spices, I used 1 tsp. cinnamon and 1/2 tsp. ground ginger.  Nutritious enough for breakfast, filling enough for a snack, sweet enough for a dessert.

Excellent. I can’t figure out why mine didn’t come out as brilliantly orange as in the photo (I made no modifications), but they tasted excellent and that’s the main thing. Next time I won’t worry as much about fully covering  my marmalade dots because I think they look nice poking out the tops as in the photo. Having them visible is also nice because people won’t be surprised biting in (I kept warning people so they wouldn’t think it was raw batter:)

This is one of my very favorite recipes. I have made them multiple times exactly as written except I use 1/2 teaspoon ground cardamom and 1 teaspoon ground cinnamon. And I use the option of using grated orange peel.

They are so good that I pop them right into the freezer and grab and defrost one in the morning to eat in the car for breakfast when I’m running late.

I can’t think of an improvement to the recipe in taste or texture.

Obsessed with these and will definitely make them again. I use extra cinnamon (1 tbsp), as well as ginger and nutmeg (1 tsp) and they turned out great. I also added a splash of vanilla. I didn’t use the marmalade and they still turned out amazing. I did make an orange cream cheese frosting that i’m keeping on the side so people can add it if they want it.
Delicious!  I had cream cheese I needed to use up so I put that in the middle instead of marmalade, and it was great!  Very nice texture for whole wheat and oats.

title: Sheet-Pan Scallion Shrimp With Crispy Rice Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: Since your cooked rice presumably comes out of a container, it's far easier to coat it with the oil in the container before spreading it out on the sheet pan.
yes i put the rice and oil in a bowl to coat, much easier
Why not just add oil to the pot that you cooked the rice in and stir? I did it and it turned out perfectly. Great recipe
I would sauté a diced onion in a bit of oil and add that and a package of frozen vegetables to the rice. And a couple of scrambled eggs with the shrimp.

Wow, we followed the instructions to a T and the rice was indeed crunchy and crisp, to the point where it fills up your molars. Flavor was excellent, shrimp marinade very good but man, a stir fry in a large pan or wok would make this dish faster and to my taste better.

Flavor of marinade is great, and enjoy this recipe for meal prep purposes. However, it is crying out for a vegetable — frozen peas or diced carrot could easily be precooked and tossed in at the end to make this a more complete dish.
@CT   Agreed on a veg…I was thinking about a side salad of English cucumber, red onion, and rice wine vinegar, with a pinch of sugar.
@CT I added stir fried snow pea pods with a bit of toasted sesame oil. Yum.

I didn’t have shrimp but I had two Coho salmon filets and it worked great.  As the rice was going into the oven, I seared the bottom of the filets, one minute in a really hot skillet so that it was easy to remove the skin.  Then cut the still mostly raw salmon into cubes and into the marinade. Followed the rest of the recipe. When scattered over the roasted rice, the salmon only took 5 minutes to cook.  Easy and yummy. I’ll definitely do this again.

Made this last night using arborio rice (the only short grain I could find on short notice) and gotta say, another winner from NYT Cooking! 
Followed reader comments and coated the rice with oil & salt in a bowl, tossed with scallion whites and then spread it on the sheet pan which was totally efficient.
At the end I opted to zhuzh it up a little by adding additional lime juice & soy sauce to the sauce. Next time: might add some maple syrup & miso? 
Anyhoo - great dinner, definitely a keeper
Absolutely amazingly delicious.  Yes, add the extra (just a little bit) fish sauce, lime juice and sesame oil right before you serve it.  The crispy rice was to die for.

Loved this! The crunchy rice was a revelation. And freshly cooked rice was just fine.Don't like spicy, so substitured sweet chili sauce for the peppers.
Aftr cooking the rice, dumped it into a bowl to mix with the oil and scallions before spreading it onto the sheet pan. Much less messy.
Why does this call for 6 cups of cooked rice when 4 servings of rice would be 2 - 3 cups?
@gyofu and I used inky 1.5 cups (leftovers) it scaled beautifully. You can even keep the sauce the same…
@gyofu because the rice grains shrink as they release moisture while in the oven.  Two of us ate the entire dish. It is amazing.

I don’t understand the 5 stars. I was not a fan. Flavors were good but as others said the rice gets hard, not crispy, and sticks in your teeth. It was unpleasant. Also putting the shrimp in the ginger garlic paste made the texture a little too soft.
Why not just stirfry this? Tasty, quick, no need to heat up an oven. And add some red pepper for color!

@Connie
Stir-fried rice doesn’t really get crispy.
@Cheryl sure it can - heat up a thick layer of vegetable oil to a cast iron skillet on high, add cooked rice and let it sit untouched for a few minutes. It’ll crisp up.
This was excellent! Perfect weeknight meal and excellent way to use up leftover rice.

A few people have said, why not just stir fry it? Of course you could, but then it would be fried rice, not crispy rice! Also unless you have a really big wok, it would be hard to stir fry these quantities in one batch.

Excellent flavor! But next time I’ll pull the rice out and add the shrimp at no more than 15 minutes. Or maybe I’ll just stir fry it as others have suggested. As it is I may never get the “crispy” rice out of my teeth. I ended up eating the shrimp and picking through the rice for a few edible grains. Even the rice that didn’t brown got way too hard. Wish I’d read the comments before cooking!

@Pedigrees childfree catdoghorse lady
Agreed. We left the sheet pan in the oven for the entire 35 minutes and that was WAY too long. Most of the rice pieces were too hard to chew! But the flavor was great, so we’ll make it again with a much shorter cooking time.

I feel like I made this exactly. Positives:  the shrimp were great.  Marinate for 10 minutes not 25-40.  The ginger has enzymes that break down the shrimp if you do it for more than 10 mins.  The rice was delicious with the sauce.  Negatives:  too crunchy and we cooked it for 30 mins.  I wonder if the oven temp reduced to 400 would be better.  
Overall very good flavors..

I essentially followed the directions but doubled everything since I had 2lbs of shrimp. I added (4) bags (32oz) of steamed and shelled edamame at the end and mixed it together. I served it with cucumber kimchi a boiled egg spiked with soy sauce and sweet chili sauce on the side. A very easy and tasty weekday meal, a small amount of prep for lots of flavor. Adding more oil, salt, and green onion to finish is key.
Really tasty, but a lot more work than I like. My own, simple rice bowls are just as satisfying.
My husband is allergic to shrimp. What fish would be a good substitute?

Was looking for a shrimp substitute, too.  Saw someone mentioned salmon.  Cubed chicken breast would probably be good.  Would need to cook it closer to 15-18 minutes so maybe start on the stovetop or add it to the pan sooner so the rice doesn't burn.
If you were going to substitute tofu cubes, would you handle it exactly like the shrimp or do something different on the prep or cooking time?
Both marinate and cook tofu longer. It will work fine.
I wonder how this would be with Barley instead of rice?
I used a grain blend called Kagayaki, that i got at the Japanese grocery store and it was great. it's barley,black rice,rye berries,red rice, black barley and short grain brown rice.
Made this last night and it was just delicious. I added miso paste to the sauce, and finished the dish with chili crisp. This one’s going into our regular rotation.
I assume the shrimp is peeled? Looks like tails are left on but thought I would double-check?

I think the food stylist used tail-on for looks, not for extra crunch. It's not a dish to be eaten with the fingers, so, in the interest of serving food that guests can eat gracefully, I use completely peeled and deveined shrimp.
@MJ  Why would one presume it's coming from a container?

Delicious! Loved the crunchy rice! Agreed with veg comments, we had this with some salad greens but next time I’ll pop some broccolini on the sides when adding the shrimp.  Also i took 10 seconds to make a second sauce to drizzle w maybe 1/2 a clove of garlic…loved all the garlic when cooked, but if you split the original sauce (with 5 cloves!!?!) feels way too much raw garlic for a sauce

I followed recipe directions and ended up with rice that could have sent me to the dentist. Roasting the rice at 450 degrees for 25 minutes was total overkill. Other than that, flavors were really good - I will definitely make adjustments to the rice in the future.
I did the rice at 400 for 25 minutes and shrimp for  7 minutes, doubled the sauce. The result was crispy (not teeth breaking) with nicely charred scallions. Delicious combo!

The family wanted an Asian tasting meal. This hit the spot. 

My modifications:

Seasoned the rice with mirin and bonito powder. 

Double the sauce proportions. Add about 1/2 tbs of dark brown sugar and 1tbs of gochujang. 

I generously sprinkled furikake over the tray before serving and had grated daikon with ponzu as a side.
An easy and great recipe. I added cilantro together green scallions at the end. The flavor was great. I may want to reduce salt next time.
My second attempt at making crispy rice - and this time it easy and successful. Watch out Bobby Flay!
Excellent!! I wouldn’t change a thing except I tossed chilie crisp oil at the end 😊
Rice was hard and hard to chew. Melissa and team, any suggestions for the rice?

Absolutely delicious. I think sushi /pearl rice is a must. I tossed thawed frozen peas with the shrimp. Those arguing for a stir fry are missing the point. This recipe is all about the chewy sticky rice. Yumm! But nutritionally a bust. So many calories from rice and oil.
I used about half recommended amount of rice (2.5-3cups) and found that to be a preferable rice:shrimp ratio.
I increased the amount of prawns by 50% for a better shrimp to rice ratio.

I left a section of empty pan for broccoli and added to the pan after cooking rice for about 8 minutes. Was too soon. Starts cooking fast when it hits the hot pan. Would wait until 15 minutes next time.
We loved the flavor but too crunchy for our jaws.

6 cups of cooked rice is too much for a recipe that serves 3-4 people. I used 3 cups of rice and there were still leftovers after 4 people were served. I didn’t change the ingredients for the sauce. The sauce recipe was perfect for 3 cups of rice.

Do you have raw shrimp in the room-temperature sauce, which is later drizzled onto the finished dish? I'd think that's a health risk. Why not drizzle it onto the dish just as you start baking the shrimp, which removes the risk and probably still conveys the flavors?
No, half of the paste is put into a smaller bowl and set aside after making it, which is what will become the sauce. The shrimp is tossed with the paste that remains in the large bowl.
This is similar to this recipe. Also delicious. A family favorite! https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1025011-sheet-pan-turmeric-chicken-and-crispy-rice?smid=share-url
We loved it.  Great flavor. Next time I might pull rice out a minute or too sooner.  But overall it was great.
Made as instructed....loved the flavor and textures!  Will definitely make again and again!
Do you cool the cooked rice BEFORE you begin Step 2?

@Cathy
Recipe calls for cooked rice

Step one oven 450
Step six ...continue to roast...
Question to I set my oven for bake or roast?

@KonaDiver

Set it to bake

I have to try this, if even just for the crispy rice.  If anyone has ever been to Lotus Of Siam in Las Vegas and had their "crispy rice and sausage" you too will want to try this.  Could to get enough of that dish.
Thoughts on trying this with riced cauliflower please good readers?
It will not work. Cauliflower has a very high water content. It will turn to mush. Just use regular rice.
For those of us who grow our own green onions (scallions), how many/ what weight in a bunch? It’s sort of maddening to have to drive to a store to count or weigh their produce.

A bunch of scallions is about an inch to an inch-and-a-half in diameter.

Sorry, I've never weighed one.

I like scallions, so tend to think that more is better.
We had this for dinner tonight as written, but I added some extra lime juice inadvertently. Most excellent and will go on our rotation.
fantastic, even with lemon juice instead of lime. Grateful for the skills of a highly trained chef, opening doors for me that i would never even have seen

Love this. Made it exactly as written, and it came out perfect. The textures and flavors are fabulous, and it's so easy. This will be on regular rotation at our house. Thank for another great one, Ms. Clark!
So good I could faint!!

We enjoyed this, and will definitely make it again. Unlike others, I found it easier to make than stir-fried rice; fewer steps and no last minute rushing. I used arborio, and it did take a full 35 minutes to see any color on the edges. Added peas at the end. Pleasantly chewy, no problem with "sticking in teeth," etc.

This was delicious! I only had 3 cups of cooked rice (brown, Jasmine) so I only used about 3/4 lb. of large shrimp, but pretty much a full recipe of everything else. I did the whole thing in a cast iron skillet, and the rice on the bottom and sides was nice and crispy. It would make Bobby Flay proud! I also added a couple of handfuls of frozen peas to the top of the rice along with the shrimp for extra vegetation.  It’s definitely something that I will add to my cooking repertoire.
Loved how crispy the rice gets in the oven! I will say that that the sauce left something to be desired. It was next level after adding hoisin and/or oyster sauce to the final product.

Maybe it's because I cooked my (brown basmati) rice in a rice cooker before doing this recipe, but the rice was absolutely inedible.  I was keeping a close eye on it, so it was not overcooked, just crispy at the edges, but still not good at all.  The shrimp itself turned out great, and the sauce is delicious.  I will probably prepare the rice separately from now on.

Loved this dish.  Be careful with roasting the cooked rice.  I left it in a bit too long and it was too crunch, with not enough soft rice remaining.  Next time I will not spread the rice quite so thinly and will keep closer eye on the browning.

I’m not sure how much the lime added to this, would have preferred a touch less with a touch more of salt. Also, this is easy to make with shrimp that are frozen. I didn’t even thaw mine—just added about 5 extra minutes to the cook time and took them out when the shrimp were fully pink.
This was delicious but a little salty.  Any suggestions on the best way to reheat so that you maintain the crispness???
Made this with riced cauliflower and it was perfect and healthier than with all that rice.  Yum!
@Cindy  brown rice is not unhealthy

The flavors are outstanding! For more variety, I subbed half the rice with diced or minced cauliflower added to the rice before it was pressed into the sheet pan.  I may leave the edges a little thicker next time, needed to be careful chewing the crunchy rice pieces. I’ll be making this again and again!

Cooked 2 c. Organic jasmine rice - made more than enough.  EVOO was not overpowering.  1 TB toasted seasame oil, 1 TB combined coconut aminos and GF tamari  , jalapeño, and juice of 1 whole lime.   Next time will add onion, peas and scrambled egg strips as others have recommended.  Also did rice at 425 for 25 minutes to avoid over crisped rice commented on by others.   Really tasty.
Wowsers!  So good. Don't change a thing. It's perfect as written.
Not great. Needed much more marinade. I didn't like the crispy rice pieces, not a great texture. I ended up adding peas and egg along with mixing in a lot more marinade to try to salvage the recipe

I used this in the spur of the moment in a blizzard. BUT all I had was rice and the sauce ingredients. I used bits of leek I had around, threw in cut up artichoke hearts from a can and put it in a bed of kale salad (lots of oil in that, too) it was soooo good.
Did anyone use parchment paper in pan or just the pan for rice?
@Coopersmith I used parchment paper for the whole recipe- I baked at 420

I totally enjoyed this recipe . It has a good combination of flavors. I used soy sauce but next time I will try the fish sauce . I think that will make this dish perfect for me. I cook in an electric oven and for some reason the center inner portion of the pan browned quicker than the edges. Next time I make this recipe I will aim for only 25 percent of the rice browning. The crunch was good but I think I would like less crisp to soft rice . I used thawed shrimp which cooked perfectly in 10 mins
Love Melissa Clark, did not like this dish. Took more time, instructions not clear enough. Again make her recipes often. This was not one I will do again.
I have some leftover white rice in the freezer. Would you (anyone) recommend warming it a bit (post-defrost) before putting it on the pan, or will just defrosting it work?
@HWI used my rice straight from the fridge.

A keeper. Delicious flavor! I took the ideas from other readers and added peas (about 1.5 c), cilantro (.25 c), and chili crisp (about 1 tbl.), and doubled the amount of sauce; however, the short grain brown rice I used got too crispy. It took a while to get brown, and in the process, the ratio of crispy to soft got way off. Next time, health considerations aside, I'll go with short grained sushi rice.
It is annoying to not list canola/rapeseed oil as a neutral oil choice.  It is the neutral oil that is most likely to be in someone's pantry already.
If you have teeth issues, probably better to make it as fried rice. Flavor was very good. Will make it in a wok as fried rice.

I do a much better job making this in a frying pan on my own. I thought the rice came out fine but I like my fried rice darker and I agree that some vegetables should be added and I did add eggs over it. Kind of plain.

My husband gives this five stars and he wants us to make it again. I feel a bit confused. Perhaps I am not a crunchy rice fan? I had two amazing bites that involved the marinade on the shrimp otherwise I thought--meh. The whole meal I questioned my math since I cut the recipe in half and wished there was sweetened (?) egg in it. I am giving it five stars because he loved it--and also because we were out of fish sauce (that was crushing) and we had to use soy sauce. We will make it again.

Yum! Before dinner, my husband said he wasn’t very hungry and wouldn’t eat much, but when I served this, he had seconds! 
I only had long grain rice, so it got a bit too crunchy, but was still delicious. Definitely going to make it again.

This was incredible. The rice is something magical. I agree with others that a vegetable would be a welcome compliment. Does anyone have ideas how peas might be incorporated into the process? If I added them along with the shrimp for the last seven minutes, would that work?

Pretty good, not really something I would choose to make frequently.   Rice didn't brown much, but was plenty crisp, so I wouldn't wait for much browning.   We didn't use fish sauce beecause of a vegetarian, but added a bit to individual servings and it really improved it.   I agree with those who suggest just making it in a wok rather than the whole oven thing.
Delicious!

This was way too much garlic for me, especially since most of it is essentially raw in the sauce that gets drizzled over. Liked the crispy sheet pan technique, will try again without garlic, and more added veggies.
Is there something missing in the recipe? The sauce component made such a small amount. There was no point to divide it and barely enough volume to mix it in the rice

Great recipe! First of all, I read all the comments and so glad I did. Halved the recipe for 2. I used Sushi Rice and feel it’s a must. It retains moisture so it works perfectly. I rinsed and strained my rice several times until the water ran clear. Cooked it in my rice cooker. Did bake the rice at 450 degrees however, I kept my eye on the browning process continually. I would touch the rice from time to time to be sure I did not overcook it! I used larger prawns and put them on over the rice at about 15 minutes. They took about 8 minutes to fully cook. The combination of flavors is fantastic. Drizzled with a little additional toasted sesame oil at the end.

lol by mistake, I totally overcooked brown rice but used it anyway.  Got nicely but not overly crisp in oven.  maybe that's the solution to rice being too hard/crisp!  Needed a veg as others have said -- I used cabbage cause all I had.  worked well.  Delicious !!!
My husband loved this, but I was not as enthusiastic. If I make this again, I will add vegetables for more variety in color, texture and flavor.

There's homemade fried rice, where you clean out the fridge, add peas, carrots, eggs and improvise a sauce, and it's comforting and yummy. This is something else. The garlic, ginger, and lime are the stars here. I used medium shrimp, cooked 7 minutes at 425. The rice, cooked first for 25 minutes at 425, was crispy in spots, sauce-soaked in others. (My husband is Persian, we like crispy parts of rice). Made with exactly  the ingredients listed and it was delicious.

Really enjoyed this.  Chose the soy sauce option for the marinade/sauce.   I don't like raw garlic, so I mashed the minced garlic into a paste with some of the salt and put it in the marinade portion to cook the garlic with the shrimp.  Because of the comments about hard rice, I did not use the oven's convection function.   Served a vegetable on the side.   The ratio of rice to shrimp is a typical carb-to-protein ratio for a meal.
I have jasmine rice on hand.  Any thoughts about this as a rice?

Hi Allison,

Jasmine rice is stickier then long grain rice, but not as sticky as sushi rice.  The fragrance of the jasmine rice will probably be a nice addition to the dish.  As indicated by the introduction, you should check the roasting of the rice sooner than directed by the recipe.

I followed the recipe to a T, too, and I did not get crispy rice at all, in spite of of increasingly the oven time considerably and even resorting to the broiler. But the flavor was good. Next time I won’t use sushi rice.

Made it pretty much to the recipe and it turned out great. Could have let the rice brown a bit more - just getting golden around the edges - but was conservative to not overcook it like some commenters reported. Next time I'd probably play around with adding more amendments to the rice to increase its flavor but I'm not sure what. This is a great basic recipe to build on.
Can you use brown rice instead of white rice?

This was excellent. I used sushi rice and reduced it to 4 cups. The rice took 25 minutes to get crispy, then the shrimp took an additional 5 minutes. I doubled the sauce and then used a third of that to stir fry slivered snow peas and red peppers, which I added to the shrimp and rice before serving.
Add a little ponzu & sriracha at the end- mmmmm
Maybe I am the only one who was dumb enough to do this but…don’t put foil on the sheet pan or you will end up using your hands to unstick the crispy rice.
No. too many strong flavours that bury the taste and texture of a well-cooked prawn.  [I refuse to call a 100g Queensland Tiger Prawn a shrimp]. Crispy rice is nice
I think baking evaporates the water and shrinks the amount of rice@gyofu
This was delish! I made 2 small changes: I didn't have lime juice so I used yuzu. I also thinly sliced and added 1/2c sugar snap peas with the shrimp. A definite keeper!
Used Japanese short grain rice and baked for 20 minutes before adding shrimp. Cooking the rice longer would have made it too crunchy and dry.
Anyone use Basmati rice instead? It’s what I have and I’m not making another trip to the store.

After seeing others' comments, I used freshly cooked medium grain rice and formed it into a 1/2" or so thick patty on the sheet pan so that the rice would brown but not dry out too much or too fast. This worked well for us. The crunchy rice still sticks in your teeth a bit, but the rice isn't overall too dry or unpleasant to eat. The flavors were 10/10.

That being said, I'd probably just make this as fried rice next time.
I thought it was good used less rice (1 1/2 cups dry, vs 2 for the recipe) and it could have used more sauce by 50%.  I would also add more jalapeno.
Really liked the umami flavor of this easy to make dish. Followed recommendation from other comments and added oil to the cooked rice before adding it to the sheet pan.
3/4-1 cp uncooked rice (for 1/2 recipe). Cooked rice 25-28 minutes. Added shrimp (large) and pkg of raw snap peas on top of rice, cooked x 6 min. Consider adding edamame instead.
The flavours were fantastic but I really don’t get the appeal of the crispy rice

I added sliced green beens to make this a one-pan meal.  I sliced the green beens into 1 inch pieces, tossed them with canola oil and salt, and added them 3 minutes before the end of the initial rice-cooking stage.
Made this and didn't change a thing. It would be just as great without the shrimp! or with Tofu! It will be in our rotation this summer as we use up our stock of brown rice.
Added a bag of snap peas with the shrimp.

I made this delicious recipe as directed. I was a little nervous because I’m so used to looking at the comments for suggestions and caveats before I give a recipe a go, and this time there were none. I did add cubed chicken breast and some fresh ginger because I could. It turned out wonderfully, so raved my husband. I guess I’m an NYT Cooking trailblazer now!

I loved this! So good! Especially the suggest to garnish with some of the onion lime juice. Never would have thought to do that! I made three small changes. 1. I added a jalapeño cut into quarters with seeds for spice when I added the carrots, etc. 2. Instead of adding salt. I threw in a Parmesan rind I had in the freezer when I added the broth. And 3. To thicken I used my emulsion blender for maybe 20 seconds.
Made it as directed and it was really good. May add some ground chicken next time, but it really doesn't need it. Made plenty and really hearty. This will go in our regular rotation!
Delish and healthy. Glad I didn’t skip the pickled red onion-my boy friend loved that garnish the most.
Based on previous comments, I added an additional tablespoon of tomato paste and finely chopped ginger. What an amazing soup - and so quick and easy to make!!
Absolutely fabulous as is with no changes!
Didn’t have gr beans, used spinach, no limes, used lemons. Delicious! The pickled onions, Parmesan on top really add goodness. Loved it !
Very simple recipe but filling and great taste. I did include fresh chopped ginder in this and it really enhanced the flavor. Great tip from Hollyahna.
@Audrey do you like wax beans? similar in shape to green beans, available canned, and often used in 3-bean salad.
Great idea! Thank you.

Made this with kidney and white beans, fresh green beans and veg broth. I thought it came out quite bland, but my husband liked it. I think I should have doubled the tomato paste - 1 tablespoon wasn't enough - and amped up the flavor in other ways, maybe with a parmesan rind or fresh herbs.

This was oddly bland.  Feeling intrepid, I added a few canned tomatoes, curry powder, cumin (quite a lot), nutmeg, and a bit of maple syrup.  I also felt it wasn't quite dense enough, so I added two small handfuls of orrecchiette.  Still simmering, but upon tasting it at this point, it seemed more flavorful.
Souperb! Doubled up on the pickled onions. They add a nice, refreshing zing.
This was easy and delicious! I don't even care for three bean salad. I ate over some farro, and it was a delicious light dinner.

Deeeeelicious. I read these comments and made a few additions: one Jalapeno, some grated ginger, extra tomato paste. Otherwise made the same as recipe.

The real star is the broth. Use a good, homemade stock if you can.

It’s excellent and easy!

My wife does not appreciate zero meat meals.  I added 1 lb of browned Italian sausage, doubled the garlic, tomato paste and ground coriander, added 1 tsp of slap-your-mama, 2 cups extra broth, 1 1/2 Tablespoons cornstarch diluted with the broth and simmered for 20 minutes.  The infused onions, lime juice, parsley and fresh grated parmesan add immensely to the

Incredible soup!  I added 1 lb of browned Italian sausage, doubled the garlic, tomato paste and ground coriander, added 1 tsp of slap-your-mama, 2
cups extra broth, 1 1/2 Tablespoons cornstarch diluted with the broth and simmered for 20 minutes. The infused onions, lime
juice, parsley and fresh grated Parmesan add immensely to the flavor.
Had no citrus so used white wine vinegar and it was fine. Splendid recipe weirdly full of flavour

Made just as described but doubled and this soup is full of flavor and health.  We loved it over rice, we love a no meat meal with so much deliciousness.  I wouldn't change a thing although the ginger sounds like it could be a nice addition.  I used the onion juice on top for added flavor and brightness.   This will be a regular in our house.

I took this in more of a Mexican flavour direction - used beef broth, added shiitake mushrooms along with a heaping teaspoon of cumin. I didn't have green beans so used hominy instead. Added cilantro at the end. Served with the pickled red onions and avocado. It was delicious!
This sounds delicious but I’m not a fan of green beans. Can anyone suggest a substitute?
@Audrey Asparagus?
Any crunchy veggie.
@Audrey Fresh peas?
Another easy and great recipe.  We don't eat onions, so originally this turned me off, but it doesn't need them.  Plenty of good flavors in a jiffy.

This is a bad recipe. I followed some of the advice on how to build more flavor, and ignored the comments about its blandness, even though it seemed like it would be bland. I would say the saving grace for my soup was the addition of fregola pasta, which makes for a wonderful texture along with the beans.  Unfortunately, this soup just lacks a delicious flavor.    I'd like to have Eric Kim fix the recipe, since whatever he seems to touch turns to gold.

A very fine, simple soup. Ha, Mr. W. A few tweaks. Go generous on veggies. 1 1/2 qt broth from veg cuttings/peelings out of freezer. 2 T tomato paste. Plenty salt and pepper. Small chunks Parmesan rind cut from 2 by 4 inch piece. 3 oz orzo to add another texture. 12 oz green beans. Otherwise, follow the program. With fresh bread and salad on porch with friends - delish.

Adapted this to my dietary requirements:
-subbed black beans for kidney
-subbed homemade tomato-free BBQ sauce for tomato paste
-omitted parmesan

I love 3-bean salad, but didn't love this. I corrected the chemistry with a little sugar and stirred in some sour cream. Together, these additions made it ok.

This is a very forgiving recipe.  I used navy beans, black beans, canned green beans and corn. I did not have black pepper so added 1/2 tsp of smoked paprika. I also threw in some chorizo. The coriander is a must. I did not have quite a quart of chicken broth. I tasted it. It was delicious so I did not add any water to thin it out.  I mashed some of the beans for a little body. The pickled onions are great. We had some leftover on sandwiches today.

My two year old ate so much of this soup. We made it as written except added frozen carrots and green beans with the broth. 

*she did pick out the celery
I used parsley instead of coriander and it was great (only because I didn’t have it)

title: Hummus Soup With Crispy Chickpeas Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: For those with dried chickpeas in the pantry, Dr. Google says, One 15-ounce can of chickpeas is equivalent to approximately 1/2 cup to 3/4 cup (roughly 3 to 4.5 ounces) of dried chickpeas. When soaked and cooked, this amount of dried beans will yield about 1.5 cups of cooked chickpeas, which matches the drained weight of a standard 15-ounce can.
I've done this with the addition of charred eggplants before, I called it Babaga-soup.
@Eric. Love the play on words! Will try that. Combining baba with hummus elements into a soup.
@Eric do you have chunks of eggplant or do you blend it with a food processor/immersion blender along with the rest?

The cilantro or dill and lemon are both essential, and sadly, because I did not have any herbs on hand, I was too sick to run out to the store, and I was eager to make this soup, because I love chickpeas and tahini and had just made a batch of my own chickpeas this morning, I left out the herbs, which was a mistake and I suspect would have provided some much needed flavor and punch, even with freshly made homemade chickpeas. To make matters worse, I suspect that this soup relies heavily on salt. which I tend to avoid due to my multiple sclerosis. My soup also turned out very thick, and I used the chickpea cooking broth. I think this soup has potential, but as I said, I believe the herbs are essential, which I stupidly omitted.

I would recommend roasting the chickpeas in the oven on a sheet pan, or cast iron pan. I made the recipe for two, but almost used the amount of broth recommended for 4-6! Very filling, nutritious, and very tasty. Salt is indeed needed, as I used a low sodium broth and found myself adding more salt.
It was ok. Honestly it was the consistency of cement even with an extra cup of broth. Kind of hard to get through.
I am confused at the appeal of this soup. I think I would just eat hummus with toppings with a spoon and call it a day.
@Melissa , you made me laugh out  loud! I’m not a lover of tahini so I’m going to use almond butter instead.

Found this in my local newspaper and was surprised that it was also here.   Almost didn’t make it based on the reviews but it was delicious!   Doubled the lemon (as I do on any recipe calling for lemon) and used cilantro.  The first step of fried chickpeas didn’t turn out crispy and may skip that step next time as it didn’t add anything except extra time.
Quite bland and uninspired.  A good waste of chickpeas.

I did not love this. It seemed very bland. I used dry chick peas rehydrated with Instant Pot. I added a carrot for nutrients. I loved the crispy ones. I may have over-chick pea'd in the soup part. I really wanted to love this. I did use parsley (fresh) instead of dill or cilantro which we are not fans of ... It just seemed to be lacking some ooomph. I thought it would be more like the umami vibe of fetteh but it just wasn't ... hoping for some tips, I'd try it again...
I followed the recipe and we enjoyed this soup a lot.  Had I had more lemon, I wouöd have put in more.
Yeah, it was ok on day one, and inedible on day two. Fun idea, but not the best use of chickpeas.

I like a lot of spice, so I used hot pepper flakes, black pepper and white pepper in soup in addition to on "crisped" (never got crispy) chic peas. I also used an entire lemon's juice. It was tasty, but felt like I was drinking humus. Maybe best as a muse bouche in the winter.
Where’s the hummus? Or is the second batch of immersion blended chickpeas considered hummus?

The soup itself is "the hummus", as it has hummus base ingredients, hummus flavor, and is added to broth (unlike hummus)  and pureed to have a smooth texture like hummus. 

The first can of chickpeas is turned into a topping for the soup.
@Christine chickpeas and tahini are the basics of hummus.
@Christine the soup is basically a recipe for hummus plus broth
Any thoughts  to pureeing chickpeas prior to adding to pot? And how about pureeing the onions prior to cooking?

My thoughts on pureeing the onions be4 cooking: they will be wet mush and will not caramelize/brown, as  it will make them release a lot of onion juice since you are breaking down the cell walls. But you can always try it and report back :)

@Not really Katie I find onions cook better* when cut with a knife and then purée to get that lovely sweet-savoury flavour perfumed throughout the whole soup to ward away bland flavour. *I have to add water to blend onions so you start with evaporating the water before the onions cook, and they inevitably burn when you're not looking/impatiently waiting for something to happen. Chickpeas blended with their water "aquafaba" adds extra flavour and makes them creamier. Easier to blend them at the beginning to ensure they get pulverised.

Make non spicy ones with cheese it crackers (could add nutritional yeast)
Reduce salt in both recipes 

Absolutely not worth the effort. Make Hummus or some other soup with chickpeas. Do not recommend.
Hate garlic - can I just leave it out and use something else for added flavor?

Tuesdays always bring me joy and love…bringing a healthy, homemade, love-filled lunch like this  gives me a spring in my step!  Last week at lunch with Gilda’s Club/Cancer Care Center of NY/CT one of the women in treatment for cancer shared with me that she wants to volunteer at an upcoming Love is… Community Supper…she talked about how doing something nice for others will help her heal and feel warm inside…that’s what Love is.  I added some paprika to finish for color and pizzazz!
Can you serv e this cold in the summer
I’m actually not a great tahini lover (I know—how can that be?!) so I’ll  either use much less tahini & waay more lemon & herbs  or may skip the tahini altogether & add almond butter instead!

This sounded weird and kinda… gross? 
BUT when you use fresh herbs and a good squeeze of lemon juice, it’s really really good.

At the beginning the recipe says:”In a large soup pot, heat 1 tablespoon olive oil over medium-high. Add the chickpeas and ½ teaspoon salt, and cook, stirring occasionally, until crisped, 6 to 10 minutes.”

Later on it says to add the remaining chick peas. What remaining chickpeas?

title: Pasta Pesto Soup With Turkey and Spinach Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: Used homemade chicken Italian sausage (fennel is lovely with the pesto/lemon!). Browned with onion/garlic. EASILY doubled so use a pound of orzo. Recipe used, method changed. HATE pasta left in broth; it swells/absorbs ALL liquid. Cooked meat/onion/garlic, pepper, added broth; added pesto when I added baby greens, so flavor was still super-bright and fresh; turned off heat, to just melt the pesto/wilt greens. Cooked pasta separately; added to bowls, then ladled soup. Ricotta on table.

Sorry if this is a duplicate message. I have always been taught that one never ever cooks pesto. And now I’m being told to cook it for up to 7 min on medium heat? Doesn’t heat destroy all that’s great about pesto and make it dark and bitter?

@Brisketboy If you’re using it on pasta don’t heat. It does affect flavor and color.
But this is a soup recipe. If you think you won’t like heated pesto don’t make it
@Brisketboy I think in this case it hold up very well. Try it and I think you’ll find it does as well. Think of it as a soup base and not a fresh addition …maybe?
@Brisketboy Tastes pretty delicious to me!! And, the aroma is sublime.

Keeper recipe!  Used rotisserie chicken leftover (about 1 lb.)  Added 2 extra cups broth because the orzo absorbs the liquid if you let the soup sit too long before eating. Added extra spinach and a bit more lemon.  Topped with grated parmesan cheese.

@Chris Same here as probably with everyone else. The cup of orzo absorbed almost all the chicken stock. Luckily I had extra on had so added about 2 1/2 cups more. And freshly grated Parmesan and fresh found black pepper to serve was great.
I'm looking forward to cooking this. I always find it is easier to brown the turkey if you add it right after the onions but before anything that is 'liquidy'. So turkey then pesto.

This is a delicious, well-balanced soup.  I followed the recipe as written except used turkey broth and leftover roast turkey out of the freezer, in the same proportions as the recipe.  Comfort in a bowl.  We didn't think it needed ricotta but some grated Parmesan was really good.  I am not sure the recipe would comfortably serve 4.  Definitely 3...

@CRH. I also made this using broth and turkey from a carcass plus homemade pesto base from my freezer. No cheese in the.base .. I shredded the turkey  and then followed the recipe exactly.. it is delicious and hearty… the lemon brings  it to life!

This  recipe sounds delicious but I have to agree with Brisketboy, pesto isn’t supposed to be cooked. Has anyone browned the turkey and added the pesto when they added the broth? Otherwise it seems the pesto will lose its brightness. Curious to hear from others.
@KG  I have heard this advice but I use pesto as a condiment like mustard or gochujang.
This was delicious and I would make it again. The only change I made was adding a bit more red pepper flakes and a few turns of the pepper grinder. I also used burrata instead of ricotta.
@Lesley did you add the burrata in pieces? Or just the filling? Did it mix with the broth?
@Lesley i like the idea of using Burrata. Good one!
@Lesley Buratta instead of Ricotta?? You gotta!!

Great recipe! I made it as written, with the exception of the pesto. I used four heaping tablespoon, half added when I added the stock, the other half added when I put in the spinach. (I did not want to cook the pesto, but I wanted it to build flavor in the broth AND enable me to gauge how much salt and pepper to add.) Three ample servings for folks looking to make a meal of this dish. I can easily see how to do a lot of riffs on this excellent recipe: Add beans instead of turkey, simply add extra veg, try different greens, replace pesto with another sauce with a different flavor profile … I think it has a lot of potential for freezing, both as-is and as a base without the pasta and spinach. Ricotta was nice but not necessary. I had the last of the soup for breakfast without any ricotta :)
Omg, this is good. How could something this simple be so good?

This was easy to modify as vegan, and so, so delicious. In preparing a double recipe, I used a supermarket vegan pesto--Fillipo Berio--and a block of tofu in a large dice, parboiled in saltwater to keep its shape. Even with skipping the ricotta, it was splendidly rich and pleased the omnivores and vegans alike in my house. Easy after a long day of work, and impressive.
Made this with one pound of ground turkey - made it a bit heartier! Otherwise made as written.

Delicious and so healthy! 
I added some celery and carrots for color! 
I agree that browning the turkey would add some appeal to the color but I the flavor was still excellent!  A keeper for sure!

I liked this. It tasted better on day 2, as many soups do. I'll leave out the lemon next time even though I adore lemon. (I dispute the notion that everything must have a bit of acid in it.) I served the soup with cubed Stella Fontinella cheese (kind of like a sharp provolone) rather than ricotta, as I felt it needed flavor. I highly recommend cooking and storing the orzo separately if you want any broth left on day 2. The orzo will absorb all of it.
I doubled the recipe (but used 1.5 c. Orzo) and added a can of Cannelini beans. An easy addition to the winter rotation!
This was great! Made with very good store-bought pesto made at local cheese shop and due to local power outages could only get chicken. Otherwise double recipe, as written. Excellent!
Really good but as others have said, the orzo absorbs a LOT of the broth - making it more stew like than soupy. Maybe increase broth AND pesto, or decrease the orzo.

I just made this soup, and my family and I enjoyed it very much. I followed the recipe except I put half the pesto. We like pesto, but not if it’s overwhelming. While I like ricotta a lot, I would not use it again in this soup. These are just obviously personal preferences.  It’s so nice to have a soup with a unique flavor. I would make it again for sure.

Made last night. It was good! Definitely benefited from the hit of lemon/acid and a little more red pepper. You’re not going to get this luscious green in the photo unless the store bought pesto is lusciously green — ours was not vibrant, nor was our soup even with the last minute addition of spinach. Still tasted like pesto (and a well-rounded dinner).
Made this yesterday. Added diced carrot, some minced garlic, and some frozen peas.  Needed to add extra stock.  Delicious!
Nice soup! I too had a quart bag of Thanksgiving turkey in my freezer and fresh kale and a jar of pesto in the fridge. Really nice on a snowy day for Sunday lunch for my teen.

Perfect soup for a cold day. I doubled recipe but realized (too late) I did not have enough pasta. Added a can of drained/rinsed northern beans. Substituting beans for some pasta=happy accident. Keeper!
This was fantastic! I added in a can of white beans as well, which was FANTASTIC!

This was so so good and easy. I used 1 lb. Ground turkey, ~5 C homemade chicken broth, probably 3/4 C pesto (dropped in frozen chunks so I’m uncertain of the volume), and added a few extra handfuls of kale from the garden. I also added the pesto with the broth step, as others suggested. I didn’t want to stop eating!

I made this for dinner and it was fantastic! Super flavourful and very filling. It came together so easily too, one I will definitely make again. The only comment I have is that if you have leftovers you’ll need to add some more liquid when you go to eat it! The orzo soaked up the extra overnight. It was still delicious and didn’t loose much flavour!
This is so easy to make and delicious! I used stock instead of broth, because that was what I had on-hand, but it was absolutely delicious!
I had all ingredients except ground turkey, so I used sweet Italian sausage. So delicious and easy!

This soup is fabulous and so easy to make.  When I told my husband what I was making, he didn't think he'd like it. He loved it and had seconds. This one will definitely be in the rotation. Because I had 1.3 lb of turkey, I doubled the recipe, which I would recommend doing. It's that good. Next time I'll try it with the ricotta.
@Diane My husband was skeptical, too! It was so delicious. I used Italian sausage and added kidney beans. I sprinkled it with Parmesan cheese before serving.
Intend to make, pointing out though, i spent months in emiglia romana region and absolutely cooked pesto (lasgana a ligure is now my preferred version now).

Ms. Clark - this is one of your better conceived soups.  I myself like the turkey because without meat sometimes it's not a meal.   Alas, a man needs coal for the furnace.  Although I like the cheese, I don't dig ricotta on salads, soups, pizza.   So I as in Marcella Hazan's rice and spinach soup I will use Parmigiano-Reggiano.  Fresh lemon off the tree in AZ makes it.

Excellent, easy, fast. I used ground dark meat turkey and Trader Joes vegan pesto - the freshest pesto that doesn't break the bank (add parm). Added a can of cannellini beans and chicken bone broth that had been slow cooked from yesterday.  I like ditalini and always cook my pasta seperate. Definitely would not have been enough liquid if I cooked in the pot.  I found 1/2 lemon to be perfect.  Next time will absolutely do the ricotta. Yum!

I'm going to convert this to a vegetarian soup and see what happens.  Maybe use a pasta more like fusilli or penne.  And, just a little bit of a complaint:  An ingredient like "1 onion" seems to assure cooks that they are in charge of deciding just how much onion they want in a soup. Onions come in all sizes.

I bought a box of orzo specifically for this soup, because in my 72 years I’ve never cooked orzo. I know, strange, right? This soup is So Yummy! I found a small jar of pesto in my pantry that I had picked up months ago from Trader Joe’s. And I had turkey with broth in my freezer, left over from Thanksgiving. Meyer lemons from the back yard. I love it when everything comes together so beautifully and easily!
Delicious recipe, came together very easily.  Thanks NYT Cooking…where were you guys when I was working full time and raising a family!
This dish is so yummy I needed to freeze the balance to stop from eating it all.   Second time making it, doubled the recipe, and added more turkey — a delightful and easy recipe.
I added chopped zucchini and used a pound of ground turkey. It ended up being somewhere between a soup and a stew, but it was delicious and I’ll definitely add it to my rotation.

Used up some leftovers to make this - ground beef instead of turkey, rigatoni instead of orzo, and added half a can of white beans. Otherwise, followed the recipe and it turned out really delicious - hearty, and perfect for an easy dinner while watching the Olympics!
Made exactly as directed minus the red pepper flakes. Very tasty. The 5-yo and 3-yo both loved it. Husband loved it too FWIW but he can't smell and he loves everything
Really nice soup!  The lemon is a MUST.  Also to be a bit brighter, only cook half the pesto and save the rest for the end.

very tasty. i used no salt broth and didn’t add any salt at all, but it was a little salty anyway because of the store-bought pesto. i cut the soup with a cup or so of water — that fixed my salt issue. i will definitely put this into our rotation.
@mimi Thank you. I found it a bit too salty, also.

Very good, easy and forgiving recipe. I used 400 g of turkey mince (browned after the onion but before adding the pesto), probably 1 1/4 cup of orzo and 1 L of stock because I was using up the last of those ingredients. The result was that it was more like a risotto than a soup but delicious nevertheless.

Melissa Clark’s recipes are always Tops in my book!
I made this today during “Hernando”.  & it is Deeeeelicious!
Used a full lb. of ground turkey (only size at store) & browned it after onions.
In place of plain chicken broth, I substituted Chicken Bone Broth for added protein, added to browned turkey & onions & brought to a simmer.
Then stirred in half the pesto & simmered gently some more.
Instead of spinach, I cooked one 10oz. Pkg. of frozen chopped Kale separately in the microwave.

Powerful flavor due to the pesto! As others have noted, it doesn't keep well with the orzo in it, as the orzo absorbs all the liquid and becomes bloated, so in future I'd separate them if I wanted to meal prep with it. But this was really delicious day-of! Would definitely use a full pound of turkey for a heartier dish.
I don’t like basil. Please suggest a sub for pesto. Maybe more spinach, parm, and ground nuts.

@rebecca when I haven’t had basil, I’ve subbed oregano, arugula (I know that sounds weird) or parsley. Just try it with something you like. I’m sure you can search alternate pesto recipes to find something. I think I’ve also seen an artichoke pesto which sounds delish.
I used TraderJoe's kale pesto.

@rebecca
Parsley pesto is delicious — might work well here.

I made this tonight and actually followed the recipe (shocker!) and I love it…will definitely make it again. I used Pesto Genovese. The only slight deviation was using Buffalo Burrata instead of ricotta. And it was fabulous!
The ground chicken crumbled and mixed with the pesto and onion so that in the end it tasted and had texture of crumbled chicken sausage in the soup

I made the soup and stirred in the pesto at the last minute. I added white beans and cooked pasta and broccoli because I didn't have spinach. I also added a teaspoon of Italian seasoning and chopped fresh rosemary. Delicious!

Great Recipe! I did add the pesto with the broth, instead of adding it with the turkey and it turned out great! The parmesan in it though did end up sticking on the bottom, but I didn't have that hard of a time cleaning it.

I love a weeknight meal that requires minimal effort and ingredients and has high payoff. This soup has deeper flavors than a soup that’s only cooked for a half hour. It’s going into our deep winter soup rotation…the spinach, lemon, and pesto remind us that warmer weather WILL eventually come. Enjoy your dinner, everyone!
Fast, easy, and delicious! Definitely  a keeper. Didn't  add extra pesto at the end but did add dollop of ricotta.  Thanks Melissa.

Really delicious.   Signature Melissa Clark to squeeze that lemon over it once it’s done but don’t mix it in…forget that step at your own peril.  If I didn’t have a great Italian market with freshly made pesto or some I made and froze and also access the hand dipped ricotta. I probably wouldn’t even attempt this.. pesto from a jar or ricotta from a tub in the dairy case, hard pass.
@anthony I think ricotta from the case at Trader Joe’s 2ork Ed beautifully.

some suggest not adding the pesto until the end
may need more broth because orzo absorbs it  
some used different meats, e.g. rotisserie chicken

Very good, very fast.  Luckily I had all the ingredients in my fridge, so it was pantry easy too.  I made half a recipe (we're only 2 people) but it was enough for 2 dinners and a small bowl left over for lunch.  I served the pasta on the side so it wouldn't absorb all the broth.  The broth was scrumptious.
Wondering how folks think risotto or rice would work in place of the orzo, to make this gluten free?

I made this because I had everything, or something similar, on hand. I used a half pound of turkey breakfast sausage, a half package of frozen chopped spinach, half of a 7-oz container of pesto, and a 9-oz package of fresh cheese mini ravioli. It came out very nice. The only thing missing was the red pepper flakes, and I will definitely use them next time.

I followed this recipe to the tee, but all of the liquid/broth boiled away. It seems like it would need two quarts of chicken broth to look like the photo? Also, there are no instructions to cover the pot, which would likely preserve the broth?
This delicious recipe is improved by adding parmesan rinds as the soup cooks.

This was surprisingly delicious! I did not expect much from it.
I doubled the amount of ground turkey (1lb total) and added more water (2-3 cups) to make it soupier. 
Since I’m fighting a cold I added some ginger together with the onion. I only added the pesto at the end as per some comments here (not sure if it makes any difference).

Easy to make.  Followed the directions except had to substitute pearl couscous for the pasta because I didn't have the orzo I thought I had.  It was great.  Definitely need to up the salt, pepper and lemon juice to get the maximum flavor.  Added grated parmesan instead of ricotta at the end which also helped with the flavor.   Cooking pesto with the turkey burger is fine, in fact it's what helps with the flavor
