We loved this soup. I doubled the turkey to use up the full package and was glad I did. We enjoyed the thicker consistency vs. a more soupy soup, but more broth could be added depending on your preference. The only other change I made was to dollop the ricotta on each individual bowl. Next time I might sprinkle some lemon zest on top of the ricotta to make it look fancy. There will be a next time!

Yum!!! Made mostly as written, though I used 1 lb. of turkey, and glad I did.  I put the pesto in at the end of the turkey browning step, just to make it easier to monitor the color of the turkey. At the end of the meal (for 2 people), as I was putting away the leftovers, I added another 8 oz of chicken broth to the leftovers.  The next day the orzo was twice as large, and still had enough broth so I'm glad I added the liquid. I thought the ricotta and pesto drizzle made it way more interesting.

I enjoyed this recipe!! I just substituted a plant-based ground for the ground turkey and I added mushrooms and omitted the pasta.  I also added a parmesan rind in the end after allowing the soup to simmer.  Super easy, with most ingredients in house, I would definitely put this into my soup rotation.  It was very successful and everyone loved it!

This soup is excellent.
Like others, I used leftover roasted turkey rather than ground.
I suggest waiting to put in salt until the broth and pesto are added, then salting to taste. I did not do this and it was quite salty. Even so, very delicious!
@Chris - pasta in soup hack ( found out by accident) - use Gluten free corn pasta. it doesn't get mushy. even stands up to freezing & reheating. I've only used LeVeneziane brand, total game changer

The broth looked gray and unappetizing....something out of Oliver Twist. One you added in the spinach it got better, and it tasted great with a dollop of ricotta. I got a lot of teasing from naysayers who didn't think it looked appealing. Would I make it again? Probably not.

Added a couple of handfuls of cherry tomatoes with the onions for color and additional flavor.  I used 6 cups of turkey broth, and then froze half for another dinner.  Any small pasta can be substituted.  Ricotta is essential when serving.

This was a very satisfying, easy to make dinner that will go into the rotation. I agree with others  that sautéing  the pesto was odd. Next time I make this, I plan to try adding it with the pasta because for the amount of pesto added, it wasn't that pesto-y.

I just made this for the second time and it’s delicious. I had sweet Italian chicken sausage so I used that instead and almost halved the pesto (Kirkland, which I stirred in with the stock) because of the spices in the sausage. So easy, and so much taste for minimal effort.
Very delicious! We changed the cheese for goat cheese. Amazing!

My modifications:
- Used rotisserie chicken instead of ground turkey
- Added celery
- Added the pesto at the end
- Mixed in a half cup of full fat yogurt for a cream element.

What a clever way to use up pesto! Delicious too. I was out of ground turkey and spinach, so I cubed zucchini and added it partway through the onion sautéing, then added the frozen meatballs shortly before the broth. This was so good. I was concerned about the cheese in the pesto being sautéed, but as long as you make sure to stir thoroughly along the bottom of the pot, it doesn't stick. The squeeze of lemon at the end gave a lovely bright note. Delicious, flexible, and going into the rotation!
Made it with Casarecce, instead of macaroni - because that’s what I had.  Absolutely delicious!!  Thank you, once again, dear NYT Cooking App!!

Don't forget to add lemon juice at the end to brighten up this rather bland and greasy dish. Also it needs to be eaten right away, otherwise you'll end up with a greasy, soggy, mushy glob of pasta as it absorbs all the liquid over night.
This is another recent example of an overhyped and underwhelming NYT recipe...
This is a great, quick recipe! Didn’t have ricotta so I added a few spoonfuls of plain Greek yogurt to individual bowls and really liked the added creaminess!

Quick, simple, and healthy! I love that you can adjust this recipe so easily while maintaining the base flavors (I added mushrooms and drizzled crème fraiche I had left over). Can’t wait to make this again!

Start with very light on red pepper flakes.  Used full pound ground turkey and extra  + cups broth plus additional onion.  Full jar of Marzetti  pesto.  Kept a few scoops to add at very end before serving for brightness.  Had no ricotta so let cottage cheese dry out a bit in sue
Ve over bowl.

Delicious! Beloved by kids and adults. Followed exactly except added chopped red bell pepper. Also put a dollop of ricotta on each serving rather than in the whole pot. Definitely adding to the rotation!
Delicious. Used store bought pesto, used elbow pasta and chicken thighs instead of turkey. Mixed in shredded parm in the ricotta and whipped and added as topping.
This was so simple and delish! Definite do again. I substituted ground chicken for the turkey and did a dollop of sour cream. So yummy.
Easy and full of flavor—great use of leftover pesto. Could sub chickpeas for meat. Grate lemon rind for fruitier lemon essence. Unless you’re eating it all at once cook pasta separately. Keeper!
I used store bought pesto, orzo, and low fat cottage cheese. It was yummy!
This was so fast and delicious.  All said to keep the recipe! Followed recipe to a T. Yum!

Freezing pesto in 4 ounce jars and lemon juice in small cubes (transferred to an airtight container) makes this recipe easy any night. Substitute a drained can of white beans for the pasta if you want more fiber and better leftovers. Just keep the spinach separate if you plan for leftovers - and you should because you will want it the next day.

Loved this & no, cooking the pesto did not make it bitter. I added lemon zest & a small amount of juice (to taste) to the ricotta, reduced the added lemon to the soup to compensate for the ricotta- delicious!

Really love the soup. When I made it the second time I decided to add some chopped canned artichokes and love the addition.
I’m gluten-free so I don’t add the orzo I simply cook Ramen noodles to order.

Really delicious. Great way for me to use it all those turkey burgers I have in my freezer in addition to all the pesto I made from last summer’s basil. As I’m on a low-carb diet I did leave out the pasta, but it doesn’t really need it.

Super easy.. definitely add the pesto at the end with the spinach. Also cook orzo separately and add with spinach.. The lemon  and ricotta are a must. Otherwise great and super easy. Oh, i added chopped garlic, cause garlic.
Quick, easy and delicious. I doubled the soup and added potato instead of pasta. Fab. Filling.

This is a really satisfying dish. Like so many others, I also found the orzo soaked up most of the broth creating more of a stew. While that was delicious, I think the solution would be to reduce the orzo by half or parboil (ugh, another step!) before adding. As a pesto addict, I did add more pesto using about 1/3 cup with turkey in step 2 and another 1/3 cup after orzo cooked. Overall this recipe is a keeper for a fast dinner.
Delicious simple recipe especially with store bought pesto. Next time I would use about 1/2 the amount of orzo though, I prefer it a little more “brothy “.

We loved this soup.  I used chicken thighs cut into 1/2 inch chunks and pesto that we froze last September.
Definitely a keeper.
I modified with TVP and a can of chickpeas. I doubled the recipe, and it was a winner. I can’t get over how simple and delicious this is. So many ways to make it your own after the first steps.

Minimum effort maximum flavor!  Doubled the recipe, I had leftover boiled baby potatoes so used that instead of orzo.  Also used half spinach/half kale since I like a heartier green.  Delicious addition to my soup repertoire.

This recipe was just OK. Not the best recipe I’ve ever cooked on NYT Cooking. I was strict with the recipe. I did notice another comment stated they don’t like adding pasta during the cooking process. I agree because it just continues to absorb water and cook and turns to mush. I wasn’t going to go the extra mile and cook the pasta separately for a trial run. I’ll make it again because it’s a quick and easy recipe but it’s a definitely  an “only for me“ dish.
Outstanding and will add to the rotation; great as a substitute to chili and very satisfying. I doubled the recipe and put in two pounds of turkey as family likes heavy protein.

First attempt I also added more broth and a bit of MSG to enhance the flavour. Ricotta did enhance for me. Then created a different version add g additional chopped Italian veggies, beans instead of orzo, and kale instead of spinach, also good!

Love this soup! Doubled the recipe but only used a generous 1/2 cup of orzo rather than the amount specified; it seems to be the right proportion. Based on comments r.e. not cooking pesto, I waited to add it my soup bowl.

I agree that the orzo absorbs all the liquid but I thought it was still tasty that way. Added the pesto after I added the chicken broth and let it simmer. Overall very easy and stress free and tasted great!
Apologize for not reading through comments, but adding 1C. Orzo....dried or cooked. 1Cup dried would not have made it soup, more like a blob. I added 12. c. dried and it still was very thick

I doubled the recipe because I had a pound of ground turkey. The soup was delicious. I think doubling the turkey and just using the original recipe would have been fine, but we have some rad leftovers. @Caryn O
Just saying that turkey is not vegetarian of course so I am not sure why it is on the easy vegetarian recipes list. :)
Great soup! I had to add extra broth because it got down to more of a stew. I'll make this again.

title: One-Pot Cheesy Orecchiette With Cabbage and Paprika Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody:
I used a deep skillet for this, but next time I would use a dutch oven or pot. Using tongs to toss the raw cabbage while it's wilting was key - did a twisting motion and scraped the bottom so leeks didn't get too singed. I do think "tossing frequently" should be included in the directions for the 15 minute cook time for the cabbage. Echoing the other commenter that said this - let the cabbage go for the full 15. Let it get brown and toasty. 

Next time, I will boil pasta while building the sauce. The pasta wasn't very seasoned. We ended up adding more salt and vinegar at the end, which I was surprised by. We squeezed half a lemon at the very end before serving. Dusting of smoked paprika and dill really made this. 

Rule in our house is to yell "there's a leek in the boat" several times while cooking leeks. :) Don't forget this necessary step!

A classy Mac n Cheese.  Gruyere makes anything special. I made it with some substitutes, which I had on hand, such as red (purple) cabbage,  a white onion instead of the leek, and chicken broth, rather than vegetable broth.  I used DeLallo Shellbows.  Otherwise, I followed the recipe, and it turned out succulent and tasty. My husband loved it, and I will be making it again, probably with the listed ingredients. A winner.
My wife and I really enjoyed this dish!  I added browned andouille sausage, thinly sliced mushrooms and white beans and it turned out excellent - a really well-rounded pasta!  Thanks Melissa
@Ryan I laughed at this review! You made something completely different, but it sounds delicious. I might try it.

This was absolutely divine! I set a timer for 15 minutes on the cabbage and I’m glad I did because I thought it was “collapsed” and ready to move on way too soon, glad the timer held me accountable to letting it cook fully as it turned out much nicer than it would have if I was eyeballing it. I also was a little nervous about the dill so tasted it without (excellent) then sprinkled a little and tasted again (not for me) so I opted not to add it. 

The flavors in this are so wonderfully layered, I didn’t even make it to the kitchen table, just ate it right off the pot on the stove 😅 this recipe is a winner!

This really caught my eye. I'm a vegan, so will try it with Kite Hill almond yogurt instead of sour cream and ground almonds and walnuts instead of Gruyère. If I'm feeling luxurious I may throw in a small handful of Good Planet olive oil Mozzarella shreds at the end. (A great brand of vegan cheese with only one gram of saturated fat instead of 5 or 6 or more per serving.)  And I'll use scallions instead of leeks.

@Bett

If you do all this, please report back how the vegan version turns out!
@Bett Rebel Gruyère is a great vegan cheese
Excellent and easy. Used smoked gruyere.

I have a comment on the author, more than the dish. Melissa Clark, you are a PHENOM! I am so grateful to you for making my kitchen life more interesting, unique and pleasurable! Your book “Dinner In One” has a permanent home on my coffee table (right beside Eric’s “Vegetable Simple’ ). And no, I am not her publicist lol! The substitutions save the day at least once a week, the recipes, without fail, have brought so much joy, inspiration, and sated bellies. So, THANK YOU!
@Lise Sasson Melissa Clark is the very best!

Made this for dinner last night b/c we had all the essential ingredients on hand. Subbed onion for the leek and yogurt for the sour cream. Also used about 1.5 C of shredded gruyère. It was good. We all liked it fine, but to me, the dill added an odd note. If I were to make it again, I would omit or sub with parsley.

@philly cook
Hmmm -dill and cabbage are a classic combo!

The only things I changed in the recipe (besides halving it since it was just me) was using a combination of cheddar and Parmesan reggiono as that was what I had on hand. It was delicious and will definitely make again.
Imagine my surprise when “One-Pot Cheesy Orecchiette With Cabbage and Paprika” only asks for half of a teaspoon of paprika.

Fantastic. Cabbage is the secret star. I had to make do with what I had (snowed in) and used truffle cheese and parmesan I had on hand, and shallots instead of leeks. I omitted the paprika because I was worried it would fight with the truffle flavor, added parsley at the end instead of dill and a bit of truffle oil to finish; it was very difficult to stop going back to the pot for seconds.... and thirds.
Cut the pasta to 8 oz but kept everything else the same

I found way too much pasta. I cut in half and still found I wanted more leeks and cabbage, which were delicious. 
1/4 pound pasta per person is excessive I believe.
@JaneCollinsdid you cut both pasta and broth in half to achieve this? Or only the pasta?
Making this right now and so far, delicious!! I cannot wait to try it all together! I am positive this will be just amazing!!
Delicious and creamy! Added hot Italian sausage

Really elegant meal. I let the cabbage and leeks cook down til caramelized, even charred in spots. Used a full tsp of paprika during cooking and more vinegar to finish it. It was smoky, tangy, creamy, and deeply flavored. Fantastic.
Great recipe! I love Melissa Clark.
Can we substitute something else for cabbage?  I find it too bitter honestly.

I'm sure you can substitute whatever you like.  Perhaps try kale or spinach.  But you might be surprised if you try it with cabbage, cooked as Melissa Clark suggests -- sliced fine and cooked slowly, then boiled with the pasta likely takes away the bitterness

Have you tried savoy cabbage? Delicate, not bitter, tender, lasts forever in the fridge, cooks faster, cheap, and works in place of ordinary green cabbage in most recipes if you shorten the cooking time.

@Ken in DFW I think a few things contribute to cabbage tasting bitter: the quality (some larger grocery stores’ cabbage is very old;it’s worth grabbing at a grocer where you know the produce turns over often) & how it’s cooked. When cooked long and low, cabbage actually becomes quite sweet. One of my favorite ways to cook it is inspired by Chez Panisse’s ‘Braised Red Cabbage,’  but I always at least double the cooking time. Even my kids gobble it up! 
When I make this recipe, I’ll probably cook the cabbage for more like 45 min ~ or more, depending on the quality & how long it takes to get that sweet, caramelized flavor. I think cabbage is underappreciated mostly because folks haven’t had it prepared properly, + purple cabbage is cheap & super high in antioxidants :)

This caught my eye as a way to use up the cabbage I had in the fridge. Nice recipe. I felt like it could have taken a little more than 1/2 pound of cabbage. Didn't have any veggie stock so used water with a bay leaf and some extra herbs. Used Fontina instead of Gruyere since Gruyere is so expensive. Finally, used whole wheat pasta and that worked fine, just took a few extra minuets to cook.
@SK19118 it *does* call for more than 1/2 pound cabbage, actually; about a pound it says, 1/2 a cabbage. 🙂
Anybody tried this with the precut coleslaw pkgs from the grocery ?  (Lazy cook🙂)

@Beverly Another (sometime) lazy cook here: I used to rely on shredded cabbage and carrots until I cooked a carrot cake that had a mouthfeel of beach sand. The precut veg dry out terribly, beyond refreshing in recipes, no matter how juicy, and consider, too, the loss of vitamins, etc. I now roll up my sleeves to slice and dice.
Delicious. This is a keeper. I love that it’s a one pot meal.
Big hit all around! Love how the cabbage reduces to a savory sweet base.  Used orecchiette, mascarpone, little extra vinegar, and topped with fresh dill. Excellent!
Yum! Used more than one leek and ended up sautéing ground chicken and mixing that in at the end for added protein. Husband loved it, I did too.

Very good. I loved the cabbage and leek base. I halved the pasta amount but kept the original leek/cabbage measurements - actually wish I made more of the cabbage/leek mixture so it would shine more.  Next time I might cut the  cheese/sour cream.

I plan to make this tomorrow, trying out some high protein (chickpea) cavatappi from Costco. Will use mandoline to slice the cabbage. I always reduce the amount of pasta in NYT recipes by half or more. I love orecchiette and I rub the dry pasta with some olive oil before cooking to keep the "little ears" from sticking together.

This is a FLAVOR BOMB!  I doubled it and took the long road for caramelization (took about an hour). Because I doubled it, the croutons called for 2 tsp thyme, which made it a bit too thyme-y IMO. Next time, I’ll probably pull back on the soy and balsamic and eliminate salt completely, or use no sodium veg broth. But, it tasted authentic!  The croutons hack with melted Gruyère made it super easy to serve. Beans add a heartiness so it can stand alone as the entree.

@I love this recipe! 
Croutons, thyme, balsamic, beans…. None of these ingredients are in the recipe. Maybe your comments belong to a different one?

@I love this recipe!
What croutons ?

Added mild Italian turkey sausage and substituted chicken broth for the vegetable broth. 
Excellent!
Reminds me of a haluski. Made it exactly as specified and it was delicious.

Perfect winter meal! I halved the pasta and used 3 cups of chicken broth.  I did have to drain some broth before adding the cheese but that was fine. Added walnuts and chicken sausage. I planned to add cannellini beans as well but forgot to thaw. Served with simply dressed arugula. Adding extra protein and veggies to dishes like this keeps them a part of my healthy diet. Loved it.

Much gratitude for the large bag of pre-shredded cabbage from Trader Joe's, about to turn, stashed at the bottom of my veggie drawer. Where did it come from? My memory fails me. But I do know that I'm really trying to waste less food in 2026, and I do know that I will need inspiration to turn the sad bag of pale green into something at least somewhat appealing to my family. This recipe inspired, and it delivered. If you are at all drawn to it, do it! It's flexible.

Delicious! I used 1/2 lb. of orecchiette + 1 can of white beans. Getting to ~1 lb. of cabbage used 3/4 of a medium-sized savoy cabbage. Cooking times worked great. Added a bit more smoked paprika and vinegar. Felt like a hearty Alpine winter dinner. I may make again during the Winter Olympics!
@Christine Did this exact thing and it was awesome!

Fantastic recipe, great flavor, delicious.  Made according to recipe but would make a couple changes, use penne pasta or macaroni and add more cabbage.  I felt the Orecchiette was too heavy and some stuck together.
Just made this and it is delicious! I used way more paprika, more pepper, other than that exactly as written. My heritage is Hungarian, this pasta has a chicken paprikas vibe, and is really hearty.

Kf I wanted to add a protein could I use rotisserie chicken and not ruin this dish? Would it mess it up, flavor or texture-wise?
Thx!
@Dan I was thinking about adding tuna — like a tuna casserole.
@Dan you could add white beans and or a little bit of sausage to add in some more protein

@Dan sounds good to me, but it makes me a little sad to see someone ask if they can add something they like. Within reason, adding something good will be good - it will be different, but good. Cooking is art. Make it your own!
Of course, you could just mix a bit of chicken into a sample of the finished pasta and see if you like it, but where’s the adventure in that, and anyway, what are the chances you wouldn’t? I say go for it and let us know it was awesome.

This dish is better than the sum of its parts (even though the parts are pretty great!). DELICIOUS. I did substitute a few things based on what I had - scallions and half an onion instead of the leek; mini elbows (actually, they were perfect); whatever broth I had frozen in cubes. Put a dollop of sour cream on top and sprinkled with a little extra dill, smoked paprika, and the green scallion ends - so, so good!

It came together easily.  I used Mascarpone rather than creme fraiche or sour cream, and it was terrific.  I had to use a bit more 1 tsp of cider vinegar when cooking the cabbage.  I sprinkled the mixture in the pan with smoked paprika when finishing, in addition to the 1/2 tsp that was stirred in with the Gruyere.  It was excellent, and makes either very large portions or really good leftovers.  We had both.  I'll make this again for sure.

This satisfied a craving for cheesy pasta and used up the end of a massive cabbage in my fridge, but I wasn’t completely dazzled by it. 

Additions & Edits: 
—Like many of us here, I find that NYTimes pasta recipes benefit from less pasta. For this, I used 12 oz. of Trader Joe’s macaroni pasta, which cooked to a beautiful al dente in 9 minutes 
—I added 1 cup of thinly sliced crimini mushrooms with the leeks. I could have added two cups (I just love mushrooms and gruyere!)
— I also added around 2 tsp of caraway seeds throughout the recipe
—The apple cider vinegar was key for me, and I used around 2.5 Tablespoons total
—I halved the creamy dairy (I used mascarpone ) 
— I used mostly chicken broth, as I had a carton open in my fridge

I was shocked by the amount of salt I used! And I could have doubled the cabbage.
@Meg I also wondered about adding some sort of seed, fennel was first thought but caraway would be great!

For some reason my pasta stuck together a lot. The leek and garlic really needs watching AND I must say, THE ADDITION OF CABBAGE WAS EXCELLENT. Personally would never have thought of adding cabbage to pasta, I used "Savoy" and liked it a lot.
@kitchenbabebklyn 1/2 small cabbage = 1 pound 450g. 1/2 small cabbage = 5 cups/5x90g = 450g. You use all the cabbage. If you remove the stems, you'll use 3/4 small cabbage
i am in awe of melissa clark, but truly, this one is not worth the effort.

If what you’re going for is a tasty cabbage, paprika version of Mac and Cheese this is pretty great. This isn’t what I was going for though so was a bit disappointed in how heavy and cheesy it was, however my husband loved it!

This needs more like 2 Tablespoons up to 1/4 cup cider vinegar for my taste. I also like it with proportionally more cabbage and less pasta. Next time I will use 3/4 of a pound of pasta. Each time I'm having leftovers, I'm sautéing more cabbage to add.

I am happy to report that if you realize way too late that you’re crème fraiche is Madagascar vanilla flavored, but your normal tendency for spices and garlic is to be quite heavy-handed, that this dish will still turn out delicious

Had some bacon to use up so started with that. Reserved and used some of the grease along with a splash of olive oil. Like other commenters, resisted the urge to short the initial cabbage cook time. Fabulous! Looking forward to repeating this recipe very soon... like today.

Used 5 scallions, reserving the greens to add at the end. Added paprika earlier, while the cabbage was cooking and added ~1/2 cup red wine to cabbage. Used Greek yogurt instead of sour cream. Added 2 more cups water initially with the quart of stock and pasta absorbed most, leaving perfect amount for the base of the sauce. Overall fantastic with great depth of flavor!! Make sure to cook cabbage at least 15 minutes before proceeding.

Made as mostly as listed. Used 10 oz conchigliette. Sautéed bacon lardons then used the bacon grease with some olive oil to start the recipe. Parsley with fresh sage instead of dill. Extra Gruyère, the bacon and more herbs were optional toppings. Easy. Tasty.

This was delicious! Reminds me of a stroganoff - super creamy and flavorful. My subs: smoked cheddar, Greek yogurt. Also crumbled and browned Field Roast’s apple & sage vegetarian sausage and added at the end. Next time I’ll add spinach to give it some greens.

I made this for my Mom and I, and it was delicious. Added some chopped sundried tomatoes at the end with the cheese and sour cream. Dont omit the vinegar. it really helps the flavor of the cabbage and helps cut the heaviness of the cheese & cream.
Love this recipe as is. My husband said he would have liked spinach added. I added sausage cause he’s a vegetarian. Flavor and richness is delicious. I’d make again!

I cooked chopped turkey keilbasa and removed. Added TJ's shredded cabbage, tsp of chili crunch, tsp of better than bullion, tsp of acv, and cooked for the full 15 mins.  

I added chicken broth and brought to a boil then added the pasta for 12 mins.  

Removed from the heat, added sour cream, TJs gruyere cheddar melange, paprika, more ACV, and the turkey keilbasa.  Finished with parsley.

As always, season in layers throughout the cooking process to ensure well balanced, flavorful food.

Muah!

What a welcome change from an Alfredo sauce for a creamy pasta dish. I followed the recipe to the letter and it came out perfect. The red pepper and dill really add a nice depth of flavor to complement the Gruyere.

This was delicious, easy and very hearty. Made more like 10 servings in my opinion! I swapped the sour cream for nonfat Greek yogurt, used a bit more paprika than called for and added sliced mushrooms along with the leeks. It tastes like if a Hungarian mushroom/cabbage soup was mac n cheese.

I’ll confess I’m a fan of box Mac and Cheese every now and again, but my least favorite parts of that are waiting for the water to boil and the mystery cheese.  In only a few more minutes this is a fancy and healthier alternative.  I’m in!
Omg.. I can’t stop eating this!! Make it.. don’t wait!
The good news? Used up some cabbage, a hunk of gruyere, and some leeks hanging around the fridge. Cleared them out! The bad news? “Homey” gooey murky pasta tasting of none of the above. No thanks.

This was delicious. I needed 5 cups of broth for 8 ounces of pasta so I suspect it would require a lot more to cook a full pound. I used a cheddar/gruyere cheese which worked well. It would be nice with the addition of mushrooms and perhaps chickpeas if you wanted to add some vegetarian protein. I added another 1/2 tsp of smoked paprika and dollop of fish sauce to boost the flavor a little.

We liked this very much. However, I used orecchiette but would use a different shape of pasta another time.  The shells nested together in clumps of 3 or 4 shells  and so didn't cook well, even with more time.  Probably because of the limited space and water compared to throwing them into a large pot of boiling water.

this happened to me too. It was incredibly frustrating and ruined the dish. I also had to stand over the dish the entire time it cooked desperately trying to separate the shells in boiling water which I was only partially successful at so many stakes that remained were under-cooked. It was a disaster. I probably wont make this dish again as I was overall underwhelmed but if I do I wont use orecchiette for sure
If you regularly stir the pasta it helps to stop them sticking together

I made the dish again the next day.  This time I didn’t add any Gruyère or sour cream. And I allowed people at the table decide how much Parmesan cheese to add. Some didn’t add any.  It was a hit for all of us!!

I was surprised by how much I disliked this recipe because I almost always love Melissa Clark's recipes. I felt the the sour cream totally overpowered the expensive Gruyère cheese so what was the point of expensive cheese if you are just going to drown it in cheap sour cream? Maybe I should have done crème fraîche but that was not in stock when I went grocery shopping and sour cream was listed at the first option. I also had a terrible time with the orecchiette shells sticking together!
I subbed yogurt for sour cream bc that’s what I had. Did not love the flavor. No yogurt would have been better. Liked the pasta and cabbage.

SPECTACULAR! 

As usual, I double all spices, garlic and herbs (and vinegar here!). I assume all recipes are written for a nursing home palate, and I'm rarely disappointed.

When buying cabbage, choose one with fresh, loose outer leaves still on... not just the hard round inner part. Cook it low and slow.

This was delicious, but i respectfully disagree with the people who compare this to mac and cheese. It just isn’t - it’s its own beautiful thing. I also agree with the reviewers who said that this could be made with a cheese other than gruyere - something else hard and funky but not as expensive would suffice i think.

New favorite. like a classy twist on Mac n cheese.  I used Labneh instead of sour cream because that's what I had in hand and it turned out excellent. next time I will double the cabbage and leeks, and maybe use fusili so it tangles up with the cabbage :)

This was very tasty, will probably make again, but fell short of oh wow, perhaps because the flavors were unusual or my slight changes; hence, 4 stars not 5.  Had Gruyère that needed to be used and the center of a blanched cabbage left from using leaves for stuffed cabbage.  Used 10 oz of girelle pasta and maybe 2/3 lb cabbage- it’s only 2 of us - but full amounts of the other ingredients. Started with less liquid but wound up needing full amount to cover pasta. Used 3/4 chicken broth and 1/4 cabbage water, because that’s what I had on hand.  Followed the suggestion of other commenters and made sure to cook the cabbage for at least the full 15 minutes though it seemed a little dry.  It did need that time. Used sour cream and parsley (not a fan of dill) and more than the tsp of vinegar as it cooked.  My husband loved the spiciness but I thought the smoked paprika was a little too much because I want the Gruyère taste.  The color of the dish was brown which could have reduced wow factor.
Any suggestions for making this vegan, please?

Just don't add the cheese and sour cream. One of my sons is vegan, and I do this sort of recipe often. I take out his portion before I finish for the rest of the family. He is free to add his vegan options!
@BG i substituted “violife” cream cheese and “follow your heart” shredded parmesan and it was excellent.
Anything wrong about doubling cabbage? Or increasing fiber another way?

@Hulahome

Just depends on what ratios of ingredients you want :)
Super delish. Melissa is the best.
Solid recipe; used mascarpone and shredded Gruyère. Browned a lb of ground pork and tossed it in to take it up a notch. Flavors work well, definitely adding to the rotation.
Criminal that this is rated 4 stars currently IMO. It’s excellent, and the cabbage in particular is so delicious in there. Have made it twice, and was happy doubling the leeks on round 2 personally.

This was absolutely delicious! I used half a box of chick pea pasta that I had in the pantry, which gave the dish a lovely earthiness and I think higher protein content than regular pasta. This will definitely go into regular rotation!

Don't skip the garlic and the cider vinegar, because I did, and the result was ... dull despite my deglazing the allium (red onion) and cabbage (savoy, shredded, blanched with a kettleful of boiling water, well drained, and wrung out in a kitchen towel) with a little white wine and adding plenty of smoked paprika with the sour cream.  On the plus side, the one-pot method worked fine.

Alas, I am a dutiful fan of Melissa Clark’s recipes, and follow them to a tee, and then make them my own. This recipe, not so much. My end result looked nothing like what’s pictured and instead was a bit of a creamy pool and quite unappetizing. Will not try this one again. But then again, there are so many other Clark recipes to try!
Tasty with spinach
Is 1/2 a small cabbage 1 pound OR is a small cabbage 1 pound and you use half.  I’d figure it out after I chopped it up but am wondering the proper wording in a recipe.

@kitchenbabebklyn
I have the same question: Should we end up adding 1lb or 1/2lb sliced cabbage to the pan?

@kitchenbabebklyn The way I read it, the ingredient quantity is “1/2 small green cabbage”. The words in parentheses further describe the ingredient quantity, i.e. 1/2 small green cabbage = 1lb cabbage.

This was really good! I scaled the recipe to three servings to suit my tastes. I had three scallions, so I used those; used a whole pound of cabbage, six ounces of pasta and two cups of stock but had to add more later on. Cut the oil down and the cheese, but used complete amounts of everything else. It reheated for lunch very nicely with a splash of pasta water (I keep it on hand) and there's another serving and a half in the freezer for dinner/lunch sometime again soon!
@Eve keeping pasta water on hand!! So smart. Never occurred to me.
I was glad to see the comment about subbing yogurt for the sour cream.  Might non-fat Greek yogurt work? I would like to know when to use that substitution in other recipes.
I used non-fat Greek yogurt and it came out great. (I did add a little extra olive oil at the end.)
It worked beautifully! As it does in most recipes. I rarely use sour cream.
throw in a can of tuna and you have tuna noodle casserole straight out of the 50s.

Wow, delicious!  I worked with what I had in the house, so purple cabbage does change the "look." I used a mix of different cheeses, Irish Dubliner, Fontina, Asiago, and sour cream (no creme fraiche), the result was amazing!

I made this recipe today.  It was excellent.  I added turkey sausage, mushrooms, and used considerably more apple cider vinegar.  The stars of the dish were the sweetness of the cooked cabbage and the creaminess of the Gruyère cheese.

@Robert Rudenstein
When do you add the mushrooms?  Thanks.

Ok—maybe I’m just not being precise enough on timing, but I just can’t make the “one pot pasta” thing work. Every time I try to add liquid and let the pasta absorb it in the dish instead of just boiling the pasta, it turns out yucky. Either too soft, too chewy, or too starchy. I just can’t make it work. The cabbage was amazing, wonderful layered flavor, but the texture of the pasta was weird. I do wish I could figure it out because = less dishes, but from now on, if I see a one pot pasta dish, I’ll be cooking the pasta separately.

Recipe looked easy and delicious, so despite having few exact ingredients, I made it with what was in the house. Used Lipton Onion Soup Mix (dry), aged balsamic, parmesan, chipotle powder, and goat cheese instead of the vegetable broth, apple cider vinegar, Gruyere, smoked paprika, and sour cream, respectively.

Such a quick and forgiving recipe for so many substitutions! Whole family loved it, and I'll make it again with whatever's on the shelf.

Made exactly as written, and used fresh dill and sour cream.  My husband loved it and gave it a thumbs up but for the color -- he thought it looked drab, which it did.  The dish did not have the red hue as in the recipe photo.  For serving we thought the dish needed pasta water to loosen it up.   We didn't have any so I brought a little freshly boiled water to the table to sprinkle on which worked well.

Do an image search for the children's book "Caps for Sale" and you will see a perfect representation of how my orecchiette behaved in a mere 4 C. of broth. If I use them again, I will probably cook them separately in a larger volume of liquid so they don't clump together so much. Or I'll simply use shells or something similar.  This was very tasty, though!

@Louise

Yes Louise Yes!!!! The hats were piled sky high, indeed. I spent some time “unstacking” as the pasta cooked in the low volume boil. I would probably choose a different shape next time to avoid the extra work. Was still delicious though.
This was surprisingly good.  I added smoked sausage, cooked rancho gordo beans, more leeks and needed to add more broth. It was hearty and delicious in one pot.
Really like the fact that I can make 1/2 of the recipe!
Made exactly as is - this is delicious.

Cooked it exactly as written.  It was delicious.   My vegetable broth was made from Better the Bouillon  vegetable flavor so did not need to add salt at the end.  I tend to follow the recipes the first time I cook it and adjust it the next time if needed.  Won't need to adjust this at all.

Followed the recipe and it has great flavor, but I had problems with the pasta as the shells stuck together out of the bag and I tried to break them apart as I stirred but I didn't try hard enough.  As a result I had clumps of shells that were undercooked and it detracted from the experience.  Overall we found it to be glorified Mac and cheese.
I don’t like gooey pasta so I put in only half cup cheese and 1/4 cup sour cream   Delicious!!  Definitely going to make it again - but with more cabbage!

Delicious and easy! I forgot to buy orecchiette so used cavatappi, and apparently it was a forgetful day because I also forgot Gruyère and had to sub a combo of Munster and Parmesan. I think it’s a forgiving recipe vis a vis which cheese you use. The cabbage just sort of melts into the whole in a way that I think even those not completely enamored of cabbage would tolerate. Everyone at my house loved it.

I need to avoid saturated fat, so would have skipped this if one of the newsletters hadn't indicated it was fine without the dairy. I made it with nearly 2 lbs of Taiwanese cabbage and used radiatori, omitting the Gruyère and substituting fat-free Greek yogurt for the sour cream/crème fraîche. It turned out great (with more olive oil, vinegar, paprika, and salt added at the end), but I think next time I'll follow the suggestions to caramelize the cabbage/leek mixture and cut the pasta a bit.
Nice on a cold winter night. Halved the pasta and lessened the broth but kept everything else the same.

This was excellent. I'll make it again. Next time, will cook the cabbage for longer until browned (adding splashes of water if needed), add the paprika earlier so it cooks, used parsley instead of dill, and will use 1/2 lb whole wheat pasta. I used whole small head of cabbage. 5 stars!

This is still delicious without the cheese. Stick with smoky paprika,key, and if you need creamy element Kite unsweetened Greek yogurt is good and has better nutritional profile than most plant cheeses.

Oddly enough, found myself with approximately 1/2 cup mascarpone and 3.5 oz of gruyere leftover in the fridge, so figured this recipe was meant to be. Made as directed, but halved the amount of pasta and broth. Was a little like Eastern Europe macaroni and cheese, or a cabbage french onion soup. Didn't love it on first bite, but grew on us the more we ate. Dill-loving husband did not like the flavor dill imparted here, though. Not sure if I would repeat.
Really good. Who thought cabbage went with pasta!

i was a little disappointed in this--it has great ingredients but melds them together to the point where they lose their identity. gruyère just becomes cheesy, crême fraîche just becomes creamy, and i do like cheesy and creamy, but i like gruyère and crême fraîche more. enjoyed it but doubt i'll make it again.

Flavors are really to my taste, I liked it a lot. I will make it again but will adjust the liquid as my result was a bit wetter than I prefer. Will also use a different pasta shape. The orecchiette tends to stack no matter how much effort is invested in stirring. Minor adjustments only…  the flavors are great!

Ok this was REALLY good. Had to try it because it had so many 5 star ratings, and turns out -  everyone loved it including my two-year old! I had to use what I had on hand - so ended up using 6 scallions (kinda small ones), and half of a red cabbage! I also threw in a can of chickpeas with the pasta. Before even adding the sour cream, dill, and gruyere, it was still really good - then the dairy and herbs took it to the next level! YUM will be keeping the items for this recipe on hand.

Easy, delicious and unique! This is my 12 year old's new favorite dinner. She loves it so much that she has a 9pm second helping before bed each night! I have made it three times within the last 10 days to keep up with the demand. Definitely added more vinegar and paprika. And the creme fraiche at Trader Joe's is half the price compared to other stores. Very helpful if you are making this in repeat!

I didn't have sour cream on hand,  so used fat-free Greek yogurt. I think it's delicious; I'm sure not as rich as the original but still delicious. Also used whole wheat pasta, a little more than 1/2 lb - just because we like vegetable-forward pasta. Definitely a keeper.

This was so fantastic. It is indeed beige, but very delicious. I have no idea how this combination of things came about, but well done Ms. Clark. Thanks to the folks who said use more cabbage and slightly less pasta, the ratio was perfect. Perfect balance of flavors and elements. Smoky, nutty, creamy, a bit of zing from the vinegar and chile, subtle sweetness from the cabbage carmelizing. I used just a small bit of dill on top and it set it off without overpowering it.

This recipe really resonated with my German roots. I used parsley because that was what I had on hand, but I think dill would have been even better. I also unfortunately used a cheap smoked Gruyere, and next time, I would use the real thing for genuine flavor. Perfect balance of ingredients. I love and trust Melissa's recipes and already love her pasta with caramelized cabbage. I took the suggestion to cook in a Dutch oven and am glad that I did.
Flathead cabbage is fantastic!! It costs more than regular cabbage but is still inexpensive. It’s far better than regular cabbage!

This is absolutely delicious, although I subbed in standard Swiss cheese chopped into bits, along with grated Romano. Sometimes, what one gains from using a superior cheese like gruyere is minimal in a heavily creamed dish like this (and deli Swiss cheese is half the price of its more expensive sibling). Also, I used fusilli pasta, which has the thickness necessary for an al dente result, but can be had at a much cheaper cost (and I only cooked for 10 minutes since I like a firmer al dente).

I haven't commented on a Melissa Clark recipe in a while, but I have to let everyone know how good this cabbage pasta recipe is! I followed all the steps, save substituting fat free yogurt for the creme fraiche. The cider vinegar gives it a complexity I really enjoyed. This will be in the regular rotation, along with Melissa Clark's roasted squash and farro recipe with feta. Another favorite.

Like a grown up Mac and cheese! I loved it. Quick, easy dinner and a fun way to hide cabbage for picky eaters (I love cabbage though...). I cooked my pasta separately and used a little bit of the pasta water/broth to get everything to come together. I used greek yogurt instead of sour cream/mascarpone - worked great and easier on the wallet :P

This is now one of my favorite meatless pasta recipes.  I used whole wheat rotini to make it even healthier, and Trader Joe's affordable cheddar/gruyere cheese blend instead of straight gruyere.  I also used a lot more of the cabbage and upped the spices a little to compensate.  I will be making this regularly from now on.

This was very interesting and a nice change up from more conventional pasta flavors. I had sour cream on hand so used that. I also followed a suggestion in another review that suggested cooking the pasta separately. After mixing all of the ingredients but the sour cream, I removed some of the dish as my son doesn't care for dairy. I love creamy pasta but in my opinion this is better without the sour cream. Toasted walnuts would be a nice addition.
@Linda Di wish I had halved the sour cream! It over powered

I generally love Melissa Clark's recipes but found this to be decidedly ho-hum as written.  I did let my cabbage thoroughly wilt and even caramelise, added some smoked paprika early AND at the end.  Even my husband, who kindly eats everything with good grace, thought it was pretty bland until I added about 3x the smoked paprika (yes, mine was fresh and pungent), dill, and even some extra chili flakes.

I made this because the combination of ingredients struck me as a little weird and I was intrigued, but it actually works. I browned mild Italian sausage while the pasta was cooking and added it at the end with the sour cream, cheese, etc. The family loved it.
Gorgeous as descirbed - I used a Boursin cheese,l and Greek yogurt for the dairy, bell pepper, scallions (instead of the leeks), and some Italian sausage.

Made this as written and really enjoyed it.  I think 3/4 lb of pasta would be better, or maybe increase the veg to times 1 1/2.  The cabbage/leek mixture is absolutely delicious and I wanted more of it in the dish.  Cooked the cabbage for about 20 minutes adding splashes of water when it started to stick.  A comforting and satisfying meal for a cold night.  It's definitely going into my Favorites file.

I made this yesterday and we liked it. I won't cook the pasta in the dish next time as it took so long to cook that the cabbage and leeks were almost melted away. I added browned, ground turkey to make it a meal. I'll add ground beef along with more cabbage and leeks next time. As an aside, cooking and cooling pasta and then reheating it lowers the amount of carbs and calories the body absorbs.

I used the whole cabbage & 2 bunches of ramps to start; shells for pasta but only 220 g; whole bunch of dill, less gruyere & creme fraiche & it came out deliciously soupy & sweet & perfect for this changing weather.
Does the cabbage come out wet? I hate wet cabbage.

@Cooper

Isn’t cabbage always wet? I’m not sure what you mean. If I t’s a concern, how would you make the recipe in order to avoid that?
this is like a one-pot haluski. very good as written, but I still think I prefer butter and onion with the cabbage.
weight for cabbage would be useful
@Aj I think it does say about 1lb
Looks so tasty. Wondering if I could substitute fromage blanc for the sour cream (high cholesterol). Has anyone tried?
is the cooking time for the pasta 12-15 minutes after it starts boiling? it seems like you would overshoot al dente if you're cooking it that long

I was looking to use up some stuff in my refrigerator, so I gave it a shot (and followed the recipe!) but it's not my jam. There are too many strong flavors competing with each other (even after letting it sit for a day).  The cabbage also created hours of unpleasant paprika-infused Gruyere burps.

I make a similar "old" family recipe and to speed things up, I put my cabbage, onions (instead of leeks) and garlic in a covered bowl with oil and microwave on high for about 10 minutes.  It usually is perfect.  You can always zap it more if needed.  (I stir it about half way through)

I never know in recipes like this if "half of a small green cabbage" weighs about a pound or if the whole cabbage should weigh a pound.  But I'm trying this as I'm fond of cabbage and cheese makes everything delicious.

Not a fan. Gloppy, but not in a comforting way. The parts were more appealing than the whole. We love sautéed cabbage and onions. We love sour cream and Gruyère. We love shelbows. Perhaps it was the cider vinegar and smoked paprika that we didn’t enjoy. A miss for us. Unsaved.
I don’t always love whole-what pasta, but it seems like a. atrial for this so try dish with cabbage and Gruyère. I might even sneak in some pancetta or bacon.
Absolute winner. Substituted ditalini (it’s what I had) and used mascarpone. Divine. Don’t skip on the dill. It brightens it.
I followed precisely and found it to be very bland.  Even after adding additional paprika salt and pepper.  No complaints, but very bland.
Made it exactly as written and this is GOOD!
Made exact but subbed ACV for rice wine vinegar -- superb and divine.

Wow wow wow, so good. Second the other comments saying to cook the cabbage a little further until it's lightly caramelized. 

Also highly recommend starting the process by cooking some crispy bacon in the pot (if you're into that sort of thing), then adding that bacon back in at the very end. Adds some extra smokiness and glorious crunch

Really good. Reminded me of a lighter, smoked Gouda Mac and cheese. Vinegar added depth. Altered the recipe to suit ingredients on hand and my desired pasta to cabbage/ leek ratio.  Used savoy cabbage in place of the more dense green cabbage the recipe called for. The savoy softens faster so cooking time prior to adding the pasta was reduced by about half. Pasta amount reduced by half. Savoy cabbage presumably releases less water so despite less pasta, I used almost the full four cups of broth the recipe called for to ensure the pasta cooked through.

This was easy and delicious! We adjusted slightly based on ingredient availability/preference using bowtie pasta, chicken broth instead of vegetable broth, sour cream, and Jarlsberg instead of Gruyère. Finished with an extra dash if ACV, salt, and parsley, and added grilled chicken on top. Yum! Next time I'd like to try it with dill instead of parsley.

I was a little skeptical, but always looking for ways to include extra veggies and fiber in my pastas. This was sooo delicious (anything with gruyere is delicious)!  Aesthetically, it was a bit weird, but orecchiette with the slight cabbage crunch was satisfying to eat. Served this with a simple chickpea salad for protein.
I made this as written, with the exception of using Trader Joe's gluten-free pasta. It is easy and excellent.

2026 is the year I eat ALL the cabbage. But really I've been really enjoying eating cabbage recently and this dish is great. I'm on my cooking journey and I didn't even think of cabbage and pasta. 
I cooked the cabbage for about 25min. I would consider using a mandoline to shred the cabbage next time. Greek yogurt as a sub for creme fraiche worked well.

many folks noted wanting more cabbage in here so i used two leeks and half a LARGE cabbage, and 3/4 pound of pasta, was perfect. cooked the cabbage a little longer, maybe 20 minutes? love paprika but didnt have smoked so added a little sweet and half sharp while the pasta cooked rather than after. gruyere+parmesan+sour cream offered the perfect sour to the sweet of the cabbage. forgot the parsley on top but i agree with others dill would taste wrong i think. made chicken thighs on the side but next time ill try sausage mixed in at the end, that sounds great.
Really delicious but when we make it next I will double the cabbage.   It needed a higher ratio of vegetables.
I started the dish by frying some chicken thighs to make it more substantial but pork mince would definitely work. My best addition was half a teaspoon of fennel seeds. Delicious.
Will be making again for sure. Followed recipe exactly but cooked cabbage a few minutes longer. Next time I might add ground chicken (or not) Great flavours.

Another winner! My husband isn't a cabbage fan, so I got a head of Napa cabbage and really cooked it down to its most carmelized deliciousness. I also added chicken and broccoli because it needed to get used. I bought gouda instead of gruyere, so I mixed in some Parmesan for salty punch. (I used 1/2 c total) I also used 1/2 cup nonfat plain Greek yogurt to lighten it up. The final substitution was Italian parsley instead of dill. I was heavy-handed with the smoked paprika. WOW!
This recipe sounded good, and has good reviews, so I made it.  I followed the recipe and I didn't like it.  I like cabbage, and I like pasta, but I didn't like them together.  And the dill was weird.

Made as directed-
My spouse said the taste was “confusing” … on his first bowl.. second bowl, he thought it a  more cohesive  dish without the extra smoked paprika.

Personally, I think I added a bit more pepper flakes than I would have liked- overwhelming!
He thought otherwise.

I will likely make it again, reduce red pepper flakes and add some mushroom or other veggies at the end.

Intriguing… thanks !
As per usual, MC comes through with a winner! Easy to make and delicious. The next time I make this I will add either some sautéed boneless skinless chicken thigh pieces or browned sausage.

So yummy.  As suggested by some reviewers, started with browned mild Italian sausage.  Didn’t have any dill.  Added some fresh sage and thyme to the cooking pasta. Finished with a lot of chopped Italian parsley.  Used campanelle.  The dish felt so cohesive.  The cabbage is decadently silky.  Used a 5.2 qt rondeau pan which fit everything nicely.
I made this without the dairy and still delicious. Did use chicken broth though

This recipe delicious, elegant, easy. I made it last night according to recipe, though I only used 8 oz. orecchietti. We had lots of leftovers (2 adults). This left a nice amount of sauce, though overnight some absorbed into the pasta. Tonight I reheated it with a bit more olive oil and chicken broth and added some leftover roasted chicken, took it off stove and added a bit more sour cream and gruyere and smoked paprika. We agreed either artichoke hearts in brine or mushrooms would enhance.
Easy and very adaptable, but the original recipe results in a delicious, fancy-restaurant-worthy dish that makes great leftovers.
This was a hit for my whole family. I made it as suggested, but it required more salt than I would have thought-  so delicious.

Good, but not as good as I had expected/hoped for. I made half a recipe, following the instructions pretty faithfully, using homemade chicken stock in place of vegetable broth. My husband loved it, but, as another reviewer commented, it seemed a bit one-dimensional. Maybe it needed some other texture; not sure. Will probably try it again sometime, but may fiddle with the recipe.
What this recipe did to my Le Creuset was a crime against humanity. I’ve never hated a recipe more. Didn’t even taste that good

Perhaps you had the heat too high. I cooked it in my Le Creuset pan and it was fine, but I could see how it would stick to the bottom of the pan if you had the heat too high, or did not stir it regularly.
This was really delish. Not something I would have thought up on my own. I only had to buy the pasta, sour cream, and gruyere and we both loved it. Great dish for a cold night in Colorado.
Ultimate comfort food! I used shells. Simple and clear.
Does this recipe freeze well? I'm one person but cook double the recipe (so I don't have to cook again).

For one person, I don't know that I'd double the recipe. At 1 lb of pasta, you're likely to get about 5-6 servings out of this. As for freezing, if I ended up with leftovers, i would have attempted to freeze it. I think it would turn out reasonably well.
Cooked as written and would highly recommend. great for a Winter night meal. Comforting and delicious.
I made this exactly as written! So so yummy!
Delightful dish - I used Creme Fraiche. The dill and parsley on top really had flavor. Will definitely make again.

Made this exactly as directed (taking the parsley option) and the family loved it. We’re vegetarian but the smoked paprika gave the dish a lovely bacon-y feel. Next time, I will use the whole head of cabbage. And thanks to whomever recommended setting a timer for 15 minutes to ensure that cabbage cooked long enough. It helped me resist the temptation to take it off the heat too soon

I used fontina cheese per a suggestion, and it was just fine. I also tested with and without the dill and creme fraiche, and determined that it really didn't need either. Serve with some Frank's Red Hot for a nice zing.
I made this per the recipe, using more parsley and less dill, and full fat sour cream.  It is really excellent. Thanks Melissa!
Very nice meal, tasty and quickly done! Glad to have very good cabbage in Viennese Supermarkets.

No. The only reason to make this is to learn the technique of slow braising a sturdy vegetable; then cooking pasta with broth in the same pan; then adding cheese and cream. But not these vegetables or this cheese. Yuck.
I found this bland and unremarkable, would not make it again or recommend it.

Really enjoyed this. I subbed bok choy for the cabbage, because it's easy to buy a pound of bok choy. It cooked down in about half the time in step 1. Greek yogurt replaces sour cream nicely. The vinegar and dill are great touches.
Definitely in my top 5 now. Easy and so delicious.

It's perfectly fine, followed recipe to the letter. Easy to make, appreciated the one pot aspect. Would I make it again? In a world filled with fantastic, equally easy recipes, no. It just doesn't sing.

Delicious. I did cook it in a Dutch oven instead of the Skillet and adjusted cook time as needed, but otherwise made as written with the called-for ingredients. Surprise surprise: It was delicious. Topped with some smoked olive oil (shout-out to The Pinehurst Olive Oil Co.). Beautiful and easy!

I loved this though you’ll probably say I made a completely different recipe.  I used collards cause that’s what I had at home. I used macaroni cause that’s what I had at home. Then I used vegan Boursin and  Violife Mexican shreds instead of the cheeses. We are vegetarian and non dairy.  Then followed the recipe without any other changes. It was absolutely delicious! I will make it again with orchette pasta and cabbage.

I loved this though you’ll probably say I made a completely different recipe.  I used collards cause that’s what I had at home. I used macaroni cause that’s what I had at home. Then I used vegan Boursin and  Violife Mexican shreds instead of the cheeses and I forgot the paprika. We are vegetarian and non dairy. It was absolutely delicious! I will make it again with orecchiette pasta and cabbage and all the spices, though it will remain non dairy

Delicious! My largest skillet would not be large enough for this so I used a dutch oven. I used parsley and ended up using much more than 1/4 cup, though that amount sounds about right for dill, which I'll try next time. I had to turn the heat way down while cooking the cabbage to prevent it from browning too much. I used full fat plain yogurt for the creme fraiche. I sprinkled lots more smoked paprika on top when serving.
@Carley I appreciate the recommendation for a Dutch oven: I was suspecting the same thing.
@Coconene Appreciate the suggestion for smoked Gruyere: I always love smoked anything.

When I see smoked paprika on an ingredient list, I never know whether to use dulce smoked paprika or picante smoked paprika. 
Thoughts? Recommendations?
Big fan of this! Used greek yogurt instead of sour cream because that's what I had and threw in a can of cannellini beans and it was delicious.

As Carley says, next time I would par-boil the pasta before combining it with the other ingredients because the orecchiette was very hard, not just al dente, after following the recipe exactly. Orecchiette tend to "nest" and benefit from a lot of separation. Tasty sauce.
Made as directed… with the addition of some ground turkey to up the protein. Seriously delicious and easy! Will likely make again soon with the other half of the cabbage.
I think it needed more cabbage. Made per recipe and it was good- just needed a little more of something. Next time will add more cabbage and a little heat.
Followed directions exactly and it is delicious! Thanks again Melissa -- you rock!

I made this as per the recipe. For me, there was too much pasta and not enough cabbage. I'm lucky enough to grow my own cabbages, and they are delicious fresh or cooked. Tasting the cabbage before I added the pasta, it was delicious. However, the pasta drowned out the flavor of the cabbage. Since I have a lot left over, mostly pasta, I might try adding some of the veggies, sausage, or tuna others recommended.

Very very tasty.  Living at altitude I had to cook the pasta longer than the recipe said but it turned out really good.  I did add a little extra water with some  veg 'better than bouillion'. You have to stir often to keep the orchiette from sticking together. I love this recipe and it was fun to make.
I am quite surprised that no one mentioned the fact that with these times and amount of water you'll end out with a soggy, soft, quite inedible pasta :(

This was great, like a grown-up, but still highly comforting, mac and cheese. I subbed cheddar and nonfat greek yogurt, because that was all I had and it was delish. I'd like to try the prescribed gruyere next time, or maybe smoked gouda. The smoked paprika makes it!

Excellent, reminds me of some Slovak pastas I love. I cooked the cabbage low and slow as suggested in the comments and folded in smoked salmon with the creme fraiche and dill. I had to Frankenstein three different short pastas with similar cook times in and ended up adding an additional two cups of water to get to al dente.
Add way more leeks, garlic, and cheese

Delicious once you add more paprika and less salt. No doubt, though, it is another Mac and Cheese 2.0. Perhaps, another vegetable - I thought about mushrooms - or, even, the addition of a protein might elevate it to more of a Paprikash-like dish.
After I made this, I put it on the smoker for 30 minutes at 160f. I like this dish and I loved it with a little smoke flavor to enhance the smoked paprika.

Well, I doubled the cabbage and the leeks and the herbs and the spices, and then I found I needed a little more crème fraiche and cheese to achieve the right consistency… but it occurs to me that what I really truly did, as a mathematical reduction, was to halve the pasta, ratio-wise.

Maybe it was just me, but I thought this dish very odd.  Too much cheese (very odd for me to say, but it was just overly heavy), too much smoked paprika, not enough cabbage.  I made the full amount of the recipe anticipating leftovers, but we just threw it out as we knew we would not eat it again.

I love the leeks and cabbage here. I made as-is with the sour cream and parsley options on the listed finishes. I would dial back the sour cream to 1/4 or 1/3c because the 1/2c was a bit aggressive and overpowered the nice flavors of the leeks and cabbage. Would increase the Gruyère a little bit instead and maybe add an extra clove of garlic for personal tastes.
excellent with toasted pine nuts.
Didn't love the addition of the smoked paprika.
Made with parsley (a suggested substitution for dill) and found it disappointingly sweet. Might try the leftovers with dill, but tempted to write this off.

Overall not a fan. I found it sticky more than creamy. It didn't taste terrible, but it wasn't all that flavorful, and it looked gross. Not like the picture at all, it looked much more dingy. 

I did add some mushrooms, which I sautéed separately and added only at the end. Only one of my kids would eat it, and only because of said kid's favorite food being mushrooms. The others tasted it and never would eat it again. Not a keeper in our house.
Really tasty! I prefer it without the paprika, but everything else is great! Don’t be afraid to splash the ACV on for a little acid to brighten the whole dish.
Easy and yummy - I doubled the cabbage and added pan fried shrimp for extra protein. It’s a keeper!

I made the pasta separately in salted water until very al dente, and took some pasta water and set it aside.  I added one boillion cube to the pasta water and added it in the end. I also omitted the crème fraiche and dill. My husband and I thought it was very good.

Wonderful comfort food recipe! I gave it 4 stars only because I agree with other reviewers who questioned the veggie to pasta ratio.  I used 4 oz of Barilla Protein+ penne and 2 c. water (+ 1 tsp Better Than Bouillon) and the dish turned out great. I had creme fraiche but no Gruyere so substituted 3 Swiss cheese sandwich slices. It worked! Fresh dill is not optional - it makes the dish.  Maybe add a can of quartered artichoke hearts? Turn it into a casserole with a buttery crumb topping? :)

I made this using emmental cheese since I had that on hand and added a splash of fresh squeezed lemon juice to finish. I was convinced my family would turn their nose up and had mentally made a PB&J for my 9 year old, but everyone loved it!!! Devoured and asked for more. So good.
This was fantastic.  Melissa Clark is the GOAT

The 1st time I followed the recipe. 2nd time turned out way better! The paprika made it taste worse imo.

My fave version that I'm adding to my regular rotation: 
3-3.25 cups broth, 12 oz pasta, 8+ cups cabbage, can of cannelini beans for protein, add a good amount of cumin with leeks and saute at least until it smells more complex, skip paprika at the end. I use my heart to measure things, but guessing cider vinegar was quadrupled and used more gruyere than called for. Fresh dill a must!
Lots of layers of flavors. Used napa cabbage b/c that's what we had. Outstanding dish.

This was fabulous!  I didn't cook the cabbage as long as everyone else and I thought it tasted great.  I didn't have grueyere and subbed in cheddar - worked fine.  I also used dried chopped ramps since I also didn't have onion and added in some sausage crumbles.  A sign of a good recipe is that it can handle tweeks and still taste great.  I will make it again.

I halved the recipe to serve two of us but otherwise stuck to the script. It was a wonderful way to use up the cabbage I didn't cook with my corned beef last week and is a great example of a recipe whose sum is greater than the total of its parts. We thought it was delicious and the flavors were surprisingly complex. It's also super easy and quick for a weeknight.

Really good. I parboiled the pasta for a few minutes because I've never had great luck with cooking pasta in JUST ENOUGH water. I had both dill and parsley so used 1/4 of each and it wasn't too much. I also sauteed the cabbage longer than fifteen minutes, as it didn't seem to have really collapsed much at that point, but after finishing this I think it might have been okay once the pasta was cooked. This one's going into the rotation.

This was easy to make a tasty. I added some canned white beans about five minutes before the pasta was done to up the protein. Do NOT forget the paprika at the end. This really made the dish. Tasted a few bites without and the difference with was very noticeable.

Melissa Clark is the 5-star Guru of our home cooking! We love virtually all of her recipes, and have printed them and put them in plastic sleeves in colorful binders. Yay, Melissa! HOWEVER this “5-star” pasta and cabbage recipe gets 2 stars from us - bland, boring, and - after two dinners - we pulled the remainder out of our freezer and trashed it! (Back now to our zillion 5-star Melissa Clark recipes!)
So good. I cooked cabbage longer-good idea. Put little more paprika-used sour cream. Wish I had more dill to add. Next time

Excellent, as often the case with Melissa Clark. Offers a good foundation for experimentation during cabbage season. 

Render fat from diced bacon and add the bacon in at the end for a crisp textural contrast. With bacon fat, you can omit crème fraiche or sour cream. 

Substitute 1/2 of the pasta with cubed turnip, rutabaga, or celery root for more fiber and lower carbs; 1/2” cubes cook in about the same time as the pasta.
Allowed the cabbage to cook for closer to 30 minutes, rendering quite a bit of liquid, then added in some chopped chicken sausage - tasted delightful !

This was great. I would definitely add more broth or maybe pasta water next time as it started to overflow my pan and was hard to get the mascarpone and shredded cheese incorporated thoroughly. (And, look I'm not made of money- I had Monterey Jack and no Gruyere, plus half a container of Mascarpone to use up, so topped it with extra parmesan and it's still very good.)

As always, ingredients matter: used bronze-cut orecchiette, Fage sour cream, m real Gruyere, and Taiwanese cabbage, but only chickpea cooking broth, and although the first three ingredients upped my rating from a 3 to a 4, this recipe would definitely benefit from using the real kind of vegetable stock that I used to make and would likely contribute to the deeper and richer color in the photo.
This is great! Would love to try it with large white beans next time. Melissa has the best pasta recipes but always looking for a healthier option. Fiber maxing lol

I'm not going to opine on flour and honey in cornbread (though as a southerner I do have opinions). But I will recommend you save a little time and effort and put the butter in the skillet and, instead of melting on the stovetop, put it in the oven and let it melt while the oven is preheating. In the meantime, mix dry things in one bowl, wet things in another. Mix together. When the butter is melted, pour it into your cornbread mix, then pour back in the skillet as directed in the recipe.
@Emily yeah, the honey is going to stop me from saving this recipe. Instead I will just add cheese and jalapeños to my old cornbread recipe.
its no longer a brown butter cornbread now
Paul: It is if you leave it in the oven until it browns a bit, which it usually does by the time the oven completely preheats. I should have mentioned that in my original comment.

If you're using coarse-grind cornmeal, you'll want to whisk the cornmeal and buttermilk together and let them rest together awhile -- several hours is best -- before combining with the other ingredients, or the finished cornbread may retain an unpleasantly gritty mouthfeel.
The recommended coarse cornmeal will benefit from soaking in the buttermilk for at least 20 or 30 minutes before the batter is made.

I followed this recipe to the letter and it completely filled up a 10 inch skillet. As soon as I slipped it in the oven, it started bubbling over and spilling over the sides. I would strongly recommend using the 12 inch skillet.
Mine was fine in a 10-inch pan. Sorry yours wasn't. Maybe my skillet was deeper.
@Eric I had the same issue. I had to quickly take the skillet out, and transfer everything to a hastily greased 12 inch skillet to finish baking.

Mine is maybe a little trashy. It includes a cup of canned {gasp} creamed corn and grated cheddar. A spoonful of sugar helps the jalapenos go down. Eaten warm with too much butter and a drizzle of honey; why don't I make this more often?
@tomrely I always add canned corn to my cornbread. It makes it better!

@tomrely 
I have made that recipe and it is the bomb!

Thank you Melissa! I have never liked plain cornbread (sorry, southerners), only making it with cheddar and jalapeno. Delighted that Ms Clark has immortalized my preference with this recipe. (I KNOW she didn’t do it for me. I’m not that egotistical.)
Looks delicious, but it has way too many calories for me.  I'll stick to my plain corn bread made with greek yogurt and olive oil instead of buttermilk.

Easy to make corn bread  taste good with 12 TBS of Butter and 6 oz of cheese - this is really food Au Gratin - like we make in the midwest - just add more cheese 

Would love to see  Melisa take the challenge to make a great cornbread without a ton of fat as a crutch-
For those who  are not into hot, like me, the presence of the cheese will temper the heat of the jalapeño.

It is a fairly liquidy batter so was quite concerned but 40 minutes did the trick.  I also added in finely chopped green onions and used pickled jalapeños ( 2.5 tbsp was more than enough for everyone's palate)

I make something very similar, with the addition of fresh or frozen corn kernels and a couple of dashes of cumin for that Tex-Mex vibe. I'll try this brown butter variation -- I have a potluck on Jan 1 so a great opportunity to try it out.
responding to the comment to melt butter in the oven.  well if you do that you no longer have a brown butter cornbread. so do with that what you will

To Emily, re: flour and honey - Amen from a South Carolinian.
And your comments on method (butter in skillet, melted in oven, then poured into dry ingredients) are exactly on-point.

Made this today for a New Year's potluck and it was a big hit. I used Monterey Jack cheese, a little less jalapeño (about 1-1/2 Tbsp) because it was for a crowd, not all of whom like as much spice as I do, about half as much honey, and added about 3/4 c. of thawed frozen corn. Next time I'll use cheddar sharp cheddar, more jalapeño, and some finely sliced scallion. Maybe some ground cumin.
I don't own a cast iron skillet. Any suggestions on what can be used instead?
@lydia. I have used steel round cake pans successfully for corn bread, melting the butter directly in the pan while preheating the oven

@Lydia. Any heavy duty metal pan that you can preheat in the oven will do. As Emily said, melt the butter in the pan while preheating your oven, then brush some all over to grease the pan, and pour the rest in the batter: it saves one pot to wash, and preheats your pan for crispier corn bread.
Followed recipe. Kind of stodgy - not spicy, not sweet, not light. Not a hit and not making  it again.

I don’t have a cultural or family tradition to judge this recipe by so I made it as written and found it to be a tasty and rich variation to the other cornbreads I’ve made before.  No need to spread butter on this one!  The cheese and butter inside will be enough.  For those who like sopping up their chili, this is not necessary a great choice since it is so moist.  On its own, it makes a nice snack the next day as well.  In a hurry, I accidentally mixed in the cheese as opposed to layering it.  It didn’t affect the outcome and may have made it better.
This turned out perfectly—got rave reviews from a professional chef. The browned butter adds some depth, but next time I’ll use maybe 11 tbs, rather than 14.
Hey y’all. How about some of that great green chile found in old New Mexico. You’re missing one of the best ways to spice up that corn bread and many other things.

Skillet cornbread is proof that God loves us and wants us to be happy. Yeah, I know I’m stealing from that saying about beer.
Question for you serious cooks: is buttermilk reconstituted from powder as good as fresh buttermilk in recipes?
No, but it will do! Use what you have, IMHO.
I am a calorie counter so when it says 370 calories per serving and the recipe makes 8 to 12 servings - which is it?
@Farron. The nutritional analysis box states 10 servings
@S in VA Like Farron, my Nutritional Info tab under the recipe shows 8-12 servings @ 370 cals/serving.  Cals/serving is non-sensical without a set number of servings.
No! Southerners NEVER sweeten cornbread!  It becomes "cake" when any type of sweetener is added!!

I make a similar version but add a can of Mexican corn. No flour, all corn meal, and a whole chopped onion with 3 jalapeños and 8 oz of shredded sharp cheddar cheese. I use regular milk and cook in a skillet. Love it!
Pray tell, what does “nubby” mean in this context?

So a few years ago I read a comment that her grandmother put in 1/3 C boiling water to batter just before putting in oven to make the corn bread moist. I thought it was crazy but I do this every time now the best moistest corn bread, sooooo good.

@Emily — I think the point to melting the butter on the stovetop is that you want to brown the butter. Which, in my opinion, is worth the very little trouble. Overall, though, I found this cornbread quite heavy. If I make it again, I will halve the amount of cheese, just sprinkling it on top a few minutes before taking it out of the oven.

Wow that is a LOT of butter, esp with the cheese thrown in
My go-to recipie has 3 tbsp plus enough peanut oil to shimmer the pan of the cast iron skillet and that feels pretty rich with the eggs and buttermilk
Made exactly as directed and it is to DIE for! Best cornbread I've ever had.

This is a fantastic cornbread. I used my 10" skillet and the grated cheese topping overflowed a bit onto the sides of the skillet. I used coarse-ground cornmeal; and though I appreciate the suggestion of letting the coarse ground meal steep  in the buttermilk had no issue of grittiness in the cornbread.
Excellent dish. Made exactly to the recipe. Next time, I'd take down the butter by 2 tablespoons. I wouldn't go lower than 8 tablespoons since the browned butter adds depth of flavor.

Cornbread is one of my favorite foods and this one surprised me.  It has turned out to be one of my favorites. For my palate, perfect companion for chili, black bean soup, morning egg crumbles and it freezes nicely. Sometimes I riff. For example, if I don’t have buttermilk on hand, I’ll use thinned yogurt.  I’ve used a combination of jalapeño and poblano (one of my fav riffs).  I’ve added fresh corn, creamed corn, Mexican grilling onion (grilled), duck eggs (richer and sturdier)….  They’ve all turned out well.  But more often than not, I make it as written and always enjoy it.

Excellent recipe. Used a whole large minced jalapeño with all ribs and seeds
Used Florida keys honey, a scant 1/4 cup
Extra salt plus I used kosher, not sea salt.  Lactose free kefir in place of the buttermilk. A blend of Cabot sharp cheddar and spiced Monterey Jack. Will make again. Brown butter a great idea!!!!

his did not turn out well for me. It was VERY greasy even though I cut down the amount of cheese by half. I looked at other recipes after this and see there is too much fat (eggs, butter, buttermilk) to gluten ratio. Also cut down on the amount of salt, which seems excessive. Very disappointing.

I made this with medium ground cornmeal, whole wheat Einkorn flour, extra cheddar in both layers, and no honey or jalapeños. Turned out great! Next time I will try to soak the cornmeal as others suggested.
Unfortunately, this one is not worth the ingredients or the time. Found it to be greasy and lacking in flavor.
" A skillet will take longer than a skillet"?
As a proud Southerner, I always have Three Rivers self rising cornmeal in my pantry. Should I assume you omit the baking powder and use the combined cornmeal and flour measurements?
Any thoughts regarding the merits of white cornmeal vs. yellow cornmeal?
I’ll go back to my dry southern cornbread.
