Will try this with freshly chopped green chile instead of the jalapenos. You can take the kid out of New Mexico, but you can't take the New Mexico out of the kid!
Throw in a handful of frozen corn kernels.
World any adjustments be necessary if you left out the cheese ?

I grew up on a version of this, no honey, my mother would turn over in her grave! Cornbread should never be sweet in my opinion. Diced onion, corn, shredded cheese, we usually used cheddar and instead of the butter we always put a neutral oil in the cast-iron skillet, heat it to almost smoking in the oven and then add your batter to the skillet, and it created a most excellent crust! This recipe came down from my grandmother who grew jalapeño peppers in her kitchen window!

Laughing, I am.  I bet you lots of money I would adore this cornbread.  But, just two of us, having a little leftover chili and, always, salad, we'd eat a little more than one portion of this.  12 Tablespoons of butter, before the added butter shown in the photo?  I will happily dream of this cornbread, but my paltry, but still pretty danged good, cornbread will have to suffice.  Thank you, though.  Pleasant dreams.
My dearest friends don't like spicey food, so I used green chilies instead of jalapenos. Works great.
Glad to hear it. I dislike the bitterness of jalapenos.

Sometimes the problem is with a user’s expectation— not the recipe.  I made this to serve with a Portuguese bean/sausage soup.  I made the cornbread exactly as prescribed; I found there was too much cheese in the bread for my meal.  Given the amt. of cheese in the recipe, I should have expected the result.  The Clark’s cornbread is seriously good, but maybe brown-butter/hot pepper cornbread—w/o cheese is better for some meals.
By mistake I mixed the cheese into the batter which resulted in a crispy crust.  Will follow the directions better next time!   Used pickled jalapenos which were great.

I wouldn't have thought a dish with that much butter in it could disappoint, but here we are. I didn't get much cheesy flavor, nor was it sweet from the honey, or spicy from the jalapeño (though that's an easy fix). It was just kind of meh flavor and texture-wise. I have other cornbread recipes that I love and I'm probably not coming back to this one. Hopefully others have better luck.
This looks great, can anyone help me with a substitute to the wheat flour? Two of my family members are coeliacs.
In my 62 years, I’ve had a lot of hams. This is the best I’ve ever had and the only way I’ll make it going forward

A rare miss from Melissa Clark. First this is too much batter for a10 inch cast iron skillet. Bake time was way off. Mine took more than 40 minutes but burned on the edges (and of course, on the bottom of the oven). I made as directed which was too sweet for my southern taste buds. Think I’ll stick to Memaw’s recipe and add some jalapeño and cheese when I want to be fancy.

The amount of buttermilk called for is 2 1/4 cups in both this and the original version of this cornbread. However, the metric amount listed in original version is 530ml while the amount listed here is 580ml. 
Just tried making this and the batter was extremely liquidy. It wouldn't set after more than an hour in the oven, the cheese on top burned. Please proofread your recipes! Many folks use the metric measurements, they use less dishes and are supposed to be more accurate.

I made this last night and it was really good. I did increase the amount of jalapeños but followed everything being the first time. The only change I would make next time would be to reduce the butter. It was just a little much for me. I thought the cheese amount was fine. Oh, I did soak my cornmeal in the buttermilk while prepping all the ingredients. I normally do that with all my cornbreads. Otherwise it was great!!
Did anyone else find that a tablespoon and a half of baking powder seems like a lot?

not my favorite cornbread ever and felt like too much work. next time I would just mix the cheese in with the batter. 

in the recipe's defense, I did have to make a couple modifications which could have contributed to the outcome, but either way, still don't recommend. 

my mods: had to make my own buttermilk instead of using actual buttermilk. ran out of baking soda so used baking powder instead per the internet's suggestion. otherwise, followed recipe to a t, with weighed ingredients.
Definitely needs a 12'' skillet

This is a good recipe. What’s all the quack  about the fat? This is jalapeño cornbread, people. You expect it to be fat. If you want diet , then use diet margarine, no cheese, one egg and skim milk. It will not taste like this recipe. I do suggest using First Lady, Roslyn Carter’s , technique of allowing batter to sit for 15 minutes before you pan and bake. This gives the ingredients a chance to hydrate and improves the flavor.

A warning for metric recipe users! I found that a cup and a half of (fine) cornmeal weighed 270g not the 180g specified, a significant difference. This might be one of the few times where more consistent results are achieved with cup measurements...

title: Delicata Squash Tart With Hot Honey and Pistachios Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody:
@lsweepup 
Certainly! Always helpful to prep what you can ahead. Here's what I as a former chef suggest:

Step 2, pre-baking the squash, maybe adding/subbing some other winter veggies like cut up carrots &/or parsnips? sliced onions? tho pre-roast each in separate areas on pan as they may finish @different times. When cool put in fridge.

Steps 3 and 4, pre-bake the pastry til edges are just golden... then leave out on the counter very lightly covered once cool for protection only, using tea towel or parchment etc. Don't overwrap or put in fridge (gets soggy) and do NOT do Step 5 ahead. 

Step 6, sour cream mix can easily be made ahead, kept in fridge.

Step 8, chop topping ingredients ahead of time but I'd keep wet and dry ingredients separate til after it's baked.

When ready, continue per recipe, considering that things that are cold will take longer to cook...might result in overbaked pastry edges! You could bring ingredients out of fridge 30 minutes ahead &/or crimp foil lightly on pastry edges if too brown before it's done.

Remember, there are no fast and hard rules. Cooking is a creative process. Enjoy!
@RudisHuman. Wow! Really useful. I’m always looking for ways to prep to reduce pre dinner or party efforts.

I made this nearly exactly as described and it turned out beautifully and LOOKED incredible, my guests wouldn’t stop talking about it! 
I sliced the squash quite thinly so I could easily arrange them beautifully. I par baked them until soft but not falling apart and then they got nicely golden and caramelized at the edges during the final bake. Be sure to check your salt levels, I ended up needing to add a decent amount of finishing salt to make the flavors pop. I chose chives as the finishing herb which was the right choice imo. The entire thing was gone within minutes 😋
In Melissa we trust!

I made this for an early commitment this morning, so prep was everything! Last night I roasted the squash (Acorn was available), grated the cheese, made the sour cream topping, and chopped the nuts. I recommend a little more squash, a little less oil, and cutting the squash into half inch slices. I cut mine into smaller bites, and it cooked too quickly, leaving some scorched bits. I thought I had my first ever Melissa Clark epic fail - chef's error! Easily fixed by baking the puff pastry as instructed, spreading the topping and baking another 10 minutes before adding the squash since it was already completed cooked. The second bake was only 6 minutes, or until the squash was heated through. Prepared as Melissa intended, 5 stars! Can't wait to try this with other veggies, and mushrooms would be particularly good.
Thanks for your suggestions and adjusted timing. 425 is too hot for delicata (speaking from experience)

-Subbed pumpkin seeds for pistachios (toasted on stovetop in dry skillet)
-Added leftover egg wash to sour cream mixture before spreading on tart
-All-butter puff pastry puffed more than expected, but not a problem

I made this yesterday and was really disappointed in the general flavor.  It seemed like it would taste really good from reading the ingredients, but it was just boring to eat.  I think some work needs to go into the sour cream cheese mixture.  It lacks complexity.
@K8R. I wonder about substituting feta for the Parmesan? There’s a carrot tart recipe that uses that mix and it’s phenomenal.
@Laura. I added sliced Kalamata olives at the last baking step for more flavor. Turned out great.

The oven temp was way too hot for my delicata squash, I think because of the added honey...many burnt and I had to chop and roast more for a briefer time. Next time I would roast the squash at a lower temp and just wait to bake the pastry until they were done. That said, the result was delicious and highly appreciated at my Christmas potluck!

Wow! What a delicious and relatively easy dish to make. I didn't realize I had to prick the dough all the way through, so it puffed up in the first bake. I poked some more to deflate it and it worked out fine. 
Also I didn't have enough delicata squash at home, so I added a couple of sliced orange beets and a red onion to the roasting pan. They were a great addition!
I'll definitely make this one again, would be great to make for guests.

I make 2 roasted delicata squash cut in crescents tossed with half circles of 1 red onion - (both sliced about 1/3 of an inch); 2 cloves garlic smashed; 2 tbsp. real maple syrup and 2 tbsp. olive oil; 1 or less teas. chili flakes (preferable aleppo chili which are milder);4 stalks fresh thyme leaves or Roast 20 - 30 at 400F - 425F. Turn a couple of times until veg is carmelized and just tender. Top tart with this.
??  I get that the "bare" puff pastry is cooked on the top rack.  I assume that roasting the squash is on the bottom rack.  What about the finished produce (combined pastry + squash)?
@DeborahT same question!

This was one of the most amazing appetizers that I have ever made!! I rarely make comments but needed to hear because it was great.  I made the delicate squash a day ahead of time and carmelized a red onion and added that to the squash.  It added another dimension and was wonderful.  Did not add any honey at the end and the result was sweet enough. Pistachios at the end added great crunch and flavor.  Reheated a few left over pieces the next day and was just as good.  This deserves 5 stars!!
Not sure the squash and pastry complemented one another. Or maybe I did something wrong. It did turn out as beautiful as the pictures though.

This was good but needed a healthy serving of each serving garnish, especially sea salt, to make the flavors bright enough. I also added a squeeze of lemon juice to try and brighten it up but the moisture wasn’t great…perhaps lemon zest in the vegetable mixture or added in the last 5 min of baking time would be a welcome addition. Might cook again, with modifications.

I felt like the hot honey ruins this dish. I tried some pieces with jalapeno ranch instead, and it was a much better fit. And the more herbs, the better… chives, cilantro, parsley. As others said, adding another veggie to accompany the squash seems like a good idea. A great starting point for a killer personalized dish.

My first time using puff pastry! Visually turned out beautifully. I included onions at another readers suggestion which I’m glad I did.  I do wish mine came out more flavorfully, but perhaps I will be even more generous with the salt, olive oil and honey next time around.  Look forward to trying this again!
It was decent. Probably wouldn’t make again though.

Just made this for a brunch party and it was a huge hit. The puff pastry part was very forgiving~ I scored too hard and it was a bit of a mess before the pre-bake but totally turned out perfect. I also used a different winter squash called sweet dumping squash and it was fantastic❤️
Just a heads up - this recipe says it takes 29 minutes, but thr baking plus cooling is at least an hour. There was clearly a mistake when this was posted, but it is well worth the time it takes!

I’m not much of a cook but had a Delicata Squash that someone had given me for Halloween decoration. Had a puff pastry in the freezer so I thought Why Not. It was so delicious. Has given me confidence to make the. Raspy Potato and Sour Cream Tart.

Can anyone comment? Recipe calls for 1 pkg puff pastry, 14 - 16 oz. All I could find was Pepp. Farm.  That box contains TWO sheets of pastry with a total weight of 17.1 oz.  I'm trying to decide if I need to prep both sheets to accommodate all the ingredients.  Or will one sheet suffice?  I'd rather not thaw 2nd one if I don't need it.  I have the rest of recipe prepped and ready to go. Thoughts?
@KNRI one sheet is fine.

My puff pastry came in two sheets and I had trouble joining them so I made two separate tarts.  This gave me the opportunity to sample one before the party.  I agree with previous reviews that the final drizzle of salt, olive oil and hot honey are key.  I had to add quite a bit of salt an hot honey to perk it up but everyone raved about the finished product.  I also added some jarred caramelized onions before baking.  I’m sure caramelizing them yourself would be better but they did add a lot of flavor.

Served at a cocktail party. The women loved it. Men not so much. Vegetable gender bias?? I used butternut squash and it holds up well in double baking. Slice with a pizza cutter. Maybe and some onion next time.

It was beautiful but kind of bland! We didn't have hot honey so went with normal honey + cayenne pepper, maybe that was the issue. The squash also roasted extremely fast in step 2 so we waited to add it to the pastry for the bake in step 7 until the pastry only had about 10 minutes left to cook. Wondering what we did wrong because overall it was disappointing, just tasted like underseasoned delicata squash and pastry.
Loved this.  Used pumpkin seeds instead of pistachios as per comment.  Used half recipe for half a puff pastry sheet cut lengthwise. Next time will try to more artfully arrange Delicata slices
Also chopped up some red onion to add to squash as per comment and would repeat
Crowd pleaser!  available for adjustments.  Allergic to pistachios, so substituted macadamia nuts.  Also did another puff pastry recipe with lox, etc., but this one was more popular.

I know, I know, I know. I changed the recipe so it’s not valid, but I’m just saying…I had an eggplant so I roasted that with half the squash. I also added finely grated fontina to the final bake instead of parm and it was amazing. I did regular honey(from our own hives, but who cares, it doesn’t matter and not everyone is married to a bee guy) and chili flakes and it was wonderful. I could have eaten the whole thing solo.

Delicious and easy!  
I made it twice and the results were better when I followed the directions on the box for the rolled sheet of puff pastry:  I didn’t roll the pastry larger and I used the oven setting on the box (400 degrees).  I also followed another’s advice and roasted the squash at only 350 degrees.
While eating, my fingers were sticky with honey, so my recommendation if it is intended as finger food at a cocktail party, is to omit the final drizzles of  honey and oil and the added nuts.  The tart is still delicious and you host isn’t cleaning up sticky prints and picking up random nuts that rolled off.  (Embarrassing.)
Any idea what one can make day before? whole thing?

I have an irrational dislike of sour cream, which I understand is fully a ‘me’ problem. But…does any kind soul have an opinion about whether this could be made with crème fraiche or another substitute?
@Sonia In Melissa Clark’s Tuna Rillettes recipe (NYT Cooking) she has used crème fraîche, sour cream or Greek yogurt. Maybe these would be options for this recipe, too.

After par baking my crust, it shrunk considerably. I later read that I should have let it rest.  Anyone else with this issue?  Otherwise, made as instructed, no substitutions, and it was gorgeous and received lots of compliments.

It tastes even better the next day.  Delicious.  Hot honey, olive oil, parsley and pistachio pieces are a must!!  

Because I don’t like puff pastry I’m giving it a 4-star rating. Next time I think I’ll try Gruyère black pepper crust instead.

I made this last night. I didn't have sour cream, so I used goat cheese. Didn't have enough delicata squash, so I substituted sweet potatoes that needed to be used up. Essentially, it turned into a mash-up with Yossy Arefi's Butternut Squash and Goat Cheese Galeete https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1023861-butternut-squash-and-goat-cheese-galette. I love flexible recipes that allow you to mix and match and use up leftovers to create something fun and delicious.

Used butternut squash, sliced thinly as it was what I had on hand. Followed the recipe but did prep a day in advance as some others have mentioned. This was ok, but I can see how adding more root vegetables, onion and/or roasted garlic could level it up. Would also recommend adding a lot more salt than you think, especially to sour cream mixture or using half goat cheese/sour cream?  I can see how this would be a big hit at brunch. I would probably take some liberties if I were to make this again, but hey isn’t that what a recipe is for, a guideline? 
Melissa Clark can do no wrong in my eyes

Prick pastry much much harder to get full penetration. 

For both sheets of pastry, add a third of a sheet tray of carrots and one can of chickpeas to roast. Add a third squash.
I made this for a New Year’s Eve party and it was a huge hit! Delicious and festive.
It was good not great.
Prepped the sour cream topping and squash ahead. This is a wonderful cocktail nosh that pleased all. Special, but still an easy lift.

Read all the comments and added a salted caramelized sweet onion on top of the squash,  salted the sour cream mix and baked everything at 400°, not 425°. Next time I'd add chopped dried cranberries to the sour cream mix and dot the top with goat cheese for tartness. I'd also add the pistachios to the sour cream so they don't fall off as you're biting into the tart.

The recipe doesn't say how thickly to slice the squash, but I sliced it 1/4" or maybe a little less and that seemed to work. I made half of it without the parm because I don't like parm, and it was still delicious. I added some caramelized onions (which I had in my freezer) under the squash as another commenter suggested; they were pretty and probably added flavor.

Still have lots of delicata squash from the garden, so thought I'd give this recipe a go. The end result was definitely lacking in flavor. I even added salt and garlic powder to the sour cream mixture, thinking flavor might be a problem. It was still bland.
Used  shelled chili roasted pistachios. 😋

OK, I had never heard of tuna rillettes, and at first glance it did not look appetizing (butter in my tuna?).  I didn't have dill or capers so I substituted finely diced dill pickle and used sour cream.  For the tuna I used Genova yellowfin in olive oil. I spread it on dark rye that I cut into small triangles.  Friggin delicious and I felt so fancy!  Tuna rillettes is now part of my life.  Thank you Melissa.

Perfect timing to have this new Melissa Clark recipe published (12/23) to make today to use for Christmas Eve "veg and fishes only" dinner tomorrow to continue in my husband's family tradition.  This is finger-snap easy to make, very good tasted as it was being made and I know it will be even better with an day's rest in the fridge before serving it.  Cook's Note:  I used Oregon's Choice Albacore, my fav for quality, in 7 oz can size w/a splash of EVOO added.

To add to my first note about this finger-snap easy to make dish, good when made and let rest in fridge for an hour if you're in a time pinch but even better with with overnight refrigeration,  remember this has butter in it so you will need to plan the time to let it sit at room temp for a little bit to make it spreadable when ready to use.   As bonus, it is economical too, even factoring cost of top quality tuna.
@dimmerswitch If you toast the bread, you won’t need to let the tuna return to room temperature. The warm toast will quickly soften the topping.
Could you use cooked salmon?

Pam - You ask if cooked salmon could be used in place of canned tuna.  Probably.  But if you search Salmon Spread on NYT Cooking you'll find several recipes for salmon spread, including two for Salmon Rillette, a simpler one from Martha Rose Shulman and a more complicated one from Dorie Greenspan.

@dimmerswitch

Have made Doris Greenspans salmon rillette for several years and always garnered great compliments. Prepare a week in advance and refrigerate until party time.

Yum! I made this as instructed, using dill from the herb list and I chose capers rather than horseradish of those two options. I used two-3.45 ounce cans of Ortiz tuna packed in oil. This was really wonderful. It was super quick and easy to throw together the day before a party. I took it out of the fridge to warm up about 45 minutes before guests arrived. And I'll admit, I ate the leftovers on a slice of baguette the next morning for breakfast. Delicious.
@Kate Ortiz is absolutely the best canned tuna in the market.
I add to the tuna a can of sardines for a richer taste
Omg I love this so much! I wish I had made it x4 because it was gone in minutes. I used capers, sour cream, and parsley, but I think any variation of the suggested ingredients

What a delightful discovery. For my personal taste, I used both horseradish and capers and amped them up. I will add more herbs and lemon zest next time, as there will definitely be many more next times. I served with patted dry English cucumber, radish slices and Stonewall Avocado Oil and Sea Salt Crackers. Everything was devoured so I am glad I doubled up to have leftovers to spread on rye toast for breakfast.
I also made this for a Christmas Day snack. Very tasty and popular. Next time I will add grated or sliced jammy egg sections for an even more enhanced appearance and taste.
... and use cream cheese instead of cream or greek yogurt and, no butter

Good recipe! Of the choices, I used sour cream, scallions, prepared horseradish, and dill and parsley (about teaspoon of each fresh herb). Next time I’ll use crème fraiche, shallot, and capers, and will increase the dill, and I think it will be even better.
Gosh this is good. Used 2 cans of smoked trout instead of tuna. Absolutely delicious for a party, and great the next day on toast!

I prepared this for a Christmas Eve appetizer. It was simple, posh, and delicious. I made according to the recipe and served with red endive, cukes and toasted baguette slices. My guests loved it! It will be my go to appetizer from now on.
Made this for the second time, this time using crème fraiche, capers, sweet onion, dill. (Last time—sour cream, horseradish, scallion, and dill.) Both ways, delicious!
I don't eat canned fish. Could I substitute fresh salmon?

Blueberrygirl - Are you able to share why you don't eat canned fish?  I buy mine direct from teh fishermen (and women) and they can it right at catch so it is really good.  But to answer your question, you probably cook make this with fresh salmon (cooked and cooled first, of course) instead of canned tuna.  But also search Salmon Spread on NYT Cooking and you'll find several options, including two for Salmon Rillette, that start with fresh salmon.
This was very tasty a little watery so watch that you strain and start with a less water to get the right consistency. Some tuna’s have more water content, normal precautions when adding liquid.

I "made up" a similar spread/rillette for smoked salmon that was surprisingly close to this...I used cream cheese (probably a little more than two tablespoons) and it was a big hit all around.  I'll try this one too - I've used all kinds of canned fish in my go to party spreads, including trout, whiting and kippers - all very good, but salmon is still my favorite.
This was really good!  I used less butter and more yogurt and capers. Also used the 2 cans of TJ albacore tuna I had on hand.  Still rocked it!   Thanks Melissa!

My grandmother (from Indiana) used to bring tuna dip to Xmas. Her recipe included mayo, cream cheese, finely grated onion, lemon juice. I loved it as a child, and make a variation on her recipe using whipped cottage cheese if I don't have cream cheese.

I live in western Washington and can my own albacore every year so I am always looking for interesting recipes to utilize my tuna cache. This is going onto the list. The Sous Chef salad on this site is another keeper- tuna cacio e pepe, not so much; maybe just me.
Melissa Clark is the BEST!  All her recipes are thoughtful, flavorful and so well tested IMHO.  This one is not exception!
Okay, but nothing special. The best part is the butter which gives it a nice spreading consistency. I might try it with diced pickles as one reviewer mentioned.

I am so stoked to be the first to review this elegant and simple recipe. Made this for Christmas Eve - was thinking of a baked in phyllo type of deal, but I was intrigued by the simplicity of jammy onions with dried fruit. Didn’t disappoint. I used a mid-range supermarket Brie and a white onion (no vidalias in Bangkok), followed instructions, served with dried fruit and some honey on the side. Delicious!
I have made this for years with dried cranberries sautéed in with the onion and balsamic. Huge hit every time.

Joining Natalie in her stoke for this recipe! Also made it for Christmas Eve and we loved it! The onions work well as an accompaniment with plant based cheeses too. We have one family member with a dairy allergy and are always looking for ways to adapt the recipe and make it work for everyone. Thank you, Melissa Clark!

I don't care for brie, but am intrigued to modify for NYE get-together:  melting blue cheese, instant-pot-overnight-carmelized-onions, combination of regular & gluten-free crackers, with dried fruit and grapes.  My mouth is already watering.
We've never had great success with caramelizing onions.  Anyone know a *foolproof* method - stovetop, Dutch oven, slow cooker, or instant pot?

@Yossarian slowly on a low flame, it takes much, much longer than the recipe tells you -between 30 minutes to an hour depending on how many you’re making. Given that, I make huge batches and keep them in the fridge.

I think NYT allows links. I always make caramelize onions in my 4 quart slow cooker. It comes out great every time. I make a big batch and freeze what I don't use. If you cook it the minimal time it will be caramelized onions. If you keeps cooking it, it becomes bacon jam which is delicious. https://www.allrecipes.com/article/how-to-make-slow-cooker-caramelized-onions/

I tried a baked Brie recipe recently and the cheese was squeaky. It could have been my prep method, but I’m curious to try this and wonder if the type (double versus triple versus Camembert) matters? Any recommendations are appreciated!
Totally forgot to add the thyme sprigs but it was still lovely. Would definitely make again!
I baked my brie for 20 minutes to get it gooey and maybe could have gone a bit longer. Perhaps my 8 oz brie was on the thick side with a smaller diameter.

The recipe had a nice flavor combination. I found that the onions took double the time stated to sauté (an hour instead of 30 minutes). This may have been due to factors with the size of the pan, or that the stove was not quiet as warm as recommended, but it is something to be aware of. Overall, the recipe was good and I plan on making it again.
Served at my bookclub.  Everyone loved it.  Very easy to make.

title: Braised Pork With Lemon, Olives and Tomatoes Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: Country style pork spareribs can be used as a substitute for pork shoulder. They are already sliced, so cutting the meat into cubes is easy. My market only sells whole pork shoulders , and these weigh 8-9 lbs
Great idea. I recently used pork spareribs for Kay Chun's Pork Chili Verde - worked perfectly! I'll try this recipe next.

@skyandstars
This was a great suggestion, cutting the meat off the rib bones was easy. The flavors in this dish are unique and outstanding. I browned meat, leeks, etc. on stove. Then placed meat and leeks in a crock pot, added tomatoes, wine, etc. into a crock pot on high for 3.5 hours. You can then add your olives with the zest, cook for another 30 minutes or so. Everyone thought it was more than delicious. I added anchovies paste and only one cup of white wine. The leftovers should be even better. We ate the dish with toasted baguette to dip into the sauce. Thanks for suggestion!
@MHV Country style ribs don’t have bones, and they are a great substitute for pork shoulder.

We made this tonight and served it over egg noodles — truly delicious! Worth the wait, which we passed chatting with our guests. Very aromatic with the cinnamon and rosemary, and the lemon and olives are really lovely additions with the pork. Heck, the flavors of the whole braise are very well balanced. (In fact, go easy with the lemon wedges for serving; that kicked my portion a bit off kilter, acid-wise.) Thanks, Melissa, for the fantastic recipe.

This was easy to assemble and prepare ahead of time.  Next time I would add a bit of sugar to tone down the acidity and use a small cinnamon stick.  Pork was fork tender and the sauce was nice and savory.  I am glad I added the anchovies!  I will make this again!
Would love to hear how to convert this for the Instant Pot.

for the instant pot i imagine you’d just do sauté function for the first few steps, then add the pork back in, seal, and pressure cook for however long is required for 3lb pork shoulder (i’m guessing 45 min but there are good guides online), then natural release and sauté uncovered again to reduce the sauce for the final step…

After searing the pork & leeks (though you *can* skip that step ), toss everything (except the lemon) into your Instant Pot. Pressure-cook on high for 20-30 min (a bit more if you're using a tougher cut of pork and/or you like it really tender, a bit less if you want discrete chunks of meat). Release whenever. Your sauce will now be really liquidy, so spoon out the meat, vigorously boil the sauce until it's as thick as you like it, and stir the meat back in to reheat.

@DM, I just made it tonight with my instant pot. I reduced the wine to 3/4 of a cup and only used the tomatoes, not the sauce from a 28 ounce can of tomatoes. It wasn’t very liquidy and I didn’t need to reduce. I pressure cooked it for 50 minutes with a quick release and I still had distinct pieces of pork, but they were so tender. It was like eating short rib. It was divine.

I made this the other evening for dinner and it came out great! It's pretty close to a tagine. I made one substitution, ~1 TBSP anchovy paste for the 5 anchovies. The pork shoulder came out fork tender and the flavors melded beautifully. I served it over polenta and it made a wonderful meal for a chilly winter evening. It was even better for lunch the next day!

I generally love all Melissa Clark’s recipes, but this one was not enough flavor for too much effort.  Not salty enough, not spicy enough, not acidic enough. The olives kind of got lost. There are so many ways to prepare pork shoulder, I don’t think I’d try this one again.

I plan to make this for christmas eve, early enough to let it sit covered on the stove and reheated when we get back from the church service ( just three of us, no kids). I wonder if preserved lemon could be added in the last 45 minutes in the oven, since I happen to have it? Most of the tagine elements are there … well maybe next time I make it.
Also, for those who thought the result was under seasoned, I would recommend making this several hours before serving and get all the cooking aromas out of the kitchen. Inhaling aromas for three hours will definitely desensitize your sense of smell and taste of those flavors. Even a 30-minute walk outdoors before dinner will reset your olfactory senses.

@Martin R I stuck to my Christmas Eve plan and made it in the afternoon, left it covered to sit on the stove and reheated it later in the evening. It was a deeply flavorful and rich dish. We had some left over, so I froze it to enjoy later on a cold January night.

I appreciate your comment about “aroma desensitization”. 

When I make a braise like this, by the time it is done I already feel sated just from the aroma.  (Plus, there is the chef’s fatigue … after x hours in the kitchen my appetite has vanished). 

My workaround is to do all the prep the day before serving and let it finish in a slow cooker.  After resting overnight (me and the meal!) I’m eager to tuck in when reheated for dinner the next day.
Follow every step. The depth of knowledge that went in to creating this recipe deserves total respect. The flavor will reveal all.

Did in the Instapot and delicious. Seared pork and leeks first, then chucked all into instapot for 40 mins with natural release, then removed rosemary twigs and bone and bay leaves. Served over noodles, all loved it.  Added olives and lemon after instapot pressure cook while heating through to reduce liquid.

Great recipe. Made per the instructions--my only deviation (sort of) was to tie the rosemary springs together for easy removal. (I don't like fishing around for branches.) The flavor is really well balanced. I was bit skeptical about the olives, but they added to the savoriness. The third cooking segment required 40 minutes in my oven. Most important, it's very easy to prepare, especially for a cold winter night. Overall, loved it and will definitely make again, plus the leftovers are terrific!

I don’t eat pork but always looking for good fall-apart-tender braises with other meats. Tried this with bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs. Other than that exactly per the recipe. Delicious! As another commenter noted, you want a fatty cut so definitely use skin-on if trying it with chicken.

Turned out to be a really flavorful dish, with a surprising combination of flavors.
Agree about using country-style boneless pork ribs. This is pork shoulder, precut into strips for very easy cubing.
Yes, use thyme instead of rosemary.
I thought the cooking times were a bit too long. 30-40 minutes instead of 45, and definitely on the shorter end of the final 20-45 minutes timing.
Yes, served with egg noodles was perfect.
We were disappointed in how little liquid was left at the end, for a sauce.
In future we will keep the Dutch oven lid firmly on, and definitely leave the pot covered for that last baking at 425. In fact,  we will add 1 cup of chicken broth with the wine.
Not familiar with Instant Pot/slow cooker use, but I image doing it that way retains a lot of the original liquid.

Great recipe.  Pork was fork tender and delicious although I also found there was little liquid left after the last baking, as Dave W noted here.  Helpful that someone else had the same issue.  Also there was a lot of fat that was not easy to just spoon off after cooling, especially since there was little liquid left as the fat was intermixed with the pork.  Will definitely make this again though and experiment with baking times, etc.

I seared the pork, then cooked the garlic with scallions (in lieu of leeks) in the remaining oil, then deglazed the pan with the white wine and added the tomatoes and herbs.  Brought the sauce to a boil, then platers the sauce over the pork in a slow cooker. 

Three hours on high, and it looks and smells wonderful. 

I’m going to let it sit overnight, remove the excess fat, and then do the final cook (with olives and lemon) in a Dutch oven.
Absolutely delicious; made it exactly as the recipe stated and it came out perfectly. Makes the house smell heavenly. Thanks for the recipe!
I made this with lamb stew meat, otherwise followed the recipe. it was amazing and easy. May try with pork, but will def do lamb again.

Can this recipe be  prepared in a slow cooker ?  It seems it could be adapted easily.
I'd like to try this for Superbowl Sunday.

I would make a few changes to this. The first is to deglaze the pan and reduce the wine by half before adding the tomatoes. The second would be to add a tablespoon or two of preserved lemon rather than the lemon zest. It adds a deeper flavor and would go well with all of the spices similar to a tagine. I might even experiment with dried apricots or prunes, which go well with pork.
I agree with deglazing the pan with the wine before adding the tomatoes … the aroma is intoxicating.
Would this work for the crock pot? If so any tips? Thank you!
I used thyme instead of rosemary and it was fantastic!
@Drew Thank you for this. I, too, prefer Thyme to Rosemary.
I used both (while quietly humming Simon & Garfunkel to myself)!  lol

Followed to the t except the accidentally purchased bone-in pork butt. Cut out the bone, cooked as directed and served with polenta. The house smelled amazing, the meal was one for the ages! Will definitely make again.
@Suz I’ll bet the pork bone would have added a lot of good flavor if you’d thrown it into the pot a,one with the cut meat. Next time!
@Equilibrist Great idea! And less waste. Pretty inexperienced cook here, but I’m learning.
@Suz a great tip: if there’s bone to be used, roast the heck out of it (30 min at 425) and toss it in. Next level flavor bomb.
This is phenomenal.

I followed the recipe as written, and I seasoned the meat the night before to marinate. 
It was flavorful and not too acidic for my family. I used green Castelvetrano olives, not the green Spanish manzilla olives. The castelvetrano olives are milder which I think suited the recipe better.
I'm assuming you can adapt to crock pot as well? ...after searing....

@Alayna That was my thought. If you can do it in an instant pot, surely the low and slow in a crock pot will work. I’d think 4-6 hours on high or 8-10 hours on low, plus the sear, although that isn’t absolutely necessary.

I thought I would love this recipe (how can braised pork shoulder not taste good!), but even without any lemon squeezed on it, I felt it was too acidic;  the flavor combination was not my favorite - my brain kept saying Italian, but the actual flavor was something a bit strange.   I don't think I would make this again.

Made half for two of us with leftovers for another dinner. Served with baked polenta and a very healthy squeeze of lemon juice. Did t find it too acid at all, but maybe because the polenta was lightly seasoned. My pork required longer cooking to get to falling apart, but it was fine and our house smelled heavenly. Skipped the 425° step completely. Didn’t want to risk losing a drop of the delicious juices to evaporation. Had just enough sauce with the add of ~1/2 c. water after 90 minutes.

Made this recently, and my main change was to cook it on the stovetop because my oven was doing Christmas cookie duty. Served over egg noodles, tho next time will try polenta. The pork was tender and delicious, with just the right amount of sauce. I will make this again!

So I guess I’ll be the cook that braved this in the slow cooker. Followed the original steps up to getting the tomato/wine mixture to a simmer so I could scrape up the seared left over bits, then transferred to the slow cooker. Kept it on low for about 8 hours. With about two hours left, I added the lemon zest olive mixture (with about an added tablespoon of capers cause why not). The smell of the house leaned more towards the cinnamon and I was worried about its combination with the other flavors. Those fears laid to rest once I had my first bite. Wonderful depth and delicious. Served over some jasmine rice I cooked in chicken broth and a lemon slice. Feels like a winter dish even with the lemon.
I followed the baking instructions, but after that much time in the oven, my stew was really dry. I did not find this recipe particularly tasty either.

Made a half-recipe, about 1.5 lbs. of well-trimmed pork shoulder, a couple of teaspoons of Red Boat Fish Sauce subbed for anchovies. Next time be sure to have fresh herbs. Try a 4-ounce can of sliced black olives, drained & patted dry, as the vehicle for the lemon zest. Meat was super-tender. Layers: polenta, grated pizza cheese, chopped spinach, pork.

Absolutely delicious served on Parmesan polenta.  After I removed the lid and increased the oven temperature to 425 and baked for the last 20 - 40 minutes, there was no liquid left in the Dutch oven.  I had to add a cup of water and stir it in on the stove to serve.
My oven is on the blink just in time for the holidays.  Can I do this in a crock pot?  A time estimate?

@Lisa, @Alayna, @Ellen Purdum and others wondering about crock pot: I seared meat, mixed it up with other ingredients then dumped in slow cooker. I only added 1c wine because it seemed like enough liquid, and I knew it would not reduce in slow cooker. Cooked on low for 9 hours (because I was off working). Then added olives & lemon zest and (mistakenly) turned it off, so it cooled down. Eventually realized my error and reheated. It was a bit soupy but honestly the broth was yummy so we just served it in pasta bowls, with soup spoons, and naan. Hearty and good, though flavor was not as interesting as I expected.
Looks delicious.  Could this be adapted to use fish instead of pork?

Wayne - You ask if this can be adapted to use fish.  Fish would not take the long braise of this dish, of course, but the ingredients could be used with fish for probably delicious outcome.  You might want to look at Joan Nathan's recipe titled Saffron Fish With Red Peppers and Preserved Lemon on NYT Cooking for ideas on technique.  Also it is delicious one.
I just made a cacciatore recipe with pork tenderloin medallions. I felt it was successful . Could I use them here, too?

@Wendy Tenderloin has way less fat than pork shoulder, so it wouldn’t hold up to a braise as well. It would work in the sense that it would still taste good and be edible, but you wouldn’t have the same fall-apart tender feel you’d get with a fattier cut.
No, they are much too lean.
Fantastic recipe as written. It’s time consuming but worth the effort. Will try a version with onion instead of leeks and dry herbs, see if this can be a panty dish.

@Wendy it depends on what you're expecting. My Dad favours lean meat so the cooks in the house (me or Mum) would remove it with tongs after 15-30 minutes (check it's tender soft) braising in pasta sauce/red sauce (tomato sauce essentially) to prevent it drying out. Meatballs stayed in longer and pork shoulder stayed in longer before removing. Red sauce cooked longer until 2 hours have elapsed. Olives (considered extras, garnishes) get added last minute to a portion of the sauce for people's preferences and because they're soft, they don't need cooking to soften them, who wants a mushy olive
Question - I always worry that stewing meat in an acidic sauce like tomato will make it dry or tough (or both?).  I’m assuming that’s not the case here, can anyone explain the chemistry?
The best side for this delicious plate are tostones (fried green plantains).

Very similar to her Instant Pot Pork Stew With Red Wine and Olives recipe. I like the idea of anchovies for saltiness. I might add preserved lemon instead of zest. And I always include some kind of beans in a braise, mayocoba or navy do best.
Don't use pre-cut stew meat; don't cut into chink before searing. Much easier to sear big pieces of pork (I had 4 big pieces), then cut it into chunks. This came from Kenji.
@loder Genius-serve like shredded pork for hot sandwiches w/Gruyère or Swiss cheese.  Thanks for the inspiration
Excellent recipe and not difficult. I cut off some of the fat first.
As large, sliced  leeks immediately fall apart into rings, what does ”brown around the edges” mean?
Delicious but too salty as written with the olives. Cut out a tablespoon of salt. Might do preserved lemon and even less salt next time for added zing.

One more thought—-we did not add any additional lemon, regular or preserved.  I agree with the comment that this would have pushed the flavor into a more astringent zone. We don’t think that would enhance the rich flavor of the dish.

Very tasty, the meat came out really tender and flavorful. Served it with rice, the lemon and fresh parsley on top, and it looked as good as it tasted. I don't have a dutch oven. So, I used a deep roasting pan and covered it with foil. I tried it with red wine also and liked it just as much.

This recipe was extraordinary great! I made it as directed. It had a very rich and deep flavor. My family LOVED this recipe. It was a different taste for us all. But we could not figure out the origins? A mediterranean flavor- what country?
@Pam F I’m thinking Greece.
I cooked as per recipe and was very happy with the results.  Much like a tajine..the flavors blended and balanced well.  Going to make again.

This was so delicious. I followed the recipe exactly, except for cutting it in half. I made it brother with some of my leftovers and just ate it as a soup woth crusty bread on the side. I might do it deliberately more brothy next time (more tomatoes in their juices?), and add celery and fennel to the base. Why not? It feels Italian to me anyway, so I might just try that direction.

This was delicious! I made it in my instant pot, so I was a little worried because no one else had done it yet. I halved the wine and only used the tomatoes, not the sauce, from the canned tomatoes. I browned the pork, just like in the original recipe and sautéed the leeks, garlic, etc. using the sauté setting. Tossed in the herbs and spices and returned the pork to the pot. Gave it a stir and cooked on high pressure for 50 minutes with a quick release. Stirred in the olives and lemon rind. Came out perfectly. Family was very happy.

This was definitely company worthy. It did take a lot of time to prep everything, but it was well worth it. We ate it over polenta and it was the best meal we’ve had in a long time!

This is a “wow” recipe.  Wonderful!

I used a 28 ounce can of Cento San Manzano tomatoes, Kendal Jackson Vintners reserve Sauvignon Blanc, included 5 chopped anchovies and otherwise followed the recipe.  We consumed the remaining wine with dinner and served with fried polenta and salad.  About 5 generous servings.

The choice of this recipe was to get a completely different flavor profile from our usual Chili and Hungarian goulash.  It worked!

I followed this recipe exactly and ended up with a really delicious meal with rice. After two days, I took what was left, added 5 cups of broth, a can of white beans, and a 5-ounce package of baby spinach, and ended up with a delicious soup that I served with some toasted sourdough bread.
Definitely make ahead.  One of those dishes that improves with age.  Also, there will be plenty of fat to skim off.  Overall, this was delightful.

I normally don't stray too far from the recipes, but I discovered that we were out of coriander powder just as I was making it.  So I substituted Ras al Hanout - results were amazing.  Very easy (and raved about) make ahead for a big party.
Can this be frozen?  I am looking for make aheads.
Barbara - You ask if this can be frozen.  Yes, this is a stew like dish and any braised dish like that freezes well.
If I don’t have a cinnamon stick. Pills I use ground cinnamon?  How much?  Thank you!
I don't like pork, but the rest of the stew seems amazing.  What would be a good substitute?
I was considering swapping in boneless chicken thighs, which take to a braise well.
Try it with veal. It will braise beautifully.
Love this recipe, but my husband and I don't eat pork. How do you think this would work using chicken thighs?
Not sure to use manzilla green olives here or castelvetrano green olives. Anyone have a suggestion? thx in advance
I found a jar of pitted Castevetrano (Vigo brand) at the grocery store and used those, generously. Green color was very pretty in the dish.
@Elle thank you! I ended up making it also with the Castelvetrano olives as well. I think they worked well.

I made the recipe as written. I think the flavor profile is good, but it is very fatty from the pork butt. I do a lot of bbq, and so I did trim mine, but if I were to make it again I would trim more aggressively, or maybe even better use a different cut. Also I feel like maybe this recipe wants a potato to be included when the olives go in. Finally, the lemon zest gets destroyed by the oven, I think keep the lemon zest out until it is out of the oven and cooling down. 3.5-4 stars.

This in no way looked like the picture (which drives me crazy). 

Taste was very bland and needs modifications. 

Suggested changes:
Omit the rosemary

Add:
1T Harissa (could even do 2T)
2T Crushed Calabrian chilis
14 oz can crushed tomatoes in addition to whole tomatoes
14 oz can chickpeas added half way through cooking

Per the suggestion of others I added lemon zest and olives right before serving.
what kind of green olives did you use? thx

We used a combination of pork roast and Whole Foods Bulgogi marinated pork they sell ready to cook in pound packages. I think this addition helped the flavor immensely. We also used two cans of tomato’s with about 4 lbs of meat. 

It was absolutely delicious and will become a staple winter stew. The polenta was the perfect base. We didn’t add any cheese to it as the stew is quite rich.

I had no time for pork shoulder, so I made meatballs. I used a food processor to grind a 1-lb tenderloin, added 1 egg, 3 tbsps breadcrumbs, 1/2 tsp coriander, salt/pepper and a 1 tbps oil. I made 1 1/2 inch meatballs that I briefly seared then set aside. Deglazed with 3/4 cup white wine, proceeded with steps 3 and 4, added the meatballs and cooked covered at 325 for 30 min. Added the zest and Espelette pepper-stuffed green olives and cooked uncovered at 425 for 15 min. Excellent!
too acidic for me, even with the lesser amount of lemon

Nice recipe for New Year’s Day.  Added leftover chorizo, bacon, bell peppers and extra onion since I didn’t have leeks when the temp changed to 425.  Amped up with cumin, Tajin, and some dried red peppers when I raised the tem to 425.  Put some foil wrapped flour tortillas in the oven and served hot with the braised pork.  Absolutely delicious, so thank you.

I made this last night with 3 lbs of baby back pork ribs I got on sale. I did cut the ribs apart before marinating, but I placed them in my Dutch oven still on the bone. My only other “adjustment was to mix the olives with ~ a Tbsp of preserved lemon paste. I’m happy to say it came out great! I did want to add some yellow corn kernels, but I didn’t have any. I think tonight I’ll add a 14 oz. can of black beans. All in all, a great (although time consuming) recipe.
Delicious with no changes. Using country style ribs as suggested by others is a good shortcut. If I had not used briny olives, I think it would have been a bit flat.

SO good. Following the recipe ended up cooking off a bit more liquid than I'd prefer - added back some water and will shorten the uncovered time substantially next time. Measured the garlic with my heart, which is always generous, and splurged with using my home-canned San Marzanos which I save for special occasions (was not disappointed!). Otherwise followed the recipe closely. Next time I'll buy the 5 or 6 pound shoulder and scale it, sad to only have one meal's worth of leftovers this time!
It tasted too strongly of lemon zest in my opinion.

@Kambria that’s weird. Ours came out perfectly cooked, even though I couldn’t get a pork butt or shoulder and only had a rack of ribs, which were very meaty. I used the zest of two lemons as I found it lacking flavor overall but was otherwise perfect.
Can I use boneless pork chops for this recipe?
Not sure if I used the wrong cut of pork roast (I used a pork butt) but the meat was not very tender and the taste was too much tomato.

I cut the recipe in 1/3rds because it is just the 2 of us.  Wonderfully velvet and flavorful sauce, and it worked fine substituting white onion for the leek, which I did not have on hand.  Will definitely make again, WITH leek if I can find it.  We served it alone, but I'm thinking this may be good over seared polenta next time, or cooked barley, which is a favorite of mine.
Truly excellent! Bye-bye beef!!

Cooked as described in the recipe except that I used rosé wine because that is what I had on hand. This made a delicious main dish for a wintry dinner party. I paired it with No-stir polenta and French lentil salad, also NYT recipes.

The taste was outstanding. The olives and the lemon zest took it over the top. But. If you make this do not follow the cooking instructions. No tilting of the the lid at the lower temp, and do not remove the lid entirely at the higher temp. The contents of the Dutch Oven were baked to a dry, sticky, hockey puck consistency. I dumped in nearly two cups of water and stirred everything to make enough sauce. The taste was there. Worth tinkering with to reach and maintain a decent amount of sauce.
Made this as written yesterday and served with buttered egg noodles. The meat was fall apart tender, so delicious. Definitely a bit of a project, but worth all the time and effort.
Way too long to make and too acid, Didn’t look nor taste all that good, although I religiously stuck to the recipe. Very mysterious ;)

Excellent recipe! I’m a fan of Melissa Clark’s recipes and this one didn’t disappoint.  I made it as described.  I was a bit confused about the type of green olives to use.  I ended up using the ones in the fridge with pimento stuffing.  It worked out just great.  I didn’t use the anchovies.
This was absolutely delicious!  The pork was so tender - loads of flavor.  Served it with side of brown basmati rice.  My family loved it!
I made this last night- the recipe sounded so good,  but it was terrible. I can’t figure out why; I like all of the ingredients but when put together the smell even turned me off.

I also made it last night.  Possibly the best dish I’ve made in the past year.  Simply wonderful.  I used pork butt-which I trimmed really well (threw away a pound of fat), Cento brand San Manzano tomatoes, Kendal Jackson Vintners Reserve Sauvignon Blanc, 5 chopped anchovies, and otherwise followed the recipe using a Dutch oven.  Stirred after 45 and 90 minutes at 325.  Only 20 minutes uncovered at 425 as I did not want further sauce reduction.  Then sat covered out of oven 20 minutes.
What part of a chicken would you use and braise in this instead of pork?
@Deb I would suggest boneless chicken thighs.

I think I have found my all-time favorite NYT recipe.   Not only was this easy to make but the flavor is so unique and satisfying that I will put it in our regular rotation.    I cooked per the recipe but found leaving the lid off the dutch oven for 40 minutes waiting for the meat to completely tenderize is too much.  Next time I'll reduce the uncovered time to preserve more of the sauce.

I made this recipe because it seemed easy and had many rave reviews. I followed to a T and it just wasn’t my favorite. The sauce is just too acidic from tomatoes plus all the olives so I didn’t even consider the lemon wedges. An entire cinnamon stick overwhelmed the flavor and completely covered up the coriander, bay leaf, and rosemary. It isn’t a bad recipe (not inedible) just not balanced/worth raving over in my opinion.

Delicious and well received by all diners here. The dish survived freezing and defrosting and waiting around, so flavors may have concentrated as the liquids were reabsorbed. The locally farmed pork butt had just enough fat to cook, but not enough to need removing. I agree that the anchovies are a useful deliverer of flavor. the recipe is part of our collection now.
We put cilantro on top, otherwise stuck to the recipe. Loved it.

I needed to save time, so I put all the ingredients except olives in a slow cooker and cooked on high for 3 hours, turned to low, added olives and cooked one more hour. It was delicious over polenta and even better as leftovers the next day.
I used boneless country pork ribs trimming most of the fat off first. I substituted 1/2 white wine vinegar 1/2 water for the white wine. A little tangy but still good.

This is an awesome way to prepare pork shoulder roast. I followed the recipe exactly. Make sure to use high-quality imported canned Italian tomatoes. It also doesn’t hurt to bump up the lemon zest a bit. Yum Yum Yum. My dinner guests fell in love. Serve served over egg noodles with sautéed asparagus and green onion on the side.

I followed this recipe exactly and it came out really good.  Would make again.  Served over polenta.  Not sure I would include the cinnamon stick next time as it stood out a little too much, but wasn't overpowering.  Will do more green olive next time. 

Several comments on timing of the uncovered portion of the cooking.  I did mine for about 25-30 minutes, and it was still plenty "saucy".  Took it out of the oven and just kept it on the stove for a bit, on a low flame to reduce it a bit more.
Im cooking this now and I’m on the 2 time of 45 minutes .the liquid in my pot will be gone in that 45 min. How come the picture shows ton of juice what do I add to this help
Are you cooking it with the lid on a Dutch oven? I had plenty of liquid following the instructions by living just a crack open.

Love it!   So rich and warm!  A couple thoughts and notes here.

I backed off the salt a tad bit and it was perfect.   The amount stated sounded high to me and it was.
I did not do the last cook time requested (lid off, 425 for 20 minutes).  The pork was beautifully tender and I saw no need to dry it out.
Next  time I will serve it on polenta.
I also think this could be made with eggplant and portobello mushrooms, instead of pork.   
Such a great sauce!
Made this in the dutch oven basically as written.  It took a long time and was not worth the effort.  It wound up being too salty and too acidic.
Served over homemade egg pasta. This was just delicious!

I made this yesterday. Seared the pork (Not searing the pork? That's crazy talk! LOL!), sauteed the leeks in the pan, and finished it in the slower cooker (about 6 hours on high, another two on low). I was concerned that between the salt (I did rest the pork first with S&P and coriander for about 45 min), the anchovies, and the olives it might be too salty. It wasn't, and the lemons brightened it up at the end.

I served it with slices of fried polenta with cheese on top. Perfect! It's a keeper.

Wowowowow. Made this on a lazy Sunday as the snow was melting and books were being read by all. Fantastic! So so so good. Family of five with a few teenagers on the pickier side. The meat fell apart. Served over egg noodles with some lemon slices, parsley and mint. Will make it again and again. This is a keeper!

This was delicious! It could have used more sauce, but I de-boned the bone-in pork butt I bought for this and threw those bones in a pot to make pork broth (which I froze for other purposes), and I will add some of that to the leftovers. The combination of flavors was exquisite!
So, it tastes great, but. . . definitely don’t use San Marino tomatoes. They don’t have enough liquid. I’ll be scrubbing my pot! 😤
I smoke butt all the time and freeze it in 2-4 serving packages.  I wonder about doing the recipe sans meat and then adding the pork at the last step.  Also shorten cook times.  Anyone?
Mine did not have much sauce. Not sure why that would be. Could almost have used another can of tomatoes. Followed the recipe to the letter and it was delicious.

Happy with this. Made mostly according to directions except used ground spices and an entire bottle of white wine (the second half was added during the last 40 minutes at 425) which kept the moisture level. Served over spaetzle.

instant pot is the way 1 hr, longer if pork is frozen. i used 2 lbs of pork. ground spices are fine. skim the fat at the end, between the olive oil and pork fat i took out 4 tablespoons of fat but could've easily doubled or tripled that. be ready to simmer to your desired consistency, i had to simmer at least 20-30 mins. i found that no fresh garnish was required. anchovies and Kalamata olives added a savory bassline that really made the pork sing. i added a sprinkle of msg and sugar as well

Made according to recipe except for slightly less liquid and instant pot. 55 min on high with natural release for 15 min. 3lbs of 2inch cubed pork. Improved of course on day 2. Served over egg noodles. Definitely will make again.

This turned out better than expected. Just for us, we will make some small adjustments. Chop the rosemary first dont leave whole. Add 1/4 red pepper flakes just for a little heat, not too much. As for the green olives we found that even though the dish was really good the lemon and the briny green olives were competing with each other. I want the green olives in there but will use a less briny version next time. Castel Vetrano will be perfect. So glad we had anchovies in the fridge I feel if they were left out you would be missing some flavor profile. It’s a great recipe but for us we wanted to tweak it just a little. Had it over rice but love one reviews comment about wide noodles. Will do that.

Very surprising flavor profile!  I never "cracked" the cover of the Dutch oven while it was in the oven.  There was just (barely) the right amount of liquid...I might float a bit more wine on top.  I did take up the idea of substituting a bit of fish sauce for the anchovy and added about a tsp of sugar.  Served with Israeli couscous and a big salad.  Looking forward to leftovers!
Does this freeze well?
I am a novice at cooking pork but really want to make this recipe. What do you do with all that fat on the country style ribs?

I loved this article.  I too tried to make every occasion different and delicious.  I once made a Mexican themed Thanksgiving, replete with cranberry jalapeno sauce and tequila braised onions.  My family hated it and looked forward to "real" Thanksgiving the next night at Grandma's.  Now we always have "traditional" Thanksgiving and I make one dish different, for my own interest and satisfaction.

Cranberry-jalapeno sauce seems interesting (esp. with orange zest/juice), if you control the heat.  An ice-cream place in Nashville sells a popular lime-jalapeno sorbet (and there is also lime-, tamarind-, and mango- jalapeno candies sold in Mexico: Jolly Ranchers briefly made these for the US market, but I guess there wasn't sufficiient demand.). 
All of these have just a hint of heat, which enhances the sweet-sour base (for sorbet, the jalapeno is de-seeded and finely minced).

Pressure cooking/Instant Pot in Step 5 instead of stovetop cooking works well. The "meat/stew" setting - 35-45 mins + natural release- is pretty effective in breaking down the brisket's abundant collagen to unctuous gelatin.
The brisket sold at Costco is pretty enormous, but I use this approach for corned beef (which is made from brisket).

Definitely better than tequila braised chicken!
Followed the recipe without adjustment and none needed i think. I recommend the point cut of brisket which weighs about 3 lb. Goes well with cucumber and dill salad.
The only thing worse than too much lemon is not enough lemon.

So I made this, following the recipe exactly. It was moist, tender and delicious — but waaay too lemony. I will make it again, but with perhaps half of one lemon. 

Before serving it, I tried to cut the lemony flavor by adding some honey. That helped a little. Still when I woke up in the middle of the night to take the dog out, I couldn’t help but sneak into the kitchen for a juicy slice! 😋

@Alyce I’ve learned to significantly cut the amount of lemon/lemon juice in every Melissa Clark recipe.  This, of course, is a matter of personal preference, but our family has less tolerance for pucker, I think!  I do love to read her articles and to watch her videos.

Smelled savory as it cooked and the flavor was...good. Not great, but not bad. The brisket can definitely be overcooked though depending on the size of your dutch oven, and how deep the brisket sits in the liquid. A more precise way of knowing when it's time to pull it would've helped me prevent overcooking.

I know! It’s a brisket recipe! But if you’re having people who don’t eat red meat, just mentioning that I did this with large turkey thighs and the results were astonishingly good. About 2 hours 20 minutes at 325 in Le Creuset, till the meat is falling apart. One cup rich chicken stock, rosemary, and a lotta garlic. At the end, be sure to scrape down the concentrated meat juices that collect on the inside walls of the pot. Latkes will be great with this.
Please advise which cut of brisket.  It’s like saying “ steak”, so many different options.  Thanks

@laurie
Based on the photo, it would be a flat.
I never can seem to find a brisket cut at my butcher. Could I substitute a chuck roast or flag steak?
@Binyamin my mom always made pot roast with chuck. It was always delicious.
Could I cook this in a crockpot instead of Dutch oven? Would it be better to cook on low or high do you think?
Is it possible to cook this in a Slow Cooker ?
@Antonia I have the same question!
Melissa Clark's wonderful writing makes my mouth water every time. Happy Holidays, Melissa. I can't wait to try this recipe.
Has anybody tried using a slow cooker for this?
There are only two brisket cuts: first (AKA flat) and second (AKA deckle or point). The names are descriptive. The photograph appears to show a first cut.
With all due respect to my mother, this is the best brisket I have ever had. Or made. The lemon is a game changer.

This was a wonderful article and explains your interest and expertise in food and food writing. What an exciting time around the family dinner table  you must have had, full of culinary surprises. I’ll try your lemony brisket though the flavor of lemon seems off in this dish but will hold off commenting more until I’ve tried it.

Delicious - a smart and simple new take on brisket.  Will make again and agree that it is holiday worthy.  Love the lemon zing.  I added bay leaves and they gave a nice fragrance without being overpowering - Rosemary and thyme can be too much in that regard.  

The addition of fresh herbs and uncooked onion on top at the end is inspired.  I used parsley, fennel fronds, and red onion - what a wonderful contrast.  Loved the way it brightened things up as well as the crunch against the soft texture of the brisket.  

Changes I would make:

1) No need to add olive oil if you have a flat brisket with a fatty top- just cook it on the fat side first and meat will brown accordingly.  

2) If you use a kosher brisket, reduce the salt.

3) I would add 4+ cups of stock instead of 2. If you cook in the oven for that length of time, there will be plenty of fat but the liquid will not be enough for gravy later.  I may just cook it in my pressure cooker next time to get it done more quickly and avoid this problem.

Big love for this at the family Christmas dinner.  I served with potato leek gratin and green beans with shallots.  All NYTimes recipes.  This brisket was a big hit and so very easy to make.  If I had a question it might be what temperature should the brisket register when finished?  My thermometer broke the low 200s and it was three hours cooking.  But loved.

My cooking fun is to never make a recipe twice in one year.  I cook most days.  I enjoyed your parents having a similar love of cooking.

Should I prick the brisket with a fork after browning it but before adding the lemon juice?  Would this help the brisket to more absorb the lemon juice?
Eager to make this for holidays!
This was interesting but somewhat underwhelming. Sauce was tasty but the meat did not seem to pick up much of the flavor of the lemon and herbs. Not sure if I will make again.
I presume when she says "preferably not unsalted" stock that she means "preferably not salted" as there seems to be adequate salt added in any case. any thoughts?
She means salted.  The "not" and "un" cancel each other out.  I generally buy low-sodium & adjust from there.

I made this this weekend with a whole brisket (20 lbs) from Costco.  It took a long time to cook to an internal temperature of 195 but it finally got there.  Put in the refrigerator overnight and then sliced the flat and served as suggested.  We all thought it was delicious.  We will likely use the other part of the brisket for chopped brisket sandwiches.
Way too much lemon. It would be great if the recipe called for a specific amount — rather than two lemons.
Even with a specified amount of lemon, not all lemons have similar notes of tang, sour, etc. Know the lemons you are using, and use the quantity that will give you your desired results.

I had the same comment and agree. Since you're adding the lemon juice at the beginning of cooking, how are you supposed to judge the end result without having a good starting point? You can always add lemon at the end. Harder to take it away without muting all the other flavors.

I only know brisket in the bbq context (ie whole, smoked). When people make at home like this, what section is typically used (i.e. the flat, point, or combination of both)? I just flipped through 10 NYT-cooking recipes and they all call for "~ 3 to 5 lbs beef brisket" (one specifically called for a whole brisket, which is typically ~10lbs). Based on the photos it seems most use the flat, but the point would probably make for a better roast or braise. Is this just a household preference? Thx

I'm only familiar with brisket in the bbq context (whole, smoked). When people make "brisket" at home, what section is typically used (i.e. the flat, point, or a combination of both)? I just checked a dozen NYT-cooking recipes and they all call for "~3-5lbs beef brisket" (one called for a whole brisket, which is typically ~10lbs). Most of the photos look like the flat, but the point is probably better suited for roasting and braising. Is this just a household preference thing?

I made this for our family Hanukkah party and it was great. I'm going to up the lemon content next time - it's a delicious but unexpected pairing with beef - but this goes in the rotation. My usual brisket has a much darker flavor and - critically for party food - many more steps. Thanks!

This is a fabulous brisket recipe! A welcome change from the usual tomato based versions. I added chunks of fresh from my CSA haul of potatoes, turnips, carrots, parsnips with a little  extra extra stock. A big hit alongside latkes for Hanukkah!

Just made this for Christmas dinner & got universal rave reviews! Cooked it 2 days ago & let it sit in the fridge to rewarm today. I followed the recipe to a T, though my brisket was about 6 lbs, so increased all ingredients accordingly. The moment I tasted it this morning I knew it was a winner!

Incredible and easy! I made it the night before as suggested. The next day I removed the fat and added 2 cups of stock before heating on the stovetop. With this addition, it was nice and juicy with plenty of flavorful sauce. It definitely needed the additional liquid. Other than that no edits!
Delicious for Christmas Eve- made the day ahead and reheated. Followed the recipe as written- so tender will definitely make again
Loved it! Only used one lemon based on other’s comments, and found the flavor delicious.

This was our Christmas dinner and what a hit! We did cook it the day before, used #2 cut as the butcher said more flavorful, only 4 T of lemon. When we warmed it up on the 25th we set aside some of the sauce and made a gravy. Everyone loved it. Very good and relatively easy.

I had a 2.5-pound cross rib chuck roast and wanted a change from my usual pot roast. My medium lemons must have been super juicy (I nuked for 10 seconds) because even with 4 cups of chicken stock, it was puckery at the end. I think I had about 3/4 cup. A suggested amount of juice would be helpful. So I added a packet of porcini mushroom farro and a bag of frozen corn and asparagus to cook in the braising liquid, then a cornstarch slurry, which mellowed the lemon nicely. Tasty.
Can this be frozen? If so, what's best way to defrost and reheat?
What about freezing this?
To trim or not to trim?
What wine would you serve with this? I say a full bodied white. My wife says a burgundy. Thoughts?

So i live at a higher altitude; around 5000ft above sea level. its also a relatively cold week where i am near the rockies and we've had an extremely dry winter. Any recommendations for adjustments?

 should i add a pan of water? lean more towards 2.5 hours or 3.5 hours? or perhaps what internal temp should i be aiming for... 

im a novice and dont quite have the eye for brisket so any tips appreciated :)
What wine would you serve with this?

The gravy or juice from the end was amazing but the brisket was drier than I wanted. That’s on me. Next time I will use 1 cup stock and check the meat at 2 hours. Again the smell was amazing and flavor from the sauce. Once you get the meat right this is a keeper!  Oh and I seared on the black stone instead.
Why has nobody made these? It's afternoon, people. The apron flag is flown.
@ButteristhenewSugar making these tomorrow!
@Vanessa how did they turn out???
Cookie week! Not cake. Not donut. Not brownie. Get outta here. I’ll make this next week though.

These are incredibly fudgy and decadent! Made 1.5x the recipe in a 9x13 so that I could use up a whole block of cream cheese (the extra would’ve gone to waste here) and so they’d still be very thick. Also used gf flour so I could share with more friends and they turned out great.
@Islita which GF flour did you use?
@Islita What type of gf flour? Will try to make these gf!
@Islita did you change the cooking time?

These are awesome! I followed the recipe as written. I baked them in a 9x13 in pan and found that they were done in about 34 minutes.

I recommend watching the video! It was really helpful. I am excited to use the leftover condensed milk to make Vietnamese Iced coffee :)
@Lydia what did you think of the thickness when you used the larger pan? Her brownies looked quick thick in the video so I’m thinking of going with the 9x13 as well.
@Castle Cook This thickness was perfect to me! I didn’t notice anything different from what I would expect from a usual brownie
@Lydia Thanks, this is the confirmation I needed to make them in a 9x13 pan too (I don’t have a 9” square pan)!
Is there a substitute for the condensed milk?  3 TBSP leaves a lot in the can…
@LER use the leftover condensed milk to make your drinkable Vietnamese coffee!
You can freeze unused sweetened condensed milk for a few months in an air tight container.
@LER I love it mixed into oatmeal. One of my favourite comfort foods!
We don't care what week it is . . . this is our first pick!

Folks!
I'm a retired cooking teacher. Brownies (and lemon bars, etc.) ARE cookies. They are in the Bar cookie category.
Other official categories: drop cookies, rolled cookies, pressed cookies, molded cookies, and no-bake cookies.

@PJW - wait, you had leftover sweetened condensed milk? Doesn't everyone just lick the leftover off a spoon demurely, then break down and scoop it out uncontrollably until it's suddenly all gone? or is that just me..
S&C Milk also comes in squeeze bottles these days, which is helpful when you need small quantities.  Use the rest to make actual Vietnamese coffee!

I don't do caffeine so I got decaf ingredients. Tasted the batter and thought "Did I burn this?" Took them to the office anyway & made people aware that I may have burned the batter. Compliments came in for the brownies all day. They have all been eaten. Several people asked for the recipe. Turns out, I just really don't like coffee. And decaf apparently tastes just fine, if you want to take them to an evening event. Easy and quick to make.
@Sophie sweetened condensed milk and coffee IS Vietnamese coffee

@Danie
It’s also ThI coffee

@Daniel

But what also makes it Vietnamese is using actual Vietnamese ground coffee such as Trung Nguyen Gourmet Blend, Legend or another is Mr Viet.  It is smooth and not sour.  And, using a Vietnamese coffee filter (a Phin) if you can get one? So the coffee drips slowly into the cup!  This makes it truly Vietnamese 🇻🇳

@Laura Morrison
And Indonesian!
Brownies are not cookies & it’s cookie week! But these look excellent and I’ll make them soon!
I’m shocked that this recipe doesn’t have any Vietnamese coffee in it
@Sophie it has coffee and condensed milk. So …
@Sophie if you use robusta instead of arabica that should do it
It’s not a cookie! But looks delicious. Definitely on my list to make!

Made these today. Follow the recipe as written. I baked them 40 minutes, at which point the edges were dry and pulling away, and a toothpick in the middle actually came out clean. Sadly, when fully cool, chilled and cut, the middle is still undercooked. I ended up using the edge pieces and throwing out the whole middle. They are tasty but sadly, I get no hint of coffee flavor. I used espresso powder in the batter  and ground espresso in the topping as suggested. While I love Melissa Clark’s recipes in general, I think there are better brownies out there.
@Janine I get no coffee flavor either, did we do something wrong
@Janine  Exactly the same experience here. An unmitigated disaster is how I described to the host when I arrived empty handed.
These were under cooked once cooled. I have made a lot of brownies and this recipe is mediocre. They were good, but not amazing. I used fancy ingredients, perhaps my expectations are too high…

To use a full brick of cream cheese, here is a roughly scaled recipe: 
1 cup (226g) cream cheese
4 tbsp (78g) sweetened condensed milk 
2 tsp ground espresso 

I found that more cream cheese topping was very welcome.
Very helpful--thanks!  I am making these today and was puzzling over the 2/3 C of cream cheese.
@exMDer VARIETY! If you look in any one of the great cookie cookbooks (i.e. Dorie's Cookies, Maida Heatter's Cookies, Zoe Bakes Cookies), you'll find brownies and bars.

Definitely 9X13 pan for me.  The brownie crumb texture after 36 minutes baking was velvety and moist but not gooey. The flavor was deeply chocolaty.  I will use this recipe again for special occasions due to the ingredient cost and calories.  
I portioned 36 cookies using the brilliant “upside down” cutting technique once the brownies cooled completely.  Microwaving worked perfectly. Be sure to cover the bowl against splatters and stir frequently.  
I used the Bakers Unsweetened bars, Philadelphia brand cream cheese and omitted top salt.  The brownies had strong cocoa-chocolate flavor but were confusingly unsweet, a bit tangy and didn’t have discernible coffee flavor despite the espresso powder and grounds, unlike Vietnamese coffee. 
Next time I will try Guittard or Trader Joe 70% chocolate, crème fraiche instead of cream cheese and leave out the coffee altogether.  
That said these brownies are really good - much better than box in flavor and texture, dramatic in presentation and easy to make.

@Dan
What is the “ brilliant upside down trick for cutting brownies?
@skyandstars watch the accompanying video. Melissa shows the cutting technique around the 6:40 mark.

I don’t usually write reviews, but these were such a flop and I am not a happy camper.
