First, they need a very large pan at least 13 x 9, because otherwise you will end up with my result which was completely undercooked and raw. You also need enough surface area for the swirl because it created a top layer that insulated the brownies from properly baking. The flavor was good, but I was only able to taste the crust. I am so disappointed in this recipe, and wasted so much money on good quality ingredients. I will never be making these again.
@Jess in Houston Agreed
@Jess in Houston Agreed!☹️
@Jess in Houston.  I agree about a 9” x 13” pan. They’re too thick and I’m making right now and they’re cooking too long. They look delicious tho.
This recipe might benefit from a use of better quality chocolate instead of unsweetened chocolate, which is usually lower quality. You know the brands...
@caffeinated I just bought Guittard unsweetened to make this recipe! There are more brands available than there used to be.
@caffeinated Whole Foods if you have one nearby, sells Scharffenberger unsweetened chocolate. Sooo good.
Made these exactly as written. All I can say is that there are better brownie recipes. Don’t bother.
@Sara in PA I agree. Mine came out rather crumbly, not fishy. Nice flavor, though.
Did anyone else find these very oily?

I made them as written, and they were delicious. A big hit. Next time, I probably would add another tablespoon of condensed milk to make the topping a little less dense and easier to swirl. And possibly a smidge note espresso powder to amp up the coffee flavor.

These are delicious!  Great depth of flavors.  

My only suggestion is that the written recipe should make it clear that the melted chocolate mixture should be removed from the heat before the addition of sugar, etc.  The video makes it clear but the written recipe does not.

And although a brownie is not exactly a cookie I think these belong in a cookie box - it is not a piece of frosted cake!  Two years ago the gingerbread blondies were a big hit (also a Melissa Clark recipe so thanks!).

The first time I made these as written. I found them to be a bit oily and was really hunting for the coffee flavor. So here are my edits:

1) reduce butter to 150-180g 
2) one TBSP espresso powder in batter and topping 

Baked in 8 x 8 for 40 minutes.
Sweetened condensed milk can be frozen successfully.
these are delicious! just tricky to add to a cookie box given how fudge-y they are.
Made them in an 8x8 glass pan and baked 50 minutes. Delicious and perfectly fudgey!

After reading the comments, I’m seeing that you should let the butter/chocolate mixture cool for several minutes before adding the eggs—wish the recipe had noted this! Maybe this is a “duh” moment for skilled bakers, but I wasn’t aware of this, so these turned out super oily.

I will say this is def a recipe that requires a bit of finesse! I attempted today and it sadly ended up coming out of the oven quite greasy. I’m hoping with time to cool and chill it will self-correct a bit. If anyone knows what i might have done wrong, please let me know!
@Calla Same. Mine are cooling now but it looks like a nightmare. I don’t think these will turn out :( My guess is that I overheated the chocolate! This is so sad!
Coffee topping also works well on a good boxed mix if you’re looking to save time on recipe!

These came out of the oven with pools of melted butter sitting on the surface of the brownie :(

2 sticks is a lot of butter, I guess. 

Why do we need to butter the pan and then put down a liner? What’s the point of buttering the pan?
@ML I’m 40 min in to the bake time and mine are doing that too. Not a baker so idk what’s going on

I think one revision to the recipe that would be helpful is to explicitly state to remove the chocolate/butter from the heat before whisking the eggs and vanilla. I had to read it several times and didn’t see anything about removing from the heat. 

I ended up watching the YouTube video to watch that step specifically. It’d be helpful if it were in the written instructions. 

My brownies are waiting to be devoured at tomorrow’s event - can’t wait!
@Snacking in NYC Agreed with this; wish I had watched the video beforehand. I followed the recipe quite literally, didn’t remove from heat, and ended up melting my chocolate chips instantly. Whoops!
@Snacking in NYC Agree! You shouldn’t have to watch the video to understand the recipe.
How does this recipe have 5 stars but so many comments about how the written instructions are lacking and how the final product turned out oily?

Re leftover sweetened condensed milk - make ice cream!! Combine just shy of a quart of half and half, a pinch of salt, the rest of the can of the milk, and any extracts you want - vanilla, mint, etc.  Stir well and refrigerate overnight before freezing in an ice cream machine. Best ice cream I've ever made (a NYT's reader's adaptation from a NYT recipe: one quart of half and half, one can of sweetened condensed milk, a pinch of salt, a tablespoon of vanilla). We customize with add-ins, too.
The absolute best way to cut up brownies is with a plastic knife, like you would take on a picnic. It glides through the brownies without sticking.

I’ve made 2 batches of these. The first I made with mascarpone instead of cream cheese and in an 8x10. The second I made with cream cheese but in a 10x15.

Because of how rich they were I personally prefer the thickness of the 10x15 ones, but the flavour of the cream element from the mascarpone ones. Either way, I gifted half of the mascarpone batch to a friend and she ate them all in one day 🤣

I was so excited for these but there should be a note in step 2 to let the melted butter and chocolate cool down a bit before adding the eggs. Even after whisking in the sugar and cocoa, the chocolate seized up when I added the eggs and the oils separated out and I got dry butter brownies.
Would instant espresso be too concentrated as a sub for the ground espresso beans?

@Me I made these yesterday and the coffee flavor doesn’t come in super strong so I don’t think you’d overwhelm people if you subbed the espresso powder. Or just skip all together and it will turn out fine!
Someone tell Melissa Clark she needs to stir the condensed milk into hot coffee to completely blend, THEN pour the blended coffee over ice. That's Vietnamese coffee - not what she was drinking.

Cookie week day 6! Baked in a 9x13 and they were plenty thick

They are not as sweet as I was expecting and very rich, would go great with ice cream
Did you need to adjust the baking time or ratio of ingredients for these?
Turn all brownies upside down to cut them purty!
The flavor is so rich, I almost wanted it sweeter. The brownie is really fudgey but bordering greasy. I did 8x8 and cut maybe too soon. Would try 9x13 next time!
Tried these today. They turned out so greasy. Even after draining the butter off the top after baking. Coffee flavor did not come through. Some of my guests enjoyed them but I won't be making again.

These turned out very heavy.  I think you can make the traditional "one bowl brownie," add espresso powder, omit the cocoa and the bittersweet chips, make the topping, and you're good. 
This outcome was very rich and oily. The "marble" topping was tough to incorporate and get the marble effect because the brownie dough was so heavy and greasy. The topping just kept sliding around the top. After it was baked, the topping just floated to the top in one big spread.
It sounds like your chocolate mixture might have started "cooking", giving you something like pudding.  The chocolate was probably too hot when you added the eggs.

I thought the recipe was okay. I don’t personally feel that the coffee comes through in any meaningful way, nor did the sweetened condensed milk in the cream cheese topping (and I added extra to the topping). I made it in a 9x13 at 1.5x scale as suggested by some commenters to maintain the thickness, but ultimately agreed with other commenters that the result was too thick, and a 1x scale recipe in a 13x9 would be the best move. The brownies are also far too fudgey for my taste (and I say this as a fudgy brownie enjoyer — but the texture was genuinely more like a block of fudge than a gooey brownie).

These are rich but so good! I made in a glass 8 inch dish and had to bake for about an hour. The video has helpful tips that aren’t in the instructions. On example is making sure the chocolate mix is cool before adding eggs. Which makes sense but would be great if it was written out.

I made these twice now and when they came out of the oven, they were so oily. I am going to reduce the butter because when they were in the oven I saw a pool of butter come up and stayed there while I was cooling them.
@Julia same thing happened to me! So disappointing

The recipe does not indicate to turn the heat off (if using a pan), when it's time to add the eggs. If you prefer your brownies without bits of scrambled eggs in them, I recommend taking it off the heat after the chocolate melts.

As others have noted - the recipe neglects to say when to remove the pan from the heat or to let the chocolate mixture cool a bit before adding the sugar and the cocoa powder. If you mess up like me and leave the pan on the stove to mix in the sugar and cocoa powder and it turns into what appears to be a chunky mess, you can salvage it by letting it cool quite a bit and then mixing in the eggs and flour. It still comes out delicious. I really wish they would go back and correct this error. It’s a lot of chocolate to waste if you miss that step and don’t know how to save it. Mistakes happen but maybe go back and fix them after multiple comments call it out.

These were super easy to throw together, and were super tasty. Mine took 40 min to bake in an 8x8. I wouldn’t say the coffee flavor is super strong and I wish the cream cheese / sweetened condensed milk came through more, but overall I’d make these again!
Could you use good bittersweet ch chocolate and reduce the amount of sugar?

I don't have any unsweetened chocolate, but I do have a box of Ghirardelli double chocolate brownie mix. Could I doctor that up with the espresso and them make the swirl topping? I have every ingredient to make from scratch minus the unsweetened chocolate.
I say, go for it!

@Ashley
Yes, you can, just add the instant espresso to the brownie mix and then add the cream cheese coffee swirl in this recipe.
Can these be frozen? If not, how long will they last on countertop? (Assuming food pirates don't gobble up immediately).
Refrigerate for a week, or freeze them for much longer. They do well with both.
@Denise Funny, I have food pirates, too, here where I live in California. What part of the world do you find them? ;)

I made these to take to the office as a Christmas treat and they were very popular! Where I live you can’t find unsweetened chocolate so I used baking chocolate with 70% cocoa and I reduced the amount of sugar by a third to compensate for the sugar in the chocolate. They were so good and very fudgy. I baked in a 20*30 cm pan and in my oven at 170 C, it took 32 min.

Used high quality (Guittard) chocolate and cocoa which really makes a difference with brownies. Microwaved the chocolate as instructed and it worked perfectly.  To make these a little more festive for Christmas, I used Rouge cocoa powder which has more of a subtle red tint.  Instead of chocolate chips, I used chopped bittersweet chocolate chunks.  The topping needs to be very soft to streak through the brownie batter and I think that it possibly needed more condensed milk to achieve the right consistency, YMMV ; I think it would help to make the topping first (and use an electric whisk!) and let it sit on the warm range while you make the batter.

I upped the espresso powder in the brownies to about 1.5 teaspoons after reading other comments. I baked in an 8x8 pan and pulled after 45 minutes. These were delicious and the topping was fun but I agree with other comments that they hardly reinvented the brownie wheel. And yet: that’s okay! Still a yummy and fun new brownie.
Scrumptious exactly as written! 8x8 pan and added 4-1/2 mins to bake time

If you can get your hands on actual Vietnamese coffee (instant and ground), do it. Vietnamese coffee has a distinctive flavour due to its use of Robusta beans instead of the Arabica beans we’re used to.

I wanted to love these brownies. I baked these last night for a Christmas party, and they turned out horribly - overbaked and tough on the bottoms and edges, absolutely raw in the middle, so raw that I would feel bad serving them to people. I followed the recipe exactly, baked 45 minutes in a glass 9x9 pan. I think the batter was too thick for these to turn out right. I think at minimum these would need to be baked in a 9x13 pan to turn out well.
Can you freeze these bars?
@D evans Yes! Melissa Clark suggests freezing them.

First time baking anything from scratch! Instead of unsweetened cocoa powder I used a Mexican Chocolate from my parent’s home town of Cotija. Proud that I spent time baking this recipe and adding a bit of myself into it. They are delicious!i can’t wait to share these with my family on Christmas Day!

I made this recipe, exactly as written, but in a 9x13 pan. Others in the comments noted that I should reduce baking time when baking in a 9x13 pan. But I baked mine for AN EXTRA 40 MINUTES (total of 75 minutes) AND THEY STILL WERE NOT SET! Totally gooey, topping more than just a little jiggly. What happened??

This was the most decadent and delicious dessert I've ever made! I should have sliced it into smaller pieces because it was so rich. My whole family was impressed. We used the extra sweetened condensed milk to make Vietnamese coffees for the rest of the weekend. I love this recipe and will surely make again next year.

I, too, did not watch the video (not realizing this was a necessary precursor to the recipe) and missed the step of removing the melted butter mixture from heat before adding the eggs and all. I had the brownies in the oven for over an hour and they just seemed to be sitting in a pool of butter. Alas, that was the last of my vanilla, so I am giving up. Always read the comments first!! No Christmas brownies for me.

Tried these again - this time giving time to cool the mixture before adding eggs. I still baked for almost an hour (8x8 glass) and ended up with quite a bit of butter in the pan, again. I let them cool and they tasted decent but were very dense. I won’t make these again; there are other great brownie recipes on NYT Cooking for not nearly as much hassle.

Made these as a NYE treat for myself. Main issue was pools of butter on top (other commenters mentioned this), and even with 45 minutes of cooking they were too gooey (not raw) in the center so they didn’t hold their shape well. Taste was excellent (I did add 1/2 cup extra sugar after reading the comments which really helped the sweetness). Coffee flavor is there but not very strong. I’m not a baker so part of these issues could have been problems with my technique as well. Overall, worth a try and with some trial and error can probably be improved !!

Will definitely have to workshop this recipe: the brownies came out raw (despite overcooking) and so greasy (despite following the recipe to a tee). You definitely have to bake in a bigger pan if you want them cooked through, and likewise, I would wait until halfway through the baking cycle before adding the topping, otherwise you create an insulating layer that prevents the brownies from cooking all the way through. Also, two sticks of butter is just too much.

These were great, but I agree they do not cook all the way in an 8x8 pan. We like our brownie rare, so this was not a problem. I'd try my luck at 9 X 13 next time, just to see. I don't think I put enough coffee in the batter, so it only tasted like chocolate (what a shame), but the coffee flavor came through in the swirl. I added an extra tablespoon of SCM to get the right consistency.
Has anyone made this with gluten-free flour? Your advice is appreciated.

@A. Eater I made these successfully last night with GF flour (I only use King Arthur’s 1:1) and they came out perfectly! I just subbed it exactly as written & had no issues. 

In my 8x8 pan, I had to bake these for the full 40 minutes. Hope this helps!

These were good! The brownie recipe is fudgey and delicious. One thing I wish is that the Vietnamese coffee condensed milk/cream cheese flavor is stronger. To me, the swirl was more cream cheese-tasting than coffee or even just condensed milk-tasting.
Leftover sweetened condensed milk can also be used to make milk tea (the kind sold by boba shops). Just add to hot tea and then chill; boba is optional.
To use up sweetened condensed milk, see. Sara Bonisteel’s Mocha ice cream recipe

Absolutely delicious!  A real crowd pleaser.  Used the larger pan, cooked the brownies for 35” and they came out perfectly.  I particularly liked the coffee undertone which distinguished these brownies from others.

Omg.  There are no words to describe how delicious and rich these brownies are. I made a double batch! The batter is the most luxurious batter I’ve ever seen, and I made it exactly as written. I used both a 9 x 9 and an 8 x 8 baking pan and both were fine. The bowl to lick is the bowl you mix the cream cheese and the sweetened condensed milk in with the coffee. Delicious!  I cut them in 16 squares and I’m going to halve them again because they are so rich.

Excellent, very rich and almost fudge like brownie. Makes a big difference to have the cream cheese totally room temp, as otherwise it’s hard to swirl. Personally suggest NOT adding the chocolate chips, as the brownies don’t need them and it can make it very molten inside. I ended up refrigerating and then cutting into really small squares, given the fudge texture.
Can you make these with almond flour or Gluten Free 1:1 flour?
TCookie - loved your post. Brownies are cookies - in the bar category :-).

Made these and used a very dark cocoa powder so mine look significantly darker than the NYT recipe picture. Watching the video was very helpful and Melissa was fun to listen to. I used an 8” square pan. Next time I’ll use my 13x9 as they turned out too thick. It was also hard to gauge when they were done, being so thick and dark. But trust me, they won’t go to waste (just to my waist lol). They are decadently fudgey. As for the remaining condensed milk, I highly recommend using it to make a mochi cake. There is a fabulous NYT Cooking recipe that is to die for: Baked Red Bean Nian Gao

Made as written, came together beautifully.  But the resemblance to Vietnamese coffee was indiscernible!  They looked and tasted great anyway, especially with an extra dollop of sweetened condensed milk on the side!

I don’t have many uses for 1/3 cup of cream cheese, so I made the original recipe with a whole block of cream cheese (8oz, 1 cup), one extra tablespoon of sweetened condensed milk, and one extra teaspoon of espresso grounds.

I've seen espresso powder in several recipes and didn't know what it was. Instant espresso that one might add hot water to? Sounds uh not good. And what would I do with the rest of it?

But in the video she says it's just ground espresso beans, like one would use to brew espresso. I have espresso beans! Bam!
Instant espresso powder is sold in grocery stores.
@Bobby a little jar of espresso powder is a good thing to have in your spice cabinet. A pastry chef trick is to add a teaspoon to chocolate recipes to amp up the chocolate flavor.

Oh man, I made these yesterday and brought them to a party. They were a huge hit! Insanely decadent and gooey and delicious! Made as written, no mods necessary. I used Ghirardelli cocoa powder and unsweetened chocolate, with the bittersweet chips. Highly recommend!

I had the same issue as a couple of others. Pools of butter and raw center. Someone suggested the batter needed to be mixed better to emulsify. Can others confirm if that might be the issue?
Thanks.

@DM Mine came out super oily, and fudgier in the middle but cooked. Going to try again tomorrow, I think my chocolate/butter mixture didn’t cool enough before adding the eggs. I also had trouble with the cream cheese mixture floating on top so I might warm that up a little as well or add a little more sweetened condensed milk so it could be thinner to have a better marble effect.
Don’t forget to take the saucepan off the heat before continuing with Step 3 (it’s done in the video but isn’t mentioned in the recipe).

The concept here is amazing but the recipe itself isn’t fully functional and doesn’t yield a result that tastes anything like Vietnamese iced coffee. These are good, dense, very chocolatey brownies however. If I were to make these again (not sure I would though), I would take the chocolate/butter mixture off the heat earlier (not clearly indicated when to do that in the recipe), I would use Vietnamese instant coffee powder, I would use the whole block of cream cheese, and I would use a lot more sweetened condensed milk. That might yield a result that tastes like the coffee we love but not sure if even that is enough because this recipe work because it is so far off the mark. Based off the comments of these being underbaked, I lowered the temp to 325 and increase the time to 45 and the brownies were cooked throughout and still moist.

If you’re going for a traditional chocolate brownie, this is not it. However, if you would enjoy a less sweet, more nuanced and flavorful brownie, you will love this.  I ended up using half unsweetened chocolate and half bittersweet, and kept the 2 cups of sugar. I did not add the chocolate chips. It was sufficiently sweet, not overly fudgy or gooey, and had a wonderful coffee flavor  undertone.

YES. very rich and fudgy flavor (I used Guittard chocolate and did not add chocolate chips). I made it in a 9" x 13" glass pan, for 35 mins. The middle brownies seemed slightly underbaked even though the I followed the recipe's toothpick test. If you love a rich chocolatey treat, these are excellent.

1.5x in 9x13 pan (with=>adjustments)
• 1½ c (340 g) butter
=> 12 oz (340 g) dark 70% chocolate callets
• 1½ t instant espresso powder
• 1⅛ t fine sea salt
=> 450g sugar
• 3 T unsweetened cocoa powder (preferably Dutch-process)
• 6 large eggs, room temperature
• 1½ T vanilla extract
• 1½ c (190 g) all-purpose flour

Topping
 • 1 C (255 g) cream cheese, softened
 • 4½ T sweetened condensed milk
 • pinch of espresso powder

~35minutes (but doublecheck this)
So good!! I’m team 9x13” on these! Quite decadent and rich even in such a pan. I’m also in favor of the optional choc chips. Why not? It’s the holidays! Delicious and crowd-pleasing.
@Sue I wish I read the comments before baking them. I only had an 8” pan and after what seems like 90+ minutes, the center is still undercooked.

I made these again today. First time they were everything everyone said; decadent and delicious. But, I wanted more Vietnamese coffee flavor? So like a true Rhode Islander I made a coffee milk syrup using Vietnamese brewed coffee and cut back on the cream cheese and doubled the S&C milk in the topping. I used the coffee syrup in the topping and drizzled a bit on top. Non one in my house will sleep the next 48 hours, but you can really taste the coffee! And I used Bob’s 1:1 GF.

Be sure to watch the video! From the written instructions, it’s not clear that you take the pan off the heat during/after step 1. You must let it cool before adding in the eggs. I used an 8x8 metal man and it took about 40 mins, cooled overnight and cut upside down in the morning. Delish!

Did 1.5x to use up the cream cheese then baked in a big enamel broiler pan. They were done at 35 min (and honestly a little overdone on one side).

They're good - decadently rich and fudgy - but I don't taste the cream cheese layer at all, which is a real bummer!

Also, wow, I ate a small one last night as I was cutting them up and slept not a wink. Maybe a little hair of the dog will help me get through today?
Also, I did add the chocolate chips and wonder if not adding them would have helped balance the flavors.

We baked this for 50 minutes in an 8x8 and although they were pulling away from the center and slightly gooey in the center, the brownie was definitely squelching when we cut into them after they cooled 🫠. Unfortunately this seems raw?? somehow??? so not sure if as written these instructions are misleading, but they did not look as cooked as they do in the video.

These brownies are divine!  I made them with King Arthur gluten free flour as my brother who is coming for Thanksgiving is truly allergic to gluten. I shared with clients and they were blown away. DELICIOUS!
@e.  I also used GF flour and they turned out great.  Baked in a 9x13 pan, otherwise followed the recipe.
Very fudgey and kinda heavy.

I’m disappointed by this brownie. It is much too dense and fudgy. Maybe ice cream could help but as is, I’m wishing I had more people I didn’t like much to offload them on. 

In looking at my favorite brownie recipe, it is almost the same, but this one has double the chocolate plus extra cocoa powder. I never thought I would say this, but it’s just too much chocolate!
These are incredible. I’d honestly 1.5x the amount of Vietnamese coffee swirl next time.

I was dismayed to see such a greasy mixture in the baking dish before baking. When I refrigerated these and turned the brownie block over to cut into pieces I could see spots of congealed butter. Reading other comments this may have been because I did not cool the chocolate/butter mixture enough before adding the eggs.  They tasted more like fudge than a brownie. I did like the tang the topping gave cutting the richness. But I couldn’t get over the fact that I was eating globs of butter and not helping my arteries.
I subbed chopped up dark chocolate-covered espresso beans for the chocolate chips and they amped the coffee flavor and made for a nice crunch. Astonishingly rich brownies!!

I made these in a 9x12 pan because I needed to maximize the number of pieces for a large group.  In my 30" gas oven they took about 32 minutes on the middle rack.  These are incredibly rich and the combo of sugar and caffeine offers quite the jolt.  A little piece goes a long way and is ideally served with a beverage to temper the richness (i.e. milk, tea, or the obvious black coffee).  After cutting, I reassembled for presentation because the swirls offer a nice look.

These brownies taste great and I’d make them again, but I have a couple caveats. Use a 9x13 pan if you have one. I used an 8x8 pan since that was what I had on hand and thought 9x13 might be too big. After 50 minutes in a 350 degree oven they passed the toothpick test, but when I cut the brownies a few hours later the middle was still a bit underdone. I have a thermometer in my oven so the temp was definitely correct. They’ll bake more evenly in a larger pan, and you can also cut into a more traditional brownie size since they won’t be as tall. They’re quite rich so in the smaller pan I had to cut them into squares a little bigger than fudge. 

My other caveat is that these are not a one pan recipe, which is fine, I wasn’t expecting it to be, but Melissa calls it one in the video. Maybe it’s one pan if you have an NYT crew measuring out all your ingredients into little bowls and doing the dishes afterward for you. If you’re looking for something with minimal cleanup, this won’t be it.
I do a lot of cooking but very little baking. Are you supposed to take it off the heat after melting down the butter and chocolate?

Very delicious brownies. Loved the true fudginess texture of them, however I didn’t love the taste. More tasted like basic coffee brownies with an underwhelming sweetened condensed milk swirl. I loved the one pot method(will definitely be doing that again). Overall I would repeat the base recipe but just stick to a basic chocolate flavour again.
Much easier to melt chocolate and butter in double boiler. This avoids the need to break up the chocolate and the concern of burning
I wanted to love these, but they were just too coffee-forward.  I found the coffee overpowered the chocolate. I will not be making them again.
the brownie recipe is almost identical to one I bake in a 9x13 inch pan. i have my doubts that it would fit in a 9x9 inch pan.... otherwise, it sounds like a good recipe!

@Mimi
Yes. It’s got almost the same ingredients as the Joy of Cooking recipe for Brownies Cockaigne!
Vietnamese coffee, at least in N. Virginia is made with cafe du monde
@LER - to avoid leftover condensed milk, one could just make a quadruple batch... or however much it takes to use the whole can.... : )
A delicious brownie but in all honesty, I got little to no coffee flavour from these. Still delightfully rich and fudgy though
@C I was wondering about that. The coffee amounts looked way too small to have an impact. I would want these to really taste of coffee.

@Mie
Make sure it is espresso coffee for a stronger
Flavor, also like coffee, expresso brands vary
In intensity!
is it 9" square or 9x13?! I'm on minute 40 and they still have a ways to go in my 9x9 pan....
@Ctrl+Alt+Del i used a 9’square and am having the same problem
Question for Melissa Clark: Why do your directions specifiy WHISK? I'd rather use my electric mixer - but if I do, will the brownies be somehow not-so-good?
I use the whisk attachments on my electric mixer to...whisk. Never had a problem so far.
The electric mixer will aerate the batter and make the brownies light and cakey.
They'll be fine.  Just don't beat a lot of air into them.  Use a low setting.
Has anyone added nuts to these brownies? I usually like crunch in mine, but wasn’t sure how walnuts and coffee would pair.
@chelsea Sounds delicious!  I would go for it. You’ll often see that pairing in cake recipes.
@chelsea oooo! How about hazelnuts?
Haven’t made yet but ingredient amounts look similar to amounts for 9”x13” pan, not a 9” square. Can anyone confirm the pan size?

Really happy with how these turned out! I used a 9x13 pan and they took 35 minutes in my oven. I also used a full block of cream cheese so I didn’t have any leftover (for me this was 224 grams) vs the 170 grams the recipe calls for. Because of this, I also increased the sweetened condensed milk from 3 tbsp to 4 tbsp (72 grams). Really appreciated the tip in Melissa’s video on flipping them over before cutting. Along with running my knife under hot water and wiping between slices, I achieved nearly perfect / clean cuts.

I followed directions and watched the video.  Melissa suggested in the video using an offset spatula. Unfortunately, I didn't have an offset spatula. Using a knife and then a spatula (as recommended in the recipe) I had great difficulty swirling in the cream cheese-condensed milk mixture.  As a result, the very thick cream cheese/condensed milk mixture ended up being mixed in more than the accompanying picture.  However - they are still delicious. I also used GF flour, turned out great.
350g of sugar was sweet enough for us!
Wondering if anyone has a sense of the internal temperature of these when they are perfectly done.

Made as written, baking in 9” by 9” brownie pan, for 32 minutes.  They’re AMAZING!  Very rich but oh so good.  We’ll probably bake in 9” by 13” pan next time to stretch them a little further for a holiday party.  Plenty of coffee taste, but it wasn’t in your face?  Maybe that’s the criticism.  Very good balancing of flavors.
These are great but VERY fudgy. I prefer my brownie with a bit more crumb, but still delicious. Especially if cut into mini portions as they are super rich.
The flavors are rich and complex. I needed longer in the oven than 40 minutes but seems to be with all my recipes. Beautiful texture when finished!

These brownies are delicious! I made one tweak to the recipe after reading other comments about the espresso flavor lacking. I subbed the instant espresso in the batter for 1 tablespoon of espresso concentrate. I think the flavor was spot on. I started out whisking the batter by hand but noticed that the butter kept leaching out. So I switched to a hand mixer to ensure a proper emulsion. I left my cream cheese on the counter overnight, so I could make these brownies first thing in the morning. I didn't have any issues marbling the topping into the batter.

Made these Saturday and the only thing that kept the four of us from devouring the whole pan immediately was the level of richness.  They were outstanding: tasted amazing, looked beautiful (cutting upsode-down is genius), easy to make, and evoked the flavors of Vietnamese coffee as promised. These are definitely entering the regular rotation.

Made twice in last week, both times in a 9x13 and they were still an inch tall. I can't fathom the size in a 9x9 pan. I used a bit more swirl in the second go around and it was still a nice balance. 

also I could still see the ground coffee bits in the 'topping' after I thoroughly mixed it in.
I made these with hojicha instead of espresso and they came out delicious!

Disappointed it didn’t actually taste like Vietnamese coffee. Just a really decadent gooey fudge brownie. Didn’t need the extra chocolate chips! 

Made in 9x9 pan and it was definitely too thick and still liquidy in the middle after baking the recommended time. Recommend sizing up to 9x13 pan as others suggested.

Made these with gluten free Bob’s Red Mill one-to-one and they turned out delicious but flawed. I had pools of melted butter on top though I did admittedly cut the sugar by 1/2 cup - next time I’d cut the sugar and butter by a quarter to try it out, I didn’t notice the missing sugar in taste. I would use the entire stick of cream cheese next time (it’s delicious and could use more) and either melt it more or use a hand mixer/immersion blender - mine still had lumps.

I made the recipe as written but baked in a glass 13 x 9 pan for 45 min. Did not use optional choc chips as it seemed to have enough chocolate flavor.   Texture was good, soft and a little chewy.  I would probably add more espresso powder and beans next time.  Maybe find Robusta beans instead of Arabica. The chilling and cutting upside down is a game changer.  Had no problem with separation of butter but I had a slow prep time and mixed the batter after the butter cooled to room temp.
These were so delicious! Perfect fudgy texture. If Will add a little more espresso powder next time for a stronger coffee flavor.
I got this! The 'burnt' smell is the espresso! I sprinkled shredded coconut 5 mins before finish, and waited 'til cooled to top 1.5" squares with half a maraschino cherry. Because Xmas!

I made these exactly as written and they may be my new favourite brownie recipe - ever. Thanks Melissa Clark! If you enjoy extremely fudgy brownies, these are the best Christmas gift you can give yourself. Cakey brownie lovers need not attempt.
I baked them in a 9” square pan and they were fudgy and decadent and delicious. I think they’d be even better in a 9x13 pan so they would be a little thinner.
Absolute banger. I made a sheet-tray's worth for a cookie exchange and they were a hit!

I loved these. I doubled the recipe and added a bit extra espresso because I found myself short about 25% of the vanilla extract (it calls for a lot!)

Cooked in two 8x8 pans - one metal, one silicon. The silicon took considerably longer in my gas convection oven but gave me a slightly fudgier brownie. 

Finally, the instructions kind of make it look like you should leave your pot on the heat after the choc melts. I recommend turning heat off so eggs don't scramble when you add them.

Hard to get the swirl deeper than the top inch or so. Result is 2/3 brownie and 1/3 mixed. These are very thick (2"or more) if made in 9x9 pan - 9x13 would be better. Deep deep chocolate - more like flourless chocolate cake than a brownie. Very moist and gooey - not sure if longer baking would fully address this (might dry out the top too much). Serving with vanilla ice cream helps to dilute the intense chocolate/coffee flavor. Easily makes 20 servings or more - a small portion goes a long way.

I made a substitution in this recipe: coconut flour instead of wheat, for someone who is gluten-intolerant. I admit I was a bit worried, but it was a complete succes. The intense chocolate flavour "cover" de coconut taste.
Absolutely insane. Made myself sick. Cut yourself a slice and then lock it away.

I’ve made these twice now. They are delicious and packed with coffee. Very chewy and rich. I reduced the salt, because I prefer a more straight forward chocolate flavor.
The recipe is spot on and easy to follow with the video.
These are so unbelievably delicious, I just want to sob.

I made two adjustments:
- I halved the recipe and baked it in a 9x5 bread/loaf pan. Took a little longer, but yielded really fudgy delicious brownies!
- I didn’t have instant espresso, so I just pulled a shot of espresso as I normally would and used 1tsp (for halved recipe) of it in the batter. Since that adds more liquid, I knocked down the recommended vanilla by 0.5tsp, and was ~2-3 grams more liberal with the flour to soak it up. 
- I subbed decaf ground espresso for the full caf espresso for the cream cheese/condensed milk topping.

I made these 3 days ago and they turned out perfectly.  My next two batches I made today I cannot get the eggs to incorporate.  The only difference is I used Guittard 100% unsweetened chocolate bars AND they were EXPENSIVE!  That was the only unsweetened chocolate the same story had at the time.  Please advise!  What should I do differently?  Frustrated because these are the brownies ever.
These are absolutely delectable brownies that I will make again, but they don’t taste anything like Vietnamese coffee.
Is it supposed to come out with so much residual butter on the top? I went the full 40 + 5 minutes in a glass pan.  Maybe it won’t look so weird when it cools.
One of the best things I’ve ever made. I used gluten free 1:1 flour instead of AP flour and they are still absolutely AMAZING.

Like some other reviewers, I found the texture of these odd, too oily. My only change to the recipe was to bake in a 9 by 13, though not sure that wax a change since recipe just says 9 inch pan, and to adjust the topping recipe to use the entire brick of cream cheese rather than leaving a tiny amount. I don’t think I will bake these again

These brownies are so good! My kitchen is cold in the winter, and the first time I made this recipe the brownie batter cooled too much and I couldn’t marble them at all. The second time around I made the cream cheese mixture first and then made the brownie batter.

They never got past too gooey/not done in the middle stage even after baking for an hour!
Is it because I used super fine sugar? That’s all I had and the only deviation from recipe.

These are SO rich - I cut into 16 squares and then cut them in half again. Cutting upside down worked great! Everyone I shared them with said “these are brownies? I thought they were fudge.” 

I do wish they had a more pronounced coffee flavor. If I made they again I would add more espresso to the swirl. 

I made these gluten feee by using bobs 1:1 flour and decreasing the butter by about 3 tablespoons as I find GF flour doesn’t hold oil as well as regular.
Made these for our building’s holiday cookie swap. Totally messed up in the prep nor did mine look as beautiful as Melissa Clark’s. Even so, people loved them.  Great recipe!

Tripled the espresso powder, replaced cream cheese with mascarpone (following everyone's comments that you can't taste the condensed milk over the tang), and doubled the condensed milk. Came out fine, but did not taste like Vietnamese coffee. I know I didn't follow the recipe, but if you can't taste either of the 'Vietnamese coffee' elements even after TRIPLING them, then maybe the base is simply lacking. I will continue workshopping and modifying this recipe until it works, and report back...
I found the same. Please do report back!

I included this recipe in my cookie boxes this year and they were such a hit I had to make a second batch. Two coworkers said they were the best brownies they’ve ever had. My neighbour asked which bakery I bought them at. Unreal!!

These are insanely good! I strongly caution using a 9x13 pan. Yes, 9x9 does give you a thicker brownie, but 40 minutes is dead on accurate for that size. Just below the paper thin crunch, the rest of the brownie is a perfect texture. 16 of these from a 9x9 pan is definitely the right size due to the richness. I would suggest perhaps making the topping first then the batter and Ms. Clark suggest having the topping and batter the same temp when doing the swirl thing. Forget restraint in eating!
Anyone else completely buzzing and dizzy after eating these? I don’t drink a lot of caffeine and these kind of made me feel insane.

These came out mostly soft and cakey for me. Only the edges were slightly chewy. Really loved the cream cheese swirl though that added a nice sour element and the coffee flavor from the ground espresso. Would love a chewier version.

Made these for my annual office Christmas party bake-off and took home second place! I used a whole package of cream cheese and eyeballed some additional sweetened condensed milk for a more visible and easier to mix into the batter swirl. Definitely a crowdpleaser and will be making again!

Delicious, but… needs more time in the oven. I’ve made this recipe twice and both times, at the 40 minute mark, the middle was goo. And not in a fun, fudge kind of way. In my oven, this is at least a 45-50 minute bake.
If I had to plan my last meal on earth these brownies would be the dessert
These were great, but we made them without the chocolate chips, so they bordered on not sweet enough. Next time, we won’t be skipping those! And I’d up the coffee.
Too funny. Had opposite reaction to using chips. I'll forego them next time.

I love Vietnamese coffee, so I've been very excited for these brownies.  Such a disappointment!  They were very bitter, almost no sweetness at all, surprising with 2 c of sugar.  Although you can taste the coffee, these don't taste anything like the yummy post - pho treat.  I won't make these again.

Agree with other posters -- these needed 45-50 minutes. At the 20 minute mark they were liquid in the middle. I also definitely prefer a not-too-sweet dessert, but these actually need a bit more sweetness to round out the bitterness. If I were to make them again, I'd probably use a bittersweet chocolate bar instead of unsweetened, and maybe skip the ground espresso beans in the swirl.
These are insane! Make them immediately and you will have more friends!
What do I do if I don't have unsweetened chocolate?
for 1 oz unsweetened chocolate, substitute 3 tablespoons cocoa powder and 1 tablespoon of melted butter.
Three tablespoons of unsweetened cocoa powder to 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil of any kind.
I followed the recipe exactly as written and they were outstanding! I served them to friends at 8pm and their only "complaint" was a happy caffeine high well into the night.
Can this work with GF flour?

Any advice on making these with espresso instead of espresso powder? Or (regular) instant coffee instead of espresso powder? I don't want an almost full jar of espresso powder in my cabinet for the rest of time.

@DavidLovesCookies I substitute freshly ground dark roast coffee for instant espresso in my baked goods all the time. It’s great! Especially in recipes that call for brown butter. I take the brown butter off the stove, add the ground coffee, and let the mixture sit while I prep the other ingredients.
Have never successfully frozen desserts-any specifics for freezing and thaw time?
If you don't have ground coffee can you use instant coffee powder for this recipe?

When the NYT published the Katherine Hepburn brownie recipe, I became accustomed to making rich and moist brownies using less flour, ¼ cup to be exact. Therefore, I’m surprised that these brownies are are as rich and moist considering the one cup of flour in the recipe. It works!
Gotta melt the butter first and coat the pan like she says.  And do it in a good nonstick pot and th batter then just slides out like a large disk to be easily spread.
Any way to reduce the amount of sugar? I love anything coffee but I cannot justify to my pancreas 2 cups of sugar.

@Liz Richey
Don’t eat the whole pan at one sitting?
Just reduce the sugar.  You don't need permission.  Maybe add some other sweetener if you must, but there's nothing wrong with a semi-sweet brownie.
The recipe calls for 3/4 tsp fine sea salt (in addition to the flaky sea salt) for the batter but the directions never mention using it and she also doesn’t use it in the video. Please clarify?
"In a large heavy saucepan set over medium-low heat, combine the butter, chopped chocolate, instant espresso powder and salt."
@Dan,  Thank you! I don't tolerate most cultured products like cream cheese, and have been searching for a substitute. I know it won't work in everything, but at least I can try it.
Can I replace the instant espresso powder with freshly ground coffee beans? if yes, what's the ratio?
@Macy per recipe, 1-1/2 teaspoons ground dark roast coffee beans will also work.
we need an NYT recipe for homemade sweetened condensed milk

It's a pretty standard recipe involving simmering milk and sugar until you get the desired consistency..  No tricks or special techniques, so no need for a special NYTimes recipe.  Proportions and procedures are easy to find online.

I reduced the sugar by 15% and didn't feel like the texture was impacted.
I multiplied the recipe by 1.5X to use up the entire block of cream cheese and baked in a 9X13 pan for 45 min. I didn't have instant espresso powder so I used espresso grounds instead.
I cut it into 32 pieces using the upside down cutting method. They turned out very rich and fudgy.
@Janine Sounds like your oven temperature is probably off (most are, to some extent). I'd check it with an oven thermometer, then adjust the temperature setting to correct for the inaccuracy.
Has anyone had success with freezing? I want to include these in cookie boxes for holiday gifting and want to make ahead…TIA!
From the introductory description: "These brownies last for up to a week when kept refrigerated in an airtight container, and they freeze well, too.'

I’m allergic to caffeine (so decaf won’t do here either) but my partner and I are cooking through the cookie week cookies this week. Any ideas for a substitution that could add something a little extra to this without being coffee?
I love a brownie but these are far too rich for my taste. Cut them into 40 so they’re bite size. Also, not tasting any of the coffee notes.

I made these GF using Bob’s redMill 1:1 gluten free flour. They came out delicious but a bit gooey. Everyone at the holiday party loved them bc they were like fudge! Since this was my first time making these, I’m not sure if the GF flour altered the texture towards more gooey. Just curious if anyone else tried this? Also, given my substitution the 9x13 pan batch came out less gooey than the 8x8.
What if I don’t use cream cheese? Is there another substitute? Do I need a topping?
@Angela if you skip the topping, it’s still a darn good brownie!

Nice Brownie!  I don't like a brownie that is too sweet or too wet.  This was the perfect in between texture with the bonus of the coffee to enhance the chocolate.  I love Vietnamese coffee and I can't say the brownie reminds me of that, but all the same, very good. The brownie was done in 28 min, so watch not to overbake.

too much butter. came out too gooey 1.5x in 9*13 pan. recipe unclear whether to add sugar and vanilla while stove is still on after turning off. also no storage directions? feels like a rushed recipe lacking important details.

These brownies are advertised as "ultra-fudgy," so if you prefer a cake-y brownie you'll need a different recipe. Also from the introductory paragraph: "These brownies last for up to a week when kept refrigerated in an airtight container, and they freeze well, too." So there are your storage instructions. Finally, the pot should be removed from the heat (and the stove turned off) as soon as the butter and chocolate are (mostly) melted. The sugar and vanilla are added off heat.
sounds like you used the wrong size pan
Tip for cutting brownies: pizza cutter works great!

These definitely need a little fridge time to firm up as they come out quite gooey. That instruction should be framed as part of the steps rather than a nice to have for neat slices. I put them in for about 20-30 mins after baking and they were still warm and gooey but kept their shape.

The comments about not getting a good coffee flavor from the topping likely come down to the type of bean. We had on hand a super dark roast that made a somewhat unpleasant cup of coffee but worked wonderfully here.

I made these last night and they’re so good!! I used semi sweet instead of bitter sweet chocolate chips and definitely should have added less sugar to compensate but I didnt mind them being sweeter than usual! I also used a 8 inch pan since that’s what I had but I would recommend using the 9 inch or larger they’re a little too dense for my taste when put in a smaller pan. Like others have said in the comments I definitely recommend using the ground espresso in the chocolate and cream cheese mix the coffee flavor is definitely stronger!! They may not be cookies but still an awesome addition to cookie week!!
Does it have to be espresso powder? Can I just used fresh ground coffee that is ground very small?
These were very gooey - baked mine for almost an hour and they still came out underdone in the middle. Very delicious though!

I just made them; followed the recipe and her video exactly and they're to die for!  Very rich and chewy with a hint of espresso.  I used a 9x13 pan and I like the fact that they're a little thinner given the richness.  They're beautiful to look at, too.  I'll definitely make them again.
I made a tray of these and took them to the office. They were DEVOURED, and everyone asked where I got the recipe. Big success!
Cooked it in a 9x13 at 325 (because metal pan) for 35 minutes, but that wasn't enough. Now I have underdone brownies, even though it passed the toothpick test. :(

Amazing. I love the textures and density 
I didn't  add the chocolate chips and I used 70%
Lindt bars as the chocolate. They could use a touch of sea salt. Definitely making these again!

I am curious if anyone else who had the butter-pooling and undercooking problems also used Kerrygold or another butter with European fat content. I made a double batch in a 9x13” and failed horribly. I wish I had seen the comments to bake a single batch in a 9x13” first.
Wow!  These brownies are so moist & yummy.  The unique marbelized top makes them quite special. They will definitely make it on my Holiday Plate of Cookies.
These were good but they were almost too rich.  I think I might prefer a more simple brownie

Made these in a 9 x 13 pan (cook time 33 mins) and frankly, can’t imagine wanting them thicker than they are. These are INTENSE, people. Skipped the chips (figured they’d be chocolately enough and they are). While these are tasty, I prefer a standard brownie with walnuts.

I made these exactly according to the recipe (except for the chocolate chips). Everything went well until baking: butter boiled up from around the topping; batter never pulled away from the sides, causing me to use the full 40 minutes at 350. The result: sides were burned. When cut, many of the brownies crumbled. Tasted good, but looked too messy to serve. And didn't taste at all like Vietnamese coffee! Will not waste good ingredients on this again.
These brownies were a huge hit. I used a 9 x 13 pan and kept everything else the same. Really rich and fudgy.
Can I use a 9x13 baking pan or does it need to be a 9x9” square pan?
I liked them better the next day cold. Really decadent and rich, even after cutting the sugar about 25%, so wouldn’t be a go to for me but I enjoyed having them around while they lasted!
Dangerously delicious!!!

Made these yesterday exactly as written. But because I only had bittersweet chocolate, just reduced the sugar. So, not quite as easy as fave brownie box mix, but it’s so worth spending a few more minutes and washing an extra bowl.

Most expensive brownies I’ve ever made and they tasted mediocre at best. Had to bake 30 additional minutes. It didn’t use a whole can of condensed milk, didn’t use a whole block of cream cheese, didn’t use a whole bag of chocolate chips….yes, I found other recipes to use these leftover ingredients but it’s frustrating. While we love fudgey brownies, these were more dense than fudgey. 

So many of the reviews didn’t actually bake them, making the rating skewed.

I had a question about cookie boxes. Maybe one of you can help. My daughter-in-law gives me a big tin of cookies every Christmas. She includes about eight different varieties: peanut butter, shortbread, nut cups, gingerbread and others. Trouble is, the gingerbread and peanut butter flavors are absorbed by the other cookies and negatively affects the flavor of the milder cookies. Do you recommend not combining different types? Thanks for your help. Chris Litka

Extremely rich and the least cakey brownie I’ve ever had, the inside kind of reminded me of a truffle chocolate, but may have had just to bake it longer. Don’t think the taste is worth the effort to make again, but fun to try
These “brownies” were unbearably sweet. Too much sugar in the chocolate mix. Will not make again
These are very fudgy brownies. And I forgot to add the chocolate chips. My guests loved them, but I prefer a chewier brownie.

These are deeply fudgy (and I didn’t even include the chocolate chips or the cocoa!)

The coffee swirl has a creamy-yet-savoury kick to it. Delicious.

I doubled the recipe and baked in a 9x13 pan. At 30 min, center was still super jiggly. Ended up going to 45 min overall but center was still underbaked with edges pretty hard and chewy. I’d recommend a single batch per 9x13. Despite all that, they still taste amazing!
These were fantastic! Now I cannot imagine making brownies any other way.
Moist Dense Delicious.... I finished each square off with a dark chocolate coffee bean.  Bit hit!
I subbed drained ricotta for the cream cheese and
Delicious. I had to put them in the freezer so they’ll be around for a while. Not too complicated recipe

I cooked mine in a convection oven for 35 minutes. The toothpick pulled out clean so I pulled them from the oven. After refrigerating and cutting them up, they are super dense and thick. They taste more like fudge than a brownie. I cut them super tiny in 1” squares but they are still too rich for me.

@Notes
Totally agree they are more like fudge than brownies - but have had rave reviews.  Just made my second batch and plan to cut them in much smaller pieces than recommended 16 for 9x9 pan.
Great flavor, but as other bakers said, that was A LOT OF BUTTER.
The amazing ingredients didn’t add up to anything spectacular; sadly, not worth the effort.

These are phenomenal! Best brownies ever. I used Lindt 78% and 90% cocoa bars for the chocolate. Follow Melissa’s technique to use an offset spatula to create the swirl and cut them upside down, keeping the knife clean. I cut them into 1” squares so they were bite sized treats!
Sorry for the typos!  Best brownies ever if I can figure out why the eggs won't incorporate.
@EllenC. Hi Ellen. Allow the chocolate mixture to cool off before you add the eggs. Whisk one egg at a time.

I made these as an entry to the cookie competition at my workplace. In the description card, I included the definition of a cookie from
Oxford “a small sweet cake” so I wouldn’t get disqualified. I won the competition!  My kids, however, found the taste too bitter. It’s the only brownie they won’t eat. This is definitely an adult dessert!
Truly excellent base brownie recipe. I already had leftover condensed milk from the NYT brown butter pistachio Rice Krispie treats. If you like a fudgey dark chocolate brownie, this is for you.

@Janine this happened to me too. I used a 9-inch round because it was the only metal pan I had.  I  thought  I had overcooked them because my toothpick also came out clean. didn't notice the goo till after I'd cooled them,  cut them,  and dug in. Anybody else have this issue?
These are insanely delicious and a flexible recipe. And they freeze well. Don’t be afraid. Just bake!!

Fantastic! I went with 180 g of butter because of the above comments about butter pooling on top. I normally take all recipes and cut back on the sugar to 70%, but the comments were that it wasn’t too sweet so I did the full 2 cups. Exceptional flavor! Love it so much!
Made these according to the recipe. Delicious! Nice fudgy texture and subtle coffee flavor
After these, there is no other brownie. Hands down. Only I might double the cream cheese part and really amp up the excellent.

I made these in a 9x13 pan without modifying the recipe at all and they were normal brownie thickness, maybe an inch or 1.5 inches tall. If I had made them in a 9x9 they might have been a little too tall for my taste.
Swapped out the condensed milk with evaporated milk and just added a spoonful of powdered sugar to compensate. These turned out rich and delicious
A metal pan is generally better for brownies vs. glass. @OregonBaker
These are the best brownies that I have ever tasted!!! So much love!!! My new go-to brownie recipe.

Oh! My! Goodness! I may never need another brownie recipe -- these are so fudgey and the flavors work so well together. And so easy to make. I used Lindt 90% chocolate because that's what I had. I didn't reduce the sugar at all but didn't find them overly sweet. I had a bag of Prince Arthur black cocoa powder on the counter from a previous bake, so used that for the cocoa. I think it added to the depth of flavor.
I made these and they turned out excellent!
I made these exact to the recipe baking in a 9x13 inch pan. After coming out of the oven, I set the pan of brownies in a water bath to cool. Just perfect!!

Made these for my Christmas cookie boxes I made them 3 days before I gifted them and stored them in the fridge with pieces of parchment in between the layers they worked stored beautifully and tasted delicious
I fear that these will be too sweet, but still want that crackly top on the brownies (plus I still want to make sure they will be moist). Tips for this, anyone??
Made this with condensed coconut milk. It did not get as yellow as the picture, probably because of that substitution i made. It still looks and tastes great.
Added some pink gel food coloring to the cream cheese/condensed milk topping for. Valentine’s Day and they look gorgeous.

These were delicious! I was a little worried that the coffee flavor would be too overpowering, as someone who isn't a coffee lover, but in the end it was perfect, and was fairly simple to make. I recommend watching the video for tips and more details!

I agree with Scott G, way off from what I was expecting. The name sets up an expectation that these brownies do not meet. I was hoping for a more prominent creamy topping and a lighter bar overall. These were INTENSE, dark brownies with a very scant, bitter swirl. And not that money is my main criterion for bar cookies, but these are not cheap to make! I want to go back to the drawing board and come up with a bar cookie that does hit those Vietnamese coffee notes because this was just not it

These were really good, too ooey gooey for me, definitely needed a bit more time in the oven (i used an 8x8 pan and baked for 30 mins)

Could use a stronger coffee flavour in my opinion!
With a do over, I wont add any chocolate chips. I think doing so drowns out the coffee flavor. Still good. Just a lot more chocolate-y than I wanted.
My friends wished there was more coffee flavor in the brownie. Also needed to bake it for about 50 min in a 8x8 pan.

Guys…. Mine somehow turned into fudge? Like they had a ‘crumb’ texture/pattern the day before but I checked the next day and they were completely condensed! I can’t tell if I did something wrong or if that’s just how they look. Either way, super tasty and not too sweet at all! Super rich!

Like many other bakers, I did not know I was supposed to take the pan off the heat before adding the additional ingredients after step two. If you make this mistake, please continue to leave comments until they fix the recipe and add the heat removal as a note in the actual recipe.

Loved these! This was my second time making them. I followed the batter measurements exactly, but I decided to bump up the swirl mixture to use a full block of cream cheese. It was absolutely delicious and is definitely my preferred way to make them now!

Turned out really well with the reminders observed to put in for a little longer (45 -50 minutes), maybe mix in semi sweet w the bittersweet choco chips, I  was skimpy on measuring the instant espresso, and taking the pot off the stove and allowing it to cool before adding sugar/eggs etc. Great texture and taste.

I made these, they taste delicious. But I ran out of time and had to rush the cooling part.  It didn't work.  When I removed the block from the pan it broke apart and then when I tried to cut them it was very difficult.  Follow the recipe and make them the day before so you can give them proper cooling time.
It's wonderful! Somehow, it's even better frozen than it is fresh out of the oven.
Is unsweetened chocolate 100/ cocoa chocolate or can lower percentages be used?
Swirling the cream cheese mixture was impossible! Any more hints?
Thank you Melissa Clark for the video on making these wonderful brownies.  It was a great help and your recipe is fantastic!

MAKE SURE YOU WATCH THE VIDEO! The author of this recipe leaves out key details in the written instructions. Nowhere does it say to take off the heat or to add in the eggs one at a time, but without doing these things, your batter will break, and the brownies will have an off-putting texture (ask me how I know). The ingredients are expensive, so I would expect the recipe to mention these things without having to watch the video. Very disappointed in this recipe.

1000/10! Between the genius of Melissa Clark and you reviewers, these minor changes made dangerously good brownies: reduced butter to 175 g. and used 1T. espresso powder in batter. To further boost coffee flavor and make more swirlable (it's a word now), I added 1-1/2 T. Crème de cacao to the cream cheese mixture (you're welcome). Baked in 9x13 pan and cut into 30 brownies. Froze immediately to avoid eating the whole lot.

I made 1.5 of this in a 9x13 metal pan, and quite a lot of butter oozed out of the top as it baked. I soaked it up with paper towels and then put it back in the oven. Not ideal. However, the finished product looks fine and tastes delicious. I like the coffee flavor and the bitterness of the chocolate. My teenager thought it was meh, he said adults will like it.

stats.Total Time: 3½ hours, plus at least 12 hours’ brining time
stats.Cook Time: 3 hours 20 minutes, plus at least 12 hours’ brining time

comments.commentBody: Melissa, the recipe and video are amazing! One question. How far ahead can I make the turkey and then reheat it before Thanksgiving dinner? Couple of hours? Does it need tenting under foil, etc.? Thanks for whatever guidance you can provide.

@Aisling one thing you can do is get a good cooler, fill it up with hot water, let it sit in there for a bit and then take the water out. You can wrap your turkey in butcher paper and leave it in the cooler for a few hrs. It rests that way and stays hot. I have yet to try this because my turkey gets done right before dinner but my friend has done this many times before
Groceries for this kind of feast are out of our reach this year; it’s frozen pot pies I think.  It’ll still be a great day with family, and that’s the important thing.

I was able to buy a frozen turkey at my local grocery store for $.35/lb.  20 lb turkey for $7.  Check store specials - they also had potatoes, yams, celery and onions on sale.  Grocery stores often have these "loss leaders" to get people to do all their shopping there.  I even got some cheap prices on frozen veggies and fresh Brussels sprouts and green beans.  Whole meal is about $20 - probably less than what you'll spend on pot pies.  My daughter brings pies for our dessert.
Happy Thanksgiving!

@ Look to what a food bank could provide. I donate more generously at Christmas and Thanksgiving so people who are struggling can still enjoy a turkey. Just consider it a little help from your friends.

@G I hope you checked your local stores and pantries for turkey, there were some crazy deals even here in the SF Bay Area (which tends to be prohibitively expensive). If there’s a way to connect on here I would love to send your family a grocery delivery for whatever you need tomorrow! Either way, sending lots of love to you and yours <3
My family demands that the turkey gets filled with stuffing. 🤣 How should I plan to adjust the cooking time for that? Thank you!
@Rachel tell them that’s not the best way to cook it, cook the stuffing separately and you can always add a bit of the drippings to the stuffing to give it that turkey flavor.
@Rachel our family wants stuffed turkey also! It’s the best!Martha Stewart has a turkey segment I watched on UTube last night in which she makes a stuffed turkey. Might be helpful?

@MDelia

I couldn’t agree more about stuffing cooked in the bird being magnificently flavorful.  I’ll be 80 years old in 2 weeks and have never ever heard of heard of either friends or family getting sick from cooked in the bird stuffing.  Use a meat thermometer to check that the temp is the same as the bird and enjoy!
For a 20 lb. Bird can you double ingredients or will that be too much liquid?
Use the Tip to figure out the right amount of salt for the dry brine, then double the other dry brine seasoning ingredients. Leave the liquid, onions, garlic etc the same.

this made a perfect turkey and roasting the neck made a deep full rich broth.

I used chicken stock and water as this was for a school. 

turkey was moist and picture perfect.
Can I follow this recipe for a bone in turkey breast?
@Melissa I hope this helps: Watch the video. Melissa has instructions on roasting just a turkey breast, but I think she also (from what I recall) takes you through the de-boning process…
@Melissa yes I would like to know as well!
I got a pre-brined turkey, can I still brine it like this recipe or should I decrease the amount of salt I use?
@Maggie decrease it, please.

How are the heart and giblets roasted? This isn't mentioned in the written instructions and in the video, only the neck appears to be roasted with the turkey (the written recipe) confirms this, but then the video cuts to a shot of what appears to be roasted giblets and heart that she and the crew are eating. Can I add them to the turkey with the neck or will this be too long for such small pieces of meat? Otherwise, how should I roast them? Thanks.
Yes you can add them to the roasting pan during the last hour of cooking.
@Melissa Clark Silly question—what do I do with them after roasting them?
@Melissa I usually chop the giblets and add them to my gravy

For everyone who bought a pre-brined turkey or a kosher turkey, you can rub the bird down with herbs and any other seasonings/spices/aromatics you like, but don't add more salt. Then let it rest uncovered in the fridge overnight so the skin can dry out a bit, which aids crisping.

@Melissa Clark
My turkey is a 4% of solution of water salt and spices.   Didn’t want that but that’s what I have.  Is that too salty to use salt
Doex all the salt ruin the drippings for the gravy?
@Anne the simple answer is no. We have used this recipe now for three years and the gravy always comes out perfect. Just don’t add any more salt..
@Anne just be sure to rinse the dry brine off thoroughly.  Very thoroughly.
@Louisei think ringing the brine off would make the skin wet, not dry as it’s supposed to be to roast up crispy
Big question I could use some help.  if I bought a pre-brined turkey, is that acceptable and/or can I also dry brine it?  or is that too much brine?
@Spencer I am wondering the same thing. Internet says decrease salt to 1/4 to 1/2 what the recipe suggests, but I’d love to hear others’ thoughts. I was not able to find an un-“enhanced” turkey.

@Jill I had no other option but to buy a prebrined butterball this year. It drives me nuts to see these recipes that pile salt on to a healthy food.  You don't need that salt, even for a turkey. Many turkeys are already "enhanced" with sugar and salt. With so many other delicious spices already in the dry or wet brine, salt is superfluous. Consumers - your guests - can salt their own portion to their liking or tolerance.

Many people buy turkeys that are not "'enhanced' with sugar and salt." The recipe Melissa provided says nothing about a wet brine. Brining does more than simply flavor the turkey; it penetrates the muscle fibers, breaks down proteins, and allows the meat to absorb and retain moisture during cooking. Other herbs don't do this. Absent the brine, you're gonna end up with a dry turkey, which many folks don't want.

After 40 years of holidays, I've discovered the secret to an easier turkey dinner. Deconstruct the bird. Take out the back bone, separate the legs, thighs and wings, and use the excess bones and wing tips to make a rich broth to be used for the dressing (cooked separately) and ample gravy. Cooking the bird separately lets me get the breast just right while I cook the legs and wings slightly ahead of time because they are so forgiving. Dry, wet or buttermilk brine are all easier this way.

@JKF agree, i don’t fully take it apart but i do spatchcock it and roast with legs towards the back of the oven. The turkey roasts at a THIRD of the time, with all the skin crispy and golden. Do not skip the dry rub ahead of time, i do mine two days before, it really adds to the flavor and helps pull moisture out of the skin, making it hug the turkey more, sealing in the juices and making it crisp wonderfully. I have tried the brine method and several others and landed here 20 years ago and have been doing it this way since!

Either way is fine. The rack is nice because the turkey will release some moisture as it dry brines so it prevents the bottom side from sitting in a puddle, but just pat it down with paper towels before roasting if you don't have a rack and you will be fine. That said, a basic rack that can fit on a baking sheet is nice to have, especially if you have a convection oven. Keeps things crisper as they cook by letting air flow above and underneath whatever you are cooking.

Unless its fine sea salt, I wouldn't use it here. The larger sea salt crystals are too big to penetrate the meat properly. If its fine sea salt, look at the tips at the bottom of the recipe.

CL, that’s nonsense. First of all, the recipe even gives measurements for using Diamond Crystal which has larger crystals than regular table salt. 

Second, crystal size is not going to make a difference to how much salt penetrates. It’s mostly going into solution with the juices in the turkey. We’re talking molecules, not crystals you can see with the naked eye.

@Chris I used massive amounts of rosemary and thyme. It really doesn’t matter. It was fabulous.

Happy Thanksgiving to my NYT’s cooking friends. I’ve loved both the recipes and the comments over the years. And I especially love Melissa Clark. For a change of pace this year, I decided to follow this prep method. Just be warned that leaving a heavy turkey on a rack in the fridge for hours on end will leave deep groves in your bird. I just flipped mine to breast side up (we’re cooking and eating our feast Friday, tomorrow) and I fear it won’t be quite so pretty - unless the roasting process reverses the grooves? In any case I’m betting it will taste so good it won’t much matter.
roasting removes the grooves

Melissa, all your recipes are fantastic. The only question "to bag or not bag?" Last year we bagged and this year we didn't. Just put this Turkey into the oven, house is starting to smell good. When I turn the temp down to 325, time to grab a beer. Have a blessed day.

I just cooked a 20-lb turkey. 12 mins per pound is WAY too long. I checked an hour before it was supposed to be done and the breast temperature was over 170. Hopefully, the brining and all the butter will save it from being totally dry.

To the woman who asked a question about the added 4% solution, I had that same issue.  Thinking about it, I just cut the salt in half. Using Crystal Kosher salt and a 13 Lb bird, I used about 2.5 Tablespoons. All the other ingredients remained the same. Better to use less salt if unsure. Now on second beer, ready to watch some football.

My 13 lb dry brined turkey is in the oven now and I'm still tryting to figure out how long it's supposed to cook! Other people have asked cooking times and temperatures and I am wishing Goddess Clark might have been the oracle before she decamped from trhe web to enjoy her own Thanksgiving Day bird!
Looks delicious can’t wait to make it!
Turkey was great, but after basting the bird every half hour, the gravy was super salty. Was I supposed to rinse the dry brine off the turkey before buttering it?

I purchased a fresh turkey labeled10 lb plus a few ounces I forgot to note, and proceeded as instructed to dry brine it over 2 days. I just took it out of the fridge and weighed it, and it is 8lb 14oz. I think the initial weight included the giblets and neck, but could some of the difference be water evaporation? Do I cook according to the weight it is now or the weight I started with?
@Rose approximate cooking time to weight of bird alone, but more importantly, use a thermometer test correct temperature. That’s the best guide!
Covered or uncovered to roast

This is the first time I have had a really crisp and brown turkey. I used and dry brine for 24   hours and used a stick of soft butter after sitting at room temp for 1 hour and it stuck great
I cooked in on Convection roast and it was a shorter time but stayed really hot while resting
Very impressed and happy
Hands down the best turkey I’ve ever made! The flavors are perfect together. Will remember this for future Thanksgiving meals!

This got rave reviews from all the family, which included teenagers! Good thing we had so many sides, or there would be no leftovers at all. I followed the recipe as closely as I could, using lemon zest, chili powder, black pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, and salt as the dry rub. I also mixed those ingredients with softened butter under and on top of the skin. Delicious! The drippings/wine/stock made a phenomenal gravy, too.

Pan drippings were way to salty and I was unable to salvage for gravy; oddly the turkey was fine in flavor although uneven in doneness, with the top done too quickly and needing tenting, and the under part actually undercooked. I used fresh herbs with the brining and I can’t say they really came through.

Best turkey EVER! I’ve done turkey and gravy all different ways and this was by far the most straight forward, and easy to manage with the best results. Best gravy I’ve made in years! The turkey neck inclusion is absolutely brilliant, Thank you, Ms. Clark!

The problem with dry brining is that the drippings are loaded with salt and they cannot be used in the gravy. This is a complete waste and it is not worth the marginal (at best) improvement in the turkey itself. After cooking Thanksgiving turkeys for over 30 years I decided to try dry briny. The gravy (which is my favorite part of the meal) was inedible from saltiness. Do not use this technique if you plan to make a pan gravy.

We made a separate gravy from giblets, plus stock we had previously frozen. We then added some drippings from this bird, and the combination produced a gravy that wasn't overly salty.
But as I said in a previous post, we found the bird itself a bit too salty. I think next time, I will cut the salt in half and double the other spice, herbs and zest. Plus I'll allow a fully 48 hours in the fridge to dry out the skin. We'll see!
2025: 16 lb turkey 2 hrs at 325 was too long. Pull at 90 min next time

Best Turkey Ever! Thank you Melissa.  I followed the instructions step by step (2.5 days of dry brining) and my turkey was beautifully browned so moist with lots of flavor.  My guests asked how I did it and I gave you credit:)

Wow. I dry-brined as instructed, for about 18 hours. Before putting the turkey in the oven, however, I used my baster to inject the accumulated juice from the pan under the skin on the breast side. The result was moist and - shockingly, for turkey - flavorful. Everyone at the table said it was the best turkey they'd ever eaten. Thank you, Melissa Clark!
I think this was the best turkey we’ve ever had. Thank you!!

My family thought this was the best turkey I ever made!  Very moist & flavorful.  We used a free, 14 lb turkey from the grocery store that came in a 9.5% solution of broth including sodium.  Tip:  Defrost in fridge 5 days before (still had icy giblets inside that were hard to remove, so take all 5 days!  Then dry brined for a day:
*1 T of salt (no more given the turkey solution)
*1 T sweet paprika
*2 T granulated onion
*4 finely grated garlic
*zest of 1 orange
* 2T chopped fresh thyme & rosemary
Best turkey ever! Followed instructions exactly as posted. Thank you, Melissa Clark.
I didn't know turkey could taste this good. My mind is blown.

This is a super informative video.  While I did not try the brining part of this (I will when I prepare just a turkey breast vs a whole bird), do not discount her carving of the bird!  I have successfully done turkeys for over 30 years BUT I hate carving.  I followed her directions on that and it worked like a charm!  Before this, I would struggle with the leg/thigh removal.   Thanks!

this was a spectacular success. we added an orange & a lemon in the cavity but otherwise followed the recipe. the bird turned out flvourful & totally delicious. the leftovers are still moist. it's perfection. thank you, thank you.

Utterly perfect Thanksgiving turkey! I began the dry brine on Monday night, and used the lemon zest and herbs. Stuffed the cavity with onions, garlic, and herbs. Note on cooking time - our bird was a 19 pound all natural fresh turkey, began the roasting at 425 for the first 30 minutes, and then reduced the heat to 325. I checked it at 3 hours, and it was 180 in the thigh, and 195 in the breast, so it was done! It rested for nearly an hour as we finished all the rest of the meal preparation, and I was little concerned it might have been overdone, but it was juicy, flavorful perfection! Just be sure to keep an eye on the roasting time, if I had let it go the suggested 4 hours, it definitely could have been overcooked. Thanks Melissa!
Hands down amazing. My go to before was buttermilk brine but this tops it in flavor, i was surprised at how moist it was. What’s the science behind it?
After 40 years of successful turkeys I thought this would be the best and followed the advice to a T.  The result was the worst ever.  Oh well, I should have known better.

Defrosted 14 lb. turkey in fridge for 3 days. Followed the recipe using the convection mode. After 2 hours checked breast and thighs,160F. Turned oven off, left turkey in for another half hour.
