Let turkey rest @ 1.5-2 hours. Breast and drumsticks perfect. Thighs and wings pink and not done to my taste. Reheated parts to 165, parts all good.
Overall, this was great. But the turkey did taste a bit too salty. I wonder whether it's possible to reduce the amount of salt in the brine without defeating the purpose.

A novice in the Thanksgiving kitchen, this recipe and video saved the day! I learned that my oven is a little hot, and my 14 lb dry brined turkey reached 180 degrees in just over 2 hrs. I’ll watch it more closely next year! Thanks Melissa!

I have been making the same labor-intensive Martha Stewart turkey for 30 years (lots of butter, wine, and basting). This recipe was so easy and allowed me to concentrate on everything else that needed to be done. I was worried it was going to be a fail, given the lack of attention.  This turkey was so moist, and everyone was raving.  I cooked a 20-pound bird, used rosemary, thyme, oregano, and paprika in my dry rub.  Next year I will be more adventurous!  
Thank you Melissa Clarke!

The "12 minute per pound" rule can be somewhat inconsistent with reality.  By using the kitchen thermometer probe, as Melissa recommends, and use it early in the process, you can avoid overcooking the turkey.  I tried smoking the bird for the first 30 minutes and inadvertently ended up finishing the cook almost 45 minutes ahead of schedule.  If not for early measurement, I would have had a very dry turkey.  Good recipe, Melissa!  Thanks.  DAD

Too Salty - Watch out for the salt.  I followed this recipe exactly and not only was the turkey super salty, as a result, so was the stuffing and the gravy (from the drippings).  This desperately needs modification.  WAY TOO SALTY.  Note the other comments - not in the "most helpful" section - about the salt.

You must WEIGH the salt as described in the Tips section. Diamond Crystal, Morton's, and regular table salt have differing weights per measure due to their differently sized flakes, so weight is the only way to be sure you do this right.

This is the best turkey I have ever made: juicy, flavorful and succulent. For a 14-pound turkey, I used 40 grams of salt plus 20 grams of Old Bay Seasoning and 20 grams of homemade baharat.  Everyone loved it.

This turkey was amazing... My bad was I had the temp up too high on phase two... I kept an eye on the turkey once I realized though and it reached temp about an hour sooner than expected. The bird came out great even so, but had I not been paying close enough attention it could have been a disaster.
This year's turkey was the best in ages.  Thanks to Melissa for her dry brine instructions and helpful video.  I'll happily make this again, as per the recipe.
Hands down the juiciest Thanksgiving turkey ever that made even better soup two days later with the bones. Melissa, you are a genius! Grateful!!
This was amazing - easy and perfect. Followed directions exactly - rave reviews from the family!!

100% skeptics going into the process and 100% believers at the table. Easiest and best turkey recipe. I used a kosher turkey and used half the salt amount for the dry brine. Delicious and not too salty. Love the start to finish video. Thank you Melissa Clark!

The past 2 years I have not had room in the fridge for the turkey to rest there uncovered.  I dry brine 1-2 days before. Put turkey on a bag. Refrigerate in bag (tucked int a full fridge). Take out of fridge and let sit out 1 hour until ready to roast.  Studs cavity. Prepare pan. Pat dry. Coat in melted butter.  Roast.  Perfectly golden crisp skin.

I dry brined this turkey in the refrigerator for two days uncovered. For the salt, I measured out the grams according to the directions which turned out to be a lot less than the number of tablespoons they use as its equivalent. The skin was crispy but pretty salty and as a result the drippings were also pretty salty soI added more water and flour to the gravy to tone down the salt.

Best turkey I’ve ever made. Brined 2 days in advance with salt and pepper, under and over skin. Followed instructions of leaving uncovered in refrigerator. I did tent the turkey with foil right before turning down temp to 325.

This was my first time doing a dry brine and let me tell you: the turkey was juicier than when I do a wet/soaked brine! It helped to watch the chef’s video like 3 times and read the preparation instructions a few times before cooking. I will be using this recipe next year again. Also, the drippings will made for a delicious gravy without needing to prepare for a make ahead gravy.

Melissa- great turkey from this recipe but the dripping for the gravy was too salty for us- and the turkey also was a little bit salty- I followed your salt amount. Should I wash some of the salt off before cooking??
thanks

I was told several times this was the best turkey people had ever had! It was moist & flavorful. I barely had any leftovers, enough to make soup and have one sandwich the next day. :)  I'm so glad I found your recipe; it will be my go-to for every Thanksgiving.

This recipe changed my whole approach to turkey.  I did all the dry brine on Wednesday before Thanksgiving and took it out of the fridge at noon Thursday.  I smoked it on a Pellet Smoker with the bird on a roasting rack and a layer of heavy aluminum foil under it with the edges curled up to catch any drippings.  This allowed the heat to circulate around the whole bird without scorching the bottom of it.  I had a 12 pound bird and followed temp settings.  Carved like the video-perfect!

Don't be intimidated by the length of the recipe. You can do this if you can follow directions. I decided not to and started at Step 5, choosing not to season and "dry" ahead of time and my natural Butterball was still crazy good. You must do the spiced butter under the skin to get the most out of this recipe. Mine was delicious with butter, salt, coriander, orange zest, white pepper and thyme. The resulting gravy was also to die for. Never basted. Did have to tent top to prevent burn.

This was a Thanksgiving triumph.  I've been dry-brining for years but somehow this was easier and more straight-forward.  The one tip I would add is keep a pot of turkey stock warm on the stove and as you slice the bird, ladle a little stock on the slices, especially the white meat, it enhances the moistness. Thank you!

I’ve made so many turkeys in my life from using the turkey bags to complicated Martha Stewart recipes and this was hands down the most delicious turkey I’ve ever made and probably my favorite turkey I’ve ever eaten, also the dry brining saved so much time on Thanksgiving day.

A guest who hates turkey went back for seconds. The meat was even juicy a couple
of days later.  (Fresh turkey, 2 day brine, 6 hours to warm up and a load of soft butter on the skin.)
My husband said this is the best turkey he's ever had. We dry brined it for 48 hours with salt, pepper, garlic powder, and onion powder. Omitted the wine - just used more broth/water

This is always my go-to turkey recipe.  I bought a pre-brined turkey so I did not do a dry brine.  I rubbed the turkey generously with softened unsalted butter and herbs and the flavor was fantastic.  I did need to add additional stock and water a few times during cooking and the drippings made an excellent gravy.  This is a simple recipe and I tend to think the easier the better when it comes to cooking whole birds.

I had never brined a turkey before, but it made a world of difference!  The meat was juicier and more savory than I imagined possible.  I also loved the video of Melissa cooking. The idea of cutting the meat into thick pieces makes sense, as the meat stays warmer that way. I still can't figure out how she cut the breast meat from the bird from the back side, but I could do it pretty successfully from the front.

Omg, experienced cook ,decades. But this blew my mind. The absolute BEST recipe ever. So good that it’s requested again for Christmas - no more prime rib or beef tenderloin. For sure! Inhaled.  Incredibly moist, delish, and the best roasted bronze skin.  We were playing games so it stayed at 450 for almost an hour( 18 lb bird) - but it was glorious. Wish someone had taken a picture of that bird. Coulda been an advertisement…..

Great recipe. I made this for guests on Thanksgiving and now I'm roasting an other turkey because we didn't have enough leftovers.  I used Penzeys' Revolution spice blend for the dry rub. It has salt, pepper, garlic,  turmeric, lemon zest and other tasty ingredients.

My 4,2 kg bird was already overcooked after an hour at 165 celcius. The dry brine worked beautifully, skin nicely brown and crispy. I havn’t cut it yet, hopefully it isn’t too dry.  Will cook turkey the same way next year, but lower the tempetature..

I’ve made this turkey twice for holidays, and it’s always excellent. I overbaked it a bit this evening, but it was still good!! I think it was due to the brining keeping moisture in. The flavor was very good too! It’s a keeper!

I made this turkey for Christmas dinner yesterday and it was hands down the best turkey my family has ever had. The brine provides the perfect amount of flavour (I added rosemary, garlic, pepper, lemon zest and poultry seasoning to mine). The white meat was so so moist. I also loved how it brined ahead so all I had to do was add some butter and some onion/broth right before cooking. A million thanks - this made turkey worth it!

Season's greetings from Finland! I made my first turkey ever following this recipe exactly, and it turned out beautifully! The carving was a bit challenging, but with some (a lot of...) help from the video, we made it. Thank you Melissa and NYT! 🌟
This was the most delicious turkey ever! I’ll be using this recipe from now on. Love the dry brine. Moist and very tasty- thank you!
I made this in my breville countertop oven and it was still the best turkey I ever made.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour, plus at least 2 hours’ cooling

comments.commentBody: You mean a 3/4, 1 1/2,  2 3/4, 4 cake?
Please read the accompanying article.

@Pretzel This cake should just be called Vanilla Butter Cake. From a professional standpoint this is a project is messing up something hoping to get something better. If other ingredients were added to 1234 cake then that would be a good way to start. Honestly, having read every last word of everything, it’s just taking a recipe and changing it because one didn’t have anything better to do. I’m sorry but, really?  It is very difficult to even get people to eat cake in some parts of the country, sad but true. I appreciate your comment but there’s a whole lot more going on in this article that meets the eye. those who appreciate the first comment have very good reason to do so. But you have inspired me. i think i will play with 1234 cake and come up with something, hopefully edible and tasty!
@Mike when you made it, how did the results compare to a strictly measured 1234? Sounds like the author considered these improvements.
The 1,2,3,4 cake is in the beauty of its simplicity.  Let’s keep it that way.
@Tina Yes, second-guessing Melissa Clark is futile. If you can get your hands on ‘The Last Course’, buy it. Written with Claudia Fleming - a delightful cookbook.
Could this be a sheet cake, instead? What would be the temp and time?

@GoodVibesOnly Hi, love your good vibes! basically any cake recipe can be switched to a different pan. Do keep an eye on it whilst baking. add 20% more time before checking. Look for any sign of jiggling in the middle. I believe someone else has made a comment about baking time, it could take 40 minutes depending on your over.

I tried this out since I always have plain yogurt on hand (but not milk as most other cake recipes call for it). I generally like Melissa Clark's recipes. I made half the recipe and substituted the cake flour with regular flour + cornstarch sifted several times. I didn't do the buttercream or layers, since I just wanted to try it as a snacking cake. It was delish! Though it did take an extra 5-7 mins for it to fully set in the middle (total of about 37 minutes w/foil on for the last 7 mins)
@Christine how much cornstarch did you add?
Unsweetened chocolate? Where do you find that?
@Anna In the baking aisle of literally any grocery store.

@Anna Hi Anna, this product is so rarely used it’s almost foreign anymore. Look for a small rectangular box, it’s above the chocolate chips. This is pure chocolate, so you get to add sweetener to the amount you like. Do read the box. A lot of work has gone into formulating recipes for that brand. Happy Baking!
Very good recipe. Best mocha buttercream I’ve ever made. 5 stars
would it be possible to use full fat oatmilk for the buttermilk due to dairy intolerance? thanks!
And how do you deal with the butter?
We would make a 1 2 3 cookie when I was growing up in the 60’s.  It was a version of the cake.  I have looked for the recipe and yet to find it.  Such a soft tea cake.

The cake is very good, and my daughter-in-law who is an expert baker says that it does have a finer crumb than other cakes. The mocha frosting is particularly wonderful, and the recipe makes enough to frost the cake very generously. Unfortunately, the method of mixing butter into the dry ingredients resulted in  flour and sugar being blown all over the place. Putting the plastic shield around the rim of my KitchenAid mixer bowl helped some, and I wish I had done that right from the start. With the frosting I tried using a dish towel wrapped around the mixer to keep the dry ingredients in, and let’s just say I don’t recommend doing that.
@MJ always start on the very lowest setting, and in short bursts, until the flour is moistened, when reverse creaming or adding any dry ingredients.
Delicious cake.  Turned out perfectly for two beautiful 9 inch layers and enjoyed by all. I used the chocolate frosting from a different NYT recipe.  The cake was just as good the following day.

I tried using the buttermilk for the icing as instructed because I didn't have cream. It curdled when I heated it even though it was perfectly fresh and the same batch worked fine in the cake. So I used regular whole milk for that step in the icing and it worked fine.
Made exactly as directed. One of my favorites ever. A little different. Will be a staple for sharing and serving at home. Many thanks.

I am not a baker. But have been cooking for some time. So, I followed the recipe as close as possible, including actually weighing the flour and sugar rather than taking the measure alone. Unfortunately, this cake was unpalatably dry. I used the Greek yogurt rather than milk. I was told that sour cream or apple sauce are also "secret" ingredients. That said, I will not make this again. Perhaps someone care direct me to a moist yellow butter cake recipe. I don't need the frosting!

This is the first cake I learned to cook (with some help) when I was 8 or 9 years old. I loved that the name was the recipe. Our recipe came out of a 1950s copy of The Joy of Cooking I think.

It was my go-to cake for a looooong time.

Great taste but it can have a fairly firm tooth if I remember.
why decaf instant coffee?
unsweetened cocoa is in any grocery store baking aisle

I've been baking for decades and when I saw this recipe my first thought was why were the ingredients were to be mixed in the order given. Out of curiosity I followed the recipe - like others, dry ingredients everywhere and an overall disappointing outcome. "Buttercream" was OK
Turned out delicious! Could go easy on the sugar though.

@Julia sugar is not just a sweetener. It is also hygroscopic, so it adds moisture and tenderizes. Screwing around with proportions in a carefully considered chemical reaction in a formula just because one thinks "it could do with less sugar" will, actually, affect the results.
I wonder why my icing turned out such a dark chocolate color, rather than the lighter mocha shade shown in the picture?
anyone have a suggestion for making this cake a bit moister?

The directions were easy to follow, but the preparation required a great number of bowls to clean up. The cake's crumb was very tender, which made it difficult to frost; it wound up looking rather lumpy, despite my best efforts. I found the frosting to be too sweet, and I recommend cutting the salt in the frosting by half.

title: Red-Wine Roasted Pears With Cardamom Crumble Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: In the accompanying article, Melissa writes of her friend Bernard, who was so instrumental in her career, but not what became of him.
@BigDnyc, Yes! Exactly my First thought!
She says, "The next September, Bernard enrolled at culinary school, then went on to work for some of the most exciting chefs in the city, including Jean-Georges Vongerichten and David Bouley."
She tells us that he went on to work for important chefs, but it seems like that was in the past. I get a sad, wistful vibe from her reminiscence of him.

I have often served this forgotten dessert. Equally delish baked in cava, with a lighter spice note such as a touch of saffron, or brandy soaked raisins or cherries. Vanilla ice cream or whipped cream. Yum.

I’m a Melissa Clark fan. This recipe and article gave me a great smile. For the last two mornings I’ve indulged in a large hearty bowl of creamy cooked sprouted rolled oats for breakfast. To make it tasty I added a perfectly ripe chopped Bartlett with cardamom, Penzeys cinnamon, Madagascar vanilla bean paste and dash of brown sugar. Yep I think it would be hard to stop eating that crumble! I’d been Looking for something different for a multi day family Thanksgiving get-together and this is it! The hosting mother introduced me to poached pears decades ago. My goto for Christmas is a Fine Cooking cranberry-pear tart with a walnut shortbread crust. Perfect pears are the best!!

Wmall Bosc pears are ideal for roasting. I cut them in half (core with a melon peeler) rather than quarters because they sit better in the pan and are easier to flip. Cadamom pods and/or a bay leaf, along with a vanilla bean and some allspice, are all good, as well as an orange peel. The r pears keep well in the fridge and gain flavor from the poaching liquid. Serve cold or at room temperature. if you're lazy, a sprinkling of granola instead of the crumble works fine.
@Charles Michener let’s say efficient instead of lazy!

I live in Hood River County, one of the leading producers of pears in the US (WA and OR are the top states).  We’re lucky enough (like anywhere near farmers’ markets or agricultural areas) to get a wide variety of products, many of which never make it to commercial markets.  Bosc would be great in this dish.  Bartlett would actually work fine,  If your market is lucky enough to have Concorde pears, they’d work very well for this, given their flavor plus how well they hold their shape when cooking.  I don’t know if any markets would still have Warren pears - they’re quite amazing for both regular eating but also for poaching.  One of our local orchards - Kiyokawa Family Orchards - has good descriptions of pears under their “Farm Stand Availability” link on their website.

I find green pears (d’anjou) to be hit or miss with that weird drying out mouth feel thing they do (yes, those are technical terms). Is that tannins? In any case, what kind of pears do you recommend for this dish and how can I avoid that weird feeling? Thanks!
@AlI   I know exactly what you mean! Bosc pears make the best baking pears in my experience.

@Ali
It’s called astringency and yes, tannins can cause it.

I’m gonna try a savory rendition for my Thanksgiving potluck contribution. I’ll be omitting the cinnamon, and instead of the crumble and ice cream, serve with blue cheese crumbles and toasted pecans or walnuts.
@PNWVanessa I hope you'll come back and tell us how this worked out.  I think your savory idea is really creative.
So excited to try this. I had red wine soaked pears in Lisbon that I will never forget.   Any more narrowed direction on what kind of red wine to use?
Can you use any type of pears for this?
I always use Bosc - interesting that they don't specify here.
Like Kathleen from Austin, I’d like to know how long this keeps and instructions for making ahead, especially if making for Thanksgiving as so many posters indicate. Thank you.
Use Beurre Bosc pears if you can, the ones with the brown skin.  Being cooking pears they have a firm flesh ideal for recipes like this.
Lots of pears out there! Which ones did you use for the recipe? If it’s the best dessert then that is key info.
Can't wait to make this! But would love advice from Melissa on which pears work best and what wines she recommends for this. Thanks!

I love poached pears, and I have always made them in a saucepan.  I can't wait to try these in the oven. They sound delicious!  Although I realise it wouldn't be nearly as good, what would be an alternative to red wine for guests who cannot have alcohol? Thank you.
Blackcurrant juice could work well, it’s rich and dark.

@Emma N. I just made this replacing the wine with pure black cherry juice bought at Whole Foods. Turned out fabulous! I added a squeeze of lemon juice to the liquid before baking in case it needed the acid. Perfect!
Recipe should specify what variety of pear to use. Obvious choice would be a cooking pear, e.g. Gieser Wildeman.
How often does one baste? starting at which point in the cooking process?
I have made similar recipes with a good Sauternes wine instead of red wine. Peaches are delicious as well

For those wondering why MC states no preference for type of pear, any pear is fine for poaching/roasting as long as it’s not too ripe and has no soft spots. I would use Bosc because it’s readily available and not too sweet. I like the texture, too. Generally speaking, I use brown pears for cooking.
Thank you for this recipe! I've had a similar dessert once and have not forgotten how wonderful it was. Thanksgiving maybe? Christmas Eve? Or both!
Can I substitute quinces for pears here?

@E Neiman just yesterday a friend brought me four enormous bumpy quince from her Chicago garden and this morning I see this glorious recipe and I agree with you that it should work nicely with Quince. i’ll try to remember to post when they’re ripe enough for me to make them.

@joan
Yes, this recipe screams for quince.
@joan Do you peel the quince?  I'd love to try this.
I’m curious as to why this is called ‘roasting’. I thought roasting meant cooking with a dry heat, uncovered. Here, it seems more like poaching in the oven.

I obviously did something wrong and would appreciate help figuring out what it was. After it cooled down the crumble became a massive rectangle crunchy cookie ! Tasty but not what I would call a crumble. I thought I followed the recipe to the letter.
@Eri same here, just break it up with a spatula or fork!

Two tips.  Definitely use Bosc pears.   I first tried with very firm Comice pears and ended up after the first 30 minutes in the oven with what might charitably be termed pear mush.  I threw those away, ran to the store, and re-used my wine bath with Bosc.  Second, that lovely red color requires not just a dry red wine, but an intensely red red wine.   A lovely Barolo merely turned the pears grey/pink.  Second round, I enhanced the mix with a California Merlot and got the red stain one wants.

When working in a Realistic et Chateau [property I did both poached pears and poached peaches at different times. Pears were poached in riesling, the world's greatest wine from the world's greatest grape, and garnished with frangiapanne "muffins", peaches were poached in sauterne style late harvest wine garnished with honey tulips.
Both were really good but a bit to unusual for a property in the US.
This is outstanding. I used Concorde Pears and did have to  remove them from the sauce after the first 30-minutes and then reduce the sauce stovetop. They were perfectly done.
I paired the NYT Lemon-Olive Oil ice cream as the creamy component for this dish. DELIGHTFUL!

The pears were delicious.  I used a medium priced pinot noir.  It's important to cook down the wine after removing the pears to create a syrup, and it is likely to take longer than 2 - 5 minutes.  The cardamom crumble DID not taste of cardamom!  I bought a new jar of cardamom just for this receipe, but the flavor did not come through.  If you want to taste cardamom, increase the amount.  1 tsp is NOT enough!
Cardamom crumble and crème fraîche sounds fantastic. My go to was always lovely little Seckel pears with lemon or blood orange sabayon. Time for a change!
Pajarito, which type of Pensey cinnamon?  THey have many, including Vietnamese, China, Indonesa??

Loved Melissa’s origins story and had to make the pears.  I liked them a lot would make a couple of tweaks. Would add some sort of acid…. Lemon zest or squeeze of lemon?  Also I used lemon time but wish I used rosemary as it needed more herbal notes.

I made the rosemary and star anise version. Divine! Fresh goat cheese, whipped with a bit of yoghurt to smooth it out, was an excellent substitute for the suggested toppings - the slight saltiness a tasty contrast to the fruit. And I agree, the crumble is irresistible! I can‘t stop dipping into it.
@CarolaOberursel Love the idea of the goat cheese!
Outstanding! Followed the recipe exactly (except for reduced sugar). Used Bosc pears (locally grown in the Okanagan) and an unusual Australian Shiraz-Viognier blend (“Black Chook”).

I've made a couple of similar desserts from Ottolenghi cookbooks, so I was excited to try this take on cooked/roasted fruit + something creamy + something crunchy/crumbly on top.  For whatever reason, this recipe didn't rock my world :-(. The red wine poaching looks gorgeous, but I prefer the taste profile from using white wine and saffron and adding a bit of vanilla at the end.  If I made this again, I'd add some nuts to the crisp topping, or maybe use almond flour.

Made as written but doubled the recipe for a dinner party of 12. Used Bosc pears and pinot. Made the pears ahead and reheated. Fabulous light dessert, not too sweet but cozy and seasonal. Will make again for sure.
For years I’ve made a similar recipe from Silver Palate that lacks the crumble. I’m eager to try this.
does the alcohol get cooked out? teetotalers in the family.

Yes. After that amount of simmering the alcohol is long gone. But, as an FYI, the non- alcoholic wine selection had grown ever larger and I’m sure your local wine merchant can help you locate a hearty red to try.

@LszapI would simmer the wine mix longer15 minutes to 1/2 hour to make sure the Alcohol is gone. A similar dessert is served in Europe to children. I will do this for mine because alcohol doesn’t mix with my medications.
The pears are only basted in the syrup, not cooked in the liquid?
The pears are cooked in the wine. Step 1 - prepare the wine. Step 2: add the pears to the pan (where the wine is), cover and place in the oven. :)
for the nondrinkers, there are good nonalcoholic wines for sale now.
I don't drink, but I would make this recipe with wine. The cooking deprives the wine of its intoxicating power I think.

@Dorothy Heyl I don’t drink either and agree that cooking cooks off the alcohol, but if you don’t want to have open wine bottles around the house I’ve used a non alcoholic red wine to poach fruit before with success.  Pomegranate or unsweetened tart cherry juice also reduces down nicely. I have a bottled fig balsamic glaze I sometimes drizzle at the end.
Fre makes a decent Cabernet.
My mom used to cook quinces with red wine and there was cinnamon sticks and cloves...Devine! Thank you from bringing back this memory, will remind her too just in case she can dig in the recipe!

La VarenIn the 1980s I had bought La Varenne's cookbook and it had a recipe for pears poached in red wine. It was a huge success. It took long hours to cook and one ingredient was black pepper corns. After many relocation s I have lost the cookbook but that recipe was tremendous.
Would sweet or semi-sweet wine work for this if the amount of sugar is decreased?
I’ve read elsewhere that, yes, reduce the amount of sugar.
Any suggestions for wines fitting the “dry but fruity” description? Would  Pinot Noir be good?

Pears poached with slices of quince in a white wine syrup are wonderful. The quince turns the syrup a lovely persimmon-color. A vanilla bean, cloves also add to this variation. I will make this red wine iteration this weekend though!
I made this once with a 2017 Pinot Noir and the color of the pears was disappointing. I made it tonight with a 2023 red blend and the color was much more vibrant as shown in the photo.

Fabulous, exactly as written.  Used a mix of Bosc and Bartlett pears - the Bartlett were the clear winner.  I had to take the Bosc out of the heat very early as the shrank and got too soft to handle.  The Bartletts held up in the heat per this recipe, and ended up deep red and delicious.  (I didn’t serve the Boscs.)  I filtered the reduced wine sauce, and served with crème fraiche, and the crumble.   Thanksgiving deliciousness!!!!

I just cooked this on the stove top in a Dutch oven, since I was short on oven space. And I swapped the crème fraiche with vanilla ice cream. Even with those shortcuts, everyone raved about this. It’s now my new holiday dessert.
Top with whipped Roquefort. A great surprise!
How long will the pears keep in the fridge?
to make this kid-friendly, what would you suggest we replace the wine with? sparkling apple cider?
The alcohol cooks off.   And besides, the quantity is so small, it's immaterial.  :-)

In the 1980s I had bought La Batenne's cookbook and it had a recipe for pears poached in red wine. It was a huge success. It took long hours to cook and one ingredient was black pepper corns. After many relocation s I have lost the cookbook but that recipe was tremendous.
Why do you put rocks in the oven?
@Healthy and tasty cook. Racks, not rocks.

Question re: Step 5: Is the goal here for a syrupy texture? So if the wine is sweet, but not syrupy, we still reduce it? Maybe describe what we are looking for first, and then how to get there if it's not quite there.
OK, I understand "star anise pod". But what are the "whole cloves" in this recipe. We talking cloves of garlic here, or we talking cloves of clove?
Cloves are a sweet spice often used in combo with cinnamon and nutmeg in desserts, compotes, mulled wine, etc. an are  available in ground or whole form in the spice aisle of any supermarket.
I am making this for thanksgiving dessert. Ive had a practice run and was pleased with the results. How many days in advance can I make this? trying to do as much as possible before the day
Could this be made ahead of time and warmed up at time of serving?

Beautiful presentation and a really fun cook. Made ours using rosemary and cloves along with the cinnamon stick. Used Bosc pears and Merlot. I did halve the pears rather than quarter which were really pretty on the plate, but I can see now that the quarters would likely be red throughout, while the halves had some pale pear flesh in the center. (Halves were tender in the stated cooking time.)

Served with the creme fraiche, but would opt for Greek yogurt next time. Crumble was delish! Will make this again!
Can the pears be made ahead of time and refrigerated for a day?

@cathy mcalpine Yes, I did this when I made this dish last time. Worked perfectly. I just reheated the pears slightly before serving so they wouldn’t be ice cold. Just be careful when handling them if they have become very tender.
Can I make this on stovetop instead? (Oven spave problem - even a day ahead)

In her words:

"The next September, Bernard enrolled at culinary school, then went on to work for some of the most exciting chefs in the city, including Jean-Georges Vongerichten and David Bouley."
@lena kae I am a big fan of Concorde pears, they have so much body and are perfectly firm, also larger than Bosc I think.
Can I substitute stevia for the sugar or is the texture of granulated sugar critical?
I used red bosc pears and they were fine. I tried baking the crumble as she suggests and found it did a lot better at 375 after I finished the pears; at 350 it didn't crisp up even after 20 minutes
I make a similar dish with an inexpensive ruby port - it’s delicious, especially with a little vanilla ice cream.

This was an absolutely delicious and unique dessert, and it made the kitchen smell like a Christmas Market. We skipped the crumble and added cardamon to some fresh whipped cream. Paired perfectly with a Godfather cocktail (Johnnie Walker Black Label and Disaronno) for a special treat.
Made the pears only without the crumble, as written. As someone who doesn't go for heavy and very sweet deserts, this was perfect.
Best crumble ever!

A really nice, easy dessert, but I think the timing is way off on the roasting. I used "firm, but not rock hard" pears and they were very soft after 30 minutes in the oven -- almost too soft. In future, I will start checking at 15 minutes.  Otherwise, excellent.

title: Pasta With White Beans, Olives and Fennel Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody:
I love beans with or as a substitute for pasta. Made this last night with rigatoni but with my own cooked cannellini beans. It was good. 
If I was to use canned beans, before adding to the pasta, I would rinse them well and then simmer in a pan for 10 minutes in a bit of stock or water with some preferred seasoning/herbs to counter the tinny taste canned beans can have.
@Bryan, used your suggestion of simmering the beans.  Thanks.
Step 4 calls for spaghetti, while the ingredient list calls for short pasta. I imagine that either would work fine...

Given that the recipe [optionally] calls for 1/4 cup chopped fresh fennel fronds, which are sold attached to the fennel bulb, would it make sense to substitute the fennel seeds for a bulb of fresh fennel?
@Luke that’s my plan. I’ll report back.
@Luke That’s what i did, starting by sautéing the fennel. The recipe as written is a good pantry recipe. As i was going shopping, thought fresh fennel would be better, and did like it

@Luke That’s what i did and think it made it a bit richer.
 Other additions I’ll consider for next time are anchovies, sun dried tomatoes and preserved lemons, though not all together.
What do I substitute if I don't like fennel?
@Margaret I would say choose another recipe. Melissa has tons of good ones out there
@Margaret thyme or rosemary would work as well.

You could probably take this in a variety of directions. Cumin/coriander/paprika; some sort of premade Italian or Greek or Mediterranean herb mix; thyme & rosemary; maybe sumac & (stirred in later) dried mint; curry powder.
Omitted fennel seed and fronds, added half a fennel bulb, thinly sliced. Bright and fast
Step 4 mentions spaghetti. The ingredient list mentions short pasta.
And the dish ran away with the spoon.
the photo shows short pasta. I am sure it will work with any pasta shape!

What sorts of olives have people tried? Black and green is pretty general and I'm curious what folks have found tasty.

I was thinking oil-cured or "sun dried" as my deli calls them.

@Chris
Chris! I gave incorrect info - the “Marrakesh Spiced Olives” brand is Casablanca Market. Lemons in ingredients but not necessarily preserved. STILL, great green olives…
Will definitely use chopped fresh parsley. It’s probably salty enough, but I might add a few bits of preserved lemon too.

I followed someone's advice to add artichoke hearts - delicious! I needed to add quite a bit of pasta water. The only advice I would give is maybe don't cook as much pasta as the recipe says. I also added lemon juice straight to the pasta - not on the side.
I can't eat these white beans.  Would butter beans work here?

Threw in some kale that needed to be used -- highly recommend! Added unexpected depth.
Reallzed too late no lemon in the house, splash of red wine vinegar did rhe trick.
i love kalamata olives here. I also love oil cured, but may lack the needed flavor.

I crushed the fennel seeds before adding them, and used Medium Shells for the pasta (they pick up the beans!). Just enough fennel flavor! Didn't saute the cannellini beans long enough to get them soft, and this time I will try butter beans. Great reviews for this recipe!

This is so good. I’ve made it several times as written. Last night, inspired by Ali Slagle’s Olive Oil Braised Chickpeas and Broccoli Rabe, I used blanched, chopped broccoli rabe in place of the olives to great effect. I fried the garlic cloves in olive oil first, then added the pepper flakes, fennel seeds and broccoli rabe, followed by the white beans and a cup of pasta water. Smashed some beans as it simmered. Tossed in the pasta, lemon juice and some parm and it was delicious.

Super easy and for a pantry pasta dish, quite tasty! I used basil instead of parsley because that’s the current window plant I have in my kitchen. In addition to using lemon juice as called for, I zested the lemon onto the pasta at the end. Next time I make it I think I’ll bump up the amount of olives. 

Thanks Melissa Clark for an easy recipe to add to the weeknight dinner repertoire!

Ok, I’m Australian, so I did add a couple of things. I basically stayed with the recipe, and it was pretty delicious.
Firstly, I  scorched a handful of cherry tomatoes. In a splash of olive oil. Added an anchovy. And a pinch of chilli flakes. Then followed the recipe from here, olives, garlic fennel seeds, beans, etc. I did add fresh parsley at the end , because that’s what I had in the garden, and thought it to be a good balance. I used pasta shells ,plus a little pasta water, and the beans seemed to be happy hiding inside. 
Very happy, and will definitely be making it again xxx

Absolutely divine, if you love salt. I used oil-cured olives and the punchy, salty tang of the olives plus the fatty salty parmesan and the slightly salty pasta, made a bit heavier and richer with the cannellini beans... delicious.
Made it with fennel bulb (sautéed beforehand) and added some lemon rind at the end. Absolutely scrumptious, bright and zesty. Was also tasty as a cold pasta salad the next day.

For me, the execution of this dish was rough going. With the sizzle of the olives came the splatter of the oil. And the sliced garlic really started to brown and get crispy within seconds of adding it to the pan. Too much pasta water was needed to keep it saucy, which diluted the flavors. 

Recommend using a splatter guard and giving the oil a minute or two to reduce in temp before adding the garlic.

Since there was a fennel bulb attached to the fronds I purchased…..used this along with some gorgeous spring Vidalia onion.  The rest of the recipe was followed more or less, using green olives, and some capers.  We wolfed this down!

This was very tasty, although I'd say the olives rise up as the dominant flavor against the more subdued fennel seed. I think I might give them a light crushing in a mortar and pestle next time to dial them up a bit.

But this is otherwise a really creative way to turn beans into a sauce, and I look forward to riffing on it!
Step 4 mentions tongs which would work with spaghetti, but be awkward with the short pasta  which is specified in the ingredients.
I use tongs to toss all shapes of pasta.
I can't wait to make this for my vegetarian book club group. I may have to test it myself first!
Can anyone recommend a good vegetable to go with? I get so tired of salad.
I want to make this recipe, but why so much salt in the pasta water?
Not very flavorful.  Also needed more beans.
Sautéed the chopped fennel bulb first, then proceeded with the original recipe.  It needed another can of beans which I added.

This was a great pantry pasta. I used canned beans and adopted the recommendation of rinsing, warming the beans with herbs ahead of time.  I warmed them with a few large pinches of orange peel and chicken stock.   I followed the recipe as written from there and added about a cup of shredded, poached chicken to the mix and added pasta water as needed.  I added parsley and half a lemon before serving.  It was very flavorful and silky.  Topped with Grand Padano cheese and pepper flakes.  Will definitely make again.  Thanks NYT
This is my go-to recipe for leftover pasta.
Yummy recipe. I added kale, red peppers, grape tomatoes, and basil for a one-dish dinner.
This was simple and yummy! I found myself wanting maybe one more element. Would be nice with chicken or sausage.
little dry so less pasta or more pasta water.  used black olives but kalamata will give nice flavor for next time.

Basically a pleasant dish of white beans with pasta added. Like others, I gave the fennel seed some love taps with a mortar and pestle before adding (and did not bother with the feenl fronds, which I didn't have). Used green olives and some green (artichoke) orchiette, and added a small chopped tomato that needed to be used. (It blended right in.) Substituted crumbled feta for the parmesan, stirred in off heat at the end.

title: Pasta With Miso, Parmesan and Pickled Peppers Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: This screams for a can of tuna, or sprats, or sardines…

Pasta cooked in heavily salted water overpowers any sauce that dresses it. All of the ingredients for this sauce are salt bombs in themselves, so I would want the pasta only lightly seasoned before I combine it with the sauce. You can always add more of something to lift the sodium level, but you can't reduce it.

Adding salt to a dish on the plate is not the same as properly salting pasta as it cooks. That said, whenever I am planning to add pasta water to a sauce that is already salty, I go easier on the salt in the water. But never without.
perfectly stated@Max Alexander, Rome

You're repeating a popular misconception. Cookbooks teach you to salt water because:
	•	starch absorbs flavor only during gelatinization
	•	once cooked, pasta cannot absorb seasoning
	•	seasoning the pasta itself allows the sauce to taste balanced
	•	the sauce clings better to properly seasoned noodles
	•	the dish becomes coherent, not layered or disjointed

This is a textural and chemical effect, not just a taste issue.

The great schooled chefs I’ve known have always said the water must taste like the sea.  It’s an Italian saying that is a figure of speech.  It’s relating that you should salt the water considerably more than you would think and not to be taken literally.
Japanese pickled ginger would work as well. The bright red version in long thin slices.

Pasta water should never be salted to "taste as salty as the sea." Seawater generally has a salinity of about 3.5% (35 grams of salt per liter). Typical pasta water should be salted at 1 to 2 tablespoons of salt per gallon (3.8 liters). 7 to 8 grams per liter is a good middle ground.
​This recipe has considerable salt content from the miso, soy sauce, Parmesan, and pickled peppers. For that reason, pull way back on the standard pasta water salt ratio and lightly salt the water.
@Luke very little of the salt ends up in the food.  Yes, you can adjust down for a dish full of other salty ingredients, but ‘as salty as the sea’ doesn’t create oversalted pasta.
@Luke It’s a figure of speech.
Excellent. No notes. ;-)
traditionally, miso is not boiled as it then loses all of the nutritional value. best to mix it in at the very end unless one's goal is simply flavor.
@A in a pasta dish with half a stick of butter, I’m going to say the miso is for flavor
Since I'm diabetic, "midnight" pasta is out of the question. BUT I've enjoyed variations of these for breakfast with an egg. Then I walk for an hour. Just as tasty.
@KateRuns i agree. An egg yoke would better add creaminess than the cheese. Also with less saltiness.
Sooo easy - fabulous flavor, especially for so few ingredients. Thank you!
Veganized by skipping the butter (a little more olive oil) and swapped the parm for 2tbs nutritional yeast. Delish and super quick! Thanks Melissa!!!
Really quick, simple, and excellent. I didn't have butter - although I will next time - and so used olive oil. Next time I'll double or + ½ the peppers (I used a cherry pepper I pickled myself).
I've never salted pasta water since my mother always over-salted it. It tastes just fine; your sauces, flavorings usually add plenty and you can always add salt.

The author of Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat would disagree-- as well as any professional chef. Do you salt bread? Rice? The vast majority of pasta water, along with its salt content, goes down the drain. Adding salt at the serving stage can never make up for omitted salt in the making stage.
Besides, if your father always burned things in the oven, would you forgo ovens? Or just cook things the right amount?

A lovely premise -- the night-time pasta raid -- and as almost always with Melissa Clark's recipes, it's tight and effective, and sensitive to the variations in our pantries. Worked well for me! And the retained pasta water advice is so useful.
YEAH!!!!!!!!! Didn’t do pickles because I don’t like them, doubled the garlic, and used pecorino instead of parm. Awesome recipe.
The reserved 1/2 c pasta water is used in step 2. More should be saved if needed in step 3.
Without pickled peppers I used chopped up kimchi at the bottom of the plate.

A fast and easy pantry meal that packs good flavor. Added frozen spinach and a sunny side up egg for more nutrition, garnished with scallions, used pepperoncini. Didn't salt the water of my soba noodles and it came out almost too salty.
Is there a difference between garlic pressed in a garlic press and grated garlic?  I don’t want to grate garlic—I have a great press!

Made exactly as directed and it turned out delicious. It was a flavor bomb. My husband was skeptical when I told him the name of the dish but he had seconds and said it was very tasty. I really liked the acid hit of the chopped pickled pepperoncini. It came together quickly as well. Funny all the discussions about to salt or not to salt. I've made pasta without salt before (accidentally), and it was bland, bland, bland, even after trying to compensate by adding salt at the end.
This is fabulous!  We just made it tonight. We did use less pasta for the two of us (4 oz total) but kept the sauce the same (just olive oil instead of butter. ). Tossed some parsley on it. Divine.
Holy salt bomb! That was the saltiest thing I've ever eaten, and I followed the directions to a "T". There has to be a mistake?
Made exactly as written using jalapeños.  It was good but nothing to write home about like the raves on here.  Super easy tho
I'm thinking a jarred roasted red pepper would work instead of jalapeño?
can I substitute red miso?

@Aja Tachai
Pressed garlic should work well in this dish.

Thanks Melissa.  Delicious with so few ingredients but complaints of being too salty.  Reduce the salt in boiling the pasta and all fine.  This will be added to my weekday list since it is a quick meal.
Delicious! I am not afraid of salt but I didn’t add the soy sauce and found it plenty salty. Super yummy.

This is almost stupidly delicious. I only had 2 tbs of butter, so I added some olive oil. I tried it in its own and WOW! But I’d read some of the other comments and decided on adding a small can of oil-packed tuna. This is a great pantry dish that I’ll be making again and again.
This is a dish I would pay 34 bucks for in a restaurant and not blink an eye. Holy cow. If you want to really level it up, add shrimp sautéed in butter.
Made this tonight. Did not change anything and it was awesome and easy. I used medium heat pepperoncini, although I had some skepticism. It was the bomb!

This was almost inedibly salty (and I alike my food salty). I think the problem is that the salty pasta water is added to the dish (yes, I know it’s also for the starch) - so the comments here that the salt in the pasta water doesn’t affect the dish are incorrect. 
Otherwise it’s delicious: quick and satisfying. I thought I’ll leave out the soy sauce next time. Maybe add olives instead of the peppers.
This was amazing!
This looks like a grown-up version to justify still eating buttered noodles and cheese. I am in!
Loved the combination with tuna fish. I did NOT make the pasta water very salty. With the rest of the ingredients, it was plenty salty for me. Quick easy dish!
This is excellent exactly as written (okay, maybe a few extra peperoncini). All the decadence of mac and cheese but way more tasty!

Oh my. This, for my household, is pantry pasta perfection. Made exactly as written, though my husband chopped up extra Pepperoncinis to top his bowl and I will too next round. And there will be many, many next rounds!

I’ve been making a version of this for a while now. I add mushrooms, usually shiitakes but any will do, in with the sauté.

I call it Umami Bomb Pasta (pr. Oooh mami). 

Creamy, flavorful, comforting. Easy to throw together. Winner.

Fantastic recipe, and easy to riff on. I used fusilli and added well-browned ground pork and snowpea leaves (towards the end) to round it out. Definitely err on the side of undersalting the pasta water - between the miso, parm, and soy sauce, there’s a lot of salt going on here as is.
The only pepper I had was a small can of chilpotle peppers in adobo sauce. I rinsed the peppers off the sauce, chopped them and mixed them with the pasta. Very good.
Added a batch of roasted cauliflower at the end and more parm. Licked the spoon.
Very nice. I don’t think this needs the garlic. Controversial?! Next time I’ll leave it out and see.

You can not do the second half of this recipe ‘while the pasta is cooking’. A major flaw in the directions. 
You need to mix in pasta water and the pasta, so can not do this ‘while the pasta is cooking. 
Cook pasta to al dents and then proceed with the next steps in the recipe.
Delicious! Made with thin wheat spaghetti.

Wow amazing! Adding to the rotation. Had some lovely mandarin and pink peppercorn pickled peppers from Brooklyn-based Dilli by Lilli. I only lightly salted the water as I had a nearly inedible salty run-in with another miso pasta recipe (which could very well have to do with the sodium level of the miso I buy). Going to reduce butter by 1-2 Tbsp next go round. It’s not too rich but the butter taste dominates slightly more than I like and I think the texture will be fine with the reduction.
Perfect pantry pasta - wouldn't change a thing. Although, come to think of it I only had 2 T of butter so subbed in 2 of olive oil. Still perfect.
Delicious and sooo pantry-friendly! My mom asked me when I could make it again ❤️
This is  an outstanding pasta dish
Use 1/2 the miso, lightly salt pasta water
I would appreciate a much greater focus on low sodium--or, better, very low sodium-recipes.
@David Kehler So you click on a recipe with Miso and cheese in it and then complain about sodium?
This recipe is amazing but i suggest doubling every ingredient- perfection!
Fantastic and so easy!
Mushrooms and/or chopped canned artichokes make a wonderful additional to this already fabulous recipe.
Loved this dish though I did start with sautéed mushrooms and leeks because I happened to have them. Did everything else as written. Delicious and will become a regular in my house
This was fantastic. We didn't have a spicy pickle that made sense so I added chili crisp and a splash of lemon and it was wonderful.
For those wondering, I found capers to be an acceptable substitute. I reduced some of the salt in the water.

This has been in my recipe box forever. Well, I’ve made this twice(!) this week—once for a lazy dinner and this morning after a long night out (always a fan of pasta or rice when slightly hungover). It is delicious. The first time I made it, it was a little too salty for my liking, so I salted the water less. I also liked a bit more peppers than what was called for. 

So easy. Try it.

title: Pasta With Lemon, Capers and Anchovies Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: I make something very similar. I add a little chicken stock to the pan with the anchovies and capers, and sometimes I like to throw in some quartered artichoke hearts. It's a delicious, quick meal that's fancy enough for company.

This is very similar to a Melissa Clark recipe that I adore and make on  regular rotation. She uses boneless chicken thighs and keeps her garlic crushed so one has to be careful not to over cook the  garlic slices in this recipe. To mix it in to pasta with lemon wedges sounds so  good I cannot wait to  make this one without the chicken.

We always make EXTRAS of that Lemon-Garlic-Anchovy Chicken...and then, another night, cut up the leftover chicken, amplify the sauce + serve it all over pasta. So, we've tasted the sauce without chicken...and find it far more multidimensional + deeper flavored WITH the chicken juices. Also, that recipe includes plenty of squeezed lemon juice, plus a lot more capers, anchovies, garlic et al. And we, I think, improve it further by adding lemon zest + deglazing the pan w/white wine. Buon appetito!

Nothing about this recipe really worked.  It is much too salty.  Rinsing the capers and soaking anchovies in water for a few minutes might help.  The lemons were not right.  I used nice organic lemons and got them to caramelize but not enough to make them sweet, as referenced in the recipe.  I don’t think I’ll try this one again!

@Kris M.
I quite agree with you. Unless you are an experienced cook you would not know to rinse the anchovies in water, towel dry and throw them into a pan of butter and olive oil. Let them sizzle and then add the lemon slices. That’s the path to getting things caramelized. She’s a good writer and she knows how to hook you into her recipe with her crisp but friendly introduction, but her recipes are not put through a rigorous testing. Then again, that’s just me.
I agree.  This recipe did not really work for me.  I thought the lemon rind was bitter even after cutting it very, very thin; blanching it; and caramelizing it.  To me the flavor was just not there.
@Alice Pope Oops, made this last night and you’re right, I did NOT know this 😅 Thanks for the tip - next time!

The anchovies basically dissolve into the sauce. You don’t really chew on them. Cutting them up speeds the process. 

Tuna can not replace the anchovies but sounds good to add as a protein. I put lemon juice in my tuns salad.

I just wanted to let you know, Melissa Clark, that I made this for a significant dinner, but with Chicken. Pasta was a filler dish. There were no leftovers to be had. This is an always winner-dinner in our house. Thank you!
@Susannah Allanic do you make it without anchovies?
@Carla B: Melissa Clark - We want to make with preserved lemons. I’m assuming just rinsing the anchovies to remove some salt would do it, plus adding a squirt of lemon if needed.

This was fantastic!  I made the lemon exactly as the recipe called for… follow it carefully.  Slice lengthwise, remove all seeds, boil in heavily salted boiling water, cut into small bits, sizzle in olive oil until carmelized. I needed to use up mushrooms, so added those to the sauce once lemon and garlic were brown, and rotisserie chicken for protein.  This was so good I made the sauce a second time to add to my prepared dish!  I needed to use up Farfalle pasta, and that turned out great.  The Italian parsley didn’t add much; next time I’ll use arugula.
Has anyone tried this with preserved lemon?
@Edith Not yet, but I love your thought and plan to try it!

I'm sure it will taste great, I love of all of these: anchovies, lemons, capers. But I wonder: where are the veggies? Are you supposed to make a big accompanying salad to make up for the lack of vegetables? To me, dinners like these just don't conform to the recommended daily intake of colour, fiber, vegetables. I would feel I had to compensate the next day. Am I an outlier or do I miss something?
@Ivy de Pauw I had the same thought and added a bunch of baby spinach to round it out. Next time I will add tuna in oil.

@Edith
Preserved lemons work great in this.
Is adding mushrooms to this a good addition?
@brian allman Yes!!!  I did that tonight and it was chef’s kiss!!!

@Carla
"Trim" probably means to cut off the stem end.

I quite agree with you. Unless you are an experienced cook you would not know to rinse the anchovies in water, towel dry and throw them into a pan of butter and olive oil. Let them sizzle and then add the lemon slices. That’s the path to getting things caramelized. She’s a good writer and she knows how to hook you into her recipe with her crisp but friendly introduction, but her recipes are not put through a rigorous testing. Then again, that’s just me.
Can you substitute tuna for the anchovies?

I've been doing a version of this for over 20 years – since I used to cook for me and my young daughter when my then-wife was at Monday night tennis. Penne, poached and seasoned chicken breast, olive oil and parmesan cheese. I called it "Lock the Door" chicken, joking that it was so good the neighbors might barge in. Now grown and moved away, my daughter still asks for LTD chicken and my partner enjoys it as a simple staple, too. Except now I might zhush it w/chopped tomatoes, fresh herbs, etc.
maybe miso? oil cured olives? think umami. @Linda

I think it's important to underline that the capers should be capers preserved in salt and not in vinegar.
Tastes so much better!
I keep reading the recipe and the comments and still see no chicken in the recipe or ingredients list, just in the comments. Where's the chicken?
@Bonnie I think they’re referring to the Garlicky Chicken with Lemon-Anchovy Sauce recipe here. It is delicious.
might be good to add one can of tuna at the end

PS: Here's that other Melissa Clark recipe—similar, but even better, for lemon-garlic-caper-and-anchovy chicken: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015290-garlicky-chicken-with-lemon-anchovy-sauce As I say, we've been making extras for years, then cutting the leftover chicken into ~3/4" squares, amplifying the sauce, heating everything + serving it over pasta. From the outset, we modify the recipe by increasing the capers + anchovies, adding lemon zest + deglazing the pan w/white wine.

This dish is delicious! I did wash the capers and anchovies to reduce salt. I also used a home made very rich chicken stock.  I used half parsley and half tarragon for the fresh herbs. Very very yummy!
What does “lemon, trimmed” mean?  I’ve done a lot of cooking over the years and this is the first time I’ve run across this terminology. (AI also is unaware of what this means!)
@Carla I did not see this verbiage in the instructions.
Thoughts on using broccoli rabe for the greens? All I have on hand at the moment.
This recipe didn’t work for me. It seemed perfect but it is super salty and super bitter. I couldn’t eat more than two bites. ☹️☹️☹️☹️

@Ivy de Pauw
I'm going to try adding white beans.
What is meant by "trimmed" in regards to the lemon slices?

@Greg Same. Am I peeling the rind off? Was ready to make this recipe and had everything out but this one ambiguous piece of information has me hesitant to proceed. Maybe I’ll just make something else….

Lots of comments that the dish is bitter. Why not just add squeezes of lemon juice to taste at end while emulsifying with the pasta water?
I feel like the extra steps Roth the lemon are just to be different and are hard to execute. You need a very thin rinsed lemon to start.
Payoff not worth it - a squeeze and some zest - done.
Love the recipe, but if you're watching your sodium intake, that may well be over the red-line!

Paula,
That thighs recipe that you mention from Melissa Clark is among my favorites. I've made it many times. And I ALWAYS burn my hand when I take it out of the oven!
If I really can’t do anchovies… suggestions to still get the flavor?  Or am I out of luck?

@Linda these are also anchovy - but maybe these will work for you? A smidge of fish sauce, or tubed anchovy paste. But if you have tomavoid anything anchovy, I’m thinking sardines. Then adding other umami boosting items like mushrooms, tomato paste (be sure to caramelize it), soy or tamari sauce. Or go in a different direction as others have and use chicken. Also, someone mentioned using preserved lemons - that’s where I’d go with this. They are so lovely added to a dish, and are never bitter.
Good way to use some leftover spinach too.
This was simply awful. I love lemon pasta but this just didn’t work. The lemon was bitter and the whole dish was too strong.

I like anchovies, but am still looking for the optimal tinned brand.   When in doubt, I like to cut anchovies up small, and fry them first in the olive oil until they dissolve.  This seems to bring out the flavor and minimize fishiness.   I think I shall use 4 fillets.

In a couple months, it will be lemon season in Arizona.   I will just grab a couple off the tree, and do this.I guess your choice of herb would depend on tastes.   Arugula the bitter would not be bad, especially with lemon wedges
I really don’t like capers is there a good alternative that could be used in this dish?
Can I use anchovy paste instead of whole anchovies?
My family does *not* enjoy eating unpeeled lemon slices, so I would remove them before serving next time. Otherwise, pleasant flavors.
Made an exactly as-is. Delicious. My currently tomato-averse middle schooler loved it. Since I’m pescatarian, I accompanied it with a side salad chock full of chickpeas for a protein boost.

@David M
Olives would be a good substitute for the capers, unless you dislike anything that's brined.

I made this tonight exactly as written (no chicken, artichokes, chicken stock etc) exactly as written and it was delicious - I had all of the ingredients in my pantry- a quick weeknight pasta dish that tastes like a weekend indulgence!
This was SUPER bitter- I don’t recommend

A little salty, but delicious! I couldn't resist adding a small tablespoon of pickled green peppercorns instead of the red pepper. From the suggested herbs, I chose flat-leaf parsley, as it does not dominate or overpower the flavour of the other ingredients.

This was delicious! I didn't have long noodles, so I used what I had, casarecce (what a great pasta! Where has this been all my life?). I also didn't have fresh herbs so threw on some dry while adding in the pasta water. Toasted some pine nuts, because why not, and sprinkled a little grated Romano on top because again, why not? The one thing I would do differently is give the lemons a longer time in the skillet.

This is so delicious and easy. I added two roughly chopped preserved lemons, grilled green olives from Trader Joes (if you haven't tried them - they are to die for!) and cut up all the boiled lemon in chunks and left it in the pasta. I also threw in all the herbs left in my fridge which was about 1 cup full after finely cutting them up in my food processor.
My guests and I did not really care for this pasta recipe.  Better luck next time.

I’m not the biggest fan of lemon and I found it gave the dish an unpleasantly bitter taste. 
I have better recipes for this pasta. Won’t make this one again.
Has anyone tried this with preserved lemon instead of the fresh, blanched lemon? Just curious if that would work as a shortcut...
I love this kind of dish, one that calls for lemon, with the Meyer lemons my friend grows. That mellow lemon quality transforms ordinary lemon dishes into something quite sublime.

I liked the resulting flavor (hard to go wrong with lemon, anchovies & capers!) although I need to add more salt to the water next time. But the lemon rind was still quite bitter even after blanching and caramelizing. How to fix that?

This was on the bland side.  I added more anchovies, uncooked, at the end.  If I'd had  more capers I would have added them too.  Oil on medium high blackened my lemon slices, but Melissa didn't say how thin to slice them, so I went on the very thin side, and they were Meyer lemons from my own tree, so a thin skin. I'll try this again.  

Molly Baz was the first one to come up with the idea of adding a dump-truck load of salt to pasta water.  I don't care for the resulting salty pasta flavor.
Great dish. I am using preserved lemons and it is amazing
I used Meyer lemons - I believe it made a difference, as they are "sweeter" than plain big lemons.  I added homemade chicken broth to mine, too, and it was incredible!
I really enjoyed this recipe…. I used a whole lemon, I doubled the amount of greens (arugula and spinach combo) and I added extra anchovies because we enjoy strong flavours. It was delicious!

Made this mostly as written, using fresh fettucine and dill. It has decent flavor, but I'm not sure the lemons boiled long enough or that the mixture really caramelized. Might keep this for some future ideas, but not 100% sold on the concept/execution.
I followed the recipe as described and wow! Wow! And ReWow! I used good quality anchovies in a jar. This was bright and delicious. Next time I will try to add fresh spinach. Easy with divine results!
Wow I loved this.  I think very thin lemon slices are important to avoid bitterness from larger pieces of lemon rind.  Agree with good quality anchovies.  So easy and delicious!

This was not particularly difficult, but it was too much effort for what amounted to some mid pasta. Make pasta; heat the anchovies in the olive oil, add garlic and red pepper flakes and cook until garlic is golden; toss with pasta. Throw on some capers and squeeze some lemon on it. Same effect for less effort than fussing with the lemons, which contribute nothing to the dish other than more work.

Tasty, but I think I’ll blanch the lemons a little shorter next time. Not enough of a lemony taste…maybe I’ll add some fresh juice? I did garnish with lemon zest. I also added shrimp and asparagus and it was a nice springy combo.

title: Sheet-Pan Ratatouille With Crispy Chickpeas Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: When it seems too tedious to cut lots of cherry tomatoes in half, I poke them with a paring knife or a toothpick. It’s much faster and it keeps them from exploding. They collapse nicely.

I put the tomatos between two plastic lids, hold the top while I slice through the middle with a serrated knife. All tomatoes are cut in no time. Tip from America's test kitchen. Your tip also sounds easy and delicious!
I crumbled some feta over the veggies for the last 15 minutes for a bit more protein—delicious!

Am I the only person in the world who despises burst cherry tomatoes? They add color to a dish but also unappealing globs of bitter seeds, and piles of nasty skin that diners shove to the side of their plate. For the tomato flavor that is essential to ratatouille, add real tomato, whether fresh or canned, that will meld into the mushy vegetables. I adore Melissa Clark and Cooking, but, honestly, it's time to get off the supermarket cherry tomato bandwagon.

@Janet I LOVE cherry tomatoes!  Roasted, burst, or just grabbing a few for a sweet treat (it IS a fruit, after all).  I’ve become quite picky about my cherry tomatoes, though, and go for the sweetest ones/ brands I can find.  I don’t find the seeds bitter at all, and enjoy the texture of the skins.
@Margaret T.  To each his/her own Margaret!  I happen to find cherry tomatoes just fine when fresh, but not so much when they are cooked.
Who are these diners? I've never seen anyone push a tomato skin to the side.
Been doing roasted rat for years. Lots of oil. Single sheet pain. No need for all this fussiness
Looking forward to trying this.  I have also moved from stovetop to oven for ratatouille.  Also - adding a few cloves gives a great additional flavour!
@Paul Shaviv garlic is a basic food group!  I often add an entire head of garlic to my ratatouille, which I still prefer cooking on the stove top in my Le Creuset  Dutch oven.
@Julie He's talking about cloves (the spice).

I've made a similar recipe and it really is easy but added mushrooms, cooking it all in the air fryer of my Breville air fryer toaster oven. The other recipe called for a small amount of white wine and balsamic vinegar tossed in, which added a lot of flavor. The crispy garbanzos sound like a tasty addition.
Going to try this, but I'm wondering if the cumin seeds are weird at all, since cumin isn't a usual spice for ratatouille?
@Kristicumin seeds taste very different to ground. So no it won’t taste like Indian food. Try it!
@Kristi The cumin seeds are for the crispy chickpeas.
@Kristi agree, never seen cumin in a ratatouille recipe.  Always see capers, but this recipe has none?

I roughly cut this in half for just two of us.  Used one can of chickpeas which I roasted in the same pan as the veggies.  I roasted everything for roughly 30 minutes, then added the tomatoes, onions and spinach for another 10.  That was plenty of roast time.  Chickpeas were crisp and the veggies weren’t mush.  We loved it.
Tasty, but TOO MANY CHICK PEAS. One can is plenty for this recipe.
We thought this was really delicious! I think 1 can of chickpeas would be plenty for the amount of vegetables. It is very filling. Yummy!
Would love a low fat version of this recipe. I need to up my vegan protein intake and greatly reduce my fat intake.

@GT I find many recipes don’t need as much oil as directed. One idea is to put the veggies in a bowl and drizzle with about 2 tablespoons of oil. Really give them a good, long but gentle stir to barely coat them. If there’s oil left in the bottom of your bowl, put the chickpeas in to use it up, adding more just enough to coat. I only used one can of chickpeas so that reduced the oil needed. If you have air fryer you can reduce the oil when roasting the chickpeas even more! For this recipe I used 1/4 cup garlic-flavored EVOO which cuts the fat in half (hubby and I love garlic on veggies!).

I have lots of regular garden tomatoes, but not cherry tomatoes. If I cut up and use the regular tomatoes, would I lengthen their cooking time, to account for their extra juiciness? How can I make this recipe work with larger tomatoes?
@Susie S cut in half and squeeze them over the sink.  Water and seeds are removed, and flesh remains. Learned from a chef and always do it in recipes like this.

Followed the recipe closely except I skipped the cumin in the chickpeas and served the whole pile of roasted vegetables over pasta.  I reserved the chickpeas can liquid, added pasta water and a little chicken broth to each serving. Fresh parm ribbons to top it off. It was fabulous.

Rather than chickpeas, I use TVP. It is faster and easier than roasting chickpea, nutritious, and soaks up the flavors of the other ingredients. After rehydrating, just mix it into the dish with all the other ingredients.

I was underwhelmed by this. I skipped the chickpeas since I wasn’t looking for a full meal. Flavors seemed muted, and I missed the slightly soupy texture of my stovetop version. And it didn’t seem any easier than doing it on stovetop. I will stick with Mireille Johnstown’s recipe in her Cuisine of the Sun.
@Sonia most of the flavor/spices are on the chickpeas

This was a really delicious ratatouille, and while it did require some attention, to periodically stir and keep an eye on it, it was a lot less labor-intensive than standing over a pot. I used chunks of fresh tomato, it came out great. One thing, I did let the drained chickpeas dry for a long while on a dishtowel before roasting, but they never crisped. They shrank and got a lot denser, but you wouldn't call them crispy. Very tasty though. By popular request, I didn't mix them in.

Just cooked this. It tasted incredible. The veggies blend together very well and basil adds a delightful freshness to the dish. Chickpeas are crispy on the outside and soft on the inside. First time adding fennel seeds to my chickpeas and they worked great.
One thing I recommend is peeling the eggplant. I found that in some cases the skin burnt and became inedible. Other than that, the dish was stunning.

Made this exactly as written and the vegetables melted together, just pure mush, completely inedible. Scrambling to come up with a second dinner on the fly on a Monday night - will not try to make again.
The whole point of ratatouille is indeed to have the vegetables melt together.
Sounds good, but why not roast everything at once for 40 minutes?

@Laurie Zucker-Conde

It seems like it’s so the chickpeas to get crispy and don’t steam in the middle of the vegetables. @melissaclark Wanna weigh in on this?

From the related article: "And one last crucial strategy is adding the tomatoes partway through roasting. This gives them plenty of time to burst and release their sweet juice, but not enough to evaporate it."

@Pandora Yes! I didn’t know that you could roast canned chickpeas, but just had them for the first time a couple days ago at a pub, sprinkled on top of a salad. They were like chickpea-shaped oyster crackers, the texture was that transformed. I’m sure that’s why they must be roasted separately, and is the explanation for the “fussiness” complained about elsewhere.
It'd be helpful to know the quantity and size of the zucchini and eggplant, rather than just cup and size measurements. I'm guessing three zucchini, one medium eggplant...?

@Lydia just get the vegetables weighed ~ go a bit over the recipe to account for trimming ends, etc.

Just weigh them at the produce stand/area. I’d get one pounders to make up for trimming ends, etc. The recipe calls for about 3/4’s of a pound for each, zucchini & eggplant.
@Mellie B I don’t think it matters. It is peasant dish…whatever is available
I just cut up veges until I have the pan full or two pans if I'm energetic.

Been doing this for years, its one of our main cooking methods, and there are ALWAYS leftovers in the fridge. We have a garden and we use whatever veges from it and the store that make sense at the time so its always changing. The leftovers are used for a multitude of other recipes.  From tacos to soup.  This is especially a good method for processing a whole LOT of veges.  We usually do two large trays at a time.  Whole garlic bulbs for later use, etc......

This is fantastic! Faster: I did it all using the "air fry" setting on my regular oven & I had dinner ready, including all chopping/prep, in just under an hour! Same temp (425) on "air-fry mode", drained the beans & dried them naked in the oven for 10 minutes. Chopped everything (smaller, 1/2"?) during the 10. Veggies prepped as instructed, in the oven for 10 (beans out). Seasoned/oiled beans. After the next 10, added the tomatoes/onions as instructed. Both pans back in for 10-15. Ta-da! Yum!

Delicious but too sludgy for me. I thought the vegetables were done just right after about 30 minutes at 425. Next time I would add the tomatoes and onion sooner and cook the whole thing just another 20 minutes, turning the oven down maybe only 25 degrees. Too much oil on the chickpeas too (and I had already cut back on it). But the flavors were wonderful. Will definitely make again with these changes.

The ratatouille is just cool enough to eat, and I’ve already consumed a third of it!  I followed the recipe exactly, it turned out perfectly, and it is absolutely delicious.  The cumin/fennel spicing is a bit unusual, and it is wonderful.  I will be making it again!  Thank you, Melissa.
The flavor profile is divine in this dish. The chickpea base was perfect contrast to the jammy vegetable mixture.  Definitely a keeper.
This was delicious. I added some cubed halumi to the roasting chickpeas to add a little more salty goodness. The basil at the end was a great touch!

Pleasant, but I was underwhelmed. I liked the crispy chickpeas but overall it was a dish that was basically softened roast veggies that were a little dry and, as another reviewer noted, the flavours seemed muted. We make another ratatouille where the roasted veggies are further cooked with tomato sauce after roasting. It's much more of a sauce and is lovely over slices of polenta. I would have like more sauce in this recipe.

Made this with a lot of variations based on what I had in the fridge. Used canned tomatoes. It was so friggin good! I think what made it so delicious was letting the veggies and the chickpeas roast on their own before adding the tomatoes which would have made it soupy. Used the spices it calls for but larger amounts.
The picture doesn't reflect the 2-pan instructions at all. I made exactly as directed and half of the veggies on the bottom rack were scorched past usability. The remaining veg was good though.

Wow, this blew me and my husband away! It took a while but was worth it. I used a big bowl to toss each ‘wave’: first, toss the eggplant/zucchini/garlic/pepper with the olive oil and salt and dump onto your pan. While that’s roasting, add the tomatoes/onion to the same bowl with a drizzle more oil, then add to your pan. Then, add the chickpeas to the same bowl to toss with the oil and spices.

Turned out great—the cumin was a delicious, unexpected dimension in ratatouille. However, the veggies cooked way faster than the times Melissa gives. Glad I checked to avoid burning.
Eggplant and zucchini doesn’t take long to brown at 425. Served with crisped halloumi; feta would be welcome too.

Cherry tomatoes are among my favorite foods. I have to buy an extra package to snack on while cooking to make sure that any make it into the recipe! I highly recommend the Trader Joe's cherry tomatoes on the vine, which have an excellent texture and flavor.

This is a very flavorful recipe but takes too much time roasting.  I roasted the chickpeas but preferred snacking on them rather than adding them to the mix.  I use the ratatouille as a topper for a baked potato with Mexican crema as an added topping.  delicious.
Please add size of sheet pan to be used!
The timing of the various elements is confusing. It's in the oven, and I'm hoping for the best.

Made this with garden produce, including thin-skin cherry tomatoes and served with polenta made in an Instant Pot. Will make again for sure. However, will probably just top with (noncrispy) white beans. I've tried a few Times recipes that include cooking chickpeas until crispy, and I can't figure out if they're supposed to be crispy like soynuts, crunchy wasabi peas, and Love Corn, which are snacks I adore. The crispy chickpeas these recipes make are chewy and dried up in the center.

A rare time - basically followed the recipe. Prepped everything in advance - but put in a bowl to add olive oil / mix/ prior to sheet pan. Did everything in sequence, for the right amounts of time except forgot to turn the oven down at the end. I'm not a huge fan of ratatouille and thought this was great. Chickpeas add a lot. Re- cutting cherry tomatoes, I halved mine. While this was baking, I brought in the irrigation hoses - and made fixes on an art project- this recipe gave the gift of time.
Utterly delicious! But, the proportion of veg to chickpea seems out of whack. Three (just about) servings of the veg, but at least 5 of chickpeas! Well, at least the chickpeas will make a nice snack.
This was really scrummy. Used extra red peppers and sprinkled plated dish with feta and pepita. Yum!

I love ratatouille, and this is a nice recipe—but it really doesn’t save any time over the traditional Julia Child stove top version. To minimize water seepage that dilutes flavor, I always salt eggplant and zucchini and let them drain for 30 minutes while prepping other vegetables. found that the toasting of chickpeas left them crispy but hollow, so even thought they may have added protein for vegans, they didn’t feel they add much to the flavor.
