The real time saving is in fewer pans to wash. That said, the ratatouille itself was delicious—a wonderful recipe for fresh produce from local farmers’ markets.

Absolutely wonderful.  I did not worry about the exact proportions of eggplant and zucchini.  And I forgot the large red pepper I selected at the store and just sliced rings from those mini-peppers I had around and that you see everywhere.

Will make it again.
Excellent result. Added some feta cheese as suggetsed by others. Perhaps the best ratatouille I have made.  Will definately make again.
Served over creamy polenta. Delicious!

This is like an elevated fried rice. The raves are right on. It’s totally yum. And totally takes at minimum twice the time. And worth it. I subbed farro for rice which was delicious and was great for surgery recovery of my spouse who is supposed be eating high protein. Yay.

You can pop your chickpeas into the air fryer with a minimum of oil and get delicious crispy results. I think the recipe I used (it was a while ago) had putting the chickpeas (canned drained) into the air fryer for a few minutes to dry out and then removed, seasoned and put back in to crisp. I’ve also read recipes that say to wait until after the chickpeas are roasted to season them. Anyone have experience or comments about this?
Super tasty! I would have added the onions 10-15 minutes earlier as all the other vegetables were thoroughly roasted except for them.

We loved this with following changes:  only had one can of Garbonzo beans which was plenty.  Accidentally added all spices to the halved grape tomatoes, so I also seasoned the garbonzos (using half since only one can) and the dish did not taste over seasoned.  I only had dried thyme and rosemary, had to sub ground cumin for whole, used smoked paprika and a dash of cayenne.  The zuccini and eggplant cooked way down so I’d use more of both next time.  Didn’t realize the amount of cooking time I’d need, so speeded it up a bit using the convection bake setting on my oven and adjusting temperatures down 25 degrees.  Topped with coarsely chopped Italian parsley and lemon wedges.  Loved the new taste and texture of roasted ratatouille!
Followed recipe exactly. Loved it. Will make it again.
The roasted vegetables were delicious. Forget the chickpeas, they were dry. Make basmati rice instead.

I followed the recipe, and we loved the way the vegetables browned and melted into one another.  I happened to have fennel stems and fronds (rather than seeds), and I added the former to the chickpea pan, along with cumin and smoked paprika, and the latter to the finished dish.
The chickpeas with double cumin are fire, but the rest was just fine.
horrible... veggies are soggy...and nobody wants soggy veggies.

Started the chickpeas in the oven while it was preheating. While preheating, I chopped zucchini+eggplant+bell pepp. Veggies went into a separate pan in the oven. I added the garlic when I gave the both sheet pans a toss. While the veggies were roasting, I chopped the onions and tomatoes. I tossed the tomatoes with balsamic, salt, pepp, and olive oil before adding them, and I pulled the chickpeas out of the oven to rest. Pretty easy and yummy.

Lots of discussion here about the tomatoes. To cut or not. To let them burst or not. Aversion to skin in cooked tomatoes.   Here's another suggestion: have the little tomatoes, don't put them in the oven at all. Toss the halves with the other vegetables when they're done.  The heat will warm the tomatoes, intensifying their flavor and they'll stay intact.  I often do that with pasta.

This is a great recipe. I browned some fresh shiitakes at the beginning of Step 3 and added a sheet of kombu and a couple of dried shiitakes to the broth that I found when looking for my oyster sauce just for a little boost of umami. 

Also (and this may be a personal preference), if you are planning on having leftovers, I would only add the amount of bok choy you plan on eating that night. It's easy enough to add more when you are reheating and it doesn't break down as much.

All of the ingredients are readily available in any Asian food market. This is super-easy to make, and very delicious. I threw in a few chicken wontons (available at Costco) in addition to the Chinese egg noodles. This reminds me of the soup that I regularly ate at Great New York Noodletown in NYC.
i learned to make this from my old BF in my 20s, except he used char siu for the protein.  i still make it occasionally for my daughter, telling her it's comfort food.

The comment section so far is hilarious. Asking if rice or egg noodles really look like that (of course they do, and they also come in dozens of other varieties) and a "Mom of 11 year old" who unhelpfully poo-poos this because why would another 11 year old want to make this (note: my 10 and 12 year olds definitely would). Kudos to everyone who is willing to step out of their bubble and try stepping into an asian grocery store, you will be rewarded for your curiosity.
Pickled mustard greens are listed as universally recognized and available, but this ingredient is new to me. Do I need to locate an Asian grocery store to find these?
yes. any asian store carries it. Weee.com carries asian ingredients.
Easily made into a vegetarian delight w/tofu and mushroom broth. Will give this a try :)
Yes, thanks for the suggestion. I don't know why my "thumbs up" isn't working...
what kind of noodles are in the photo? looks like spaghetti, but not sure rice noodles or egg noodles come in that shape.
I believe those are the Chinese egg noodles that you find fresh in the refrigerated section of Asian supermarkets.

@Rachel
…or Zabar’s if that helps 😎
What can I substitute for pickled mustard greens?
the pickled mustard green is optional. celery would give you a little extra flavor.
@Stevie A little sauerkraut or kimchi would add the touch of sour.
Simple recipe, quick to make. Very tasty. I added some soy sauce, white pepper, cayenne pepper, and toasted sesame oil. A winner.

With dried Chinese egg noodles in the pantry (available at any Asian Market), frozen roast chicken and a jar of chicken bouillon, this dish can come together in 15 minutes. Add the bok choy at the last minute so it doesn’t overcook. Adding sesame oil, light and dark soy sauce and miso after turning off the heat adds depth.

The 11 year old in this article is pretty exceptional. I have an 11 year old raised in Brooklyn his whole life, and he said that he didn't want to make this soup and would not even taste the soup if I made it, even with different or no vegetables.
Wait a second.  Are you generalizing about ALL 11-year-olds based on the reaction of your son?  Seems like other commenters disagree.  Who knows, if you made it, he might eat it & even enjoy it.

My 11 year old not only loves this but also gladly scarfs down anything from Portuguese caldo verde to raw oysters. Her most requested meal is sticky miso salmon bowls. Please don’t generalize kids. Just because yours has a limited palate doesn’t mean others do too.
@Another Mom of an 11 Year Old. Amen to that.  I have family members who, as toddlers, ate all sorts of leftovers for breakfast, happily.

When I was in the second grade, my mother went back to work as a school nurse. Being a T1 diabetic, she needed to eat at a consistent time, and so enlisted my 11yo brother and me to each be responsible for cooking dinner one week night while she commuted home.  We could cook what ever we wanted as long as it was a "square meal" and we gave her the menu before Tuesday night grocery shopping.  

I share this story often, for a variety of reasons.  That my very traditional mom had her son sharing responsibility for cooking, doing the laundry, and cleaning, and her daughter mowing the lawn and shoveling snow (in the suburbs of Buffalo, mind you.)  That when my brother was in high school, one of his meals was steak with mushrooms and a wine sauce - cooked in foil in our living room fireplace.  That carrots, peas, and corn are not vegetables, they're starch.  "Color-texture-taste," was another bit of guidance our mom gave us when we were coming up with our menus.

Lastly, as a Gen X 8yo, I was letting myself in after school to debone a chicken to make Hawaiian Chicken from the back of a can of Dole Pineapple.

Love this article for sparking memories and for the fun in reading the comments section.  Best wishes to all.
Any dish with all the recommended allowance of saturated fat and half the sodium has got to be delish…and it was!
Great idea. Chicken wontons!

YD, I was confused at first, too, until I reread Step 1. Melissa suggests marinating the chicken in the fridge for an hour. I'd skip that and make this dish - essentially a variation of ramen noodle soup - in under half an hour.@YD

@Karen Lynne
Thank you, Karen.

I never use commercial chicken broth. I don't know why it's always the basis of all recipes. It doesn't taste nice. If you  are using raw chicken to start a soup, you are making chicken broth. You'll save a bit of money and produce a tiny bit less waste without the prepackaged broth. It'll taste cleaner and better.

@Asil I couldn’t agree more. I have yet to find a commercially made broth that tastes remotely like the stuff I make. Most taste like dish water to me. I use an 8 quart stovetop pressure cooker to make my own broth but I’m sure an instant pot or similar device would work as well, and I use whatever appropriate ingredients I’ve saved in my freezer such as chicken bones, chicken skin, (pan drippings from roast chicken —which can be too much flavor for something like this recipe but great for the basis of a soup— ) top greens from leeks or scallions, the center stalks of celery with leaves, onion, carrot etc… a few peppercorns, NO added salt, and I learned that a small splash of white vinegar will help release all the goodness from the bones and cartilage… Cover with a couple or three quarts of water (depending on how many ingredients are in the pot and how much maximum capacity your pressure cooker allows…) I cook it at full pressure (15 lbs.) 30 minutes with natural release. Strain, cool and freeze in varying size jars. I do this maybe every 4 months or so as needed. I guarantee, you will never return to using store bought stock. I skip the temptation to flavor the stock with bay leaves, thyme, etc., which can be added later if desired; this way you have the most chicken tasting broth possible. The fat that rises to the top can be used for sautéing for recipes that taste better using chicken fat.
my simple chicken stock recipe is about 2 lbs mix of chicken pieces or bones. 1 onion, 2" piece ginger, 4 quarts water. bring to a boil and let simmer for a minimum of 1-2 hours.

Made as written but when tasting the finished soup, had to add bouillon, sesame oil and more oyster sauce to get any flavor out of the soup. I would try making it again with 8 cups of chicken stock and omit the 4 cups of water. The broth really lacked depth.

I had to scale this down to two servings and substituted sauerkraut for pickled mustard greens, as the latter is nearly impossible to find and I didn't want to make batch from scratch. Sauerkraut was the recommended substitute according to several sources. I had some cooked chicken thighs that I used instead of cooking them as described in the recipe.

Bottom line, inexpensive, quick, easy, delicious and reheats well. I recommend gently reheating it in a sauce pan rather than a microwave.

I would remove the poached chicken from the broth as soon as it's done or you will end up with overcooked, rubbery tasteless chicken even if only cooking another 5-7 minutes.  This applies to making chicken soup as well,  if you want to be able to enjoy the chicken meat. As soon as it's done, remove it from the bones, put the bones back in the stock and continue cooking for as long as possible. wings and backs & feet stay in the whole time ofcourse!
In my  house growing up, you got to pick what you wanted for your birthday dinner. When my brother was 7 or 8, he requested steak au poivre. Still a foodie. You never know.
Pickled mustard greens or preserved mustard is available on - you guessed it -Amazon. Store  the unused portion in a container in your fridge - lasts a LONG time….maybe forever.

This sounds delicious. I went to a large Asian market and found most of the ingredients. But, not the pickled mustard greens. (Many items on the shelves were labelled in Chinese; so, I may have missed it.) Back home, I searched online and could not find a place to buy it. I did find recipes and may try making a little. I'd appreciate any suggestions. Thanks.

@Carole
Amazon- preserved mustard or pickled mustard greens.

Simply ask an employee. They'll know or can find someone else who will.

I don't read Chinese either (or Vietmanese, Indonesian, or Thai, for that matter), but can generally find the items that my wife (Chinese) sends me out for.

I had a daunting experience with on line purchase of pickled mustard greens ( probably the fermented kind) so proceed with caution. Looking for a diy version I found a charming dissertation and recipe on Woks of Life. The procedure is similar to preserved lemons, On my list.
Ingredients list notes chicken broth, but Step 3 says stock.  I’m assuming stock is the better option??
This would probably also be delicious with tofu instead of chicken and vegetable stock. Not traditional perhaps, but healthy.

Made without oyster sauce, subbed soy sauce and a bit of sugar, subbed sauerkraut for the pickled mustard greens. Added a bit of tobacco as needed some heat and I didn't have chili oil or chili crisp. It was comforting and pretty easy to make.

I guess what confuses me is why wouldn’t you just cook the noodles in the broth with chicken and bok choy?  I mean, you just add them when you know the amount of time, like with the bok choy. Then you don’t have to wait for the chicken stock to come to a boil after you waited for the noodles water already. Am I missing something?
@BarNotKitchen. The noodles are cooked separately because they release starch as they cook, which can make the broth cloudy.

This looks delicious! I have a question about the cooking time, listed as 1h20 at the top of the page.  The cooking time indicated in the directions, however, total less than 20 minutes.  If the cooking time is longer than that at which step of the process should the remaining time be added? 🙏🏾
@YD The extra hour is for refrigerating the chicken after seasoning in step one.
@Shannon Thank you!
My husband loves to make Asian noodle recipes so I encouraged hi to try this. Easy and delightful. Thanks for the recipe.
My immediate family members cannot (EoE, food allergies) or should not (undiagnosed food intolerances that won’t kill them but are an issue) so I s

I didn’t have access to pickled mustard greens and the bok choy at my market was pricey and didn’t look good, so I split the difference and used fresh mustard greens. I used chicken thighs, which didn’t get tough, and vegetable bouillon, which I had on hand. I enjoyed the mild, comforting flavor but I admit I added a few drops of vinegar to perk up my serving.

I made this according to directions. We loved it. This would be a lovely soup to make for someone recovering from an illness. I like the lightness of Asian style chicken soups. Very refreshing as well as easy to make.

title: Roasted Chicken With Vinegared Grapes Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: As a vegetarian, I will not have the pleasure of eating this dish, though I will try the pickled grapes. I came here just to say that the accompanying article in which this recipe is featured is delightful. Ms. Clark is a fabulous writer, no doubt due to her love of Jane Austen novels. I miss my teenage motorcycle jacket phase...

@Christina. Make meatballs from beyond burger or impossible burger. Use the indicated spices. I have converted all my favorite stews/tagines in this way. The most important thing to me has always been the sauce and spices.  It works! And my little westie goes crazy when she smells vegan meatballs roasting. :)

Truly inspiring. Let's not forget Washington Irving's description of Bracebridge Hall and its overwhelming feast! Maybe we could start a trend. Then there's Martin Walker's Bruno series, with tasty interludes. What other authors could be added to this cornucopia of food appreciation?
@Jeff I belong to a book club that meets every month for dinner at a members home. Now our dinners are focused on the food inspired by the book.
@CB similar group in my life. RET . Read, eat, talk. Great fun.

The chicken was perfect, and still juicy and delicious two days later.  The pickled grapes and onions were lacking. I think they might be improved by using fresh lemon juice or lime juice with a bit of sugar with the salt to pickle them, rather than the ounce of wine vinegar I used as directed.  The onions were not completely pickled, and the vinegar gave the grapes a decidedly off (too sour) flavor. The taste didn’t harmonize with the chicken.
Red currant jelly added to pan drippings makes for remarkable gravy to add to a remarkable roasted pheasant!  If no pheasants come your way then chicken will do.
Donna Leon's superb Giudo Brunetti detective series set in Venice, with mouth-watering details of numerous Italian dishes
@Melanie Power - also, there is a Brunetti cookbook!
It’s a bog standard (in UK) roast chicken with a few grapes thrown in, the main difference being (here almost unthinkably) with no sign of any roast potatoes.
No "sides" are mentioned. I guess we're being trusted to cook our own.

As a vegetarian, I will not have the pleasure of eating this dish, though I will try the .  I came here just to say that the accompanying article in which this recipe is featured is delightful.  Ms. Clark is a fabulous writer, no doubt due to her love of Jane Austen novels.  I miss my teenage motorcycle jacket phase...

Melissa, the article about this recipe is a Delight! As a lover of Austen novels, I plan to make this and will add it to my favorites, as many of your recipes are for me. And when I read I’ll now pay more attention to any food mentioned.
I'm not sure why this is getting 3 stars. it's lovely and (in this day and age) unique.

Delicious. And now could someone find the recipe for a dish that is at the heart so many novels by another great female British writer? Barbara Pym's novels often feature a dish served to hungry young Church of England curates: boiled chicken (presumably whole) smothered in white sauce with lemon slices as garnish. I haven't been able to find anything like this on any website.
Is it related to Coronation Chicken? TBH I did some searches but couldn't find anything related to CoE re chicken.

There is an excellent recipe for whole boiled chicken in Marguerite Patten’s book Classic British Dishes (Bloomsbury Publishing, London, 1994) on page 177. The book also has a recipe for Velouté sauce (page 196j which is likely the white sauce you mention.
Coronation Chicken is actually Coronation Chicken SALAD, a lightly curried chicken salad served at Queen Elizabeth's coronation some 70+ yrs ago and now widely served throughout the UK.
Used split breasts instead of a whole chicken, which cooked in 45 mins. Also substituted a flavored white balsamic vinegar, which worked well. Delicious dish and the sauce pulled it all together.

Can this be done with a chicken that's been spatchcocked? I don't see why not personally, with cooking times adjusted, bigger wire rack, and no stuffing in the cavity. I've always felt that spatchcocked was truly a 19th century Jane Austen word.  Probably isn't but it is still a great idea to contemplate!

@Marc Braun I spatchcock every whole chicken I roast. With this recipe I just put the chicken in the rack open side up and nestled the herbs in there. I also dry age every chicken for at least 8 hours but preferably overnight. Nothing better!
Bravo Melissa
Question: There is no mention of pan drippings for the sauce. Shouldn’t they be used?
Couldn’t the grapes/onions be roasted in the pan drippings??
@Susan that is exactly what I thought reading this recipe for the first time. Throw the onions and grapes in the last 20-30 minutes of cooking the chicken!

This was by far the best roasted chicken I have ever made or had (and I’ve made a lot!). I roasted a 4.8lb chicken for 1 hour and 20 minutes, and it was unimaginably juicy and delicious. I served with roasted fingerling potatoes seasoned with herbs de provenance. My family is generally against potatoes and “weird things” like roasted grapes, and every single person had multiple servings (including 4 kids under age 15). I did find the proportion of onions to be off, so next time I’ll roast 1.5 and pickle only .5 with the grapes. Truly delicious.
Look in any British cookbook and you’re apt to find boiled chicken or turkey, my favorite with green  grape white sauce

@john golden, Camden Maine

Wow, that does not appeal to me in the least. I think I’m generally not a big fan of traditional English meals.

This was delicious! I followed the recipe exactly, but had to substitute pistachios because I didn’t have the walnuts. The next time I make this, I think I will add some chicken or some white beans. Otherwise, it was delicious, easy & a crowd pleaser.
Hmmm, this sounds like maybe it was for a different recipe? Wish I knew what it was.

Just to be clear, the sheet pan is the bottom layer, the wire rack is inside the sheet pan and the chicken sits on the rack - the usual order of business, correct? As written, the chicken sits on a sheet pan which sits on top of a wire rack, but I don’t think that is what’s intended.

"Place the chicken, breast-side up, in a rimmed sheet pan on top of a wire rack, if you have one (helpful but not necessary)."

The chicken sits on the wire rack that's in the sheet pan. (Though it's not mandatory.)
Cranberries are an obvious new world substitute for gooseberries. Who needs to wait for Thanksgiving.

I was wondering if people had left the grapes in bunches, as Melissa suggests in her intro? When I did that with another NYT recipe the first time, the stems ended up hard and nasty. I then read the comments and some mentioned that they had taken the grapes off their stems for that very reason. 

I'm definitely going to make it, and very much enjoyed Melissa's accompanying article!
Deb Perelman’s Smitten Kitchen has a great recipe for Chicken with shallots, grapes and olives that is really excellent too and maybe a bit quicker
This was delicious. Next time I will cook the grapes and onions in the drippings.
Used white vinegar vs. wine vinegar as directed.  It was light and bright, enhanced the dish.  Very delicious and juicy.
WOW. This was simply splendid.
Very fun article to accompany a recipe I plan to enjoy soon. Melissa Clark’s inventive recipes are invariably excellent.

If one could get their hands on gooseberries, could the recipe be followed as written? I appreciate the thoughtful substitution for grapes, but it would also be fun to try the “original!” Great accompanying article, I enjoyed reading it.

Sounds interesting.  Some commented that grapes were lacking.  I would look for the sweetest grapes - maybe red or moon drop.  I wouldn't go overly sweet, like cotton candy ones.  Thompsons, the usual green grape, are sweet when picked very ripe, but that's not going to happen for store bought, and same for purple.  This is a dish to try when the best grapes are in season.
Just made this for Sunday family dinner. We loved the harvest flavors, the juicy chicken, the simple ingredients. It’s a new favorite.
Poor chicken. A life ended to be a 15 minute meal.

11/10.  Epic.  I didn't have white wine vinegar so I used champagne vinegar and I feel like that really gave it a good depth of flavor. Also instead of using water to make the sauce I scooped out some of the chicken pan drippings.
Delicious!  And yes, I did mix it all up a bit, and the butter and the drippings and spoons of that put on the chicken...  Yum.
The chicken was excellent, the grapes and onions were not.

Simplified this for a weeknight dish & used chicken thigh. Rendered the skin first on a cold pan, and then topped the grapes, onions w the chicken thighs. the pickled grapes were a great contrast to the crispy skin. And leftover roasted grapes, onions was excellent to toss in with sautéed kale the next day

My husband made this and it was absolutely delicious. The subtle flavours of the herbs, grated nutmeg and crushed coriander seeds were glorious, and the vinegared grapes were a game-changer. This preparation provided the perfect counterpoint to their normal (sometimes over-) sweetness.

title: Vegan Gluten-Free Chocolate Chip Cookies Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

stats.Cook Time: 20 minutes plus 1 ½ hours freezing time

comments.commentBody: Hi All - Thanks to everyone's notes, the recipe has been corrected. Use 1/4 cup butter (56 grams) and add the vanilla with the vegan butter. And yes, the cookies will work using dairy milk and butter.
I wonder if you could use dairy milk and butter if you don't need them to be vegan? Thanks Melissa for a great recipe!

@SallyT

I used regular milk and butter, because I need gluten free but not vegan. They came out great!
Has anyone tried these with regular butter? Planning to make these but will only use real butter. Is it a 1:1 swap?
@mary I used real butter and whole milk and a 12 ounce package of chocolate chips. It worked great.
WAAY too much sugar.  I used tahini and maple syrup instead.  came out delicious.  Try it.
@Peggy K Can you share the amounts of each you used?
@Peggy K please share your tahini and syrup measures

A quick internet search says you can substitute a 50/50 combination of tahini/maple syrup for brown sugar at a 1:1 ratio (so for this recipe that would be 1 cup plus 1 Tbsp tahini and 1 cup plus 1 Tbsp maple syrup to replace the 2 cups plus 2 Tbsp brown sugar) but you will likely want to decrease the amounts of other liquid ingredients in the recipe. Would love to know what worked for Peggy K and what works for others!
1/4 cup butter is not 113 grams. It is 56.5 grams. Which measurement is correct?

Don't stock oat flour?  No need if you have a blender or food processor - just throw in any kind of oats.  A high-speed blender will turn 2 cups rolled oats into 2 cups oat flour in about 30 seconds. 

(You can make your own almond flour too, though it's a little more finicky.  Search the web for homemade almond flour for tips.)
I'm curious why soy-free vegan butter is specified. Does soy-based butter act differently in cooking? Or is it deemed less healthy?

Vegan butters are tricky in baking.  The spreadable ones have a lot of water in them, and they often contain soy. The harder ones, often comtain palm oil, which has its environmental concerns.  Best to find one whose flavor you like and use it, adapting recipes as you become familiar with how it performs.
I thought the suggestion for tahini seemed solid - much less  processed and adds a lovely umami note to baking.

@LA LMFT at our restaurant we have a ton of folks that request soy free items and originally we used coconut oil but found that a leftover cookie a few days later wasn’t good so we switched to a vegan butter and went with the soy free version.   Happy baking
Did anyone try this with a replacement for the almond flour (I’d need to due to nut allergies)?
When do you add the vanilla?  With the milk?
@Cookie lover Yes, add vanilla with the milk.

Just made the dough and portioned out into 57 gram cookies (about 2 ounces as Melissa calls for). I only got 22 cookies - not the stated 36. Followed recipe to a T.  This happen to anyone else? No report on taste yet. Will bake tomorrow.
@anne I came here to ask the same! just mixed up and scooped out my dough. I only got 16 cookies, and my scoop is just under 2 ounces.
@Karen I think this is an error in the recipe. If you weigh ingredients and make 1oz cookie balls, you’ll get right around 36 cookies

Thinking about swapping in some vegetable oil for the melted vegan butter… what’s the worst that could happen? That’s not a rhetorical question. Interested in any thoughts on what is the worst that could happen.
Is it important to use two types of flour?   For those with nut allergies, what could substitute for the almond butter?

The texture of this particular cookie really relies on that almond flour, I think; you could replace it with more oat flour, but the texture will be more grainy than dense and chewy, and you'd likely need to adjust the amount of liquid. The recipe doesn't call for almond butter, and any (dairy or non-dairy) milk can be used for the liquid, but if you can't eat nuts, I think that you're best off consulting a different recipe.
I only got about 13 cookies using a quarter cup scoop. Either the scoop is too big or the yield advertised as 36 cookies is wrong.

I realize it would be different if I used all almost flour instead of using oat flour, but would it be an ok (albeit different) cookie? I’m thinking this might be a good option for my husband’s family for Passover.

Why preferably soy free? Are people still spreading scary tales about soy "estrogens" Soy is a superfood and an amazing bean. The "estrogens" people worry about are not estrogen, but estrogenic compounds that are much milder, hormonally, than our own hormones, and protect us against cancers. Soy protein is an important part of many diets, worldwide. And T. Colin Campbell, once the head of nutrition at Cornell proved it's healthier than any animal product. Read The China Study to see what I mean.

@Bett my guess is that request comes from the original recipe, where they are probably trying to create a cookie for a wide audience. I agree with you 100%, but if I ran a bakery where customers kept asking about soy, I would probably just avoid it in order to avoid those conversations.

So good! I’m really impressed by the texture. I subbed in coconut sugar and reduced it… I think 1.5c probably would have been enough. I never say this, but I also wish I reduced the chocolate, maybe to 7 ounces or so. A little flakey salt on top could work well, too.

Thank you for passing this along.
Please elaborate on 
Step 3... form them into cookies
Pucks that will spread?  If so how large?
Full sized cookies?  If the fat is 1/4 cup, then I suspect they don't spread too much but then they are baked 2" apart.
My grandson has an egg, tree nut and gluten allergy.  What can I substitute for the flours?
Also curious about Peggy K.'s measurements of tahini and maple syrup. Peggy - are you out there?

@Linda Houser I would swap both the almond and oat flours for a GF free flour blend. That's more likely to work than replacing nut flour with a non-nut flour. As an aside nut flours are medium flours so you can replace one medium flour for another. A medium flour is high protein, high moisture - it helps bind the mixture together. Chickpea flour is one example but would change the flavour?. Information is "sound" from "from the larder" website, but I can't find the PDF with the information I need and half remember sighs
I made these with the (too much) vegan butter.  ALL over the oven!  I'm also wondering if this is too much baking soda?  I think they taste like baking soda!
These look good, but one of our colleagues is not only celiac but also cannot eat oats due to allergies. What might substitute for oat flour? Tapioca? Rice flour?

Made these today. They turned out more candy-like than cookie-like. Worried that I got the proportions wrong. I made my own oat flour using rolled oats in the food processor but I'm wondering if I didn't process it long enough.
it looks like I should have flattened the cookies out before I baked them.  I just slightly flattened the balls, but I wish I had made them thinner.

These were pretty darn good, tasty, excellent texture. I froze them overnight in a silicone ice cube mold, cutting the portion size in half (out of laziness). Took about 12 minutes in a toaster oven. I used regular flour and butter, but I'll try the vegan original soon. Lotsa sugar, though. I want a sweet treat to hang out in the freezer, but these might not be it. I'm looking for a recipe that is very good, but not so incredibly good my husband and son thaw and eat them all.
I made these as written and they were amazing out of the oven and the next day as well!  Thank you for a wonderful vegan cookie recipe.

Be helpful if a NYT chief would write a version for those of us, FOR HEALTH REASONS, do not use sugar. Sugar substitutes do not perform as sugar does, so trying to simply sub Stevia, etc., isn’t very successful. Suggestions would be appreciated.

For those looking for brown sugar substitutions: When replacing a cup of brown sugar with a combination of tahini and maple syrup, first, use about 2/3 cup of the combined liquid sweeteners (around 1/3 cup tahini and 1/3 cup maple syrup, adjust to your preference) for every cup of brown sugar called for in the recipe, and then reduce other liquids in the recipe by approximately 1/4 cup to accommodate the added moisture from the liquid sweeteners. You may also need to reduce the oven temperature.
Why is soy-free butter preferred? I'm curious.
could Iuse real butter?
Same questions as other, can dairy butter be used in place of vegan? And correction for amount. Thank you

will the cookies flatten out in the oven if the dough is frozen as balls of dough?
Thanks
What other flower than almond I have a nut allergy
Can I use regular flour? And I'm craving cookies with big chunks of chocolate. Not tiny chocolate chips. Is there a vegan way to do that? I assume you can't use regular chocolate bars in baking
@Elin buy vegan chocolate bars and break them up
Oat flour has gluten in it… not safe for celiac
@Sophie no it doesn’t. Oats are GF
there is GF oat flour! You just have to buy that kind.
@Sophie correct. Bob’s Red Mill has GF oats
Anyone know why the dough needs to be frozen before baking? Can’t I just make the cookies after mixing the ingredients?

The dough is extremely soft and spreads a lot even if they’re frozen. I followed the directions except for substituting dairy butter for the vegan butter and mine came out flatter than the picture. Tasty, though!
@J Bean Thank you for explaining! That makes sense.
FYI  Not all sugar is vegan-much of the sugar sold and used in the United States us bone chard "animal bones" as filtering for whitening and refinement.
About how many does this recipe make?

I really wish these 'gluten free' recipes based on oat flour would explain that regular oat flour and other oat products are NOT considered gluten free. You must buy specifically labeled gluten free oat products if you actually need these to be gluten free. Also, if you are making these for someone with Celiac disease, be advised that about 10% of people with Celiac cannot eat oats of any kind - even the ones marked 'gluten free'.
Just realised I bought salted vegan butter. Is unsalted assumed if not specified? What have folks been using?
Used earth balance butter and oat milk.

Love these!
Regarding the number of cookies…what I’ve found is that measuring the dry ingredients vs weighing the dry ingredients leads to two totally different experiences.

When we measure with the ol’ measuring cups, I think this ends up using more of the flours. The cookies look more like a traditional choc chip cookie (pudgy in the middle) - and we’re able to usually get in the high 20s to low 30s depending on how we’re feeling about our dough ball size that day. Just eyeballing it.

Today I used the kitchen scale I got as a gift for the first time, and noticed that the weighed flours are quite a bit less, volume-wise, than the measured. I also weighed out 2 oz balls bc why not just weigh everything? And whaddya know - We got 22 balls. The dough was more wet, and the cookies, upon baking, spread out flat - much more like they are at the actual LB Kitchen (where we’re lucky enough to eat every so often). 

We generally keep a freezer bag of frozen dough balls going each week, and can bake them to order each night after dinner.

I found the dough impossibly sticky until I hit upon the idea of freezing it for a while (refrigeration alone didn’t work) BEFORE forming it in to cookies: much easier to handle that way. Delicious and interesting texture. Lovely to have a cookie that both my celiac relative and vegan relative can enjoy along with everyone else! Great hit at Thanksgiving. Thanks Melissa!

I halved the sugar (per another comment) but then doubled the liquid PLUS needed to add at least 1/4 cup more (I used Stevia maple syrup) and the batter still did not reach a "sticky, wet, batter-like dough"! I made this the first time and they were like dry hockey pucks. I'm trying a second time today and just kept adding non-dairy milk until it was wet enough. Fingers crossed.
Can I skip the freezing step? What will be the consequences?

title: Vegan Gluten-Free Oatmeal Raisin Cookies Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: Hi All - Thanks to everyone's notes, the recipe has been corrected. Use 1/4 cup butter (56 grams) and add the vanilla with the vegan butter. And yes, the cookies will work using dairy milk and butter.
@Melissa Clark what about the sugar? They are extremely sweet!
The amount of sugar in this recipe is mind-boggling (about the same as the flours and oats combined). And that without counting the high sugar content naturally present in the raisins. Sweet poison!

It does seem excessive, doesn't it? I looked up my regular oatmeal raisin recipes and they're usually more like a 1:2 ratio of sugars:flours+oats (leaning a bit more to the sugar side, of course). Is this because of the vegan/gf ingredients? Or simply that the LB Kitchen cookies require this amount for texture?

1. I assume one can use daily milk and butter and get the same results.
2. No raisins!!
3. Chocolate chips and walnuts, please, and thank you.
@Lee Osherovsky you request “no raisins”, with multiple exclamation points. You also want chocolate chips and nuts.  Um… this is a recipe for oatmeal raisin cookies.
Lee, there's a chocolate chip version here! https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1027284-vegan-gluten-free-chocolate-chip-cookies
Can you use butter -if you're not vegan?

I followed the recipe to the letter and my cookies are *massive* flat lakes of non-cooking batter. They have been in the oven for over 25 minutes and show no sign of firming up. I'm a 50-year-old woman who is generally quite skilled in the kitchen. I'm not sure where I went wrong with this one, but feel pretty badly that I apparently am less competent than a preschooler.
Difficult to believe a toddler can be fully engaged in cookie making for 3 hours. I'll take a pass because the sugar content is stratospheric.
I used half the amount of sugar and tahini instead of butter
@Barbaraand and did they work? Did you like them?
I agree with other commenters regarding the extreme amount of sugar. I wouldn’t eat it myself, much less feed it to a 3 year old.

Size for 36?  I suggest 2 Tbsp (1oz). 
The 2oz (1/4 cup) makes 18.
The sugar was 1/2 cup too much, but this recipe is still a keeper. I added shredded coconut to mine; will add chocolate chips and/or nuts next time.
Even some oat products are not GF due to cross contamination in processing.  Just a caution to check closely for certified GF oats and oat flour if you’re cooking for someone with celiac disease.

Followed the recipe (used Miyoko’s butter and oat milk) except: 

used chocolate chips instead of raisins

1/2 cardamom and 1/2 cinnamon instead of one or the other 

Thank you!

Delicious!
The comments on this recipe got me giggling
No cookie recipe for toddlers (or adults for that matter) should have 2 cups of sugar. It's almost criminal!

Thank you for this! GF and vegan!  What a treat!
Step 3: ... to form cookies
This could use some clarification. Should they be pucks which will spread during baking? If so, for the 1/4 cup measure, how big should they be?  Should they be pressed into a 3 or 4" cookie, assuming little to no spreading? The low fat content makes me think that they don't spread very much. Would you please elaborate?

I wish it stated it somewhere in the recipe, but if you are actually making these cookies to be gluten free, you need to use oat products that are specifically labeled gluten free. Standard oat flour and oatmeal products are NOT considered gluten free due to the large amount of cross-contamination with wheat. Look for certified gluten free oats or 'purity protocol'.
Did the cookie bake the same way with less sugar?
These cookies are insanely sweet. I am planning to use half the sugar (or even less)  the next time around. With less sugar these will be amazing.
These cookies are SOOOO good!  I did cut the sugar to 1.5 cups.
Has anyone tried grinding oats in a food processor if you do not have oat flour?
loved the comment to use tahini instead of vegan butter, plus reduced sugar by 1/2 cup.  these were excellent. have to smash them down before or during cooking because mine would not spread.
how long will these keep, after baking? (are they candidates for shipping?)
I am looking for great gluten-free cookie recipes. But like everyone else, I do keep careful watch of sugar. Can these cookies be made with less sugar and still be good?

@Cathy, I saw a comment from a baker who successfully subbed 1.5 cups coconut sugar for the 2 cups of brown sugar on the chocolate chip version of these cookies.  I’m going to try it & will post an update.
This seems to be just a regular oatmeal cookie recipe substituting vegan butter and vegan milk for non vegan.
@MD it also uses oat and almond flours rather than wheat and it doesn’t contain eggs.

Thanks, Melissa! Delicious GF cookie with, admittedly, small modifications for the non-vegan here: subbed in melted butter for the vegetable oil, and soaked raisins with an egg and some of the vanilla then added as directed. Next time, 2 cups sugar will do.
Made with oat milk and earth balance butter

I am confused by the high rating for this recipe. I just baked it, exactly as in the recipe, and it is an unevenly baked mess. Even baking it for 15 minutes gives me a very raw and goopy center. And wow is it too sweet. But my main complaint is the baking. Makes me wonder if maybe I should skip the freezing step??? I rarely have a fail of a 5 star recipe here.

I’m not sure where I went wrong with this one. I followed the recipe as written but my cookies spread so far apart and still look gooey in the center. I might try less sugar next time. The taste is good though!

I made these today and they came out perfectly. I topped each cookie with a couple flakes of Malden salt to balance the sugar. I didn’t have oat flour so used pastry flour, so mine weren’t gluten free. Mine baked for 13 minutes.

stats.Total Time: 1 hour, plus at least 1½ hours’ cooling time
stats.Cook Time: 45 minutes, plus at least 1½ hours’ cooling time

comments.commentBody: I baked these yesterday because I had a jar of lemon curd that I needed to use up. It was just shy of a cup. So as was recommended as a possible sub for lemon curd in the video, I added a little drizzle of homemade raspberry jam to the curd. Lemon and raspberry are a great pairing. From there I followed the recipe pretty close, but had to leave out the poppyseeds because I didn’t have any on hand. The lemon raspberry bars turned out really good. I’m sure the bars would also be great with the poppyseeds, but you still have a very good treat without them. My only recommendation is to use good butter since this is a butter heavy recipe—you will taste the butter, so use one you would want to eat on toast. I like Kerrygold.

@Lynn B.
Thank you Lynn. I avoid poppy seeds but thought this could be a delicious recipe without them. As a novice baker I wasn’t sure how to proceed. I enjoy lemon curd and raspberry.  I’ll try the suggested hints!
@Lynn B.  Thank you for reviewing them as “lemon Raspberry bars.”  I think I’ll have to keep reading to find out more about how the poppy seeds worked out.

@Lynn B. I followed your suggestion by using lemon curd and raspberry jam (I had both in the fridge) and it was out of this world! Easy to make and since I have another jar of homemade lemon curd, I’ll be making another batch next week.

I never leave reviews but for whatever reason, lemons to be used up and poppy seeds, and not a baker, this recipe sang to me.  So I made it today.  Wonderful, relatively easy but have the lemon curd cooled before you put it together.  I found a microwave lemon curd recipe on the internet which was easy and very good.  Never the baker but this will be a keeper  and made frequently.  Delish!
Would be helpful if the ingredients and process for making lemon curd were included in this recipe.
@Dindi 100% agreed. Very disappointing
@Dindi https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022279-the-easiest-lemon-curd?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share

@Dindi, she did include it in the full article. You can often find links with additional tips and related recipes in the full article, which is almost always linked at the top of these recipe excerpts (look for “Featured in:…”) I see Linda R shared the lemon curd link below.
I haven't made this yet but I'm totally doubling the recipe when I do.

Oh dear…..these are really good.  I’ve already eaten a couple.  Made exactly as written. Wouldn’t change a thing.  I had curd on hand from another recipe.  With that caveat, these cookie bars were a snap to put together.  Well done Melissa.

Full disclosure: I deviated from the recipe to use what I had on hand as I needed something quick for a boat party and I did not plan well. I used lime instead of lemon rind. I had some IKEA lingonberry jam and some strawberry jam, which I mixed together. I added lime juice to the jams because I didn’t want to waste the limes after I zested them. No poppy seeds. :( I added a generous teaspoon of ground ginger and a half teaspoon of cardamom with the flour. The entire batch disappeared quickly, had everyone guessing what was in the filling (raspberry was the common theme) and asking for the recipe. I now have lemons and poppy seeds on my grocery list. And a tip to butter the pan before the parchment: take your stick of softened butter lengthways and make one swipe down the pan. You just need a bit to stick the parchment in place.
I have celiac and made these with GF flour. Did a combo of homemade lemon curd and raspberry jam. SO GOOD! For my celiac buddies always looking for recipes to hijack, this is a good one.
@Avery S what brand of gf flour do you use? Thanks!
@Heidi R. The Bob’s Red Mill Cup For Cup, or the Thomas Keller brand Cup 4 Cup seem to consistently work best.

What characteristics to look for in a store bought lemon curd?  I’ve had some that are almost starchy, some glisten like  home made but I don’t buy or make often enough to make an informed choice for these yummy looking bars.
Bonne Maman make a great lemon curd!
Bonne Maman and Trader Joe's make good lemon curd.
Trader Joe's is very good.
Why are we buttering the pan if we’re covering it with parchment?

@Cynthia R
The butter makes the paper stick to the sides.
@Cynthia R I didn’t bother with the buttering the pan step and it worked out fine.  The dough was firm enough to hold the paper in place!

@Cynthia R
Why waste butter? I use cooking spray to stick the parchment paper to the pan. I agree with the person who suggested using parchment on all sides-just cut two strips for the sides not covered.
Made these using even more lemon curd (1.5 cups) and they were so good! Beautiful texture; somewhere between crumb cake and shortbread.

Worth all five of the starts it has! Next time, I will line the pan with two pieces of parchment so that there is an overhang on all four sides, because even with a well-buttered pan, I found the lemon curd stuck hard to the unlined sides. By the time the dough was perfectly golden, the lemon curd around the edges was burnt, but it was easy enough to trim a thin strip off each of the edges, and then they were perfect.

What’s with all the anti- poppy seed comments?
There are ten million other lemon bar recipes online, so why go to this one, if you have an aversion? I love them & would classify them as a somewhat magical spice, in a similar class as saffron. Lemon & poppyseeds go together so beautifully, and I can’t wait to make these!

I made this exactly as written with leftover homemade curd that I had in the freezer, and chose the almond extract instead of the vanilla - I think lemon and almond work really well together. The bars are delicious! If you want to make these in a 9x13 pan I would double the recipe, but for these bars a little goes a long way. Perfect for a treat with some tea or coffee.
Used store bought cherry preserves instead of lemon curd and added a squeeze of lemon to the dough to make sure it came together but otherwise followed the recipe as written. Excellent results.

Wonderful recipe, delicious results! I have filed this in my "Cannot Go Wrong" folder. :-) I used all of a Trader Joe's 12 oz jar of lemon curd, a regular hand held mixer and baked in a 8" glass pan (lined with parchment paper per recipe). The key is allowing the pan to completely cool. I had to use my refrigerator (and even my freezer for a bit) to hurry that process along as I was taking this to a party. Everyone raved!

This recipe is truly a stunner. It hits many of the high notes of a lemon curd tart without the fussiness. Buttery and bright, it’s a lovely summer reinterpretation of what is essentially a shortbread jammer for other midwesterners out there. 

Super easy to execute. I increased the volume of ingredients by about 25% because my pan is 9x9 and had no trouble. Err on the side of baking longer - the shortbread is best when golden brown and that takes a little longer on the bottom. I baked it for about 50 minutes total. This is a winner, Melissa!
My apologies, but I am not a fan of poppy seeds.  Would like to make these delicious-sounding bars.  Could you please suggest a replacement ingredient.  Many thanks!

@Ann N I made them yesterday without the poppyseeds and they were just fine without. For what it is worth, I added a tablespoon of almond flour, but I doubt that was even necessary. This appears to be a very forgiving recipe.
If you watch the video, Melissa does suggest other fillings, like raspberry jam.  You could also do lemon bars and omit the poppyseeds.  I haven't made these yet either
I am the same and was planning to just omit them. No good?

I made this last night as I had some lemon curd in the freezer.  They ended up being a little more savory than expected.  I rarely say this but next time I might add 20 g more sugar just to get a little more caramelization on the crust.  I think if using jarred lemon curd the sweetness would have been just right.   

Other than that, delicious!
Do they freeze well?
@Beverly In the video Melissa says they do freeze well.

I never write recipe reviews, but I've made this three times in as many weeks and I have to say something. I live in a communal household and it's tricky to find recipes that everyone likes. THIS is not only a crowd pleaser that disappears immediately, it is so easy! It only uses pantry staples! It comes together in 20 minutes when I panic and realize we need dessert! It keeps in the cabinet and tastes great warm or cold! Paired with King Arthur's microwave lemon curd and my favorite shortcuts, refrigerated lemon juice and lemon extract subbing for zest, it punches FAR FAR above its weight. Highly recommend.
Truly fabulous. Followed the recipe exactly, using almond extract and Bonne Maman lemon curd. I felt there was a little excess dough so used it to make a couple of little cookies…
@Ruth I made it this way and everyone in the dinner party found it to be cloyingly sweet. I think the almond extract gives a sweeter flavor even in the absence of more sweetness.

If you don't like poppy seeds, I would think that a finely chopped nut would do the trick, perhaps almonds, lightly toasted first.  My choice would be pecans since they make any shortbread better.  Not too many since they contain more oil.  
Or just leave them out since they are an addition and not an integral part of the baking process.

Has anyone made this with orange zest and orange curd?  
Seems like a beautiful combo - orange/poppy seed

Might try that tomorrow.

These were delicious. A keeper!

I used homemade apricot preserves because I have a lot of it in my refrigerator, and the recipe introduction suggested that as an alternative. I used about an extra half cup and it turned out great.

I think this recipe is very forgiving. I had to use a glass pan and that didn’t seem to matter. I could also see adding an extra tablespoon of poppyseeds to the dough next time. I think that’s just personal preference and depends on what texture you want the dough. 

I used the almond extract because I think that goes better with apricot. I used the zest of only one lemon to avoid that flavor interfering with the delicate apricot taste. 

I would err on the side of over-baking rather than under-baking these. Next time I would get them a little more brown before taking them out of the oven.

I liked them best the day they were made because They were nice and crisp. I thought they got too soft in later days. 

I also suggest tasting your poppy seeds before adding them to the dough. They go rancid quickly and bad poppy seeds taste terrible.

I made these today for the first time.  I think they are good but would have liked a bit more lemon flavor.  It seemed that the lemon curd was swallowed up by the cake. I might use a bit more curd next time.

I suggest that you taste your poppy seeds before adding them in. Poppy seeds go rancid quite quickly, and sometimes aren’t good even just bought from the store.

Bad poppy seeds ruin recipes! Bleh!

I used a jar of Bonne Maman lemon curd 12 oz, 360 grams.  I thought I would have extra, since the recipe only called for 280grams  but the jar only yielded 1 cup?  Should I use the weight given or the volume measurement?  

I only had 1/2 cup of white sugar so I added brown sugar to make up the difference.  The bars taste delicious.

I made this exactly as written including making my own lemon curd. But as I was putting it in the oven I noticed I’d lined the pan with waxed paper, not parchment paper. So, I did my best to transfer the whole thing out of the pan and then slid it back into the parchment lined pan. It was quite jumbled, crusts and curd intermingled. Still delicious! My advice: don’t listen to a good audio book while baking!
Ok. My old mixer doesn’t have a paddle. Does it matter?

@Rachel I do not think the mixer paddle is key here. You could use a hand-held mixer or mix it by hand. You can watch the accompanying video for a few comments about the desired texture at various stages.
Trader Joe's Lemon Curd is very close to homemade!
True. That’s what I used and it was like homemade.

Made these using orange zest, almond extract and apricot curd - very tasty!
Lots of potential variations on this one.

These are wonderful and pretty easy. The curd is delicious - I used Bonne Maman. I cooled them in the fridge for half an hour which worked nicely, since I wanted to serve them more quickly than after an hour and a half of waiting (I know I know poor timing on my end). Instead of parchment, I used baking spray on the pan, and they came out flawlessly. Really nice recipe that will definitely make again.
Even if you’re an idiot like me and add black sesame seeds instead of poppy, it still tastes good. Next time I’ll pay attention.
Will these hold up in the freezer?
@JBBerlin - In the video, Melissa said the bars will freeze well!
if you don't have a stand mixer can you make this?

Absolutely. Use a whisk for Step 2 and an electric hand mixer for the rest. If you don't have a hand mixer, either, it will take more time to cream the butter and incorporate the flour, but it can be done with a spoon and some elbow grease.

These were so delicious! I made Molly O'Neill's Lemon Curd from NYT Recipe Box (didn't bother straining it). I had run out of vanilla so used maple syrup. Couldn't really taste it with all the yummy lemon flavor. This recipe is a keeper!

These were absolutely delicious! The poppy seeds are deliciously crunchy, and the lemon curd cuts the richness of the dough perfectly! I used the Molly O'Neill's Lemon Curd recipe, and it made the perfect amount.

So glad I made these  - and will be making them more and more!  The shortbread crust is fantastic and I can imagine all the variations that are possible.  I used homemade lemon curd using The View’s From The Great Island microwave lemon curd which took about 10 minutes and was fabulous.  No reason to use store bought if you like to futz in the kitchen.
Made these vegan by using Earth Balance sticks in the dough, and homemade vegan lemon curd with Myokos. Delicious

@Sophia

Would you be willing to share a little more about how you made vegan lemon curd? What recipe did you use? What did you substitute for the eggs? Thanks!
@cocomero I used this recipe: https://addictedtodates.com/vegan-lemon-curd/ It worked out very well!
Cooked (almost) as directed, only replacing the flour with an AP Gluten Free flour. Delicious!

I've made these twice now and cannot get over how good they are. It definitely hits both my lemon bar and lemon poppyseed scone/muffin cravings all in one little package. 

I let the edges get a little too done my first time through, and it can turn to a burnt taste pretty quickly. But I pulled them out right as they were starting to show some browning on the second attempt, and everything was perfect. 

Will 100% be a repeat recipe for years to come.

I followed the recipe to the letter, using Kerrygold unsalted butter and Bonne Maman’s lemon curd. These bars were good but not fabulous. I love Lemon Bars and I love Poppy Seed cake—I prefer either of those treats to these. Too bad—I was really looking forward to a fabulous and easy new dessert!

I made this recipe for Christmas, but I couldn’t find my 8 x 8 square pan, so I made it in a springform and then cut it into thin wedges.  The only thing I did differently was I put one teaspoon of vanilla and one teaspoon of almond extract. Everybody loved this recipe. It had a wonderful sweet tang of lemons but the poppy seed dough was fabulous. It made a short bread type of crust and on top but the Lemon was popping up between on top. I love almond extract as I’m used to eating almond poppyseed cakes, but increasing the almond did not take away from the lemon or the depth of the flavors. I would absolutely make this again.

If you make your own curd and use it before it’s fully thickened in the refrigerator for 3 hours, the cookie part won’t be crunchy and it’ll take forever to bake. I somehow ended up with too much topping, too. Maybe my mistake (?) I think if you do everything right, it’s a lovely recipe.
How do I get into my recipe box to recover a recipe I saved there?
@Eileen N. When you are on a recipe page, look at the lower right hand corner. There is a grey Recipe Box icon. Tap on it and it will take you to your saved recipes.
Click on Your Recipe Box at the top right corner of the page.
Can you substitute plum jam for lemon curd??

Not a natural born baker so this question may be obvious to everyone but me, but regarding the parchment, instructions say, “…line the bottom sides with parchment, leaving 1 inch of overhang all around”. Is the parchment supposed to hang over two sides, like a sling, or over all 4 sides?

I can't wait to make this.  I am not near a Trader Joe's during the summer.  Does anyone have a good homemade recipe.  Please respond with one.  
 To the person who asked about freezing curd I saw this online...."Can You Freeze Lemon Curd?  Yes, lemon curd freezes quite well. You can freeze the curd for up to one year. Thaw in the refrigerator for about 24 hours before you need to use it."
How important is it to use a metal pan. I often see the recommendation, but I’ve replaced all my metal pans over the years with glass
I just asked this same question 4 hours after you did!
I’ve made a similar version of this recipe a number of times - shortbread, fruit, shortbread crumble. But this recipe yielded unbelievably tender and tasty results. Highly recommend.
Can this be made in a tart pan?
I’m not seeing when to add the vanilla listed in ingredients—so I added it with the poppyseeds, which is when I realized I hadn’t added it yet.
Followed the recipe, except substituted gf King Arthur flour for the reg flour. Divine. Very rich so a little goes a long way

Delicious. I am a sucker for both shortbread and lemon curd. The poppyseeds add a nice crunch. I'm already thinking how I might adapt these into a shortbread sandwich cookie for holiday gift-giving. Aside: I usually make the BA lemon curd, but Molly O’Neill's on this site is more tart (slightly less sugar), and I liked that in this recipe. Nothing about it is too sweet. Perfection.
I can’t seem to find info on how to store these. Should they be refrigerated? Thank you for any guidance!

Another banger from Melissa. Used Molly O’Neil’s Lemon Curd recipe for this which makes the exact right amount for the bar recipe. Fresh poppy seeds are a must - open a new jar for this! I used Meyer lemons for the curd and Eureka (normal) lemons for the zest in the bars. I’ll absolutely make these again.

Couldn't find the 8 inch metal pan so used glass, with the buttered parchment. It worked fine, just a few minutes extra on the baking time. Made these exactly as written and they were a huge hit. I made them one day in advance for a party and stored them uncut in the pan. They held over well but I suspect they were best on the day they were baked.
This was excellent, made as written except that I used Molly O’Neill’s lemon curd recipe for the filling, which gave me more control over the sweetness. Most store-bought lemon curd is too sweet.
Soooooo good. Made my own lemon curd for the first time and was surprisingly easy. Make sure you save enough dough for the topping bit.

So buttery! That's a classic Melissa Clark recipe, right there. Yum. I can't decide if it's too lemony (and I didn't think such a think was possible) or if I can taste the poppy seeds or not, but it is lovely. I used Bonne Maman curd, but might test taste the Trader Joe's or Stonewall Kitchen to see if it might be more my taste (Chances I would make my own are pretty small, so!)

Followed recipe with a few exceptions. Used zest of three lemons (imho no such thing as too lemony), divided dough into 12 parchment cupcake liners and adjusted cooking time. I used Wilkin & Sons lemon curd. They turned out beautiful and received rave reviews from my test-taster friends. I did make one with Seville orange jam, it didn’t bubble and caramelize around the edges like the lemon curd did. Will be keeping that in mind when trying variations of this recipe. Can’t wait to continue experimenting with this recipe.
@Carol B. Tiptree Lemon curd (aka. Wilkin & Sons) is excellent and works great in this recipe. I’m excited to try this next with their Passion Fruit curd.
Has anyone used a glass 8x8 dish to bake these cookies?
I considered it but then realized that the shortbread needs metal to bake properly, evenly, and to get the right crumb.
I did because it's all I had, and it turned out fine. Metal may be better for the crumb, but they still tasted amazing.
If serving the next day, can these be baked and stored at room temperature?

I have tried so many different lemon bar recipes!  Until now I had not found one to commit to!  This buttery shortbread base and the not-cloyingly sweet curd is perfect for me! I will put this on repeat…

Made as written except I used an entire jar of Trader Joe’s lemon curd. This recipe is fantastic. Not too sweet, not too lemony. My husband isn’t keen on lemony desserts and he loves this. Use good butter and don’t skip the poppy seeds. They add such a nice texture. Comes together quickly too! I’ll have this part of my dessert ro-to.

Perfect as is! The 2nd time I tried subbing TJ's guava jam and omitted poppy seeds and it was fantastic. Also, because I prefer a darker crust, I blind baked for 15 min before adding the jam. Such a versatile recipe!

To accommodate some gluten free friends, I made this using 2 cups of almond flour in place of the all purpose flour and took the option of using almond extract in place of of the vanilla to enhance the almond flavor, and they were delicious!
Truly wonderful cookies, though not easy of you're making your own lemon curd (I didn't know it had to cool for 3 hrs so these became a two day affair). And yet! Buttery, lemony delights!

Also what in the world are these disappointed comments about the shortbread being "dense". That is a defining feature of shortbread, there is no egg or leavener, all shortbreads are therefore dense buttery crumbly and delicious. Don't expect otherwise.

My daughter: “I like Lemon Bars, but these are a whole lot better than Lemon Bars.”
They really are. Plus, I added a few dollops of raspberry jam on top of the lemon curd and swirled it in. 
I feel like the cookie base would be excellent with more almond extract and cherry preserves too.

Followed the recipe but couldn't find lemon curd and for some reason apricot preserves spoke to me. Also, I do not have a stand mixer so used my old school mixer with the double spinning things. Dough was crumbly but when put in the baking pan and pressed down, it mushed together fine and texture was good. I was really worried this wouldn't come out so great with the apricot substitution. It was fantastic.
@Jen D. So glad to see this! I love the apricot idea!
Suggestions for alterations for high altitude?

I’m at 3,500 ft and didn’t have any issues with recipe as written.  King Arthur flour has a wonderful conversion table to high elevation baking. Just go to their website and search for elevation and it will come up.
So easy and delightfully tart on a hot summer day .
I’d love to hear more about Poppy Seeds as an ingredient.  Whats up?

@MigueXo
Lemon & poppyseeds are quite common together in cakes, breads, and cookies. A great combo of flavors.

In the video, Melissa mentions whipping the curd to aerate it before assembly but I don’t see that anywhere in the recipe. I’d like to avoid more dishes from blending or whipping the curd and am guessing it doesn’t make a difference in taste or texture?

@Nina

You don’t need to whip the curd. She was just referring to the recipe she used to make the curd, which does whip it up. It’s not important to this recipe.
These are not what dreams are made of. They are only to okay lemon bars. They require 2.5 hours and lemon curd.
Will a glass baking dish work?
Can these be frozen?
This recipe makes a delicious bar cookie.  Very much like a shortbread texture with the zing of lemony zest.  I followed all instructions as written
Can these be made subbing in 1:1 gluten free flour? Any guidance available?
These were outstanding!!
Without a stand mixer, what might be the best way of attempting this? I have a little electric hand mixer with whisk attachments, but I fear the texture would be all wrong. Any suggestions?
What is a nut-free substitute for the poppy seeds?
Why does it need to bake in a metal pan. I have a glass square pan on hand.

@Mary H.

Give it a try. Your baking time might be different though.
Delicious! Subbed chia seeds for poppy because I didn’t have poppy seeds and it worked.

Wasn't able to finish the recipe as described.   I found that the softened butter was too soft and instead of being able to separate a cup of 'crumbly' dough to apply on top of the lemon curd...I had a blob of dough that simply wasn't good for dropping on top of the curd.  I ended up simply spreading all the dough in the pan & baked w/o curd. I tried to watch video but, for some reason, it won't play.   Insight/tips/ what am I missing???

@Leslie S

Bummer that happened! If that happens again, try throwing topping like that in the freezer for about ten or fifteen minutes.
Oh man is she ever right about the choice pieces being the ends with caramelized curd. A sincere sensation. I mistook black sesame seeds for poppy seeds but still turned out great.
Select your lemon curd with care!  Some are just TOO tart.
I made these exactly as written (using the almond extract option) and they were excellent! Another Melissa Clark winner. So easy and delicious.

Omg, these are the most delicious cookies ever!  
Yummy, and I followed the directions exactly (except I doubled the recipe)!
can these be made ahead and refrigerated?

I’ve made this salad twice now with apple cider vinegar instead of apple cider. Both times by mistake, because I wrote the ingredient list down wrong. But I wouldn’t change it because it’s really good! I can’t imagine not having that zing to the farro now.
Divine! I used a combination of almond extract and lemon extract.
Melissa is my favorite baker to follow and I have my best baked good recipes from her, but I did not like this one. The dough was too savory and had a funny texture.
I used a 9x9 pan and baked for same. Turned out just fine and made smaller squares I cut not seem too tall.
Baking in 350, is that in Fahrenheit or Celsius?
Made today using lemon curd from Trader Joe's, and absolutely delicious.

I cooked down about a cup of frozen blueberries with the juice of a lemon and a bit of sugar while preparing the dough, and layered it on after the curd and before the crumbles. Delicious addition that pairs very nicely with the lemon poppyseed, and ups the tartness.

Made my own curd as I had an abundance of lemons. My dough wasn't super crumbly, not sure why, but still worked. My only add, i'd use a tad more curd as I LOVE lemon :)
Very "shortbread"  like flavor on the dough...a keeper for sure.

I don’t have lemon curd, nor am I inclined to make it.  I would like to make these tonight with what’s in my pantry.  Would a thin layer of fig jam be a reasonable alternative (flavor-wise) for the curd layer?
The directions state to use a metal pan but I only have glass and don’t want to purchase one. Is there an adjustment to baking time or temperature to compensate?

Melt in your mouth burst of flavor. I cut mine into mini bite-sized - pop one in your mouth for an explosion of everything good in this world: butter, lemon, sugar. Nothing overpowers any other flavor, they meld beautifully. You can see the reaction on people's faces as they savor the bite.

I’ve now made them with many different kinds of jams, and this turns out deliciously every time! 

I did put seedy jam through a sieve to avoid having berry seeds with the poppy seeds. Plum, berry, apricot, everything is great!

If I had the right size metal pan, I would use it, but I don’t, and my glass pan works.
A hard no.  Pastry is bland, purchased curd (Bonne Maman) very sharp.  Rose Beranbaum's recipe, or the ubiquitous 70's lemon bars if you want something quicker, are better.
These are so delicious. My only comment is that I cut them into 25 squares instead of 16. They're rich, so they were plenty big. Trader Joe's lemon curd was perfect.
Made these as written and they came out great. Used Trader Joe's curd and, as suggested, cut them into 24 pieces.
Does this need to be refrigerated if  not using it until the next day?

Tasty and the recipe is versatile.  You can swap out the poppyseeds, lemon zest and jar of curd for whatever.  Last night it was almonds, lemon+almond extract and apricot jam with a bit of sour cream because I ran low on butter.  Maybe better than the original.

I've made twice in the last week. First as written. Second with 1/2c more lemon curd - the full jar I had. First time, very caramelly edge bits and not much softness to the center layer of curd. Second time, lemon curd layer was still visible/soft and this is how I'll make them again. Would be a DELIGHT with meyer lemons. Use a sharp knife to slide along edge to free the square from the pan.

I think how this turns out for you will depend on the zing of your lemon curd, and how much sweet/tangy you want. Pre-made curds will be a bit sweeter, homemade can get as zingy as you want. Worked like a charm!  Much better than the first time, when the chunks wound up too refined/smoothed out.  This time looked just like the photo provided!

These bars are delicious and super easy!  Melissa cautions not to overmix the dough, but the recipe has you mixing in the vanilla, then the flour, then the poppyseeds.  In the video, she throws all three in at the same time, which makes MUCH more sense, and really minimizes the risk of overmixing.

Delicious.  I made the lemon curd.  I have never made a curd before and just loved it.  I only made one addition to the bar recipe.  I added chopped pecans to the topping because I'm a nutty gal and can't have too much crunch.

From her article:
 You can substitute other fruit, so long as you adjust the sugar content. For sweet cherries, berries and stone fruit, use about half as much sugar called for. For sour cherries, keep the sugar where it is.

I’ve been growing and eating rhubarb for many decades.  For anyone unfamiliar with cooking with it, the  raw stalks can be red or green.   Once cooked, they fade to a pinkish tan. Ok, i adore rhubarb. Adore it, i tell you. But spoil my perfectly delicious & valuable spring bounty with a thickening agent, coconut flakes, vanilla or rose water, too much sugar, and waste an entire cup of almond butter?? No thanks. 
I harvested my 1st pound of rhubarb yesterday and cooked it up with 1/4 cup of sugar, pinch salt, and a tablespoon of water. Simmered about 8-9 min, until it fell apart and the moisture cooked off, spread it with a knife onto a 9x11" rectangle of puff pastry, baked, cut, and ate. No thickener, perfectly sweet-tart, absolutely delicious.

@Name Goes Here:  I make a rhubarb sauce from a Mark Bittman recipe and use it with fish or chicken, swirl in yogurt, or just eat plain. Place sliced 1 lb rhubarb, 1/3 c sugar, and a pinch of saffron in a small pot over low heat, and cook about 20 minutes (stirring occasionally) until the rhubarb becomes saucy. You can add salt & pepper, but I don’t because I think it’s perfect without it.

I made this a couple nights ago and it was really good. I was surprised how moist and fluffy the cake part is. I used unsweetened flaked coconut and it was plenty sweet for us.  I might try reducing the about of butter in the topping a bit next time.  That’s not for a calorie thing but because it was more runny and I felt like it puddled on the sides a bit (because the cake was a little domed). I was glad I had left the parchment paper go up the sides of the cake (but don’t leave much poking up because that could catch fire under the broiler). Or I might poke holes in the top of the cake or let the topping sit and thicken a bit as the butter cools. Oh, and do try to keep the cake level by walking it up the sides of the pan after you pour the batter in. I didn’t but will next time - and there will be a next time!  
PS - if you don’t know what I mean by walking the batter up the side of the pan: tilt the pan and roll the batter around the entire outside of the pan to create a “high water mark” that gives the cake something to climb on in the oven as it rises. I always do that for tiered cakes and quick breads but didn’t think of it here.
@Rietta Amazing tip, thank you!!
@Rietta thank you for the tip! I never heard of walking the cake. I will try this.

A sweet walk down memory lane for my taste buds in seeing this recipe that is almost exactly that a family friend first baked for us in the my grade school days in the early 60's, JFK was POTUS, and I still have in recipe card form, the sharing 'technology' of the day, in a box in my pantry.  Her recipe used nutmeg but otherwise, pretty darned close.  I am eager to try this comfort-bake recipe and for a little while escape the chaos in the country in the zen of the kitchen.

I just made this and can confirm this is a very good recipe. I temped my melted milk and butter mixture to 150F before pouring into the batter. Note the batter is very runny, but bakes up into a light and fluffy cake. Used unsweetened coconut as there is plenty of brown sugar in the topping. It might be too sweet otherwise. The topping went onto the cake just after it had finished baking. Easy to make with excellent results. Recommend.

Cut the granulated sugar to at most 1/2 cup in the cake. The topping contains 3/4 cup brown sugar, for a ratio of 1 cup flour to 1 1/2 cups sugars.   I cut the brown sugar to 1/2 cup and added chopped walnuts in addition to the coconut (unsweetened). Even though I love Melissa Clark’s recipes you want to taste the cake not just sugar.

This is the cake of my childhood… the one I learned to bake as part of a Girl Scout badge; the one that was whipped up (because it’s fast to make) and taken to new neighbors who just moved in; the one taken to potlucks. It’s homey, delicious, and comforting. I have and treasure my grandmother’s annotated recipe, which is quite similar to this one. Whatever recipe you have or use, I hope everyone will bake this cake soon to share with loved ones. Thanks for resurfacing this oldie-but-goodie!
We used to call this butter crunch cake in 70s home ec!  Sometimes would substitute almond flake for coconut. Delicious then, delicious now!

My mom was a county level politician.  She printed campaign cards with her version of this recipe "Lazy Daisy Oatmeal Cake".  Her version had oats and chopped pecans in the topping. This cake was my childhood favorite in the 1950’s. It was our dessert for family beach picnics, boat rides and other gatherings. I began making it again during Covid and everyone LOVES it!

This cake is from the windswept northern Danish town of Hjallerup. The "Drømmekage," or Dream Cake, emerged in the 1960s from the quick thinking of a local homemaker named Jytte Andersen. Having reportedly forgotten about a baking competition for her homemakers' association, Andersen improvised what would become Denmark's beloved national treat. Today, this unpretentious dessert graces bakery display cases across Denmark, and if someone bakes for a public event odds are it'll be this cake!

I inherited my late father-in-law's recipe box and this recipe was used with his famous pineapple  upside down cake.
