Pour batter on top and bake for 30-35 minutes.
Oh, we ate this endlessly when I was growing up! A version of it was on the back of the Bisquick box!  I am so tickled to have the recipe!
I brought this into my husband tonight in a little bowl with tea. He looked at me with hopeful eyes and said “Cake?” I said “Yes kid. Cake.”  The cake is heavenly.
I have celiac disease. I followed the recipe exactly but substituted Bob's Red Mill 1to1 Gluten free Baking Flour. It was delicious!
@Mary Carbone Yay thank you for testing and sharing!
My mother used corn flakes instead of coconut because many of us kids didn’t like coconut at the time.
Dear home cook...I don't think it would come out the same if you substitute but if you have some time to test it out, make both versions: 1 with whole milk and one with with almond milk. But I

Wow! Delicious and one of our new favorites. You must love coconut, for sure, as the topping brings a big bang to this cake. I used unsweetened coconut flakes as that's all I buy.  The topping, once broiled and cooled, firms up and tastes almost like caramel.  The vanilla cake was perfect and would be a great cake on its own.  Overall, a winner for sure.
Love it. Very easy. I did use less sugars than suggested and it was still quite sweet. I burned the coconut topping (even watching it) but it is still tasty.
Has anyone tried making this using gluten free one-to-one flour?
My grandmother passed down this depression area recipe to my mother and me. It was always my favorite birthday cake. Buttercream can't compare...
This is the Hot Milk Sponge of my childhood, I’m 83. Tastes buttery even though only 2 tbs in cake. Happy to be reminded to make it again.

Making this recipe is the worst thing I have ever done because now I’m going to polish off an entire cake BY MYSELF in a day. Unbelievable. I didn’t think cake could be so good. Rich and sweet but moist and airy.

A recipe fr a lazy cake that was seems to have been lazily written. I was left with many questions. Examples: “beat in flour”…all at once? A bit at a time? And,, why am i beating in the vanilla at the end so as to pummel the batter even more? And…Am i meant to de-pan to cool? (I can’t see how) or does this get served from the pan. THere were a couple others but i will leave it at that.
Can it be made with gluten free flour?
When I started reading all the nostalgic reviews I put away the stand mixer and got out my 5-speed Sunbeam Mixmaster in harvest gold. I have no regrets.
@Elena Totally! My 95  yo Mom still has hers. I have one from the 80s that is partially broken but still beats just fine. I bought a back up beater a decade ago that I have yet to use!
Will this recipe work without coconut or is there a substitution I can use? Thank you.
You might try topping the cake with a streusel made by combining sugar, flour, rolled oats, chopped nuts, and melted butter. Google "streusel topping" for detailed recipes.W

I just made this (twice already) and WOWZA, super easy and so delicious. I used a tsp of cardamom and vanilla. I also used unsweetened coconut based on others comments about it possibly being too sweet. I like the nostalgia that this recipe holds. Life was simpler, and this cake represents that with how easy it was to make!
the equivalent of a 9' round pan would be an 8" square pan.
@Ted Wow, that is helpful, thank you! The photo shows cake squares so they should have mentioned that.

Our grandmother , who passed away at 101, made this cake on a regular basis.  I watched her as a child and she never used a recipe.  It looked so simple and it seemed like when we went to her house she'd always have a fresh one waiting for us.  Thanks for bringing back one of my beautiful memories from the 60s.  Now I need to make it for my family.  She called it her broiled coconut cake 8x8 pan always
it's called "Bienenstich" (beesting) in Germany. it's also very popular (and imo better) made with almond flakes.
This was delicious as-is, no need to mess with the recipe.
Memo to self: Don't bake this in a silicone pan.
I substituted brown sugar for coconut sugar and swapped out the shredded coconut for flaked almonds. Let it cool in the pan and will slice up. YUM!

I just made this cake following the recipe exactly (at least I thought I did!) but it did not rise.  The thin cake looked normal and tasted great.  Any thoughts in Bakeland?
Thanks!

@Lous One commenter mentioned that he/she felt the cake was too flat when made in a 9” square pan. Others also mentioned that a 9” square pan was too big based on squared area. They recommended an 8” square pan or a 9” round which both have basically the same surface area. Hope this helps. I’m going to use an 8” square when I make this.
No way that cake lasts 3 days in my house. More like 3 hrs...

Wow, I just made this. Couldn't resist cutting into it while still warm. The cake is so tender, topping a bit sweet, but delicious. I used a non-stick pan, which I buttered. No need for parchment paper.

Thought I had coconut for the topping but didn’t so substituted in sliced almonds and finely chopped dried apricots. Husband didn’t know any better and loved the result. Said it was so good I could sell this cake. Lol

Planning to make this as a birthday cake for a friend. But I have a question about Step 3. After describing how to heat the butter and milk, there is a statement, "Pour the hot mixture into the eggs, beating briefly until you have a smooth, runny batter." The step before that (Step 2) describes beating the eggs and sugar and then adding dry ingredients. So...there are  more ingredients than just eggs in the bowl at this point (Step 3). Can someone clarify? Is this just an error in the recipe?
It should say “pour the hot mixture into the egg mixture.”

Well... I think I may be the only one who found this recipe WAY too greasy and sweet! (There is one other reviewer who may have said something similar.) The cake itself was fine. But the topping with 1/2 cup butter and 3/4 cup brown sugar was much TOO MUCH! And I used unsweetened coconut, so that is not what contributed to the excessive sweetness of this cake. Perhaps people's opinions regarding this cake are overshadowed by memories of Home Economics class, which I never especially enjoyed.

I made this for an Easter potluck and everyone was loving it. I used cardamom instead of vanilla and I made it vegan by using Earth Balance for butter, and ground flaxseed instead of eggs 
(1tbsp   3 tbsp water = 1 egg)
Amazingly my  mother used to make this cake.  in the 1950's.   I thought I had the recipe but what I had written didnt work.   time to try it again.
Good flavor. I too thought the cake thin. Next time I will bake it in 8” square glass pan so I can skip parchment and cut and serve from pan
@Meidana You might want to be careful with a glass pan when you broil the topping. I believe the glass will shatter under the high heat.
Extra lazy would be to use a yellow cake mix and add this frosting!
@Hazel but it wouldn’t taste at all the same! I suppose you could try adding cardamom to the cake mix.

I really enjoyed this! I made a few adaptations: cake: steeped 5 green cardamom pods in my milk and butter mixture, used coconut sugar instead of regular sugar. Topping: no sugar, 150 grams condensed coconut milk, stick of butter (same), 1/2 cup heavy cream, dash of coconut extract, sweetened shredded coconut, 5 cardamom pods(removed before I added the coconut)

So yummy and easy!

This is the second time I have made this since the recipe appeared a few weeks ago. Sadly, I did not have coconut the first time (used oatmeal) but wanted to try again as it should be. Having said that, I did not make the full quantity of the icing as it was a bit rich for my liking. A little schmear goes a long way sweetness-wise. The other trick up my sleeve that I used was repurposing my checking for doneness wooden skewer stick to poke holes in the cake before adding the topping so it drips down in. It was so much better the second time I am sure there will be a third, fourth and so on.

Just made this yet again.  Will I ever stop?  Nope!  Everyone who tries it loves it.  So easy, so good.  This time, blasphemous as it may be, I stirred in chocolate chips and used pecans in the place of coconut.
I made this with Bob’s Red Mill Measure for Measure Gluten Free flour for my son with Celiac, and it turned out amazing! Definitely a good recipe for that substitution if you need it.

Home ec! Two memories: bro g laughed at for washing fruit with dish soap but then triumphing when my baked Alaska was the only one that didn't melt.

My 5th grade grandson and I have been cooking together for years and I think this recipe might be our next attempt.

I was not familiar with this cake and since it sounded so yummy read the comments before tackling it.  Decided to cut back on the sugar as one of the commentators suggested - great idea!  And because I couldn't figure out to I would get the cake with all of the "goo" out of the pan I used a springform pan, well greased, etc.  The cake is perfection and easy peasy!
I wish I'd used a springform pan. Great idea!
First of all, this cake rocks. Second of all, I added a tablespoon of rum to the batter in place of vanilla/cardamom.
This cake won't last 3 days anywhere.  It barely lasted 30 minutes in our house.  We couldn't stop eating it.  BEST CAKE EVER.  And so easy to make.

So delicious and so easy to make. Made it for a dinner party last night and everyone loved it. I added a few drops of almond extract along with the vanilla and it worked nicely. Batter doesn’t seem like much when it goes in the pan but puffs up beautifully in the pan. Will definitely be making this again many times!
I always wonder how NYers with tiny kitchens and stoves with broilers on the bottom not in the oven (me) are supposed to broil anything in a pan.
Delicious! I made the version with vanilla but may try cardamom next time! I didn’t have a 9x9 pan so I doubled the recipe and used a 9x13
Tasty cake! I used an 8" square pan, as I don't have a 9" one.  The area of an 8" pan is 64 sq. inches, and the area of a 9" round cake pan is 63 2/3" - roughly the same.
Fanny Farmer’s 11th edition has this cake, too, in the one bowl cakes section. She called it Lightning Cake. One of our family’s fave fast cakes🤍
Delicious little cake, the cardamom really distinguishes it. I used 1tsp cardamom + 1tsp vanilla, baked it in a springform pan, and used unsweetened coconut in the topping.
I really (really) dislike coconut.  Has anyone tried a substitution for the coconut?  Maybe sliced or flaked almonds?  Other suggestions?
My mother-in-law’s recipe from the 40’s says coconut or chopped nuts.  I too would try almonds.  Love this cake!
@Crystal. Chopped pecans are delicious too!
With a drizzle of chocolate on top, this would be a Somoa cookie cake. Yummmy.
I used buttermilk as opposed to whole milk. Worked great.
@Adam thanks for the tip. I have some buttermilk to use up and I love a good buttermilk cake!

Easy and delicious. Don't be afraid to brown the top well. Mine took more than 1-4 minutes, though I did not raise the rack, and did not achieve color in photo. Was chewy, not brittle and crunchy but sill very good. Will raise rack to broil next time.
Tasty and easy to prepare.  Will definitely make it again.

This was such a hit as a birthday cake for my grandma’s 84th birthday! I added fresh berries around the rim of the cake and the tartness of the berries nicely balanced the sweetness of the cake. I will definitely make this again!

I grew up loving Lazy Daisy Cake which my Mother would make regularly. A simple school night dessert.  However this recipe is not that... This recipe which I made as written,  is far too sweet and too much fat.  The topping needs half the butter at most and the entire recipe could be half the sugar. I roasted and ground the cardamon and glad I only used 1/2 tsp instead of the 1 tsp .  Plenty strong. I happen to like cardamon but most children  would not .

Such a great cake! The cake is tender and delicious. I accidentally put TWO teaspoons of cardamom in the cake. It was a great mistake. Tasted marvelous. It is definitely hard to cut because of the crusty, delicious topping. Oh, well! Next time I will add chopped pecans.The grandchildren loved it. We all did!

Hi, I just wrote the adaptation for wheat free, dairy free, refined sugar free version - and I see now that the photo shows much higher risen fluffy cake. I will try adding half again the baking powder next time, or maybe doubling it another time - could be the weight of the brown coconut sugar (and/or the ingredients in the Cadia vegan butter) weighed it down - it will never be "fluffy", the one I made was flatter and more dense - but very delicious!

@Kimberly
More likely the wheat-freeness was the culprit there. It may never get fluffy but if it tastes good and meets your needs it is still a success!
Delicious. Made Gluten free by weighing my GF flour out. Used flaked coconut instead of shredded

Just made this again and couldn’t be happier with the result.  Made as written, with one exception:  no coconut.  No one in my home is a fan, so I substituted semi-sweet chocolate chips and pecan pieces.  WOWOWOWOWOWOWOW!  Deeeeeeevine!  I’ll make this again.  So easy, so simple, so perfect.
Super easy and super satisfying….added pecans to topping.
My grandmother’s copy of this recipe uses cream for the topping. Even more gooey and yummy.

This is really something special. So easy, soft springy cake, buttery toasted coconut, and all it takes is an hour and pantry staples. This has become my go-to "there are no treats in the house but I don't want to work very hard" baked good

Sounds a lot like the "Bisquick Velvet Crumb Coffee Cake" I made many, many times
through the 60's 70's and 80's!! Now that I no longer use Bisquick, I'm excited to
make it from scratch instead :) Thanks NYT,  this recipe makes a nostalgic reader happy!
Any recommendations on substitutions for the shredded coconut when baking for folks with nut allergies?
I would confirm with those who are allergic that they can't eat coconut. Technically it's a seed, not a nut (parent of allergic kid here)

@Alison L

 - coconut is not a nut but an edible fruit of the coconut palm, a tree of the palm family.

My reaction to "cardamom (optional)"?

LOL, not optional if you're Danish! (or had ancestors who were.)

I really want to make this, but I don't want to eat a whole cake in 4 days. I need a half-size baking pan. 32 square inches. where's my ruler?
Could add some pecans to the topping?

This cake is so easy and delicious. I used unsweetened coconut for the topping and I think this is the right move. In addition, I browned the butter for the topping and that is worth doing. I added a touch more salt because I love a salty savory topping. I weighed all of the ingredients and this made mixing much easier. I added vanilla and cardamom because why not? I live at 7,000 feet of altitude so I made the following adjustments: 3 eggs, 3/4 tsp of baking powder, 1 tablespoon more of flour, and 1 tablespoon of additional milk in the batter. Highly recommend making, so delicious.
I followed the instructions exactly, using a 9” round pan, and the cake tasted good, but was almost as flat as a pancake 🤷‍♀️
This was so easy and is delicious if not a bit sweet with the topping.  But I am not a very good baker so love this easy-peasy recipe.  Oldies are often goodies!

This was such an easy, and tasty, cake! Used  a 9 inch square pan, sweetened coconut (because that's what I had) and broiled from the second highest rack placement,  so not the closest, but close enough and took 5 minutes. I could have lived with unsweetened coconut/less sweet topping, but my husband loved it with the sweet. I will try nuts in the topping next time - almonds would be nice. The cake was a delicate crumbs, could be good by itself.
Bake 22-32 minutes. Can someone please narrow down the time? Do I start lightly pressing at 22 minutes and every minute thereafter?

It depends on your oven.  Turn it on well before you put the cake in, to be sure the temp is consistent, in the whole oven.  I generally start at the shortest time, and then add in 2-3 minute increments, while testing.  Once you think it's close, a clean toothpick confirms it's done.
@Karen agree it depends on your oven. I have found mine to be a little “slow” and started testing at 30 minutes; the cake was done at 32.

My family (3 adults, 2 kids) ate the entire cake in one go. My son in law was on a 'cheat day' so that's his excuse, but no excuse for the rest of us, except that the cake is utterly delicious. Even with the singed coconut
Fantastic. This is greater than the sum of its parts and is ridiculously simple to make. A new Go-To. Five stars!
My late father-in-law had a 3x5 index card called Hot milk cake, but used it as a base for pineapple upside down cake. I make it for my husband when he is missing family.

@Ann Marie

I love that ♥️
The best part of cooking is this, right here.
This was absolutely delicious but I found it a bit too sweet. I’d half the sugar, both in the cake and the icing the next time.

I followed the recipe as written and it was excellent. My crowd, though, is not a huge fan of coconut. When I made it a second time, I omitted the coconut altogether and the topping melded into a thin, brittle layer of caramel over the delicate, spongy cake. Perfection! I honestly can’t decide whether I like it better with or without the coconut. I’d better make it again to help me decide. 

(This is also great recipe if you have a juvenile sous chef hanging around in need of something to do)
Made this exactly according to the recipe and used vanilla extract. Amazing ! It’s now one of my favorite.
I really don't like shredded coconut so I used finely copped walnuts instead.

Made to recipe, this cake is flat and overly sweet and greasy. Made to Fanny Farmer recipe and it’s delicious, fluffy, but still really sweet. For my third time, I’ll stick with Fannie Farmer and pull back on the sugar.
Do you remove the cake from the pan while it is cooling? Is it frosted while still in the pan?
I believe you would leave it in the pan since it goes back under the broiler.
Take the cake out of the oven, apply the frosting and put back under the broiler for a few minutes until browned and bubby. No need to cool the cake first. This is how I did it and it worked great.

After you bake the cake, remove it from the 350 degree oven and switch your oven to broil. Spread your topping on the just baked cake, and pop it back in the oven under the broil setting. Watch it carefully. Mine took 4 minutes. Then allow the cake to cool in the pan on a wire rack.  Once it begins to cool, the topping firms up into a delicious almost caramel like topping.  Slice and serve from the pan.  No need to remove the entire cake from the pan.
do you all think the recipe can be doubled and baked in 2 separate pans?
Is the instruction to add scalded milk to egg mixture missing something? Shouldn’t it just read add to batter as you have already added the dry ingredients?  I am confused.

Phillip D - Re your question about this in step 3" "Pour the hot mixture into the egg, beating...," you are interpreting this correctly that it means to add the warm milk and butter mixture into the batter of eggs, sugar, flour etc you created in step 2.

Oh my. This cake is absolutely delicious and so easy to make. 

I keep reading in the comments how many folks made this cake in home economics in prior decades. It makes sense to me because the recipe while easy, explores a few different cooking/baking techniques. 

This will end up being a frequently made recipe in my house.

I love the idea of walking the cake up! I didn't& it atull turnedout perfect (:  I did only use 1/4 cup of butter for the topping,(I left the other half of the stick of butter on the counter by accident while momming!) I'mglad nkw as it is not running & has a nice goeyness too it! @Rietta

Anyone have any idea of how to add a touch of chocolate.  Yummy cake the reminded me of the Samoa Girl Scout Cookie (I think those have two names maybe Caramel Delight in other states?). Anyway those have a chocolate component too.  Chocolate chops in batter would melt in the hot milk I think. Maybe pressed into the topping right after removal from oven?

Made this today in a 9” square pan. Cake baked up fluffy but only about an inch tall. What rise did y’all get in the square pan? Otherwise it was lovely! Used cardamom and subbed oats in the topping based on suggestions in comments (since we have a coconut hater at our house).
Mine was about 2" tall. I did what another commenter suggested, which is "walk the batter up the sides of the pan" before I put it in the oven. I think it helped the cake climb/rise up the sides.

Delicious! I used about 1tsp of cardamom instead of the vanilla, as someone else had suggested, and also added zest of 1 lemon to the batter. For the topping, I used sliced almonds and some pecans, a bit of grated nutmeg, and only about half of the suggested amount of butter.
Shredded coconut is extremely not cared for in my house... would this work with, say, slivered almonds instead? or chopped pecans?
Yes, see "Swedish Almond Cake" by Dorie Greenspan on this site.

Made this today, never had it before so followed the recipe exactly the first time, and think there's an error with the topping ingredients.  Do you really mean for a 1/2 cup (8 T.) of butter or a 1/2 stick (4 T.) melted with the brown sugar and coconut?  Delicious but what a greasy mess! 
In looking through the comments I note someone referred to the Fannie Farmer recipe (Marion Cunningham revision); that recipe calls for just 1 T. butter in the batter and only 3 T. for the topping.
@Dan I cut way down on the topping amount. The first time I made it, it was a little too much for me, over the top indulgent and not necessary.

@Dan I think you need to let the topping mixture cook longer. I thought it seemed heavy on the butter when I first began to heat it up, but once it began to boil, the butter incorporated completely with the sugar and milk.

To save time I used a boxed yellow butter cake mix in a 9x13 pan and made the topping recipe - but used the entire 12oz bag of coconut.  The topping was thin, but I think that helped as it wasn't overly sweet.  The office loved it.

Unfortunately, I seem to be in agreement with those who do NOT have a nostalgic memory of this cake. Yes, it is easy, and it's edible, but it wasn't delicious. The cake is a bit dry and the topping is too sweet, even after I cut the amount of brown sugar in it. Won't be making this again.
I made this dairy free - substituted homemade oat/coconut milk and used vegetable oil instead of butter. I added grated dates to the dried coconut topping and it was incredible. Great recipe.

So stinking GOOD!! I think I had a piece of this cake every time I walked into the kitchen. The coconut caramel is everything you could ever want and the cake comes together so fast and easy. Perfection!!

Loved this. We had tons and tons of fresh raspberries, it's that season, so I added a raspberry/lemon juice/confectioners slurry on top of the cooked cake, under the coconut topping, then broiled. Everyone loved it!
I’m allergic to coconut, but will try it with just the brown sugar and butter on top. I’m at high altitude so will look for those adjustments.

So excited to make this cake today that I remember so fondly from my childhood. Recipe is great.  Turned out fantastic.  One change I made.. where it calls for 2 tsps Vanilla, I added 1 tsp Vanilla and 1 tsp Fiori di Sicilia.  Gave it a wonderful orange/vanilla flavor.  Delicious!

I love this cake, I have made it on numerous occasions. One thing though, I struggled to get a consistent result with the topping.. My  Scooby-Doo senses told me that the weight and the measurement were not equal. Can confirm that in my case one and a quarter cup of coconut weighs a mere  55 g,, much less than the weight stated  in the recipe. Mystery solved!! I will continue to make with confidence and spread the joy that is this delicious cake!

Made this for my husband’s birthday almost as written, and it was fluffy and delicious. Used cake flour because I had some on hand, and 2% milk. Skipped the parchment paper and just buttered and floured the pan. Instead of using a pot on the stove, I heated the milk and butter, and made the topping, in a glass measuring cup in the microwave. 

Will make this again - low effort for a very good return.

Not sure why this has five stars (I gave it three). I used room temperature eggs and followed the recipe closely but the cake was dense and flat. Also, heed the advice of others: cut down on the butter and sugar! I should have trusted my instinct that a whole stick poured over the top would be excessive and greasy. If this is what “home economics” teaches, I’m glad I never had to take it.

@Jenna M
If that happens, always check the expiration date on your leavening agent .

The version I often made in the 60s had oatmeal in the cake and also in the topping. I seem to remember adding the topping when the cake was nearly baked. By the time the cake was done the topping was also cooked and bubbling. My two young children loved it !

This is a family favorite. I poke holes in the cake before spreading the coconut/butter frosting on top to let some of that buttery topping seep into the cake. This recipe is so easy and so delicious!

I have made this cake several times.  It is easy to make, and a favorite in our house.  The cake is tender and moist, and the topping elevates it to "special".  Lasts for several days well covered, at room temp.  Also easy to increase measurements proportionately for a larger cake. Tip: Be careful when you brown the topping!  It browns very quickly.

This is a treasured recipe that my Grandmother used to make for us when we were visiting as kids.  We have always called it Hot Milk Sponge Cake.  The recipe, on an old recipe card of my mother's has been passed around the family and is easily our favorite dessert.  We do have one difference in the recipe, in that our grandmother always used cream when preparing the topping rather than whole milk.  Either probably works, but I think the cream might provide a slighter richer taste.

I didn’t have high expectations for this cake yet was absolutely delicious!  Super simple to make and the coconut topping on top of a surprisingly springy, fluffy cake was incredible!  I checked the cake at 18 min and pulled it from oven at 20, added topping and broiled.  Perfection!
New baker here: can someone explain how to serve with the parchment paper on the bottom of the pan.  Doesn’t it get in the way when you cut wedges and serve?
LakeEffect, see step 7--you'll cool on a rack and the parchment will stay behind in the pan:)

I would forget the parchment paper completely. Even the suggestion to use it like a sling doesn’t address the problem that you won’t be able to remove the paper from the bottom of the cake without first turning the cake upside down. I made the mistake once of using parchment paper on the bottom of a cake that couldn’t be turned over  -  the paper ended up wrinkling and tearing and was a real pain.

@JudyF
I used parchment on the bottom of the pan. Once the cake was cooled, it was quite easy to turn it out of the pan, peel off the parchment and set it on a serving tray. I find it much easier to cut and portion out this way.
I have made it under the name Lazy Days Cake. Maybe it’s considered lazy because the butter and sugar is not creamed together but rather the butter is melted in hot milk.

My grandmother used to make this as a quick coffee cake when she didn’t have her famous poppyseed rolls or apple or cheese strudel available. I’m almost positive she used bisquik as the star ingredient. Still remember that topping. I think she added chopped walnuts if they were available.
Doesn’t matter what this cake is called, I call it amazingly delicious. I’ve been needing an easy dessert and this will be in the queue from now on
I want to make this esp the broiled topping but I just do not like coconut - any good ideas for substitutions?
You can used chopped walnuts or sliced almonds.

My Grandma had a Lazy Dazy Cake recipe so I thought I would see if it was the same as this one.  Her cake is rather different (involves Quaker Oats soaked in boiling water and cinnamon and nutmeg among other differences).  The icing is more similar to this one, but uses evaporated milk and includes chopped nuts (which means pecans in my grandma's recipes).  I guess that’s the difference between a Daisy and a Dazy!😂

Fantastic, easy cake. Use a touch less sugar.  And yes, this is my norwegian M-i-L's "Hot Milk Cake" recipe... in the hand-typed booklet, the description mentions that it's 'easy enough to bake for unexpected company'. It's a favorite in our house; it's our 'weeknight birthday cake' go-to.
Mom used chopped walnuts instead of coconut.  A real staple for family gatherings.

Broil the topping!  (Watch closely; it should bubble but not burn.) I've been making this "Broiler Cake", from my mother's recipe for over 60 years. A small cake is much better for a single person or couple who should not polish off a whole layer cake while it's fresh. We serve it with vanilla ice cream.

Started making this cake in the early 90’s. The recipe from The New Basics Cookbook called the recipe Helen’s Coconut Cake. It’s a family favorite and I e had so many requests for it from people who’ve “never had anything like it”. So delicious!

I learned to make this (as a kid) from the recipe on the Bisquick box. They called it Velvet Crumb Cake. After forgetting about it for years, it resurfaced in my memory in the past few months—easy and yummy. Bisquick makes it really easy, but this recipe looks pretty simple, too.

Like other commenters, I fondly remember my mom making this cake for my birthdays 50-60 years ago. I like this recipe's call for a 350 degree oven, which is more forgiving than her 400 degrees. She used to add chopped pecans instead of coconut to the topping, which was delightful. This recipe is a keeper.
What can you substitute for coconut if you dont like coconut?
I bet you could do.... chopped pecans or walnuts.
Chocolate ganache with nuts maybe!
15 seconds in the microwave restored it to the original warm yumminess
Easy and everyone liked it. Less sugar would be good. Also I found the cake quite firm.

I’m pretty sure I remember this cake, or a similar one, from a recipe on the back of the Bisquick box in the 60’s.  Does anyone else remember this, or is it just me?  It was the first cake I ever made and it was delicious. I can’t wait to try this recipe.
This looks very much like the Bisquick Velvet Crumb Cake.
Wanted to make this but had no cocoanut. Toasted some chopped pecans and substituted those instead. Goodness. What a taste treat!

Made as written, this is great. The cake changes character over time. After cool down, slices are a bit dense, then 12 to 24 hours later, they become lighter. Not a criticism, just part of the fun. Liked by all family members.

I'd love it if you would list the ingredients in the order they are used. Doing a "mis en place", I had to measure 2 tablespoons of butter, then search down the list for the milk to heat it with. What is your current logic for ordering ingredients?
Delicious, but I would halve the topping next time.
Can anyone advise baking time at High Altitude?

I just baked the cake at 7000 feet altitude, and it came out fine. Made the usual recipe adjustment of slightly less baking powder. Didn't need to reduce the butter. I think it was done at 25 minutes. Wasn't longer than the top time given, for sure.

OMG—I didn’t screw it up!!

By “transfer” what is meant is put the cake in its pan on a wire rack to cool. This cake is typically cut into squares while in the pan after cooling and served from the pan—no need to remove it from the pan! :-)

@Intrigued. I would suggest making a sling with parchment paper. When you line the pan, extend the parchment paper up opposite ends, long enough that you can use the parchment to pick up the cake when it’s done. This technique is also very handy for things that tend to stick.

Danger! Parchment sticking out at the top is likely to catch fire when you put the cake under the broiler to cook the icing.

@Lois A couple of things come to mind:
1. Your baking powder might be old. Check the "best by" date on the can and consider it an expiration date - baking powder is not expensive to replace, and a recipe fail due to tired baking powder is a heartbreaker (ask me how I know...)
2. Eggs for a cake have to be room temperature, even if it doesn't specify that in the recipe.  If your eggs are cold out of the fridge when it's time to start the batter, cover the eggs with warm tap water for about 10 minutes. If the cake needs separated eggs, you can warm the whites and the yolks in separate bowls placed carefully in a larger container of warm water. I learned this trick from the lovely folks at the King Arthur Baking Hotline.
3. Finally, keep a reliable oven thermometer on hand. Just because the oven says it's reached 350 degrees doesn't mean it's so (my oven lies).

Thanks very much for those tips - all really good and always helpful!  

Coincidently, I saw your reply after taking my 2nd attempt out of the oven. I followed the Fannie Farmer leads in the messages and checked my copy, sure enough, mystery solved! What a trip down memory lane this recipe is.  This was the go-to cake in our house when I was growing up in the 1950s: quick, easy, and inexpensive.  I haven't made it in years, but when I get home from vacation I will certainly be making it.
Such a great cake! Super quick, simple in-the-pantry ingredients. Love the texture contrast. Used unsweetened coconut.

i just made a huge mistake with this cake and it still came out delish.  i didn’t make sure to read the entire recipe thoroughly.  i thought i had the cake part down and only skimmed the topping.  while i was mixing and adding ingredients i only added 60 ml of milk to the cake instead of 118 ml.  i had popped the cake into the oven and was reading the topping portion when i realized what i had done.  i thought the batter looked great! well it still turned out tasty.  maybe would have been a bit fluffier with more milk but it was good! also - i didn’t have coconut so i used almonds.  next time i’ll use a little less milk in the topping - and a little more in the cake. but really - very forgiving tasty recipe!

What an utterly delicious, easy cake! Made mine with BOTH vanilla and freshly ground cardamom, why choose!  
The cake part  is almost identical to lovely French tea cakes called Madeleines, if you skip the coconut topping BUT use the butter in the cake instead. Dry ingredients, then butter, MUST be folded in GENTLY so as not to deflate the eggs. No milk in recipe. No coconut topping - just a light dusting of icing sugar on cooled cake. A no fancy madeleines pan. Voilà!
Can this be made relatively gluten-free?

This was the cake we always made for Birthdays. I have my Great Grandmothers recipe card. Her recipe called for heavy cream in the frosting instead of milk which gives the frosting a nice consistency.
Made it as written, but remember eating it from a loaf pan as a child. Less cake with more topping this way!
This was really good! I made it with the cardamom, which gave it a slightly exotic twist. Has anyone made it with cardamom AND vanilla? I wonder how that would be.
This icing is also excellent on top of a banana cake, which is how my great grandmother always made it. I think I’ll go make it right now because why not?

Question: If you wanted to omit the coconut (not a fan!), should you adjust the proportions of the other topping ingredients in any way? There's a lot of coconut called for, so I wondered if leaving it out would lead to a runny topping. Would love any suggestions!
Aren't classic square pans 8" and not 9? Anyone use an 8 inch pan?
Square pans come in lots of sizes. The most popular sizes for home baking are: 8-inch, 9-inch, and 10-inch.

Made this based on comments.  Figured if 8th graders in home ec class can make it, I can.  It is very good, light cake in contrast to heavier substantial frosting, it works, and frankly anything with coconut for me is a winner.  Would and will make again.
Easy-peasy and DELICIOUS.

This cake is SPECTACULAR. The texture is superb, one of the best I've had in my 25 years of life, I don't think I'll make a cake any other way again. I halved the recipe (don't yell at me, it was all I could manage) but it was great in a loaf pan. My friends loved it, I can't wait to make it again next weekend!

I like this, especially the topping, but the cake itself had a strong taste of "egginess." Could I have possibly scrambled the egg when I poured in the hot milk? I poured in a stream of milk and whisked quickly, and the batter was smooth. Did I overcook it? Is the egginess just part of the flavor?

I served this to my family and friends the other night after having received it in my inbox. I'd never heard of this cake. I already had all of the ingredients so figured, why not? I'm so glad i made it. Everyone loved it! It was so good. I made as is and it turned out moist and delicious, and the topping hardened up to a nice chewy kind of consistency. Next time I might drop some chocolate chips on the top after taking it out from under the broiler.

My Grandma used to make this cake, but without any coconut. I never learned how to make it, so I was so excited to see this recipe. I tried it today, but I encountered the following issue: once I added the icing and was putting the cake under the broiler, the cake started to peel away from the pan and all the icing seeped down under and around the sides. Anyone else encountered this?Any suggestions on how to keep it from happening when I try it again?
This is so good. Buttery deliciousness! So easy to make.

So easy to make and my grandkids loved it.  Reminds me of the 'hot milk' cake my grandmother made for my generation.  I used dark demerara sugar in topping for an extra rich taste; definitely don't need sweetened coconut as a result.  And used 1 tsp vanilla and 1/2 teaspoon cardamon - worked great.
This was simple to make and delicious. Kept well.
Oh my gosh, this cake is an absolute dream! So easy to make, so soft, fluffy, buttery, coconut-y! Plus, it's easy to throw together with pantry staples. This is going to be a potluck go-to!!!
My mother used to make this cake in the fifties.Now I’m nostalgic for her great baking.
So good! I baked in a round pan and it was delicious but I think I’ll use a square pan next time because cutting small portions will be easier. Truly a delicious cake and topping.
This cake ought to be classified as  a controlled dangerous substance.  It is absolutely addictive.  Delicious!

I was unsure about using the paper but did so anyway. It turned out ok but I can forsee problems so will try it next time without. when I transferred the cake to another container, I took it off. I didn't use the cardamom. Everyone enjoyed it. Was just enough for a light dessert.
Cake has a wonderful texture, but tooth-achingly sweet.

I was excited to bake this recipe because I remember eating it as a child in the ‘70s. Either this isn’t the same recipe or my standards have changed because I don’t understand all the outstanding reviews. But I cook for food snobs. I would rate this as “okay”. The cake is bland and not fluffy.

Maybe nostalgia plays into the love of this cake because this is a 2-star recipe. I could certainly imagine this recipe written in cursive on a recipe card. From the photo, I recall eating this in the '70s and I thought it was better back then but I was a child. The cake is bland but the topping is good, it didn't taste too sweet. The 22-32 minute baking time range is ridiculous and too imprecise. I would prefer a better cake with a softer, moist crumb, usually more complex and precise recipes.
Great recipe. I should have used a bit larger pan, pan. The 9" square wasn't big enough to hold all the delicious topping.

I made this cake for Easter and it was a big hit. One really needs to enjoy coconut -- there are so many people who don't care for it. But for coconut lovers like myself and my family, this cake was out of this world! I was so grateful for Melissa Clark's instructions and hints. I'm not much of a baker but this cake made me look like a pro!
Yum. This is also called a Queen Elizabeth Cake (at least here in Canada) although I have no idea why!

First time baker here, so pardon my ignorance. What does “butter the pan” mean? what does “best in flour” mean? 
Can I skip parchment paper and the topping all together ? 
Can I add cranberries to the batter ?
Thank you all!!
@Mark butter the pan is so that the cake doesn’t stick to the baking sheet, cranberries would be a very interesting addition. Good luck!
This recipe brings back memories of my mother’s baking skills.  The smell, texture and taste were perfect.

Substituted with thai coconut milk, both batter and topping and it does wonder. The topping came out a bit caramel-ish with some nice toasted coconut flakes. I only had salted butter so I opted out the amount of salt listed on the recipe, and thought it might be on a salty side for the cake, but! the saltiness picks up the sweet coconut-flavor nicely.
My grandma from rural Nebraska made this and 4 generations of her family continues to make and enjoy this recipe. It’s quick to make and most ingredients are on hand other than coconut.
Oh my goodness, this was out of this world! It melted in my mouth like a dream. I followed the recipe exactly, and it came out beyond perfect. My family loved it!

This is the best cake and so easy to make. It's better the second day, the top's caramelized frosting seems to sink into the cake. It also freezes great! My aunt and mom made this in the fifties and sixties. I make it for family parties.
Excellent !!!

My husbands’s childhood favorite so when I stumbled across it in the NYT I had to bake it for his birthday. (I lost his mom’s recipe). He loved it but the cake did not rise as expected. Not sure why…but it would have been better had it been light and fluffy.

@pforsyth I’ve made this twice now (both with GF flour) and it doesn’t rise much. I think the picture is a real close up. That said the second time I made sure the eggs were really room temp and added a half tsp more baking powder and the rise was better 1.5” as opposed to 1” the first time.
This was amazing. I used coconut milk instead of dairy and it was perfection.

Just had this cake in Copenhagen at Juno Bakery…so delicious!  The cake was seasoned lightly with cardamom (of course) and incredibly tender. They made the cake in a loaf pan, which I had never seen for this cake coming from the U.S.  Now to be able to re-create perfection once home
My cake did not rise
so yummy!
This seems like my kind of thing, but there are some comments about it not rising. I might add extra leavening if I make it. Will report back if I do

wow, just wow! thought sweetened coconut would be too sweet and I'm glad I used unsweetened coconut "shreds". I also prefer vanilla paste to vanilla extract. can't wait to try this with cardamom. keeper for sure!
Legendary Danish cake!

This was difficult and did not really work. The bottom burned before the top was properly browned. I had to turn it over and cut off the burned bottom. Also, the overall cake, topping and cake itself, is very, very sweet! I finally squeezed some fresh lemon juice and sprinkled some lemon zest directly onto the cake topping to ameliorate the sweetness. This helped a bit and made it a lemon coconut affair but did not really fix the overall problem of too sweet, too fussy cake. Thanks!

I used Bob’s Red Mill Egg Replacer, except instead of using water in the egg replacer I used liquid whipping cream (I believe it’s called heavy cream if you are American). This allowed the “eggs” and the sugar to whip up nicely inland build volume. It came out DELICIOUS!

Any thoughts of adding blueberries into the batter?  Helllllllp.
I love this cake and have made it a thousand times, but got nervous about the berries.  Need to use em or lose em.
@Stan That’s a hard no for me.
Much better the second day. The coconut icing seems to melt into the cake, making it even better. It also freezes well!
I’d put less sugar next time. I’m not used to so much since I started a clean diet 3 months ago, but if you want to indulge and likes coconut, this is a wonderful cake.
So easy to bake and so yummy?! If you’re not a big fan of coconut maybe add less than what the recipe suggests, but overall simply loved it!

Not sure what I did incorrectly as once I finished baking cake had great loft (2-3”) then added coconut frosting to broil. Cake collapsed to about 3/4”.Not much cake but tasty.  Thought I followed directions per written
Good!  I subbed one very large duck egg for the two chicken eggs.
my stove doesn't have a broiler!  Any other way to finish the topping?
Has anyone tried this as cupcakes?
Where do you get a 9-inch square pan? Maybe a specialty kitchen shop?
@Caryl I certainly don’t have one! I’m making this today and planning to use my 7 inch square plus a loaf pan. The area should match a 9 inch square.

@Caryl
I got mine on Amazon

This was the perfect recipe for a lazy afternoon. The process was fun while still being easy, hitting all the high notes of holiday baking without any of the hurdles. Added both the vanilla and the cardamom, and the resulting sponge was delicious and fragrant. Will be sneakily serving it for breakfast to my kids, and I’m sure they’ll be no complaints.

This didn't exactly work the first time I made it, but with modifications the second time around, it turned out great. I found the coconut mixture to be too dry the first time, and as a result it wouldn't adhere to the cake. It really just slipped off with every slice. To fix, I used slightly less coconut (75g) and doubled the milk amount to make a saucier caramel. While I baked this in a 9" round pan, I opted for slicing into squares as opposed to wedges to help keep the coconut on the cake.

Made this with unsweetened coconut and sprinkled some chopped walnuts on top before hitting the broiler.  As readers wrote, it's really important to keep an eye on things from there. Next time I might incorporate chopped dried pineapple into the cake batter.

This is one of 2 cakes that  were the first I ever learned to make while growing up on a farm in central Nebraska in the 60s and 70s. Still a favorite and the broiled topping is the best part. We also used a caramel frosting when we didn't want to fire up the broiler in the middle of summer.
Do you have to broil the topping? I don’t have a broiler.
Perhaps you could use a torch as for creme brulee?

I am so excited to try making this cake for my family, but my husband won't even try something that has coconut in it...would it be sacrilege to substitute the topping, and how would one recommend doing that?
@Cherri Perhaps chopped pecans?
My mother made a version of this using heavy cream and we called it Sweet Cream Coconut Cake.  It was a favorite and I look forward to trying it again.

I first encountered this recipe in my 60"s version of Fanny Farmer Cookbook. For years I have been making it without the topping ( but I sometimes do) for a delicious strawberry cake type shortcake. Just split it in half horizontally, fill with fresh cut and mascerrated berries and top with whipping cream. The best!!!!
Wouldn’t pouring hot liquid into an egg mixture cause the eggs to cook indoors spots resulting in small lumps?

@Simone Shapiro
You have to pour very slowly in a very thin stream while simultaneously whisking the eggs.
Mom made this when I was young.  It's good to see that others remember it too.
My mother made this in the 50’s but her recipe called for a can of Carnation evaporated mild and chopped pecans rather than coconut. We LOVED it!!!
I made a similar cake in the 1970’s, and do not remember the cake being so tender and delicious. The cardamom is a perfect addition.
The cake I used to make as a child was an oatmeal spice cake with this broiled icing.  I won a blue ribbon in our scouts club for that lovely recipe.

This has been a go-to cake in our family for years/decades. Easy to make, delicious for dessert but just as good in the morning with coffee. And sturdy enough to easily transport to a potluck. Always a hit!

Gluten free worked wonderfully with the cardamom. I whipped up the eggs and sugar like a tornado, then used Bob’s Red Mill (original bean-based all purpose) + xanthum gum and *barely* beat it. Out of the oven last night for late-night snack it was light and buttery-crunchy. This morning for breakfast it’s denser and creamier. Perfect!
When I was at university in the 1960s I boarded with an elderly lady who made a cake like this--I remember her (and the cake) with great fondness.

The cake is sort of meh - not much flavor, and a little dry - but the topping totally rocks. As others, I reduced the sugar in both the cake and topping, otherwise, made as shown. I'll try the topping again on a richer/moister cake.
This recipe brought so many wonderful memories. My grandmother made this cake when we went to visit. I love using her recipe, it’s a good one!

I can't believe I made this cake! It gave me the confidence of a pro to temper the eggs, and most importantly it's delicious. It had the cake crumb of my favorite coffee cakes. I subbed in a TBs of coconut oil in the batter and topping and swapped quick oats for shredded coconut. I also snuck in a dash of rum in both the batter and topping. Will make again, it's easy eating, a little too easy, my strategy will be to bring it as a dessert and leave it at someone else's house.

To make celiac friendly/ gluten free I substituted 1/2 cup almond flour and 2/3 cup sweet rice flour in the batter in place of the wheat flour. Used nutmeg and lemon zest in place of cardamom because it’s what I had. Cut down butter to 6 tbs in the topping so the whole recipe used one stick total. Baked for 25 minutes. It turned out excellent, would make again!
Well, that was disappointing!  The “frosting” was easy, but, to my taste, it is not good and it doesn’t look attractive.  I hope that you’ll like it better.

I made this in a 9” springform pan based on previous comments complaining about the annoyance of getting the cake out of a regular cake pan. I wish I had not. The sides are too high so the broil part did not get the edges browned/crispy. don’t repeat my mistake. 

I also don’t understand why this is called “lazy” as it required far more attention and steps than other snacking cakes on this website. My kids liked it but prefer the lemon snacking cake with coconut icing to this cake. (They are in a coconut phase!)

Just my two cents.
My college professor’s wife made it, I loved it, she gave me the recipe and told me “The boys love it.”
"that cake genuinely so good"  texted from the 16 year old boy.. so you know it's good.

I haven't made those version of this cake but have made a recipe by the same name that I got from my mother-in-law. She in turn got it from an elderly woman who was a friend of the family. I made this version multiple times when I was working and raising my son. I loved the recipe but haven't made it in years. I will have to dig out my recipe and if it differs, I will try this one. Thanks for the great heirloom recipe!
came together super easy and makes a lovely cake !
Mine came out like a mochi brick. What did I do wrong?
Mama used to make this at least once a month.  There was always cake in the house!  I loved it then and love it now.

Omg. Such a delicious cake. I wouldn’t change a thing. I opted for ground cardamom instead of vanilla. I also used unsweetened coconut, and I’m glad I did because I think sweetened would be unnecessary. This recipe is a keeper for sure.
@L Underhill “I wouldn’t change a thing…but I changed several…”  ;)
@sarahsarah the recipe lists both of her “changes” as options so no, she didn’t change a thing.
I also used unsweetened coconut 'cause that's what I had.  I would have preferred to use cardamon, but I would have had to shell and grind cardamon pods first.

This is basically a Hot Milk Sponge Cake that is my “go to” for every family event. The topping is use is 1 stick butter, 1 cup sugar, and a heaping tablespoon of cinnamon. Melt in a small pan ( same one used for the milk), pour

This is similar to a Hot Milk Sponge Cake that is my “go to” for family and events with neighbors. The topping I use is 1 stick butter, 1 c sugar, and a heaping tablespoon of cinnamon. Melt the butter in the same pot used for the milk, add the sugar and cinnamon and cook for a few minutes. Remove baked cake from oven, pour and spread glaze, pop under the broiler for a few minutes, remove and cool. Best cake ever!
Did not turn out well - perhaps the proportions are off? Runny topping and fairly dense cake structure.

This cake is stupid good.   Fantastic delicious on a million levels.  I made it 3 times in one week.  My wife brought some to work and her co-worker said "this is the best dessert i ever had in this office" and lot's of people bring proper baked goods into her office.  

This flavor profile reminds me of the old favorite entenmann's Louisiana Crunch Cake.  The caramelly toasty coconut is the nectar of the gods.

This is a nice bouncy cake.  I wouldn't say it was fuss free though because you have 2 separate heating components to the recipe. The broiling can be fussy.  My oven is electric and the broiler is poor so it took about 10 minutes with rotation about every 2 minutes...so a little fussy.
BUT, overall results are delicious with the brown sugar, butter and coconut topping.
I recommend but this is not as lazy as the title suggests!
Love this recipe! I made it over the weekend with my son who is in 2nd grade. Pretty simple, but we reduced the sugar to 1/2 a cup. Came out delicious.
How did you get it tout of the pan?

I did not expect this cake to taste this good! Not too sweet - moist yellow cake with crunchy coconut. Yum! Almost liked it better the second day - the topping adhered better. (It separated a bit on the first day). I did not have any problems with the cake sticking to the pan… but I was very generous with buttering of the pan.
Cake is cooling now.  In a few hours I'll find out if it worked.

After reading the comments I went back to Fanny Farmer’s Lazy Daisy Cake and Joy of Cooking’s Broiled Frosting.  Back and forth I went, comparing recipes, especially sugar and butter amounts. I made adjustments that suited me, taking something from each recipe, and I am happy with the resulting mashup.  For example, 3 tablespoons butter instead of a ½ c of butter for the topping worked for me, with ½ cup brown sugar and ½ cup coconut and 2 tablespoons milk.  (Didn’t have cream.). I used 1 teaspoon each of vanilla and cardamom-yum.  Makes a good coffee cake.

As someone also "born in the 1900s," I don't know how I haven't had this cake before. My husband made it for my birthday last week and I'm in love!! As noted in other comments, he only used 1/2 cup sugar in each, the cake and the topping, and only 1 stick of butter for the whole cake (2T less for the topping), as well as unsweetened coconut.  It's still very sweet and very delicious! We will make it again.
This is even better after sitting for 1-2 days... really let the topping "melt" into the cake.
My husband who is by no means an experienced baker made this cake for my birthday and it may have been the best slice of cake I have ever had.
A fabulous recipe. When I made this cake recently for a circle of friends, everyone's face lit up.

My cake rose in a dome like shape and then the topping pooled at the sides of the pan.  I used a 9" round pan. When I poured the batter in the pan, I wish I'd smoothed the top out so that it was flat.

I just made this cake for my daughter who loves a pure sweet flavor. it was good, but the cake was only about the height of a lemon square. I double checked the ingredients when I saw how little batter there was, and I think everything was right. To get close to the picture above, I would have to double the cake recipe and keep the topping the same. I used a 9" square pan, as specified. Did I do something wrong?

Made this as my first ever attempt at baking. Easy peasey. Came out great. If you love coconut, this is a great little treat. Got good reviews on it from several 'cooks/bakers'. I did good. Great recipe to try your hand at baking.

Delicious! Made as written and will bring to my grandma tomorrow for her 89th birthday. Her grandparents were Danish and she grew up having a coconut cake for her birthday, so I was inspired to make this for her after reading the description and having all ingredients on hand. Had a sliver to taste it and the cake is so airy and buttery but light. The topping is sinfully good, it has a good chew to it and the coconut flavor is delicate. Fabulous cake, very easy. My 6 year old did most of the work.

Great recipe. I’ve made it twice and I was impressed at what a lovely tender crumb the cake has and how well it keeps. I followed the instructions verbatim with one minor change the second time. I had less than half the quantity of coconut required so I cut the topping ingredients in half  and used the amount of coconut I had on hand.  It turned out better and looked more like the photograph.  I adore coconut so I normally would not say something is better with less coconut, but in this case it was.

title: Turkey Meatloaf With Parmesan and Red Wine Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: Line a sheet pan with parchment paper.  Put the meatloaf mixture in the 9x5 pan, then place the pan upside down on the parchment paper.  After about 30 minutes, the meatloaf will have set up.  Remove the sheet pan, remove the loaf pan, and then return the meatloaf to the oven to finish baking.  Your loaf will not be greasy in the slightest, and will be formed perfectly for slicing later.

@Matt Nousak - your suggestion sounds great in concept, but I am genuinely confused on one point: if, after 30 minutes, you’ve removed both the sheet and loaf pans, what is used to hold the meatloaf when it’s returned to the oven?
Remove the sheet pan from the oven. Lift the loaf pan from the meatloaf; it has been inverted over the meatloaf. Then put the sheet pan back into the oven.

@Matt Nousak

These instructions are extremely confusing to follow (put the meatloaf on a parchment-lined sheet pan and then turn it upside down? Remove the loaf pan? Which loaf pan?) I have no idea why so many people upvoted it.

This could be a Thanksgiving dinner in a meatloaf pan! Especially friendly for solo seniors who love the tastes, aromas, and leftovers.

I've never used ground turkey, but now I'm going to give it a try. Maybe substitute store-bought stuffing mix for the bread crumbs. This recipe opened up a world of possibilities!

@Regina 

Do NOT use 99% fat free turkey. Dry as sawdust.
@Regina T wonderful, creative idea!! I always use regular ground turkey (NOT white meat!! Sawdust) for everything calling for ground meat. No complaints.

As an avid turkey meatloaf maker, I want to warn readers that  ground turkey varies vastly in taste and texture  It depends on how much fat, white, and dark meat is mixed.  I use whole milk mozzarella( not always easy to find-- part skim is watery in meatloaf). The texture varies.  I was brought up with catsup on top but make a tomato slurry that varies with odds and ends of refrigerator leftovers like this recipe  Midwestern meatloaf adds a Lipton dry  mix. Don't knock it till you try it.
@Betsey P the salt tho.

@Betsey P. All well and good except this recipe DOES NOT call for mozzarella, it calls for Parmesan & while I’ve been known to have made a meatloaf or 2 with onion soup mix , this is definitely not the recipe to try it with! It would completely change it!! And please people do not add a ketchup (catsup) topping to it!! Try this just way it’s instructed!

For turkey meatloaf, do NOT use all white meat turkey -- it comes out dry. Also, all meatloaf benefits from adding shredded or finely diced veggies -- I use my mother's recipe of grated carrot, and finely diced onions and celery, but grated zuchs are also very good. This is especially important for turkey meatloaf, since the meat has way less fat than ground beef or pork. Herbes de provence tastes good, too.

@Rachel Kreier
How much grated vegetable do you use?
I have always done Onion, Carrot and mushrooms for the same reason! Helps it come out much more moist and a good way to get in some extra veggies!
@Rachel Kreier agree. It ups the veg count for the day too. I throw mushrooms into the food processor with onions, garlic and some shredded carrots. Mince it up and add to the party.
Have you tried meatloaf sandwiches with onion, pickle chips, cheese, mayonnaise and ketchup?

Followed the recipe exactly as written; used an Oregon Pinot Noir.  It's edible but neither my husband nor I liked the flavor or texture.  Perhaps it was overcooked since at 60 minutes I checked with a thermometer and it registered 165, 10 degrees higher than the recipe notes and 5 to 15 minutes less cooking time.  In any case, we won't be fighting over the leftovers.

@Amy H.-I agree. I followed the recipe exactly as stated, but just like you, there’s something about it  I don’t like. I can’t exactly pinpoint what it is or maybe it’s the combination of everything. It’s a shame since I find meatloaf to be a comfort food.
The meatloaf from Tuscany, the Beautiful Cookbook is really the classic way to make it.

@Steve
What is the recipe?
Everything but the kitchen sink! Despite the added fat of the Parmesan it was dry.
You don't say whether you used turkey or beef. Either way, the fat content of the meat would determine a dry result if ratio wasn't at least 15% fat.
Try adding about 1/3 cup grated zucchini per pound of ground turkey to help prevent dryness. Don't squeeze the moisture out of the squash.

@Jean H

Use grated carrots
For those who do not eat dairy, does this recipe work without adding the Parmesan cheese?
@MarcRock of course it would…it will have less flavor but its not a necessary component of meatloaf.
You could add some nutritional yeast for more umami.Kate
@MarcRock or add the anchovies from the original recipe

The glaze turns this recipe into a creative twist on a classic! Made this largely as written except for an added a glug of Worcestershire sauce into the meatloaf mix. Pulled at 150F to keep it moist. Served with crispy leek colcannon, peas, and a glass of the red Beaujolais used in the glaze for a hearty meal. The flavor is a bit unique and I'm undecided about how I feel about it. Drippings are very flavorful and I'll use them another day to make a quick pan sauce.
@Sunny crispy leek colcannon sounds amazing!

It was ok.  Unexpectedly, my wife and I both were put off by with the strong wine taste.  That much red wine sounded like a good approach for a two pound meatloaf, but it kind of overpowered other flavors.  Since there appeared to be more than enough sauce, if we made it again, we would cut the wine to 1/3 cup and add a teaspoon of sugar.

@Bill Mac
I agree about the amount of wine being overpowering (my husband loves red wine but it was too much for him!) Were I to make it again, I would also reduce the amount of wine, and add celery and carrots to the mix to compensate for the loss of liquid.

Everyone asking about @Matt Nousak's comment ... some meatloaves are made in a pan.  E.g., Ina Garten's (5 lb!!) turkey meatloaf is specifically free-form.  I like Matt's idea ... kind of like building a snow fort using plastic-shoebox snowbricks.

My basic meatloaf is a 2lb riff on Ina Garten's (and in a meatloaf pan, so I'm not consistent, but) .... anyone who's made this... how do they compare?
What kind of turkey - white or dark meat? Or does it matter?
It doesn't matter. Dark meat will be moister, but you can use white, or a mixture of white and dark, if that's your preference.

This makes an excellent meatloaf. I used 1 lb of mixed turkey for a 7" loaf pan, chopped parsley and celery leaves for the herbs, and 1/2 of an egg. I wasn't completely satisfied with the tomato mixture--I might leave out the soy or even substitute ketchup for the tomato puree. Hit 155 in about 55 minutes. Came right out of pan, Moist and yummy. Served with mashed potatoes with caramelized onion.

Just tried it both hot and cold. Hot, it's a bit over the top. Serve it cold and you end up with a kind of low-rent paté. In a good way. 
Made with 1 lb beef and 1 lb turkey. Pork would be a good addition.

If anyone reading this had any idea how much turkeys suffer on their way to becoming... meat loaf, they would not considered eating it. Not only do turkeys suffer horribly, but the environmental impact of these factory farms is... unconscionable.
Remove the sheet pan from the oven, remove loaf pan, return sheet pan to oven.
This makes a lot more sense than the original comment to remove all pans.  Thank you!
I have a meatloaf pan with holes in the bottom for fat drainage - would that be appropriate to use here? I'm guessing no.
No is right. You don't want the wine mixture to exit through those holes.
Responding to the “too dry“ criticism I added an extra egg and that seems to have done the trick. Also put in a dash of Worcestershire as was suggested elsewhere. Everyone at my table is very happy.
I have had many wonderful meals from NY Times recipes.  This is not one of them.  Awful.  Flavorless, tough (a meatloaf should not be tough).  Meatloaf is very simple; why mess around with a classic?

This was vile out of the oven, and inedible the next day.

The Orange Zest turns bitter overnight and ruins the dish.

What a waste of great ingredients.
My entire family gave me six thumbs down. I followed the recipe and measured precisely but the orange flavor was too strong and completely unnecessary in meatloaf. Too salty too.

One and done for me. I have many other meatloaf recipes that are much better. It was rubbery like turkey meatloafs tend to be. I made the wine sauce with a canned tomato sauce so it was very thin. When I added the second layer of meat it totally displaced the wine sauce so there was but a thin red line between the layers.
Need to figure out what to do with the leftovers.

Huge Melissa fan. Huge. This was a major disappointment. More work than my standard turkey meatloaf (Ina Garten) and a weird flavor profile. Seasoning too aggressive for my taste and the individual tastes didn’t blend into a harmonious whole. Orange zest particularly questionable. Lots of fat and liquid in the pan st the end. Not a repeater.
I love to try new recipes but this one misses the mark. Orange zest should be eliminated and overall taste was terrible.

I made this exactly as written, and texture was quite hard and dense.  The taste was OK.  It’s slightly odd to taste orange in a meatloaf.  If I were to make it again, I would simply add the liquid to the meat directly, as opposed to pouring it in the middle and on the top.  I think that would better moisturize the meat.

Made it exactly as written. Seemed awfully spongy and dry for the 60 minutes the recipe calls for. The orange zest permeated the loaf with cloyingly fruity flavor and smell. Tried it the next day with no success. I'm probably going to end up crumbling it into tomato sauce for spaghetti. Maybe third times a charm.
Tasty, moist and memorable, this is in my comfort meal rotation. Served over a cushion of mashed potatoes sweet potatoes and a side salad. Washed them down with a jammy Cabernet Sauvignon.

@Rob Steven’s 
Well now I’m going to try it
This was far too much orange zest. It was the only flavor I could taste.

made it as directed and it was a bit dry. Also the orange zest was a bit overpowering.  Needed to eat it with ketchup.  For leftovers I will add more tomato sauce to improve taste.   Not my favorite recipe.
Has anyone tried freezing this?  I was thinking of freezing the mixture in the pan and once thawed adding the final panko topping before baking…..thoughts?
@s of course you can freeze it.
For those of us who don't use alcohol when cooking, what's a good substitute here?
@Alicia Put your wine in a pot and cook off the alcohol.
@Spidee You're assuming I have alcohol, I do not drink nor have any alcohol. I also have a baby that would be eating this.

I have made a turkey/ground beef version over many years.  For filler I use ground up fresh mushrooms and a drained can of Muir Glen diced tomatoes with mild green chilies. This, all in addition, to my usual ingredients: eggs, parsley, thyme, onion, grated carrot, bread crumbs and yes a bit of ketchup.  Shape into a loaf that runs the middle of 9 x13 glass Pyrex dish and on either side I pour the juice + water from the diced tomato can.  My hard to please young grandchildren even like it.

Made this tonight. Followed the instructions exactly except I forgot to get an orange so used lemon. Anyhow. Poured the wine mixture in and truly doubted that this would be edible. So much liquid! I was wrong. So good and I will be making again!

i tried this as it struck me as a healthy meatloaf. my partner liked it but he doused it with yellow mustard and ketchup. i was disappointed. did i use the wrong kind of red wine? too much garlic and orange zest? i’m not sure. i’m guessing this is an unforgiving recipe because the turkey does not on its own have enough flavor to carry the recipe or make up for a lack of exactitude in measures or the wide variation in flavor profiles of what i would call “garlicky sangria”. i will go back to frank bruni’s mother’s meatloaf which delivers much greater satisfaction for the same effort.

I haven't made this recipe yet, but my hack for turkey meatloaf is 1/2 ground turkey and 1/2 hot Italian turkey sausage (cases removed). The sausage adds more flavor, a bit of kick (but not too much), and usually has more fat than ground turkey, which is what you want in a meatloaf, IMO.
I was disappointed in the outcome. My first time using turkey, and it was dry and surprisingly dense. The orange and red wine dominated, and left a slightly bitter after taste.

I think this is the worst meatloaf I have ever made. Came out very dry, when I inverted it, the top 1/4 separated from the bottom 3/4. the orange rind is too strong for my family's taste. No juice or sauce whatsoever. Sadly a waste of time and ingredients. Not worth tweaking to make again.

This recipe has one big flaw, one small flaw (the Panko needs to be specified by weight), and tastes FABULOUS, at least when made with basil as the herb.

The big flaw is the ultra-fussy two-layer approach. It's very difficult to place the second layer of the meat mixture. And then each slice of the baked loaf has two separate layers! Going forward, I'm going to press all the meat mixture into the pan at once, pour all the liquid over it, and enjoy the same great taste without all the bother!

Not sure why there are so many negative reviews about this recipe. Swapped Worcestershire sauce for soy sauce and added some ketchup, but otherwise followed to the letter.  Halved it, though — used 1 lb of ground turkey, etc. — This is a delicious meatloaf. Will be in the rotation for years to come!

I was very excited to try this, but did not enjoy the result at all. The orange flavor was far too forward and overwhelmed everything else. I ended up pitching the whole thing; pity, as I usually love Melissa’s recipes.

i'm never really comfortable w/ commenting on a poor review; but I feel its important to share my review. I wondered as I was mixing ingredients; that the Parmesan cheese amount was perhaps a bit much. Well; it was - I understand the umami effect; but this meatloaf was dense; not moist and literally tasted like Parmesan cheese - it was actually inedible from our standards. I'm not sure if (?) measurements were off and I haven't yet read any other comments - hope some of you read this

I've made this recipe four times now, and I have to say it has the best flavor of any meatloaf I've ever had! The first time I made it, I made it exactly as directed except I subbed one anchovie for the orange zest, as mentioned in the accompanying article. As written, this has a very firm texture, which may be perfect for sandwiches but isn't my first choice for same day eating. Now I set aside the topping, then mix the remainder of the flavorings in a large bowl before adding in the turkey.
I just saved this. I don't think it can go wrong! I'll report back after trying it, but my guess is that Melissa strikes again.
How about using classic meat loaf mix (beef, pork, veal)?

I used 93% lean ground chicken , cooked to 165 cuz bird flu and used  an extra deep shorter loaf pan as I could not locate my 9x5 one. It came out deeply spiced and rich tasting . I also realized mid recipe  that I lacked an egg and subbed in about 1/8 of a cup of cottage cheese.  Remember any dryness  can be cured when serving cold by adding  condiments .Mustard, mayo, tomatoes or any of your own favorites will add some moisture.
Way too salty. I would probably cut salt by half if I made it again.
The meat was dense and didn't care for what little flavor it had.
This was great. Made exactly as described. I'm looking forward to meatloaf sandwiches tomorrow!

I don’t see any mozzarella in the recipe as one reviewer noted. But I agree about adding the Lipton’s dry onion soup mix - as my mother did back in the 50’s, 60’s and as I do today. 
Looking forward to trying this recipe!
I made it the other day and thought it was yummy - much more flavorful than the usual meatloaf.
Has anyone tried freezing and reheating this meatloaf? I'm planning on freezing some meals for when baby arrives!
Have this on my dinner roster for next week, but minus the orange zest, which just doesn't "do it for me" in meatloaf.  I love it in certain Catalan-style recipes, but not in meatloaf.

I’ve been making turkey meatloaf for a few years, experimenting with recipes. Ground turkey found in most grocery stores is either too lean or too wet. You can grind a mix of white and dark meat from your own turkey, or have your butcher do it.

I'm a vegetarian, but I saved this recipe because it would also make a wonderful vegetarian lentil loaf, subbing cooked green or brown lentils for the turkey. I usually use my mother's meatloaf recipe with lentils. It tastes just like her meatloaf did and it also makes great cold loaf slices.
Sorry you eliminated the anchovies.  Wish you had provided the anchovies.   Would a can of anchovies be right or should it be less like maybe 1...2....3...and any detail on how to include them.

Well, after reading through the comments I’ll stick with the glaze my Mum concocted and family has liked for years. I do love trying new recipes for NYT, hmmm comments not very encouraging here. 
I’ve used dark turkey meat with a mix of sherry, brown sugar and ketchup as the topping (added half way through the cooking process. And the family did fight over the last piece.
It was fine, but a bit salty -- salt, Parmesan and soy sauce will do that.  There was nothing wrong with it, but I wouldn't make it again, nothing particularly exciting about it.
@Nancy Pomegranate juice is a great substitute for red wine, you might need a little vinegar to cut the sweetness

I have all of the ingredients and was planning to make this recipe tonight but the comments are so discouraging, I’m going to drink all the wine and make my tried and true turkey or chicken meatloaf with chili sauce. The better the chili sauce, the better the meatloaf.
Oof, was excited to try this twist on meatloaf. No bueno. The orange zest was too heavy a note, the red wine did not accentuate the dish as I hoped it would. It was moist though!
Could use use red wine vinegar instead of the wine?

I substituted a 50/50 mix of ground chuck and pork as suggested.  I broke up the meat with my fingers, not wanting to over mix, everything else was exactly as written.  Even the peas and mashed potatoes.  And wow!  We loved it.  Not dry at all.  It was almost like pate de compagne.  It was 165 degrees when I pulled it at 75 minutes, but no harm done.  Great on a week night, even if the wine/tomato/cheese/orange zest/garlic...took a few extra minutes of prep time.  This was just a winner.
I'm torn on trying this recipe. Generally I half Ina Garten's recipe and for the glaze I use a combo of Heinz Chili Sauce, Brown Sugar, and a tiny amount of Dijon.
Renee Erickson is a wonderful chef and this is very typical of her deep flavor and attention to buiding layers of that flavor. Delicious!
This was just awful. Dry, tasteless and total waste of good enough ingredients. Even the tomato mixture was ill conceived

Delicious. I made this to the letter of the recipe.  No alterations needed.  It's moist (no need to add shredded zucchini).  Remember you are adding over a cup of liquid in the center and on top to be absorbed.  Grated Parmesan adds moisture too.  No need to do pan gymnastics to make it not greasy - it's not.  Ground turkey is lean.  I wish I had more liquid in the pan when finished to serve over the plated meatloaf, it was delicious. I used 93% lean for the turkey and cilantro for the herb.

I sauteed the turkey for a little while before mixing it in with the egg/crumbs/etc. so that it didn't turn into a block of meat like this did the first time I made this. 
I also added some carrots, celery, and onion, and toasted the Panko, all to incorporate more texture. 

For the sauce, I used 1/2 cocktail sauce/1/2 tomato puree, because I found—the first time I made it—the dryness of the wine (and I used a Bordeaux) with the flatness of the tomato contrasted with what I expected for a glaze.

For tomato sauce, I used an already opened jar of Rao's. I also cooked the wine sauce a little to remove the alcohol flavor. I also added a 2 tbs of olive oil just because.  It turned out great served hot AND cold for sandwich

This was a horrible tasting meatloaf.  Don't bother.  I think it was the orange and the cook time.  Plus the meat was too hard and dry - should have added more liquid when mixing the meat.  I've made many meatloaves in my time and this was by far the worst.

Used two packages of 85% lean ground turkey, which was 2.6 lbs, so quite a bit more than the recipe called for. Pan was overflowing a bit. Added a little extra panko and parmesan, 1/8 tsp of red pepper flakes. Used a cabernet Sauvingon wine and fresh flat leaf parsley for the herbs. It turned out great - very moist, with delicious flavor.
Made as directed.  Disappointed.  Came out mushy and the orange zest was overpowering.

My husband loves all meat loaf (loaves?) so I had to try this interesting one. It did not disappoint! Used lean ground turkey and lean organic grass fed beef; had no issues with greasiness (or dryness either). Next time I will omit the orange zest - the flavored seemed out of place. May omit panko on top next time and just sprinkle Parmesan - the panko on top did not crisp and was mushy. Served with spaghetti and marinara sauce. The sauce was delicious over both.
Look forward to leftover subs!

I was intrigued by this recipe and the unusual technique. I thought it would taste like Thanksgiving - the herbs, the orange zest - but I found it kind of sour and the texture was weird. Don't think I will repeat.
