Ok- made this and read EVERYONES comments. I did make changes based on what l read. So long comment… that said it is easier than it seems and my 5 year old grandchild even asked for seconds!

Used 93% turkey so added 1/2 shredded zucchini & a carrot for moisture- some said too dry. I kept the orange zest, used Worcestershire sauce instead of soy- but doubt it would matter in the scheme of things- it’s just what l had. I used 1/2 cup red wine and a bit more crushed tomato with 1 TBS sugar- my advice is cook it and taste (see what works for you- it was delicious). 

I would say the weirdness is in the layering process- 1/2 meatloaf, then tomato sauce, the rest of the meatloaf and then the rest of sauce. For those who said just throw on parchment paper and cook without a loaf pan- l ask- how? The two halves would slide off. 

I tried the suggestion of using parchment paper-and cooked in loaf pan, but misread and inverted after 30 mins removing the pan- next time will do as suggested by a user and cook upside down - or as recipe says in pan & see which is better. 

Point us- the recipe is a keeper- when a 5 year old who turns up her nose at nearly everything asks for seconds- we will be making again to try numerous iterations of baking. Only thing- it does kinda come out as two thin layers on top of each other (due to tomato layering process- most seeps in)- looking forward to the cold meatloaf sandwich. 5 stars :)

By far the best meatloaf I’ve ever made! Comforting and hearty without being bland. I paired it with some toasted cheese bread and seared Brussels sprouts in red wine and Dijon mustard. 10/10 easy weeknight meal that I’ll make whenever it’s a little chilly

Absolutely the worst meatloaf we have ever made.  We threw it out and my husband could not get the taste out of his mouth for days.  Seriously disappointing and we have been making beef and turkey meatloaves for generations.

This was a disaster! It was not a very pretty presentation and the two layers split apart and were not cooked evenly. It’s not my oven as I have a professional oven that is great. And yes, I  followed the recipe to a T.
This was just not good.  The flavors of the wine and the orange are overwhelming to the turkey.  This dish does not encourage second helpings or leftovers.  I tossed mine out.

This recipe was a total surprise. I didn’t cook a lot before the pandemic but now I try a new recipe every week or so. I followed the instructions and only had diced tomatoes, so the ‘sauce’ portion was a bit saucier than I expected. I crossed my fingers and hoped for the best. 

Wow. I guess the drier meat with the panko absorbed everything because there wasn’t much liquid left, just an incredibly delicious meatloaf that I will definitely use for sandwiches. The combination of orange zest and the wine  and the Parmesan was so delicious. And I forgot the herbs! I will definitely make this again.
Use more orange zest and full 1 1;2 tsp of salt. In my fast oven set at 350 and check at 50 minutes. Also add sautéed onion or shallot.

I used a pound of ground turkey and one shredded zucchini. Also didn’t have breadcrumbs so substituted with finely ground white cornmeal. Texture is moist and delicious! Somehow the sauce layer disappeared from the middle, but I might simmer up a batch to serve on top.

Oh my God, this was fabulous. I've always found meatloaf to be "meh," but this version had incredible flavor, likely due to the wine (I used Chianti), Parmesan and basil. It was also very moist, contrary to another reviewer's comment. I made one for friends who loved it, too. This is comfort food that's company-worthy.
I followed the recipe and was very disappointed. The meatloaf was very dry and too dense. I make a lot of turkey meatloaf and have used a lot of different recipes and this was simply not very good.

Meatloaf lover here. Followed the recipe exactly and usually love Melissa's recipes, but I will not be making this again. The texture was awful (dense and dry, as others have noted) and the combination of orange zest and tomatoes (both of which I love individually) was super-yuck. It didn't even make for good leftovers. I'm going back to my standby ground beef meatloaf recipe with a ketchup and vinegar glaze on top.
This is wonderful. I served it with buttermilk mashed potatoes and spinach salad. It is worthy of company and a new family favorite.
Not very good or worth the time.  After cooking this, all I tasted was the wine.  No turkey, no spices or herbs, nothing.  (And, yes, I followed the recipe exactly.)
I read this recipe and was so excited. So I made it tonight and was so disappointed. It was bland and didn't have all the flavors of the wonderful things that went into it.
Didn’t enjoy this. Tasted too much of wine. The slices split at the layer of tomato sauce. Not worth the fuss.

I combined 1 lb ground beef with 1 lb tourney as I only bought 1lb turkey and had beef in the freezer. The meat was perfect, but the 1 tsp of orange zest in the sauce was way too much! The orange flavor overpowered all other flavors, even the wine.
No verve in this meatloaf.  Boring.
I made this but it was dry and tasteless. Will try another recipe. Don’t recommend this one.

This ended up being a very dense meatloaf. My personal preference is for a lighter looser loaf. Mine was oddly dry even though it was literally floating in liquid when it went in the oven. We also didn't enjoy the flavor. Won't be making this one again.
Weird, and oddly dry at the end given all the liquid.
I’m going to use my leftover NY Times Bolognese sauce. Can’t wait to try this tomorrow.

So, I have a “meatloaf pan.“ It’s a 9 x 5 loaf pan with holes in the bottom around the periphery. It rests in a deeper pan which catches drippings as the meatloaf bakes. If I try to use this pan for this recipe, will the tomato-wine mixture will leak out into the reservoir and not cook the meatloaf properly?

Meh. Added about tbsp minced ginger, shallot, worchestershire, minced rehydrated dried mushrooms,  low sodium soy and anchovy paste to meat to give it umami ground turkey is missing. Still was kinda bland. Sauce in middle of loaf was nothing special and kinda list in the mix.
For lazy cooks, mix white and dark turkey 2 to one. One cup oatmeal. One jar salsa.
Does anybody have a suggestion for a substitute for the red wine?

Matt Nousak--I am still confused.  If I turn the raw meatloaf pan upside down on parchment to cook for 30 minutes won't excess liquid in meatloaf mixture just run out of the mixture and onto the parchment paper lined sheet pan?  Wouldn't it be better to put 1/4 of wine mixture in bottom of pan, before adding meatloaf mixture?
@Anne in Maine Read Step 4 again. No turning the loaf pan. Sheet pan only there to catch any liquid overflow.
I can't have alcohol is their a substitute for the red wine?
Beef stock is a common substitution.
The meat was dry (too much Panko?) and the sauce had an interesting flavor but was too thin.
For those of us allergic to eggs, can this be made without it?
@ laura murray, the point is that nothing holds it and you get a crust and it's not sitting in moisture and grease.  It is "set up" after about 30 minutes and hold it's own shape.
I like to add 2-3 slices of bacon to the top of a good turkey loaf. Makes if 5 stars!

As much as I wanted to like this because I’m trying to cut down on red meat, the meatloaf turned out dry and very dense.  I cooked it exactly as written to the correct internal temperature. I also thought the taste of the glaze was a bit strange and off-putting.
Interesting loaf & sauce.  I did substitute the soy sauce with reduced sodium Tamari. I used 93% lean ground turkey. No problems. Moist & delish.

We made this tonight after reading about it in today’s Food section. It is delicious! It doesn’t look that good and we think could use maybe a tomato sauce but even without we both gobbled (sorry) it up. We made exactly as directed in recipe.

Best use of ground turkey we’ve come across.

I should have added that our regular meatloaf is Giuliani Hazan’s from Every Night Italian which has pork, beef, and veal so is certainly not dry but way more meat and fat than we usually want but is also delicious.

Sounds like a lot of breadcrumbs to me and somehow the addition of wine doesn't seem necessary. You definitely need a dark/white ground turkey blend. I always add minced or grated onion and chopped parsley, and ketchup. A mix of minced red, green, orange, and/or yellow bell peppers works quite well, too, and gives it a nice zing and sweetness. As for toppings, it's hard to go wrong with the classic ketchup drizzle.

I thought the meat was perfect (moisture wise).. used plain ground turkey (it is just a mixture of both white and dark meat). Comments about ground white meat being too dry are well founded. I myself did not like the orange, as I found even that small amount too overpowering, and not a good addition for me (your mileage may vary). I chose rosemary as my herb and it was delicious — again, a personal choice because I find rosemary and meat to be a match made in heaven. I don’t waste really good wine on a sauce, nor do I like drinking bad leftovers, so a good mid-range Cabernet worked great on both ends. I would definitely make this again, but as the French say, “sans l’orange”!
Sounds great, but when I make it, I'll use my usual combo of ground beef and pork.

Very good variation on meatloaf. We loved the tomato forward flavor. We also love Ina’s garlic forward recipe, btw.  And all the hoopla over Matt’s suggestion on pans!  Geez!  It was a reasonable suggestion, Matt. I understood exactly what you were suggesting. Thank you. I chose to cook as the directions specified and had no problem. I used a standard 5x9 loaf pan and there was an inch of rim above the surface of the meatloaf that easily prevented any spillover.

I didn't want to open a bottle of red wine, so I changed up the sauce:

- 6oz can of tomato paste
- two tablespoons jarred garlic (I know but I was in a rush)
- 1/2 cup ish balsamic vinegar
- 1 tablespoon soy sauce
- 2 tablespoons coconut aminos
- few glugs olive oil

Turned out great- I kept adding the vinegar until it had a ketchup consistency. I think the olive oil helped keep the whole thing juicy.

This recipe aligned so closely with my beef meatloaf that I decided to use that recipe. 
The difference was using 3/4 cup old fashioned oatmeal to the mix instead of panko, together with 1/2 cup ketchup and 3/4 cup chopped onion. To avoid dryness that could be caused by over mixing, i first mixed the oatmeal, ketchup, onion, 1 tbsp Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 tsp salt, 1/4 tsp pepper, them added them to the turkey. Reduced turkey to 1.5 pounds. 5 minutes b/f it was done, topped w/slices of cheddar. Took 55 minutes.

Used a whole beefsteak tomato resulting in excessive tomato sauce topper. This recipe as written ends up dry and is missing that classic tomato/mustard/brown sugar topper.
Not a fun twist on a 1950s staple.
Which ground turkey should be used? The 99% fat free white turkey or the (I think it's) 93% slightly brown turkey? Thanks in advance.
I think 93% is going to be better
I used dark meat turkey and it was really good. This was a hit!
Pomegranate juice is a good red wine substitute but you might need a little vinegar to cut the sweetness @Alicia

I added 1 small zucchini, 1 carrot, 1 shallot - shredded for baby or chopped small. Chopped tomatoes small. Added paprika, oregano, red wine vinegar, soy sauce, and garlic when I cooked these vegetables. I did basil and green onions for herbs. Kept rest of recipe same except cooked in a 9x9” for approximately 40 minutes or until 165. Feedback was to skip orange zest next time, I liked it in there. Was delicious. Wanted to get veggies in for baby and he liked it, added apple sauce and baby cereal for him.

I wanted to like this meatloaf. I used a silicone sling inside the loaf pan to make it easier to remove (King Arthur Flour one). But, I had problems. Mixing in the panko was more difficult than some recipes, hence it required more hand-squishing, which may have made the meatloaf a little more dense than I'd have liked. When I lifted the meatloaf out of the pan with the sling, the top half slid right off and landed on my kitchen counter. Taste was okay. Nothing to write home about.

We made this last night and knowing how dry white meat turkey can be, I opted for dark meat turkey and followed the recipe.  It was amazing.  I used convection roast setting on our oven to cook it and it was nice and crispy, firm yet moist enough. Will make again.

I usually make a freeform turkey meatloaf using basically whatever leftovers I have in the refrigerator, and it's always good, but my family is sick of it, so I tried this recipe and made it exactly as written. It smelled and tasted delicious—I used all dark meat and cheap Pinot Noir. The only real difference was the overall density. This meatloaf is ridiculously dense. It's almost like mystery meat—maybe I did something wrong? My usual recipe is super light.

I made this last night, even after reading many negative comments because I had all the ingredients at home. This was NOT good. I did take a note from several that said to reduce the amount of wine to 1/3 cup. It was very dry, even with the sauce. I thought it would seep through for moisture but it didn’t. This will not be in my rotation. Another reviewer was turning into bolognese… great idea!
Great! Very flavorful and so different from the usual turkey meatloaf/burger recipes that I normally rely on. I'll be making this again!
Great meatloaf. Good spice and nice tomato-y accent. Made recipe as written...yes, it's true.
I only made two minor modifications to this - used lemon instead of orange and only 1 garlic clove for the meatloaf.  Parsley for the herb. Came out extremely moist and amazing.

Made as directed.
Next time would:
 use crushed tomatoes (maybe drained) ,
add more garlic and herbs to the meat mixture.
 Not compact meat so much into loaf pan
• first try was a little to dense, sliced well.
  Needed more herbs and salt (my bad)
  Center filling too runny, would be better if it stayed more in place.

Made this last night!!
Added mushrooms and a bit more than a pinch of red pepper flakes as well as a dash of Worcestershire sauce.   Started oven temp at 425° for 10 min then turned down to 375°, it was perfectly ready 70 min later.  This was probably the best meatloaf dish ever!!!!
So delicious, juicy, lots of juice in the loaf pan for the potatoes!! 
This is a WINNER worthy of any dinner party!!!
Followed the directions and found that 65 minutes was too long to bake. The internal temp was closer to 175 degrees than 155.  Interesting taste.  Next time, I will test at 55 minutes.

I followed the recipe exactly as directed. I did not like the taste. There’s something about it I can’t pinpoint, but I believe it’s too much wine. I’m not going to finish what I made. I’m tempted to do this again only reduce the wine and increase the crushed tomatoes. Not sure if that’s going work. It’s a shame it didn’t work out for me because I love meatloaf. It’s a real comfort food.

This meatloaf was delicious. I used a mixture of ground turkey and ground pork. I moistened the panko with a little milk before adding it to the meatloaf mix — I think that helped to keep the meatloaf moist. It did cook much faster than the time stated in the recipe. In a 9x5 loaf pan, the meatloaf was done in only 45 minutes.
This was an excellent recipe and it turned out perfectly, not to mention plenty of leftover for later.

Coleen
5 days ago
Great suggestion! My one question, at some point should you flip the meatloaf back over so the wine/tomato/panko mixture can brown? Or should you put that on the bottom of the loaf pan since it will be at the top when you remove it? Col
Col: I happened to do this baking the meatloaf upside down method because I was having trouble with my oven.
I waited until I had flipped the meatloaf to add the topping. Worked great.
Loved it!! It's a keeper!!
I added chopped cilantro and parsley as well as the leaves to give it some added texture.   Minimal salt is needed.
This is very firm and dry. I would highly recommend using at least one pound of ground beef to raise the fat content.

Not great. I don’t really understand the point of the two layers. The Parmesan flavor is lost and the red wine topping is only ok. Honestly, just make a regular meatloaf and play around with ingredients. We almost never waste food, but sad to say half of this was composted. Just not good.

I usually love the recipes for NY Times Cooking but this one was so inferior. The meatloaf was very dry and dense even though I added a pound of pork sausage to one pound ground turkey to improve fat content. Oven temp at 375 for 65 minutes was just too much. Orange zest was an odd and off putting ingredient. This recipe had too much prep with such  a disappointing result that I would not try it again. The peas and the mashed potatoes were the stars of this meal. NY Times Cooking can do so much better.

I’m a big fan of Renee Erickson’s restaurants and her cooking, but I think I will not use the orange zest when I next make this recipe. Everything else went fine. We enjoyed the panko topping and the band of tomato/wine in the middle. The meatloaf was very moist and flavorful.
We loved it! Followed directions in recipe exactly. Appreciate recipes that try something new with different taste combos. Makes life interesting and not so ho hum.

Another example where it would REALLY help to have listed the ingredients as "1¼cups panko bread crumbs, divided." In putting things together I knew the panko was added to the turkey mix, and I added it based on the ingredient listing, which is easier to read/access. I missed needing to reserve 1/4 cup and only saw it when it was too late. The practice of using ", divided" is to help avoid just such a mistake.
I don't think I ever want to make meatloaf any other way again - I love it!  And yes, it's brilliant for meatloaf sandwiches, which I adore.
I added the rest of the can of crushed tomatoes and a little extra wine. It came out very moist and you cna really taste the red wine in the tangy sauce!

My favorite meatloaf recipe! We used beef instead of turkey because my husband needs to increase his iron intake. I only used half the orange zest since some commenters were put off by it and I don't like citrus to be too overwhelming.  Everyone who had it thought it was great. I'll definitely make it again.

I don't understand all the reviews saying it was dry or too orange. I made with a blend of turkey, pork, beef and veal. came out super juicy and a crunchy top. wine added a real depth of flavor. Parmesan cheese brought forth a richness. Five stars!

I’ve never been a fan of ground turkey, so this was a departure for me. I made it reversing the amounts of wine and crushed tomatoes, deleting the orange zest, and using 85% lean turkey.  I am genuinely surprised that both my husband and I really liked it.  Next time I might add a dash of Worcestershire for some extra umami.
So I didn’t have a loaf pan, but I made the recipe exactly as noted. It was TERRIBLE. Will never make this again. I should have known better, because whats a meatloaf without onions?

Although I followed the recipe, the crusting was fine, it has an unappetizing look being a flesh color. No one in the family would eat it other than me. I sliced it up, made sandwiches with lettuce and catsup, and froze the rest for later. I would cook a turkey meatloaf again, but I need to consider the ingredients for the optics.

The orange zest was a great addition. I’m not sure why the recipe wanted you to put a layer of the sauce in the middle, it just makes the loaf fall in half. I will make it again but add all the sauce on top.

I have to admit that I use NYT recipes all the time.  I love them.  I consider myself a vey good cook, very.  I will accept any challenge and make anything, anytime.  This recipe, however was an epic fail.  As far as I am concerned, I did nothing wrong, followed it perfectly.  Just a bad meld of flavors and the orange...yuck!!!  I actually discarded it (and I never waste anything!!!).
My advice, just do not make this one.  Skip it.
This is a fantastic recipe! I made it as written (except maybe I added extra Parmesan) with organic 93% lean turkey. Not sure why it has such mixed reviews. I’ll be making this again.

Love Melissa's recipes but this was awful.  As always when making for the first time, I followed the recipe exactly except used a scant t of zest and only cooked for 60 minutes which turned out to be too long.  It was dry and dense and the orange flavor was so pervasive and unpleasant that I dumped it in the compost after 2 bites.  My partner didn't like it either so a total waste of 2 lbs. of turkey and some good parmesan, not to mention the red wine - should have just poured it in my glass.
Also, I used dark meat ground turkey - the lean ground turkey tastes like sawdust, i never use it.

I hated the orange zest in this. I didn’t use very much, but it totally overwhelms all the other flavours- and I used 5 garlic cloves. If (and it’s a big if) I remade this I might use lemon zest instead. At least I now know I don’t like orange flavoured savoury food.
I added a small onion, finely chopped, to the meat in step 3. Can't imagine a meatloaf without onion!

This packs a ton of flavor and holds together very well.  I would agree with some comments, wine flavor was a bit strong for my wife and I.  Next time I will cut the wine in half and increase the crushed tomato to compensate.  Also, I will line the loaf pan with parchment, to ease lifting out, as the separated halves split when lifting with offset spatula.

Yum.  We loved it.  I used 93% fat free ground turkey and added 1/4 cup olive oil to increase the moistness and decrease the cholesterol and trans fats.  (on a cardiac diet).  The orange zest was a fabulous addition to the sauce.
Followed recipe to the tee...delicious...wouldn't change a thing.

I made this recently and had mixed results. The meatloaf mixture itself was moist and tasty, but the wine-crumb mixture on top and around the edges was very bitter. I'll try it next time with crushed tomatoes but no wine.
Not a huge fan of the addition of orange zest so I'll probably skip that next time I make this. Otherwise really good!!! Will definitely cook again.
this was good but not great. we didn't love the orange rind and the overall flavour was just kinda bland.
I gotta say - not good. Edible, but the flavors were meh.

Made this last night & it was great.  Heeded advice from @Rachel Kreier & added diced carrots, celery & onion (mirepoix) to ground turkey which kept it from drying out. Cooked for 55 minutes.  Looking forward to left overs.

Made per recipe with one exception - I always add 1 tablespoon olive oil per pound of turkey. If you want a turkey doorstop this is your recipe. Flavor and texture when hot not too bad, but not good when cold. Will not be using it for any leftovers.
Made with beef and omitted orange zest. Delicious!
not a redo, lacks the taste that one expects from a meatloaf.

I made this meatloaf exactly like the recipe and used it throughout the following days in lots of different ways: with mashed potatoes and peas, with spaghetti and marinara (cut up to make a kind of quick meat sauce), with couscous, great sandwich. This is going into rotation.
Would not make this again. I cut the recipe in half and maybe that was the problem. It was very dry and I didn’t care for the tomato, orange, wine sauce.  IMHO

This is one of the most polarizing recipes on this site, and from Melissa Clark! I made it exactly to the recipe (other than halving it) just to see for myself. Personally, we enjoyed it as a new variation on meatloaf. I might leave out the orange zest next time as I felt it was a bridge too far, but my husband loved it.

Made this with dark meat turkey one lb and 20
Oz so more than recipe called for. Didn’t put as much wine and added Worcester sauce, 1/4 c diced red pepper and carrots. Came out very moist and delicious. I put all ingredients in one bowl so didn’t follow recipe exactly. Served with mash potatoes and it was so yummy!
Don't do the upside down nonsense suggested by Matt Nousak. It's messy. You lose the topping. Ruin a pan. Listen to Melissa.

I love wine but this was very wine forward. It could have used some shallot. Maybe cooking the wine down a bit to reduce the acidity. There are a lot of turkey meatloaf recipes out there. I’d recommend finding another one.
Oh my heavens, this was awful.
Sadly this was a miss. The orange was bitter and the flavor was strange and I dumped the whole thing :(

I’ve tried this twice. Had only one pound of meatloaf. The second time I tried to be very precise but I have to say, I thought the amount of breadcrumbs was far too much. I could see blobs of white and I mixed it pretty well with my hands. The result looked like big gobs of fat. It was really unappetising.

I found that the meatloaf was too dense and the sauce was not saucy enough for so much meat and the split layers did not work out, essentially causing me to have two separate pieces of meatloaf when slicing. However, the overall flavor was good, the orange zest was a really nice touch.

Maybe I did it wrong, but mine wasn’t dry at all. I cooked it for 65 minutes and the internal temp was 170F. There was a substantial amount of liquid in the pan. I used ground turkey blend and a spectacular California Grenache, Syrah, Merlot blend. My biggest challenge was adding the second layer before cooking. But wow!! What a meatloaf! I really liked the savory notes in what is usually pedestrian ground turkey.My wife’s second comment was: “This recipe would make amazing meatballs!!”
It's a little...odd. Is it the orange zest? May have used too much--didn't measure.
I couldn't believe how moist this came out! I doubled the garlic, because I always do for everything, and I thought the flavor was complex and not overpowering. Even my eight-year-old ate it!
Decent turkey meatloaf recipe. The wine and tomato sauce keep it moist, but the orange zest is completely unnecessary. It is over powering and cloying. I’d make it again without the peel.
What can be substituted for the wine

My husband and I thought this was delicious! I used 1 pound 93% turkey & 1 pound 99% turkey. I did not want to open a large can of crushed tomatoes so I combined 1/4 cup tomato paste with 1/4 cup water for the tomato ingredient. I did not have fresh herbs on hand but will look forward to adding that the next time I make this!

The flavor is excellent - the orange zest is prominent.  Not really sure what the red wine "sauce" does.  I used crushed tomatoes and might try diced tomatoes next time since all the liquid drips out to the sides.  Cutting the meatloaf ends up a mess of pieces falling all over.  Again, flavor is great, but presentation is lousy.
Too much liquid? Maybe cut the volume by 50%? I found it wasn't firm enough to slice. Less liquid might allow it to bake firmer and produce a crisper crust. But still, first attempt.
I felt like it was heavy on the red wine. Did not really care for this one

This was a great meat loaf.  I think I used the 93% ground turkey and the higher-fat would have been more flavorful.  I have a meat loaf pan that has a device that lifts the meat loaf out so I didn't do the parchment paper stuff.
The orange zest overpowered. We enjoyed it with ketchup, and will scrape the sauce off and have sandwiches with ketchup or bbq sauce so as not to waste it. But I wouldn’t make this recipe again.

This turned out great. Definitely took 175 mins. I did half turkey and half pork. I blended some fresh tomatoes with wine etc.  Orange zest added an interesting note to the flavor. Definitely needed a sheet pan. I was glad I covered mine with foil. Overall was great.  Will make again.

YUM! So different from your classic meatloaf. I was skeptical of the orange zest…don’t skip it! The zest pairs so well with the umami flavor of the soy sauce. I cooked this exactly as the recipe called for, EXCEPT I used an 8” x 8” ceramic dish bc I didn’t have a loaf pan (not a baker or habitual meatloaf maker). Turned out perfectly. (Okay, I also used regular bread crumbs bc I was out of panko.) Will make again, for sure!
Made this with 1 and 1/4 piund turkey, almost as much cheese and panko, and tomato sauce made same as recipe but didnt use it all. It came out perfectly and not greasyat all.
I made this recipe as noted, but instead of turkey used ground beef, 80% lean. It was absolutely delicious, and the leftovers the next day even better.

I made the recipe tonight as written. I don’t think that it was worth the effort at all. I’ve had many much better turkey meatloaves before, from written recipes to concoctions I put together myself. 
This meatloaf had a very odd flavor. The orange was a bit off putting. It tasted odd with the turkey which I didn’t expect. It also had a vaguely sweet taste, also not good. I used very dry wine so I didn’t think that caused the weird sweetness. The texture wasn’t dry but unpleasantly mushy. Maybe too much bread crumbs? 
I’d find another turkey meatloaf recipe. Melissa Clark’s recipes are usually right on point but this one wasn’t. I pitched the whole thing in the trash and we didn’t finish what was on our plates. So sorry.

Prepared as written, with Syrah as the red wine, passata as the tomatoes and basil as the herbs. Roasted potatoes and hatch peppers as the sides. Great weeknight dinner (with meatloaf sandwiches likely as tomorrow’s lunch).

This was a mixed bag in my house. My two boys loved it, but my husband hated the texture and we both found the flavor bizarre - bland but wine-y. The sauce was way too much liquid and way too much wine.  And the bread crumb slurry on top was a soggy mess. I had to drain the pan during cooking.  I'm confident I could tweak it but I don't think it's worth the effort.

Amused by the discussion around Matt's suggestion about pans, even though I did not try it and was happy with the results of following the recipe as written except for using half ground turkey and half ground beef and using tomato paste for the tomatoes/sauce, I'm weighing in with an additional concern, about the wine-mixture topping, i.e., what happens to it when inverting the loaf pan onto the parchment paper . . . ? My meatloaf was done in an hour, and was moist and delicious.

Followed all measurements and instructions carefully but this came out way too moist for me—kind of like a wet, meaty stuffing. I could taste the orange zest, which was nice, but couldn’t taste the wine. Kind of a bland loaf. I did use ground turkey with a higher fat content than usual which I’m sure contributed to the moisture. Maybe I’m just a person who prefers dry meatloaf.

The alcohol didn't cook out of the red wine so it had a slight alcohol taste and the wine makes the panko on top turn purple. The top half of the meat doesn't stick to the bottom half because of the wine layer. I got purple, wine flavored, meat-Twix bars.

Starting this year, Every week we are choosing  a NYT recipe to make. So many flavors come through this meatloaf! The red wine was pronounced nicely yet, like others, felt the orange zest overpowered and had a wonky taste with the other ingredients. I wonder as a leftover, if this will be better. Especially as it crumbled when serving, maybe it will be better as leftover as it sits in frig. I would make This again and keep out the orange zest.
@Sharon — Can concur: stay away from the orange zest. It’s probably

If you turned a meatball into a meatloaf this would be it. 

I didn’t measure out the orange zest but will next time as it was a bit overpowering. Also for next time, I’ll add sun dried tomatoes to the mix and wait until the liquid has cooked off the top before sprinkling with panko.

Made this a year ago, inverted. It was hard and flavorless. I’d forgotten it when I decided to make it last night, then saw my old notes. 
This time...
15% fat turkey, 50/50 with pork. 
Anchovy. 
More garlic, basil, chives. 
Red onion. 
Italian seasoning.
And... draped it all in bacon layered across the loaf pan bottom. 
Did not invert.
Super-juicy and flavorful! (The bacon didn’t cook enough to eat, after 75 minutes!) We’ll have leftovers quite happily tonight. Will even remake, on purpose.

Ran out of space above. 
I only used the bacon because I had leftover and as an experiment. There are better things to do with bacon. Would not use it again for this, although I do think it was a plus.

title: Baked Lemon Pudding With Blueberry Jam Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: First time made as recipe is written.  Used weights where provided.  Very lemony-good but very sweet.  Second time, made per recipe except for reducing sugar by 10% to 180 grams.  That worked to keep structure in the dish (sugar aids structure as well as adding sweetness) and make it taste more lemony-tart.  But this is quite a sweet dish so if that's not what you're looking for, move along. ;-)  A hit at my house.  Baking time in glass 9" pie dish (23 cm) was just shy of 40 minutes to perfect.
@dimmerswitch wonder if just fresh blueberries not jam would be good snd less sweet?

@Katy hi there! I used fresh blueberries right out of the freezer and they were wonderful. This is the only mod I made to the recipe. I let it bake for about 5-6 min longer only because the berries were frozen. Their texture was jammy and full against the lovely airiness of the lemony custard! Hope this helps!

Going to try this with fresh raspberries, mashed with a fork and then dolloped onto the lemon pudding as directed in the recipe. Raspberries are so delicious with lemon curd, maybe this will be even better!
@Kristi I was thinking same approach but with blueberries. Please let us know how it turns out!
Could you make this gluten-free with almond flour?
@Bake A Weil I have found the best GF flour alternative is King Arthur’s one for one
@Bake A Weil I use King Arthur’s 1:1 gluten-free flour mix for all-purpose flour substitutions, which I’m planning to use here, too!
I also use King Arthur's Measure for Measure GF flour to substitute for AP flour. Usually works beautifully, and I prefer it to others I've tried over the years.

We ate this dish at 3 different temps.  Warm, about 15 - 20 min out of oven - more silky.  Room temp - more souffle like, a little pillowy.  Refrigerated to cold - more curd like.  Our favorite for flavor and texture was room temp.
Eggs, lemons, milk, jam...a real old school, easy comforting dish! Superb!
Any suggestions for a alternative to milk
@Frida I just made this with oat milk and it worked! Note that I still used butter. I’m only ~lightly~ lactose intolerant!
@Frida unflavored, unsweetened almond milk works well, we used plant-based butter as well.
@Liz Lactose free whole milk works in recipes every bit as well as milk. I use it exclusively.
Love the NYT Lemon Pudding Cake recipe, how is this different?  Does the slight variation in same ingredients produce a different texture?
@Carolyn to me this tasted more like the filling of a lemon bar but more runny. The lemon pudding cake has way more cake on top.
Simple and yummy - only change I'd make next time is to add a bit less sugar. I love lemon deserts but find them to sweet.

This was good, and not hard to make. I followed others' suggestions and reduced the sugar and upped the lemon zest. I also found a really basic blueberry jam recipe (pint of blueberries, 1/3 cup sugar, 1/2  tsp cinnamon, Tbsp lemon juice; put in small saucepan and cook over medium heat for a half hour, stirring often.  It thickens as it cools), so I made my own, which took the sweetness in the recipe down a notch, too. It even came out looking like the picture! I will definitely make this again.
Can you use fresh blueberries?
I was just thinking about making these with fresh blueberries made into a compote.  Several people think this is very, or even too sweet so you could make a less sweet compote.

@MiddleGround I tried with fresh blueberries, and I think they added a little too much water when they popped. There were little puddles of blue water around the berries. I think making them into a compote would work better
Simple and delicious!!!
Very easy and great Return on time spent :). Will make again. I followed the recipe with only small mods : 150g of sugar instead of 200 and a tad less lemon juice.
Just made it, with homemade blueberry preserves. I used buttermilk, and will try it with reduced sugar next time. But it’s easy and tasty. A very old-fashioned dessert. Quite good.
Could you use frozen blueberries instead of jam?
Replaced blueberry jam with marionberry. Be careful not to overbeat the meringue. Delicious!
@Isaac A, Thanks! Was puzzling over jam choice and have a jar of marionberry right in front of me.

We loved this. I used just a little less sugar (185 g). I massaged the lemon zest into the sugar before adding the butter. This diffuses the lemon oil and intensifies the lemony-ness of the final product (and provides a lovely bit of aromatherapy as a bonus!).
Can I pour this in a pie shell and bake?
@A Melissa in her recipe commentary said "pie plate" works. But only if you send me some.
I'm not an experienced pie maker, but I don't think the custard is thick enough for a pie; also, as it bakes it forms a cake layer on top.
This was very lemony! And sweet! And rich! Proceed with caution!
Got an Advent Calendar full of tiny jars of Bon Maman jams, one of which was raspberry-rose… delicious with the lemon.

Yummy. Topped up the lemon juice from my juiced lemons with some bottled lemon juice. Did not have blueberry jam so mixed a cup of frozen berries with a tsp of sugar & lemon juice, warmed until the juice released then added some cornstarch to firm it up, which resulted in about half a cup of thickened mostly tart berries to dollop on the batter. The result is perfection.
We make a spiced blueberry jam every year in Canada from the berries we find on our property there. I think this would be awesome with that jam and I can’t wait to try it!
We thought of using haskap berry compote. If we could only stop eating from the jar!

I baked in 6 4-inch ramekins for 19 minutes. (I first set the timer for 15 minutes and then I gave it 4 minutes more.) This is super delicious. Definitely company worthy.
But I have one complaint and I never complain about Melissa Clark recipes. I mashed the butter with the sugar, lemon zest and salt. All good. After adding the egg yolks, juice and milk there's no way you can wisk to combine. Butter floats in little blobs. I had to get out the hand mixer and there is no guidance for that.

@Nadine Per the recipe, add the egg yolks before the other liquid ingredients and mash them in. Then add the milk and lemon juice and whisk. That way worked fine for me.

Also, make sure your butter is at room temperature.

Anyone try it fresh blueberries?
It seems like it would be a bit sweet me
@Frida-It works perfectly well with non dairy milk particularly Soy or Coconut

I have made Karen DeMasco's steamed lemon pudding many times,  since it was published in the NYT part of the menu development for the wedding of Chef  Tom Colichhio in August 2001 as part of column called The Chef.  Do you think the blueberry preserves would work with the lemon pudding in ramekins?   and steamed in a bain de marie?
Great recipe.  Very fresh and bright.  After reading comments, I used 2/3 c sugar and could have used less. Will make again

Made this for some fellow lemon-loving ladies at our annual Souper Bowl party (we don’t watch the game, but we get together and everyone brings a soup to try, it’s very fun and fabulous and good), and it was a hit. Made as directed, was concerned with how wet the batter was when pouring in the pie dish. Let it bake for about 10 mins longer than suggested and it set well, did sink for me just a tiny bit but that may be because I added extra jam dollops. Also, wasn’t paying attention in the jam aisle and grabbed blackberry preserves by accident— turned out beautifully anyway. Feedback included, but wasn’t limited to, “The best lemon dessert.” Will be making again!
Made as written but substituted jam with 1/2 cup of fresh blueberries mixed with teaspoon of lemon juice and a sprinkle of sugar. Beautiful.
Gosh this was easy and outstanding!  Wondering if I could bake this in an aluminum pie pan as I plan to make it as a give-away item.
@Enigma I would NOT bake acidic food in aluminum.

@Enigma. 
Look for yard sale or thrift shops ceramic or glass containers to give away-no return required and no aluminum in the trash.
I made this with GF flour, dairy free butter, and coconut milk. It was divine! So good to find a pudding that still tastes delicious when I make necessary dietary changes.
To combat sweetness, how about adding all fruit preserves that have no added sugar.

Concerning GF options, I would probably use King Arthur's GF "measure for measure" flour rather than a nut or bean flour since it will have the closest texture and behavior to actual wheat flour. I'm GF and I have had good results with it for most substitutions.

Made it for a neighborhood potluck.  It was a hit.  I used what I had on hand, so substituted blueberry jam with cranberry jam and used 1/2 cup cream and 1/2 cup of skim milk instead of whole milk. Also my new oven cooks faster, so I could have taken it out in 25 minutes.
I followed others' comments and put in about 10% less sugar. It was still plenty sweet.  I loved this recipe so much I am looking for similar recipes. Any ideas?

Yummy!  I used slightly less sugar and the Stonewall Kitchen blueberry jam with lots of tiny Maine blueberries.  Other than the aggravation of zesting the lemon, a pretty easy dessert.  I did not agree that it was too sweet but I had juicy lemons so the flavor may have been a bit more sour as a result.

Baked for the first time yesterday. After reading through comments made two adjustments to tone down sweetness: cut the sugar in half and added a rounded tablespoon of cornstarch to the flour. Five stars. And I enjoyed it more after a night in the fridge- the lemon seemed more pronounced.
Could this be turned into a single crust pie?
Gluten free Measure for measure flours are pretty good, especially if you don’t understand the chemistry.   ps I don’t
I was wondering if anybody has tried baking it in ramekins for individual portions? thanks

I just made this, and I got the beautiful soufflé top, but the bottom is more lemon syrup than lemon pudding. Any suggestions?
Thanks!
@Joe same here. Trying to figure out if I did something wrong. Seemed like too much liquid.

@Silvia 
I’m having similar questions, did it need to cook longer or sit longer prior to eating

I just took my out of the oven, let it sit for 10 min and had the same issue! I baked it in a ceramic grain dish. Unsure what could be the cause, other than perhaps it may need to be baked in a bath to help firm up the bottom?

This was amazing! Everyone loved it. It served six of us, just. I think we would all gladly have had more. Ignoring all suggestions to the contrary, I followed the recipe to the T--except that I used 2% Lactaid, which seemed fine. O served it room temperature thanks to a suggestion (and so I could enjoy our dinner party). Next time, I'm going to try serving it warm, just to see. We decided to serve it without ice cream, and I think that was the right choice. As advertised, it was firm and custardy on the bottom and light and soufflé-ish on top. Amazing. Would definitely make again!

Made this today, cut down on the sugar by a couple of tablespoons and dolloped in homemade roselle hibiscus ginger jam. Texture like a lemon bar without the crust. Bright, very lemon forward taste, not too sweet as some have commented- more tangy than sweet.
leftovers are one of my son’s favorite breakfasts
Has anyone made this with Meyer lemons?
Yes - I used 4 Meyers to get 1/2 cup of juice; didn't get a full 2 tbsp of zest since the lemons were small (but very juicy).
Do I grease the pan?

I found this a bit too lemony for my taste.  Easy to make - I reduced the sugar a bit, as per suggestions below.  My suggestion, if you are short on time:  after you've whipped the egg whites, pour into your electric mixer the batter, and whip it there a bit again!  Saves time, betters mix quality.
I made this Super Bowl weekend . Followed the recipe to the key . Absolutely delicious !!! Easy and tasty , will definitely make again .

I followed the recipe to a T and it came out deliciously lemony tart, which was balanced by the sugar.  The blueberry jam balanced the tartness as well.  It got better as it sat in the fridge but didn't last longer than 2 days because it was outstanding.  I used a 9" pie plate and next time I would use something smaller so that I have a taller pudding to scoop into.  I liked it cold and my husband, lover of all things lemony, preferred it warm.

I was obsessed with trying this as soon as I saw it! I had never heard of baked pudding before.
I was so careful not to knock the air out when I was folding the egg whites in but it still came out with a soggy bottom and the blueberry jam basically disappeared.
What did I do wrong?
It was still amazing flavor wise but it didn't have the nice texture all the way through just on the top.
Sincerely,
Wondering in Denver

Easy! But definitely could reduce the sugar to 2/3 cup and (as I’m learning my oven runs hot) lower temp so it doesn’t brown too fast and cooks all the way through. I had lemon curd on bottom but happy to scoop that part onto thick plain yogurt for breakfast. Will try again.

Definitely a winner!!! Super easy. I had fresh blueberries so made a quick compote w blueberries, sugar & lemon. Let it cool completely & it worked perfectly. My sister & I had to stop ourselves from eating the whole thing right out of the pan. (Used a deep dish blue glass pie plate & it was lovely.)
Any thoughts on using Oatly milk instead? Cow dairy allergy here :(

This looks so delicious, but I am lactose intolerant. Are there any suggestions for how I could adapt this with a nondairy milk or other liquid? (I've made puddings and custards with soy milk, oat milk, and thinned out yogurt, but the quality varies and I suspect depends on the specific recipe.) Thanks for any help!

I make way too many jams for the same reason that some people buy them.  I suppose any jam would be delicious, but just wondering if anyone has tried strawberry, fig, apricot, blackberry, raspberry . . . 1/3 cup won't help much to delete my stores, but just wondering.  Also, any recipe ideas for using up a full jar of jam?
I made it with blueberry lavender jam.  It was very good.
@Lisa G. British recipe Jam Tart. Fill pie case with any jam. Bake!
Use jam to make a fruity sauce to use with pork, chicken or sausages. Lots of guidance for the technique on the web.

Love this. But too sweet for hubs. Tried substituting with a mixture of xylitol and monk fruit 25% less, and it was a flop. Missing all the fluffy texture. Anyone have any luck with making it less sweet? Maybe some cream of tartar?

I've been in the habit for a few years of substituting monk fruit sugar for regular sugar, using about 2/3's monk fruit to 1 cup sugar. It has worked (don't like xylitol or stevia, but monk fruit made by Lakanto maintains the taste and texture.

I had really high hopes but this fell flat. I cut the sugar down by 50% and it was still way too sweet. Texture might have changed because how much sugar I took out.  It really just wasn’t my favorite. Lemon and blueberry were great together.
Reduced 1C sugar to 3/4c and still waaay too sweet.

First time made as per recipe instructions, but next time I'll add just a bit of flour (say, 40 grams) to have the pudding more structured and I'll use fresh blueberries in lieu of the jam, which melts too much while in the oven. Excellent in any case

This was good, but has the same problem for me as any cold flour with acid (lemon bars, pasta salad, etc.): a strong bitter aftertaste that renders such things inedible. 

My kids liked this but I couldn't eat it. Evidently they don't get the aftertaste. Does anyone know what this is or how to avoid it?
Really an excellent, quick dessert—raspberry jam dollops were terrific!
Made this three times and loved it every time.  Love that it can be eated at various temps -- so helpful when planning a dinner with friends (they loved it too.)  I used a little less blueberry jam.

Yum yum yum. Followed suggestion to reduce sugar by 50% and it was perfect, with a nice cakey top and gooey pudding-y underneath. Used sour cherry jam because that’s what I had. About 35 minutes and a light dusting of powdered sugar, and I’ll definitely be making it again

This was so good! After reading the comments, I was a little concerned about how sweet it might be, so I very minimally cut back on the sugar and used fresh raspberries, and blueberries. It turned out divine. Just the right amount of lemon Tang versus sweetness. Definitely will be making it again.
This was very pleasant and indeed very lemony.

@Carolyn, the lemon pudding cake is baked in a water bath, which is how it separates into a delicious custardy bottom and a cake like top.  I haven't tried this yet so don't know how largely the same ingredients without the water bath (though less liquid altogether) creates a pudding texture.  Have to try it to compare!
@Pickle. Recipe doesn’t say warm bath , but that makes sense. The pudding part came out too loose. Maybe that’s why.
Could I sub plant based milk & butter?
Should the glass dish be greased?
No need.  I have always made this without any butter.
Can one substitute strawberry jam?
@Robert A.—Why not? I think it would be delicious.
Way too sweet,,,,,,adjust sugar,   but I like the idea
What consistency should the batter be (before adding beaten egg whites)? Mine was pretty fluid, but seems like it should be thicker…
That's how mine was and it turned out fine
Would this be able to be made ahead and frozen?

Made two pans of this pudding without reading the comments first (lesson learned) and they are way too sweet, inedible. I’m wondering if there was a typo in the recipe?. Do I throw both away or any suggestions?

This is lemon meringue without the pie shell!! YUM!   A very adaptable and solid recipe. I used soy milk and I used raspberry jam and it turned out well.  Tonight I might try real raspberries with the jam on top.

I made this, per directions. My husband made blueberry-lemon jam to be spooned on top, and it was amazing! The jam cut the sweetness and gave it an almost mouth-puckering flavor (we love lemons!). This will be on regular rotation during lemon season!

I see that some people are adding the batter to the egg whites once beaten, and then mixing both with beaters. Since the recipe calls for folding in the egg whites, will mixing together with beaters make any difference?
I concur with others who noted how sweet this was. I cut the sugar by 30% and still found it to be far too sweet. I won’t major this again.
This was way too sweet!   It looked amazing, but we won’t make this again.
Adhered to the recipe (using my homemade jam) and this was perfect—a delicious, perfect balance between sweet and tart.
I will mash my fresh blueberries and mix a wee bit of starch in. Hopefully that will reduce extra liquid and the sweetness people have commented on.
I was wondered if anyone tried using allulose as a sweetener in this recipe.
This is a super delicious and very easy dessert. Another winner from Ms Clark!
This was such an unexpected hit. It's tart and while it says it feeds six, can confirm that four hungry men cleaned the dish QUITE easily.
Just wondering if you could reduce the sugar a bit, without compromising the texture?

Natalie - You ask "...if you could reduce the sugar a bit, without compromising the texture?"  Good that you know sugar adds structure as well as sweetness.  (I find a lot of home cook / bakers don't know the structure part.)  When I want to reduce sugar I start with the original recipe.  Then on later tries I start with a 15-20% redux to see how that goes.  And take it from there.  Good luck for delicious outcome.  As written this is very good even if quite sweet.

I wonder if this would make a good Pie. I think the crust might help with folks feeling it's too sweet. 
Thinking of 2025 State Fair.
Easy and delicious.
Can this be re-warmed after being refrigerated?
Hubby just asked the same question as we are licking the bowls! I'm thinking reheating would produce an "overcooked" taste and texture like reheating scrambled eggs. I might reheat a bit to find out.
Has anyone tried cooking in individual ramekins?

I made this yesterday.  It came out of the oven looking beautiful and just like the photo, but when I cut into it (long after it cooled), I got lemon flavored liquid goo. I followed the recipe. What did I do wrong?
We got the "goo" too, but, it thickened up as it cooled. Flavor sooooo good we don't want to abandon it, will play with oven temp/cook times and pan size.

Same here.  Lemony goo.  I tried a similar recipe that had less milk ( 3/4 cup vs. 1 cup), extra egg  ( 4 total), and more flour ( 1/2 cup vs. 1/4 cup).  this made a big difference.  The pudding part was not thick, but was not runny.  And the cake was light.
Did you measure by weight or by cup? Maybe weighing will help with the consistency?
No eggs. What desserts can flax “egg” be used in?
First time cooking exactly as written. Very lemony! Needed to sit until room temp to get a "pudding" texture on the bottom. Looking forward to serving this cold. Could be an excellent Summer dessert.
This was a delicious success. I was skeptical of the texture as it was going into the oven but it came out beautifully.
I made this last night and it was very liquid.  Tasty but so runny!  I am at 6000 ft.  is there something I should have done differently?
My husband is diabetic so I would like to make a sugar free version.  Does anyone know if the sugar can be replaced with a monk fruit blend?
Filling is tangy and sweet. The unexpectedly tender base is a KEEPER. I'll use this in place of graham cracker crumb crusts in the future.
I made it with only half the sugar and we all found it was too sweet. I also did not zest the lemons very finely, leading to a polenta-like consistency at the bottom. Won't do it again.
It was too runny. Didn’t care for it. The berries didn’t help. I thought it made it runny by adding the berries.
I don't have a glass pie dish but a ceramic Polish pottery one. Will that make a big difference?

Made as written but this did not work at all, it was a runny mess after an extra 10 minutes in the oven. We're avid bakers and have made baked pudding before without issue, maybe our jam was too runny? But we are now an hour late to our dinner party as we scramble to throw something else together. Not worth the risk, try something else.
Followed directions perfectly and got a runny mess. I recommend the suggestions of more flour, a starch and more egg yolk.

I used a little less than 1 cup sugar, and more lemon zest, as described in another comment. 4 small egg yolks/whites cuz that what I had. LOVED this dessert. Served at room temperature. Spot on. Excellent lemon curd flavor, slightly cake-like on the edges, creamy as jazz towards center. This could be done with any jam, but blueberry was outstanding and I'd do raspberry, too. I'll make this again and again.

I thought this was wonderful. Per the suggestion of others, I put almost all of the batter in the pan before I added the jam. I reserved just enough batter to barely cover the jam, and it came out perfectly with the jam in the middle of the cake. For those of you who have issues with the cake sticking to your Bundt pan, I always chill the Bundt pan thoroughly before brushing it with melted butter. This allows the butter to harden a bit and stick in place in all of those nooks and crannies. I used half cinnamon and half cardamom in my sugar, and the taste was wonderful. I think next time I would add some lemon zest to the batter as well.
@carla i love this tip about chilling the bundt pan!

@carla
But if you refrigerated the pan before adding melted butter, wouldn't the butter harden and then the cinnamon sugar coating wouldn't stick to the sides of the pan?
@carla really great suggestions!

So some of the jam sank to the bottom of the Bundt pan and stuck. It wasn't a crisis, simply pried those bits loose, reattached, flipped cake over and the taste was wonderful.
Next time, I think I'll put more than half of the batter on the bottom in an attempt to avoid this issue.
@Sapelo thanks for the tip! I used 2/3 of the batter on the bottom. Still had 3 small spots leak though so also advise to be extra delicate with spooning in the filling.
@Sapelo same for me. It’s still delicious but now looks very rustic!

This cake has a nice flavour, much like a coffee cake, and is moist. The outside has a crispness which is more like a buttermilk doughnut than a jelly doughnut. I think adding some lemon juice or zest to the batter would add a nice touch, as well as a lemon sugar glaze instead of the butter and cinnamon sugar. I had so much butter and sugar leftover after brushing the finished cake, I think that could be cut by 1/2 or more.

@No Tariffs Please
agree…cut the sugar cinnamon butter in 1/2  (for coating pan and finishing cake ). takes maybe a tablespoon to fully coat inside of pan.
@No Tariffs Please yup I’ve got to agree you can cut the sugar and cinnamon by half. And still have plenty
@No Tariffs Please how much zest would you recommend adding? Novice baker here
Would a lemon curd work in place of the jelly? Appreciate any guidance -

@Maire
What a great idea!

@Maire I tried this with curd. Couple of thoughts: the curd is really delicate and basically absorbed into the batter. Delicious flavor but not the seam of custard I was hoping to see. I am going to try again - this time injecting the curd into the baked cake, like a jelly-filled donut. Fingers crossed!

@jennifer
Great idea to try injecting lemon curd into the cooked cake. Did anyone try this? Any feedback on this idea?

Thanks, MC. The perfect showcase for the last of my luscious, homemade dark cherry jam! 

Omitted the cinnamon to let the cherry-almond flavor shine, but think it would be lovely with other fruit preserves. Perhaps even cardamom sugar with blackberry next?! Or cranberry, with a dash of orange zest, post-Thanksgiving?
@Western Winnie   yummy that sounds delicious!!!
@Western Winnie good call(s)!!!

@Western Winnie
A friend gave me some blackberry jam. I followed your lead and cut the topping cinnamon to 1 tsp and added a 1/2 tsp of cardamom. I added a 1/2 tsp cardamom along with nutmeg and a few drops of lime oil to the cake. The cardamom is fantastic.

I used a pastry bag (actually just rolled parchment paper) to "apply" the jam into the layer of batter. Nice, even line when cut. Made it with fig jam (received 4 jars of it at Christmas!) which was great with the cinnamon and not too sweet. Oddly, I also received a vanilla & fig scented candle--so this cake must have felt right at home.

I baked this lovely cake today. I used 2% milk & it was fine. I used Stonewall Farm Wild Maine blueberry jam which was an excellent choice as it is full of fruit & not too sweet. I will definitely make this again. Nice recipe using pantry ingredients and no electric mixer.

I made this twice today.  I agree with another commenter that it feels more like a buttermilk doughnut texture but the tang is missing, so it is perhaps a little flat-flavored.  I might sub in some sour cream next time.  My 2nd cake stuck to the non-stick pan and that was annoying, but I'll try it again with a silicone pan and see what happens.  It is a pretty cake, and the unbroken cake from today is going out as a gift.
@Umami I hope you update us. I would love to know if the cake still has a crisp exterior when you use the silicone pan

@Umami

What do you think about using buttermilk?

Silicone pan worked perfectly, the crust was crisp.  I reduced the butter by a couple of tablespoons and added a scant 1/4 cup of sour cream.  I think buttermilk would be a good idea, I might try that next time.

And for the comments about all of the leftover cinnamon sugar, I put my leftovers in a container last time I made this, and used that today.  And it is there to put on oatmeal and toast, so there's no waste.

I agree with the previous reviewers. Next time I make it I will put in 2/3-3/4 of the batter, as I did experience light sinkage on one side of the cake. It was not a huge issue, but would have liked the jam a little more centered and it will sink some during baking.  I also only used about 1/2 of the cinnamon sugar.  My husband's comment, "There is no dessert I would have preferred tonight more than this."

Tasty! This easily fit in my smaller, 10c Bundt pan. Cake is moist and the jam quantity is good. Overall it seems like the cake would benefit from more batter. Even after putting about 70% in the bottom and being careful about jam placement, a bit of jam leaked through and caused the cake to stick in one place. I’ll try again increasing the batter x1.5
@Michelle in NY If you’ve since made it at 1.5 X the batter, did it work better? Can you give us (me) the amounts you used? I stink at math! Thanks!

This was the easiest cake ever and the flavor did NOT disappoint! Followed others guidance and gave plenty of bottom layer so my fruit did not bleed through. I sprayed my pan with baking spray instead of butter because there’s so much other butter and that’s easier. I doubled the vanilla, added. Tablespoon of sour cream and used seeded raspberry preserves as well as cardamom. I think this cake calls for too much sugar and I will just do a cup next time instead of 1.5. My husband lost his mind at how good this cake was and wants me to make it with apricot and pecans next.
Used standard Bundt but it did not rise to the top of the pan. I made a lemon glaze, used raspberry jam and it was good. I think I overbaked it a little as the outside was almost like a bread crust
Lemon marmalade in place of jam with lemon sugar on top? Trying it today.
This is a great idea, also want to try it with lemon curd
What size bundt pan should I use?  If my bundt pan is too small can I use extra batter in muffin tins?

I don't have a Bundt pan. My makeshift "Bundt" pan, ( a round pan with an empty, weighted tomato paste can in the center) would be too small.
I looked at the recipe and really wanted to make it, so I am going to try muffin tins. I am pretty sure it will work.
@june Thrift stores almost always have Bundt pans.

to Heidi:
I am a Salvation Army junkie. I see Bundt pans all the time there.
I just do not have the space for more equipment. The makeshift one usually does the job.
I made the recipe in muffin tins. Turned out okay, just a little too sweet.
Nice! Love how simple it was to make. I cut the recipe in half and used a small Bundt pan.

Great recipe.  I made 12 cupcakes, used King Arthur Measure for Measure gluten free flour.  Reduced sugar to 1/2 cup. Used cupcake liners. No need to grease tins.  Did not have whole milk, substituted 1/2 cup 2% milk and plain non fat Greek yogurt to equal 3/4 cup of milk.  Did not use nutmeg, husband is allergic. Topped with cinnamon sugar before baking, and dusted with confectioners sugar when cooled.

I will add that I had too much cinnamon sugar left over and would reduce by almost half next time. I did also flip the cake over and bushed the remaining melted butter & cinnamon sugar on that part, too .

I have a recipe for Linzer muffins that is quite similar and an all-time favorite with my family.  Lemon zest and almond extract are the additional ingredients, which create a depth of flavor I love.  There is no topping which is fine for muffins.  Love the idea of a lemon glaze.
Before I make... would this be good for breakfast? Or is it more of a cake?
@Diana E cake is perfect breakfast food!
@Diana E had this for breakfast this morning and can confirm it was a very good idea.

I wasn't careful about doing exactly half so the jam was a little too close to the top and it stuck. It was still delicious and easy enough to make on a Sunday afternoon with kids trying to "help" me. I'd also reduce the sugar and butter for the topping next time, although my kids didn't complain about the extra sugar on top.
Awww!  I miss having my kids help! All grown and flown the nest. Will try this recipe again when grandkids come for a visit.

Definitely more like a coffee cake. Mine came out a bit dry, so maybe another commenter's suggestion to use sour cream might be helpful (or risk a bit more jam). I also cut the toppings (both the butter and the cinnamon and sugar) in half and had plenty.
Hi!  Does anyone know if this recipe would work with almond flour for a gluten free version?  Or have experience with substituting wheat flour in general.  New to gluten free world.
@Aimee I am celiac and always use Bob’s Red Milk cup for cup and never had any issues!

@Aimee I have not tested it in this specific cake, but I recommend King Arthur 1:1 Gluten Free Flour. I sometimes use almond flour in baked goods that specifically call for it. Almond flour makes a great substitution for graham cracker crumbs. The NYT has a gluten free almond flour chocolate chip cookie recipe that’s excellent.

This recipe had me at “ONE BOWL” but also managed to really have a true donut-y texture and flavor. I screwed up and combined the butter and cinnamon sugar and attempted to coat the Bundt pan with that. I think it improved the crust and made it even donuttier! There was a lot of butter-sugar mix left over and I dumped it on top of the finished cake and how could that be bad?

Used Bob’s Red Mill 1:1 gluten free flour.  Subbed organic nonfat Greek yogurt for milk which made dough stiff.  Thrive Market organic blueberry fruit spread.  Cut sugar back to 1 cup - next time will cut to 3/4 cup.  Topping amount was way too much and cake would be fine without it.  Must add more than half of dough to pan and then spread fruit.  Stiffer dough made making a channel for the fruit easier and no leakage.  Very tasty and was well appreciated.
Has anyone tried a gluten-free blend instead of regular flour?
This was amazing and a huge hit with guests. Super easy and delicious the next morning with coffee. I also just used about 1/2 the butter/cinnamon sugar for the outside. And blackberry jam. Perfect.

I think you omitted that after cooling the cake for 10 min. in the bundt pan, and removing it by "flipping it upside down on a rack", that you have to flip it over again so that rounded top is upright and can receive the melted butter and cinnamon sugar glazing.

@ALIX HOWELL
Flipping it once from the pan onto the rack will leave it on the correct position.
would nutella work instead of jam?

This is a keeper! My spouse and I managed to stretch its consumption over a week. Now it’s gone, and I am sad! The only modification I made was using buttermilk because I didn’t have whole milk (and reduced the leavening because I like at a higher altitude). I had a triple berry jam, and it was perfect.

For gluten free folks out there, I’m happy to report this works beautifully with the King Arthur Measure for Measure GF flour blend. I always weigh ingredients so added it all exactly according to the recipe. I did add one extra egg as I find most gf flours need some extra moisture and hydration. It popped out of the Bundt mild beautifully after one hour of cooking, no sticking! I put the jelly on top of the batter and saved just enough batter to cover it, so none sunk in. Looks like the photo! So delicious. I skipped the extra melted butter on top as I liked the slightly crispy outside.
My jam also sunk to the bottom of the pan, not nearly as pretty as the photo.  I agre that the topping is probably too much.  But still was tasty.  Nutmeg was a nice touch.
Has anybody made this at high altitude?  Wondering if it would need adjustments.

I try to make recipes as close to the recipe as possible before making modifications ( hate the people who post entirely different receipts for comments  from the get go) but I did follow the suggestions about the batter (put about 3/4 down before jam- you don’t need much to cover) and I did add lemon zest to the batter -and used 1 tsp cardomom in place of half of the cinnamon. I love cardomom and do this in other recipes. Those changes were all good. (And the batter suggestion is MANDATORY they should really change the recipe). I liked it and will make again with additional modifications: for my taste, the cake is too dense, buttery and sweet. I will sub out some of the butter for sour cream and reduce the sugar. I will also add another teaspoon of extract- probably almond- needs more flavor. Also, you only need about a third of the cinnamon sugar they suggest. You can cut down. I am using the rest for toast and cereal now but would definitely not make as much in the future. But worthwhile and interesting came that can be dessert or breakfast- my favorite!

This cake is a bit flat - I used a standard Bundt pan but I think it needs a smaller pan for better presentation/better rise.  Also had issues with jam leakage - the first bake it all settled at the bottom so wasn't great getting out of the pan and looked like a mess.  I baked again, because of course, it was going to a dinner.  The 2nd time I saved more batter to apply after the jam - it did turn out better but still was not quite there.  I will try a smaller pan next time and see if that helps
Half of the cinnamon-sugar recipe is plenty.  Even with lemon zest and additional grated nutmeg the cake lacks flavor—vanilla might help.  There isn’t enough batter for a 12-cup Bundt pan.

I think a lot of folks are missing the keyword “generously” here. I made this recipe exactly as written, and it turned out perfect. For the topping, I used the full amounts of butter, sugar, and cinnamon called for and used all of it. I generously coated the entire Bundt pan with the melted butter and cinnamon-sugar mixture, then once the cake came out of the oven, I brushed the remaining mixture over the entire exterior of the cake. My Hanukkah guests raved about it!
Made today. I only had 2% milk but it worked out fine. I used Stonewall
Nice idea, but too sweet for me. I could have skipped the topping maybe, or used less sugar in the batter.

So, I experienced many of the same problems that the other bakers have talked about. The next time I make this, I will double the recipe. I used all 12 cup Bundt pan and it didn't even fill it to halfway. Plus, the jam sink to the bottom of the pan and my cake stuck. Next time I make this, I will double the recipe and put the jam on the top. It is going to sink anyway. Other than that, it's a really good cake. I'm especially fond of the crispiness of the exterior.
What size Bundt pan?

Turned out perfectly exactly as written. Used homemade blackberry cassis jam and had no issues with the jam sinking or the cake sticking. I used a 12 cup bundt (45 minute bake time) but will use a 10 cup next time (and there will be a next time).

Re Altitude adjustments: Cooking at 7,000 feet altitude, I always reduce fat, sugar and baking powder. However, this cake has less fat and sugar than some other bundt cake recipes. I might try it as is, maybe reducing the baking powder a pinch.

I took advantage of previous comments (and common sense) by substituting buttermilk for whole milk--not sure why that wasn't a go-to ingredient for this cake-donut-flavored cake in the first place. Also: made 1.5x the cake batter called for in the recipe, which helped with preventing any jam leakage, and used local marionberry jam (yum). It ended up needing a full 50 minutes of baking time, but it was so delicious! I will definitely make it again, as it would surely please a crowd.

This was great! As suggested in the comments, I put about 3/4 of the batter on the bottom, used a spoon to create a trough for the jam and barely had enough batter to cover the jam. It stuck to the bottom in only one small place and I think that’s because I dug a little too deep in the area with my spoon. I too had a lot of butter and cinnamon sugar left over so those proportions can absolutely be scaled down. My kids loved the cake but not the blueberry jam. Will make it again with 4 fruits jam

I never bake, but could not resist the description as a large jelly doughnut.  I followed the guidance of some commenters to make 1.5x batter and put most of the batter before the jelly.  Even with 1.5x batter, it did not rise to the top of the bundt pan.  The batter seemed super thick and had no issue with any of the jam sinking or sticking.

Came out tasty!
Delicious! Made the cake exactly as written, except I used Pam on the pan, then continued with the recipe.

This is a fun, easy cake to make, the results are spectacular.  I put most of the batter in before adding the jam.  Worked like a charm and it ended up in the middle. I'm going to make it again today with cherry preserves and add a smidge of almond extract.  I used a 10-cup Bundt and it was the perfect size.  If using a larger Bundt I'd suggest 1.5 times the recipe.  Be careful not to overbake. I let mine go too long bc the part closest to the jam still came back with a wet toothpick. 5 stars!

Thank goodness for all the helpful comments. Best hack was chilling the pan. The butter starts to firm up immediately and it's much easier to dust with the sugar. Using a piping bag for the jam is great. Do add a bit more vanilla and some sour cream if you want. In spite of trying to fill the pan with almost all of the batter, the jam still settled. So the one comment I will follow the next time is 1.5 times the batter. The cake released from the pan quite easily and tastes great.

I’m a seasoned, deliberate baker. Following this recipe (save for reducing sugar in batter by half,) by way of preparing my Bundt pan, measuring all the ingredients ahead of mixing the batter, and washing the bowls (yes, you need several bowls to melt butter, mix topping, measure dry ingredients, and then the utensils,) it took me three (3!) hours before it was ready to bake. I found that layering the batter-jam-batter in the pan was extremely fussy. My cake required 45 minutes to bake. It released so fast, that part of the cake landed on my hand (holding the rack, as it was being inverted) and lava-hot jam burned me good! Happily, this cake was made for my family, not guests. The flavor of the cake is fine, but I’m not personally reminded of doughnuts. If I used the amount of sugar called for in the batter, it would be inedible. If you are looking for an excellent berry cake that is delightful for breakfast, and any time of day, consider making Samantha Seneviratne’s crumb cake. I sub fresh raspberries for the cranberries, but any berry will work. IMHO, it is much easier, faster, and more delicious. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1020590-cranberry-crumb-cake
@alisonia Halving the sugar will negatively impact the cake’s structure - something seasoned bakers are careful to avoid.
@alisonia. Try the recipe with the ingredients called for. It’s a dangerously delicious treat!

Thanks for all the tips. 
Piping the jam into the batter using parchment paper made it so much easier. Also used cardamom, allspice, and cinnamon in the batter.

OMG. This is absolutely delicious. I had to use a tube pan (because I don’t own a bundt pan), and the only thing I can imagine that would be different is that I couldn’t get the cinnamon and sugar to cling to the nearly-vertical sides of the cake after it came out of the pan, but it is still delicious. The cake is tender and flavorful, with a moist, tight crumb. The crust is almost crisp, as it really were a fried doughnut. I followed the advice here and put the jam on about 3/4 of the batter in the pan, and even so it all sank to the bottom (top when inverted onto a plate). No matter, though - this is amazing!
If I only have 2% milk, how will that affect the cake? Would it be less moist?
Any suggestions as to what type of jelly to use to get that old fashioned jelly donut from the bakery?
They are usually filled with a seedless raspberry filling/jam. Hope that helps!
No Bundt or ring pan on hand (and I'm only baking for two, anyway). I'll try cutting the recipe in half and using a loaf pan.
I am not seeing how the cook time is 3hrs. I almost past this recipe up due to the time needed but I believe it is a misprint.
There's two hours of cooling time...
I wonder how well this cake would travel in the mail being sent overseas.  It looks so good I’d like to send one to my friend in the u.k.
Would love to bake this but can’t do dairy. Would oat or other non dairy milk work- perhaps adding some sour cream (non dairy)?

Followed the recipe to the letter. Unfortunately the jam sunk to the bottom and I couldn't get the cake out as a whole. Rather bland in terms of taste as well. I think it needs more sugar. I will not be making this again.
Good tip on using seedless jam. I wonder if mixing the jam with a little simple syrup or lemon juice would make it less dense and less likely to sink?

@Elizabeth I haven’t tried it, but my guess is that thinning the jam with any liquid will make it more likely to spread out into the cake (rather than keeping it together as a core). The key to less sinkage is probably just putting more than half of the batter underneath the jam in the assembly process, so the cake is baked before it can sink too far. The bigger the Bundt pan, the more this will be an issue (half the batter will be a thinner layer in a 12-cup pan than in a 9-cup pan).
Think you could make muffins with this instead of a whole cake?

This can be made in a smaller Bundt mold. The batter fills maybe half the standard 10-12 cup mold pictured. (Same used at home, barely rose halfway.) Added Meyer lemon zest to sugar mix before incorporating into liquids.
Can i make this with a regular round cake pan instead of a bundt pan
I'm a cinnamon addict, so the amount was fine with me. I wouldn't suggest cutting it by that much. Some of my melted butter mix coated the bundt pan, as per recipe.
What size bundt can is recommended?
Well, yes, you mix the batter in a single bowl, but by the time I melted 2 portions of butter and made the cinnamon sugar topping, I had a lot to clean up!
I placed 2/3 of the batter in before laying down the jam , plus added some sour cream and grated lemon peel. Turned out perfect.  I would use more jam !

I had trouble with the Jam sinking to the bottom of the bundt pan and it stuck and pulled apart the cake when I flipped it. not a big deal, still very tasty cake. would make again and maybe use less jam after most of the batter is in. the butter sugar coating was fussy and I had a lot of melted butter and sugar mix left over.

I made exactly as directed EXCEPT I used a tube pan because all my bunt pans are too contoured to risk a clean release.  The cake
still stuck as the jam sunk to the bottom despite putting it just below the surface.  The result resembled the famous plum tart .    The flavor however was fantastic.   The raspberry jam pairs perfectly with the texture of the cake.    Will definitely make this again (and hope I don’t eat the whole thing the next time around)

I made this today and I’ve never gotten so many compliments on a cake!
