I used more than 3/4 of the batter before adding the jam, and topped with the small amount of remaining batter to prevent any leakage of jam/sticking. I also added the zest of one lemon and about 2 tsp of cardamom to the batter and some to the cinnamon sugar coating. Some comments have said that the cinnamon sugar coating is too much, but I used nearly all of it by coating generously both times.
this was divine. but i found that it def needed 50mins in my oven

This was very good. I loved the texture and followed the suggestions to fill the bottom with more batter then pipe around the jam and top with the remaining batter. The jam ended up in the middle when done.  I live at high altitude (8500ft) so I had make some adjustment: added an extra egg, cut the sugar by 1/4, cut the baking powder in half and increased the temp to 370, decreased bake time to 40 minutes. I am always happy when baking works out the first time.

Based on comments, I added about 1/3 c of plain Greek yogurt for moistness and used buttermilk instead of whole. I also put more batter in the  base layer. 

I vigorously stirred up my raspberry jam to loosen it first, which made it easy to spoon into an even ring. The jam didn’t leak through, but I was surprised at how much it sank. 

Rave reviews from neighbors and family. The nutmeg and cinnamon sugar helps give it a donut vibe. Easy to make, keeps well, popped out easily from my decorative Nordicware pan. I’m looking forward to trying other fruit jams.
Very tasty though I should have baked it for 40 min instead of 50.  As another person pointed out, way too much butter and sugar mix for the topping.  You can easily cut that in half
@Kathy I agree I made this yesterday and  I did 45 and it was too much.
After making this twice, I discovered that if I chill the jam overnight, it does not sink into the cake.  It always stays right where it is put.
Alternating dollops of last seasons citrus marmalade and plum made for a beautiful cake!!

I followed this exact, and used the Whole Foods 365 brand blueberry spread (in the jam/jelly aisle). This came out perfect. The blueberry spread is very thick, and made of tiny blueberries. There was no spread/leaking. Huge hit at work!

I did a modified version today at the request. I puréed apple pie filling as the jam and followed the recipe, and used two muffin tins instead of a Bundt. Also fab. Melissa Clark recipes never fail!

Which size


I wish I had used a much smaller pan. This is a very small cake batter for so much jam. Also Pam would help a lot.

Delicious! Followed the instructions and put half the batter in the pan topped with the jam and the other half on top. The jam was a bit unruly, when I topped it with the rest of the batter the jam slipped outside the center. Anyone has a tip for that please let me know! The cake came out cleanly. Reheated and poured the butter/cinnamon/sugar on top, it hardened as the cake cooled. It was crunchy cinnamon-y scrumptiousness, with the flavor of the dough and the Bonne Maman 4 red fruit jam *****!

I liked the crust on the outside and the Bundt (it’s not a cake) was pretty easy to make, but overall I found this recipe very bland. The jelly provides the much-needed punch of flavor. I think it would be better with a buttermilk and a very heavy dollop of vanilla paste.
Two things make this a winner: One bowl and the simplicity of the flavors. I accidentally added more nutmeg than what the recipe calls for and that really gives it a special kick.
Delicious! Made this twice, second time with apple jam from NYT and it was even better.
I used a 9.5 Bundt pan and its was way too big. Hardly enough batter! I would probably try an 8” next time.
Has anyone subbed oat milk or soy milk for the whole milk?
Two hours wait time? Seems cruel. Forty one minutes left. We are salivating

Made it as written, with mixed berry jam and sugar reduced to 1 cup. It's a good cake, but not as easy as its "one-bowl" title suggests.
- As others noted, it would be helpful if the size of the bundt pan was specified. Mine was too large for this recipe
- Cinnamon overpowered the berry jam flavor. Next time I wouldn't use it at all, and add lemon zest to the batter instead. Cinnamon may work better with a different filling.

Very yummy, and not difficult, with the caveat that I did not succeed in preventing all the jam from sinking and sticking to the pan; some did that. I didn't greatly mind this, but it might be better to add more than 1/2 of the batter to the bundt before adding the jam. I agree that the "one bowl recipe" designation is misleading to the extent that it suggests the recipe will not generate very many dishes. I generated a goodly number. The sugary "crust" on this cake is remarkable! Make it!

This well over cooked for me, I would reduce the oven temperature. The bottom of the cake was very dry. The way I read the recipe was to flip the cake out of the bundt tin and butter and sugar the decorative top but perhaps I was meant to butter and sugar the dry bottom? I agree with others there was a ridiculous surplus of cinnamon sugar. 

I used a raspberry conserve which was very thick so had no issues of seeping elsewhere into the cake. 

I thought there really wasn’t much flavour to this cake. Maybe a different jam and as some suggested lemon, and/or more vanilla would help.
Very good except I will reduce the nutmeg to ½ tsp

Made this and swapped the milk with buttermilk. I also had a lot of sugar left so I kept putting it all around while having the cake on a rack with a tray below. Whatever spilled down to the tray I would collect and repeat the sprinkling process until there was no sugar left.

I’ve got a bounty of jam to use up and have made this cake numerous times. I love it! Filling a bit more in the bottom and carefully spooning jam are both great tips! I’ve also started to riff on the cake/jam combos. Right now, I have a coconut curry cake (cream of coconut and coconut milk subbed for whole, a little less sugar, a lot of garam masala and some turmeric for nutmeg) with hot habanero apricot jam in the oven. Smells amazing! I found a recipe online that used some of the flavors I was looking for and it’s so tasty!!
Has anyone tried this with gf flour?
What if you used PAM to spray the pan, would this eliminate problems of getting cake out of pan?

@Mary Jane Brisbane 

@Mary Jane Brisbane I used non-Stick bakimg soray in a silicone mould (which I NEVER butter or spray, plus the said cinnamon-sugar mixture) and it still stuck in some places :-/

I used the Pam baking release spray with the flour in it, then brushed with butter & sprinkled with cinnamon sugar, and it released beautifully & cleanly from my Bundt pan (which is one of those very decorative types, with lots of small ridges)
What size bundt pan are you supposed to use? 9-cup?

A standard bundt pan is 10 inches in diameter.  I baked this once in a standard sized pan, and made it again in a smaller 7 inch diameter silicon bundt pan.  This recipe filled my 7 inch pan to about an an inch from the top (it did not overflow as it baked), but it needed a lot more baking time for that depth, so I had to reduce the heat to bake at 325 degrees or it would have been over-browned on the outside.
Does it work in a tube/angel food pan?
@Stefanie O'Keefe of course it would
Hi. Has anyone used nutella instead of jam?  If yes, did it sink?  Thanks!
What size Bundt pan please?

@EMCooks

I asked same question!  Someone said standard size?

I used the pampered chef bundt pan (the blue one from a few years ago) and it came out perfect. No sticking with a generous coating of the butter and cinnamon sugar. It last 24 hours. Now I have to make another one.

@EMCooks
I used the 75th Anniversary Bundt which is a 12 cup - a 10 cup would be perfect.

Anyone have recommendations for minimizing the cake from sticking to the pan? I used about 2 T of butter with a pastry brush and still ended up with a terrible mess after sitting for 10 minutes and trying to pry it loose with a knife.
This was really good but unfortunately the jam seeped through and then stuck to the pan. As a base, I put down more than it called for. It was still a hit but it looked pretty rustic!
Has anyone tried this with very chunky homemade applesauce?
@map I did this today with pureed apple pie filling! Worked great.
Following several comments, I put 3/4 of batter in Bundt before adding jam.  I was very disappointed that it still floated to the top of the cake.

Made this yesterday, there's a very small slice left. Used some homemade blackberry jam a friend had given us. I tried a small slice (sliver) yesterday after it cooled and it was amazing, had a small piece with my coffee this morning and sadly that's all I'll get. Husband, kid took care of most of it and I had brought my father a couple of slices yesterday to try. Super easy, so worth it, definitely 5 star. I was thinking this afternoon how good it might be with some apple pie filling.

Hey, foodies. I want to try this recipe. Is that oven temperature in Fahrenheit or Celsius? I need to know. My oven doesn’t do Fahrenheit, so I’m guessing it should be 180° C? The ingredients portion convert to metric nicely but the oven temperature in the text of the recipe just says 350.
Andy, 350 f
It didn't take enough jam so we likely should have made a deeper trench from the jam addition. The cake was still very tasty and enjoyable.

This came together very easily…I think because this is a one bowl recipe the wet ingredients don’t really smooth out until you add the flour that glues everything together.  I found the amount of nutmeg to seem high so I reduced to 1/2 teaspoon and still found it off putting in a sweet application.   I haven’t tried a piece yet so hopefully the cinnamon sugar finish balances it out.

Very nice recipe. Be VERY generous brushing the cake pan with the butter; you'll have more than enough for the final step. Ditto coating the pan with the topping. You could easily cut the topping ingredients by 1/3 and still have enough.
Based on comments I weighed 3/4 rather than 1/2 of the batter for the bottom; it looked like I was running low so I used ~2/3. Even so, I barely had enough to cover the jam. The Concord grape preserve stayed in place.
Nice flavour and very easy to make. Put three quarters of the batter at the bottom and cherry jam on the top. No leakage or sticking.

I made this for a teacher luncheon and I have to say I’m a tad sad that it didn’t rise more (and I even bought fresh baking powder for the occasion). However, it smells amazing and it looks like an oversized sugared donut (with a secret core of jam!). I hope the teachers like it!
my cake wouldn't come out of the pan

Came out absolutely perfect I was so impressed!! Super easy to throw together but the payoff is huge. Agree that a little lemon juice or zest would be a great addition. I did a combo of four fruit and strawberry rhubarb jams and I think the tartness of the rhubarb was essential!
Does lemon curd work?
I followed the recipe and this turned out very dense. I understand that it’s supposed to pair well with coffee, but you can make tastier baked goods with the ingredients and effort.
This was awful. The jam sunk and made the top stick to the tin. Prized it out but it's a total mess.

Had no milk so I used buttermilk instead. Added a bit of baking soda to compensate for acid. The resulting cake is quite dense and bland. Next time, will try the batter part as written because my cranberry/blueberry jammy filling was a hit.
Could buttermilk be used in place of milk? Asking after some saying needing tang flavor?
@Hungry me I used buttermilk. The cake was a little flat which I attributed to the baking powder.  Next time I’ll use 1tsp baking powder plus 1tsp baking soda (which will react with the buttermilk)
Could oat milk or soy milk be a substitute for the whole mile?
I would recommend much less sugar, but still a delicious cake, thank you. Probably would do the max (20-25% less) but 10-15% less if risk averse
Epic fail. I did the .5 recipe and my batter was more like a cookie than a cake. Definitely not spoonable. Thoughts??? Help!

The flavor is quite nice but it’s actually a little fussier than a “one-bowl cake” would indicate. I used cherry jam, which is perfect for this recipe. I wasn’t entirely happy with the baking results. I took the cake out at 45 minutes when it was quite brown and bounced back. However, it needs to bake longer as I had some soggy, packed-down cake on the bottom. I’ll probably not make it again.
As another baker commented, way more cinnamon sugar than was needed (and I have a sweet tooth). Terrific recipe!
Has anyone tried making a Boston cream version with custard filling and a chocolate ganache topping?

I had been wanting to try this for a long time.  Finally did. It was OK, but I probably wouldn't bother making again. A few tips - put more than half of batter in bottom before jam because the jam does sink as it cooks. Makes way more cinnamon/sugar mix than needed. Mine was fully cooked in 40 min.

I cut back on the milk a bit and added greek yogurt which made a thicker batter. The jam stayed in place better as a result. Also replace butter with an equal volume of canola oil to reduce saturated fat. Finally, grease the pan generously! Results were excellent.
With a smaller Bundt pan and covering the jam with a scant amount of batter, this cake would be a success. That said, would doubling the recipe bake a cake as excellent?
All the jam went to the bottom for me. If I make again, I think I will try marbling.
Frustrating fail - I'm an experienced baker and used my (usually) trusty non-stick bundt pan and oiled it well - but this cake stuck to the sides and bottom of the pan.
I just made the cake and it turned out doughy. Not sure where I went wrong?!
I had great hopes for this cake.  I found it too sweet.  I did refrigerate the batter for 15 minutes and found the jam didn't sink.  Cook it for the full 50 minutes.
I reduced the sugar to 200 ... but even that is high for what I'd normally use in a cake this size but thought oh well it's a doughnut!!

I made a cake similar to this with a jam filling. The trick to prevent the jam from sinking is to bake it for a short period of time and then add the jam to the batter. The jam was added after the cake had been baked for one third of the time, so try adding the jam after 15 minutes.

One of those rare instances when I made something without reading the notes first so had my heart in my mouth about the jam sinkingto the bottom. It didn't - behaved perfectly. Phew. However, agreed that there is far too much cinnamon / sugar mixture, could happily be reduced by half. And I'm not sure that it wouldn't be better next time using granulated sugar for coating the tin but icing sugar for the topping.
This was so delicious I’m making it again. May try a loaf pan this time. Wondering the baking time. Thx

Didn’t  care for this recipe. Jam didn’t sink but cake was dense and I found it bland. I had high hopes but will stick with my go to Jam Crumb coffee cake recipe. Think it would have been better with some sour cream added…
Has anyone frozen this cake?

I made this last night and it was delicious but visually a disaster. I put more than half of the batter in, then the jam, and I got leakage above and below. Miraculously, I was able to get it out of the pan ok.
Not sure what the issue was, but I served it my book club anyway (a friendly crowd) and everyone loved it.
I'll make it again and hope for a prettier cake next time!

Many thinks to love about this recipe, but it definitely needs some tweaking. First, lemon zest in the batter, non-negotiable. Second, it’s noticeably dense/chewy. With a ton of melted butter (not creamed), a lot of flour, and just a little milk, the poor baking powder is literally doing all the heavy lifting alone, and it’s just impossible. Next time I’ll add as much sour cream or yogurt as milk, with 1/2 tsp baking soda. And perhaps I’ll give up on melted butter entirely and just cream the thing with a hand mixer. The reality is that there are no shortcuts to aeration…

title: Sheet-Pan Gnocchi With Sausages and Peppers Recipe
author: Melissa Clark

comments.commentBody: I think the sausages look and taste better if browned first before putting in the oven.
@Garrett.  And then reduce the pan juices with a little dry wine, pour over all before serving!

Snowy stay home kinda night tonight. Made exactly according to recipe. Wonderful, easy peasy dinner with great flavor. Gnocchi cooked perfectly as directed contrary to concerns raised by others. Melissa would never lead us wrong. Perfect dinner for a cold snowy night!

Haven't made this yet, but have made the other Crispy Gnocchi and Sausage recipe and it's awesome. That one cooks 20-25 minutes at 425 and the gnocchi are perfect. I think 40 minutes would turn them into dust. Watch it if you try this recipe! (Maybe add gnocchi half way through?)

One word: delicious. I made this exactly according to the recipe, using four Italian chicken sausages, with one exception: I turned my oven to convection for the last 15 minutes to aid the browning. I seem to have purchased extremely juicy cherry tomatoes, and the sauce they created, while delicious, hindered browning until I did the convection trick. The recipe produced four generous servings for my family, and a small gnocchi snack for the cook’s lunch tomorrow. Thank you, Melissa Clark for yet another wonderful sheet pan recipe.

I find it often takes quite a bit of time for gnocchi alone to develop a nice chewy crust in the oven. With (juicy) tomatoes in the mix, wouldn’t it be better to roast the gnocchi BEFORE adding other ingredients?
I thought this was WONDERFUL! It’s now my go-to sausage and peppers meal. So easy. The garlic, olive oil, oregano, and red pepper made it so flavorful, and the gnocchi made it substantial. Love it!

We followed the recipe nearly as written, except for pre-browning the sausages which was unnecessary, and a different mix of peppers. Using store bought gnocchi is essential (the whole point of the recipe). Everything cooked perfectly at 425 for about 30 minutes. We loved the dish. Another hit from MC.
@WT Curious - what peppers did you use as a substitute? I’m not a fan of bell peppers

Very good and easy. My first time buying shelf stable gnocch and I admit that the long list of ingredients made me shudder. This would not work with fresh gnocchi. While this was a good recipe, I’ll go back to my normal addition of new potatoes instead of gnocchi.
@Kathryn Perhaps look for a different brand next time if you want to try again. Mine has six ingredients including lactic acid for preservation.
Making this stove top gives you a lot more control, particularly the gnocchi. Delicious.

I make gnocchi from time to time.  I can't imagine gnocchi at 425 degrees for 35-40 minutes being anything other than burnt crisps.  if anyone has actually tried this recipe, I'd love to know how it came out.

@hp
This recipe will only work with shelf stable gnocchi.  Your homemade gnocchi would, indeed, be a disastrous substitution.

Thanks to Andrea, RC, and Garrett for your comments on this recipe. They were very helpful as I made it tonight for dinner. Divided the seasoned oil in half and tossed vegetables separately from the gnocchi. Then, I browned the sausages before putting them on the baking sheet with the seasoned vegetables.  Keeping the gnocchi aside, I roasted the sausages and vegetables for about 12 minutes, made an empty spot in the middle of the baking sheet and spread in one layer in that spot. I cooked it another 10 to 12 minutes, turning the gnocchi over about halfway through. It was a good recipe and I will probably make it again. Next time, I think I will take the seasoned gnocchi and pan fry it on the top of the stove, since they were still a little pale but nothing else needed to be cooked longer. Maybe I'll use the same pan I browned the sausages in to keep the clean up to a minimum and to give the gnocchi some additional flavor.
@'Cilla thank you for this summary. Much appreciated. I'm following your tweaks!

I make a chicken sausage version of this dish, sans gnocchi, but I do it on the stove. In a frypan, you can get a good caramelization on the onions, then add elements in succession. I cut the sausages into large chunks, so they get more browning on all edges. There is no reason the above recipe could not be  done this way. I think it would taste better. I would crisp the gnocchi separately, and stir them into the rest of the ingredients at the end of cooking.

@Peaches - Thank you for your tip that this would also be good with chicken sausage. However, I wonder how you can say that stove-top cooking would improve this recipe when you haven’t actually made this recipe. One of the beloved features of sheet pan recipes is that you mix the ingredients up, put them in the oven, and then can go about anything else one desires until dinner is done cooking.
I made this tonight and just put everything on the tray. It cooked up very nicely. Some of the gnocchi was crispy which was fine.
I made this, and it was excellent. I suggest doubling the number of peppers.  The sausage is good but you can omit them and it would still be a great dish.
@Michael Paluszek totally agree! I also thought you could slice up the sausages and bring it as a “salad” to a pot luck!
Very nice and easy> Delicious.

I have been doing this for a few years now once I discovered I could make a complete dinner on one pan. Add a piece of parchment for quick clean up, and it will rock your world! I typically cut up my sausages, but either way works. I always have a package of gnocchi in the pantry when I'm needing a quick dinner that has everything - protein, veggies, and some carbs. You can even do this with chicken tenderloins or meatballs. Just adjust the cook time as needed. Drizzle pesto as a finish - MWAH!
I served over arugula and added a splash of red wine vinegar when served. Highly recommend the addition!
Would Trader Joe's frozen Gnocchi al Gorgonzola work in his recipe?

@SusanB
I think the sauce would burn and the Gnocchi would not crisp.  If you’re determined to try it I would roast the sausage & veggies and then finish on the stove top - cooking the Gnocchi per directions and then adding the sausage & veggies.  Maybe reconsider what herbs and in the end you would not be following this recipe at all.
I would say sear sausage on top of stove to enhance flavor and color!
I tossed some radicchio with olive oil and balsamic and added it in the last 10 minutes.  Delicious!

I’ve made this a bunch and the only thing I change is I double the garlic, add dried basil, and use more salt. I think the most important change though is turning the temperature down to 390-400, and definitely not going for 45 mins. I find that otherwise the gnocchi gets mushy. At a lower temp the gnocchi stays crispier.

My husband wanted sausage and peppers for his birthday dinner. So, I was happy when this recipe popped up. I made it according to the directions and it was delicious! The recipe is easy to follow and it comes together quickly. Thanks NYT!

Browned the sausages first, and cooked for about 25 min. Added some spinach to my mixture. My gnocchi was cooked through so I pulled them without the gnocchi getting crispy, but I didn’t miss the crispness anyway. Served up with a sprinkle of feta - mega delicious, will definitely make again!

This is a winner!
Made it as recommended with a few minor changes. We lightly browned the sausages before adding to the gnocchi vegetable mixture and added the cherry tomatoes after 15 minutes. The final touch was lightly drizzling pesto over the melange before plating. This really enhanced the flavor.

This was a wonderful and easy meal.  I followed the recipe as written except taking the advice of one or two others, I switched from regular oven to convection in the last 10-15 minutes.  I thank all of you who post your comments.  I do enjoy reading them all.  Some suggested a more complex prep with pre browning, etc.  While this may have added a bit to the final product, for me the recipe got 5 stars for ease of prep and clean-up resulting in a delicious meal.  I love these sheet pan meals!  Thank you NYT!

Cooked as is! This recipe is delicious and dead simple. Paired with a salad on the side ”sweet” side (i.e. dates, fruit…) it makes a very nice recipe for dinner guests. Most all the prep can be done ahead so you can just mix and match. Yum.
To Newbie: I use a 13" x 18" Nordic Ware baking sheet, and I line it with aluminum foil so it's not such a mess to clean up.
@Jo Ann thank you so much, I’ll buy something similar
We aren’t huge fans of gnocchi in my house so I subbed it for small cubed potatoes. Turned out delicious! I would definitely make this again.

I cooked the sausages separately because they were chipolatas and were smaller than usual sausages. I don't think the tomatoes belong in this dish from a flavor or texture perspective and I definitely added red wine vinegar at the end to the veg and gnocchi. But I wasn't wowed by the dish and not sure I would make it again.

Delicious! I made as directed, using red onion and adding some white mushrooms to make up for the deficit of a 9 oz. package of gluten free potato gnocchi. The caramelization was perfect and the only change I will make next time is to add another colored pepper, as others have suggested. And there will be a next time very soon!

Made this the other night - as with all recipes I adjusted to suit what I had in hand (no onion so used shallots - added mushrooms) - and as has been mentioned by others I was concerned about lack of browning on the sausages but an extra 5 minutes in the oven solved that - re the suggestion of browning them first, I don't know but I would imagine that may leave them in danger of being overcooked ?? Anyway net result it was a fantastic tasting dish, far exceeding expectations - will have again !

I went to the store to get the ingredients and forgot the Gnocchi.  I still made it with everything else and served it with some hard rolls I had.  Wow!  This came out so well and is so so easy.  I cut the sausage up into chunks to mix it in with the veggies and it's helped make a lovely sauce.   

I did not find I needed to brown the sausage - the veggies and sausage all had a little char on the due to the high temps.
What does the term “shelf stable” mean. Would these be gnocchi that you would find in the dairy case?

@Sue E No, these are vacuum packed gnocchi that you’ll find in the pasta aisle.  They generally come in a small flat plastic tray, about a pound more or less, depending on what brand your store has. I picked up a package yesterday for this recipe, and it was $1.98.

I like these sheet-pan gnocchi recipes, but not with whole sausages - the flavors don’t meld.  I use bulk sausage and pinch off bits to scatter among the gnocchi and vegetables.  That way each bite has a mix of flavors.  And refrigerated fresh gnocchi work great, that’s what I always use.

Very easy and flavorful and filling. I used a good Andouille sausage. Was skeptical about the gnocchi but it turned out perfect. I did brown the sausage a little bit for color. Added a handful of already cooked broccoli and baby spinach towards the end. Will put on repeat.

Followed the recipe much closer than usual and it
 came out as billed. The sausage browned nicely, the gnocchi crusted, everything was nicely cooked at 40minutes. This goes on the standard rotation. I did have two variances: added some cremini I needed to use, and mushroompepperandonion is a no brainer. Used sweet onions because I always do.

I think I overcooked the gnocchi.  I followed instructions until the 25 minute point, and the gnocchi were still un-golden, with the sausages still in need of browning.  I added two 5 minute additions to the roasting, but by that time half of the gnocchi where tough to the bite, and half were still undercooked.  (The sausages and vegetables were tasty.)   Should we try something like this again, I think I would brown the protein separately, and use fresh gnocchi on its own schedule.

Made this simple, one-pan recipe last night. My family gave it a unanimous thumbs up.  It’s colorful and flavorful.  I’m sure there are tweaks that would make it even better, but after working all day, I just really appreciated the ease of prep and the TRULY one-pan quality. Thanks NYT Cooking!

Cooked exactly as written using Italian sausage links. It was an easy and tasty meal. The sausages browned beautifully in the oven, and the gnocchi came out with a wonderful mix of chewy and crispy textures. There's no need to alter the recipe at all.

We made this last night and it was a big hit.
Since I have two hungry teenagers at home, I’ll try the following tweaks next time:
More gnocchi, more tomatoes— and less salt.  The sausages (we used chicken Italian sausages) and Parmesan cheese are plenty salty already.

Made this tonight, came together easily and will make again, but needs more peppers and tomatoes.  Cooked at 425 for 25 minutes and then turned on the air fry setting for an additional 5 minutes to get the gnocchi a bit more crispy.  It could have used another 5 minutes.  But very easy and tasty!

Went much bigger with veggie cuts (i.e., whole garlic cloves and pepper quarters, and small/halved cocktail tomatoes), put into in a heavy ceramic baking dish, then substituted pierogies for the gnocchi, and cooked lower + slower (375 ~45m-1hour). No flipping, only shaking. Was phenomenal and so easy (and pierogies are likely even better than the original recipe). YUM!

We made this tonight subbing GF shelf stable gnocchi and eliminated the sausage since I'd made fresh meatballs the day before and used them instead. Wow! The gnocchi were very tasty and had some bite/tooth and a concentrated potato flavour lacking when boiled.  My Italian nona would be impressed.  Will make again and again

Absolutely delicious. We added some broccoli, and used mini gnocchi. We did roast on convection for the last 20 minutes to help crisp the sausage. A great dish to make anytime of year, for any occasion.

Someday the all-in-one sheet-pan recipe will fall deservedly out of favor. In the meantime, I should know better than to fall for recipes like this. Grilled (or broiled or pan-fried) sausage with sauteed onions and peppers is simple and classic and incomparably better than what you end up with following this recipe. A loaf of good, crusty bread is incomparably better than gooey, tasteless "shelf-stable" gnocchi (I used DeCecco, in case you're wondering) roasted along with everything else.

I made this for the first time exactly as shown. It came out perfectly and was delicious! The oil and herb mixture was lovely. I resisted the urge to par boil or brown the sausages, which was a good call. Yes, shelf stable gnocchi are needed. Next time I will serve it on a bed of wilted spinach and mind the size of my garlic cloves. I used two large ones, which was a bit much.
I didn’t have onion on hand so I added butternut squash. I also sprinkled on paprika, garlic powder and onion powder. This was amazing!

Just made tonight, mostly to the recipe.  I cut the hot sausage links into chunks which gave them a better chance to fully cook.  Sprinkled Italian seasoning rather than just oregano.  Baked about 30 minutes, stirring once, and then broiled for a couple of minutes to brown the tops. Keeper!
I have tried pan-frying several shelf-stable gnocchi, and will now only use DeCecco brand gnocchi no.401. No, I do not work for them! My grandkids love these gnocchi browned in olive oil and butter.

Has potential, but I will need to try again. I sliced my onions thin and they burnt to a crisp. The gnocchi were overdone after 30 minutes in the oven - they were too tough and chewy. Maybe it matters that I used chicken sausage, which is less oily. My thoughts for next time: brown the sausages on the stove first, add a lot more tomatoes (my hope is that they'll burst and add some liquid to help with keeping things softer), and either shorten the time or lower the oven temperature.

Wow!  This was fantastic!  I added a bit more garlic, did one orange and one bell pepper, used red onion and just a bit more oil.  Everything else as written.  I will definitely put this in our rotation!  FYI, if you make this with already cooked chicken sausages, I would recommend adding them the last 10 min or so just to warm!

Delicious!  I incorporated a few reader’s notes such as cutting the sausages in half and tossing them in the oil as well, and turning the oven to convection for the last 10 minutes. Served over a plate of spinach which wilted just enough under the hot veggies.
Great weeknight dinner!  I like to pour a few tablespoons on heavy cream over before grating on the parm.  Then as you scrape the crispy gnocchi off the sheet pan it forms an parm garlic cream sauce.
Made it with green & yellow squash(instead of peppers), because that's what i had, worked beautifully. Otherwise followed recipe as written.
I love this recipe! Made exactly as written,  it's fantastic.

In our house a sausage and bell pepper with marina sauce pasta was our go to easy comfort dinner. My husband suggested we try this recipe and it instantly became our new favorite. Thank you Melissa Clark and NYT cooking. Only change is we use two red, yellow or orange bell peppers instead of green. We use hot Italian sausages and crushed red pepper.
I use a pound of Italian pork sausage meat and scoop it into little balls.

We love this dish, and I've made it 4 times (so far) and it's consistently delicious.  I use chicken Italian sausage, and let people add parm to taste.  The gnocchi always comes out perfectly chewy in the suggested roasting time.  I do bypass the putting everything in a big bowl step, and just stir everything together directly on the sheet pan.

I used shishito peppers because I had an abundance of them from my garden. Otherwise I followed the recipe and it was excellent. 
I am definitely making this again.

I dispute the direction to thinly slice the onions and peppers. I cut mine 1/4 inch and I would go to 1/2 inch next time. Cooked the full 40 min and it would be just right with thicker cut veggies. I added in some fennel seeds, because why not!

Tasty! Easy prep and very forgiving with ingredient combos. Didn’t have enough tomatoes or peppers, threw in some cabbage. Didn’t have any Italian sausage, threw in breakfast links (IKR). Warm, hearty, got my veggies in AND have some leftovers for tomorrow :)

Outstanding! Made exactly as written, but followed another comment on using convection setting.  The sausages browned beautifully, the gnocchi were delightfully crispy and it was ready in 25 minutes.  I served my portion over arugula to up my veggie intake.  Will definately make again and again. Enough leftover for lunch for hubby and me.

One of the best NYTC recipes I have EVER made! We love veg, so I doubled the peppers to 4 (one of each color) and put in 1.5 red onions, along with 1.25x the spices, garlic, and oil. I roast it on a giant full sheet pan. Takes longer than advertised to cook by maybe 10-15 min, but so worth it! It's o regular rotation around here for weeknight meals, but is fancy enough for casual company. Seriously, make it tonight!
I mistakenly used fresh (store bought) gnocchi. Wish me luck.

I cook for a vegetarian and a meat eater so I look for recipes that can be adapted to serve both. Would this work if I separated the sausages to half of the gnocchi mixture?  Would there be enough fat to brown the gnocchi?  I’m sure I could add more olive oil.
I've never tried shelf-stable gnocchi  --  would frozen, like Rana's, work as well in this recipe?
The calories per serving and the fat per serving are really large! How would you change that?
We make a version of Crispy Gnocchi and Brussel Sprouts in brown butter, prepared in a skillet, frequently.  I’m a fan of shelf-stable gnocchi and can’t wait to try this variation from Melissa Clark.
Could you substitute tofu for the sausage?

Going to try this tonight. Given the comments about somewhat pale sausages, I think that this dish cries out to be made in the air fryer, as the convection heat should give the sausages a browned finish without the need to dirty a pan on the stovetop searing them. Will report back!
I made this the other night. Fantastic and very easy.

I have been making a version of this frequently, for the last 5 years or so. I make the sausages separately under the broiler or sometimes we grill them, then we slice them, and add them to the mix when the gnocchi are done. I chop the red onion coarsely instead of in slices, but I'm sure that doesn't really matter. This is one of my family's most requested recipes - we absolutely love it.
How long would you suggest cooking without the sausage?
Does anyone have a gnocchi brand to recommend? I tried De Cecco and all I could taste were the preservatives.
@Witloof — DELALLO, made in Italy, 85% potato. They crisp very nicely (Gnocchi with Burst Tomatoes).
Has anyone used frozen bell peppers and onion mix with this?
Has anyone tried it with gluten free gnocchi?
@Beth Gnocchi is made with potatoes so it’s gluten free
@Marylouise  potato gnocchi is usually combines steaming hot potato with wheat flour.  In that case it is not gluten free.
Followed the recipe and it came out perfect. Loved it!

I browned the sausages first. I doubled the sausages, the gnocchi, put 3 peppers, 1 onion and 1.5 cup of cherry tomatoes. I baked at 450F for 30 min and added parmesan the last 5 it was very good. No garlic and no salt, tasted fabulous.

This is an easy to prepare and tasty meal.  I liked that it is roasted in one pan at the same time.  I substituted turkey Italian sausage as that is what I had in the freezer and it tasted great.  I will definitely make this again!

This was so good!  I added crumbled goat cheese with tomato and basil from Aldi's at the end and it really stepped it up a notch.  Next time though, I would use loose sausage rather than links, as I could spread the meat around the gnocchi and peppers.
My wife was fasting yesterday, This recipe popped into my inbox and I made it last night. It was absolutely delicious. I had one helping and my son and the rest. I will definitely make it again.

This was very simple and very nice. I didn't have Parmesan but I thought cheese would be a nice add, so swapped it for a feta style cheese (which is very different from Parmesan - I know! But it worked very well). Definitely making this again.
Oil the pan before putting ingredients on, this was delicious, but tough to clean pan afterwards.

It’s just meh. Discovered that I’m not a fan of crunchy gnocchi. 
Not much else to recommend it.

great recipe - so easy! i added nutritional yeast, paprika & a lil lemon juice before baking - & only had the flip the sausages twice in 40 minutes. topped with cilantro & thai basil def will make again.
Has anyone made this with pre-cooked Italian chicken sausage? If so, do you put the sausage in initially, or add it later, since it only needs to warm, not cook? Your experience appreciated!

Spectacular. What a great comfort meal on a cold winter night.  We had some chorizo and used it instead of Italian sausage. Gave it a little Iberian twist. Paired with a nice Spanish red wine…fantastic!
This is so unusual, delicious, and easy!  I substituted Trader Joe’s spicy Italian sausage and tomatillos and used more onions and all red peppers

Well, 35 minutes in my 425 oven would have made this dish a pile of ash... I rescued it at 30 and had perfect sausage, overdone veggies, and mostly inedible gnocchi.  Having said that, the flavor was terrific and I now know what adjustments to make (sausages first for 10 min, then add the veggies and dumplings) so next time this will be amazing!
Yummy!

Used GF Gnocchi (shelf stable) and followed recipe. Everything was good, except those gnocchi, which were too tough and got stuck in my teeth. I felt this needed more acid, so I finished it with a squeeze of half a lemon and then stirred it all in.

Tip: I kicked the oven over to convection for the last 15 minutes, which helped with browning those sausages up nicely.

Terrific recipe. I used hot Italian sausages and roasted them first to get them nicely browned, then cut them into quarters and put them in with the rest. I also substituted Aleppo pepper for the red pepper flakes. My family absolutely loved it.

I loosely followed this recipe using vegetables that I had on hand (bell peppers, poblano peppers, brussel sprouts) along with the shelf-stable gnocchi.  The one major difference is that I cut the hot sausage into 2" chunks and pan fried them on the stove, adding to the sheet pan at the very end.  While this increased the amount of cleanup, it decreased the amount of fat in the meal, and it came out delicious.
Absolutely Fantastic… made as recipe says…. Can’t wait to make it again.

I love soft, pillowy gnocchi and this was not it.  Followed the directions exactly except for adding more tomatoes.  Gnocchi was tough and dry.  I covered everything with tomato sauce for some flavor.
Followed the recipe exactly, it was delicious. The clean up was a breeze, a keeper recipe for sure.
@LK We used a mix of “mini sweet” red and yellow peppers.
Worked well. Made it with chicken sausages. The cherry tomatoes really were flavor bombs. A keeper.

Made this in a Cuisinart air fryer using bake mode and it was done in 30 minutes.   Used a fully cooked sweet Italian sausage, flipping them a the 15 minute mark.  Didn’t have a green pepper but had excellent results with red and yellow mini peppers.  Used World Market Parmesan Gnocchi.  Excellent.
We like a lot of caramelization so I sliced the sausages to get more crispy bits and cooked on 425 convect for 40 mins, tossing halfway. Then broiled for 2 mins at the end, came out perfect

I made this with everything except the tomatoes which I thought I had but did not. I added thin asparagus instead we thought it was a good addition and would keep that with the tomatoes. The gnocchi cooked perfectly, like crispy little potato puffs.
Would this recipie work if I used fresh, store-bought (refrigerated) gnocchi?
Made this today on the stovetop, browning the sausages first and then adding the seasoned vegetables. I also added halved brussel sprouts into the mix. Delicious!

Oh, I wanted this to be a hit, but it fell flat for us. Mine came out salty, we didn’t care for the chewy gnocchi and it felt like it needed some brightness. I did add a squeeze of lemon which helped add some much needed moisture as well. Oh well, worth a shot!

This was perfection! I browned the sausages briefly but I don’t think it was necessary and won’t next time. The gnocchi was soft and a bit chewy with browned edges, and everything was done after 35 minutes. I think I will add another half cup of tomatoes next time - they were so jammy and yummy!
The gnocchi didn't really cook even when I added a little broth to the pan part way through cooking.
YUMMY! I followed the recipe, and it turned out perfect. This will go into regular rotation.

Excellent weekday dish. Reduced oil, cheese, and salt by half, added a chopped anchovy, multiplied garlic and red peppers by 5. Gnocchis were nice and crispy.  Added 5 minutes to the cook time for the hell of it. 5/5

Love this!  Had a couple of folks over for dinner & made according to recipe....ok, truth be told, doubled the garlic (cuz that's who I am), added a pasilla pepper & 1/2 a red onion, probably doubled the tomatoes - cut in 1/2 - & used some weird chickie asiago brats instead of IT sausage.  Forgot the parmesan & parsley and it still turned out *great*!  Will really, really try to make it according to the recipe next time.  Promise.....I think.

I will definitely make this on a regular basis. I followed directions, but used good chicken sausages, which I split in half long-ways before this went in the oven on a tray lined with aluminum foil, for easy cleanup. I did not know what to expect from shelf-stable gnocchi (found in the regular pasta aisle at the supermarket), but they came out crispy on the outside and soft on the inside. I baked for 37 minutes, and then sprinkled a little Parmesan and red wine vinegar before serving.

Melissa Clark knocks it out of the park again with this one. A true sheet pan meal - everything goes in at the same time, everything comes out at the same time. I didn't have gnocchi so I cooked up some cavatappi to toss things with. Next time I will drizzle with pesto and maybe even add some cabbage for the gut microbiome.

Made this tonight for the first time - within a few bites the teens asked if this could be part of the regular dinner rotation.  Followed recipe fairly closely, only alterations were doubling garlic, dumping in whole 10 oz container of cherry tomatoes, used brats because grocery ran out of Italian sausage, and drizzling/coating the sausages in olive oil after nestling them in the sheet pan.  Using more tomatoes gave more liquid to keep the gnocchi chewy and moist, and coating the sausages negated the need to brown separately.  I did turn the sausages over again at 35 min and added another 10 to get them browned and crisp on both sides and the veggies caramelized.  Everyone was hungry and started eating before I added the Parmesan, but it wasn’t missed.  Highly recommend!
Made with fresh bratwurst.  Great weekend football dinner.  Next time will reduce roasting time as the peppers were overcooked.

Made the recipe as written with the exception of  doubling everything but the sausage so I could have leftovers for the week (used two sheet pans).  It was delicious!  My gnocchi didn't come out as crispy as other sheet pan dishes but my tomatoes made a lot of juice.  I also tossed the sausages in the bowl with everything so they were coated with olive oil and had no browning issues.  I think others in the notes are overcomplicating the simplicity of a sheet pan meal -trust the process.

This is the first time I have commented on a recipe for NYT, but this one was delicious! I doubled the recipe and cooked it on 2 pans, but did everything as suggested. The flavor was amazing and everyone loved it! This will become a regular on our rotation! Thank you!
I would add another red or orange pepper to the mix, but otherwise, wonderful!

Using six sausages I followed this recipe. The end product lacked balance. Next time there will be some changes. Four sausages would have been better. I will increase the amount of the peppers to at least one and one-half each. I will decrease the amount of the gnocchi. With these changes the balance of ingredients will be better.

I made this as directed, and it was quite tasty.   Not spectacular, but good. I bought baby plum tomatoes, instead of cherry tomatoes, and put in far more than a cup. It all roasted up very nicely. The shelf-stable gnocchi was crisp on the outside and creamy on the inside, as advertised. I find that, as written, it makes about three servings.
I thought this was a great recipe. I often make gnocchi, sausage and peppers on the stove. This is a nice variation and brings out the sweetness in the tomato and nice crispy gnocchi.
Delicious! Next time I'll add one or two more peppers, depending on their size.
Excellent recipe! I made it with chicken sausage & it came out terrific.
This was delicious! As always, when trying a new recipe, I follow without deviation.  No real changes. I do like  to cook sausage on a lower temp, for longer retaining some fat but no complaints.

This is a far better dish than the level of effort would indicate. It's perfect as written for a quick, comforting dinner after a nasty day at work. The gnocchi are chewy, some are crispy, and all are delicious. I like to serve it with some greens sautéed with garlic. Chard and escarole are particularly nice.

I only had hot Italian sausage meat out of the casing. I did brown it slightly before adding to the other ingredients. The result was terrific! The texture was uniform; there were morsels of everything in each forkful.  Both hubby and I loved it. What an easy, tasty meal!

Made it tonight. Was amazing! Baked for 40 minutes. Do agree with another person that next time I will probably hold up on adding the gnocchi until about 10 minutes in. Some did become a little too crisp but otherwise terrific dish!
This dish came out very well, I did leave out the green peppers (Yuck). I will make it again in the future.

Perfect as is.  I don't know how thin is thin for slicing, so I sliced the onion and peppers just under half an inch wide.  I used the hot Italian sausage from Trader Joe's.  Everything cooked just right in about 35 minutes; the sausage was brown, the veg was soft, and the tomatoes jammy.  The gnocchi was all over the place in terms of some chewy, some crispy, and some melt-in-the-mouth soft.

Holy cow this was good. If you’re a mushroom person, quarter a container of mushrooms and add them. You won’t be sorry. I also recommend lining the pan with oversized foil for easier cleanup. 

I used a “stuffed gnocchi” that I had (tomato and mozzarella) and it came out wonderfully. Will go into regular rotation.

Incredibly easy and worth the wait in the oven. I'd love to see more recipes this simple and delicious. Might add green olives next time to give it a punch of acidity and complexity. I missed seeing the suggestion to spoon this dish over a bed of arugula and will give that a try too. Using hot Italian chicken sausage to cut calories and fat made this a guilt-free indulgence.
Cooked it with Cumberland chipolata sausages- delish!! The shelf-stable gnocchi is a clever suggestion. Will be cooking it again this evening!
Make without the gnocchi and instead add cauliflower. Maybe reduce oil by 1 T.

I made this almost as described. Only had 2 orange peppers so used those and my oven stinks, so I  upped the temp up to 440 degrees. This will be a new go to dinner. Super delicious and the sausages and gnocchi browned beautifully without any pre-browning. Maybe the oven was working better than I thought? In any case, served with baby arugula tossed with a little store bought lemon vinaigrette. Also used the mixed color cherry tomatoes and a sweet yellow onion which were great. Terrific recipe.
Way too hot, way too long a cook time. I consider this a bad recipe as written. Onions burned to a crisp. Very disappointed

I would actually rate this one 3.5 stars if I could.  Easy, tasty, but nothing spectacular.  I think I did put the sheet pan into the oven a little too early, though.  Was in a rush and didn't wait for it to get quite to 425 degrees.  I think this may have created too much steam so things weren't as browned.

This is replacing another sheet pan sausage recipe in my rotation (the other recipe has potatoes instead of gnocchi as the main difference). I spray the sheet pan with olive oil cooking spray in addition to the oil in the recipe. I also cut the sausages into about 4 pieces each, which really helps them get cooked through and brown. I cook for 40 minutes, stirring halfway through.

Give the foil a light coating of oil also, if using a foil lined baking sheet. The gnocchi stuck a bit but this was delicious! One person didn't care for the texture of the gnocchi when roasted this way but still loved the dish. We will definitely be making this again
Can anyone recommend what size baking sheet I should buy to make this recipe?
Used all the listed veg and gnocchi plus broccoli and some mushrooms to half the sausage.  Was delicious and had plenty of leftover veg for other uses (e.g. on top of avocado toast!).

Because some commenters complain, the sausages look too pale, I set them a top of the vegetables rather than nestled in the vegetables. Additionally, I drizzled them with a little bit of olive oil and salt. Turned them halfway through, and re-oiled. Beautifully brown. I also added some whole garlic clove to the mix in addition to the grated garlic. This recipe is an absolute keeper.
Made exactly as directed absolutely delicious

2/26/2025: A very forgiving recipe. I had only green peppers and 1/2lb small raw sausages. Instead of garlic I added  large shallot sliced lengthwise. Next time I’ll use 6 shallots halved lengthwise. I’ll also double the cherry tomatoes. I used a 9 by 13 roasting pan, but next time I’ll use a larger pan so everything can spread out better. It served 2 for dinner with enough leftover for one light lunch.

Made everything exactly like how it’s written except i doubled the garlic and only had a white onion on hand. Really quick and simple dish, awesome for when friends are coming over with wine and gossip and you wanna throw something together quick. This recipe is a keeper.

made this with chicken sausages and added a few glugs of red wine vinegar. it couldn't be this easy, I thought. 

it was!
Easy and delicious

I'm a fan of sheet pan recipes but this one is not 5 stars. It was pretty uninteresting. In our house we would refer to a dish like this as ballast. The gnocchi were dry and there were no pan juices. Thank goodness for the Parmesan cheese added at the end. might be better with potatoes then gnocchi.

It's good and very easy to make. Served it over arugula. Drizzled some balsamic dressing over it because it looked kind of dry. I think I sliced onions too thin because they had pretty much dissolved by the end of cooking.
Followed the recipe except left out the tomatoes and used chicken sausage.  excellent and will make again

Made this tonight for the first time, and it was a hit. Used hot Italian sausage, and with the red pepper it was spicy, but not too much.  The only thing I would do differently is to double the amount of cherry tomatoes.
This recipe is perfect. No notes.

Bulk sausage torn up into about 15 pieces.
Tomatoes halved.

Love this recipe! So versatile and so easy, used poblano peppers and fresh chorizo sausage. Yum! Can’t wait to riff on this with different sausages and veggies. The gnocchi are perfect on the sheet pan, come out chewy, not gummy! Will keep a package of gnocchi in the pantry from now on!

Absolutely delicious!! I love garlic so I added extra along with a little more red pepper flakes. I did follow other comments and sliced the sausage links into 3 chunks. The sausages were perfectly cooked and browned. The gnocchi had a lovely crispness but soft inside. I topped the dish with some toasted pine nuts and my entire family loved it!
5 stars, especially because of the ease. Maybe 20min active time and ready in an hour. Crazy easy and still very tasty. Perfect weeknight meal.
Made this for a girls’ night. It was SO easy and SO tasty, fresh, light. Mine was not this crispy and dry — the tomatoes and veggies made a delicious sauce.

Yum! Works great as written. The only "extra" things I did were to double the garlic and throw in a couple handfuls of arugula at the end. The tomatoes got saucy and the sausages perfectly browned after 40 minutes. The gnocchi got chewy-crispy. The family raved and had seconds (lucky I made a double recipe).
Incredibly good!  40 minutes worked for me.

If everyone in our family of four likes a dish it is called a 4 four meal — this received a rare 4 forks! Me, the cook because it is so easy. And everyone one else because they liked it a lot. I would add one more bell pepper to up the veggie content. Even the 2 folks who are lukewarm on tomatoes didn’t even mention them. Took advice from other commentators and ran it on convection mode for half the time. I also flipped the sausages.

Made this almost exactly as is - delicious and so easy. Gnocchi came out perfectly. My sausages were already cooked, so sliced them and stirred into the sheet pan mixture for the last 15 minutes of cooking. Don't skip the parmesan and fresh basil when serving. Might be good served with a squeeze of lemon too.

Made what I thought would be 2 servings: Doubled garlic, used two jumbo hot dogs sliced, added broccoli, 5 cloves garlic but no hot spice, whole package gnocchi, cherry tomatoes from garden, Italian seasoning, 1/2 orange pepper, white onion. Baked at 450 for 20 min and was done. Squeezed a bit of lemon on top. No cheese. Plenty of liquid from tomatoes. I would eat more if I could. Enough left for tomorrow.  Delicious, easy, will make again. Perfect for meal after long day.
really great, but too much salt for us. We would half the amount called for
I think with the Parmesan and the sausage one need not add any salt at all, or just a smidge of kosher salt to finish the dish.
I thought this was average on Day 1, but liked it much better the second day. Don’t skimp on the black pepper.

I forgot the tomatoes when making this for the second time and they are definitely necessary! Gnocchi get too chewy otherwise. I think it is better with the onions and peppers sliced thicker, so they don’t disappear. Also added fennel seeds, which was a nice addition, I think.

Followed as written apart from using homegrown frozen roasted tomatoes (added last 10 min). Delish overall but definitely benefitted from a drizzle of marinara. Would probably ditch the gnocchi (or boil them next time) as they were a bit dried out.

This is a go to favorite at my house! First of all it smells amazing when it’s cooking. Reminds me of a really hearty vegetarian pizza from the 90s! It’s so easy to make and so delicious and makes enough for leftovers if you’re in a two person household.

Made this tonight: precooked the sausages in air fryer for about 10 minutes while I mixed everything else up. Sadly, my boring oven is not convection so, while the finished product tasted good enough, nothing was crispy like we like it even at 450. After reading comments I added a spoonful of pesto and served over arugula with Romano and it was fine. Convection would’ve made a huge difference!

Out of tomatoes and don’t want to run to the store? Raid your pantry for tomato paste, either in the tube or the can. I took the advice of others and browned the sausages in a pan, separately. Tossed the other ingredients together at a slightly lower temp than called for.  Halfway through tossed the gnocchi and peppers with 3 oz of tomato paste, you could use more or less. This turned out very well, which is fortunate because we are just back from travel and there was nothing to eat.
Why would you put sausages in a gnocchi dish? Besides these aren’t authentic Italian sausages, the Italians don’t put seeds in theirs.
Cooked as directed and think it’s definitely 5 stars. Would probably leave out the gnocchi and next time just serve with crunchy French bread and everyone would be just as happy

I was disappointed in this -- I've loved other NYT sheet pan sausage or chicken dinners and the Crispy Gnocchi with Burst Tomatoes and Mozzarella that's in the NYT Easy Weeknight Dinners cookbook, but this fell flat. I followed the instructions as written and baked it at 425 degrees for 35 minutes, but the gnocchi turned out too hard to chew and too crispy. The onions were burnt to a crisp. The sausages and veggies were fine, but I was really looking forward to gnocchi.
Nice and easy dinner.  The veggies seemed to make it softer rather than crispier, though the sausage and gnocchi had some crisp.  Not sure why the veggies stopped the crisping.  Tasty anyway.

Used 12 oz. package of Aldi Organic Chicken Apple sausages from the freezer, doubled cherry tomatoes, used 1 1/2 red and yellow peppers, and added one Granny Smith apple cut in large chunks for a bit of sweetness.  30 minutes total cook time using 400 degrees on convection setting.  Turned sausages at 15 minutes.  Added dried basil to the spice mix in addition to the chopped parsley garnish.  Wondered if I should have cut tomatoes in half to release more juices. Flavorfful, easy, delicious.
My gnocchi were hard as rocks. I will make this dish again, but will substitute potatoes.
I've always precooked the gnocchi and they come out very pillowy after roasting.

this was a really good! I followed the instructions. the cook time was good. I actually cooked it on convection bake at 400°. I probably could have reduced the cooking time by a little bit because a few pieces in the corner of the pan got charred. this recipe made a ton of food! I'm a big eater and this made at least six servings. the recipe states it's three to four servings but they must be awfully hungry people. there was so much that I had to cook it on two sheet pans
I used Beyond Sausage plant based sausages and it came out great! Quick and easy!

One tweak -- use besan (aka gram flower) instead of all-purpose flour. Besan is a kind of flour made from chickpeas, available from some supermarkets and most South Asian markets. In addition to making the recipe gluten free, it also fries better than flour. Besan is used in many Indian recipes, including fried dishes like pekora. Even though I like to eat eggs and glutinous breads, I find this makes a better latke than any egg/flour-based recipe I've tried.

Made these tonight using besan (chickpea flour) in place of the all-purpose flour to keep these gluten-free.  And to go a step further, we also made the non-vegan version (Melissa’s recipe too — almost identical to these but w/egg). This vegan version was considered just as good, albeit slight different, than the traditional. One person who ate both said the vegan latkes were hands down the better version.

No reason to use an egg ever again. LOVED these!!!!
Could these be done in an air fryer?  Any suggestions for time/temp?

What's glorious with this shared vegan recipe is that readers like me who don't use eggs (or dairy) have excellent, reliable recipes -- suitable for everyone -- without having to tweak ingredients. Thank you.

I made these in the air fryer, and they came out great.  I made my patties and brushed the top of each one with olive oil, and I brushed the bottom of the air fryer basket with olive oil.  I did about 25 minutes at 400, flipping halfway after the top was nice and brown, and my family gobbled them up.  I have never made latkes before, so I do not have a basis of comparison, but these were quite delicious.
I also meant to note that I used chickpea flour per the suggestion below.
Vegan Latkes? These are latkes.
@Mike Sevrrin Without the traditional eggs, so vegan.

You can buy potato starch at many stores. It's a powder, similar in texture to corn starch. Bob's Red Mill is one company that makes it. Certified gluten free. This is what I used for latkes, rather than flour. Works great. https://www.bobsredmill.com/potato-starch.html.
@rose cedars I found my most recent batches to be a bit too chewy with potato starch, but perhaps I should tweak my measurements and not do a 1:1 sub.
Can you use potato starch that comes in a box?
@Ilene yes you can. We do that every Passover. And I’ve never used baking powder in latkes.

Instead of waiting for the starch to drain from the water, can I just use the potato starch I already have in the cupboard, mixed with the flour? Would love to know if anyone has done this! Will definitely try the chickpea flour as well. I hate flax egg; it ruins the flavor of everything.

The best commercial version I have found is Trader Joe's potato pancakes and made in an air fryer. 
Crisp outside, fluffy, incredible inside.
Addictive.
They used to be larger. So eat more.

These were wonderful!  Based on other commenters’ suggestions, I used a mix of chickpea flour & potato starch in place of the flour. When adding to the hot oil, I packed a 1/4 cup measuring cup with the potato mixture, and turned it out into the pan, flattening it with a spatula. Cleaning up the edges and adding more oil as needed while cooking, I didn’t have any problems keeping them together.  Definitely will make these again, now that I know I don’t need to use egg!

I would suggest shredding the potatoes into cold water and then rinsing and drying in a salad spinner.  Easier to separate the starch that way as well. Will try besan, good idea! Didn't use baking powder and didn't miss it.
This recipe is delicious and the egg is not missed. However. It does not yield 3 dozen latkes even if quite small. By no measure is the yield that many.
I made these for a party and they were a huge success. The baking powder made them light and fluffy.
Rave reviews from family I thought they tasted faintly of baking powder. Very easy to make, cook quickly and are very crisp on outside, tender on inside.

Non-vegan here— this is my new go-to latke recipe. I can’t believe I’m deviating from my mom‘s recipe, but this just fries up perfectly, freezes as well and reheats in the air fryer. It’s idiot proof!!
Can these be made in advance and either frozen or put in the fridge, and then reheated in the oven?

Note on yield—I grew up with latkes as the centerpiece of a Hanukkah meal, and this quantity just doesn’t do that for 4 people. I wish I’d trusted my gut and grated many more potatoes. Still good though, and easier than the winging-it egg-free versions I’ve made before. I do like the flavor of mixing in some other root veggies, but I wonder if the cohesiveness of these latkes was partly because I didn’t sub out any russets for carrots/sweet potatoes/etc.

This was fabulous as-is. No fuss. So simple! 
Great with sour cream + chives.

Tasted great and held together just fine with the recipe as is. I did make sure to extra squeeze out the liquid before adding to the dry mixture then put the combined mixture in the fridge for about 30 mins
Made this in the air fryer.  400 degrees F, 10 minutes on each side.  Sprayed with oil spray.
I found it easier to cut the halloumi into small pieces then mix the oregano and garlic with it in the bowl so you can put it all in at once
@JP. That would be so much easier! Just tried these tonight. Trying to stuff that into then pepper before the cheese was very difficult- and I had larger peppers.

@JP this is exactly what I just did. Cubed the halloumi, mixed it with the generous amount of fresh thyme from my porch, diced garlic from the basement, mix together and convection bake on 350. At the end I do a convection broil on 450 for a minute. Perfection.

In my experience, halloumi is best eaten warm because it becomes rubbery as it cools.  In Cyprus, where slices of halloumi are grilled and served as appetizers, all table chit-chat stops when the halloumi is served.  Talk resumes after the soft warm cheese is devoured.

I'll be trying this festive-looking recipe.

I have a nearly identical recipe I frequently make, usually using Queijo Coalho cheese.  I also usually add bacon on top of the cheese, and use either Little Gem, endive, or radicchio as the 'holder'.  Always a winner, great for lunch with fruit, or for breakfast with eggs.  

This is so easy to prepare, very adaptable to tastes and what you have on hand, and looks festive and  lovely.  You'll want to keep mini peppers on hand so you can always whip some up!

At the same time it is simple to roast cherry (or tiny plum) toms cut in half from stem end to base, preferably so the seeds are exposed. Either just put then on a cookie sheet, or you can remove the seeds with the flick of a finger, or poke in slivers of garlic. Then bake at any temperature from 400 degrees F to as low as 360 until semi-dry and some are beginning to brown a little. Loosen from the tray as soon as it has cooled enough to hold without a potholder.

Made these tonight as part of a mezze platter. Delicious! Used fresh thyme, garlic (2 extra cloves) & black pepper, and per the recommendations here, mixed the halloumi with the herbs and then stuffed the peppers. Served them at room temperature with olives, toasted pita and hummus.
I'm not a haloumi fan but the recipe looks fabulous. Is there another cow cheese that has a similar melting quality?
@Lilysdad I would give mozzarella a try!
@Lilysdad  Try feta. It crumbles more & melts a little more, but it should give a satisfying result.

@Lilysdad

I use goat cheese with herbs or plan. I stuff the peppers and place them on a baking sheet. I sprinkle with oregano and drizzle with olive oil. So easy and so delicious.
My only variation to this show stopper was to use Feta mixed with a slight bit of sour cream. Lots of garlic and fresh dill mixed with the cheese. Yummy!
I wanted to love this, as I have many other Melissa Clark recipes.  But this tasted boring and was too much work for the (very little) delight involved.  I will delete it from my Recipe Box.
When you flip the cheese-stuffed peppers, don't you lose the cheese to the baking sheet?  How do you avoid this?
@Elizabeth Ollen I didn’t flip them and they were perfect
@Elizabeth Ollen I used tongs for control

these recipes look amazing -- I love the NYT for cooking!  But I find that no matter how simple the recipe is, it always seems to include something that I have never heard of or would find difficult to access.  Like Halloumi.  I had to google it.  Would it hurt the writer so much to say goat's milk cheese, feta, any semi soft-semi-hard tangy cheese would work?
@randy In 2023, the US imported over 5,000 tons of halloumi.  And isn't it a bonus to learn something new?

@randy halloumi is a little more difficult to find, but worth it if you can.  It has a very particular quality that holds up really well for grilling/roasting and also has a unique flavor. The other cheeses might taste good, but will not be equal, imo.
@randy They sell halloumi at Aldi. You can probably find it not too far from home assuming you’re not somewhere super remote. It’s pretty good!
I made these in my air fryer tonight and they are awesome. Check half way to turn. Amazing!

Wow! So easy and simple and full of flavor. I used the same bowl after salting and oiling the peppers and tossed the halloumi with Italian seasoning . I also used halloumi-style fries from Aldi. I'm not sure they're the real thing but they tasted delicious and were a fraction of the price. Really easy to stuff too.

I love the Halloumi style fries from Aldi! Very affordable!
I crisp the ‘fries’ in a pan then cut into cubes to use as croutons  for salads or pasta
With fresh oregano and garlic these were so delicious and the colorful peppers made it look festive as well. I also made it with feta and oregano which was equally good.

This was wonderful.  In addition to pre-mixing the cubed halloumi in a bowl with garlic and herbs, next time I will not toss the peppers in oil before stuffing.  I'll try brushing on the oil while on the sheet pan.  I had difficulty stuffing the peppers whilst they were sliding out of my hand.

In the convection oven, I did not need to flip.
The whole family will enjoy this delicious treat.

Couldn't find haloumi, so used paneer, which was ok. But we would've have probably preferred something like mozzarella that would melt. Despite that minor change, it was excellent and both agreed we'd make this again.

So Good!
Three of us fought over 8 peppers. Served with loaf stuffed with tomato sauce, onion slices, mozzarella cheese and a salad.
Read the notes so I put oil, garlic, herbs (basil&oregano), S&P, dash cayenne in large bowl. Tossed the peppers in the bowl getting oil,etc inside& outside well coated.
Cut the halloumi and tossed in same bowl.
Stuffed halloumi into peppers. 
Convection Roast 25 mins.
Did not flip them.
Eaten too quickly to dip in anything.
Wonderful little treats
Less salt or taste halloumi first
Delicious! Used paneer mixed with garlic and oregano like JP suggested and fake crostinis I made from sandwich bread.
The Halloumi sold here has mint in it. Is that normal, or weird? The photo looks mint-less.

I was planning to make this to bring to a dinner party.  Since I have to travel 30min to the party home and after reading comments that halloumi cheese is rubbery when cooled I decided to use feta instead.  There was one comment about using feta mixing with a little sour cream, fresh thyme and garlic. I decided to try this.  The issue with feta is it would melt and pool outside of the peppers.  I had to scoop the cheese back into the peppers after roasting and add someone cheese to make up for the loss of spilled cheese.  Nevertheless, the result was delicious.  Everyone at the party loved it.  The flavor is sweet (from the peppers), salty (from the feta) and a bit tangy (sour cream). Very nice.  I served it with crostini using ciabatta bread from Trader Joe’s and that was excellent as well. 

I would definitely make this again, but I will either use something to keep the peppers up to keep the cheese from spilling out or roast the peppers and cheese separately and stuff the peppers after roasting.  Will report back the next time I
Thought this was smeh. Maybe I needed to roast it longer??

I used Fontina cheese that needed to be used up. As suggested, I tossed the cheese with all the flavorings and then stuffed the peppers. The one change I’ll make in future is to arrange a piece of foil so that the peppers stand up and keep my soft cheese inside. I know it wouldn’t have been a problem if I’d used halloumi. Even with the cheese pooled around the roasted peppers, this was delicious. Will definitely make again.
thanks for the tip about adding the spices to the cheese, made this very easy

I’ve stuffed jarred Piquillo peppers with marinated cubes of feta and black olive bits which were delicious but this sounds like it’s right up there for a quick appetizer too!
Will get some mini peppers next shopping trip!
I had gorgonzola so I used that, these were subtley divine.  Sweet, tangy, salty.

hmi first saw halloumi cheese at a farmers market years ago. The cheesemonger cut small cubes and grilled them on a hibachi. It takes longer to use grilled cubes in this recipe, but the char is extras delicious.
As others, I've made this recipe with the goat cheese, garlic and herbs. So good.
I stuffed the peppers with cubed queso fresco and added fresh sage. Amazing!

This was a very basic dish that my family found a little plain. I agree with the comments that this may be better with a cheese other than halloumi (which kind of fell out of the peppers and wasn't nearly as tasty as grilling it).  That said, I served it over rice pilaf with an arugula, pear, white bean salad and it was a healthy, easy meal.
Has anyone prepped these completely  in advance? If so, is a day ok for full prep before baking?
I painted the crostini with pesto then topped with cooked peppers, delish
This just seems to beg to be grilled rather than roasted in the oven. I’m going to give it a try.

This was delicious. Used fresh marjoram. Didn’t turn half way and it was fine. Also mixed the halloumi,garlic and herb together for stuffing. Prep time was more than 5 minutes. Will definitely make again.
All the halloumi exited the peppers once it melted, leaving tasty but cheeseless peppers in a sea of melted cheese. Delicious, but I’m not serving this to third parties.

We made this for a party last night. While it turned out well, and we will likely make again, the prep time was not at all accurate for us. I would plan for at least 15 minutes of prep before and then another 5 for final serving assembly. We used fresh chopped sage as our herb and served on toasted freshly sliced baguette (oil and flaky salt).

I made these for a party and everyone enjoyed them. I did as others suggested and cut the cheese up small enough and then mixed it with the garlic and oregano for stuffing. The cheese was definitely not as enjoyable once it cooled, but I'll make these again and make sure I have a warming plate of some sort underneath to help with that.

Made these last night for a Christmas dinner party, met with raves! To make things easier, after tossing the peppers in oil, I stood them up inside a mini muffin tin making it super easy to add the garlic, herbs and cheese. I then baked them right in the tin, no turning needed!
Love this recipe! And have occasionally adapted to use a large sweet pepper that has been halved to make a delicious dinner!
I should've put less salt, I didn't realize halloumi would be so salty. I put a lot of different herbs from the garden and garlic, it was delish.
I needed protein so I added some Spanish chorizo.
So delicious!

DARN. This looks so great but I was hoping some of this fabulous community would already have posted ideas of what did and did not work. As the acknowledgement goes, I used to be indecisive... now I'm not sure...

@KMoodus I’ve done fresh heirloom tomatoes and blue cheese—that combo warms up in your oven very nicely. A char on it from the broiler is good too. Drizzle with olive oil before or after. Lots of black pepper. Add a touch of green arugula and give it that Italian festive look. Great time of year for these. They are tasty look good and easy! Thanks for the reminder NYT.

@K Moodus I am going to try pear and Gorgonzola with a bit of olive oil and garlic. I am also planning one with tomato, basil, and mozzarella as these seem like great combos that work well in other areas!

It looks beautiful indeed, and I understand that an unfussy presentation is intended, but the pieces of prosciutto, arugula, persimmon and such randomly strewn across the cut pieces of focaccia looks to become a sloppy mess as guests serve themselves. Perhaps best to sacrifice appearance for cleanliness by cutting after fully assembled.

I brought this to a gathering, and people kept reaching for more. Excellent suggestion that seems effortful when you don’t have time to cook. I fried shallots in butter for the base. I halved the butter, but I think I’ll use 1/4 of what it calls for next time since it was a little rich. My grocery store was out of ricotta, so I used crème fraiche instead. Added a generous amount of arugula, sprinkled toasted walnuts, shaved Parmesan, and prosciutto. Drizzled hot honey, balsamic vinegar, and a generous amount of olive oil. Added flaky salt and peppered well. It is an easy crowd-pleaser that looks impressive without much effort.
It says cook for 10 minutes…when and what? Somehow I don’t see it.
Is buying a ready made focaccia and spreading on store bought toppings really considered a recipe?
It is when you're a non-cook looking for something easy to bring to a very large gathering! And that excludes hot dishes or any kind of dip (too many hygiene issues).
Does this get cooked before or after the toppings are added, and at what temperature?
you add the toppings to a fully-cooked focaccia

Fabulous recipe . My homemade pumpkin focaccia was a little dry so I used both butter and a homemade goat ricotta for the base. I had some leftover bacon, arugula , a tomato , some thin sliced onions, a small amount of broccoli . I topped with grated Parmesan . This may be one of the best easy recipes I have made in a while. I think I will do both butter and ricotta next time , as well. It was a thick focaccia.
What's the best vegetarian option for this treat?

I am sorry but this seems rather obvious. The recipe says the toppings are optional and then it says: 

Tip
Top your focaccia with any of the following: prosciutto, salami, baby arugula or baby kale, cherry tomatoes (halved if large), olives, thinly sliced red onion, radicchio (sliced into ribbons), sliced persimmon, toasted walnuts, shaved Parmesan, crumbled blue cheese, bocconcini or mozzarella (torn into pieces), capers, pepperoncini, anchovies or sardines. --> It's very clear which are veg.

We make a pizza, copied from a local place that specializes in flatbreads, that has chunks of roasted butternut squash, and blue or gorgonzola cheese. Could add some thinly sliced red onion if you like.  At the last minute add baby arugula for a bit of a wilt. Could add sage if you like. Yummy!

Made and everyone loved it. I added honey into the ricotta for a sweet flavor as the base, added baby arugula, topped with salt and pepper.  drizzled balsamic vinaigrette and topped with candied pecans.
I couldn't find any focaccia of course at two different markets so I made the "fast and easy focaccia" from G. Ko on this website and it turned out lovely.
Do you cook this?  At what temperature and for how long?  Before or after toppings?
I don't think so. The focaccia is already cooked though I suppose if you wanted to warm your toppings up for some reason.
Slice BEFORE you top. Doing it after makes no sense.

My friend made a fresh focaccia for this. I riffed on the ricotta suggestion mixing in artichoke parts I sizzled the prosciutto and used beautiful persimmons… lots of black pepper: here’s the problem: the bread to topping ratio was too high … focaccia too thick. I wished it had been 1/3 or even a half as thick. Now I have 3/4 of it in the fridge the day after 😔

Agree photo vs reality would be quite a gap for a pickup appetizer, but ricotta and toppings on toasted (cooled) flatbread seemed reasonable. I spread ricotta, cut and reassembled on a board, and layered appropriately sized prosciutto and sliced persimmon on the individual pieces. Not as pretty as the picture, but good! I didn't want to mess with a green, but on reflection "scattering" blue cheese and arugula would work. Best not to overload and start with things that anchor well on the bread.

I make a variation of this that bakes together- essentially pizza crust with a thin layer of either BBQ sauce or yangnyeom sauce, with peach slices, torn burrata, thinly sliced shallots and fresh basil. If I'm feeling decadent, I drizzle truffle glaze and prosciutto would work too. Then I bake until crispy. Beware: peaches are very juicy and this may drip in your oven!

We had some very nice "boards" when we were in Verona last year. They basically used the toppings on a board or platter and served the bread with an olive oil dip on the side, instead of under the other stuff. Much easier to eat.
I made this recipe this evening, but was very confused with the instructions or lack of .

I make this for dinner. I make the base with focaccia (or something TJs pizza bread) ricotta, olive oil (i use garlic olive oil) salt and pepper. Heat at 375 for 10 minutes. Then cut into 5 x 5  pieces. I put out toppings like arugula, tomatoes, avocados, figs, prosciutto, or whatever I have left in the fridge and people pick what they like. It’s a great meal when people who can’t agree what they want to eat.

I don’t see anything about heating an oven or cooking this before adding toppings!  And I’m concerned that the focaccia may be tough if it’s not heated.  What’s the recommendation, here?  To add the ricotta then heat in oven for 10 minutes at, say 375 or 400?

I had a version of this recently at a great bakery cafe in Rome. They put high quality ricotta, pesto, arugula and speck between 2 layers of whole grain house made focaccia and it was fantastic!  Use your favourite ingredients is the best way forward and enjoy it!
